FPMT  BASIC PROGRAM  
STAGES OF THE PATH

Middle Length LAM-RIM 
(LAM RIM ‘BRING BA) 
 
 
  
 
    
 
by Geshe Losang Jamphel 
NALANDA MONASTERY  
15 FEBRUARY - 9 JUNE 2010  
LOWER SCOPE  
 
  
Oral commentary translated from th e Tibetan by Ven. Sonam Tharchin 
Transcribed & lightly edit ed by Ven. Ven. Lobsan g Jinpa and Tom Cerny.  
 
Middle Length Lam-Rim  translated by Philip Quarcoo

1 WEEK ONE  TO SEVEN : 15 FEBRUARY   9 JUNE  2010  
 
Oral Commentary to Lama Tsongkhapa’s  
Middle Length Lam -Rim 
by Geshe Losang Jamphel  
 
Note: Middle Length Lam -Rim by Lama Tsongkhapa with additional outlines by Trijang Rinpoche , 
translated from the Tibetan by Philip Quarcoo © FPMT Inc. is indented.  
 
 
WEEK ONE: 15  19 FEBRUARY 2010  
 
MONDAY 15 FEBRUARY 2010  - SESSION 1 
ADVICE  
We have now completed two years of our program, and for the whole third year we 
will study the Middle Length Lam -Rim, composed by Lama Tsongkhapa. The quality 
of these Lam -Rim teachings is that they contain all the extensive teachings of the 
Buddha arranged according to a gradual sequence. Through this arrangement of the 
gradual path, it is made very easy to train in them and put them into practice. As a 
result,  it is very easy to proceed along the path.  
  In these teachings of the Lam -Rim we have, on the one hand, an extensive 
presentation of all the different teachings from the point of view of the small, 
middling and great scope practitioners. But if you want to put all the teachings into a 
nutshell, then it all comes down to the minds of definite emergence, bodhicitta, and 
the correct view of emptiness. Consequently, in a more extensive way, you can talk 
about all the practices being of the three scopes, but f rom a more condensed point of 
view, you can include them all into The Three Principle Aspects of the Path.  
 If we relate these three principle aspects to the practitioners of the three scopes, 
then the first practice of the small scope is connected with th e mind of definite 
emergence. This is the case because all the practices described for the practitioners of 
the small scope are like the path that leads up to the mind of definite emergence, or in 
other words, they are the preliminaries required in order t o attain it. You could say 
they are the cause for attaining this mind of definite emergence, and as a result, the 
practices of the small scope are indispensible. Such a practitioner is mainly practicing 
with the motivation of seeing future lives as more im portant than the present life, and 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 2 – week one to seven  without such an outlook, you definitely won‟t be able to develop this mind of definite 
emergence.  
 The practitioner of the middle scope is a person who is mainly aiming at his 
own liberation based on this mind of definite  emergence. Without this mind of 
wanting to liberate yourself, then you will never proceed to a mind that wants to 
liberate all other sentient beings from samsara. From this, we can see how the practice 
of the middle scope, i.e. aiming for one‟s own libera tion, is the prerequisite for 
attaining full enlightenment. The third principle aspect, the correct view, has the 
capacity to pacify the obstacles faced by the practitioners of the middle and great 
scopes, which is why they have to rely on it.  
 When we lo ok at all the required practices in the context of the three scopes, 
then all these produce the path that we need to walk. There is no other path, either 
greater or smaller, than right these practices described here. All the texts of the 
Tengyur and Kangyu r can be categorized into these three scopes, and as a result, there 
is no shortcut or any more extensive presentation to be found anywhere else.  
 Therefore, we need to understand how the practices of the small scope are 
something we need to practice, beca use without these, there is no way we could attain 
to a practice of the middling or great scope. Moreover, without relying on the small 
and middling scope there is no way to enter the great scope, as there is no elevator 
carrying you straight to the higher  scope without firstly relying on the lower ones.  
 
Now we need to come to know what the practices of these scopes are, and that 
is done by studying the texts that describe them. As a result, it is pretty much 
impossible to enter any of these three scopes without previously studying them.  
 All of us have come here with the great aim of engaging into dharma studies. 
Therefore, I ask you to perceive our studies like a university course. In order to first 
go to university, you would have to go to secondary sch ool, and only then would you 
be allowed to enter university studies. In the same way that this student couldn‟t skip 
secondary school, and immediately go to university, a student can‟t just skip the small 
scope in order to directly study the higher one.  
 Since we all came with this motivation to engage into vast studies, which will be 
our main project here, we should now also act in accordance with this motivation. If 
on the side you also engage into different meritorious activities, like doing migtsema 
prayers or accumulating a hundred thousand Tara praises or prostrations in order to 
support your studies and dispel obstacles, then together with the main activity of 
studying, this will be very excellent.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
3  Here I am talking about daily practices. Let‟s say o n a daily basis you recite one 
mala of the migtsema. This is something that would be supportive and beneficial to 
your studies as Tsongkhapa is also the author of the text we are studying. Additionally, 
making these requests to Chenrezig; the manifestation  of the compassion of all the 
buddhas, Manjushri; the manifestation of the wisdom of all the Buddhas, and 
Vajrapani; the manifestation of all the activity of all the buddhas, all being united in 
Lama Tsongkhapa himself, and making requests to him while see ing him as the guru, 
would be a very powerful support in your main activity of studies. This practice is 
actually a form of guru yoga. I said there is no stronger or better way to accumulate 
merit than doing practices that are based on your guru, and as su ch, this migtsema 
prayer is such a powerful support to your studies. I would advise you to do one mala 
of the migtsema prayer each day.  
 There might be some people who don‟t have so much respect for studies, and 
say that what is really powerful is deity yo ga and tantric practices. Some people might 
have taken a lot of initiations, and do a lot of tantric practice, and as a result, they 
would think that doing studies is quite a low form of practice. Here I said it is correct 
that deity yoga is very powerful,  and it is important to engage in it, but while doing this 
we have to ask ourselves if we actually have the basis for this tantric practice, which 
for example is the mind of definite emergence. We have to check if we have this mind 
in our continuum. To go beyond that, in order to liberate all beings from samsara and 
gaining complete enlightenment, we have to check if a sincere and actual aspiration 
(referring to a longing kind of faith) for this mind is really present in us. And with 
regards to the empty na ture of all phenomena in samsara and nirvana; we have to 
check if we have a strong aspiration to actualize these three minds, because if we don't, 
our practice of deity yoga will just be an absolute waste of time. If the correct view of 
emptiness is not pr esent in our continuum, then there is no point to the whole practice 
of deity yoga.  
To do these practices without these minds might not be harmful as such, but if 
someone engages in, for example, the practice of accumulating mantras with a negative 
motivat ion, like gaining power over others, then the effect could be to take a rebirth as 
a spirit or ghost. There are stories of people who did this and were reborn in such a 
state, but this is not due to the mantra itself, but rather due to the motivation of th e 
practitioner. The mantra or tantric practice is actually the very refined essence of the 
whole Mahayana, and as a result, using this practice with a negative motivation can 
turn out very bad.  
Anyway, this is not our case, as we only accumulate mantras t o support our 
practice and to develop the three minds we have been talking about, but if you only 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 4 – week one to seven  recite mantras, it won‟t be enough because you need to make effort with regards to the 
practices that bring about these minds. Therefore, through studying wel l, we can begin 
to attain these three minds.  
 When you take any tantric initiation or empowerment (a jenang  or wang) if you 
don‟t have the correct view of emptiness, then you won‟t be able to visualize the lama 
as the deity or generate yourself as a deity.  Additionally, for it to be a Mahayana 
practice you need to rely on bodhicitta, for which you need the mind of definite 
emergence as a basis. As a result, you need to have all these three minds present in 
your continuum, which is why we have to make effort  to develop them.  
  
What is a mantra? The very meaning of the word is „mind protection‟. In what 
way are we then protected by the practice of mantra? In secret mantra or tantra you try 
to overcome ordinary appearances and the grasping at them, i.e. you try  to develop 
pure appearance. You guard your mind by giving up the impure appearance, and the 
grasping at it as inherently existent. In order to let go of this grasping you need to 
understand the emptiness of these phenomena. Therefore, when we talk about m ind 
protection, it comes right down to the mind realising emptiness, as well as the other 
two minds of definite emergence and bodhicitta.  
 Many masters point out the benefit of reciting the migtsema, and this is indeed 
the case when the studies remain your  main objective. One always has to remain aware 
that the main activity is one‟s studies, and putting it aside in order to recite mantras 
will not lead to any realizations.  
  
Considering all of this, we need to understand that we all came here to pacify 
our mental continuum. We didn‟t come with the goal of looking for faults in others or 
to make problems. As you came with the wish to stay for a long period of time, you 
should see Nalanda as your home, and make great effort at having only conducive 
relations hips with everyone without exception. You should reflect on that this is the 
place you chose for your studies, and therefore see it as your home. This is especially 
true with regards to the monks, but also for the lay people.  
It would also be good to gene rate the wish to engage in all kinds of activities to 
support your home. Therefore, if there is any small task to be done, you should pick it 
up without hesitation, even if it is cleaning toilets or bathrooms. You shouldn‟t think 
„this is this person‟s tas k and not mine‟, but rather, if you see anything that needs to be 
done, you should take care of it.  
  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
5 So for example, since we are a lot of people, if you take a shower you should always 
check that you leave the shower in a good condition for the next pers on to use it.  As 
you are only one person, there will only be a small amount of dirt that needs to be 
removed, so this you can do immediately.  
 
As we are forming a community, we should try to communicate and speak with 
one another. The other extreme of ch atting too much is of course also not good, but 
we should definitely engage into conversations with others, but not to the extent that it 
will harm our studies. So for example, when having lunch you can always try to sit with 
someone else in order to get i nto contact with different people. You might think, „oh, 
this person I don‟t like, I don‟t want to talk with him‟, but this is a mind we should 
definitely aim at destroying, as it only brings harm.  
 All what I am saying here is right like it is said in th e Lam -Rim. This text is only 
about how to tame the mind, and therefore everything described here is very useful 
and can be immediately applied to tame our mind. As a result, we should try to also 
put it into practice without hesitation, and apply ourselves  to changing the mind on a 
daily basis. By doing this, slowly but surely, the mind will change. Expecting a sudden 
change is impossible.

  Page | 6 – week one to seven  Middle length explanation of the stages of the path to enlightenment  
to be practiced by the three [types of] beings  
with supplementary insertions and a thematic structure  
CHAPTER I  
INTRODUCTION  
 
Respectfully I prostrate at the feet of the venerable saints w ho are full of compassion.  
 
This mainly refers to Manjushri, and talks about the holy ones who have great 
compassion, along with all their other limitless qualities. When we consider the loving 
concern that parents have for their only child on the one hand, and the compassion of 
the holy ones on the other, then there is just no comparison. To this compassion Lam a 
Tsongkhapa prostrates with his body, speech and mind. Again, this is talking about 
holy beings that have this great compassion for all sentient beings, and Lama 
Tsongkhapa wants to follow their example and urges us to do the same.  
Through following thei r example, and being inspired by the holy ones who even 
have this great compassion for limitless beings, we should definitely have love and 
compassion for each other in our small group here at Nalanda, and not just by 
avoiding harm towards each other, but by also trying to benefit each other.  
 We need to understand that if we are able to bring about this outlook towards 
others, we will definitely experience the effect of Nalanda as a most wonderful place, 
find it a pleasure to be here, and possibly never w ish to leave. If instead we just look 
for faults in people, then the effect will be that we will just dislike this place, and end 
up not wanting to stay here. As both these outlooks are created by our own minds, it is 
up to us what we want to do.  
 
 
 
  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
7 MOND AY 15 FEBRUARY 2010  - SESSION 2 
Before doing any kind of Dharma activity, it is good to go for refuge to the 
three jewels. When we take refuge, saying, “I go for refuge to the Buddha, the Dharma 
and the supreme assembly until I have gained the essence of e nlightenment” this 
„essence‟ refers to attaining the Dharmakaya. Saying it in this way is an expression of 
the Mahayana refuge. So in general, of course, we go for refuge to Buddha, Dharma 
and Sangha, but here, saying that we go for refuge until we have at tained 
enlightenment refers to the Dharmakaya, which is the essence of enlightenment, as 
well as the motivation of the Mahayana. You want to attain Buddhahood, being the 
goal of the Mahayana.  
Then we move on to the next lines, which refers to becoming enl ightened in 
order to lead all sentient beings out of suffering. This refers to the actual generation of 
bodhicitta and can only be achieved through listening to the stages of the path in the 
correct way, and then reflecting and meditating on them. Therefor e, the first lines refer 
to the Mahayana refuge and the second two to the motivation of bodhicitta.  
 The next point refers to advice on the appropriate way to listen to the dharma 
teachings through abandoning the three faults of the vessel and generating the six 
discriminations. This will be taught further on in the text, but we can already talk a bit 
about it here. The vessel refers to how to listen to the teachings in that one needs to 
be very focused, be sitting in a stable way, and keeping the mind foc used on the 
teaching. If you don‟t listen attentively, then you won‟t be able to retain what you‟ve 
heard.  
 This type of text is sometimes referred to as The Smaller Lam -Rim. Other 
people might make a threefold division and call it The Middle Length Lam -Rim. 
 
  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 8 – week one to seven  EXPRESSION OF VENERAT ION AND THE VOW OF C OMPOSITION  
1A  Expression of veneration  
Bhagavani, Lord of this w orld system of forbearance,  
J ina’sii regent, J amgon, Lord of the Dharma,  
Manjughosha, single father of all the sugatasiii, 
Nagarjuna and Asanga, foretold by the J ina,  
bow ing to you w ith respect  
 
1B  Vow of composition  
I shall once more  
give explanations here in a summary fashion  
of the gradual path of the most profound view  
and of vast conduct - making it easy to access.  
 
Lama Tsongkhapa is expressing his veneration here. In the first line he is showing 
veneration to all the qualities of body, speech and mind of the Buddha, and in the 
second line he is referring to the offspring of the Buddhas; the bodhisattvas. Principal 
amon gst these two he is paying homage to Jamgön; who is Maitreya, and to 
Manjugosha; being Manjushri, who  are matchless with regards to their understanding 
of selflessness. Additionally, as the supreme disciples of the Buddha, they have taken 
on the responsibi lity to support and see all His deeds through to fruition through their 
magical emanations into all the different realms and places. Keeping this in mind this 
veneration is shown. He also shows his respects to the two trailblazers, Nagarjuna and 
Asanga. Fu rther on, homage is paid to the mother of all realisations, which is the 
actual perfection of wisdom, a phenomenon that is indeed praiseworthy, as well as very 
deep and difficult to fathom. This homage and deep veneration of body, speech and 
mind is also p aid to the two trailblazers Arya Nagarjuna and Asanga who made this 
perfection of wisdom accessible by explaining it through their perfect commentaries.   
What is Lama Tsongkhapa‟s intention in showing his respect in this way? Through 
this, he once more wa nts to present the most profound view, and the vast conduct that 
is presented here in all the gradual stages of the path that must be trained, in an 
abbreviated way that is easily understandable and accessible.   
 
 
  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
9 GENERAL INTRODUCTORY EXPLANATION OF THE  INSTRUCTIONS BEING T AUGHT 
HERE  
 
{ 2} The Dharma to be explained here consists in how the fortunate are lead to the level of buddhahood 
through the stages of the path to enlightenment. It summarises all the points of the J ina’s excellent 
speech, [follow ing]  the tradition of the tw o great trail -blazers Nagarjuna and Asanga, constitutes the 
Dharma system of supreme beings progressing to the state of omniscience, and contains all the stages 
w ithout omission that the three [types of]  beings need to practice.  
 
Here one might ask Lama Tsongkhapa, “What kind of dharma do you want to 
explain here?” His answer would be that he wants to present the essential key points of 
all the utterances of the Buddha, as they were recorded in the Kangyur in a way that 
the way the t wo great trailblazers Nagarjuna and Asanga had previously revealed them 
rather than it being something new. These two great masters are called trailblazers 
because they revealed all the Mahayana teachings of the Kangyur that had previously 
been covered up and not fully understood by anyone. It was at this point that these 
two masters came along and again revealed the meaning of the Mahayana from the 
Kangyur.  
So following them, Lama Tsongkhapa wishes to expound the Dharma in this 
text in the same way, and reveal this to those fortunate beings who have the merits to 
understand them all, and be led to enlightenment. Again, he wants to present all the 
stages of the path to those beings of great fortune and merit so they can quickly attain 
the state of Buddhaho od. 
 
The scholars of glorious Vikramashila [monastery] considered it vital to start off w ith three preliminaries, 
i.e. the greatness of the author of the teachings, the greatness of the teachings and the w ay to explain and 
listen to them.  
 
Furthermore, he  says that he wants to follow the tradition of the scholars of 
Vikramahsila, which was similar to the ancient Nalanda Monastery, but of a different 
tradition. According to those scholars, it is important, before going into the actual 
text, to present three  points. These three points expounded in the Tradition of Kamala 
Shila are a kind of preliminary to the students being led through the steps of the path 
in which Lama Tsongkhapa makes his fourth point.  
 
  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 10 – week one to seven  In accordance w ith that, the guide to the stages o f the path to enlightenment has four [parts]: Show ing 
the greatness of the author in order to show  that the teachings are venerable, show ing the greatness of the 
teachings in order to generate respect tow ards the instructions, how  the teachings w hich are g reat in tw o 
w ays should be listened to and explained, and the stages by w hich a disciple should be guided through the 
actual instructions.  
 
SHOWING THE GREATNESS  OF THE AUTHOR IN ORD ER TO MAKE THE TEACH INGS 
VENERABLE  
 
First: the author: Generally speaking,  the present instructions are the ones that the venerable protector 
Maitreya gives in his Ornament of Clear Realization . In particular, they are based on Lamp on the 
Path to Enlightenment , so that its author is also the author of the present text. Other th an under the 
name of Great Master Dipamkara Shri -jñana he is also w idely know n as the glorious Atisha.  
 
The greatness of the author is shown in order to show the greatness of the source of 
this Dharma. Lama Tsongkhapa says that the general source of this D harma is the 
protector Maitreya‟s Ornament of Clear Realization  (Abhisamaya lamkara ), and by saying 
this he is giving a very great source for these Lam -Rim teachings, as this text is a 
commentary on The Perfection of Wisdom  sutras. Additionally , he is saying  that the Lam -
Rim teachings are actually stemming from the Lamp on the Path to Enlightenment , which 
has been composed by Lama Atisha, and as a result, we could say that this Middle 
Length Lam -Rim is also composed by Lama Atisha.  
 In conclusion, we can say that the author of the root text is the master Maitreya 
and the actual author of the actual source is the glorious master Atisha. This of course 
begs the question as to what kind of person this master Atisha is. In the following 
section, Lama Tsongkhapa is talking about Lama Atisha‟s personality in three points.

  Page | 12 – week one to seven  TUESDAY 16 FEBRUARY 2010  - SESSION 1 
QUESTION :  Does one need a realisation of all the three principle aspects of the path 
in order to practice tantra?  
ANSWER : I was not talking about realisations of the three principle aspects of the path. 
Of course, it is best to h ave these realisations, and with them to engage in the best 
forms of tantric practice , but otherwise what you need is an understanding of these 
minds. As the practice of tantra is the supreme form of  Mahayana practice, you need 
to have the motivation of bo dhicitta. Here again, the best would be to have an actual 
realisation, but if this is not present , then you should generate as strongly as possible a 
mind that is aiming at Buddhahood. When I said that we need bodhicitta , he was 
actually saying that we nee d love, compassion and bodhicitta. Naturally, it is best to 
have a realisation of these minds, but if this is not the case, then we have to try to 
generate them as strong as possible, culminating in a state where we aim as much as 
possible for enlightenmen t. On this basis, we can then say that we are actually 
engaging in Mahayana practice.  
 This type of aiming at enlightenment can only be strongly generated in a 
continuum that has a good understanding of the mind of definite emergence. With this 
kind of mi nd, you wish to liberate yourself from samsara. If you don‟t even have this 
for yourself, how could you possibly wish for all sentient beings to attain 
enlightenment? As a result, this needs to be present in order to develop bodhicitta. 
Nevertheless, here again, as this is a practice of the middle scope, you first need to 
train in the small scope in order  to bring forth these minds.  
In order to enter into the best practice of the Mahayana, the secret mantra, you 
would also need a thorough understanding of emptiness. This is true for all four 
classes of tantra (action -, performance -, yoga - and highest yoga tantra). In tantric 
practice, you need to visualise the teacher and yourself as the deity, and the Mandala 
has also to be generated. All of this is genera ted out of emptiness, and in order to do 
this, the best possible way is to have an actual realisation of emptiness. If this is not 
present, then you need to have at least a thorough understanding of it. You need to 
have analysed it, and through this , gain some understanding of emptiness, and this is 
what will enable you to perform these practices.  
 The sound „mantra‟ translates as protection of the mind (from the ordinary). In 
our continuum, we have ordinary, i.e. impure appearances and a grasping for thes e as 
inherently existent. If you are protected from impure appearances and the grasping 
towards them, then no afflictions can arise because there is no basis for them to do so. 
As a result, your mind is protected.

  Page | 14 – week one to seven  2B1B -2A How Atisha attained the positive qualities of comprehensive scriptural knowledge  
1 How Atisha learnt the areas of general knowledge  
First: By the age of 21, having studied the four areas of know ledge common to Buddhists and non -
Buddhist - language, logic, crafts and medicine - he had become an excellent scholar. In particular, the 
great Drolungpa relates how , at the age of 15, having h eard the Lines of Reasoningviii just once, he 
debated w ith a non -Buddhist intellectual renow ned for his erudition and beat him - w hich spread his 
fame far and w ide.  
 
Lama Atisha also studied the Buddhist scriptures at this young age as can be 
seen from the ex ample about the Lines of Reasoning .  
 
QUESTION :  Between the ages of 21 and 29, he studied mantra. This would 
indicate that he already had some understanding of bodhicitta and emptiness while not 
having yet met his guru Serlingpa, from whom he received the  teachings about 
bodhicitta. How is that?  
ANSWER : We have to understand that in general he had already studied Buddhist 
texts like this Lines of Reasoning,  which are Mahayana texts. As this is all about 
bodhicitta, he definitely studied the texts that expl ain how to develop love, compassion 
and bodhicitta. From this, we can understand that he had already studied these topics, 
and probably already developed some kind of simile to the mind of bodhicitta, and the 
minds necessary for tantric practice.   
 
2 How Atisha learnt Mantra  
Then he requested complete empow erment from the master yogi of the main temple on Black Mountain, 
the glorious Rahulagupta w ho had received direct visions of Hevajra and a prophecy from Vajradakini, 
{ 4}  receiving the secret name J ñana -guhya -vajra. Up to the age of 29 he studied the Vajrayana under 
numerous highly realized gurus and thereby became know ledgeable in all texts and instructions. When 
the thought “only I am know ledgeable in the secret mantra” occurred to him, dakinis pointed out, in his 
dream, several volumes of mantra he had not seen before. That low ered his pride.  
 
Black Mountain is probably very close to the palace where he was living, and where 
highly realised yogis lived. One of them was in a state of having continuous v isions of 
Hevajra, which in Tibetan means he was seeing the face of the deity all the time, just as 
Lama Tsongkhapa continuously saw the face of Manjushri. This phase, where he 
engaged into intensive studies of secret mantra, lasted for eight years from th e age of 
21. His main master during this time was Rahulagupta, but he was also relying on other 
masters from this temple.  
 Through this, he became an utterly skilful scholar, but also developed pride 
through the thought of being unsurpassed in this knowled ge and being especially 
outstanding. This was at age 29.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
15 3 How Atisha was ordained and learnt the collections of inner knowledge  
Both in his dreams and directly the gurus and protectors then made know n to him that vast benefit 
w ould accrue for the teachi ngs and many beings, if he took monastic vow s as appropriate. Upon their 
encouragement he had himself ordained. The great upholder of monastic discipline, Shilarakshita, an 
elder of the Mahasamghika tradition w ho had attained the concentration in w hich you  single-pointedly 
enter [ultimate] reality on the path of preparation, officiated as abbot. Candragarbha received the name 
Shri Dipamkara -jñana.  
 Afterw ards, up to the age of 31, he studied the low er and upper divisions of inner philosophical 
know ledge, in  particular, for 12 years, the Great Detailed Explanationix under Guru Dharmarakshita in 
Odantapuri. As he w as very w ell versed in the scriptures of the four original traditions, he had an 
infallible grasp of the tiniest details in modes of behaviour that s hould be cultivated and rejected 
according to the various philosophical schools, like [for instance the monastic rules concerning] accepting 
gifts. 
 
There is a variety of Vinaya lineages, and Atisha was ordained in the Mahasamghika 
lineage by Shilarakshita , which is probably another name for Dharmarakshita. 
Dipamkara seems to have something to do with butter lamps, as in Tibetan it is 
translated as the one who acts like a butter lamp [Commentary of Geshe Jampa Gyatso: 
Dipam means lamp and kara means great] . I said that maybe the whole name is referring to 
that his transcendental wisdom is shining out  like the light of a butter lamp. I‟m half 
joking here, as this is a mere guess.  
 The divisions of inner philosophical knowledge refer to all the different 
philo sophical schools of Buddhism like Vaibashika, Sautrantika, etc. The Great D etailed 
Explanation (Mahavibhasha)  is the most important text of the Vaibashika School and 
deals with Vinaya and Abhidharma. Through this, he became a great scholar of the 
Abhidharm a and especially the Vinaya, being knowledgeable in all the different Vinaya 
lineages, but especially the four main ones. One could say he was so skilled, that he 
was the crown ornament of the Vinaya teachings. We can compare this to His Holiness 
the Dalai  Lama being the crown ornament of the Buddha‟s teachings nowadays.  
Presented here has been the way in which Atisha gained the knowledge of the 
scriptures.

  Page | 16 – week one to seven  2B1B -2B How Atisha attained the realizational qualities of its correct accomplishment  
1 How all realizational qualities are contained in the three trainings  
[2 How Atisha was accomplished in the three trainings]  
2B1B -2B1 How all realizational qualities are contained in the three trainings  
Second: As, generally speaking, all the scriptural teachings b y the J ina are contained in the three precious 
collections, the realizational teachings are also contained in the three precious trainings.  
 
All the scriptural teachings are referring to all the Buddha‟s teachings, which are 
contained in the Kangyur or the  three baskets (Tripitaka), Sutra -, Vinaya - and 
Abhidharma -. In addition, concerning the realizational aspect of the teachings , or in 
other words the actual practices, all of them are contained within the three higher 
trainings of ethics, concentration and wisdom. The higher training of ethics is the basis 
for the other two. This is expressed frequently in all the sutras and tantr as. We can talk 
about three types of ethical discipline of the Pratimoksha, the bodhisattva, and the 
secret mantra. Amongst these three , the first one is the ethical discipline of individual 
liberation (Pratimoksha), and this part of the text mentions how to guard these vows.

A Ethical discipline  
B His training of concentration  
C His training of wisdom  
2B1B -2B2A  [Ethical discipline]  
1 Transition to ethical discipline  
2 How Atisha was actually acco mplished in ethical discipline  
3 Proper protection [of ethical discipline]  
2B1B -2B2A -1 Transition to ethical discipline  
{ 5}  In this regard the training in ethical discipline is often praised in the [Buddha’s] excellent teachings 
and in the commentaries as the foundation of all good qualities such as the training in concentration 
and wisdom. Therefore, it is necessary, first of all, to have realizational qualities in terms of ethical 
discipline.  
  
2B1B -2B2A -2 How Atisha was actually accomplished in ethical d iscipline  
A How Atisha possessed  the highest vows of individual liberation  
In this regard there are three [kinds]: How he possessed the first of those, the highest vow s of individual 
liberation: Having received the bhikshu vow s, he protected them the w ay a  yak protects its coat - a yak is 
so attached to its coat that it rather risks its life, trying to save a strand of tail hair that has got caught 
on a tree, than to part with it, even if it sees its life is being threatened by a hunter. That is how Atisha 
protected every detail of the foundation of trainings, not to speak of the major [rules of the] foundation of 
trainings w hich he had undertaken, so that he came to be called the elder w ho is a great upholder of 
discipline.  
 
The previously mentioned transla tor‟s praise is an excerpt from what is called 
the extensive translators praise, which contains a verse prais ing Lama Atisha for 
entering the vehicle of the elders and becoming supreme within it. This vehicle is also 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
17 known as the Vinaya. There it praises h im for taking the vows of a Gelong, and then 
protecting them as a yak protects his hair, meaning he held them more dear than his 
own life.  
 
B How Atisha possessed the bodhisattva vows:  
Bodhisattva vow s: He practiced  numerous instructions for training the mind of enlightenment w hich is  
rooted in love and compassion. Especially, in attendance upon Serlingpa, he trained for long  in the 
highest instructions passed on by the venerable Maitreya and Manjugosha through Asanga and 
Shantideva. Thereby the mind of en lightenment that cherishes  others more than oneself, arose in his 
heart. This aspirational mind gave rise to the mind of active involvement and due to his excellent 
behaviour, i.e. his engagement in the trainings follow ing the promise to train in the vast conduct of 
bodhisattvas , he never transgressed the rules of Buddha’s children.  
 
Here is described how Atisha first trained in love and compassion , and then 
proceeded to develop bodhicitta. The highest instructions refer to the instructions of 
the deep or p rofound path, and to the vast or extensive path. Having received these 
teachings through the lineage of the master of Shantideva, he trained in them for a 
long time until he had attained actual bodhicitta. Firstly, the mind that cherishes others 
more than oneself arose as aspirational bodhicitta , and on the basis of this, engaging 
bodhicitta later arose.  
Through this , he engaged in great waves of bodhisattva activity, which he 
guarded right down to the smallest detail. He is described as someone who engaged in 
great activities for the sake of sentient beings through a bodhicitta mind of 
overflowing compassion and speci al intention for all sentient beings. On the basis of 
this, he engaged into the bodhisattva activity of the six perfections and never 
abandoned them or become  dispirited .  
Again in the translators praise there is a verse of praise that is describing the 
way in which he generated his bodhicitta. Firstly, he developed aspirational bodhicitta 
by relying on great love, compassion and the special intention. And then on the basis 
of this he actually took on the bodhisattva vows and generated engaging bodhicitta 
after which he trained for the sake of all sentient beings in the six perfections, and 
during that process he never let a single being slip out of his concern. Nagtso, the 
translator is finishing this verse saying, “to this compassionate being I prostrate”.  
There is another line where the translator Nagtso is expressing his veneration by 
saying, “the one who completely let go of his own goals, who completely took on the 
goals of all others, this one is my lama‟.  
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 18 – week one to seven  C How Atisha possessed Vajrayana vows  
Vajray ana vow s: Ow ing to the fact that he had the meditative stabilisations of the generation stage 
w here one’s ow n body is seen as that of a deity and of the completion stage of the [indestructible] vajra 
mind, { 6} he became chief among yogis. In particular, he  protected his tantric commitments properly by 
not transgressing the prescribed rules.  
 
This shows that he practiced both the generation and completion stage in the most 
suitable manner. Again, there is a verse about this in the translators praise where it  is 
said that having suitably entered through the door of the Vajrayana, meaning he 
entered into the Mandala by relying on his tantric guru Rahulagupta, he practiced and 
mastered the generation stage (generating himself as the deity) and completion stage 
(by generating the vajra mind). In this way, he entered into amazing tantric practice 
while completely hiding this fact from the world and practicing in a secret manner. To 
this, Nagtso prostrates.  
Within doing these practices he never transgressed any kin d of commitment, 
Samaya or ethical discipline or their boundaries. This means he guarded his ethical 
discipline not occasionally , but at all times i.e. he was always possessed by a 
mindfulness and introspection that allowed him to stay within the boundarie s of 
ethical discipline. In Tibetan it says, „by relying on mindfulness and introspection you 
have never done anything which is not ethical discipline. And by being connected with 
conscientiousness there was no covering up of faults, and therefore you are someone 
who has no downfalls or faults‟.  
Referring to Sojong, I said that Atisha is someone who would have no reason of 
coming to this ritual, as he would have nothing to purify.  
The last line is especially referring to him never ending up in any kind of  
behaviour with which he would incur a downfall in any of the three types of ethics. I 
said this verse is very wonderful, and as it only has four lines, it would be good if we 
could memorise it.  
 
  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
19 2B2B -2B2A -3 Proper protection [of ethical discipline]  
Not only did he courageously undertake the trainings in ethical discipline of the three vow s, he also 
upheld them as he had promised. He kept the vow s w ithout transgressing the rules associated w ith them, 
and even if he transgressed them ever so slightly, he quickly, very quickly purified this by means of a 
suitable ritual restoring the given vow .  
 
Since we are people who are listening, reflecting and meditating on the Dharma, when 
we hear stories like these, we should react with rejoicing, be utterly happy th at this 
happened to Lama Atisha and see him like a role model. You should see yourself like 
someone who wants to follow him, see him as your guru , and you should make 
aspirational prayers to also be able to keep your ethics as well as he did. In the 
meanti me, try to practice your ethics to the best of your abilities.  
 
His training of concentration has tw o [aspects]: Common concentration: He had achieved the 
serviceability of the mind of calm abiding and, as regards uncommon concentration, he had the extreme  
stability of the generation stage. Furthermore, he completed the three or six years of modes of conduct of 
yogic realization disciplines.  
 
Lama Atisha attained calm abiding together with which comes a serviceability of the 
mind. With such a mind, all the adverse factors to this concentration like laxity and 
excitement, distraction and so forth are pacified. As a result, one is able to keep his 
mind focused on the object for as long as one wishes.  
The uncommon concentration is a specific form of calm abidin g on the 
generation stage. There are presentations of the gross and subtle generation stages, and 
of great stability within them. All this comes from the field of secret mantra.  
The three year and six years practice refers to specific modes of meditation of 
mahasiddhas (great meditators).

His training of w isdom has tw o [aspects]: The common one: He had attained the concentration of

The uncommon  one: he had attained the special concentration of the completion stage.  
 
The uncommon one is connected to the tantric level , and the concentration called the 
vajra mind. This we heard before in the translator‟s praise where it was said that he 
entered thr ough the gate of the diamond vehicle, generated himself as the deity and 
brought about the stability of the vajra mind.  
 
The deeds he did for the sake of the teachings  are of tw o [types]: his deeds in India and his deeds in 
Tibet.  
 
2B1B -3A Deeds  in India  
First: At the palace of great enlightenment in Bodhgaya he defeated proponents of base, non -Buddhist 
doctrines by means of the Dharma three times, thus maintaining the Buddha’s teachings. Eliminating 
the taints of ignorance, misconception and doubt also w i th regard to the teachings of his ow n upper and 
low er systems , he spread the teachings, so that he w as regarded as a crow n jew el by [proponents of] all 
factions beyond partiality.  
 
The place of great enlightenment in Bodhgaya is probably a place next to t he big stupa 
there. The three times refer to that his opponents completely lost this debate , and how 
he was able to bring about harmony and pacification between the disharmonies wi thin 
the different Buddhist traditions . However, all this was much smaller t han the vast 
deeds  he carried out in Tibet.

2B1B -3B1 Clearing up misconceptions about the Dharma and restoring the tradition  
Deeds in Tibet: Lha Lamax { 7}  and his nephew sent the tw o great translators Gya -tson-seng and Nagtso 
Tsultrim Gyalwa to India one after the other. As they took great pains again and again to invite 
[Atisha], he came to Upper Ngari during [the reign of] Jangchub Oxi and w as requested to purify the 
Buddha’s teachings. Thereupon, he spread the teachings by composing the Lamp on the Path to 
Enlightenment , a text bringing together all the points of sutra and tantra and condensing them into 
applicable stages, as w ell as other texts. Specifically, he spent three years in Ngari, nine years in Nyetang  
and five years elsew here in Central Tibet and Tsang teaching the fortunate all the scriptures and 
instructions of sutra and tantra w ithout exception. Thereby he restored the tradition of teachings that 
had declined, made those that remained flourish, and corrected those that had been soiled by the errors of 
misconception, ensuring that the precious teachings w ere freed from all flaw s.  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
21 Lha Lama and his nephew were both sending missionaries to India who were 
translators. They were called great translators a nd were already quite high scholars 
when they invited Atisha to come to Tibet.  
Since Lama Atisha was the most outstanding lama present at that time it was 
difficult to recruit him , so they did their utmost to persuade him. While Lha Lama was 
going to India, he was captured by the king of Nepal and sacrificed his life to get 
Atisha to Tibet. His nephew, Jangchup O did everything to liberate his uncle from 
prison but failed. His uncle said to him that he would die in th e prison, but his nephew 
should go to India to invite Atisha to Tibet. Therefore, Jangchup O joined the two 
translators who were already in India and with them tried to persuade Atisha to come 
to Tibet , and  repeatedly made offerings to him.  
 There were many obstacles and a lot of gold offer ings had to be made to the 
king who didn‟t want to let go of Atisha and to various ministers, but as Atisha himself 
was a Bhikkshu he didn‟t have any use for gold offerings. After repeatedly inviting 
him, they eventually succeeded, and Atisha returned with  them to Tibet. Upon his 
arrival, he first went to a region called Ngari , which is eight hours by car from Mount 
Kailas.  
Jangchup O addressed Atisha saying that the teachings were originally very 
excellently established in Tibet by Dharmarakshita and Padm asambhava, but after 
some time the Dharma in Tibet degenerated and all kinds of corrupted practices were 
established. At that point, the meaning of the Dharma had become unclear, and this is 
why he requested Atisha to clarify the meaning of the Dharma in T ibet, as he was the 
only one who achieve this goal.    
 Having been requested , in this way Lama Atisha composed The Lamp on the Path 
to Enlightenment  as a response, which is a text in which all the essential key points of 
sutra and tantra are presented. Af ter completing this , he travelled to Nyetang, which is 
maybe fifteen minutes by car from Lhasa. There he stayed for nine years making it his 
main dwelling place. After that, he went for some years to other regions, and in all this 
time he was able to fully  clarify and rectify the Dharma in Tibet, where he stayed until 
his passing away.  
 With regards to all the lineages stemming from Nagarjuna, Asanga and so forth, 
Atisha was able to fully clarify the meaning of their teachings. Of course, since then 
there have been all kinds of degenerations, but not as grave as at the time before 
Atisha come to Tibet. The worst thing after that was the invasion of the Chinese, who 
made the sun of the Dharma vanish  within Tibet .

  Page | 22 – week one to seven  QUESTION :  Who are the important disciples of Atisha and the monasteries that were 
founded?  
ANSWER : There are people who continue the lineage of Lama Atisha, and we are 
such people. Since we are studying the Lam -Rim, who‟s author, according to Lama 
Tsongkhapa, is Lama Atisha, we are his disciples. In Lama Atisha‟s lineage, the Kadam 
lineage, there were many small and big monasteries founded of which the most 
important one is Reting Monastery. The bigger monasteries have even survived to the 
present day.  
 
2B1B -3B2 Identifying the three causes of exce llence in the composition of commentaries  
There are three causes of excellence in the composition of a text clarifying the Muni’sxii intentions: being 
learned in the five areas of know ledge, having received instructions for the application of their meaning 
which transmit the instructions of the perfectly complete Buddha in an unbroken line of exalted 
[masters], and having received the permission to teach through the vision of a protector deity. If a text 
can be composed w ith any one of these [causes of excell ence] - it w ill turn out all the more excellent if all 
three are complete. This great master [, Atisha, ] possessed all three.  
 
This shows that it is not easy at all to compose an outstanding treatise. At Atisha‟s 
time there needed to be some certain condi tions present to write a commentary, not 
like nowadays where everyone who wants to just writes a commentary. Most likely, 
until the Chinese invasion all treatises were composed with the author having these 
three conditions present. Nowadays, though, all ki nds of things appear. In Lama 
Atisha all these three qualities have come together.  
 
QUESTION :  Is there any damage for us in receiving teachings from a teacher whose 
lineage has been broken?  
ANSWER : This in itself would not be harmful , what would be of harm is if this teacher 
doesn‟t know the Dharma. If he knows his Sutra and Tantra well, then there would be 
no harm . However,  if he just makes up all kinds of things, then this would be a 
situation worthy of compassion as there might be people who think that what he says 
is correct. As a result, such a teacher and his disciples would receive a lot of 
negativities.  
 It is very likely that many of us here will give Dharma teachings in the future. 
Therefore, it is very important that we onl y teach a Dharma that we really underst and 
on the basis of our own studies. Just to sit there and give all kinds of made up advice is 
really a situation incurring many faults, and therefore, it is very important for you to 
engage into these studies of very  high quality in order to later have the ability to pass

virtue, and would most likely accumulate many faults.

2B2A  Identifying the teachings  
Second: As for the teachings , the source text of the present instructions is Lamp on the Path to 
Enlightenment . 
 
2B2B  Presentation [of this text] as the three: complete, easily applicable and a particularly noble 
system  
Although J ow o composed numerous texts, the Lamp on the Path to E nlightenment  is their perfect 
completion - like a root. As it indicates all the points of both sutra and tantra in summary fashion, it is 
complete in terms of content. As it makes the gradual taming of the mind its main topic, it is easy to put 
into practi ce. As it is ornamented by the instructions of the tw o masters { 10}  w ho w ere skilled in the 
systems of the tw o trail -blazers  
 
xiii, it is a particularly noble system compared to others.  
 
The two masters who were skilled in the systems of the two trail -blazers  are Serlingpa,  
and I guess  maybe the second one is Sintipa from the praise shown above, but this is 
not sure.   
 
The greatness of these instructions of this text has four [aspects]. The first of these [is] the greatness of 
recognizing all teachings as non -contradictory.  
   
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 26 – week one to seven  A Identifying the teachings  
Everything the J ina has said.  
 
B How all these teachings are recognized as non -contradictory  
They all come to be understood as one person’s path to Buddhahood. That is to say some are primary 
points of the path - others are appropriate secondary points.  
 
All the teachings of the Buddha we find in the Kangyur are regarded to be for each 
and every single person. What all of them need to understand, abandon, realize and 
cultivate is connected to the four nob le truths. And the being who taught all these 
things without omitting anything is the Buddha. This talks about starting from going 
for refuge right up to the last practice bringing you to complete enlightenment. As they 
are all instructions for a single pe rson they are not contradictory. But some texts are 
specialized, teaching more the wisdom side, others more the method side. But as you 
have to practice method and wisdom together this is not contradictory. Also some are 
main points and others secondary bu t still there is no contradiction.  
To understand what is meant by Primary and Secondary points we can take the 
example of the Prajnaparamita sutra. There the main point is emptiness and only as 
side points things like the six perfections and so on are pre sented. Another example 
would be the Bodhisattvacaryavatara where in the first chapter the master Shantideva 
is praising the good qualities of bodhicitta, which is the main topic and in the second 
chapter he talks about offerings, purification and so on, w hich are branches or 
secondary points. Later on the sixth chapter on patience is also a side topic in 
comparison to the main topic of developing bodhicitta. But anyway all of these need to 
be trained in.  
 
C How it is demonstrated through scripture and logic that it would be contradictory not to accept 
this 
In this regard it is the bodhisattva’s w ish to w ork for the w elfare of the w orld. For that purpose it is also 
necessary to care for all three types of disciples and therefore train in their respective  paths. This is 
because it is explained by the venerable Maitreya that know ing paths of the three vehicles constitutes the 
means of realizing the bodhisattvas’ goals.  
 
This talks about the aims of bodhisattvas, who want to help everyone to achieve their 
respective goals. They want to support the practitioners of the three types, being the 
Hearer -, Pratyekabuddha - and Bodhisattva practitioners. To help them in their paths it 
is necessary to also train in their respective paths. This is expressed by the vener able 
Maitreya who says that the bodhisattva needs to know the three vehicles. This means 
that the states of these three need to be generated in one‟s own continuum in order to 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
27 help sentient beings from one‟s own experience. Also the protector Nagarjuna 
expressed how one first would realise these states oneself and then help others in 
achieving them. In order to lead others to the state where they can realise these things 
one needs to have an unmistaken understanding, otherwise only confusion will come 
about .  
This is expressed in a line in the Pramanavartika: „by first understanding this 
yourself then you can explain it to others‟. The meaning is that if you want to explain 
Dharma to others, you first have to understand it yourself. Also Lama Tsongkhapa 
hims elf says that if you don‟t understand this point as it is then you won‟t be capable 
to present it to others. And similar things are also expressed in the Ornament of Clear 
Realization. There it says that the project of the bodhisattva is to work for the wo rld 
and all sentient beings which means that if one wants to lead the objects to be subdued 
(the three kinds of practitioners) you need to have generated an understanding in your 
own continuum first. Also in the Perfection of wisdom sutra (especially the o ne of one 
hundred thousand lines), it says that in order to lead the three types of practitioners it 
is necessary to know and understand their paths in your own continuum and also to 
engage into the activities of their paths.  
If we are asked to explain th e Dharma, even though we might not have an actual 
realisation, it is important that we only explain things as they are described in the texts. 
In other words that we only teach what we have studied and analysed ourselves. This 
shows how important it is to engage into studies of the Buddhist texts. If you are 
asked about a certain topic and you say in one text it says this but in another it says 
that, then it shows that you don‟t understand. Because even though the wording in two 
texts like the Bodhisattvaca ryavatara and the Lam -rim might be different, the meaning 
is exactly the same.  
Then there are different sections like the sutra and tantra. But even though there 
are some differences to be found, all of them express exactly the same meaning, so 
there is a bsolutely no contradiction as said in the headline above. Between sutra and 
tantra there are certain aspects which are not in harmony but the tantric teachings are 
relying on the sutra teachings therefore we can‟t say they are contradictory. An 
example for  such a disharmony would be that in sutra we say that merit for three 
countless aeons has to be accumulated. In tantra there are methods to accumulate 
merits in order to become enlightened in one life and one body. But both are saying 
that it is necessary to accumulate merits and actually it is the same amount, just that in 
tantra this amount is accumulated in one life. So this shows that they are actually in 
harmony. In Tibetan there is some kind of sweet of which it is said that its sweetness 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 28 – week one to seven  comes from t he butter with which it is made. Similarly here the sweetness or power of 
the tantric teachings comes from the butter of the sutra teachings.  
Therefore all the instructions of the Buddha are to be studied by a bodhisattva 
and not just the Mahayana. If we w ould go into more detail with this it would be very 
difficult to understand as this is the topic of the Abhisamayalamkara which is 
presented from the point of view of the Madhyamika Svatantrika. The main view that 
has to be cultivated by the Hearers is the  view of selflessness, for the Pratyekabuddha 
practitioner the main view is the one of non -duality and for the bodhisattva 
practitioner the actual view of emptiness. The bodhisattva needs to train in all these 
different views in order to help all the three  types of practitioners.  
Put in a nutshell what is going on here, is that the bodhisattva needs to know 
with regards to each of the three practitioners what they need to know, abandon, 
actualize and cultivate. This, again is because he is supporting everyo ne to practice 
their respective path and attain their respective goal.  
 
 
WEDNESDAY 17 FEBRUARY 2010  - SESSION 1 
I D Establishing that the teachings are non -contradictory  
In the Mahayana path there is the common and the uncommon. The former is that w hich originates 
from the Hinayana collection of scriptures - except for some peculiarities that are not common, to do w ith 
the thought of aspiring to individual liberation and the like.  Apart from that, it is not that faults are 
partially ceased and qualities a re partially completed in perfectly complete buddhahood, but rather all 
kinds of faults have been eliminated and all types of good qualities are complete. Consequently, as the 
Mahayana, achieving this, also brings about the cessation of all faults and the development of all good 
qualities, the path of the Mahayana comprises all the various aspects of qualities of abandonment and 
realization of all other vehicles. { 11}  That is w hy the sections of the Mahayana path, w hich brings about 
Buddhahood, comprise all  the Buddha’s teachings, for there is no utterance of the Muni that does not 
cause the cessation of some faults and the development of some good qualities, and because it is not the 
case that all of this is not brought about by the Mahayana.  
 
Here we have to remind ourselves of a quote in the sutras that states that one needs to 
understand all the paths of the three types of practitioners and engage in their 
respective activities. However, this does not mean that one needs to train in all aspects 
of, for ex ample, the hearer path where one trains to achieve liberation only for oneself. 
This would be an uncommon aspect. The common aspects would be impermanence, in 
the nature of suffering, empty, and selfless. A bodhisattva would also have to 
understand and gen erate all these in his continuum.  
 There are differences in the goals of the practitioners. Therefore, the 
bodhisattva has to deal with the different practitioners in different ways. For example, 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
29 it wouldn‟t be appropriate to tell them to give up their se lf-cherishing (for their own 
liberation), because since they have a strong form of this self -cherishing, they just 
wouldn‟t respond. Rather, he would have to tell them that if they want to achieve their 
own liberation, he would explain to them these aspect s like impermanence, suffering, 
empty and selfless.  
  
A Buddha is not someone who just has a portion of good qualities and 
abandoned a portion of faults, but on the contrary, he manifests all good qualities and 
completely eliminates all faults. Therefore,  a path leading to this state, the Mahayana 
path, is developing all good qualities and eliminating all possible faults. In addition, the 
development of all the good qualities and elimination of faults of the hearer or 
Pratyeka Buddha path are included in t he Mahayana path, and as a result, all such paths 
in these first two vehicles will be cultivated in the Mahayana.  
 Because Hinayana Sutras were also taught by the Buddha, it is definite that they 
will eliminate some kinds of faults, and as a result, they are part of the Mahayana path, 
and therefore should be seen as something which is to be trained in to gain complete 
enlightenment. Therefore, one never should put down the Hinayana Sutras saying 
these are Hinayana and these are Mahayana Sutras, because if you did have such an 
attitude, you would be looking down on teachings of the Buddha.  
 
E Clearing up doubts  
If you think: ‘That may be true for the Paramitayanaxiv, but not for those entering the Vajrayana’: 
Although the w ay you train in the Paramitayana - in countless clear distinctions that clarify giving etc. - 
is different from mantra, the behavioural basis - the generation of the mindxv - and the coarse form of the 
path of training in the six perfections, the behaviour, is nevertheless the same and theref ore something 
shared by them. In Vajra peak  it says:  
Not even to sustain one’s life,  
the bodhixvi mind should be abandoned  
and: 
The practice of the six perfections,  
must not be abandoned ever.  
 
This is also taught in numerous other mantra texts. In many authentic sources for the mandala ritual of 
highest yoga Tantra  it says that a set of tw o vow s must have been taken: a common and an uncommon 
one and indeed, the former refers to bodhisattva vow sxvii. The precious teacher also saidxviii: “My guru w ho 
knew how  to accommodate all the teachings by means of the path [as if in] a square… ” These w ords 
form the basis of a major investigation.  
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 30 – week one to seven  While, as we discussed, it is true that there are some differences in  the two vehicles, 
the practice of the six perfections, on a coarser level, is common to both, and as its 
basis is one of bodhicitta, both need to be present in order to practice secret mantra.  
Bodhicitta is seen as so precious that not even at the cost o f one‟s life should 
one give it up. The same holds true for the practice of the six perfections, which are 
based on a mind of bodhicitta. The bodhisattva vow comes right down to vowing to 
practice these six perfections.  
The quote by the precious teacher D romtönpa shows that Atisha was able to 
bring all the teachings together without losing any of the points within them. This is to 
be understood in the context that in Tibet at this time, some people were only 
practicing Sutra, and some only Tantra, and they  were despising each other. The tantric 
practitioners especially despised the Vinaya. This is why Dromtönpa says here that 
Atisha was able to unite these two types of practitioners.  
 As previously mentioned, all the teachings are to be practiced by one si ngle 
person. This means you can‟t attain the state of Vajradhara i.e. become completely 
enlightened by only practicing Tantra. As a result, you could say the perfection vehicle 
is a cause (like the trunk of a tree rather than its branches) or part of the t antric 
vehicle. By this, we can understand how all the teachings are in harmony with each 
other.  
 
2B2C -2 The greatness of all of the Buddha’s teachings dawning on you as useful instructions  
Second: the greatness of all of the Buddha’s teachings dawning on you as useful instructions  
 
A The distinction of pure and impure manners of comprehending  
Anyone construing the great scriptures to be explanatory teachings that do not contain the points of 
practice w ho thinks that guidance indicating the meaning of the e ssential points of practice exists 
separately from them, and holds that even in the holy Dharma the explanatory and accomplishing 
teachings are found in different places { 12} , w ill create an obstacle for the origination of deep respect 
tow ards the immacula te Sutras and Tantras  as w ell as the treatises clarifying their intent. One should be 
aware that the karmic obscuration of abandoning the Dharma is accumulated by despising them, saying 
that they only outline external know ledge w ithout presenting the inner  meaning. Hence, for those w ho 
w ant liberation, the infallible, supreme instructions are indeed the great scriptures. Nevertheless, it is 
possible that, due to poor intelligence etc., one cannot reach certainty through relying on these scriptures 
only as s upreme instruction. Therefore and one must seek certainty w ith respect to them by relying on 
excellent direct instructions, but one should not think that the great scriptures do not contain the heart of 
the matter because they only outline external know led ge while the direct instructions are supreme because 
they present the inner meaning.  
 
First Lama Tsongkhapa is pointing out a wrong approach to the Dharma. In Buddhism 
we can speak about the five great texts, and within these the text on logic is called th e 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
31 Pramanav artika, the Treatise on V alid Cognition . One might say that this text is all about 
logic and reasoning, but doesn‟t talk about practice, which you only get from the oral 
instruction of your lama. This outlook is described here as faulty. Another example 
could be someone suggesting that monastic debate is a useless activity, and assert that 
it would be better to practice instead. If you really use all your logical and analytical 
capacity to understand the philosophical texts through analytical medi tation and 
debate, and then you are despised by someone who considers it as better to sit and 
undertake a practice like accumulating mantras, then this is exactly the kind of faulty 
attitude described here.  
 If someone wants to bring limitless benefit to others, then he can achieve this 
goal by studying the valid teachings. Therefore, one would study the texts with the 
outlook of asking what the text is saying about the method to change the mind. One 
will analyse and explore this, and put it to proof in or der to know if it really works. 
This is similar to buying some clothes; where we don‟t just take any kind of shirt and 
immediately buy it, but we first check it for its material, size and so forth. In the same 
way, you apply your logic and analysis to the texts, and through this, you will be able 
to apply the teachings and actually change your mind. Apart from this approach, a 
change of mind won‟t come from anywhere else.  
 Again someone might say, “You don‟t need to study the Lam -rim, but what you 
need to practice is found somewhere else, from this guru or lama”. This person 
distinguishes between where one gets the knowledge and the practice. If someone ends 
up making this kind of distinction, then this person will probably end up wasting his 
time and his l ife, and not getting anywhere. If someone would put all the presentations 
of the Sutras, Tantras and commentaries aside and look for a different instruction for 
his actual practice, then this attitude would become a great obstacle, he will most likely 
find faults with the Dharma and end up abandoning it. This is the worst amongst all 
the negative deeds.  
Therefore, Lama Tsongkhapa says that those wishing for liberation and so forth 
will find the most excellent instructions right here in these texts containi ng Buddha 
Shakyamuni‟s advice. To put all the great commentaries of Nagarjuna and his spiritual 
sons aside, and go for advice to one‟s lama is a very strange attitude indeed.  
 The reason for this attitude is identified by Lama Tsongkhapa as a lazy mind 
that lack confidence, and rather than engage into studies and excellent deeds, he tries 
to undertake some other practice. Of course, if one is completely incapable of studies 
or is not willing to engage in them, then maybe it‟s better to ask the lama for adv ice as 
this might be better than doing nothing at all. However, even in this case, one should 
maintain respect towards the great texts and not think of them as mere knowledge.

  Page | 32 – week one to seven  WEDNESDAY 17 FEBRUARY 2010  - SESSION 2 
 
B How all the Buddha’s teachings dawn o n you as useful instructions  
The great Yogi J angchub Rinchen said:  
Someone w ho has reached certainty concerning a handy little volume, cannot be 
said to know all the topics of direct instructions, this is said about someone who 
understands all of the Bud dha’s teachings as instructions.  
 
We need the kind of understanding Great J ow o [Atisha]’s disciple Gompa Rinchen Lama expressed 
saying he had ground his body, speech and mind to dust in a single meditation session on Atisha’s 
instructions and that thereby the understanding had now arisen in him that all scriptures are 
instructions.  
 
This Jangchub Rinchen is most likely one of the great Kadampa Masters. The point 
here is that someone might have studied a small Dharma book and decided that it was 
sufficient,  when in fact the whole mass of the Buddha‟s teachings need to be 
understood as necessary instructions. This refers to someone who says that there is no 
point in devoting time to extensive studies, but it would be enough to engage into 
practice having stud ied an abbreviated or concise instruction. The effect for this 
person would be to become stupid and confused, as well as the possibility that he may 
even spread this confusion to others. Such a person would think that instructions only 
refer to oral instru ctions from a teacher, and due to his misunderstanding enters a 
mistaken path.  
 Lama Tsongkhapa himself says that it is impossible for there to be an 
instruction that is just an ear whisper of a lama. This means that whatever instruction 
there is, for exam ple by Nagarjuna, it will show up in the volumes on Sutra and Tantra, 
just as all the advice of Atisha can be found in the Lam -rim teachings. The idea that 
the lama might just secretly spread his knowledge to only one person is nonsense. We 
have already me ntioned how some of the Sutras and Tantras teach main points and 
some teach secondary ones, but still all of them are to be practiced by an individual. 
This is the actual instruction.  
 The story about Gompa Rinchen Lama says that by meditating on the scrip tures 
of Atisha, all the negativities, all the blockages of his body, speech and mind are 
destroyed, and he suddenly understood how all scriptures are instructions.  
 
In the w ords of the precious [Drom] Tonpa:  
 
If, after studying the Dharma a lot, you feel the need to search elsew here for a 
w ay of applying the Dharma, you are mistaken.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
33  
One could give your study of the Lam -rim here in Nalanda as an example; if while 
studying this text you look elsewhere for a practice, then this attitude is mistaken. I see 
that most of us have the text in front of us, and as we have a whole year to study this 
he feels that it is quite possible to really get to know this text if we engage into good 
studies. Moreover, if you have a good understanding of this great text, yo u will be able 
to answer the questions of others, you will know how to practice, and how to do 
placement and analytical meditation and so forth. In this way, you will really uphold 
the teaching, and you won‟t have to turn to someone else and ask what to pr actice. If a 
Tibetan would do this, he would be considered as completely crazy.  
 Someone might be studying the Dharma for a long time and come to know it, 
but still he might not know how to apply it and turn somewhere else for instructions. 
If this happens , it is a clear sign that he hasn‟t understood or trained in the correct 
way. 
 
Likew ise, those w ho study the Dharma a lot over a long period and, completely ignorant as to how  to set 
about it, develop the w ish to practice Dharma, do not understand and ther efore err in the manner 
described above. { 13}  As it says in the Treasuryxix: 
 
The Buddha’s teachings have tw o aspects:  
their nature is scripture and realization.  
 
In accordance w ith this dictum, the teaching is nothing apart from the scriptural teaching and the 
realizational teaching.  
 
In Tibetan, the term for Buddhism is literally „the Dharma of the insiders‟. This shows 
that all the advice of Lama Tsongkhapa, Atisha and all the Kadampa masters is present 
in the scriptures and commentaries, and that all of t hem are either of the scriptural or 
realisational type. In the scriptural teachings, it is stated exactly how to apply the 
Dharma, and this is up to you. If you do this, you will be able to generate the 
realisations in your continuum, which is what the rea lisational teachings refer to, and 
your task is to apply the Dharma in order to work towards these realisational 
teachings.  
 That being the case, we can understand the relationship between these two 
teachings as cause and effect; to the scriptural teachin gs, you listen and study, and then 
by applying them you can bring forth these realisational teachings.  
 
The scriptural teaching is how  the Dharma should be practiced and establishes the manner of 
accomplishment, while the realizational teaching is what is [thus] established. Since one practices in 
accordance w ith w hat is to be established, these tw o act as cause and effect. To give an analogy: w hen 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 34 – week one to seven  you have a horse race, the horse is first show n the course and afterwards, likew ise, it runs there. It w ould 
be ridiculous to show it a course and then have the race elsew here. When it comes to attainments, how  
could it be appropriate to accomplish something after having determined something else through listening 
and reflecting? That is also expressed in the last  volume of Stages of Meditation : “Whatever has been 
realized by the w isdom arising from listening and reflecting is precisely w hat should be cultivated by 
means of the w isdom arising from meditation. J ust like a horse runs along the course it has been show n.” 
 
The scriptural teachings show you how to engage in the Dharma, how to run the race 
of Dharma practice. However, it is you yourself that has to run this race, since the 
Dharma can only perform the function of showing you what the racetrack looks like. 
As to the second part of the analogy, it would be laughable if, for example, you would 
study the Lam -rim here, and then turn somewhere else to do some other practice. In 
the same way that the horse runs the track it has been previously shown, you should 
meditate on the same instruction that you have previously listened to and reflected 
upon. If you would listen to one thing, reflect about something else, and then meditate 
on yet another thing, what would be the outcome of that?  
 To engage into studies like the Lam -rim, but then not know how to practice the 
Dharma, and turn somewhere else is utterly mistaken. If you would turn to advice 
from another lama, how could this lama have any better advice than what is expressed 
here in the Lam -rim, or in the Bodhisat tvacharyavatara? Not even His Holiness the 
Dalai Lama could give any better advice.  
Why this is discussed here in such detail is because of the headline that 
discusses the greatness of the Dharma. Moreover, the point is that there is no higher 
instruction  to be found anywhere than here in the Lam -rim. That being the case, I 
want to express that he really thinks that our excellent situation here, to be able to 
study the Lam -rim for a whole year, is an extremely fortunate one. We must have 
amassed incredible  merit to be able to enjoy such a situation.  
 
C Indication of analytical meditation as a means of making all teachings appear as instructions  
Thus, through summarising all the topics of paths set out in the excellent teachings and commentaries - 
starting from how  to rely on a spiritual teacher up to calm abiding and special insight - they are all 
condensed into stages of practical application in terms of performing stabilizing meditation w here 
stabilizing meditation is required and analyzing w ith the w isdo m of individual investigation w here 
analytical meditation is required. Thereby, these instructions guide you so that all the excellent teachings 
daw n on you as instructions. Then the certainty develops that they should be understood as supreme 
instructions , and the misconception that they should be understood as mere background [know ledge] for 
the Dharma rather than as actual instructions { 14}  is completely reversed.  
 
All the different teachings and instructions of the Kangyur and Tengyur, as well as all 
the commentaries of the Tibetan masters and all their key points are fully present in 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
35 the Lam -rim. There is not a single essential point that is missing. In addition, all the 
points that are to be cultivated by placement and analytical meditation are also 
included here.  
 By engaging in correct studies on these teachings, it will dawn on you that all 
the different teachings of the profound and vast path are actual instructions, and you 
will come to know that they are the unsurpassed and supreme instructions. In addition, 
another effect will be that you will generate a very strong conviction and faith 
concerning the great texts and their composers. For example, out of this great faith 
you will want to take great care of the texts and put them in a high place in  your room.  
 When we talk about faith in the Dharma, a fully -fledged faith would necessarily 
stem from having listened and reflected i.e. having studied the Dharma. Someone who 
has not studied will merely have a blind or confused faith, which has the effe ct of 
swinging back and forth between different instructions of various lamas, and making it 
a very weak faith. A strong faith is born out of having studied.
