2B2C -3 The greatness of allowing you easily to discov er the Jina’s intention  
Third: the greatness of allow ing you easily to discover the J ina’s intention  
A For beginners the Jina’s intention is not easy to find without a guru’s oral instructions  
Although the great texts, teachings along w ith their commentari es, form the supreme instructions, 
completely uneducated beginners cannot find the intention - even though delving into it - w ithout relying 
on excellent direct instructions, and even if they find it, they spend a lot of time and enormous effort.  
 
This ref ers to people who are beginners in the Dharma, and that without listening to 
the great teachings; they will be unable to practice.  
It is also a mistaken attitude to only remain in one‟s own room, just do one‟s 
own studies and not listen to a teacher. Such  an attitude is an indication of things not 
going at all well, and that there will no positive results from one‟s practice. There 
might be a possibility of gaining some small understanding, but gaining a good 
understanding would take a very, very long.  
  
B It is found through a guru’s oral instructions  
If they rely on a guru’s direct instructions and the like, it becomes easy to understand.  
 
2B2C -4 The greatness of a grave misdeed stopping by itself  
Fourth: the greatness of a grave misdeed stopping by itsel f: 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 36 – week one to seven  A Showing that abandoning the Dharma as the biggest misdeed  
As explained in the White Lotus [ Sutra] and the Chapter of the Truthful One , it amounts to 
abandoning the Dharma, if you hold that some utterances of the Buddha are means of attaining 
Buddhahood w hile others are obstacles to Buddhahood, dividing them into good and bad, appropriate 
and inappropriate or Mahayana and Hinayana, and t hereupon holding that a bodhisattva needs to 
train in some and not in others because you fail to understand that all the w ords of the Buddha directly 
or indirectly teach means of attaining Buddhahood. In the Sutra Gathering All the Threads  it says that 
the karmic obscuration due to abandoning the Dharma is so subtle that it is hard to recognize. 
Concerning the grave disadvantages that ensue if you abandon the Dharma, it says in the King of 
Concentration Sutra: 
Someone here in J ambudvipa  
tears dow n all th e stupas found,  
someone abandons a class of Sutras 
the latter’s misdeed is far more grave.  
 
Someone even murders arhats - 
as many as sand grains in the Ganges  
someone abandons a class of Sutras 
{ 15}  the latter’s misdeed is far more grave.  
 
Although there g enerally appear to be many w ays in w hich the Dharma might be abandoned, the one 
described previously seems to be the greatest, w hich is w hy w e should try hard to give it up.  
 
It needs to be understood that everything the Buddha said was in order to bring a bout 
a practice and method for gaining complete enlightenment, and therefore all the 
Dharma is to be regarded highly. Some people might say that some of the things the 
Buddha taught may even be obstacles to enlightenment, or they might assess the 
teachings  into being of low - middle - or high quality, or they might say that some are 
valid and some invalid. Alternatively, they may say that the Hinayana teachings are low 
teachings or that the bodhisattva needs to only train in one practice, but not another. 
This is due to a lack of understanding, and would incur the misdeed of abandoning the 
Dharma which is seen as the gravest misdeed. Such an attitude is despising the 
Dharma, as well as the Buddha himself.  
 At this point there is a reference to the Sutra  Gathering All the Threads , which 
refers to the subtle karmic obscurations one accumulates when abandoning the 
dharma, and the difficulty in getting rid of them.  
Where it says, „as many as sand grains in the Ganges‟, the number of sand grains 
should be understoo d as innumerable. If we imagine the negativities accumulated by 
destroying all the stupas in the world, and compare them to the negativities incurred 
through the Mahayana and Hinayana teachings being lost, than the latter is far worse 
than the former. This  is the case because if the teachings of the Hinayana and 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
37 Mahayana are lost or destroyed, there would be negativities accumulated with regards 
to each and every sentient being, and the obstacles created to them reaching higher 
goals or attainments. In addi tion, each and every Buddha and bodhisattva would be 
harmed because they also need to rely on the teachings. As a result, compared to this, 
the presence or absence of the world‟s stupas is a rather small thing just as even killing 
all these Arhats is also rather small.  
The motivation of a person abandoning the Sutra is a motivation of harming all 
the Buddhas, bodhisattvas and sentient beings. Merely saying that one personally 
leaves some teaching aside does not commit this misdeed in itself, but if you dec ided 
that a Bodhisattva needs to train in one thing, but not another because its Hinayana, 
and you look down on it in this way, then you would commit a great misdeed.  
 
QUESTION : But even sitting in your room and despising the Hinayana still harms no 
one. It‟s this person‟s problem, and it doesn‟t cause anyone to lose the path to 
enlightenment.   
ANSWER : It is not necessary to directly harm someone to accumulate great negativitie s. 
For example, just sitting in your room and being very angry or jealous towards 
someone accumulates a lot of negativity.  
That someone destroys all the teachings is somewhat impossible. Therefore, this 
refers more to this harmful intention of despising t he teachings. Of course, the 
destruction of all the stupas in the world has to be understood as an analogy in this 
case, as it would be impossible for this to be accomplished by a single person. Mao Tse 
Tung for example was really intent on destroying all the stupas and statues in Tibet, 
but couldn‟t succeed.  
For example, in the context of the ten non -virtuous actions, the three of mind 
are not directly harming anyone. But still, the last one of these three, wrong view, is 
the worst of all the ten. This wr ong view refers to the denial of karma and its effects, 
or denying the four noble truths.  
 The point here is that we should really be in charge of our mind, and block out a 
great many of these misdeeds from happening.  
 
QUESTION :  How does abandoning the Dharma go together with some teachings being 
definitive and some being interpretative, like the different turnings of the wheel?  
ANSWER : In this case, there is no abandonment involved. The Buddha himself said 
that everyone should a nalyse his teachings and not just accept them on the basis of 
blind faith. The appropriate analysis needs to be applied to manifest, slightly hidden 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 38 – week one to seven  and very hidden phenomena. He said his teachings need to be checked just as someone 
buying gold would cut, rub and burn the gold in order to test its quality.  
B When there is a realization along the lines of the first two greatnesses, the misdeed stops by 
itself  
That is to say since this is reversed simply by gaining certainty regarding what w as show n above, th e 
misdeed stops by itself.  
 
If you identify how all the teachings are instructions, then you would naturally not 
abandon the dharma, and this great misdeed would stop.   
 
C You should come to realize such certainty from the White Lotus Sutra  and other text s 
This certainty should be sought by studying the Chapter of the Truthful  One as w ell as the [Sutra of the] 
Excellent Dharma’s White Lotus . The other w ays of abandoning the Dharma should be learnt from the 
Sutra Gathering all the Threads .

QUESTIONS  
STUDENT : When we talked yesterday about abandoning the Dharma, I said this aspect 
of belittling has to be part of it. But what does this actually mean to belittle the 
Dharma?  
GEN-LA: An example here would be practitioners like us who are practicing the 
Mahayana and we relate to the teachings of the Hinayana by saying, “We as Mahayana 
try to practice the bodhisattva practices and shall not practice things described in the 
Hinayana scr iptures, because it is Hinayana. Because it is Hinayana, I‟m not going to 
deal with this at all.”  
STUDENT :  Therefore, if someone abandons the Dharma or not depends on the reason. 
If we say this teaching here does not lead to enlightenment, this is belitt ling and 
abandoning the Dharma. However, if I say I‟m not going to practice this because it 
doesn‟t bring me to enlightenment as quick as the other teaching, this probably 
wouldn‟t be abandoning or belittling the Dharma.  
GEN-LA: With regard to this second  one, “I will not practice this teaching because I 
have a quicker way”, this is not belittling the Dharma, and is therefore not abandoning 
the dharma.  
STUDENT : If abandoning the Dharma is such a heavy karma, why is it not part of the 
five heinous crimes?  
GEN-LA: Because it is heavier than these five! They are not called „heinous‟ in Tibetan, 
but more like limitless crimes. It means without border. So although it is called 
limitless, or without border, abandoning the Dharma is much heavier than these.  
STUD ENT: So would abandoning the Dharma qualify as a wrong view as described in 
the ten non -virtuous actions?  
GEN-LA: Yes, it‟s a wrong view. This is because when you have a mind that abandons 
the Dharma, it is a mind that views the Dharma wrongly; looking in the wrong way at 
the Dharma.  
STUDENT : Does abandoning the Dharma fit the definition of wrong views as described  
in the ten non -virtuous actions?  
GEN-LA: Yes, abandoning the Dharma is in harmony with this definition. [ to student ] 
Posit the definition of wrong view as given in the ten non -virtuous actions, and then 
we can see if it fits this definition.  
STUDENT : Tha t which fulfils the five characteristics of a wrong view.  
GEN-LA: Then show the five characteristics of a wrong view.

GEN-LA: Here we can think about someone who does not accept past and future lives. 
If they hold this view, it means they don‟t accept karma, actions and their effects. This 
is one example  of a wrong view. But within this category, you have some that engage 
in virtue, who like virtue, are generous and like to help others and so on. So here 
someone engages in virtue and has this 10th non -virtuous action of wrong view; that 
actions don‟t have  an effect, and that there is no past and future lives.

GEN-LA: This would have to be further explored. Some may have abandoned the 
Dharma in this situation, some might not have.  
 Something that makes clear what abandoning the dharma is, and something 
where there is actually danger of this happening to us is this; if someone says, “I‟m 
actually a tantric practitioner and therefore the perfection vehicle is not so important 
to me, because I‟m practicing secret mantra. And also because I‟m practicing secret 
mantra, what is not so important to me either is ordination, going forth. So because 
I‟m practicing secret mantra, these other practices are low and so on”. This is clear 
case of despising the teachings, despising dharma, abandoning Dharma. Here we have 
to understand our own teacher; the Buddha himself was an ordained person. In 
addition, his main disciples over time like the protector Nagarjuna, the master 
Aryadeva and so on , they were also ordained. So we can see this was clearly a very 
important practice to do. Therefore, if this person now says, “I‟m practicing secret 
tantra, so for me these teachings have no meaning, I don‟t have to practice this, this is 
a low teaching,”  this is a clear case of despising and abandoning the Dharma.

41  Alternatively, if we say we are following the Mahayana Dharma, so the Hinayana

 Also for example someone practices Highest Yoga Tantra, and he is saying

  Page | 42 – week one to seven  EXPLANATION OF HOW DHARMA POSSESSED OF T HE TWO GREATNESSES SHOULD

2B3 How Dharma possessed of the two greatnesses should be listened to and explained  
A How to listen  
B How to explain  
C Generally, how to proceed at the end  
The third one has three points: how  to listen, how  to explain and generally how  to proceed at the end.  
 
2B3A  How to listen  
1 Considering the advantages of listening to the Dharma  
2 Developing respect towards the Dharma and those who teach it  
3 The actual way to listen  
The first one has three points: consid ering the advantages of listeningxx, developing respect tow ards the 
Dharma and w ho teaches it and the actual w ay to listen.  
 
2B3A -1 Considering the advantages of listening  
 
There are limitless advantages of listening to Dharma. Its unperceivable, you can‟t 
pervade it with your mind.  
  
First: from the Verses on Listeningxxi:  
 
Through listening you come to know  the dharmas,  
By listening to Dharma teachings, you come to know the two truths, the four noble 
truths, the ten non -virtuous actions, or in general you come to know about karma and 
actions, what is a positive action, what is a negative action. All this you come to 
understand from listening.  
 
through listening misdeeds are turned aw ay,  
For example, by listening to the Dharma you come to understand what a heavy 
negativity abandoning the Dharma is, and from understanding the strong karmic 
outcome you turn away from that. Alternatively, knowing that engaging into the ten 
non-virtuous action s accumulates negative karma that leads you to the lower realms. 
Then not wanting to be born into this situation, you turn away from this. You turn 
away because from listening you understand what kind of results come from these 
kinds of actions. This is wh at is meant by turning away from misdeeds. So first, you 
listen to the dharma, and then you understand what kind of activities bring what kind 
of results, and then you don‟t engage.  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
43  
through listening all trivia are abandoned,  
 
All pointless activities a re abandoned. Here we have to understand negative 
pursuits, or negative activities. What we would abandon here after listening to the 
Dharma is negative acts or negative motivations. If you avoid negative motivations, 
then you avoid negative acts, and if y ou avoid negative acts then the negative results 
will not ripen. So in this way through listening to the Dharma all pointless activities, or 
negative acts are abandoned.  
 
through listening nirvana is achieved  
 
By listening, you come to know what is to be taken on and what is to be 
abandoned. Concerning your daily practice, we can understand what is to be taken on, 
what is to be abandoned and what is to be purified and so on. You come to know what 
you have to abandon and put aside, what you have to practice  on a daily basis. All this 
comes from listening to the Dharma.  
 Also listening to the Dharma is leading you to an ethical discipline that turns 
away from wrongdoing.  
 Previously we said how Lama Atisha had guarded his vows, and by how relying 
on mindfulne ss, introspection and conscientiousness he did not engage into anything 
that was not an ethical deed. He did not make the non -ethical into something ethical. 
By relying on these factors of mindfulness, introspection and conscientiousness, he 
was able to no t transgress. We should train in the same way. This is what is meant by 
training in ethical discipline, turning away from wrong doings. This all comes about 
from knowledge or listening.  
 When you achieve this turning away from the meaningless, you come to state 
where you are able to lead your mind to remain on a virtuous object. You stay one -
pointedly on virtue as long as you want. This comes from turning away from the 
meaningless, and this in turn comes about from having listened to the Dharma.  
 
  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 44 – week one to seven  These four [lines] say that in dependence on listening an understanding of the points that should be 
adopted and abandoned gradually develops. From this know ledge - ethical discipline that counteracts 
w rongdoing. Then, once you have turned aw ay from meanin gless pursuits, concentration w here the mind 
can stay on a w holesome object as long as you w ish arises. Then, through training in wisdom realizing 
the suchness that is selflessness, the root of samsara’s fetters is cut and you attain liberation.  
For examp le, this realisation of calm abiding comes about by turning away from 
the discordant class of calm abiding, and this is first achieved by listening to the 
Dharma.  
 There is a prayer that says:  
  “After having listening to the Dharma, that what you have he ard, you should analyse 
by way of the four reasoning‟s. And by doing this again and again, day and night, you will come to 
understand. In this way, may I be able to cut all doubts.”  
 
TRANSLATOR : What are the four reasonings?  
GEN-LA: These are found in many  places;  
1) Reasoning into dharmadatu  
2) Reasoning of dependent arising  
3) Reasoning of valid cognition  
4) Reasoning that is accomplishing tasks  
 This last one; an example of logical or reasoning mind that is accomplishing 
tasks would be with regards to the building of this gompa. It was first conceived by 
Jean-François; how it needed to be built, how it would look afterwards - this was such 
a logic al mind perceiving how to achieve tasks.  
 A reasoning into reality or reasoning of nature would be for example when you 
say, “Why does water flow downhill and not uphill?” This is its nature. Water flowing 
downhill would be such a reason by nature, or by reality.  
 We talked here about how things you‟ve listened to are to be analysed with four 
reasonings, and through this you can cut your doubts and come to understand it. This 
is especially talking about how you can understand selflessness. That someone com es 
to a realisation of selflessness or an understanding depends on having analysed with 
different types of reasoning again and again. This is only possible if he first engaged 
into studies, and listened to the presentations about selflessness. Someone who has not 
engaged into vast and extensive studies of the teachings of the Buddha will not have a 
realisation of emptiness. This realisation is not an object of knowledge; it doesn‟t exist.  
 By listening to Dharma presentations, and really understanding the D harma, 
then you come to the stage where you can cut the ignorance that is the root of 
samsara, and by doing so attain liberation. This attainment of liberation is entirely 
coming from listening, or in other words from studies.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
45 Therefore, this listening an d studying to the Dharma is of utmost importance, 
and as dharma practitioners we should cultivate the mind that there is nothing more 
important than this attitude.  
All the other aspects are secondary or branch activities. The main thing is to 
study the Dh arma.  
 I have encouraged you, if you feel capable, to recite a daily practice such as 
refuge 100,000 times, or the Migtsema prayer, over a year or a longer period of time. 
These are called preliminary practices ( Ngön -dro in Tibetan), and are quite supporti ve of 
our main activity of studying the Dharma, when you would a little each day. You put 
these in whenever there is time, but if someone says that they are going to stop 
everything and do their Ngön -dro practices, this is probably making an obstacle to 
Dharma practice, but as a secondary activity these practices can be very supportive.
 The main activity is always listening and reflecting on the Dharma. If someone 
goes away from this listening and reflecting to only doing his Ngön -dro practice, then 
this wo uld be the wrong way. In this case, it would be better if he only does listening 
and reflecting on the Dharma. If someone says, “I‟m going to take a break from the 
studies and go and do 100,000 prostrations or mandalas, and not listen and reflect on 
the Dh arma for a while,” this again would be the wrong attitude. We can also 
understand this from the great texts like Bodhisattvacharyavatara, the Lam -Rim, the 
Lojong texts and so on; they are not constantly emphasising how important it is to do 
these prelimina ry practices. What they do constantly talk about is how important it is 
to study the Dharma, to listen to the Dharma and to reflect on the Dharma, and the 
benefits of doing such. They do not emphasise these preliminary practices, but they do 
talk about the se practices as a side practice. We should understand these texts as being 
the guru‟s instructions. There is no better instruction to be had from the guru in 
comparison to instructions that are given in such texts as the Bodhisattvacharyavatara, 
Lam-Rim an d Lojong texts. We should understand the relationship between these two, 
and not leave the main activity to follow the branch activity.  
 As is said by Gundag Lama : “You should not act in the way that you let go of 
the main thing and go for the branches .” This would be like letting go of the trunk of 
the tree, and being more concerned with the branches of the tree.  
 In the text, another benefit of listening to the Dharma is that faith is generated. 
If you listen to the Dharma, and you study it, then you come to understand what it 
says. Through this understanding, then a strong, irreversible, high quality faith is 
generated that you will not turn away from. By understanding, you generate a strong 
faith, and no one could turn you away from that. the opposit e would be someone 
whose mind is carried away by someone in one direction and then another.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 46 – week one to seven  Some base their faith on an energy they felt with someone, or a feeling they had 
when they met somebody. They say, “I had a good feeling with this person,” and on 
this basis they have faith. This is very changeable, as your feelings are very changeable, 
and as such, is unreliable because they are actually of the type of the suffering of 
change. On the other hand, by listening and reflecting on the Dharma, you come t o an 
understanding of the Dharma, and on this basis, irreversible faith is generated.  
 By studying and listening to the Dharma, and understanding what is presented 
here, another effect is great joy arises in this mind and this joy is presented as being 
very stable.  
 For example: someone might have been a buddhist for 5 to 10 years but still 
they have to really check in their mind if they are a buddhist, or a convinced buddhist. 
They might say, “I‟ve been around for these many years, and when I look in my mind, 
I‟m kind of a buddhist with regards to things like karma and so one, I have some faith 
in that. I have some faith in the qualities of the refuge jewels and in going for refuge, 
but I still have some doubt.” This is a clear sign of not studying, analy sing and 
listening to the Dharma. We have therefore not been able to find stability or certainty 
with these topics, and so we are unconvinced.  
 
  In the J ataka stories , too, it says:  
He w ho through listening fills his mind w ith faith,  
{ 16}  grow s firm rejo icing in that w hich is excellent.  
Wisdom is born and bew ilderment vanishes - 
 
This last line means that first you are listening to the teachings, and different 
types of intelligence or wisdom arise. There‟s one type of wisdom from listening, and 
from this comes a wisdom of reflection. Many different wisdoms are generated on the 
basis of listening. Then from the wisdom of reflection comes the wisdom of cultivating 
or meditating – there are many wisdoms coming from this. All these come from the 
wisdom of list ening to the teachings, and all act as antidotes to confusion or 
bewilderment. On the one hand from listening comes wisdom, and on the other, 
confusion vanishes.

In the previous verse it says, “this is well worth paying for.” We could say here 
that one‟s own knowledge is of the type that can buy something. We are shrouded in 
this darkness of our ignorance, and by listening we are able to generate all kinds of 
wisdom. This wisdom is like a light that is able to clear away the 
darkness/bewilderment in us. These different wisdoms from li stening, reflecting and 
cultivating and so on are like money. In that by having these kinds of wisdoms we can 
buy new things, we can buy all the different realisations, virtues etc. for our mind, 
whilst at the same time getting rid of the old things; the i gnorance and so on. Further 
on, it says these virtues that we get from listening are positive aspects that no thief can 
take away from us.  
 
the w eapon destroying the enemy – ignorance  
 
This ignorance, which is completely pervading our continuum, is describ ed here 
in the Tibetan as something „completely obscuring‟ now and throughout our past. It is  
to be seen as our enemy, and that the wisdom comes about on the basis of listening is 
like a weapon that enables us to destroy the enemy of ignorance.  
  
best of  friends giving guidance in skilful means;

Great faults  are great misdeeds. We could refer to a great mass of these strong 
misdeeds as an army that causes you problems. However, this wisdom arising from 
study is  also like an army that is able to destroy them, so it is like the foremost of all 
armies.  
   
it is the best fame, and treasure and splendour as w ell.  
 
If someone is interested in fame or having a great name, then this wisdom 
coming from listening can bring about great glory and fame. As it says here, it is also 
the best fame and splendour. Treasure  here means that from this wisdom of listening 
you can pr oceed to the wisdom of reflecting, and from the wisdom of reflecting to the 
wisdom of cultivating. Then gradually more and more good qualities will be 
accomplished through the wisdom of listening until all good qualities are attained 
without exception. Thi s accumulation of good qualities is like a treasure, and this is 
what treasure means in this line.

2B3A -2 Developing respect towards the Dharma and those who teach it  
Second: from the Ksitigarbha Sutra : 
 
Listen filled w ith one -sided faith and respect,  
w ithout any mockery nor disrespect tow ards them,  
w orship the exponents of the Dharma,  
producing the notion of them as resembling Buddhas.  
 
  Then we have a quote from the Ksitigarbha Sutra , or the Earth Essence sutra . 
Firstly, this is telling us to listen with respect to Dharma teachings, and to abandon the 
opposite class; mockery or disrespect to the Dharma teacher and the Dharma 
teachings. Al so to listen by seeing the expounder of the Dharma as resembling the 
Buddha.  
 Mockery  here in Tibetan means a mind which is focusing on faults or a mind of 
fault -finding. We have to avert this mind, and listen to the Dharma without finding 
faults. Disresp ect refers to a mind of despising the Dharma or the Dharma expounder. 
Worship  means we should have great respect and even have a discrimination of them as 
resembling buddhas.  
 
  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 50 – week one to seven  In accordance w ith this quotation you should view  them as resembling a Buddha, offer them service w ith 
things like lion thrones, w orship them w ith gifts and give up disrespect.  
 
Je Tsongkhapa then says to view the one teaching the Dharma as resembling the  
Buddha. Then the way to offer service to them is to offer a Lion throne, give gifts and 
so on. In any case, we should relate to them without any kind of disrespect.  
 
In the Bodhisattva Levels  it says that you should be free from arrogance and, tow ards the  teachings and 
those w ho expound them, from disregard and you should respect those tw o.  
 
Here there is another quote from the Arya Asanga‟s text The Bodhisattva  Bhumis . 
He says you should be separated from arrogance and from any kind of scorn towards 
the Dharma, or the one who is propounding the dharma. Quite the opposite, you 
should have respect for these two.  
 
And in the J ataka stories  it says:  
Sit on a seat that is very low ,  
develop the glory  of discipline,  
see w ith eyes imbued w ith joy,  
w hile drinking  the nectar of the w ords.  
 
As has been said above in The Bodhisattva Bhumis , we should act without 
arrogance or pride. We should take a low seat in comparison to the one propounding 
the dharma. We should subdue our mind, and with this subdued state listen to the 
Dharma. One should be full of joy, liking and happiness to listen to the Dharma. This 
should be reflected in one‟s gaze; in one‟s facial expression. See with eyes imbued with 
joy it says here . One should listen to the Dharma in this way; like drinki ng nectar. This 
nectar is said to be very amazing, this is how we should experience listening to Dharma 
words.  
 
Bow  dow n in respect and concentration,  
w ith mind that is utterly2 pure and free of stains  
like one w ho is ill { 17}  heeds the doctor’s w ords,  
respectfully listen to the Dharma.  
 
 One should generate a respectful attitude to listen, and single -pointedly – this 
means to be concentrated – not be distracted. Pure; there is also the word utterly here. 
This means a very awake mind. The opposite of whi ch is when you are in a teaching 
                                            
2 Added according to Gen -la 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
51 and you are almost falling asleep. Utterly pure is not that, but an aspect of being clear 
and attentive. Free of stains  refers to being free of any kind of faults or stains.  
An analogy is then used. A sick person sees a doc tor. Because the patient wants 
to healed of the illness, he‟ll listen with great attention to what the doctor is saying and 
what he is prescribing. In the same way, we should, with great respect and attention, 
listen to the dharma just like the patient lis tens to the doctor.   
2B3A -3 The actual way to listen  
A Indication of how to listen in general  
B The need to listen, having related this to one’s mind  
C How to keep in mind the purpose of attainments  
2B3A -3A  Indication of how to listen in general  
1 Giving up the three faults of a vessel  
2 Relying on the discriminations  
 
The third one has three [points]: Giving up the three faults of a vessel and relying on the six 
discriminations.  
 
2B3A -3A1 Giving up the three faults of a vessel  
First: If a vessel is turned  upside dow n or held the right w ay round but unclean or clean but w ith the 
bottom leaking, then even if rain is made to fall on it by the gods, it cannot go in, or even if it goes in, it 
w ill be polluted by the dirt and therefore w ill not fulfil the purpos e of being drunk and the like or even if 
it is not spoiled by dirt, it w ill not stay but leak out.  
 
This is easy to understand and quite straightforward. This analogy is for the 
different faults that are wrong ways of listening to the Dharma. This upturned  glass for 
example on my table, no matter how much you pour onto it, no liquid would get into 
it. This relates to when we sit and listen to the Dharma, but our mind is distracted and 
thinking of something else. This is similar to the vessel that is upside down, nothing of 
the teaching will enter the mind.  
 In the second example, you are not distracted, but while you are listening you 
have wrong ways of appending or relating to it, mainly because you have a wrong 
motivation or only looking for faults. This is similar to a glass that is the right way up, 
which you can pour things into, but it is dirty. You can pour liquid inside and it will 
stay in, but whatever you pour into it will be unfit to use. Similarly, if we have this 
mind, with this wrong motivation  or attitude that is only looking for faults etc., then 
the Dharma will enter, but it will be polluted.  
 The last analogy of a wrong way to listen to the Dharma is this: a glass that is 
the right way up, it is clean so whatever you pour in is not polluted  or unfit to use, but 
as there is a crack in the bottom of the glass, it will leak out again. You will never be 
able to fill this glass, and whatever you put in will be lost. Similarly, here is the 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 52 – week one to seven  situation of someone who sits in Dharma teachings, listens  attentively, is not polluted 
by any wrong attitude or motivation, but he doesn‟t have this mental set -up or effort 
of also keeping it in mind, or later relate it others. If you ask him what was taught, he 
has nothing to say because he‟s already forgotten it. We should make effort to receive 
teachings in a way that we don‟t forget them.  
TRANSLATOR : How would you do this? What would you do to not forget the teaching 
whilst listening to the teaching?   
GEN-LA: You listen to the teaching, and then you ask your self “So what was taught? 
What was the topic? What was he talking about? What was he talking about after that?”  
TRANSLATOR : Is this talking about reviewing after the teaching, or during the 
teaching?  
GEN-LA: Right in the teaching, you ask yourself, “What is he actually teaching about 
now? What is being said now? How is this proceeding?” Therefore, with this kind of 
mental activity, you counteract this last kind of fault.  
 On the other hand, if any of these three states similar to these unsuitable vessels 
happen to you during the teaching, then there‟s not much point in you listening to the 
dharma. In that case, we definitely need to be separated from these three faults as 
shown in the analogy of the three vessels.  
 This first vessel, which is upside down,  shows a mind which doesn‟t properly 
listen but is distracted. The second is a vessel, which is the correct, is dirty – this refers 
to someone listening to the Dharma but with a wrong attitude or motivation. The third 
is a vessel, which is also the correct  way up, is clean, but is leaking due to a hole in the 
bottom. This was similar to someone who attentively listens to the teachings with the 
correct motivation, but immediately forgets everything due to not reviewing the 
teachings and thinking about them. We need to be free from these three. We want to 
be concentrated, attentive, with the right motivation, and we want to keep it in mind.  
Some teachers say; “Listen well, have a good intention, and don‟t forget what I‟m saying.”  
What is described here is refereeing to actual situations. For example, with 
regards to the first one, not listening: In the three monasteries of Ganden, Drepung 
and Sera, in generally everyone listens to the teachings and is attentive to what is 
taught. H owever, there might be someone in the back who thinks the teacher can‟t see 
him, and he might nod -off and sleep. Then when everyone leaves he‟s still there, 
asleep! This can also happen in the big assemblies, like when the puja is over, this one 
guy is sti ll left there sleeping, and this is of course very shameful and a great 
embarrassment.  Actually, he‟ll sit there during the event with his friends on both 
sides and then when the event is over his friends will very silently creep out, leaving 
him there!  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
53  This can also happen in the classes. Some teachers are quite old and there 
eyesight is not so good any more. There may be some people in the class who aren‟t 
interested in the books, so they stay at the back of the class where the teacher can‟t see 
them, and sleep or play something. If the teacher could see them, they would get some 
kind of punishment, or beating!  
 Some teachers also have a lot of things around them which they can throw at 
students who are sleeping! These people come to class and fall asl eep, or follow all 
kinds of thoughts. There are not just a few, but quite a lot of these.  
 These teachers are teachings many different classes, and the people that come to 
them are all from the same class. When they finish, another class arrives, which is  
another form and a different text, and so on, and then again another level of class and 
another text arrives. Within each of these classes, there are some that come and then 
sleep.  
 It also happens that the teacher falls asleep. This happens because ther e is one 
class after another, with no break between them. Sometimes they are exhausted and 
fall asleep.  
TRANSLATOR : Do the students then sneak out and go home?  
GEN-LA: No, they stay and make fun of him!  
 These teachers are doing this work for maybe 30 -50 years. Some are doing this 
long-term. The students of course at one point finish and leave, but the teachers are 
continually teaching.  
  
 In the same w ay it is to no great purpose to hear the Dharma, if you sit in a place w here it is being 
explained, but do not listen w ell, or listen, but take it w rong - w ith a faulty motivation and the like - or 
if, even in case these faults are not presen t, the w ords and the meaning you grasped at the time of 
hearing are not consolidated and get lost through forgetfulness etc. Therefore, one should be free from 
these [faults]. The antidotes to these three are set forth in three phrasesxxii in the Sutras: “Lis ten w ell - 
intensely - and keep in mind! ” And in the Bodhisattva Levels  w e are told to listen with the desire to 
know  everything, single -pointedness, attention, a humble mind, and the thought of all sentient beings.  
 
So these three faults of a vessel, we need to abandon when we listen to Dharma 
teachings. The antidotes to these can be understood from the sutra quote [above]:  
“Listen well  – intensely - and keep in mind! ” 
 
  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 54 – week one to seven   Listen well – you should have an excellent, positive attitude whilst listening.  
 Intensely – you are attentive to the teachings rather than being distracted and 
wandering of.  
 Keep in mind – you should try to hold what you are hearing and not forget it.  
 
This is also expressed in the Bodhisattva Bhumis  [above]: “You should have a wish 
to understand all.” If you have this wish, then you should listen single -pointedly and 
concentrated. You should listen very intensely, attentively, and also you should collect 
your mind by not being distracted. This means you should put all your mental capac ity 
into listening to the Dharma.  
 One could have been a buddhist for quite some time, and not understood well 
the Dharma. This is because that even though you have been in many teachings, you 
have not listened in the correct way, and therefore have not c ome to a correct 
understanding. Even if one has met many lamas, you still have not gained great 
understanding because of being distracted and so on. All of this comes from us sitting 
in these teaching situations without the wish to understand everything, w ithout being 
single -pointed, without listening attentively, without having gathered our mind (but 
instead we were distracted), and we didn‟t put the full capacity of our mind and 
thoughts into this activity of listening to the Dharma.  
 Lama Tsongkhapa has  intensely presented here the benefits of listening to the 
dharma. He has first quoted from the Verses on Listening , then, on this basis he 
discussed the benefits. Then he quoted a long piece from the Jataka Tales, then from 
the Ksitigarbha Sutra , then aga in from the Jataka Tales . In this way, Lama Tsongkhapa is 
intensely discussing the benefits of listening to the Dharma, and is showing a lot of the 
sources for these – he is pointing out how all these come from sutra. He is presenting 
all these sources to show it is coming from the Buddha himself, and not just from 
what Lama Tsongkhapa is saying. By doing this, he is pointing out that it is advice 
from the Buddha himself. The Buddha himself is pointing out that it is extremely 
beneficial and precious, and t here are a lot of benefits in listening to the Dharma in 
this way.  
It is good for us to reflect on all these benefits, and we can then come to regard 
listening to the Dharma as something very precious. When we have that, then we‟ll 
find it very easy to se parate from the three mistakes – unsuitable vessels – whilst 
listening to the dharma. Knowing the benefits of listening to the Dharma, which is 
something very precious, it then becomes easy to separate from these faults. In 
addition, you understand that by  listening to the Dharma you can get rid of all the 
afflictions, you can achieve liberation, attain the omniscient state of a Buddha – that 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
55 everything you want to get rid of you can be abandoned and so on. If you know these 
benefits, then you‟ll be able to  generate these six discriminations.  
 In other words, by understanding the benefits of listening to the Dharma, and 
by regarding them as very precious, you‟ll be able to, on the one hand, abandon the 
faults of listening to the dharma (similar to the three  vessels), and on the other hand 
be able to generate in your mind the six discriminations.  
 
THURSDAY 18 FEBRUARY 2010  - SESSION 3 
 
We have to listen to the dharma on the basis of the six discriminations.  
2B3A -3A2 Relying on the six discriminations  
A Contemplating the discrimination of oneself as a patient  
B Contemplating the discrimination of the one expounding the Dharma as a doctor  
C Generating the discrimination of what has been taught as medicine  
D Generating the discrimination of persistent pract ice as the cure  
E Contemplating the discrimination of the Tathagata as an exalted being  
F Generating the wish for the Dharma tradition to last long  
The second one has six points: contemplating the discrimination of oneself as a patient, of the one 
expound ing the Dharma as a doctor, subsequently generating the discrimination of w hat has been taught 
as medicine, the discrimination of persistent practice as the cure, contemplating the discrimination of the 
Tathagatas as exalted beings and generating the w ish for the Dharma tradition to last long.  
 
2B3A -3A2A  Contemplating the discrimination of oneself as a patient  
First: In Engagingxxiii it says:  
 
Even w hen stricken w ith ordinary illness  
{ 18}  one has to follow  the w ords of the doctor  
how  much more so, if a hundred  
ills like lust strike you dow n all the time!  
 
Here the quote from Engaging  refers to Bodhisattvacharyavatara . Even with any 
physical sickness we would go to a doctor, and attentively listen to his advice. With the 
Dharma, we are talking about an illness wh ich is many, many more times intensive 
than a physical suffering; we are talking about the suffering of our mind.  
What we are suffering from is a sickness that has been with us since 
beginningless time. What we mean here is afflictions like attachment, de sire and so 
forth. These are continuously with us, and that we are continually born in samsara and 
staying in these states of suffering is due to these afflictions - these are the causes of 
our suffering. It‟s a very strong sickness and as it has been with  us for all that time it‟s 
something that is not easy to treat.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 56 – week one to seven  So what we need to do first is to come to know this sickness of ours, we need to 
understand it.  
  
As is mentioned here, ow ing to mental afflictions like attachment etc. w e always suffer from  diseases 
causing long -lasting and severe suffering that is difficult to cure. Therefore w e first need to diagnose them 
as such. Kamaw a said: “If it is not in accord w ith the actual situation, our meditation goes off in the 
w rong direction. We have been st ricken w ith a serious illness due to the three poisons, the illness is very 
grave, but w e do not know  w e are ill at all.”  
 
So regarding our situation, the Yogi Kamawa  is quoted. Here we are entering 
into activities that which is pointless, and harmful, due  to being under the power of 
the three poisons. They are so powerful, and so they are said to be the greatest 
sickness. Their intensity is so great, but whilst this is the case, we are not even aware 
that we are sick.  
 This sickness, which is in the form o f our ignorance, attachment and anger – is 
of a very great strength, the cause of our incessant suffering, and deeply rooted in us. 
So what is our task now? We need to come to know this, because until now we have 
not even been aware of it.  
 Because we are  ailing from three poisons (attachment, anger and ignorance), we 
have to experience birth and death without any freedom. We have to endure these 
without any say from our side. Not only that, but we also have many things happen to 
us that we don‟t like, wha t we don‟t wish for, and what we wish for doesn‟t come 
about. They all come from these three poisons.  
 We need to understand that we have this great sickness of the three poisons of 
attachment, aversion and ignorance, and in order to do that, we need to g enerate the 
discrimination of ourselves as being a patient.  
 
  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
57 2B3A -3A2B  Contemplating the discrimination of the one expounding the Dharma as a 
doctor  
Second: If w e are stricken with a serious illness like for example a w ind or gall disease, w e look for a  
specialist in medicine and meeting one w e are extremely happy, listen to w hat he says, treat him w ith 
respect and acknow ledge him. In the same way one looks for a spiritual guide teaching the Dharma and, 
having found one, accomplishes w hat he says, taking  it not as a burden but as an ornament, acting w ith 
respect and reverence tow ards him.  
 
In the case of us having a serious illness of the wind, gall or phlegm type – if we 
had any of these strong sicknesses, we would immediately try to rely on a good docto r. 
We‟d be very attentive to what he says, show him great respect, we‟d be eager to hear 
what advice he has to give and whatever he tells us to do, we would do. This is how 
we‟d relate to a doctor if we had a severe physical sickness.  
This spiritual sickn ess we have is more severe than any physical sickness, and 
because  of this, we should listen very attentively to what the spiritual friend says. We 
should follow exactly what he is saying, and just like having a physical sickness where 
we‟d be very happy to see the doctor, we should have joy towards the spiritual friend 
who is telling us the antidotes to our sickness of the three poisons.  
Just like in the case of this doctor who is giving a diagnosis of our physical 
sickness and telling us what we need to  do in order to work to remove the sickness, we 
follow this exactly. Similarly, the spiritual friend is identifying the poisons, our spiritual 
sickness, and is giving with his diagnosis the way we have to deal with it. Just like we 
do with a doctor for a p sychical sickness, in the same way, we should take this advice, 
put it into practice, and work in the way advised by the spiritual guide. In this way, we 
are generating the discrimination of the spiritual guide as being like a doctor.  
 
2B3A -3A2C  Generatin g the discrimination of what has been taught as medicine  
Third: J ust as a patient greatly appreciates the medicine put together by the doctor, one regards the 
instructions and teachings given by someone expounding the Dharma as the most important thing, 
making an effort to hold them in respect and not w asting them through forgetfulness and the like.  
 
Here we are talking about discriminating the Dharma as medicine. In the case of 
someone who is physically ill, he will have received medicine from the doctor a nd 
some instruction on how to use it. Then he will take great pains to follow exactly the 
instructions on how to take the medicine. For example, wherever he goes he will take 
his medicine with him. As soon as he arrives somewhere, he will think, “Do I stil l have 
my medicine? Did I leave it somewhere? Do I still have it with me?” He‟ll be very 
concerned about having the medicine, and about taking the medicine.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 58 – week one to seven  In the same way with the Dharma practice given to us by the spiritual guide, we 
should have a simi lar mind of being very careful with it, and in this way, we‟ll be 
generating a discrimination of the Dharma as medicine.  
 
2B3A -3A2D  Generating the discrimination of persistent practice as the cure  
An ill person having received advice and medicine understa nds that if he doesn‟t 
take the medicine he will not be cured. Understanding this he will taken great pains to 
take the medicine at all times, and exactly in the way prescribed. As we know, some 
medicines taste horrible, but nevertheless this person, with great effort, will still take 
his medicine.   
Just as in the case of the where sick person knows the medicine does not have 
the capacity to heal him if he doesn‟t take it, likewise the person with the sickness of 
the three poisons also understands that with  the advice of the spiritual guide, if he is 
not taking it, then it won‟t have the capacity to cure him of the three poisons. 
Understanding that, the practitioner who understands himself to be spiritually ill is 
understanding that the cure comes from persi stent practice. In this way, you generate 
the discrimination of persistent practice as the cure.  
The problem here is to have received a lot of instruction and advice from the 
spiritual friend and whilst having received this advice, not putting it into practice. It 
would be like someone having listened to the advice of a doctor regarding his physical 
sickness, then making effort to get the medicine, maybe spending a lot of money to 
buy it, but not take it. His sickness will not be healed. In the same way for us, if we 
have received instructions from the spiritual guide, and we don‟t put them into 
practi ce as prescribed by him, then our afflictions, and especially the three poisons, 
will not be diminished and we will not be healed from this sickness.  
 
1 The contempt of striving for words without practicing them  
Fourth: An ill person sees that his illness  cannot be alleviated, if he does not take the medicine the doctor 
put together, and takes it. Likew ise, once you see that attachment etc. cannot be eliminated w ithout 
practicing the instructions the Dharma teacher has taught, you should practice diligentl y and not make a 
great effort at vain w ords of numerous classifications w ithout putting them into practice. As a matter of 
fact, w hat good w ould it be to a leper w hose hands and legs are coming off to resort to a dose of medicine 
once or tw ice. { 19}  Likew i se, for us who have been stricken since beginningless time by the w icked basis 
of illness that is the mental afflictions, it is not sufficient to practice the meaning of the instructions only 
a couple of times. This is w hy w e should examine all the complet e parts of the path with discriminating 
w isdom and be as diligent as a flow ing river.  
 
Here it is talking about a leper in a very desperate state. In general, we can talk 
about those people with strong sicknesses such as a strong skin disease where the sk in 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
59 falls off etc. If they just take the medicine once or twice, they will not be able to get 
rid of it completely. Therefore, instead of just taking the medicine once or twice, we 
need to take it for a long time, so the time factor here is also important.  
 In our case, we have this situation of ailing from this extremely severe and harsh 
sickness of the three poisons of attachment, anger and ignorance. Because this is so 
deeply rooted and severe, it is not enough to listen just once or twice to the advice of 
the spiritual guide and just put into practice once or twice. Firstly, we need to engage 
into listening to the complete presentation of the Dharma, and by understanding this 
we‟ll come to a state where we understand all the Dharma teachings as being 
something to be used.  
As it says in the four greatnesses – the second of which was the greatness of all the 
Buddha‟s teachings dawning on you as useful instructions . For this to come about, which is 
necessary for you to apply this medicine, you have to engag e into prolonged studies 
and come to know the complete presentation of the Dharma.  
 
It is like it says in the Praise of Confessing:  
... That is to say our minds are always obscured,  
for long w e have served the causes of our ills  
how  can a leper w hose hands  and feet fall off  
and w ho takes his medicine but rarely derive benefit?  
 
 Here the text expresses that it is so essential to generate this discrimination of 
oneself as a sick person; as a patient. Because if you have that, coming to know your 
own state of  having attachment, anger and ignorance, then all the other discriminations 
will come along naturally. If someone has a strong physical sickness, then he‟ll be able 
to do everything that is in his power to act on this sickness. For example, if someone is 
normally very stingy with his money, the moment he becomes extremely  sick, he‟ll give 
all his money away to counteract the sickness. In other words, he has now become 
capable to use his money where he wasn‟t before. In the same way, if we can bring 
about a good understanding of ourselves as being sick, then all the other 
discriminations and factors will fall into place.  
 However, if we just pay lip service to this kind of teaching, and we say, “Yes 
we‟re all so sick, yes I‟m suffering from these poisons,” b ut in the depth of our heart 
we don‟t have any interest in this situation, then it will be impossible to destroy the 
afflictions in our continuum.  
 This means we won‟t be able to attain to holy aims or goals. We‟ll just have a 
mere listening or mere knowl edge of the Dharma, and from that there will be no 
change.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 60 – week one to seven   Similar to having gained the advice from the doctor on how to take the 
medicine, and we are then engaged in so many preparations to take the medicine - but 
don‟t actually take it - in all these p reparations there is no healing of our sickness. So 
our situation would be like that.  
 
2 The importance of the discrimination of oneself as someone ill  
That being so the discrimination of oneself as someone ill is very important. If it is present, the other ones 
also arise. How ever, if this remains merely w ords without the meaning of the instructions being 
accomplished for the sake of eliminating the ment al afflictions, you w ill only be a listener. You w ill 
resemble an ill person w ho is not rid of his illness if, having sought a doctor, he only cares about the 
medicinal composition and does not take the medicine - says the King of Concentration [Sutra] . It also 
says: 
 
If, after I have explained the excellent Dharma,  
you don’t apply it w ell, although you heard it,  
it is like w ith patients w hose pouches are full of medicine,  
but w ho still cannot cure their ow n diseases.  
 
What the Buddha is expressing here to his disciple is, “I have explained the 
Dharma to you. I have told you how to deal with the three poisons in your continuum, 
and you have listened. But now you are not applying it.” This is like someone with a 
physical sickness, has relied on the excellent doctor, has received medicine, and then 
keeps the medicine around him in all kinds bags, like hand -luggage. Like carrying a big 
box of medicine around with him, but never takes it. On this basis then he never gets 
cured. Similar to this is someone who is n ot applying the dharma.  
 
And in the Guide :3 
 
These should be put into practice physically,  
w hat w ould be gained by uttering just the w ords?  
Would it be beneficial to the patients,  
if they just read how  the medicine is meant to be used?  
 
Again here, we have  a patient who needs an operation or needs to take 
medicine, but he is not engaging, instead he sits and reads about his sickness, just 
reads about what the doctor says. Maybe he reads a medical book on the sickness. 
Actually what he needs is the operation , but through his lack of action, the sickness 
will not be cured.  
  
                                            
3 Referring to Shantideva‟s Bodhisattvacharyavatara or Guide to the Bodhisattvas Way of Life. Chapter 5, v109.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
61 3 The instruction to act [accordingly], having identified [the meaning of] “persistence”  
Therefore the discrimination that persistence eliminates the disease should be developed. “Persist ence” in 
this statement signifies putting into practice the topics of adopting and abandoning [of modes of conduct] 
from the spiritual teacher’s instructions. { 20}  Now , to put them into practice you need to know  them and 
for that you need to listen and stu dy. Again, after studying and know ing you need to practice and 
therefore it is crucial to engage in the meaning of w hat has been studied to the best of one’s abilities. 
Otherw ise, at the time of death, you w ill have regrets with regard to what you have not  accomplished. 
You w ill resemble an actor imitating others or someone interested in sugar eating only sugar cane.  
 
Therefore, this discrimination of persistent practice as the cure  is very 
important. This persistence means we have received the advice from  the spiritual guide 
about what is to be practiced and what is to be abandoned, and then one has to put 
this into practical use.  
We need to put this advice of the guru into practice. But before we act we need 
to understand, and in order to know or understa nd we first have to listen. After 
listening, we come to know, then that what needs to be done is to engage; we have to 
act. What we have heard about the meaning of the Dharma, we should put this into 
practice as much as we are able.   
What happens if you d on‟t put this into practice? Our time is running out, at 
one point this human life will be over and then there will be the time of death. At the 
time of our death, we will experience a mind of great regret or sadness because we will 
realise what we have mi ssed and failed to do. This can be further expressed by two 
analogies.  
If you want to become and expert dancer or actor, you need to train in those 
skills. You need to study, to work at it, to learn the skills and then finally you‟ll 
become an expert danc er or an outstanding performer. However, by just mimicking 
another dancer or another famous actor, this will not happen. You will have to make 
an effort, to study in this and to train.  
 Alternatively, another analogy is if you want to eat sugar, but inste ad of making 
the effort to get this sugar, you chew on sugar cane. As a result, you won‟t get the 
experience of sugar because it‟s not the same. You need to actually go for the sugar.  
These are analogies for someone who has not engaged into the actual act .

  Page | 62 – week one to seven  FRIDAY 19 FEBRUARY 2010  - SESSION 1 
FUTURE OF STUDY AT NALANDA  
I would like to say something about what we will be studying in the future. Now 
we are in the third year of the Basic Program, and throughout this year we will be 
studying the Middle Length Lam -Rim. Next year we‟ll look at several topics, the main 
one will  be first chapter of the Uttaratantra (Sublime Continuum of the Mahayana) and 
the fourth chapter of the Abhisamayalamkara (Ornament of Clear Realisations). In the 
final year, we will enter a year of tantric studies, and at the end of the program will be 
a retreat. This program of studies is to be seen as a first step  
Then in the next stage, there will be a new Basic Program,  and for those who have 
completed this current Basic Program, there will be a Masters Program. Those who 
have completed this Basic Program can move up to the Masters Program, which maybe 
seven to ten years long. Therefore, we will have two programs running  at the same 
time.  
 If we compare the Masters Program topics with what they study in the Three 
Great Seats (Ganden, Drepung and Sera Monasteries), they are exactly the same. 
Specifically, Perfection , Madhyamaka  and the Abhidharmakosha  (Treasury of 
Knowled ge). The three great monasteries would also study the logic text Pramanavartika  
(Compendium on Valid Cognition) and Vinaya, but these are not part of the Masters 
Program .  
When in the Masters Program we study Perfection , in the East they study 
Abhisamayala mkara (Ornament of Clear Realisations). When we study Madhyamaka , 
they study the Madhyamakavatara  (Entering into the  Middle Way ), and then we study 
the Abhidharmakosha  – so these three texts are studied exactly like in the three great 
seats of Ganden, Drep ung and Sera.  
If someone in the Masters Program is engaging into full studies, then he will be 
able to achieve great aims, this means he will be qualified as a teacher. Then by 
teaching to others, great benefit will be achieved because it is only possible  to be a 
teacher once you have engaged into studies. When we go to this stage of having two 
programs running at the same time, a second teacher will come to Nalanda.  
 Therefore, it is clear how important it is to engage into these studies. I see there 
are many students here who are still very young – to those I ask to generate a strong 
enthusiasm to really go for these studies. Buddhism is not about making prayers, or 
accumulating mantras or pilgrimage, but rather it is about studying and listening to the 
dharma. This is also expressed by way of the three circles, the first of which is called 
the circle of listening and reflecting. This is to be understood as the main activity. In 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
63 the Middle Length Lam -Rim it says we should listen to the Dharma, and through  this 
come to understand the Dharma and then apply the Dharma, otherwise it is of no use. 
Within this sequence, the most important step is to listen or to study.  
 Listening and studying the teachings means vast and extensive listening. We 
have to hear a l ot. Only when you have this vast study can you have a correct 
understanding. Then when understanding comes about, you have to put it all into 
practice. Through what you have studied, you have to put it into practice as much as 
you are able.  
 It might be y ou are in a place like this monastery for many years, but whilst you 
are here, you don‟t really engaging into listening, studying and reflecting. As a result, 
you don‟t come to understand the Dharma, and through not having understood the 
Dharma you don‟t h ave something you can practice. You don‟t have the basis of 
understanding the dharma, so therefore you cannot practice the dharma, and great 
meaning in your life is lost; you just pass the time becoming older and older. In this 
way, your life is quite empt y, a waste, and then it‟s possible you generate sad thoughts 
or regret, “All these years I have been in Buddhism, and I have no benefit me at all.” 
This situation is not the fault of this monastery, and not the fault of the Dharma - but 
it is the fault of not having engaged into study and reflecting. Therefore, it is one‟s 
own fault.  
 People should therefore understand how important studies are, and we should 
generate a strong confidence and enthusiasm thinking, “I will definitely stay and 
complete these s tudies right until the end of the Basic Program. Not only that, but I‟ll 
also engage into listening and reflecting on the Masters Program.” If you spend your 
entire life in this way, studying the Buddhadharma in a fully -fledged and high -quality 
way, and on  a daily basis, you practice as much as you can, then this is the best way. 
This is much better than going to a cave for example, or like going to Bodhicitta Land 
and having nothing to reflect on. If you don‟t know the Dharma, then there is no way 
to culti vate anything up on a mountain. In comparison to that, it is much better to stay 
here for the rest of your life and practice on a daily basis as much as you can. If you 
practice in this way, there will be no regrets at the end of your life.   
 Especially w ith regard to the monks here, they should definitely see this 
monastery as their monastery – this is where I belong, where I am living. This is my 
monastery, this is my home for life, and as such I am the owner of this place, and this 
is where I‟m practici ng the Dharma.  
In this way, you identify with this place as your home. For example, when you 
want to leave  you should ask permission from the director to leave, for the time that 
you want to be away. Then when you return you should again see the director a nd say 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 64 – week one to seven  you are back. This is all connected with identifying this place as where you live. Being 
a monk, you have a monastery. A monk without a monastery is something very strange, 
quite surprising, and actually quite embarrassing. A monk has a monastery, a nd this is 
your monastery, this is what you should see as your home.   
 The new and young monks, being of a rather young age, you also will become 
40, 50, 60 etc. You should visualise that at that time, you will be here. Of course, 
sometimes you can go awa y for weeks or months, but then you come back. Even if you 
go away for a year, you come back to your monastery; back to your home.  
 You really need to generate the feeling of this is my place. Especially with the 
young monks, you should generate the thoug ht this is my place, and have the wish to 
stay here in your home for your whole life. This is in the context that you have already 
accepted to remain ordained for your entire life. You have all the conditions and 
studies here, you can be here and live your  monk‟s life and engage into study and 
practice through continuously engaging into the Basic Program and the Masters 
Program and so on. If you do this in a very excellent way, then you come to know all 
these texts, and it is possible you become a qualified  teacher as a result. Then through 
being a qualified teacher, you might then go to a Dharma centre, maybe even for 
several years. Even then, you still come back to your monastery. The dharma centre is 
not your monastery, Nalanda is your monastery and you s hould come back here and 
generate the feeling this is my home and my monastery.  
 If you as a monk stay in your monastery, and at one point the thought comes 
up, “I should leave this place and stay somewhere else.” What do you want to do in 
this other plac e? Why would you go there? What is the benefit? If you think, “I‟m 
going to Bodhicitta Land because I want do retreat,” - this is different. If after many 
years someone has done all his study, has come to know the Dharma and now wants to 
put it into practi ce, then that‟s different. But even then your home is still here, not at a 
retreat land. Maybe someone says, “I want to live in a house in Lavaur or Toulouse, 
maybe I want to live in London or Paris". There‟s no point in this, actually it is 
detrimental. I f you go to live in London or Paris, there is no benefit to you, actually 
you might get crazy in this place. How would your visualise you life there? If you stay 
in a Dharma centre and teach there – it is very difficult to stay in a dharma centre. 
Maybe yo u think to stay with your parents or relatives. How long are you going to stay 
with them? How do you imagine this working out? This is not conducive, you need to 
be here in a monastery. If you just stay for one week with your parents, it‟s completely 
clear  that there will be problems coming. Therefore, your dwelling place is right here 
in this monastery.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
65  The gist here is that your continuous living place is this monastery. Temporarily 
I might go somewhere else, but my continuous place of living is here in  this 
monastery. This kind of mind we need to generate. We need to have clear in our minds 
that where I am living for the rest of my life is this monastery; this is where I will 
always come back to, my central dwelling place.   
  
In my own case, I am a mon k of Sera. When I go back, I just stay for a few days, 
maybe 10 days. When I am there, when other monks see me they say, “Oh, this is a 
Sera monk. This Geshe is one of our Geshes.” There is always a place where I can stay, 
people will always offer somewher e for me to stay. I‟m always allowed to stay in Sera 
because I‟m a Sera monk. If people ask where I‟m from, I would say “I‟m a Sera 
monk.” It‟s the same for us, we should say, “I‟m from Nalanda, I‟m a Nalanda monk.” 
The feeling that is connected with this is that when you go to other places, you feel 
you are with other people, like you are in -front of other people somehow, but when 
you come back here, then you feel some relief, “Now I am home.”  
 By staying in a monastery, there are also rules. For example,  one of the essential 
rules concerns how the younger monks have to relate to the elder monks. They have to 
look at the example of the elder monks.  
There is a saying that goes: “In a monastery people should stay in the same way as the 
feathers of a bird, a nd should not stay in the way of the hairs of a horse.”  
This refers to the younger monks being under the elder monks, and listening to 
what they say. Firstly, they are all monks. If someone says, “What about this elder, I‟m 
also ordained” – it‟s true, they  are all ordained, but there is a big difference in the 
length of ordination. The relationship between the younger and the elder monks 
should not be like the hairs on a horse, in that they all stand up, rough, with no rules 
or relationship between them. In  contrast, in a monastery it‟s like the feathers of a bird 
where the upper feathers are lying on the lower ones, and in this way they are 
completely smooth and cover all of the bird. This is one of the essential rules of the 
monastery, and how you live tog ether; the younger have respect for the elders. Of 
course, the elders also have to abide by the rules, and it is necessary that they should 
hand down and teach the rules to the younger ones.  
 When I ask who is the owner of the monastery, it‟s not that Ven erable Tendar 
or the director of the monastery is the owner. The director might change in years to 
come, so it‟s not that Venerable Tendar owns this monastery. So who owns it? It is 
owned by the monks, the monks who are saying, “This is my monastery, this is my 
home.” Then they would also have the attitude of, “I‟m taking care of this place, I 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 66 – week one to seven  want this place to be good, I want to engage in this place.” As a result of this attitude, 
it is the core community of the monks that own this place.  
 Taking the responsibility for the monastery is not only the job of Venerable 
Tendar, but rather, it‟s everyone‟s job. The responsibility is with the monks of this 
monastery.  
 To the lay people, you should use this place as if it is yours, but it is not your 
home. The  monks welcome you as you are studying here, you are welcome here, and 
no one has the right to say you can‟t be here, but this is not your home. Whilst this is 
the case, you can use this place as if it was your own, just as the monks use this place.  
 You can also have this outlook of this is the place where I study and practice the 
Dharma, accumulate merits, and live for these activities. This is my densar  – place of 
reliance used in terms of the three monasteries in India.  
 As this is the home of the mon ks and their responsibility, they need to take on 
the responsibility of how the monastery comes across and appears to visitors, 
volunteers etc. These people come and see Nalanda as a place where the Dharma is 
practiced and studied, and so on, and it is the  responsibility of the monks that people 
see this in a positive way. You should have a loving concern and care for them, so that 
they can generate a positive and happy attitude towards Nalanda.   
 If for example, unrelated visitors or lay people arrive to see what goes on here, 
it is good for them to see people here engaging into vast studies, how the monks have 
a positive way of acting with the outside world, and that amongst themselves they have 
a regulated and disciplined that follows the rules and so on . For example, this could 
include seeing how the younger monks have respect for the elders. This is something 
that we monks have to take care of, that this kind of behaviour is seen, and we do this 
by actually living in this way.  
Then lay people who are l iving and studying here, can also engage into this kind 
of activity; so that people can see this is the kind of place where rules are followed and 
studies are undertaken etc.  
 From my own life in the monastery, I never engaged in any activity which 
people  from the outside would think, “This is very negative behaviour for a monk.” 
Instead, I have cultivated a mind that sees everyone in my Kamtsen  as my family. I 
disciplined the younger monks myself to follow the rules, and to only have a friendly 
attitude t oward others, and show respect. With this, they were able to look up to me 
and learn from me. Everyone in the Kamtsen  has generated a mind that is interested in 
Dharma studies, and now when I go back to Sera and meet these people, they have all 
turned out well. In our monastery, in the same way, by cultivating good behaviour and 
good ways of relating to others, then from the outside people will see this and say, “In 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
67 this monastery they have a good way of behaving and a good way of relating to 
people.” Then on this basis, you might be able to offer some Dharma advice to them, 
and they would accept it. This kind of relationship we should to try to cultivate.  
 I feel that amongst us there is not much back -biting or pointing out each other‟s 
faults, but in gene ral, we have to be aware of this fault finding in others, and we 
should be reducing this as much as possible. We should direct it towards our own 
mind and our own faults. I would like to request that you cultivate this in the best way.  
Regardless of being  a monk, nun, or layperson staying here in this monastery, or 
even if you are only staying here a few days, weeks, month, years or your whole life. 
Regardless of all this, we should see this as our dwelling place, and that we don‟t want 
to create any probl ems for these people we are living with.  
 We have to remind ourselves that we came to here to pacify our problems, we 
came here so that problems do not arise.  
 What comes about through making small or great problems? It is actually 
creating negative karm a, or negative actions. Dissolving problems and entering into 
excellent modes of conduct is called virtuous conduct, and, in a nutshell, it is simply 
that.  
 It is also good for the four nuns we now have here to generate the mind that 
thinks that this is t heir monastery,  as they don‟t have a nunnery, but they stay outside 
the monastery. They can‟t use the monastery in same way as the monks can because 
you can‟t stay here overnight, but they can still generate this mind, “This is my 
monastery, and I will not generate problems for people living here. etc. Due to a lack 
of conducive conditions, there is no nunnery here at the moment, but I can relate to 
this place being my monastery, and relate to the community in the same way.” You 
can‟t use it in exactly the s ame way as the monks, but you are ordained just like the 
monks and you should see it in the same way as the monks do.  
 To those who plan to be become ordained in the future, they can also develop 
the mind that in the future this will be my home.  
 Those o f you who have already been here for quite some time, you should have 
no regrets about time you have spent here. You should think, “I have listened to the 
Dharma for such a long time, I have been here together with others in a harmonious 
way. During all th is time, I have not made big problems and I have abided by the rules. 
Actually on a daily basis I have worked in virtue, and accumulated virtue.” In this way, 
you have a positive outlook on your time here. The opposite of this is thinking, “I did 
this yest erday. And then I did this, then I did that ...” You only think about the 
negative things that you did, you beat yourself up and generate a regretful mind. This 
is not helpful. You should look at how things went well during this time.

For example, if we are watching a movie, and in this movie there are the 
different heroes being played by actors, and they sing and dance and so on. When we 
watch this kind of movie, we are constantly focusing on the good qualities of this actor 
and the part he is playing. We are not sitting there and looking at his faults. We are 
enjoying this movie because we are constantly focusing on the good qualities. In the 
same way, someone who li kes visiting museums would go to these places, only looks at 
the good qualities and enjoy these aspects. He‟s not going there to look at the faults, 
but I think there are people like this! This is very strange because there are only old 
things in museums, but still these people delight in these old things. At least with a 
movie there is something new, new dances and so on, but they are still focusing on 
good qualities, and not looking for faults.  
 The point here is you should see the good qualities of someo ne else and 
generate joy about them.

qualities of someone else, but constantly looking for the faults. Therefore, the faults of 
delighting in words means delighting in talking abo ut others faults which brings about 
your own utter degeneration. The first part talks about seeing the good qualities in 
others, the second part about how you degenerate by constantly talking about someone 
else‟s faults. Another example is in this next ver se:

In the bark of sugar -cane there is no essence,
