This sugar cane is a plant that has a bark. This is about the sweet taste that you 
get from this sugar. A little is already in the bark, but the essence is in the core of the 
plant, where the sugar is. If someone chews on the bark, they will get a little of the 
sweet taste, but they will not get to the essence, the actual sugar.  
 
Similarly,  w ords are like the bark,  
the taste is like the meaning of those w ords.  
 
There is only a little sweetness in the bark of the sugar cane, so we shouldn‟t 
focus on the bark. The words are similar to the bark here in that they are not the main 
focus, but the meaning is the main thing, like the sugar.  
 
Consequently abandon delighting in w ords,  
alw ays b e conscientious reflecting the4 meaning!  
 
We have to abandon delighting in words. This “words” means talk which is 
finding fault in others. This is a wrong conduct and something we need to abandon. 
We are asked to use the factor of conscientiousness and become the owner of our 
speech.  
 
QUESTION :  Why is the definitive meaning to be preferred to the interpretable 
meaning? Geshe Sopa says the meaning of this verse is to be summarised in the four 
reliances5, this being the third reliance. I understood this means both of these types of 
sutras are vali d, but are just presenting a different way of understanding the same 
thing.  
GEN-LA: These interpretive sutras are of benefit. Here we can talk about the first 
turning of the wheel of Dharma where the Buddha taught that all phenomena exist by 
way of their own defining characteristic, but in actual fact they don‟t. This is an 
example of the Buddha teaching an interpretive meaning, but we need to go beyond

  Page | 70 – week one to seven  the interpretive meaning and get to the actual meaning. We need to think here about 
why would the Buddha  teach this, or rather to whom would the Buddha teach this? For 
us, we need to go to the definitive meaning.  
 If we just accept what the Buddha says in interpretive statements such as all 
phenomena exist by way of their own defining characteristic, then yo u are not 
penetrating to the actual reality. This is like going for the bark of the sugar cane. You 
have some sweetness there but you don‟t actually get to the essence, which is the 
sugar.  
  
“Delighting in talking” is again referring to delighting in faul t finding behind their 
backs. Saying, “he has that” or “he does that.” Like talking about a monk when he is 
not here, saying, “he does this” etc. We should abandon this kind of talk. We should 
reflect on the meaning of what has been expressed in these vers es using this factor of 
conscientiousness.  
Here we have completed this long description of the fourth point, which was 
Generating the discrimination of persistent practice as the cure.  We cou ld also 
call this earnest practice or intensive practice. What wa s described here was how we 
should see the good qualities, and not focus on or talk about the faults using 
conscientiousness in this case and so on. All this has to do with this earnest practice.  
 
2B3A -3A2E  Contemplating the discrimination of the Tathagat a as an exalted being  
The fifth one consists in developing respect, having brought to mind the teacher of the Dharma, the 
Bhagavan.  
 
Here you would reflect on how the Bhagavan has acted only to benefit sentient 
beings, only to bring happiness to sentient b eings, lead them to happiness, and to 
counteract all kinds of suffering and negativities of sentient beings. In order to do this 
he has taught many dharmas. Then think, “I don‟t have the virtue to be in the presence 
of the Buddha, but I do have the virtue to be in the presence of the spiritual friend 
from whom I receive the teaching, the teachings which come from the Buddha 
Shakyamuni.” By reflecting in this way you come to establish for yourself a 
discrimination of the Buddha as being holy being or an exal ted being.  
 
2B3A -3A2F  Generating the wish for the Dharma tradition to last long  
The sixth one consists in the thought: how nice it w ould be, if based on listening to such a Dharma, the 
J ina’s teachings remained in the w orld for long.  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
71 Firstly, you reflec t on the amazing qualities of the dharma you are listening to, 
and how it clears away all suffering, and brings all happiness. You are able to be in the 
presence of such a Dharma through listening to it. Therefore, you first generate the 
thought of how for tunate you are, and then you generate a wish, “by me listening to 
this Dharma, may this dharma of the Buddha not go out of existence, but pervade all 
the six realms, but not only that, may it spread not for a short time, but for a very long 
time.” You thin k. “It would be unhelpful and a pity if it would not abide a long time 
for all sentient beings.” Then you generate the wish, “May it abide long with all 
sentient beings.”  
 
 This was the presentation of the six discriminations. We should abide by these 
with great awareness. When listening to the Dharma we should generate the 
discrimination that we are a  sick person. We should generate the discrimination of the 
Dharma as medicine and the discrimination of the one teaching the Dharma as being 
like a doctor. Then there was a presentation of how to generate the discrimination of 
persistent or honest practice being the cure - how you are dealing with the sickness. 
The fifth is the discrimination of the Tathagata being a holy being and lastly the 
discrimination o f the Dharma to remain long. We should generate these six 
discriminations.  
 
QUESTION :  Where are these six discriminations coming from, as some have scriptural 
quotes and some don‟t.  
GEN-LA:  Once place is a verse in The Four Hundred Verses by Aryadeva. A ctually, this 
comes from the sutras themselves. In addition, there‟s a quote in the Lam -Rim 
Chenmo from the Earth Essence Sutra that is given as a source for this presentation.  
 The point made here is that we need to be separated from the three faults of t he 
unsuitable vessels, and to generate the six discriminations. In other words, we need to 
listen while being free of the harmful factors connected with the three unsuitable 
vessels and we should listen together with the helpful factors of the six 
discrimi nations. This is what this comes down to.  
 Regardless of if you are listening or explaining the Dharma, you should make 
sure you don‟t remain separate from this Dharma in that you are over here, the dharma 
is over there, and in between there is a big dista nce. Whatever is explained in the 
Dharma, you should think it is explained to you, for you. Whatever advice I give here, 
is something one should take as personal advice. It‟s not that I‟m talking to an empty 
space in the middle of these two rows of student s, but it is given to everybody - you 
should have the attitude of this being individual personal advice.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 72 – week one to seven   In The Four Reliances, the first is that one does not rely on the person, but one 
relies on the words. “Person” in this context refers to the person‟ s name or renown. 
The name is not the point here; it is what is said that‟s important. The goal is not that 
person, the goal is Dharma, and to get this we need to rely on the words.  
 For example, if you need a great name, here is one - the protector Nagarjuna. 
Apart from his advice what benefit is there? If he was flying in front of us now we 
would think “Oh, this is Nagarjuna flying to and fro in front of me.” But we‟d get no 
benefit from this, the teaching is what will benefit us.  
 If we think about the Buddha for example, the person himself, there is no 
benefit from just that, especially as we do not directly encounter the Buddha. Even if 
we did, he would address these words to us, “I am not able to take away the suffering 
of sentient beings with my hands, or wash the negativities of sentient beings away with 
water.” The sole thing that the Buddhas are doing is teaching, and these teachings need 
to be taken on and practiced. This als o means that we have all the activities of the 
Buddha's body, speech and mind, but what we really need is his speech. His body, we 
can gaze at; his mind, we can‟t even perceive that. What we need is his speech; his 
advice. This is the important one for us.   
 Therefore, what we need is the Dharma teachings, but if we are just relating to 
them with us here and the Dharma “over there”, then this is a perverted way of 
relating to the Dharma and there could be no change within us. We need to relate to 
what is be ing said as being told directly to us, and we need to examine ourselves with 
that. We need to check if our mind is similar to how the Dharma is prescribing or not. 
If not, what kind of practices do we need to engage in and so on. This is the way to 
relate to the Dharma.  
 When we get up in the morning we want to wash, and so we use a mirror. We 
look in the mirror and we check the state of our face, what faults we need to work on 
etc. We then wash and maybe do all kinds of things with make -up, women would do  a 
lot in this area, seeing a lot of faults and so on. Not only that, they then try to add 
some good qualities with jewellery, ornaments and so on. This is what we do in the 
morning, we look in the mirror, see the faults, then correct them by washing or ad ding 
make -up or ointments, then we add some extra features with jewellery etc. So similar 
to that, seeing the Dharma text as like a mirror, we look into this mirror of the 
Dharma and it is telling us our faults. In this way, we can understand what is going  on 
in our continuum and see what is a fault. We can also understand how to relate to this 
fault, how to repair it, what to do with this ugly aspect, how to clean it and so on. We 
can also understand how to generate all kind of good qualities, just as we a dd jewellery 
and ornaments to the body.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
73 Therefore, this is what‟s going on; we‟re looking into the mirror of the Dharma, 
seeing what kind of ugly aspects we have, and we then do something about them. Then 
we bring forth many good qualities in the mind lik e an ornament.  
 
2B3A -3B  The way in which one needs to listen, having related this to one’s mind  
Apart from that, if you keep your ow n mind aside w hile explaining or listening to the Dharma, the 
Dharma remaining separate from it, then this w ill result in w hatever is being explained to miss the point. 
Therefore, it is necessary to listen in order to re ach certainty concerning one’s ow n continuum. To 
illustrate this: { 21}  if you want to examine w hether or not there is dirt or some other impurity on your 
face, you look at yourself in a mirror and, understanding that there is, remove it. Likew ise, w hen you  
hear the Dharma, your faulty behaviour appears in the mirror of the Dharma, w hereupon you feel 
anguish thinking: ‘that is w hat my mind has come to’. Then, as you engage in eliminating faults and 
attaining qualities, you necessarily train in accordance w it h the Dharma. It is like in the J ataka stories  
[w here] the son of Sutasa asks Prince Chandra for Dharma teachings in these terms:  
 
Beholding the forms of my bad behaviour,  
in the dazzling mirror of Dharma,  
anguish arises forceful in me,  
and I turn tow ards the Dharma.  
 
Know ing this to be the thinking of a [suitable] vessel for hearing the Dharma, the Bodhisattva gave him 
teachings.  
 
 
FRIDAY  19 FEBRUARY  2010  - SESSION  3 
By looking into this mirror of the Dharma, we come to know what is going on in 
our contin uum, and we come to see all the dangerous and difficult things in our mind. 
When this is explained to us and we see and accept them, and then engage in all kinds 
of activities to counteract them.  
This is shown in a quote from the Jataka Tales. Just like w e would look in the 
mirror and immediately act on the faults we see, we look into the mirror of the 
Dharma and say, “I can see all the faults of my body, speech and mind,” and by seeing 
this we then turn towards the Dharma.  
In this Jataka story,6 Sudasa as ks Prince Chandra (“Prince” here in Tibetan is a 
word similar to „son of the conqueror‟, meaning Prince Chandra is a bodhisattva) for a 
Dharma teaching. The bodhisattva Prince Chandra first checks if this Sudasa is a 
suitable vessel to teach the Dharma to,  and having analysed and seeing he was, taught 
him the Dharma. In our case, how we become a suitable vessel depends on us, and we 
do this through abandoning the three faults of the vessel and cultivating the six 
                                            
6 Jataka Tales, no. 31  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 74 – week one to seven  discriminations. Whether or not we become su itable vessels for the Dharma depends 
entirely on us.  
When we give the Dharma to someone else, we can‟t just give it out to everyone 
because it is precious. We have to check if they are a suitable receptacle, and this 
especially needs to be checked in the case of Secret Mantra. For example, you wouldn‟t 
give an initiation to just anyone, but you would check if they are keeping samayas, if 
they are trained in the path etc., and if this is so, then one can give an initiation into 
Secret Mantra. When we refer to “Secret” in this context, it does not mean it is to be 
kept secret from everybody, but it is to be kept secret from someone who is not 
prepared for this kind of teaching.  
The bodhisattva Chandra gave the teachings to Sudasa after first checking if he 
was a suitable vessel. The same thing is true with the Buddha himself. The Buddha 
turned the wheel of Dharma three times. In the second turning he taught the 
Prajnaparamita  sutras, where he said that things don‟t exist from their own side. This is 
the actual  teaching. However, just because this is the most precious teaching doesn‟t 
mean the Buddha taught it to everyone. In the first turning of the wheel for example, 
he said that all phenomenon exist by way of their own defining characteristics. In the 
third t urning, he said that some do exist in this way and some don‟t. So depending on 
the respective sentient beings to be subdued, knowing their different capacities, he has 
given the different teachings. For those who feel there is existence by way of their own  
defining characteristics, this is what he taught and so on. He didn‟t give out the highest 
Dharma, and that what is the most difficult to understand, to everyone just because it 
is the highest Dharma.  
 
2B3A -3C  How to keep in mind the purpose of attainments  
In short, thinking: ‘I shall attain Buddhahood for the sake of all sentient beings, to attain that, I need to 
train in its causes. Apparently, it is necessary to listen to the Dharma for that, therefore I shall listen to 
the Dharma’, you should  generate the mind of enlightenment, consider the benefits of study and listen 
w ith joy, abandoning the faults of a vessel.  
 
The third point is how to keep in mind the purpose of attainments. This is a 
summary of what was presented above. Someone has to th ink, “I shall attain 
Buddhahood for the sake of all sentient beings.”  He is aiming at the goal which is 
Buddhahood for all sentient beings.  
To attain Buddhahood I need to train in the causes for it. To attain these causes I 
need to engage into listening and studying because I can then understand what the 
causes are, and then train in the causes. By thinking in this way, one generates the 
bodhimind.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
75 Someone aims at the attainment of the all -omniscient state of a Buddha for the 
sake of all sentient being, and in order to gain this result, he needs to train in the 
causes. In order to do this, you need to know what the causes are, and in order to 
know the causes you need to listening and study the dharma. Then you can train and 
practice, and having practiced you can attain the goal for the benefit for all sentient 
beings. From this, one can generate a mind seeing the benefits of studying, and this 
also becomes a form of generating the bodhimind.  
One should then generate a mind that considers the benefits of s tudying and 
listening. This means one goes back to all the quotes from these verses; the Jataka tales 
and so on. When one reflects on this, then one generates a mind of the benefits of 
listening. One then understands that through listening to the dharma on e comes to 
know the Dharma, and how to practice. Moreover, by knowing how to practice one 
can then destroy all the afflictions. This is because all the afflictions come from not 
knowing the reality, not knowing the dharma.  
 
From this text called the Verse s (Verses on Listening7)  
Through listening you come to know  the dharmas,  
through listening misdeeds are turned aw ay,  
through listening all trivia are abandoned,  
through listening nirvana is achieved  
 
  Through listening, you come to understand the dharma. Through understanding, 
you can come to see what wrong actions you did in the past, that these can be purified, 
and that you can refrain from engaging in these negative acts in the future. All these 
nega tive actions can be identified through listening, and then you can turn away from 
pointless activities. If you are turning away from these, then this is a path where you 
can attain the state of nirvana.  
If you are considering the benefits of study, then s eeing these benefits, there will 
be a certain joy generated in listening to the dharma. This comes about because 
through knowing the benefits of the Dharma, a certain taste for the Dharma comes 
about. Having this taste, you‟ll want to experience the Dharma , then you‟ll be able to 
easily abandon the three faults of the vessel and easily adopt the conducive conditions 
of the six discriminations.

  Page | 76 – week one to seven  2B3B  HOW TO EXPLAIN  
1 Considering the benefits of explaining the Dharma  
2 Generating respect for the teacher and the teachings  
3 What kind of attitude and activities to explain with  
4 Distinction between who the explanations should and should not be given to  
The second one has four points: Considering the benefits of explaining the Dharma,. generating respect 
for the  teacher and the teachings, w hat kind of attitude and activities to explain w ith, distinction 
betw een w ho the explanations should and should not be given to.  
 
This firstly refers to what kind of benefits there are to explaining the Dharma, 
and secondly in order to generate respect for the teacher and for the Dharma - which 
means to the specific teacher on that occasion and the specific portion of text that is 
being taught. Thirdly, it refers to what kind of attitudes and activities one should 
engage into wh en one is teaching the Dharma. Fourth, to whom one should teach the 
Dharma and to whom one should not.  
2B3B -1 Considering the benefits of explaining the Dharma  
 
First: In the Treasuryxxiv it says:  
Giving Dharma, free of mental afflictions,  
teach according to  sutra and other true texts.  
 
In its auto -commentary it says: “Therefore, those w ho explain the Dharma incorrectly and w ith an 
afflicted mind craving for material gain, respect and fame, { 22}  corrupt their ow n great merits. 
Therefore, a pure motivation to teach the Dharma is extremely important and just like Ngargompa  said, 
it is crucial to review it beforehand: “I have never explained the Dharma w ithout meditating upon 
impermanence before the session.”  
 
Listed here are all the minds to avoid when teaching  the Dharma. Firstly, 
explaining a wrong Dharma, or an afflicted mind – looking for gain or respect of some 
type. If any of these minds are present, then the one who is teaching degenerates their 
accumulation of merit as a result of having these minds.  
 If you have an attitude of receiving fame and respect whilst teaching dharma, or 
receiving some gain or material goods, your teaching of the Dharma will be of no 
benefit. However, if you teach the dharma without that attitude, then there is amazing 
benefit.   
 Lama Ngargompa says here that before he explains the Dharma, he always 
reflects on impermanence. He is making sure he does not have a wrong motivation. In 
other words, he is establishing a good motivation.  
 
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
77  In Exhortation to the Supreme Intention  tw o groups of 20 advantages of giving the gift of 
Dharma in the absence of material things and the concern for gain, respect and the like are set forth. 
Also, in Drag Shul Chanxxv it says that the merit of a householder giving immeasurable material things is 
exceeded by an ordained person giving a single verse of Dharma.  
 
 If you teach the dharma without  an interest in material things, wanting respect 
and so on, then this kind of giving the gift of Dharma is addressed in the sutra called 
Exhortation to the Suprem e Intention . Here it talks about two groups of twenty advantages 
of teaching the Dharma in this way, so this means forty advantages.  
 These forty are listed in the Lam-Rim Chenmo8. For example, your recollection 
consciousness (memory) gets stronger, all kinds of wisdom are generated, and in 
general your mind as a whole is getting more stable.  
Then there is a quote from the Sutra Requested by the Householder Drag Shul Chan . I 
think this is a little problematic as we have many lay people here. What this mea ns for 
example is that if a business man is selling all kinds of things and makes a great profit, 
and from this profit makes offerings on a vast scale, compared to this, an ordained 
person giving just a small teaching creates far more merit.  
  
2B3B -2 Gene rating respect for the teacher and the teachings  
Second: When uttering the Mother of the Buddhasxxvi the Teacherxxvii himself arranged the seat and so on. 
Accordingly, the Dharma is a field of veneration even for Buddhas, so one should bring to mind the 
positive qualities and kindness of the Dharma and its teacher, generating respect.  
 
This second headline firstly relates to how the Buddha was teaching the Perfection 
of Wisdom Sutras  (Prajnaparamitasutra) . He prepared a throne on which he was teaching 
as even a fu lly enlightenment one shows respect to the Dharma. When someone is 
explaining the precious Dharma, one should sit on a high seat. This is a way to show 
respect to the dharma, just like the Buddha when he taught the Prajnaparamitasutra .  
 You might also hav e a place where you put the dharma books, maybe with other 
statues and thangkas etc., then when you prostrate, you prostrate to the dharma texts 
and in this way show respect to the Dharma. Then you are showing respect to that 
which is the most precious, wh ich is the dharma.  
 As Buddha showed this respect towards the dharma, and made these 
preparations when teaching the Prajnaparamita sutra, if a fully enlightened being shows 
his respect to the dharma with this type of veneration, then with regard to us, we 
should definitely show respect and veneration to the Dharma.  
                                            
8 lrcm Vol.I, bottom of page 63.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 78 – week one to seven  The dharma is not precious because it has been taught by the Buddha, this is not 
the correct reason why it is precious. We should regard it as precious by reflecting that 
the dharma is the supreme method to destroy all the limitless sufferings of sentient 
beings, and as a result, it is extremely precious. In this way, we generate respect for the 
dharma, and then by that, the one who taught the precious dharma is also a precious 
object.  
 
2B3B -3 What kind of attitude and activities to explain with  
A Attitude  
B Activities   
Attitude  here means the way of thinking and the motivation for the activities 
you engage in.  
 
The third one has tw o points: attitude and activities.  
2B3B -3A Attitude  
First, [the Sutra] Requested by Sagaramati  puts forw ard five discriminations: generating the 
discrimination of oneself as a doctor, of the Dharma as medicine, of the one listening to the Dharma as 
a patient, of the Tathagata as a holy being and of the mode of the Dharma as abiding for a long time as 
w ell as the development of loving kindness tow ards the people around.  
 
Here the Sutra Requested by Sagaramati talks about five discriminations that need 
to be generated when explaining the dharma. In addition to these, there are other 
things that should  be absent like anger or jealousy. Any laziness of putting things off 
should be missing, discouragement or fatigue, praising yourself and talking about other 
people‟s faults, being interested in material things like clothes and food, should all be 
absent w hen explaining the Dharma. Instead, we should have the attitude that sees that 
merits coming from teaching are the cause for my own happiness and is connected 
with my own and the enlightenment of others. These merits that come from my 
teaching the Dharma w ill become the causes of happiness.  
 
 The envy [born from] anxiety that others are superior, the laziness of putting things off, the 
discouragement of fatigue due to explaining things over and over again, praising oneself and chit -chatting 
about other peop le’s faults, the reluctance to lend texts as w ell as concern for material things such as food 
and clothing should be abandoned, thinking: those merits from teaching for the sake of my ow n and 
others’ enlightenment are the provisions of my happiness.  
 
We sh ould not have any type of laziness or tiredness when we are teaching to 
others. If someone is explaining the Dharma, and finds it very hard or is exhausted or 
is falling asleep, the effect is people will find it difficult to listen and they themselves 
will get tired of the presentation. However, if a teacher is very old, and they are 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
79 exhausted after putting in a lot of effort because of their age, then this is a very 
different situation.  
  
We should generate these five discriminations [attitudes of explain ing the Dharma]:  
 Oneself as the doctor  
 The Dharma as medicine  
 The ones who are listening as patients  
 The Tathagata as a holy being – reflecting he re on the good qualities of the 
body, speech and mind of the Buddha that are  difficult for us to pervade with 
our mind.  
 Wish for the mode of the Dharma to abide for a long time  
 Also mentioned here is developing an attitude of loving kindness towards the 
people listening to the dharma . This is very important - that the one who is 
teaching has a loving concern towards his audience.  
 
Then we need the absence of:  
 Envy  
 Laziness  in terms of putting things off – it‟s quite obvious in that if we have 
this, the teachings don‟t come abou t 
 Discouragement or fatigue  
 Anger  
 Praising oneself  
 Looking for faults  
We should be free of all these faults. Instead we should think, “I‟m now 
teaching some Dharma in orde r for myself and others to gain  enlightenment . This is 
also an outstanding opp ortunity  to accumulate great merit. I have received this 
opportunity, and  I‟m very  fortunate.” By thinking like this , a great happiness is 
generated, as well as enthusiasm for engaging into this Dharma activity and 
accumulation of merit. This finishes the attitudes.

MONDAY 1 MARCH 2010  - SESSION 1 
QUESTIONS  
QUESTION:  On page 8 of the root text, relating to all the teachings as being non -
contradictory, it mentions they are to be understood as one person‟s path to 
buddhahood. Does this mean all beings that follow the buddhas te achings, heading 
towards enlightenment will have to take all the teachings of the Buddha as their path 
to enlightenment?  
GEN:  The meaning here is the path to buddhahood is only one path. All the different 
teachings and different commentaries etc, all show one path. Within that, they show all 
the topics to be understood in sequence. For example, you can study one volume like 
the Lam -Rim, or many volumes that are more detailed - but all present the different 
points that are to be realised. In works that are m ore extensive, there are more outlines 
and more subtle points, but in the end it comes down to a more detailed explanation 
of the same path.  
 So one persons path to buddhahood means that a path which is for one 
person, is also for every other person.  
 For example, in the Bodhisattvacharyavatara, the master Shantideva 
describes the limitless benefits of the mind of enlightenment, and then how one 
generates this mind. The mind training texts (Lojong) also say what we need to start 
with is the bodhimind. We need to remove the discordant class of the bodhimind; self -
cherishing, and there are different ways to remove these two minds of self -cherishing 
and self -grasping. In the Lam -Rim, through the small and middle scopes you can come 
to the practitioner of the great scope, which means being able to generate the 
bodhimind. All these texts have the same topic of how to generate the bodhimind.  
 Other texts are mainly talking about the view that realises emptiness, and 
here it also makes clear that you need to rely  on the bodhimind that is supported by 
the mind that realises emptiness. Then we have the complete path to reach the goal of 
enlightenment , like a bird that has two wings. All the teachings come down to this 
same motivation; the wisdom which is realising emptiness and the bodhimind. It‟s the 
same in tantric teachings but they use different words like „counteracting ordinary 
appearance.‟  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
81  It all comes down to these two practices, and they are all for one being to 
practice. The self -cherishing and self -grasping minds are faults present in us, and all 
sentient beings, and because of this, the Buddha taught the Dharma.  
 The main hindrance to attain liberation is the ignorance grasping for a 
self, and for omniscience, the main obstacle is self -cherishing and the knowledge 
obscurations that are the imprints left by self -grasping. In order to gain high 
attainments, we need t o generate the bodhimind and wisdom realizing emptiness in 
order to destroy these two enemies.  
 In this text, it states that if you listen to or explain the Dharma, you 
should avoid the Dharma being separate from you. Instead, it should help you to 
recogn ise your faults, as you would use a mirror to recognize your faults and then 
engage into actions to correct them. Similarly, we use the Dharma to understand the 
different faults in our continuum, and then engage into activities to remove them.  
 For the sa ke of all sentient beings to gain the state of enlightenment, I 
need to attain enlightenment myself. “I will bring this about.” This attitude is the 
bodhimind. This is the motivation, but what you actually need is enlightenment. To 
attain this, you need to  rely on the path that leads to enlightenment as taught by the 
Buddha. To follow this path you first need to study what that path actually is, then 
understand it, and then integrate it into your own continuum. To walk this path you 
need to understand the h indrances that are to be abandoned; the afflictive 
obscurations and knowledge obscurations. This mirror of the Dharma needs to 
function for you, so you can understand the faults in your continuum, and in order to 
fulfil your motivation of attaining full en lightenment for the sake of all sentient beings, 
you need to study the Dharma.  
 Those who are of the Mahayana type, whether humans or gods, all have 
the same motivation for their practice, which is to attain enlightenment for sake of all 
sentient beings. The actual practice of the Mahayana is to achieve the causes that will 
bring the effect of enlightenment, and whoever has this motivation has to train in the 
accumulation of the causes for enlightenment. The training is the same, regardless of 
what positio n or rank you have in society, whether it be a prince or a beggar, in that 
the causes for enlightenment have to be created by eliminating the two obscurations. 
These factors are present in all sentient beings, humans and gods. Therefore, whoever 
wants to g o for the Mahayana path needs to remove these by training in the causes for 
enlightenment. How? By understanding the causes. How? By listening to what the 
causes are. Then through study, you can bring forth the causes.  
You would need to know for example t hat the objects of this path are  the two truths. 
The path is the two factors of method (six perfections) and wisdom (wisdom realising 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 82 – week one to seven  emptiness), and the result of this is also two fold – the dharmakaya (truth body) and 
the rupakaya (form body). You can o nly know this by study, and as a result you will 
then understand and come to cultivate them. This is to be done by a prince, a beggar 
or whoever wants to attain enlightenment. Therefore, it is to be understood as one 
person‟s path.  
 In brief, what you are  aiming for is the dharmakaya and the rupakaya, and 
to attain these you need to train in the path that is method and wisdom. To do this you 
need to understand the two truths, and to do this you need to study the two truths. 
Whoever wishes for enlightenment  has to walk this path. The Buddha teaches for 
exactly this reason; in order to attain these two bodies. Therefore, this is the one path 
going to enlightenment; all the practices are for one person who is aiming at 
buddhahood.  
 
 We have let go of so many circumstances in order to come to Nalanda, 
and practice the Dharma. What we need is the Dharma to come into our minds. This 
means is we have to make effort to connect to the Dharma rather than only studying 
and it remaining separate from us. We shouldn‟t t hink the Dharma is to be studied and 
not experienced. Whilst we are studying, we need to think that every single word is 
personally for us, taking every single word and checking it against your own 
continuum. “Is my continuum in harmony with this?” If so, great - then we can 
increase it. If not, then we need to find the time to generate these positive states of 
mind.  
For example if we engage into vast studies over a long time but still nothing happens 
in our mind, then all these studies were only of a smal l benefit. Rather, we should aim 
to fully integrate them into our continuum.  
 Listening to the dharma is for the sake of knowing the Dharma. Then we 
can use it like a mirror, to see what faults we have and correct them.  
 If we are not doing this, then the  teacher could even be Lama Tsongkhapa 
himself and nothing would happen. You have to do this yourself; you have the power. 
Lama Tsongkhapa would not have any form of blessing that he can give you to make 
you understand. Integrating relies fully upon you.  
Some people who have an amazing amount of positive merits and imprints might rely 
on a lama who gives specific advice, and from this, all their previous imprints ripen. 
Very few are like this. Nevertheless, even for those people, what ripens are imprints 
accumulated in previous lives from listening, reflecting and cultivating. For us 
however, we still have to accumulate these by studying, listening and integrating the 
teachings into our continuum. Even if we take many tantric initiations and even come 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
83 to th e level of directly seeing the deity, if we don‟t integrate that, nothing will happen. 
This deity cannot bestow the realisations on you like a blessing. You yourself have to 
generate the wisdom by studying the texts, and then come to the realizations yours elf, 
this will not happen through blessings.  
 For example, when we talk about the benefits of teaching the Dharma, we 
need an attitude of understanding that from all the different types of generosity, it is 
the giving of Dharma that is the supreme. We sho uld not engage into explaining with 
the wish to gain something material, or to gain respect. Rather we should have a mind 
understanding the benefits of teaching the Dharma.  
 To generate respect for the teachings there is a story of how the Buddha 
himself prepared the throne for teaching the Dharma. He himself prepared the place, 
showing his high regard for the Dharma. We should also meditate on the kindness of 
the teachings and the teacher (the Buddha) and generate great respect.  
 Reluctance to lend texts  means teaching the Dharma but not being 
humble. Teaching to gain outer material conditions should be abandoned.  
What is conducive? Think: “Myself and all others, we need to attain the state of 
buddhahood, as a result, for that purpose I will teach. This is an activity of limitless 
merits.” The benefits which come from explaining the Dharma are the real causes for 
one‟s own happiness, this is something to rejoice about.  
 
2B3B -3B  Activities  
{ 23}  Second: Having w ashed and dressed in immaculately clean clot hes, you sit dow n 
in a clean and pleasant place on a cushion on a Dharma throne. If you then chant a 
mantra subjugating demons, demons and gods of the demonic category w ill not get 
closer to you at all than a perimeter of 100 joyanas  and even if they do, they w ill not 
be able to create any obstacles, as it says in [the Sutra] Requested by Sagaramati. 
Therefore you should chant that mantra and - w ith an extremely radiant facial 
complexion - give your explanations conjoined w ith the conditions for ascertaini ng 
their meaning - examples, proofs and quotations.  
 
The emphasis here is on cleanliness in front of others. If a teacher was dirty and 
sloppy, then the audience would look down on them and have a bad attitude towards 
the Dharma. The place should also be c lean and attractive, setting up a throne with a 
cushion. Then the teacher would sit on this throne.  
 The text mentions the recitation of a mantra that subjugates demons. 
Here we can think of reciting the heart sutra, mantras, and other prayers that dispel  
obstacles like the ones we do here before the teachings.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 84 – week one to seven   The effect of this recitation is demons and gods of the demonic type 
cannot create obstacles. We should think that all the obstacles are dispelled by this 
activity, and that these harmful beings a re unable to come close or, even if they do, 
they are unable to act in a harmful way. The sutra requested by Sagaramati explains 
this and probably contains the mantra.  
 An extremely radiant facial complexion refers to not having a dark face 
whilst teachin g but having a radiant face. In addition, you should  be able to explain 
the meaning and the side topics by analogies, reasonings as well as quotations from 
scripture.  
 
 
MONDAY  1 MARCH  2010  - SESSION  2 
 
QUESTION:  Why do teachers snap their fingers before they sit on the throne?   
GEN:  When they snap their fingers, they recite this prayer,  
 
A star, a fault of vision, a lamp,  
An illusion, a drop of dew, or a bubble,  
A dream, a flash of lightning, a cloud –  
See conditioned things as such.  
 
By reciting this, they remind themselves how all products are impermanent and all 
phenomena are empty and selfless. This helps destroy pride.  
QUESTION:  How does it help destroy pride?  
GEN:  If you understand you are changing mom ent by moment and constantly flying 
like an aeroplane towards death, there is no room for pride. The verse is recited to 
bring impermanence to mind.  
 In Bodhgaya, His Holiness the Dalai Lama explained this very same thing 
in detail, but I only caught half  of it. Maybe it can be found in the recordings of this 
teaching. If your mind is distracted, then you do not understand. I was actually quite 
close to His Holiness, but due to being distracted, I didn‟t understand anything. By the 
time I realised what was  being explained, I‟d missed it!  
 The normal explanation that lamas give for this practice is along these 
lines. Snap the fingers, recite the verse and become mindful of impermanence and 
selflessness. The verse gives so many analogies, all of which are exa mples that are fake 
in their appearance. In this way, you can destroy the different afflictions like pride, and 
it becomes helpful to your mind before teaching.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
85  The last line of this paragraph, conditions for ascertaining their meaning  is 
more like “the ascertained meaning and its branches.” We should explain by these 
three, examples, proofs and quotations. Here it becomes clear why you need to study 
the dharma. You can‟t give examples or be confident of the main points and such if 
you haven‟t studied. Yo u also need to explain secondary points, just as you would need 
to refer to the leaves, not only the trunk of a tree in order to explain it. To do this you 
need to have studied the topic in detail. It is the same for analogies, reasoning and 
quotations – for example quoting from a master such as the protector Nagarjuna. This 
makes clear that before teaching you need to have studied in detail.  
 
2B3B -4 Distinction between who the explanations should and should not be given to  
Fourth: In the Sutra on Discipli ne it says: “do not act w ithout being asked to”. 
Accordingly, you do not teach w ithout having been asked and even w hen someone 
asks, you should examine the vessel. How ever, know ing someone to be a vessel, it is 
acceptable to teach even w ithout having been asked, as it says in the King of 
Concentration [Sutra]. Further modes of conduct are set forth in the Sutra on 
Discipline.  
 
This first line means we have to request teachings and from this the teacher accepts. 
The wrong way would be to go to someone and sa y, “I have come to teach you the 
Dharma.” Even if you are requested, you have to check the person requesting is a 
suitable vessel, and if not, you wouldn‟t teach them even if they did request.  
 If someone is a correct vessel, then it is suitable to teach them, even 
without a request being made. If we see there is some ripening in their minds, and by 
giving them advice their minds would come to full fruition, it is acceptable to give 
them teachings without a request.  
 
QUESTION:   What does further modes of c onduct mean?  
GEN:  For example, you wouldn‟t teach to someone who is standing up, someone who 
is eating and drinking, carrying tools or implements, wearing head gear, or talking 
pointlessly etc., – this all has to do with lacking respect for the teachings. When the 
Buddha taught he showed great respect to the Dharma by preparing the throne etc., 
likewise we should not teach to people who are doing all kinds of unsuitable activities.  
 For example, someone might put their elbows on the desk and their head 
in their hands – such a person is obviously not focused, and an unsuitable vessel. If 
someone gets up and moves around, eats and drinks etc., these are signs they are not 
showing respect to the Dharma so they are unsuitable to teach to. This is explained in 
detail in the Lam -Rim Chenmo . This is what is meant by further modes of conduct.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 86 – week one to seven  For example, it would be unsuitable if someone was sitting on a higher seat than the 
person teaching the Dharma. If His Holiness the Dalai Lama is listening to teachings, 
he sits on a lower seat than the person explaining the Dharma.  
  When the His Holiness the Karmapa was young, maybe 12 or 13, he was 
taught whilst sitting on a high throne, and the person who taught the Dharma would 
sit on a low throne. A lot of discussion resulted from this, but the fault was not with 
the Karmapa, as it is sure that he did not insist on this himself. This would have come 
from the attendants, they would have set up the higher throne for him.  
QUESTION:   What about the Buddha teaching in Vultures Peak, the bodhisattvas 
were in the air and would have been  higher?  
GEN:  They would have been floating in the air below the Buddha; none would have 
been above the Buddha. Vultures Peak is quite a high vast space so others are able to 
sit below the peak.  
QUESTION:   When teaching the Dharma, why is standing up cons idered 
disrespectful?  
GEN:   If the lama is sitting on the throne, and everyone else stands up this is very 
strange. Especially if a tall person stands up near the front! From this angle, we can say 
it‟s not practical or respectful. If there is pain in the knees then one can go to the back 
to stretch, this is different and perfectly acceptable.  
 For example, there is standing in the Sojong ceremony. For Getsüls, the 
actual rite is where three of them would come to the one Gelong. At this point, there 
is a l ot of showing respect, bowing and so on. This small ritual is the actual Sojong, 
and as a result, the Getsüls should carry it out very well and memorize it. If you do, 
then you can reflect on the meaning. For the Gelongs, the actual Sojong is listening to 
the sutra recited after the Getsüls have left. All the other sections of the Sojong 
ceremony are preliminaries to these two.  
QUESTION:   At one point Gen -la prostrates towards us. What does this mean?  
GEN:  At that moment, you people become extremely precio us. It‟s really like this. At 
that moment, you become the actual sangha. Sojong depends on the sangha being 
complete – if it‟s not there, you can‟t perform Sojong. At that moment, if there are 
enough monks present, then they are very precious and so I show  respect to those by 
whom Sojong can happen – even for me because I also want to do Sojong. At this 
point, the groups prostrates to the Lama, not the altar. I want to express my 
appreciation for the sangha being together. I can‟t prostrate to you all so I prostrate to 
the middle, but I mean to prostrate to the whole Sangha.  
 
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
87 2B3C  Generally, how to proceed at the end  
 
1 What to do at the end - the actual things to do:  
The roots of virtue of teaching and listening in this w ay should be sealed  by means of 
pure aspirational prayers such as the Prayer of Good Conduct.  
 
2 The benefits of this  
There is no doubt that, if the Dharma is explained and listened to in this manner, in 
just one session the advantages stated above w ill arise. Due to the act of listening  and 
explaining the Dharma hitting home, all the karmic obscurations accumulated 
previously by not respecting the Dharma or those expounding it etc. are purified and 
all the ones being new ly accumulated are nipped in the bud.  
 
3 The general mode of behavio ur of saints  
Due to the manner of listening hitting home, the instructions set forth bring about 
benefit in the mind. Seeing this, all the earlier saints in general { 24}  w ere diligent 
about this and especially the former gurus of this instruction persevere d at it w ith 
utmost diligence.  
 
4 The advice to cherish them, as it is a great fault to turn away from them  
These are evidently great instructions - if one does not reach certainty about this and 
one’s attitude does not change - as is often the case - the Dharma, so profound and 
vast, no matter how much it is explained, w ill be like a god fallen to [the rank of] a 
demon and that very Dharma w ill become an aid to the afflictions. Therefore, “if you 
err on the first of the month, [you w ill do so] until the fi fteenth “ as they say and since 
it is like that, the wise strive at this method of transforming w hat has been heard and 
explained into the path and make every opportunity to explain and listen into 
something w orthw hile. For this is the best preparation for  teaching the instructions.  
 
After having taught the Dharma, the lama then dedicates using prayers such as the 
King of Prayers, which is what the Prayer of Good Conduct refers to. As this is quite 
long, we can also use two verses from the prayer, which we recite here after the 
teachings.  
JAM PÄL PA WÖ JI TAR KHYEN PA DANG  
Just as the brave Manjushri and Samantabhadra too,  
KÜN TU ZANG PO DE YANG DE ZHIN TE  
Realized things as they are,  
DE DAG KÜN GYI JE SU DAG LOB CHHIR  
Also I dedicate all these merits in the best way,  
GE WA DI DAG THAM CHÄ RAB TU NGO  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 88 – week one to seven  That I may follow their perfect example.  
 
DÜ SUM SHEG PÄI GYÄL WA THAM CHÄ KYI  
I dedicate all these roots of virtue  
NGO WA GANG LA CHHOG TU NGAG PA DE  
With the dedicat ion praised as the best  
DAG GI GE WÄI TSA WA DI KÜN KYANG  
By the victorious ones thus gone of the three times,  
ZANG PO CHÖ CHHIR RAB TU NGO WAR GYI  
So that I might perform the noble bodhisattvas‟ deeds.  
 
The whole King of Prayers  is included in these t wo verses.  
 If we dedicate all the virtue of explaining with the correct attitude and 
actions, then all the negativities accumulated in the past of showing disrespect to the 
teachings and the teacher, which are a lot – all become purified. Also having now 
listened with a correct attitude, this acts as a hindrance to the same type of negativities 
being performed in the future.  
 If you listen to or explain the dharma in the correct way, then the mind 
changes and there is great benefit for you.  
 The holy bei ngs of the past have with great energy engaged into listening 
and explaining in this way. When they listened and explained, they made great effort to 
have the correct motivation and good conduct. The linage lamas of the Lam -Rim also 
intensely acted in this  way, to have the right motivation and activities. We should then 
also be careful to adopt these practices.  
 If we can listen or explain the Dharma with these correct attitudes and 
activities, there is great benefit. If we don‟t have these, then great neg ativities come 
about and listening to or explaining the Dharma becomes a friend of the afflictions, in 
that we might generate disrespect to the teachings or the teachers. If the one 
explaining wants clothes, money, fame and so on, then his explaining the D harma 
becomes a friend of the afflictions.  
QUESTION:   Is there a difference in the outlook westerners should have in 
regard to the teachers, like not seeing them as our lama for example? Like Venerable. 
Fedor for example, or a lay person in a centre who i s leading discussion groups - is it 
harmful for us not to see them as a Buddha?  
GEN:  Here we are talking about relying on your lama. For someone who is not your 
lama, we can think of this person as our Dharma friend, and as a result, we then we 
wouldn‟t ma ke prostrations to them. We wouldn‟t have to generate the six 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
89 discriminations in this case. This is more like being with your classmates, and just 
listening to some of them explaining things.  
 
 
MONDAY  1 MARCH  2010  - SESSION  3 
QUESTION:  The text says that if we listen in the proper way, it will purify our 
misdeed of not respecting the Dharma. What about the factor of regret? Isn‟t this 
needed to purify?  
GEN:  If we think of the wisdom realising emptiness, this mind purifies oceans of 
nega tivities, but is there a mind of regret here? No. The bodhimind also purifies, but 
does it include regret? Again, we would have to say no. For example, if in this life we 
generate disrespect for the Dharma or the teacher, we would recall that and purify wi th 
the four opponent powers. In general, we need these four powers but there are 
positive minds that purify without the mind of regret.  
 In general, we can generate regret towards the actions of this life that we 
can recall. However, we are unable to recal l many more from previous lives, and for 
those, we can‟t generate regret. We could do it in this way; by analysing that we are still 
lost in samsara due to having done things in the past, and from this, we can conclude 
that there are negativities left in o ur minds, which we would then purify. We can‟t 
really generate the mind of regret here as we can‟t recall what we did.  
 
QUESTION:  Could Gen -la explain what he meant earlier by a class situation where 
people are just listening to an explanation.  
GEN:  I did not say that it‟s a situation that is a classroom, but it is similar to this. I 
also did not say that it is unsuitable to see Venerable Fedor as your teacher, if you can, 
then this is fine. If not, then you should see him as a Dharma friend.  
 A class is  not like a classroom situation, we‟re talking here about being in 
the same year or form with whom you progress through the same classes. We don‟t 
have classrooms in India, or Tibet, but what we mean here is debate. In a form, some 
might be already quite g ood in the text, they would explain it to others and some might 
listen. Some students in the same form or year would decide to see them as a teacher, 
and some would opt to see them as a Dharma friend.  
 The form consists of people who have entered into the  education system 
at the same time, but they are not necessarily the same age. For example, some forms 
have 300 students, and the minimum these days is 100. They stay together for 15 -20 
years, but due to their differences in intelligence or effort, from am ongst the same 
form there are great differences. Some become outstanding scholars, and some come 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 90 – week one to seven  to know nothing – but all from the same form. At the end of these years, however 
many are left, they all become Geshes.  
 They don‟t become Geshes by having tak en a specific exam, but because 
they survived the 20 years! The title Geshe itself doesn‟t show much, it doesn‟t show 
that someone is particularly scholarly as there are different titles corresponding to 
different levels within “Geshe”.  
 Every year the for m has to sit a compulsory exam. Some students don‟t 
study at all, and when they get to the written part they have nothing to write. In the 
debate exam, they have nothing to say but they still go in order to fulfil the 
requirement. People who don‟t know any thing still go to the exam and pass. If you 
don‟t study, you just sit the exam and progress to the next year.  
 The ones who study well don‟t take holidays as they have so much to 
study and practice. The ones who don‟t study have a lot of time free!  
 We c ould say there is a small Geshe, but the actual exams are in the sixth 
year of the Lharam class. These small Geshes could also know the texts well. In 
general, the education continues into the six years leading up to the Lharam exam, and 
by passing all thi s, you become a Lharampa Geshe. However, even in this exam there 
are different levels. You could pass with 80% - this would be someone who can 
explain the Dharma well but not in the most excellent way. Someone who passes with 
95% or 100% - they can explain  the Dharma in a full way. Of course, some students 
only pass with 10% or 20% and can only explain the Dharma in a small way.  
 There are also some who don‟t become Lharampa Geshes, even though 
they did excellent studies, because they couldn‟t finish the c lasses. They studied until 
the 18th year, but in the last two years (to Lharampa Geshe level) they couldn‟t finish. 
Maybe they get sick or travel somewhere for example. These would not be Lharampa 
Geshes, but still have a very good understanding.  
 For exa mple, someone might study for the first five years and then engage 
into all kinds of work and no longer study, but still he goes to the three -day annual 
exam. The first day being the written exam, the second day the debate exam, and the 
third day the recit ation exam. In this way, he still passes. He still has to get up and 
debate in front of his form and still writes his exam, but he usually becomes quite 
embarrassed at not knowing much.  
 If this person is timid and humble, he‟ll be very embarrassed, but i f he‟s 
someone who is very bold and funny, he‟ll make jokes in front of his friends as he 
walks through the assembly to the exam. Westerners won‟t do the full course as they 
can‟t stay for the whole time, they want a holiday or to see parents and then miss  the 
exam date and fall to the year below.   
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
91  Also if one doesn‟t go to debate, they you have to pay a fine. The Tibetan 
monks don‟t have money, so they go. Western monks have money, so they don‟t go!  
 
Holy beings of the past made great effort to listen a nd explain in the right way, and we 
should emulate this.  
 This includes when you study Dharma, you shouldn‟t procrastinate with 
the current topic but engage with it. Just as they do in three great seats, they encourage 
each other to not postpone the topic . For example düra  which is taught in the 
beginning, they encourage one another not to postpone it as they won‟t be able to 
study it later - as later there will be another topic.  
 Bringing this into the path is based on the excellent motivation and 
condu ct of listening to and explaining the Dharma. We should to try integrate this. If 
you are listening to or explaining the Dharma, you need to establish a good motivation 
and activities. This is an essential aspect of all Dharma practices, and this is said t o be 
the supreme advice.
