EXPLANATION OF THE STAGES BY WHICH A STUDENT OF THE ACTUAL 
INSTRUCTIONS SHOULD BE GUIDED  
 
2B4 The stages by which a student should be guided through the actual instructions  
A The root of the path: how to rely on a spiritual teacher  
B The stages of how to train the mind once you have relied  
The fourth one has tw o points: the root of the path - how to rely on a spiritual teacher 
- and the stages of how  to train the mind once y ou have relied.  
 
2B4A The root of the path: how to rely on a spiritual teacher  
1 The somewhat elaborate explanation for generating certainty  
2 The condensed presentation of how to sustain it  
 
The first one has tw o points: the somew hat elaborate explanation for generating 
certainty and the condensed presentation of how  to sustain it.  
 
The way one relies on a spiritual teacher (sometimes translated as guru devotion) is 
dealt at the beginning of the text as it is most important. For someone of the 
Mahayana lineage the guru is ve ry important. What does it mean to be of the 
“Mahayana type? Someone who wishes to follow the Mahayana path and to understand 
it, hear about it, practice it and attain the result. Such a person needs to rely on a 
Mahayana guru in the correct way.  
 The goo d qualities of all the buddhas and bodhisattvas come from having relied 
on a spiritual friend, and the individual faults that have been purified from their minds 
also come from having relied on the spiritual friend.  
  
2B4A -1 The somewhat elaborate explana tion for generating certainty  
A The need for relying on a spiritual master since all good qualities depend on him  
B The actual way to rely  
 
2B4A -1A  The need for relying on a spiritual master since all good qualities depend on him  
First: As all goodness, starting off from the development of a single positive quality 
and the decrease of a single fault in the mind of the disciple has its root in the sublime 
friend, the w ay to rely on him is important in the beginning.  
 
 
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
93 This section already expresses how im portant it is to rely on the spiritual friend.  
 If we look at the different studies we would do in the world, like to become a 
doctor, we would necessarily need to rely on a teacher. If this is the case for worldly 
activities in this life, then we definit ely need to rely on a teacher for states that we have 
no familiarity with whatsoever, like enlightenment. We have no experience of the goal 
we wish to achieve.  
 All excellent joy and happiness depends on the spiritual friend as its root. This 
means all the joy we experience in future lives completely depends on the spiritual 
friend.  
  
2B4A -1B  The actual way to rely  
1 Characteristics of the one to be relied on, t he virtuous spiritual friend  
2 Characteristics of the one who relies  
3 The way he relies on him  
4 The benefits of reliance  
5 The drawbacks of not relying  
6 Summary of the meaning of these [points]  
In this regard there are six points: characteristics of the  one to be relied on, the 
virtuous spiritual friend, characteristics of the who relies, the w ay he relies on him, the 
benefits of reliance, the draw backs of not relying { 25}  and a summary of the meaning 
of these [ points].  
 
The second point, one who relies,  refers to the student. The third point is the way a 
student who has all the characteristics relies on the spiritual friend who has all the 
characteristics necessary to be a spiritual friend.  
 
2B4A -1B1 Characteristics of the one to be relied on, the virtu ous spiritual friend  
 
A Identification of a “guru”  
B His characteristics  
2B4A -1B1A  Identification of a “guru”  
First: Although, generally speaking, in terms of the individual vehicles, a lot is said 
[about this] in the [Buddha’s] excellent w ords along w ith the commentaries, here only 
a virtuous spiritual friend guiding on the path to Buddhahood, the Mahayana, by 
gradually guiding on the path of the three [types of] beings, w ill be presented.  
 
The sayings of the Buddha and their commentaries present many desc riptions of gurus, 
and in many contexts. Gurus in the context of the Vinaya, in the context of Perfection 
texts etc. Whilst there are these different presentations, here we are talking about 
someone of the Mahayana type who leads all beings by way of the p ath of the three 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 94 – week one to seven  scopes to the state of enlightenment. The description that follows is about such a type. 
  
2B4A -1B1B  His characteristics  
1 Highest characteristics  
2 Middling characteristics  
3 Minimum characteristics  
 
First, we have a complete description, then a middling description and then a bare 
minimum of defining characteristics.  
 
2B4A -1B1B -1 Highest characteristics  
A Good qualities he himself needs  
In Ornament of the Sutras it says that the disciple needs to r ely on a spiritual teacher 
possessed of ten properties:  
A spiritual teacher w ith discipline, calm, complete peace,  
more qualities, effort, rich in scriptural transmissions,  
w ho has thoroughly realized suchness, is eloquent,  
loving, and no more discouraged - on that one rely!  
 
This quote is from the protector Maitreya. The guru is depicted here as having ten 
qualities, and a disciple needs to rely on a guru that has these ten aspects.  
With discipline means if a teacher has not disciplined his own mind, it would be 
impossible for him to discipline the mind of others.  
It is important to rely on a guru who has pacified his own continuum. Any small 
attainment or small realisation in itself  is not sufficient. Maybe someone does a little 
meditation, studies a little and becomes a little calmer or more disciplined, but just that 
is not enough. Some might give this person a great name, but this name alone is not 
enough. We need to rely on someo ne who has qualities that are shown in the teachings 
of the Buddha. Like the ones listed in the verse from the protector Maitreya.  These 
qualities are those that are included in the three higher trainings. Discipline, (ethical 
conduct) calm (concentration)  and complete peace (wisdom).  
 
Furthermore it says that someone who has not disciplined himself, for that reason is 
not in a position to discipline someone else. A spiritual master disciplining others has 
to be someone w ho has first disciplined his ow n mi nd. You may w onder: “Well then, 
in w hat way does it need to be disciplined?” To accomplish anything at all that occurs 
to you and then label it a realizational quality because it exists in the mind, is of no 
benefit. This is why a system of mental discipli ne compatible w ith the general 
teachings of the J inas is necessary. That has been ascertained as the three precious 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
95 trainings, and therefore, ethical discipline and the other tw o have been set forth. In 
this regard “disciplined” refers to the training in e thical discipline. In the Individual 
Liberation  it says:  
In accord w ith the unruly horse of the mind  
alw ays w ilfully straying aw ay:  
a bridle strutted w ith hundred sharp nails - 
that’s individual liberation  
 
This quote is heard by the monks during Sojong .  Here it shows that the guru needs to 
have pacified his mind. For the monks, it‟s one thing to become a monk, but it‟s quite 
another thing to keep the vows pure. To keep all the vows one needs to train in ethical 
discipline.   
 If you lack damtsig  there  are many problems of harmony in situations such as 
between a student and teacher or in a couple. This can also be applied here. As monks, 
we have all kinds of accepted behaviour to guard, and this is not easy. You have to 
restrain your mind with this disc ipline, and here we are taking about a guru who has 
done this.  
  
Someone skilled at breaking in wild horses tames them by means of a good bridle. The 
sense pow ers pursue w rong objects { 26}  like w ild horses. Ethical discipline subjugates 
them as one engages in inappropriate activities and through many efforts  makes one 
engage in w hat ought to be done. Someone trained in it has likew ise tamed the horse 
of the mind.  
 
We need to restrain our senses instead of letting them run wild, but to stop the senses 
is not easy.  
 The analogy here is instructing a young horse, which h as never been ridden 
before to become suitable for riding. This doesn‟t happen easily, as one has to be 
persistent over a long period of time, but eventually they will be suitable to be ridden. 
This is similar to how you train your wild mind using the vows .  
 Another analogy would be someone wanting to get a driving licence. They take 
many lessons and have to study etc. Then he takes his exam and fails. He studies again 
and fails again, but eventually he takes the exam and passes. This last time he is very 
careful to make no mistakes so he can pass! This shows that disciplining the mind is 
not easy, you really have to make effort and stick with it. Then you‟ll come to have 
excellent conduct of body and speech. It doesn‟t come from nowhere, but from 
training.  If you are careful, then this difficult task can be achieved.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 96 – week one to seven   People who are not able to discipline and subdue their minds are like those who 
become monks and disrobe. To subdue the mind you need to follow the advices of 
ethical discipline, and if that is followed, then one will turn out very well.  
 This term “a physical basis” means different things depending on the context. 
For example, in the context of Secret Mantra the physical basis is someone who is the 
best basis for tantric practice. The best b asis is a Gelong , the middling a Getsül  and 
the smallest is a layperson. These different levels of subduing the mind are reflected in 
the different bases for practicing tantra. This is why the Buddha was a monk for his 
whole life. All the Mahayana teache rs like the protector Nagarjuna, Aryadeva, 
Chandrakirti, Shantideva, Atisha and so on, were also tantric practitioners who held 
the vows of a Gelong, showing the best physical basis is a Gelong.  
 Some of us might think that in order to do tantric practice  we don‟t have to rely 
on Vinaya and so on. This is completely wrong; the best basis is to be a Gelong.  
 The same is true for Lama Tsongkhapa and his two sons , they were also 
Gelongs for their whole lives. His Holiness the Dalai Lama is also a fully orda ined 
monk, and all the traditions look up to him. He constantly says, “I‟m a Gelong of the 
Buddha Shakyamuni.” He doesn‟t express himself as “I‟m the Dalai Lama” which is 
nothing more than a title.  
 His Holiness has composed many books, and there will alw ays be an 
acknowledgment in each one of who wrote the text. This would say something like 
“this was written by the Shakya Bhikshu, Tenzin Gyatso.” This shows he is a fully 
ordained monk and his joy in this. He wouldn‟t sign it saying “this was written by a n 
emanation of Chenrezig.”!        
This has great meaning. In general, it says in the Lam -Rim that the Gelong is the best 
basis for the practices, including Secret Mantra. In Secret Mantra there is a description 
of many different levels of practitioners, a nd within that, it is clear the Gelong is the 
best type. So becoming ordained within this life is something very precious. If the 
thought arises, “Maybe I should leave this ordination,” this would be utterly wrong 
and create great problems, so we should ne ver think like this.  
 At the very end of the Middle Length Lam -Rim there are two verses of prayer , 
and in the colophon His Holiness the Dalai Lama express himself as the author of 
these verses in the way mentioned above.  
 That the senses follow all kind  of objects and engage them in an incorrect 
manner is like the analogy of the wild horse. This is what we need to subdue with great 
care, just like one trains a horse. If something happens despite this training and we go 
towards activities that we shouldn‟ t, we should immediately keep it within the 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
97 boundaries, like the student who takes great care not make mistakes in his final driving 
test, otherwise he wouldn‟t get his driving licence!  
 And through many efforts refers to that one needs to utilize all kin ds of 
activities to subdue the mind. You engage into the direction of the worthy, while 
abandoning the unworthy. Through ethical discipline and training you subdue the wild 
horse of the mind. When we talk about Vinaya, subduing refers to body and speech. 
Senses refers to the body and speech, but here we are talking about the horse of the 
mind. Once we do this, the body and speech will automatically become subdued.  
 
“Calm” means having developed the training of concentration w here, based on 
mindfulness and introspection with regard to engaging in positive and counteracting 
unw holesome behaviour, the mind abides in a state of inner calm. “Complete peace” 
means that based on calm abiding of a serviceable mind the training of w isdom has 
been developed through i ndividual analysis of the correct meaning [of reality]. 
How ever, it is not enough merely to have developed - thus, by means of the three 
trainings - the qualities of realization that act to tame the mind. As the quality of 
scriptural know ledge is also need ed, [w e have] “rich in scriptural transmissions” 
meaning that the three collections  and so forth have been studied extensively.  
 
Calm  refers to engaging into excellent activities and turning away from faulty ones. We 
need to rely on mindfulness and intro spection to do this; to discern what is faulty and 
what is excellent - then you can collect the mind inwards – pacify the mind and achieve 
concentration.  
 Complete peace  refers to calm abiding that brings a serviceable mind, and on this 
basis you attain r ealisations, or a mind which is called special insight. Correct meaning  
here refers to emptiness. Individual analysis  refers to a mind that analyses on the basis 
of calm abiding focusing on emptiness, and within that a wisdom which is analysing 
emptiness, and so generating special insight with regard to emptiness. This describes 
the best state.  
 
QUESTION:  Does the teacher have to have this characteristic?  
GEN:  Within this category of the highest type, yes, he needs to have calm abiding and 
special insight observing emptiness.  
 The question is then asked, “If someone has these three higher trainings is that 
enough?” No. It‟s not enough to have the discipline of a Gelong, and a calm abiding 
that is focusing on emptiness and a special insight with regard to e mptiness. He also 
needs to have vast scriptural knowledge because he wants to teach the Dharma.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 98 – week one to seven   Rich in scriptures refers to someone who posses great learning in the three 
baskets, Vinaya  (Discipline), Sutra  (Discourses) and Abhidharma  (Knowledge). The guru 
should have listened and be very learned concerning these three.  
QUESTION:  Earlier we said the teachings of the Buddha has two aspect, scriptural 
and realisational. If the first of these three qualities is to have a realisation of th e three 
higher trainings, wouldn‟t this mean he already has a scriptural understanding of these 
three baskets?  
GEN:  In general, this is the case that in order to have realisations you would have to 
be quite learned already. However, someone might keep his ethical discipline pure 
without having studied much.  
QUESTION:  But how can you keep the vows without having studied them?  
GEN:  The Vinaya is very vast, much more than just the instructions concerning what 
is to be avoided. To explain the Vinaya  in an unmi staken way you would need to study 
it a lot, but you can have pure ethics without this study.  
 You yourself might have strong faith in your teacher and correctly rely on him 
in action, but if you had to explain how to do this to others, and you are not rich in 
the scripture, you wouldn‟t be able to explain.  
 For example, with your own language, which you speak very well, someone 
might ask you to teach it to them. Even though you speak it perfectly it is quite 
another thing to be able to teach it. If you want to teach your language, you might 
even have to go back to school!  
 
 
TUESDAY  2 MARCH  2010  - SESSION  2 
 
QUESTION:  This analogy of the horse, what is the meaning of the good bridle and the 
hundred sharp nails?  
GEN:  We have to imagine a horse pulling a c art. This young horse has to be subdued 
so it does the work. You put the reigns and bridle on the horse so you can make it turn 
where you want. The 100 sharp nails in accordance means nails that are in the cart and 
keeping it together. If the nails are not  there, the cart will fall apart. For example, in 
accordance means in my glasses there are the correct nails or screws, otherwise they 
would fall apart! These are analogies for how you need to subdue the mind. Bridle here 
means being peaceful. You need to create a peaceful situation, and then all will go 
according to your wishes. Ethical discipline here would be similar to taming the wild 
horse of the mind, or similar to the nails that hold everything together according with 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
99 what is needed. What we are aimi ng for is a mind that is subdued in order to engage 
into right conduct.  
 The last line says this is the individual liberation, excellent here refers to the 
method which leads to this excellent subdued and peaceful state. We also want an 
excellent physical and verbal behaviour, the method to which is this individual 
liberation.  
 We could also say we need to rely on mindfulness and introspection to pacify 
the mind that is like a crazy elephant. This is expressed in the Bodhisattvacharyavatara . 
Mindfulness and  introspection then become the excellent method. We need this 
trained and disciplined horse so it pulls the cart, similarly by relying on mindfulness 
and introspection we tame the mind to bring about this excellent behaviour of body 
and speech. The excelle nt method is the Vinaya or Individual Liberation.   
 
QUESTION:  Why is a Gelong the best basis for tantra and the other practices?  
GEN:  Such a Gelong has taken on a lot of vows, and it is he who guards them. By this, 
he becomes an outstanding physical basis .  
TRANSLATOR: What is this physical basis, does it mean the body changes?  
GEN:   Yes. When we say precious human rebirth, in Tibetan we would say a precious 
human body. Why is it precious? Because it has the freedoms and endowments. In the 
same way, a Gelo ng is an excellent physical basis because of the vows. Out of all the 
precious human rebirths, he is ordained. Out of the ordained, he is the highest type, 
and on top of this, he has the highest motivation. All these together make him the 
supreme person to  practice tantra, in the same way as described in the tantric 
teachings.  
 In the Lam-Rim Chenmo  when it talks about Atisha‟s life story, there is a praise 
by the translator Nag -tso which says, „you took ordination, and taking the vows of a 
Gelong kept the m in a way that other Gelongs would prostrate to you. Then you 
generated yourself as deity‟ - meaning he practiced tantra on the basis of being a 
Gelong. From these verses, we can see he was a supreme tantric practitioner because 
he was a supreme Gelong.  
 This is mentioned in the sutras and in the tantric teachings. Here we are talking 
about a Gelong who has taken on all these vows and works on keeping them. This is 
the supreme Gelong.  Guarding the vows of a Gelong or a Getsül is about 
guarding physical an d verbal activity. The vows are described as being something 
physical. If a layperson and a monk are side by side, the eye would see the same kind 
of body. „Form‟ here means the subtlest type of form. This is not of form source, but 
from phenomena source, which you can‟t see. We also have form in a dream – like 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 100 – week one to seven  colours and shapes etc., but we can‟t take a photo of this. The form that pervades the 
body of a Gelong or a Getsül is such a form coming from phenomena source, so just 
on the physical level there is  a difference.  
 
According to Geshe [Drom] Tonpa:  
For someone to be called a Mahayana guru, he must generate boundless 
understanding w hen he explains, and when he practices, he must show  w hat is of 
benefit and direct significance in the last days of the te achings.  
 
This is about the quality of being rich in learning. Someone who explains the Dharma 
needs to give specific advice to each person, and he needs to provide beneficial 
answers to whatever questions the students have. This can only be done if you kn ow 
many scriptures.  
 We need someone who can immediately answer with what is unmistaken and of 
most benefit, rather than having to think a lot. This also comes from being rich in 
scriptures.  
 
“Realization of suchness” refers to the special  training in w i sdom - realization of the 
selflessness of phenomena. Alternatively, it is mainly held to be manifest suchness. 
How ever, even if this is not present, it is said that this [criterion] is also fulfilled by a 
realization through scriptures and reasonings.  
 
The characteristic is a special training in wisdom, a direct realisation of emptiness 
(manifest suchness). This is someone on the path of seeing or above. If he doesn‟t 
have this, he at least needs to have a realisation based on the scriptural teachings.  
QUE STION:  What is the difference between the third characteristic, complete peace 
and this one, realization of suchness ?  
GEN:  Complete  peace means wisdom, but refers to many types of wisdom. Like a 
wisdom realising impermanence, or realising gross selfless (a person not being 
permanent, partless and independent or a person not being a self -sufficient substantial 
existent self ). These ar e all forms of the higher training of wisdom. The text says this 
fifth characteristic is also a higher training of wisdom, but because special is added it 
refers to the realising of selflessness. Both are forms of training in wisdom, but this 
fifth is a sp ecial training in wisdom because it refers to the higher training of wisdom 
directly realising selflessness.  
 Is it enough to have all these? No. Of course, there are lamas who have these, 
but still they need another characteristic of having more qualities  than the disciple has.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
101  
That being so, even if he has scriptural know ledge and realizations, it is not enough 
for them to be inferior or equal to that of the disciple and therefore someone w ith more 
qualities is needed. In the Verses  it says:  
People rel ying on someone inferior w ither,  
those relying on one of their peers stay the same,  
those relying on leaders achieve excellence.  
Therefore rely on those ahead of you  
Possessed of discipline, perfect peace,  
and the most exalted w isdom - 
if you rely on any su ch a lord,  
{ 27}  you even end up ahead of him.  
 
If you rely on someone who has less qualities than you, then you yourself will 
degenerate. Those relying on one of their peers stay the same means you don‟t change 
at all. Therefore, rely on a guru who has hig her qualities than you do, and then you‟ll 
develop more and more.  
 
That is w hy. Puchungw a said: “As I listen to the accounts of the saints, I look up to 
them” and Tashi: “I take the old men at Radreng as a model .” Accordingly, w e need 
someone w ith more q ualities as a model to look up to. Those six properties like this are 
qualities you yourself attain [for your ow n w elfare].  
 
What you are looking for is a role model to look up to, someone who necessarily has 
more qualities than you do. A Kadampa Geshe is also quoted here, Geshe Tashi. He 
says that when he looks at the biography of Lama Drom -dön-pa he gets inspired and 
takes him as a role model.  
 This is very important. You need a role model you can look up to and achieve 
similar states to him. This means someone who has good outward behaviour and 
inward behaviour - how he thinks and what states of mind he has.  
 If you want to become a teacher in the future, you need to attain these six.  
 
 
 
B Positive qualities necessary for the welfare of others  
 
The rem aining ones are qualities for taking care of others. As they say:  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 102 – week one to seven  The Capable Ones  do not w ash aw ay evil w ith w ater,  
they do not remove the suffering of beings by hand,  
they do not graft their realizations on others - 
they liberate, teaching the truth of reality’s nature.  
 
This quote from sutra expresses that the lama teaches the path to you, and from your 
side you have to engage in what has been taught. Other than this, they cannot benefit 
you in any other way .    
 As is being expressed here, apart from t aking care of others, having taught them the 
path in an unmistaken manner, nothing can be done w ashing aw ay negativities w ith 
w ater and the like. Out of the four characteristics for this, “eloquent” means 
experienced w ith regard to the stages of guidance a nd skilled at putting the meaning 
across to the minds of those to be tamed. “Loving” means having a pure motivation to 
teach the Dharma and teaching motivated by compassion w ithout regard for material 
gain or respect. We need someone like Potow a [w ho] said  to Chengaw a: “Son of 
Lima, how ever many explanations of the Dharma I have given, not once have I 
claimed the applause as my ow n. There is no migrating being that is not pitiable.” 
“Energetic” (“w ith energy”) means constantly delighting in the w elfare of o thers. 
“Having abandoned discouragement” (“no more discouraged”) means not to become 
w eary due to explaining things over and over again, but rather to bear the hardships of 
explaining.  
 
Eloquent  refers to being skilled in explaining in a way that the disci ples understand the 
teachings. Loving  is a caring attitude, which is a very important quality for the guru to 
have.   
 The conversation between Potowa and Chengawa express that when you teach 
you shouldn‟t have the motivation to be thanked afterwards or fo r offerings to be 
offered to you.  
 Pitiable  refers to that there is no one who is not in such a state, and as a result, 
one should teach with such an attitude.  
 The third quality is energetic which is the same as joyous perseverance. When 
you teach you sh ould do so with great joy thinking, “this is a time where I can benefit 
people” and you do this with a mind of great joy. You should be without a motivation 
of wanting them to be grateful to you afterwards, but rather have a motivation where 
you can put up  with all the hardships, which is the fourth quality.  
 Having abandoned discouragement  means you don‟t feel exhausted by explaining 
things again and again and forbear with the hardships connected with explaining. If 
there is benefit, then you make the effo rt to explain.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
103  This completes the list of the ten qualities a spiritual friend should have, and 
this was in the context of the highest characteristics they should have.  
 
 
TUESDAY  2 MARCH  2010  - SESSION  3 
 
2B4A -1B1B -2 Middling characteristics  
Potow a said : “The three trainings, realization of suchness and a loving heart - those 
five are the main thing. My master Shangtsun is not very { 28}  learned in everything, 
as he cannot bear disenchantment either, he does not impress on his mind the kindness 
he receive d. How ever, as he has the above[ -mentioned] five, w hoever is in his presence 
benefits. Nyanton is by no means eloquent and each time he makes a dedication the 
only thing he know s is that, again, nobody understood anything, but as he has those 
five, people near him benefit.”  
 
There is a quote here from the Kadampa Geshe Potowa, and they have a very difficult 
dialect to understand. Does not impress on his mind probably refers to this master 
Shangtsun would explain with a grim expression rather than being radi ant and smiling. 
This can also be used as a method, like when Marpa was speaking to Milarepa he 
would use s stern face to tame Milarepa. But in our context here - this is lacking a 
quality.  
 Whoever is in his presence benefits means people benefit from his explaining 
the Dharma, but as he is not eloquent he teaches in a very rough way, “It‟s like this, 
it‟s not like this”, not using nice language etc.  
 This aspect of showing an angry face is used by some Tibetan lamas as a 
method. They might even show an aspect of not being interested in teaching, or even 
sending people away. Maybe he only teaches a very little, but within that it has great 
meaning and gives plenty to think about.  
In the Tibe tan culture, the disciple wouldn‟t get angry with the teacher. If the teacher 
gets angry the disciple, they would only think, “I did something wrong” but never 
return the anger. This would be good for westerners to adopt! I‟m referring here to the 
behaviou r of the monks in the three great seats, rather than other places of Tibetan 
culture.  
 
   
2B4A -1B1B -3 Minimum characteristics  
A The actual minimum of characteristics  
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 104 – week one to seven  Even if it is difficult, due to the times, to find someone w ho has the complete set of 
characteristics like that, it is said that you should not rely on anyone whose faults are 
greater or w hose faults and positive qualities are equal, but on someone whose positive 
qualities exceed the faults.  
 
In our time, it is difficult to find a teacher w ith the complete set of these 
characteristics mentioned above, and as a result, you would look for a lama who 
doesn‟t find it difficult to teach or one who has more qualities than you.  
 With these ten qualities, we should try to find a guru who has at lea st two (a 
fourth) or one of these (an eighth). Having a loving concern is very important, and 
they should also be rich in scriptures. Actually, this is indispensable; you can‟t do 
without this quality.  
  
B The expediency of association with the characteri stics  
Such a spiritual master bringing about complete liberation is a perpetual root of 
aspiration. Therefore, those w ishing to rely on a spiritual teacher should be aw are of 
them and make an effort looking for someone w ho possesses the characteristics. Th ose 
w ishing to have disciples rely on them should also strive to acquire these 
characteristics.  
 
QUESTION:  How did the Lama Nyanton benefit people if no one could understand 
him?  
GEN:  When Manjushri appeared directly to Lama Tsongkhapa, he would tell him t o 
write down many things. Lama Tsongkhapa would sometimes say, “I don‟t understand 
what I‟m writing” and Manjushri would say “It doesn‟t matter – just write it down and 
you‟ll come to understand it later.” The same applies for Lama Nyanton. He said 
benefic ial things, but they weren‟t understood immediately.  
 The teachings of His Holiness the Dalai Lama have recently become quite deep. 
In Europe, the teachings he gives are quite detailed, and I wonder who in Europe 
understands them. Maybe they don‟t, but th ey do get some imprints. It‟s not that there 
is not benefit at all, of course there is benefit.  
 For example, some people recite 100,000 times the 100 -syllable mantra but they 
don‟t know what it means. Even upon completing the number, they don‟t know the 
meaning, despite it being important in order for the practice to be powerful. This is 
quite regrettable, but we can‟t say there is no benefit at all as they have still 
accumulated the 100,000 mantras themselves.  
 Some amongst us might decide to do a retre at to count many mantras, and most 
of our time would be spent reciting them, but before we engage into this we need to 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
105 know what the mantra is about and how to reflect during the recitation, otherwise it 
becomes difficult. If you are not prepared in this w ay, you might end up thinking in an 
opposite way to what is meant for the practice.  
 
2B4A -1B2 Characteristics of the one who relies, the disciple  
A Faults of someone who does not have and benefits of someone who does have the five 
characteristics  
 
Second: From the Four Hundred :  
Impartial, intelligent, full of endeavour - 
thus is explained a vessel for teachings.  
Otherw ise qualities of the teacher,  
have no effect – there’s no change in the listener.  
 
As it says in the commentaries: someone endow ed w ith the three characteristics is a 
suitable vessel for listening. If all three are complete, the positive qualities of the one 
expounding the Dharma appear as positive qualities and do not appear as faults. Not 
only that: the positive qualities of the listeners al so appear as such to that person and 
do not appear as faults. If the characteristics of a vessel are not complete, the listener, 
under the pow er of his faults, w ill classify even an extremely,, perfectly pure spiritual 
teacher w ho expounds the Dharma, amon g those w ho have faults, and perceive an 
exponent’s faults as qualities.  
 
B Explaining the specifics of the [various] characteristics in detail  
{ 29}  In this regard “impartiality” (“unbiased”) means being w ithout bias.  
 
You are not attached to your own syst em, and don‟t have anger towards the systems of 
others.  
 
If you have a bias, it w ill make you obscured and you will not perceive positive 
qualities,  
 
If you are under the influence of these two (attachment or rejection), you will be 
unable to see the posi tive qualities of the teacher, and as a result, you will be unable to 
realise the excellent meaning of their teachings.  
 
due to w hich the meaning of excellent explanations is not found. It is just as it says in 
Essence of the Middle Way:  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 106 – week one to seven  With a biased - thus anguished - mind   
peace can never be realised.  
 
If you are falling into these minds of partiality, then liberation cannot be attained. 
Actually the meaning here is being tormented by this bias – attachment or rejection. 
You‟ll be utterly unhappy whil st listening to the teachings where you should actually be 
full of joy.  
 You should examine your mind to see if you have this attachment to your own 
system and rejection of others, and if so, you should get rid of it, otherwise you‟ll not 
get the essence of the Dharma.  
 
To be biased means to be attached to one’s ow n approach and to hate other people’s 
approach. Having examined something like this in your ow n continuum, you should 
give it up. If you w onder w hether that by itself is sufficient: even though impartial, 
someone to w hom good ways of explaining appear as faulty ones, due to not having 
the intelligence to distinguish them, is not a suitable vessel. Therefore one needs to 
have the intelligence that understands those tw o. Are those tw o sufficient? E ven if one 
has the tw o, [impartiality and intelligence], someone w ho is just like the listener to the 
Dharma in a [lifeless] picture is not a suitable vessel. Therefore one [also] needs to 
have great endeavour. The commentary states that adding the tw o, re spect for the 
Dharma and the expounder of Dharma as w ell as an attentive mind, five 
[characteristics]  are set forth.  
 
TRANSLATOR: This word here translated as approach in Tibetan is the same word 
for system, like a religious system or the four systems of T ibet. Does this mean you 
might like Buddhism and not Christianity, or like Gelugpa and not Kagyupa?  
GEN:  This is not the meaning here. It‟s more like you might sit in a teaching and find 
faults with the teacher, saying things like, “my previous teacher did  things this way, but 
this teacher does them that way, I think the previous teacher was better” and so on.  
By having his kind of bias in your mind, you don‟t get the benefits of listening to the 
teachings.
