QUESTIONS  
QUESTION:  How does the habit of some Lamas scolding their translators go together 
with what Lama Tsongkhapa says about the teacher having to show a radiant 
expression?  
ANSWER:  Marpa always showed a wrathful expression to Milarepa. This is sometimes 
necess ary. Some people can be guided by friendly words but others only respond to 
this rough type of behaviour. There is a reason why we have Yamantaka in his wrathful 
aspect. There are many ways to tame disciples (one word for disciple is „the object to 
be disc iplined‟). When Chöden Rinpoche for example, scolded the translators in 
Institute Vajra Yogini and in Barcelona up to the point that they didn‟t translate for 
him anymore, I am one hundred percent sure that there was no actual anger in his 
continuum. Anoth er aspect is that Tibetans are different from westerners. To young 
Tibetan children you have to be a bit harsh, otherwise they wouldn‟t respond well.  
I, up to now, have never shown any wrathful aspect with Tharchin, my translator, but 
maybe from now on he needs to be a bit more careful! In my own Khangtsen in Sera 
monastery, I use a lot of harsh words with the students, and therefore they are quite 
afraid of me. One time I needed to punish seven of my monks. At that time, I had to 
do this because I was thei r room teacher, and therefore I was responsible for their 
behaviour. If the room teacher wouldn‟t discipline his fellow monks, for whom he is 
like a parent, then he himself would get a very severe and embarrassing scolding from 
the Geckö (disciplinarian). The room teacher has to take care of his fellow monks in 
all aspects of life, seeing to them studying properly, their food and medicine, and so 
forth. A different kind of teacher is the Pecha - or scripture teacher, who isn‟t 
responsible for the monks‟ beha viour and, since he has so many disciples, might not 
even recognize them on the street. This is how it works in the monastery. The room 
teacher has this kind of family relationship with his disciples. He has all the 
responsibility.  
 
QUESTION:  Can we infer  from the homage shown in the beginning to which Pitaka 
this Lam -Rim belongs?  
ANSWER:  In the Lam-Rim Chenmo  homage is expressed to Manjushri, which shows 
that it belongs to the Abidharma  pitaka. If the homage is expressed to the Buddhas and 
Bodhisattva, th en the teaching belongs to the Sutra pitakas, and if the homage is to the 
omniscient one, it shows that it belongs to the Vinaya  pitaka. In our case, here 
especially referring to the Lam-Rim Chenmo , at the end of this text we have a section 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 108 – week one to seven  about special i nsight. In the English translation, there are three volumes, and this one 
is the third. In addition, this is clearly connected with wisdom, i.e. the Abidharma  
pitaka. The first two volumes are more connected to the Sutra  pitaka, so you could say 
that you h ave different sections being subsumed into different pitakas, but mainly we 
would put the text into the Abhidarma  pitaka.   
 
B Explaining the specifics of the [various] characteristics in detail  
{ 29}  In this regard “impartiality” (“unbiased”) means being w i thout bias. If you have 
a bias, it will make you obscured and you w ill not perceive positive qualities, due to 
w hich the meaning of excellent explanations is not found. It is just as it says in 
Essence of the Middle Way:  
 
With a biased - thus anguished - mind   
peace can never be realised.  
To be biased means to be attached to one’s ow n approach and to hate other people’s 
approach. Having examined something like this in your ow n continuum, you should 
give it up. If you w onder w hether that by itself is suffi cient: even though impartial, 
someone to w hom good ways of explaining appear as faulty ones, due to not having 
the intelligence to distinguish them, is not a suitable vessel. Therefore one needs to 
have the intelligence that understands those tw o. Are thos e tw o sufficient? Even if one 
has the tw o, [impartiality and intelligence], someone w ho is just like the listener to the 
Dharma in a [lifeless] picture is not a suitable vessel. Therefore one [also] needs to 
have great endeavour. The commentary states that  adding the tw o, respect for the 
Dharma and the expounder of Dharma as w ell as an attentive mind, five 
[characteristics]  are set forth.  
 
Unbiased means to have no attachment towards one‟s own approach and no anger 
towards the approach of others.  
The line referring to excellent explanations appearing as faulty, I read as, when you 
listen to the teachings then the teacher will explain to you what is excellent and what is 
mistaken. Therefore, the good and faulty ways of explaining here actually mean 
explainin g what is good and explaining what is faulty, and that you need to have the 
intelligence to discriminate between the two. I for example, have two or three different 
teachers and some are more skilled in explaining something than others. Therefore, I 
need t o have the ability to distinguish between good and not so good explanations. If 
you don‟t have such intelligence, then you won‟t be able to see the faults in a bad 
explanation and might even mistake it as an excellent explanation. This talks about the 
highest form of disciple who has the ability to understand what is presented in the 
teaching. Another type of disciple will not have the ability to understand however,  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
109 many times something is explained to him. So the point is that you need to be 
someone who has  some kind of wisdom (we talk here about the object ascertaining 
mental factor of wisdom; tib: sherab ; its definition: It strongly differentiates the qualities 
of things that are to be examined. It has the function of overcoming doubt.).  
 
WEDNESDAY  3 MARCH  2010  - SESSION  2 
This discrimination comes about due to your study of the dharma. You need this 
„intelligence coming from studying‟. However, the first two aspects of impartial and 
intelligent are not enough; it needs someone who is really aiming at gener ating the 
realisations. The analogy of the lifeless picture should be understood in the way that 
someone drawing a picture will be very careful and to avoid any mistakes, he will not 
look within himself, but only to the outside. However, here we need someo ne who 
does the opposite and looks within himself for the faults. This effort of aiming at the 
dharma talks about someone who has the intention to actually integrate the words he 
has heard. Therefore, the three types of disciples will have the aim to put a ll the 
instructions of their respective scopes into practice.  
There are two additional characteristics presented, and these are to have respect for 
the dharma and the teacher, and to have a focused attentive mind. These two stem 
from the commentary writte n by Chandrakirti on the Four Hundred Verses  by Aryadeva. 
If in the text you find a phrasing like here where it just says „the commentary states 
that adding the two...‟, then this always refers to the main commentary written on the 
treatise. An example wou ld be the Madhyamakavatara  by Chandrakirti to which he also 
wrote an auto commentary, being the main commentary. Therefore, if we read in the 
Lam-Rim that the commentary referred to here is the commentary by Chandrakirti 
then from this we can infer that th e master Aryadeva didn‟t write an auto commentary, 
since if he did it would be the main commentary itself. We need to make effort 
towards generating these five characteristics.  
  
C Specifics of achieving the conducive conditions and eliminating the adverse  conditions for 
having the four characteristics  
That being so there are four: (1) great endeavour w ith regard to the Dharma, (2) an 
excellently attentive mind w hile listening, (3) great respect in the Dharma and the 
expounder of Dharma and (4) holding to g ood explanations having rejected faulty 
ones. Intelligence is a conducive circumstance for those four and the impartiality is 
w hat eliminates adverse circumstances.  
 
Adverse circumstances refer to taking sides with attachment to your view and anger 
towards the views of others. This is eliminated through generating impartiality.   
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 110 – week one to seven   
D The need to strive for those characteristics  
You should examine w hether or not all the attributes that w ould make you suitable for 
being guided by a spiritual teacher are complete and cultivate joy, if they are. If they 
are not complete, you should strive henceforth including future lives at the causes for 
making them complete.  
 
You should s trive henceforth  refers to that you have to analyse in your own continuum 
whether you possess these characteristics, and if you don‟t have them all, you need to 
make effort to achieve them. If you find out that you do possess all of them, then you 
should g reatly rejoice in this because you then become a suitable vessel to be lead 
along the path by a highly qualified lama. However, if you lack them, you won‟t be a 
suitable vessel, and it won‟t be possible for you to be lead along the path by such a 
lama.  
 
2B4A-1B3 The way he relies on him  
 
A Explanation of the need to rely on someone with the characteristics  
B The actual manner of relying on someone who has the characteristics  
 
2B4A -1B3A  Explanation of the need to rely on someone with the characteristics  
{ 30} Third: Thus someone possessed of [the characteristics of] a vessel should examine - 
the way explained above - w hether or not a teacher has the characteristics and if so, 
obtain the kindness of the Dharma from him.  
 
Here we have a student possessing all t hese characteristics w ho wants to train in the 
dharma . He is asked to check whether the guru has all these previously explained 
qualities, and if he finds such a guru, he should engage in receiving the dharma from 
him.  
2B4A -1B3B  The actual manner of relyin g on someone who has the characteristics  
1 The manner of relying in thought  
2 The manner of relying through actions  
 
With regard to the manner of relying on a spiritual teacher from whom you generally 
obtain the kindness of the Dharma and w ho, especially, guides your mind w ell by 
means of perfectly complete instructions, there are tw o points: the manner of relying in 
thought and the manner of relying through actions.  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
111 In relying on a spiritual teacher, in general you will be given the kindness of the 
dharma  and specifically the perfectly complete in structions to guide your mind.  
 
2B4A -1B3B -1 The manner of relying in thought  
A The root, training in confidence  
B Through remembering his kindness, developing respect  
The first one has tw o points: the root, training in confidence and through remembering 
his kindness, developing respect.  
 
This is the essence of what Guru Yoga is about is to generate respect and faith towards 
the lama and his kindness. Guru Yoga refers to practices like the Lama Chöpa, the 
Gand en Lha Gyäma, or the Six Session Guru Yoga. If you are able to generate such a 
trust, then based on this it will be also easy to have virtuous minds like self -respect 
(sometimes translated as shame) and embarrassment, which are key points for our 
practice.   
 
2B4A -1B3B -1A The root, training in confidence  
 
1 Explanation of confidence as the basis of all qualities  
 
First: In the J ew el Tala Dharani it says:  
The preparatory practice of confidence bears - like a mother - 
all positive qualities, guards them and make s them increase.  
This says that confidence gives birth to positive qualities that have not yet arisen and, 
once they have come into existence, it sustains and increases them.  
 
The first quote here expresses how faith (synonymous with confidence) is so ver y 
essential. Here, there is on the one hand faith in the lama, and on the other hand faith 
in karma and its effects. There are also other types, but these two are the most 
important ones.  
If you have generated faith in the lama, at a later time you will a lso be able to 
generate faith in karma and its effects. Therefore, the first type of faith is a cause for 
the second.  Buddha Shakyamuni, the Thirty -five Confession Buddhas or different 
Yidams are objects in which we might have faith, but the highest bless ings actually 
come from faith in the lama. All the enlightened qualities of body, speech and mind 
come from faith in the lama, and as a result, this kind of faith is incredibly important. 
From our own side there is nothing more important than the guru, nei ther the Buddha 
nor the bodhisattvas. He is the one showing to us what needs to be adopted and what 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 112 – week one to seven  needs to be abandoned. If you have some sickness, and there is a doctor nearby who 
you could rely on, then however many famous doctors there might be in the  world, it 
is only the one that lives nearby who could be of benefit to you. The others cannot 
provide what you need.   
In order to receive the blessings, to be a suitable vessel in which they can be 
poured, we have to rely on making ourselves into such a suitable vessel. Therefore, 
first, we have to accumulate these characteristics of a suitable disciple and secondly we 
have to train in faith in order to make us receptive to the blessings. The way to train in 
faith is to make clear to yourself that even al l the limitless Buddhas and Bodhisattvas 
are not more kind than your own guru. He is the source of everything for you, and 
there is no other way to follow. In addition, the guarding of present qualities and their 
increase is due to the kindness of your lam a. The abandonment of even a single 
affliction or fault also comes from relying on him. When it says to rely on a guru, then 
that means right the one you have encountered. We need to generate a state of mind 
free from all doubts and obstacles and generate a completely pure faith. This is our 
job. In addition, it is necessary to abandon pride. Instead, you should engage into 
showing respect.  
 
In the Ten Dharmas  it also says:  
That w hich guides to liberation,  
the foremost yana  is confidence.  
Therefore, the i ntelligent,  
rely on pursuing confidence.  
In those w ho are w ithout confidence  
w holesome qualities do not arise,  
Likew ise, in seeds that w ere burnt by fire,  
no green sprout can germinate.  
 
In these terms confidence is said to be the foundation of all positive qualities through 
forw ard and reverse pervasion.  
 
 This quote deals with the topic by giving the reasoning with regards to good 
qualities arising from faith. This is similar to a seed, which can give rise to a sprout. 
Just as no sprout can come ab out if there is no seed, in the same way, if there is no 
faith, no good qualities can arise.  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
113 2 Identification of confidence  
Generally speaking, there are many types of confidence: in the J ew els, in actions and 
effects, and in the Four Noble Truths, how eve r, here it is confidence in the spiritual 
master.  
 
3 The need to discriminate a Buddha with regard to the spiritual master  
Furthermore, as for the way of looking at the spiritual master, it says in the Vajrapani 
Empow erment Tantra:  
Oh Lord of secrets, how  should a disciple see the master? J ust like he w ould see 
the Buddha Bhagavan.  
 
This talks about generating a discrimination of the guru being an actual buddha.  
 
{ 31}  Similar statements can be found in the collection of Mahayana sutras and in the 
vinaya. Their meaning is this: If you understand that someone is like a buddha, a 
mind conceiving faults in him will not arise, but rather a mind thinking about his 
positive qualities. Likew ise, w ith regard to a spiritual master, you should purposely 
abandon any c oncepts of faults in every respect w hile training the mind conceiving 
positive qualities.  
 
If you see someone as similar to a Buddha, then you will not look at his faults but will 
only perceive qualities, and in this way, the Lama really becomes your virtu ous friend. 
You should make a special effort in abandoning the mind that sees faults in the 
teacher.  
QUESTION:  Is this saying that while the lama does have faults you don‟t look at them, 
but only at his good qualities? Or do you actually have to see him as a Buddha?  
ANSWER:  The best is to see him as an actual Buddha. Where it says in the Lama 
Chöpa “You are my guru, my Yidam, you are the Dakinis and Dharma protectors ...” 
and later on “from now until enlightenment I shall seek no other refuge than you” an d 
so on, you should generate this kind of outlook with regards to the lama. Whatever 
faults you do see, you can perceive as skilful means from the side of the lama, and 
think that you have an appearance of faults where there actually aren‟t any.  
When Mila repa went to find Marpa, Marpa was awaiting him in the aspect of a farmer, 
ploughing a field. Milarepa approached the farmer and told him he wants to see Marpa. 
The farmer said he won‟t be able to see Marpa anyway, but if he could come to a place 
up there to see him. Of course, later he saw right this farmer sitting there as Marpa.  
Within the teacher student relationship, Marpa punished Milarepa strongly but 
Milarepa only had the outlook of his guru being Vajradhara, and he took all the 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 114 – week one to seven  scolding as a blessing. In this way, you should deal with the perception of any faults in 
your gur u.  
There is the following conversation between Geshe Drom Tönpa and Lama Atisha: 
Drom Tönpa said: “Here in Tibet we have many people engaging in all kinds of 
dharma practice, making a lot of effort without achieving any realisations. Why is 
that?” Atisha answered: “All the great or small qualities of all the vehicles come from 
faith in the guru and you Tibetans don‟t have any faith in the gurus but treat them as 
ordinary beings. So therefore you are not getting any attainments.” Drom Tönpa also 
requested s ome heart advice from Atisha to which he answered with: “Faith, faith, 
faith.”  
 
 
WEDNESDAY  3 MARCH  2010  - SESSION  3 
 
4 The disadvantages of conceiving faults  
In addition, the above tantra says:  
Apprehend the master’s positive qualities,  
do not ever apprehe nd his faults,  
apprehending his qualities you w ill gain siddhis,  
apprehending his faults they cannot be gained.  
 
You should act accordingly. Thus if positive qualities predominate in a spiritual 
master, but you think about him in terms of the few faults he  has, this will become an 
obstacle to your ow n accomplishments. Even if faults predominate, if you train in 
confidence from the perspective of his positive qualities without thinking about him in 
terms of his faults, this w ill become a cause for accomplish ments to come about. 
Therefore, w hen it comes to your ow n spiritual master, whether his faults are big or 
small, contemplate the disadvantages of thinking about him from that perspective, 
repeatedly generate a mind to abandon this and make it stop.  
 
There are high, middling and low siddhis. The low ones bring an increase in life span, 
and they allow you to heal, get an entourage, riches, etc. The eight types of middling 
ones have to do with magical powers like flying, and they are attained on the basis of 
calm abiding. The supreme one, being in the context of tantra, is that at the end of the 
completion stage you attain to the supreme state of Buddhahood.  
 
QUESTION:   Is it indicated here that the guru can have faults?  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
115 ANSWER:  Yes, this might be the case, bu t you don‟t look at them but generate the 
discernment of him being a completely enlightened Buddha.  
 
If you are able to focus only on the good qualities in a lama who mainly has faults, 
then this will enable you to attain the siddhis. This needs to be unde rstood as advice 
for someone who already relies on a guru, but before you rely on a guru, you need to 
check him for the above -mentioned qualities. As a result, before you have chosen a 
guru all the freedom is with you. Similarly, before buying something yo u are 
completely free about your decision. Once you take it home though, it is up to you 
whether you perceive qualities or faults with it despite already buying it.  
QUESTION:   In some small monasteries, there is just one teacher and no 
freedom to choose. H owever, the person being the teacher might have all kinds of 
faults.  
ANSWER:  It is not that by listening to his dharma you have to rely on him as your 
guru. If you take him as your guru, this is still up to you, even if there is only one. 
Nevertheless, if you have already been in the dharma for a long time and you still 
haven‟t found a guru, then there is a great loss. You might even die before having 
found one.  
Another aspect is that you want to rely on him for a long time. Therefore, if you 
choose a guru  but the next day he leaves to a distant place, then you won‟t get the full 
benefit from him being your guru. The monks in Sera Monastery for example take the 
teacher leading them through the texts as their Guru. Also with His Holiness the Dalai 
Lama, from  whom they get many teachings, they have such a guru -disciple 
relationship. Opposed to that, they might take initiation with some lama who moves to 
some other place after the initiation. This is also a type of guru, but more from the 
point of view of only having a dharma experience with him. However, the one they 
would refer to as their main guru is the one leading them through the texts; the one 
that gives them all the dharma teachings.  
 The situation here in the West is quite different. In the monasterie s in the East a 
new student would get advice from others regarding to which lama he should go to. 
Then he can choose which lama to rely on. In the West, there is no such choice, and 
therefore it is not necessary to rely on such a teacher. If for example, o ne is in a 
dharma centre where there is only one teacher, and you like him, then you can take 
him as your teacher, but if you don‟t like him you can still go to the teachings without 
necessarily taking him as your teacher. On the other hand, once you have engaged into 
such a relationship then that‟s it! You have chosen and there is no further change 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 116 – week one to seven  possible. You then have to structure your relationship with him according to the 
advice presented here.  
In my own case, I relied on only one guru who was leading me through the texts, but 
by now he has passed away. I relied on him from the beginning up to my Geshe exam. 
I don‟t know if I saw him every day but I definitely went to all the classes he taught, 
and in the very year I finished my Geshe exam he passed away. I was also relying on 
other lamas like Kensur Losang Tenzin, now being the Jangtse Chöje. With him, I was 
studying for four or five years and I also relied on Khensur Losang Delek Rinpoche 
for th ree or four years before I did my Geshe exam. Additionally, I studied with 
another two lamas for about two years, and over the years I also received a lot of 
teachings from His Holiness who became one of my root lamas. The ones who are still 
in Sera are th e Jangtse Chöje and Kensur Losang Delek. I took initiations from several 
lamas, to which I also refer to as my gurus, but the main ones are those I just 
mentioned.  
 One has to look at the lama who was most kind to oneself, and on that basis, 
regard as you r main guru. For Lama Atisha, one of his many teachers  was the master 
Serlingpa and therefore he took him as his main teacher. Also Lama Tsongkhapa had 
many teachers but amongst all of these he regarded Jetsun Rendawa as his main guru.  
 
QUESTION:   Is it v ery important to see one of our gurus as our main guru? Do 
we have to choose from amongst them who is the most kind?  
ANSWER:  If in this very moment someone would ask me who my main lama is then I 
would have to say it is His Holiness. You don‟t necessarily have to choose a main 
lama. However, in general to find out who your main lama is, you would look at which 
of them is the kindest, but of course this doesn‟t mean that you forget the other ones. 
If you want to condense all of them into one guru, then you c an imagine how the 
others are contained within your main lama, like when all the merit field is condensed 
into your main lama. If you see all gurus as equally kind, then you don‟t have to choose 
and can practice guru devotion by including all of them in th is way.  
  
 QUESTION:   If we do the Ganden Lha Gyäma, we do guru yoga for 
someone (Lama Tsongkhapa) with whom we can‟t encounter.  
ANSWER:  This is a wrong approach. We have to clearly identify Lama Tsongkhapa as 
being one with our kind root guru. This we do  in the Ganden Lha Gyäma, as well as in 
the Lama Chöpa. There one refers to the main lama in front as being Lama Losang 
Thubwang Dorje Chang. This Losang refers to Losang Dragpa (Lama Tsongkhapa), 
Thubwang refers to Buddha Shakyamuni and Dorje Chang is Vaj radhara. All these are 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
117 seen as one and are to be understood as being your kindest root lama. Therefore, you 
always do the guru yoga with regards to who is your main guru, and you always 
visualise him as actually being present.  
When in the Ganden Lha Gyäma  the light from Maitreya‟s heart comes forth as Lama 
Tsongkhapa then you understand this as being your main guru. In this way, you 
practice the Ganden Lha Gyäma on the basis of Losang Thubwang Dorje Chang.  
Here one would try to break down any kind of ordi nary appearance with regards to 
one‟s own main lama, but instead try to bring forth pure appearance of the lama and 
regard him as Losang Thubwang Dorje Chang; as being one with these three aspects. 
The other verse expressing this clearly is the one where i t says, “You are my guru, my 
Yidam, you are the Dakinis and Dharma protectors ...” with this kind of attitude one 
destroys the ordinary appearance of the lama and generates a pure appearance.  
 
 QUESTION:   What if in the past you relied on a lama whom you s aw as the 
kindest, but for some reason you can‟t be near him anymore, and you meet some other 
lama whom you then perceive as being even kinder?  
ANSWER:  In general, you would never let go of the lamas you had in the past. 
Similarly, you might have been hung ry and thirsty in the past and someone invited you 
for food and drink. Years later, you end up in the same situation and someone else is 
inviting you for food and drink, and again you feel that this person is very kind. 
However, he is not more kind than th e person who invited you in the past. Both are 
kind in the same way. So similarly, a lama who benefited us in the past is not less kind 
than the one benefiting us now. The same is true with the parents of this life and the 
parents of the past life. Apart f rom referring to both as very kind, you would never let 
go of one of these lamas. The Tibetan way between choosing would be to look at 
which one of two lamas whom you view as equally kind, has benefitted you the 
longest. Another way is to check with regard  to whom you are generating the strongest 
faith. So for example if you had one lama for two years, and then you have another 
lama for four years whom you feel to be equally kind, you can then switch to him.  
 In the case of Lama Atisha, he relied on Serlingpa for 12 years, making him also 
the lama on whom he relied on the longest time. In the second half of Lama 
Tsongkhapa‟s life, he relied on the master Rendawa for the longest period of time. In 
addition, one could rely on the lama with regards to wh om in one‟s mind a sign of 
mental closeness is appearing. To whom you feel closest, this lama you take as your 
main lama.  
 
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 118 – week one to seven   
THURSDAY  4 MARCH  2010  - SESSION  1 
 
ADVICE AND QUESTIONS  
When reciting The Prayer of the Lion -Face Dakini [Page 13 in the Nalanda Pr ayer Book]  we 
should pray for the dispelling of all obstacles in general and especially the obstacles to 
our dharma practice. We should have in mind the strong wish that all these obstacles 
to our bodily health, our lifespan and dharma practice, any mental  problems, or our 
wealth, as well as anything related to our friends and companions may all be dispelled. 
All of these, may they be turned away, become non -existent and be pacified.  
We have to be aware that we definitely do have obstacles; there is no one  without 
them, other the Buddha. In the same way, everyone also has obstacles to his or her 
life. Nowadays, there is a lot of upheaval from the elements and they threaten people 
in many places. We are so fortunate to be living in this wonderful place, when  
elsewhere there are earthquakes, storms, floods, etc. This morning for example I got a 
phone call from a friend in Taiwan where they had a strong earthquake, from which 
many of the small houses crumbled. My friend‟s water bowls and statues even fell 
down.  In comparison to this, our place is quite peaceful and doesn‟t pose many 
obstacles. This we should keep in mind and be happy about. Nevertheless, we do have 
obstacles and we do this prayer in order to dispel them.  
 
QUESTION:  In a situation where Guru dev otion is not so strong, while doing the 
Ganden Lha Gyäma, would it be appropriate to visualise the master in front of you as 
an actual emanation of Lama Tsongkhapa instead of your Guru, in order to overcome 
the obstacles to the devotion to one‟s actual Gur u? 
ANSWER:  This method could be applied, but it would not be a perfect way of doing 
the Guru yoga practice. In general, for it to be the real practice, we have to rely on our 
own Guru. It is good to have faith towards many buddhas and bodhisattvas, and we 
accumulate virtue in this way, but the actual realisation of this topic of relying on the 
Guru would not come about through using the method you suggest. It was said in the 
text that in general, there are many types of faith; like in the three jewels, karm a, the 
four noble truths, etc. but here we are talking about faith in our teacher. We definitely 
need to generate a faith in the Guru that allows us to see him as our sole object of 
hope and trust for this life and future lives, and as our protector and re fuge.  
 If you take an empowerment, first you wash yourself, enter to gompa to make 
prostrations, offer a Mandala and then offer the Torma. Then at one point, the first 
thing the lama will explain is not to perceive him or the place in ordinary form. He 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
119 would explain how he already did the self and front generation of himself as the deity, 
and since this has already happened you shouldn‟t see him anymore as ordinary, but as 
the deity. There is a great meaning to this as he is expressing the way to rely on t he 
spiritual friend. If we rely on the Guru in this way, we will receive all the benefit from 
doing this practice correctly.  
 If there is both a representation of Chenrezig and your Guru present in front of 
you, then to prostrate to the image of Chenrezig  rather than your Guru would be 
unsuitable, as he is more worthy than this representation. He is your sole hope and 
object in which you place your trust and mind in order to go for refuge. The support 
comes from him and not from the image. In general, it i s good to show respect to 
statues and stupas, etc. but much more worthy of veneration is the Guru himself.
 Something very important in the text has been said; in that the generation of a 
single good quality and abandonment of a single fault come about due to the lama. 
From him comes this purifying and accumulating effect. Of course, in general it is 
extremely important for yourself to practice, to keep your ethical discipline and so on, 
but other than that, you also have to keep your samaya, or pledges towa rds the lama. 
These are referring to the way in which the disciple correctly relies on the virtuous 
friend. If someone says that pledges are there between disciple and Guru, then he 
means that the disciple is correctly relying on him. There is a rather rec ent commentary 
on the Lama Chöpa by His Holiness the Dalai Lama that exists on CD, and I think it is 
available in English as well. There His Holiness is explaining in detail about how to 
rely on the spiritual friend.  
 Remember when I told you about how Dr omtönpa asked Atisha why the 
practitioners in Tibet didn‟t achieve any realisations, and Atisha told him it was 
because they didn‟t correctly rely on their Guru. Therefore, you see how this practice 
of Guru yoga is of greatest importance.  When referring t o this practice, we are not 
just talking about reciting a text but really engaging into it. Concerning relying 
correctly in thought, it is expressed in the text that you mainly have to train in the root 
of faith, and how you have to remember the Lama‟s kin dness and generate respect 
towards him. It doesn‟t talk about reading this or that book or reciting some text. This 
is neither said here or in the Bodhisattvacaryavatara , or in any of the Mind Training 
(Lojong)  texts.  
 
QUESTION:  What about someone who studies the dharma from books and has quite 
some faith in the Buddha and the dharma, and tries to practice earnestly, but doesn‟t 
have a real teacher. Would there still be benefit from this?   
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 120 – week one to seven  ANSWER:  Yes, there is benefit from this kind of dharma practice, and also virtue will 
be accumulated and the persons knowledge of the dharma will increase. Since the first 
gate, i.e. the correct reliance on the spiritual friend has not been entered, also the other 
realisations will not come abou t. In the context of the Mahayana, for example, the first 
gate is Bodhicitta and without entering into it, you can‟t proceed to any other levels.  
 
QUESTION:   What actually is the meaning of realisation? Does it only refer to 
big topics like emptiness?  
ANSW ER: It means that something special has arisen in the continuum. For example 
Bodhicitta, Definite Emergence or Emptiness. It only talks about the good qualities of 
the dharma, for example generating pure faith in the Guru without any doubts.  
 
QUESTION:  Is doubt with regards to the Guru always a result of pride?   
ANSWER:  Actually, it doesn‟t refer so much to pride but to a lack of faith. Pride can 
be a cause for this lack, but there are also many other causes.    
 
QUESTION:   What should the disciple do if sh e disagrees with the teacher on a 
point?  
ANSWER:  In this situation, she should recall the kindness of her teacher and in that 
way try to restore her faith. In general though, if there is a valid point of disagreement, 
you would just directly tell the lama that you couldn‟t do a certain thing. If you have 
engaged into a relationship with a lama without having checked well before, and 
afterwards you see many faults in him that are actually present, then it is ok to say 
good bye to him and walk away. Examples would be if he sets you out on negative 
tasks like killing sentient beings, stealing, etc. this is even expressed in the Lam -Rim 
and we will come to it later.  
 
By focusing on the Guru‟s faults, obstacles for gaining any attainments will arise and 
therefor e you should make effort to get rid of this faultfinding. This is done by 
reflecting on the disadvantages of finding faults.    
 
5 The way of restraining concepts of faults  
If concepts of faults arise due to carelessness, a lot of afflictions, etc. you sho uld apply 
yourself to restraint through confession. If you accustom yourself in this manner, you 
may see that there are a few  faults, but since your mind holds on to the side of positive 
qualities, it will not become an obstacle to your confidence. For ins tance, the great 
J ow o upheld the view of the Madhyamaka, w hile Serlingpa upheld the view of the 
True Aspectarian Cittamatra which is w hy, in terms of view , one w as higher than the 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
121 other. How ever, as he discovered the general stages of the Mahayana path and  the 
mind of enlightenment relying on him, he regarded Serlingpa as unequalled among 
spiritual teachers.  
 
Due to a lack in conscientiousness or an overflow of afflictions you might end up 
thinking about the lama‟s faults. You would remedy this through conf ession and by 
restraining yourself from repeating it in the future. If you cultivate in this way, then 
you will be able to bring your mind back to the side of the Guru‟s qualities, and there 
will be no hindrance to your faith. This also should be understoo d from Atisha and his 
teacher. Atisha was not focusing on the fault of his teacher presented here, but on the 
supreme teachings he received from him. Through recalling the kindness of the Guru 
great respect should be generated for him.  
2B4A -1B3B -1B  Remem bering the kindness and being respectful  
Second: { 32}   
In the [Sutra of the] Ten Dharmas it says:  
He looks for me w ho has roamed in cyclic existence for a long time; he 
awakes me from a long time of obscuration and torpor due to ignorance; he 
pulls me out as I sink w ithin the ocean of existence; he show s good paths to 
me w ho has entered bad ones;  
he frees me w ho has been bound in the prison of existence;  
he is a doctor to me w ho has long been tormented by illness;  
I should generate the notion of [of him as] rain -clouds, pacifying me who has 
been ablaze w ith the fire of passion and so on.  
 
There is no one else coming to look for us in Samsara, and pulling us out of it except 
for our Guru. For a very long time we have been obscured by the darkness of 
ignorance and our wisdom was asleep, but form this sleep it is our teacher who is 
waking us up and clearing away the darkness. Cyclic Existence is referred to here as 
being like an ocean that we have been completely sucked into. It is our teacher who is 
rescuing us from the ocean where we‟ve been for so long, steering us clear of wrong 
paths and pointing us towards the right path. He is the one liberating us from this 
prison -like existence that we are chained to through karma and afflictions. For such a 
long time we have been sick and suffering from the afflictions, and it now that the 
Guru has shown us the remedy to this seemingly incurable disease. He is the great 
raincloud extinguishing the fire from which we are ablaze.  
 
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 122 – week one to seven  THURSDAY  4 MARCH  2010  - SESSIO N 2 
 
The Array of Stalks  [Sutra]  also says:  
‘These are my spiritual friends, expounders of Dharma,  
exhaustively teaching the qualities of all that is  
thoroughly teaching the conduct of bodhisattvas’,  
w ith these thoughts in mind I came to this place.  
‘As they give birth to all that, they are like my mother,  
as they give milk of virtues they are like nurses,  
they cleanse me completely by means of enlightenment’s branches.  
These spiritual friends completely exorcize harm,  
they are like doctors releasing from death and old age,  
show ering rain of nectar they are like Lord Indra,  
like the full moon they flourish w ith w hite qualities  
they show  the direction of peace, just like bright sunlight,  
regarding friends and foes they are like mountains,  
they have minds as undisturbed as the sea,  
they give perfect support, some say: “like boatmen”‘  
w ith this in mind, Sudhana, I came here.  
‘Bodhisattvas bring forth my understanding,  
Buddha’s children cause enlightenment  
these [beings, these] friends of mine are praised by Buddha’  
w ith such w holesome thoughts in mind I came here,  
‘As they save the w orld they are like heroes,  
they have become the captains, protectors and refuge,  
they are { 33}  the eye bestow ing happiness on me’  
w ith thoughts like these I honour my spiritual f riends.  
 
[The thoughts] expressed here should be remembered through a tune to go with the 
verses, substituting oneself for “Sudhana”.  
 
The line referring to milk of virtues  and the line before it are analogies to show how the 
lama brings us to all good qua lities. The showering rain of nectar  refers to dharma. The 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
123 white full moon  is a symbol for everything being present and complete, like the white 
qualities of the dharma in their fullness, being shown to us by the virtuous friend.  
The direction of peace  is to be understood as liberation, and the bright sunlight  can 
be understood as them being the clear (or bright) sunlight that clears away the 
darkness of our ignorance. The line referring to friends and foes  refers to us normally 
having dislike for our enem ies, and attachment to those close to us. The teacher‟s mind 
is not wavering between these two extremes but is stable like mountains  and undisturbed 
as the sea  (especially referring to not being disturbed by hate). They are teaching these 
states to us, and this should also be seen as that we ourselves are constantly going back 
and forth between attachment and anger, and enemies and friends, and it is the 
virtuou s friend who is showing the way to obtain a mind that is not wavering like, but 
stable as a mountain. The line that refers to like a boatman , seen in the bigger context, 
shows how the virtuous friends are teaching us about the mind of equanimity, with 
whic h we are fully able to support enemies and friends alike. A boatman will not make 
a distinction between people, and is willing to support anyone coming to him to cross 
the river.  
In the next line, the Tibetan says „by a mind like that Sudhana has come her e‟. I read 
this in the way that there is the young Bodhisattva, normally referred to as Sudhana, 
and he has come here through a mind free from anger and attachment. Or you could 
also say that through this mind, the Bodhisattva Sudhana has come about, meani ng 
that he generated bodhicitta and through this entered into the Mahayana. His story is 
expressing the great intensity of looking for a spiritual guide. Furthermore, it says, 
„producing the mind that I am a Bodhisattva.‟ This refers to someone who generat ed 
bodhicitta and therefore can say full of confidence in himself and his path that, “I am 
a Bodhisattva”. This has come about due to the guidance of his teacher.  The same 
confidence is being expressed in saying, „I am a son of the buddhas‟. “The one who is 
producing enlightenment” meaning, producing its causes, again refers to the spiritual 
friend.  
As a result of all this, the spiritual friend is really to be praised as a Buddha. Again, the 
one who set me on the Bodhisattva path so that I can now say tha t I am a bodhisattva, 
and that the causes for complete enlightenment is generated, has come about due to 
the teacher, and as a result, it is highly appropriate to praise him as being one with the 
buddha. These are the words of the Bodhisattva Sudhana, whic h he spoke in order to 
recall the kindness of his teacher.  
 
Through his kindness, we are protected from the lower realms and able to penetrate to 
the actual way of how things exist. He is the one who is clearly showing how to 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 124 – week one to seven  identify what is a happy or u nhappy path, and what good conduct is. One protecting 
all the world refers to someone who is protecting all the beings in the world, and this 
hero is our virtuous friend. This is similar to a hero protecting all the beings in his 
area. He is the captain, a  skilled guide, the one leading us to the city of omniscience. 
Nowadays you would probably talk about the pilot of an airplane! He is the eye, clearly 
showing us the path to happiness. The bodhisattva says that by this kind of mind, 
meaning a mind that is aware and mindful of all the kindness he is shown by the 
teacher, he is serving and showing respect.   
He puts all of this into these poetic words in order to bring about mindfulness of the 
Guru‟s kindness. He didn‟t just say, “oh, the Guru is kind”, but he  put it into eloquent 
words that would strongly remind himself of his kindness. As a result, we should also 
engage into the activity of being aware of the lama‟s kindness, and train in faith and 
respect in the way it is shown here to us by this bodhisattva .

The second one:  
In the Fifty [Verses about the] Guru it says:  
What need is there to say a lot of w ords here?  
do everything  to make your Guru happy  
give up anything that does not please him  
apply yourself to that, examine that!  
This w as said by Vajradhara himself:  
‘spiritual attainments follow  the Guru’,  
aware of this, use anything there is,  
and make your Guru totally[, utterly]  happy.  
 
In brief, you try hard to please him and to give up w hat he does not like.  
 
This is quite straightforward. What  it comes down to is that you should engage into 
anything that pleases your Guru, and give up anything that you can analyse and 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
125 discover displeases him. By understanding what is said here, one should try, through all 
possible methods, to please the Guru.  
 
2B4A -1B3B -2B Condensed explanation of the three means of pleasing [him]  
The three means of pleasing [the Guru] are (1) offering material things, (2) serving him 
and paying him respect w ith body and speech, and (3) practicing in accordance w ith his 
advice.  In this regard it says in the Ornament of the Sutras:  
Through homage and pure offerings and service  
and practice you should rely on a spiritual friend.  
 
2B4A -1B3B -2C The extensive explanation of those three  
1 Offering material things  
 
As regards the first  one it says in the Fifty [Verses]:  
Through things not normally given - your son and your w ife  
and through your life you should forever rely  
on the master of y our sacred commitments  
needless to say: through fickle possessions [as w ell].  
 
In this verse, our children and wife, and even our life and body are expressed as 
objects to offer our guru. This is also referred to in the Bodhisattvacharyavatara  where 
Shantideva talks about offering the body, which sounds like actually cutting it up an d 
giving it away. You should not physically do this, but it is done only in order to 
generate a mental determination. If you would give away these things, then there is no 
doubt that you should do the same with other resources as they would be insignifican t 
compared to one‟s life or children. The line that says not normally given  makes it clear 
that obviously your son or wife are not something to be offered away. Therefore, if in 
this context even these things, normally not given, are becoming suitable to b e offered 
to the guru, then the normally given offerings are definitely suitable.   
And: 
Giving that amounts to alw ays  
offering gifts to all the Buddhas.  
Offering that accumulates merit,  
w hich tur ns into the highest attainments .

  Page | 126 – week one to seven  2 Serving and paying respe ct with body and speech  
The second one [involves] bathing him, anointing him, massaging him, w rapping him 
up, cleaning him, nursing him w hen he is ill etc. and talking about his positive 
qualities.  
 
QUESTION:  Does one praise the Guru in his presence?   
ANSW ER: If Tharchin for example sees his Guru, Geshe Sönam Gyaltsen, and 
immediately starts to praise him, then Gen -la would probably think that Tharchin has 
lost it! It would be more appropriate to praise the Guru for example in long life pujas 
or when about him to others.   
 
3 Practicing in accordance with his advice  
The third one [consists in] { 34}  practicing without going against the instructions, this 
being the main one. In the J ataka stories it says:  
The offering to be made in return for his help,  
is prac tice in accordance w ith his instructions.  
 
This is the main form of relying through action. If you receive instructions and then 
practice in accordance with them, then your practice becomes the supreme form of 
offerings. At this point in the Lam -Rim Chenmo  the point comes up about how 
inappropriate it would be to follows a teacher‟s instruction if it was obviously wrong to 
do. An example could be that the lama that it would be inappropriate for you to stay as 
a monk or that it would be better to disrobe or not to guard the three types of vows. 
You would not follow such advice, and this is also expressed in the vinaya itself where 
it says that if someone sets you on doing something wrong, then you should abandon 
this person.   
 
2B4A -1B4 The benefits of relian ce 
A Benefits such as one’s approaching the state of a buddha  
Fourth: It says [in the scriptures] that you w ill approach the state of a buddha, that 
the Jinas w ill rejoice, that you w ill not be deprived of virtuous spiritual friends, that 
you w ill not fall  to the low er realms, and that you will not easily succumb to bad 
karma and afflictions. As you do not transgress the conduct of bodhisattvas, 
[remaining] mindful of it, your accumulation of good qualities w ill grow  higher and 
higher and all your temporary  and ultimate goals w ill be accomplished.  
 
Not succumbing to bad karma and afflictions  refers to that you will be able to do the 
difficult.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
127  
B Bad karma is exhausted and [the benefit] exceeds even that of offerings to numerous Buddhas  
Furthermore, through  serving and paying respect to the spiritual teacher, the karmas 
for experiencing the low er realms is exhausted directly in this lifetime through only 
slight harm to body and mind or experiences in dreams. It is said that there are 
enormous benefits that o utshine the roots of virtue of making offerings etc. to 
innumerable Buddhas.  
 
In this part, two points are presented in one. The first one, that bad karma is 
exhausted, is very important to know. Through serving the virtuous friend, evil 
negativities that  would have ripened as strong sufferings, like a rebirth in hell, are 
transformed into smaller sufferings like nightmares or small aches. By experiencing 
these small situations, a greater negativity is purified. Such is the outcome of serving 
and showing r espect to the Guru. The second point is that the roots of virtue coming 
from innumerable offerings to the Buddha are being outshone by the virtue coming 
from reliance on the Guru.  
After seeing the benefits, which are almost unfathomable, now we will look a t what 
happens if you rely in an unsuitable way. If you rely in the wrong way, then great 
danger can come about.

2B4A -1B5 The drawbacks of not relying  
A The way it ruins this life and future ones  
Fifth: If you make s omeone your spiritual teacher and then back off in your reliance on 
him, a lot of illnesses and negative forces w ill cause harm in this life, and in future lives 
you w ill have to experience the immeasurable sufferings of the low er realms. In the Fifty 
[Verses]  it says:  
Do not ever agitate the minds  
of your masters - not in any w ay,  
if you do so due to some delusion  
you w ill necessarily roast in hell.  
It has been explained authentically,  
that all those w ho disparage their masters,  
w ill be dw elling in horrific hells - 
such as the Avici - that w ere taught.

Whoever hears a single verse of Dharma,  
and does not perceive the spiritual teacher ,  
w ill be bor n among dogs a hundred times,  
and afterw ards as someone of low  cast.  
 
B No positive qualities are attained and earlier ones deteriorate  
Apart from that positive qualities that have not arisen do not arise and those that 
have deteriorate and vanish.  
 
C The disadvantages of relying on unwholesome spiritual friends and bad companions  
If you rely on unw holesome spiritual friends and bad companions, again, positive 
qualities diminish, faults increase and there w ill be undesirable results. Therefore it 
w as taught  that this should be avoided in every w ay.  
 
 
 
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
129 2B4A -1B6 Summary of the meaning of these [points]  
A The importance of proper reliance on the root of perpetual striving  
Sixth: That being so, the instructions widely know n as Guru yoga should also be 
understoo d according to what has been explained before. It w ill not be enough to do 
the visualisation in just one meditation session, though. When you do one Dharma 
practice from the bottom of your heart, you should long rely on a spiritual teacher w ho 
guides you u nmistakenly. As Chekaw a also said about that time span: “w hen you rely 
on a Guru, there will be doubtful questions w hether to give up”. Accordingly, since 
there w ill only be loss w ithout profit if you do not know  how  to rely, it becomes evident 
that the cy cles of teachings about relying on a spiritual teacher are more important 
than any other ones and the root of perpetual striving.  
 
Up to now, the way of relying on the virtuous friend has been explained, and all of this 
should be applied in the practice of  guru yoga. In other words, what has been 
explained up to now is the meaning of guru yoga. Doing just one session of this 
practice, or rarely doing it is not enough. If you really have the sincere wish to engage 
into dharma practice, then you have to const antly rely on a virtuous friend who will 
guide you along an unmistaken path.  
 The quote by Geshe Chekawa is very terse in the Tibetan. “When you rely on 
the Guru there are abandonments and doubts”. This means that if you rely in the 
correct way, you will be able to attain all the good qualities of abandonment, turn away 
all the faulty aspects and clear away doubts. In this correct way of relying, you first 
have to understand what is the correct way. If you don‟t know this, then there will only 
be loss. The refore, you first have to come to know through study this cycle of dharma 
teachings concerning the point of how to rely. Once you have studied this you can 
steadily practice in accordance with it, and then slowly the fruitions will come about. 
Other people  who want quick results, and jump into the practice of guru devotion 
without previous studies, won‟t get any result. As there are these benefits in relying in 
the correct way, these instructions of how to rely become the most important dharma 
instructions,  and this way of relying will be a base for constant aspiration.  
 This cycle of dharma teachings can become “the root of all aspirations” being 
translated here as the root of strong resolves, but I read it as becoming the root of all 
attainments, or the ro ot of all good dharma qualities. However, just wishing for them 
without correct reliance won‟t make them come about.  
 
B The need for confession and vows, if that proper reliance does not come about - because it is a 
great fault  
With our coarse mental afflictions w e have often allow ed many faults of reliance on a 
spiritual teacher to occur in regard to listening to the Dharma - not know ing how to 
rely or know ing how  to but not doing so. It is difficult for an awareness of that to 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 130 – week one to seven  develop through confession and vow s. { 36}  Having understood the advantages and 
disadvantages as explained previously you should therefore sincerely confess any 
occurrences that are not in accordance w ith proper reliance, developing many strong 
resolves.  
 
There are two groups of people, one doesn‟t even know how to rely, and the other 
knows how to rely but doesn‟t act accordingly. They already received teachings from 
many lamas but accumulated a lot of misdeeds because they haven‟t correctly relied on 
them, even though they were listening to their teachings. We need to purify these kinds 
of negativities and practice restraint in order to protect us from future ones. 
Furthermore, we are urged to study what has been previously explained so we come to 
know what the drawbacks of not relying, or relying in a wrong way. If we are unable to 
bring forth the correct way of reliance, then from the bottom of our heart we should 
frequently engage into purification and restraint (binding ourselves by vow with 
regards to th e future).  
 
C How the realization of reliance is not far away, if you act in that manner  
If you act in that manner, you w ill before too long become like the bodhisattva 
Sadaprarudita and the young Sudhana w ho w as insatiable in his quest of a spiritual 
teacher.  
 
Sadaprarudita  is the bodhisattva whom we have already encountered in the 
Bodhisattvacharyavatara, and is also called “bodhisattva continuously crying”.  
 Now we come to a large new part of the text...

131 2B4A -2A1 What to do during the meditation session itself

2B4A -2A1A  What to do in preparation  
1 [Cleaning your dwelling and setting up representations of body, speech and mind]  
2 [Laying out offerings]  
3 [Sitting posture and mental attitude]  
4 [Visualiazation of the merit field]

First: the six preparatory practices:  
2B4A -2A1A -1 [Cleaning your dwelling and  setting up representations of body, speech and mind]  
Follow ing the life story of Serlingpa you should clean your dw elling and set up 
representations of the exalted body, speech and mind.  
 
2B4A -2A1A -2 [Laying out offerings]  
Beautifully lay out an arrangeme nt of offerings that w ere honestly acquired.  
 
2B4A -2A1A -3 [Sitting posture and mental attitude]  
On a comfortable seat straighten up your body. Assume a suitable position in cross -
legged or half cross -legged posture making sure that the mind is fused w ith g oing for 
refuge and bodhicitta.  
 
2B4A -2A1A -4 [Visualisation of the merit field]  
In the space in front of you should imagine that there abide the lineage lamas of vast 
conduct and profound view  and countless buddhas, bodhisattvas, arhats, arya hearers, 
and pratyekabuddhas along w ith their attendants, [thus] visualising the merit field.  
 
Here we touch on the Merit Field as presented in the Lam -Rim. It is the same as the 
Merit Field used in the Lama Chöpa. There you have the lineage of the Vast Practice, 
start ing with Maitreya and going on to Asanga and Vasubandhu. On the left side there 
are the masters of the lineage of the Profound View, starting with the protector 
Manjushri, going on to Nagarjuna, Arya Asanga, Aryadeva, and further to Chandrakirti 
and so on.  At the back is the lineage of the Blessed Practice and in front, surrounding 
them are all the buddhas, yidams of the four types of tantra, bodhisattvas, 
pratyekabuddhas, hearers, dharma protectors and so on. In the middle is Lama 
Tsongkhapa, or more preci sely, Lama Losang Thubwang Dorje Chang. He is to be 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 132 – week one to seven  identified as your actual kind root Guru. You visualise him and all these in front of 
you. All the worthy objects of refuge are assembled here in front of you, and in order 
to accumulate merit you go for refuge in them.  
Through visualising all the teachers, the accumulation of merits from this is the 
conducive condition for the arising of the path in your continuum, and the clearing of 
adverse conditions through purifying negativities. Just by sitting cross -legged in fron t 
of this Merit Field, you won‟t achieve anything, but you have to engage in the practice 
of the two accumulations and engage into purification of the adverse conditions. This 
is especially important for us beginners. The essential points of these two prac tices are 
brought together in the practice of the Seven Branches (or limbs). Activities like 
cleaning the assembly hall, offering water bowls, together with the right motivation, 
already become causes by accumulating merits and purifying negativities. Ther efore, we 
should do these things very carefully. Even while talking with others, if we use very 
kind and positive words, and especially if it is of benefit for the other person, then this 
is accumulating virtue by the way of your speech. This is something that you should do 
continuously on a daily basis, rather than delay them or put them to one side.  
The purification practice is to be seen especially in connection with having obstacles to 
generating the realisations of correctly relying on the virtuous fr iend. These obstacles 
come about through not reflecting in the correct way on his kindness, or not 
generating respect or following his instructions. Due to this, we accumulated 
negativities and because of this, the realisation of correct reliance didn‟t co me about 
yet. Therefore, we especially need to purify these obstacles.  
 
 
FRIDAY  5 MARCH  2010  - SESSION  1 
 
QUESTIONS  
We have read about practicing guru devotion by relying in thought, in action, and even 
with guru yoga – but if we really want to gain attain ments we wouldn‟t do this only 
once, but for a prolonged period of time.  
 If we think, “How should I see the Buddha?” - We should see him as the guru.  
QUESTION:   To take someone as your guru, do you need to have personally met them 
or have been present a t their teachings?  
GEN:   With the lineage lamas, like the gurus of your guru, or the gurus of their gurus 
going all the way back to Lama Tsongkhapa and Buddha Shakyamuni, you would rely 
on them without having met them. However, with the guru of this life, you need to 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
133 have met them and hear the dharma from them. Also in our text, it says you need to 
rely on them for a long time and receive the nectar of the teachings from them.  
QUESTION:   What about if you hear their teachings on DVD and you take them 
as you r guru - but haven‟t actually met them?  
GEN:   In this case, the person would not become your guru. You need to meet them 
directly, listen to their teachings and request them, and in this way, the person would 
become your guru.  
QUESTION:   Is it enough to g o and listen to the teachings or do you have to 
request the teachings yourself?  
GEN:  In general, someone else has already made the request for teachings. If you then 
go and listen, then this is sufficient. If you listen with the unmistaken discrimination 
that this person is my guru, then the connection is made.  
 We have to remember the topic of the suitable disciple and their three qualities. 
Then we have to find a guru that has characteristics of the three categories: highest, 
middle and minimum. After th is, you would go to their teachings and think, “this 
person is my qualified guru and from them I want to take the nectar of the Mahayana 
Dharma”. Then by listening to this teaching, they become your guru. A mere morning 
session would be enough to generate this thought that the person is your guru. 
Actually, even one verse would be enough.  
Someone might go to many teachings from the same person but doesn‟t have this 
attitude. Even though he has listened to many teachings from them, this person does 
not beco me their guru.  
QUESTION:   How is this verse to be u nderstood where it says if one does not 
perceive the spiritual teacher, will be born among dogs a hundred times ? [root text p27 -28]  
GEN:   This is someone who has generated faith in a lama and wants to hear the 
teachings, maybe even requested the lama to be their guru in person. With faith he 
goes to the teachings, but then sees faults with the guru – this is the situation referred 
to here.   
 In the Lam-Rim Chenmo , it says one first generates strong faith, and then if one 
takes teachings from a guru, even just one verse - then you‟ve already chosen this 
person as your guru. If you then generate doubts and follow these doubts, you create 
many  problems for yourself.  
 We first need to check the guru. There is a saying, “do not choose your guru like 
a dog runs after meat.” After meeting a teacher, we shouldn‟t immediately take them as 
our guru, but we should investigate.  
 We need to check their  outer and inner aspects. Outer aspects are their 
behaviour of body and speech. Inner aspects are their thoughts and opinions.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 134 – week one to seven  QUESTION:   How do we investigate a lama if we are told to not see their faults?  
GEN:   We need to do both. First, we need to anal yse their qualities and faults, then 
when we‟ve decided they are our guru, we should only see them as a fully enlightened 
being.  
QUESTION:   But not these two at the same time?  
GEN:   When we are seeing them as a Buddha, we do not look for their faults. The re 
are only qualities because they are a Buddha.   
QUESTION:   In the quote, spiritual attainments follow the guru  [root text page 26]  – who 
is Vajradhara referring to?    
GEN:   There‟s only on e Vajradhara. It is your guru. He is not to be seen separate from 
your guru, if we see the guru as separate from Vajradhara then something has gone 
wrong.  
 If we think Vajradhara or the Buddha is a statue and our guru is an ordinary 
being, then no realisations will follow. This is how we are to understand this phrase, 
Lama Losang Thubwang Dorje Chang . 
 In this phrase, Lama  refers to our own guru. Losang  refers to Lama Tsongkhapa 
and Thubwang  to the Buddha – the powerful Muni. Dorje Chang  is the Tibetan for 
Vajradhara. We should see all these as one continuum.  
QUESTION:  When talking about the merit field, Gen -la said we had to visualise Lama 
Losang Thubwang Dorje Chang, but in the guru yoga book by Kyabje Zopa Rinpoche 
we are to visualise Vajradhara. Which one should we visualise?   
GEN:   The meaning is always the same – whatever you are visualising, it is your guru 
in that aspect. If you visualise Vajradhara, it is your guru in the aspect of Vajradhara. If 
you visualise Lama Losang Thubwang Dorje Chang, it is your guru as Lama Losang 
Thub wang Dorje Chang. It is the same for a Chenrezig practice, it is your guru who is 
present, or a Tara practice - it is your guru. Only the aspect changes according to the 
context. In the Lam -Rim you visualise your guru as Lama Losang Thubwang Dorje 
Chang. I n the six -session guru yoga, which is a tantric practice, you visualise your guru 
as Vajradhara.  
QUESTION:  In the Lam-Rim context, would Lama Losang Thubwang Dorje Chang 
have the face of your guru?  
GEN:   If you can, this is the best way.  If you don‟t visualise the face of your own 
guru, then you‟d visualise the face of someone else. Why would you use the face of 
someone else?  
QUESTION:   When I‟m trying to visualise the merit field, I‟m not sure which one to 
use for the Lam-Rim teachings. One version ha s just Buddha Shakyamuni in the 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
135 middle, which I understood was more to do with Lam-Rim. Another has Lama Losang 
Thubwang Dorje Chang, which I understood was from a Tantric perspective.   
GEN:   You can choose either.  
QUESTION:   Outside of the mediation ses sion, when we see the guru, are we to 
visualise them as a Buddha (or deity) or is it more a recognition in the mind of them 
being a Buddha in ordinary form?    
GEN:   If you have the thought, “this person is my kind root guru and a Buddha” - this 
is enough without having to visualise. If you‟ve come far enough to have this 
discrimination, that they are an actual Buddha, this very thought is the realisation of 
guru devotion.  
QUESTION:  When doing guru yoga, is it possible to visualise another guru who is not 
your root guru in the primary position to increase your faith in the other guru?  
GEN:   You don‟t need to swap with other gurus, instead you should think all the gurus 
are one nature or one continuum. Apart from the different aspects, they are all one 
guru.   
QUESTION:  What is the difference between seeing the guru as Vajradhara and seeing 
the guru as an ambassador of Vajradhara, which Gen -la mentioned last year?  
GEN:   To see the guru as a replacement of Vajradhara (ambassador means 
replacement in Tibetan) i s like thinking, “I do not have the karma to view the 
Vajradhara aspect directly, but I do see this guru aspect.” Apart from this guru aspect 
of Vajradhara, there is no other Vajradhara to be found. If we talk about an 
ambassador, or replacement for the go vernment – for you there is no government 
apart from the ambassador. This is the one you have to rely on. It comes down to the 
same, this guru is Vajradhara, and the replacement of Vajradhara is Vajradhara. 
Perhaps the analogy is inadequate here.  
 First o ne thinks, “does such a thing as Vajradhara exist?” If yes, then how can 
you encounter Vajradhara? The only way for me, is through the guru, who is 
Vajradhara.  
To actually meet Vajradhara you need to prepare, you need a visa, this you can get 
from the amb assador of Vajradhara! The money and the flight -ticket you need to get 
yourself.  
QUESTION:  Does the focal object stay the same before you see them as a Buddha and 
after you see them as a Buddha?  
GEN:   There is a change, in the first situation you don‟t nee d to see them as a Buddha, 
in the second you do. In the first situation, there is no way to receive the blessings of 
the Vajradhara. In the second situation there is.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 136 – week one to seven  QUESTION:   Are the guru and Vajradhara of one substance, and therefore both 
appear to a direct perceiver?     
GEN:   In Prasangika system there is no substance, tzä (rdzas)  in Tibetan – everything is 
merely imputed by thought.  
TRANSLATOR:  The word substance I‟m translating is the same one we use when 
talking about the Cittamatrins (Mind -Only  school).  
GEN:  This substance, which is posited by Svatantrika Madhyamika and below, is 
posited in the Cittamatrin context as the valid sense perception and its object are of 
one substance, is not accepted by Prasangika Madhyamika.  
QUESTION:   Does the gu ru actually have to have the realisation of Vajradhara? 
Kyabje Zopa Rinpoche says they don‟t have to; it‟s more to do with your faith that 
they are Vajradhara, whereas they might not actually be.    
GEN:   It‟s not about whether they have attained the state  of Vajradhara or not. We 
have seen several mentions in our text concerning the faults of the guru, that he might 
have faults. From our side, we want to see them as Vajradhara because we definitely 
don‟t want to see their faults, we only want to see their good qualities. Just like 
Atisha‟s guru Serlingpa had a lower view, but Atisha didn‟t refer to this point, he only 
looked at Serlingpa‟s good qualities. He was seeing him as Vajradhara.  
QUESTION:  If two people go to the same teaching, one sees the teacher  as a Buddha, 
and the other does not, would one person have a valid consciousness and the other 
have a wrong consciousness?  
GEN:   That one of these two minds is valid or not depends on the object. If the 
teacher is Vajradhara and you have a discrimination of this – it is valid. If not, then it 
is not valid. Here we have to investigate. It‟s not that all discernments have to be valid. 
This is more about visualisation - which is why faith is mentioned. If it is valid, then it 
is established by valid cognition , and as a result, there is no need to talk about faith 
because you know.  
 If you know this glasses case is something to put glasses in, then you‟ve 
understood this with valid cognition. If someone comes along and say, “oh, this isn‟t a 
glasses case”, the n apart from having compassion for them, you will not waver from 
your understanding. If someone has a valid cognition of the guru being Vajradhara, 
then it is established, it has nothing to do with visualisations or faith. Even if this 
person kills fish in  front of you, you will never waver from this because it is 
established with valid cognition. This is a very high state, at our level were talking 
about visualisations and discerning things in a certain way. We wouldn‟t refer to our 
view as a valid cogniti on, or even a wrong consciousness, but more like a correct 
assumption (from the seven -fold division of mind ).  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
137 FRIDAY  5 MARCH  2010  - SESSION  2 
The preparatory practices we looked at yesterday were cleaning the place where you are 
practicing, setting up representations of the exalted body, speech and mind (according 
to the tradition of Serlingpa), and then you would lay out beautiful and honestly 
acquired offerings. You would also have the correct attitude, rather than for it to look 
impressive and to be praised. Then the sitting posture - sitting comfortably upright, 
trying to do the full lotus or half lotus. There is also an explanation of the se ven points 
of Vairochana . Then one needs to go for refuge and generate the bodhimind from the 
bottom of one‟s heart.  
 We then visualise the merit field, or our own lama in front of us plus the linage 
lamas, buddhas and bodhisattvas, aryas, hearers and th eir attendants and so on, as well 
as limitless lamas being present. This field is for the practices of accumulation and 
purification.  
 For this path to arise, we need the conducive conditions. This is the 
accumulation, and to get rid of the adverse condit ions, the obstacles, we need to 
purify.  
 There are the six practices we need to do in preparation, and these can be 
presented in two ways. The fourth practice is visualisation of the merit field, the fifth 
is accumulation and the sixth is purification. Al ternatively, accumulation and 
purification are the fifth point and offering a Mandala the sixth. In the 
Bodhisattvacharyavatara there is no mention of making a Mandala offering, so there 
the fifth would be accumulation and the sixth purification.  
 Next on e engages into the seven limbs, which Lama Tsongkhapa explains in 
detail. This refers to the King of Prayers, the prayer of Samantabhadra.
