2B4A -2A1A -5B1A  [The limb of prostration]  
1 [Prostration combining the three doors]  
2 [Prostrations of the three individual doors]  
2B4A -2A1A -5B1A -1 [Prostration combining the three doors]  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 138 – week one to seven  { 37}  With regard to the limb of prostration, the prostration combin ing the three doors 
is [set out] in the single stanza “Whoever...” This is not [prostration] to the w orld 
spheres of one direction and to the buddhas of one time. Rather, focusing on all the 
J inas residing in the 10 directions and three times, you prostrat e body, speech and 
mind to them w ith respect and from the bottom of y our heart.  
 
[The first verse of the King of Prayers]  
You lions among humans,  
Gone to freedom in the present, past and future  
In the worlds of ten directions,  
To all of you, with body, speech, and sincere mind, I bow down.  
 
When you do these prostrations, you don‟t just refer to this moment in time, or to just 
one area, but to all the limitless buddhas in the three times and ten directions. You 
prostrate with sincere respect with your th ree doors.  
 If prostrating to a single Buddha accumulates extensive merits, what about 
prostrating to all the Buddhas? - this brings limitless, unimaginable merit. When we do 
prostrations here in the gompa, we should prostrate to all the limitless buddhas in the 
three times and ten directions.  
 Then follows three types of prostrations.  
2B4A -2A1A -5B1A -2 [The prostrations of the three individual doors]  
A [Physical prostration]  
 
As regards the prostrations of the three individual  doors, there are three points : 
Physical prostration [is set out in] the single stanza “excellent behaviour...” You focus 
on all the Jinas belonging to the directions and times as the objects of your mind, as 
though you directly perceive them. Emanating your own body in manifestations as 
numerous as fine particles you should prostrate. Furthermore, having generated the 
pow er of confidence in the excellent conduct of the objects, you are motivated by that.  
 
With the energy of aspiration for the bodhisattva way,  
With a sense of deep resp ect,  
And with as many bodies as atoms of the world,  
To all you buddhas visualized as real, I bow down.  
 
This verse mentions bodies as many as atoms of the world. The number of atoms in 
the world is something beyond our comprehension. With this amount of bodies that 
we‟ve visualised, we do prostrations to the three times and ten directions. Just one 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
139 body brings limitless merits, so visualising prostrating with bodies equal in number to 
atoms of the world will bring unimaginable merit.  
 If we prostrate wit h a mind of great faith to all the conquerors in the three times 
and ten directions, try to imagine the amount of merit we create!  
 
B [Mental prostration]  
Mental prostration [is set out in] the single stanza “on one fine particle...” - on top of 
each minut e particle buddhas as innumerable as dust particles reside encircled by 
bodhisattvas. You generate the resolute faith of recollecting their positive qualities.  
 
On every atom are buddhas numberless as atoms,  
Each amidst a host of bodhisattvas,  
And I am confident the sphere of all phenomena  
Is entirely filled with buddhas in this way.  
 
Here we try to become aware of the smallest particle, or atom. Then on this one atom 
are buddhas and bodhisattvas equal to all atoms, on just this one atom. Then we 
imagine that every atom is covered with buddhas and bodhisattvas in the same way. 
With a mind recalling all their good qualities, we prostrate.  
  
C [Verbal prostration]  
Verbal prostration [is set out in] the single stanza “praising those...” From each of the 
bodies innumerable heads emanate and from each of the heads again innumerable 
tongues w hich sing inexhaustible praises of the objects’ positive qualities to sw eet 
melodies. Here “melodies” are praises and their “branches”, that is their causes, are 
the tongues. The w ord “ocean” refers to a lot.  
 
With infinite oceans of praise for you,  
And oceans of sound from the aspects of my voice,  
I sing the breathtaking excellence of buddhas,  
And celebrate all of you gone to bliss.  
 
Here we bring forth continu ous inexhaustible praises to all the objects of prostration. 
Visualise your limitless bodies having limitless heads, and each of those limitless heads 
has limitless tongues. Using pleasing melodies, one then praises the objects of 
prostration.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 140 – week one to seven  The root te xt next explains three words in the Tibetan. Melody means praises or 
eulogies. Branches mean their causes, which are the tongues, and Ocean refers to being 
innumerable.  
 
2B4A -2A1A -5B1B  [Respectful pleasing offerings]  
 
1 [Surpassable offerings]  
2 [Unsurpa ssable offerings]  
 
2B4A -2A1A -5B1B -1 [Surpassable offerings]  
Surpassable offerings [are set out in] the tw o stanzas “the best flow ers...” The best 
flow ers { 38}  are marvellous specimens of divine and human flow ers. Garlands are 
many different kinds of flow er s mixed and strung together. Both together [refer to] all 
actual and imaginary flow ers. “Cymbals” are the sounds of traditional instruments 
and the like. “Scented unguent” is a mixture of fragrant perfumes. “Sublime parasols” 
are the finest of parasols. “B utter lamps” are fragrant, radiant lights from things like 
incense or butter as w ell as radiant jew el lights. “Incense” refers to combinations of 
fragrant substances and to a single one. “Sublime garments” are the finest of clothes. 
A “supreme scent” is wa ter and the like pervaded by a scent that fills the billion w orld -
systems w ith fragrance. The “vessels of pow der” are packaged pow ders of fragrant 
incense suitable for scattering and burning or the coloured sand pow der of a mandala 
in multiple layers w ide and high like Mount Meru. “Arrangement”, joined at the end 
to all the above, [means] a lot, decorated and variegated.  
 
Beautiful flowers and regal garlands,  
Sweet music, scented oils, and parasols,  
Sparkling lights and sublime incense,  
I offer to you victorious ones.  
 
Fine dress and fragrant perfumes,  
Sandalwood powder heaped high as Mount Meru,  
All wondrous offerings in spectacular array,  
I offer to you victorious ones.  
 
These are the surpassable offerings. Beautiful flowers  means worldly flowers o f gods and 
humans, the best in the universe. Garlands are individual flowers put on a string. We 
can offer actual flowers or visualise them. Actual flowers can be either real flowers or 
artificial ones. Sweet music  (lit. cymbals in Tibetan) is all kinds of  music from all kinds 
of instruments. Oils are mixtures of perfumes, especially oils you would use for 
massage or for the skin . Sparkling lights  (lit. butter lamps in Tibetan) means perfumed 
butter lamps that radiates bright lights and fragrances. Nowadays  we have beautiful 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
141 electric lights, so we can offer these. I don‟t think electric lights are also fragrant! 
Bright lights from jewels are jewels radiating their own light.  
 These cover all the things in the two verses from the King of Prayers . We can 
visua lise these and make actual offerings. It‟s good to offer actual things like water, 
flowers etc. Whilst we can buy and offer these, we can also use the mudras ( arghyam, 
padyam etc ) – but best is to have actual offerings. Through visualising the offerings, y ou 
can make them far more elaborate and precious than you can buy or prepare. In the 
second chapter of the Bodhisattvacharyavatara , the master Shantideva makes a much 
more extensive list of offerings.  
 
2B4A -2A1A -5B1B -2 [Unsurpassable offerings]  
A The actu al [offerings]  
Unsurpassable offerings [are set out in] the single stanza “w hatever offering ...” - the 
surpassable ones are offerings of w orldly beings, therefore this here is [about] everything 
good that is emanated by pow erful beings such as bodhisattva s.  
 
With transcendent offerings peerless and vast,  
With profound admiration for all the buddhas,  
With strength of conviction in the bodhisattva way,  
I offer and bow down to all victorious ones.  
 
Surpassable offerings are those made by worldly beings. Now we look at unsurpassable 
offerings, which are those made by bodhisattvas who have great power and emanate 
many excellent offerings. We imagine we make offerings just like them. This is similar 
to the dedication prayer:  
 
Just as the brave Manjushri and Samantabhadra too  
Realized things as they are,  
Also I dedicate all these merits in the best way,  
That I may follow their perfect example.  
 
Here we want to dedicate as the bodhisattvas do, because we haven‟t reached a level 
where we can offer like they do. We think, “I also want to dedicate like them, with 
their great power of mind.” Just as the bodhisattvas bring forth all these emanations 
and excellent offerings to all the buddhas and bodhisattvas in all directions, so I too 
will make offerings in the same way.  
 In general, we do offerings before the great recitations, like Lama Chöpa, 
arranging the tsog etc., or before Sojong. These offerings should be clean and 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 142 – week one to seven  attractive, as these two aspects are very important. The offerings you make in your 
room sh ould also be clean and attractive. If you offer tea, make sure the cup is also 
clean or you are offering a dirty vessel to the buddhas and bodhisattvas. First clean the 
cup, pour the tea and then offer. Lamas often give the advice that we should make 
clean  and attractive offerings.  
 We shouldn‟t forget the context here. We first visualised the complete merit 
field with the feeling that all the gurus are present in front of us. This is who we 
prostrate to, accumulate merit with, and offer to. With this is mi nd, we should be 
careful our offerings are clean and attractive.  
 A second point here is about motivation and refers to the last two lines of this 
verse; with strength of conviction in the bodhisattva way, I offer and bow down to all victorious ones . 
Thes e two lines are to be applied to all the above verses.   
 
B The motivation for prostrations and offerings  
The last tw o lines are to be joined to all the previous [sets of] tw o in order to complete 
them. They indicate the motivation of prostrations and offe rings as w ell as the object.  
 
2B4A -2A1A -5B1C  [Confession of negative actions]  
Confession of negative actions [is set out in] the one stanza “desire ...” In dependence 
on the cause, the three poisons, and by means of the three bases, the body etc., their 
nature is that I have done them (that is, that I have actually created them), that I 
have made others create them, and that I { 39}  rejoiced in those created by others, all 
of w hich are generally subsumed under “w hatever I have done”. If through recollecting 
their faults, you regret those done previously and sincerely confess them with a mind of 
restraining from them in the future, the increase of deeds done earlier and the 
continuity of future occurrences w ill be severed.  
 
Every harmful action I have done  
With my body, speech, and mind  
Overwhelmed by attachment, anger, and confusion,  
All these I openly lay bare before you.  
 
Natural deeds are ones that come about without much intention. For example, a 
natural misdeed of the body would be walking from one pla ce to another, and killing 
many insects. We don‟t wish this, but it will happen and some small negativity is 
accumulated.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
143 This can also happen with speech, without intending to harm another person a 
negative word might slip out. Opposite to that would be a direct action coming from 
the intention to harm.  
 We did these actions of body and mind or we got someone else to, for example, 
we got them to steal or kill. We also rejoiced in someone else‟s negativity. In this case 
our own body and speech didn‟t enga ge in the act, we didn‟t get someone else to do it, 
but were happy they did it. For example, if someone was using strong words to 
someone else, and a friend would think, “Yes, that‟s quite good, I hope he says some 
more,” this would be accumulation of nega tivity by rejoicing in someone else‟s 
negativity.  
 With purification, we first recollect the drawbacks of these negativities. We 
become mindful that the effect is suffering and how we‟ll have to experience this 
suffering in the future. Then we regret those  actions we did in the past, and exercise 
restraint concerning negative actions in the future. With these minds of regret and 
restraint, we sincerely and honestly purify.  
 These are two of the four opponent powers. There is also the power of the basis 
and the power of applying all antidotes. These two stop or hinder the previously 
accumulated negativities. The power of regret and the power of restraint stop one 
engaging into new negatives in the future. The power of the basis refers to going for 
refuge in the three jewels, relating to all sentient beings with bodhicitta etc. The power 
of the antidote can be many things such as reflecting on emptiness, meditating on 
impermanence etc. These stop the ripening of past actions. The past action has already 
been d one, but we can work on stopping the effect.  
 
 
FRIDAY  5 MARCH  2010  - SESSION  3 
 
2B4A -2A1A -5B1D  [Rejoicing]  
Rejoicing [is set out in]  the single stanza “the J inas of the ten directions ...”  
As you recall the benefits of the five persons’ virtue, y ou develop joy.  
 
I lift up my heart and rejoice in all positive potential  
Of the buddhas and bodhisattvas in ten directions,  
Of solitary realizers, hearers still training, and those beyond,  
And of all ordinary beings.  
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 144 – week one to seven  Here one cultivates joy after having recalled the benefits and good qualities of the five 
beings. These are buddhas, bodhisattvas, solitary realisers, hearers (two types - those 
still training and those beyond) and ordinary beings.  
 
2B4A -2A1A -5B1E  [Requesting for the wheel of Dharma to be turned]  
Requesting for the w heel of Dharma to be turned [is set out in] the single stanza “in 
the ten directions ...” You exhort those w ho have aw oken to complete buddhahood in 
fields of the ten directions, are detached and have found unobstructed exalted 
know ledge, to teach the Dharma, emanating the same number of bodies for a long 
time. 
 
You who are the bright lights of worlds in ten directions,  
Who have attained a buddha„s omniscience through the stages of awakening,  
All you who are my guides,  
Please turn the supreme wheel of Dharma.  
 
Both the buddhas and bodhisattvas are requested to teach the Dharma. Specifically 
requesting those who are in meditative absorptions to come out of this state and teach 
sentient beings.  
 
2B4A -2A1A -5B1F  [Supplication]  
Supplication [is set out in] the single stanza “Show ing the passage into nirvana ...” 
You supplicate those w ho show  how to pass beyond sorrow  in fields of the ten 
directions to stay, not passing into nirvana for as many eons as there are particles of 
dust in the universe in order to bring about the benefit and happiness of migrating 
beings.  
 
With palms together I earnestly request:  
You who may actualize parinirvana,  
Please stay with us for aeons numberless as atoms of the world,  
For the happiness and well-being of all wanderers in samsara.  
 
We request those beings that could pass into parinirvana, not to do so, but to dwell 
here longer and teach to benefit sentient beings. We request them to stay for kalpas  
(eons) equal to the number of atoms of the w orld. One kalpa itself is already an 
immense length of time.  
 
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
145 2B4A -2A1A -5B1G  [Dedication]  
The limb of dedication [is set out in] the single stanza “Prostrations and ...” All the 
roots of virtue represented by the previous six limbs are made common [proper ty] of all 
sentient beings and never come to an end since they have been dedicated w ith strong 
determination to perfect enlightenment.  
 
Whatever slight positive potential I may have created,  
By paying homage, offering, and acknowledging my faults,  
Rejoic ing, and requesting that the buddhas stay and teach,  
I now dedicate all this for full awakening.  
 
From the previous six branches you accumulate virtue, and these roots of virtue you 
dedicate for all sentient beings to attain the state of omniscience. You can do this 
either by seeing yourself as being a representative of all sentient beings, and through 
your enlightenment bring all sentient beings to enlightenment. Alternatively, you can 
dedicate without thinking about yourself, and that only all other sent ient beings 
become enlightened.  
You should understand the meaning of these words, not letting your mind wander off, 
acting with great honesty. If you rely on what was taught, you accumulate a limitless 
amount of merit. This means that dedication is someth ing very important.  
 If you dedicate in the right way, then the merits become inexhaustible.  If you  
dedicate these merits to have a long life, healthy body or harmonious relationships, 
this is not a Dharma action. When we dedicate, it should be for beyo nd this life. If 
someone employs the small scope practices, they would dedicate the virtue for future 
lives to turn out well. This is already a vaster approach. Those of the middle scope 
would dedicate not just for future lives, but also for all the causes , karma and 
afflictions, to become completely pacified. Someone of the great scope would dedicate 
not just for that, but for the vast attitude of benefiting all sentient beings. If you are 
aiming at the Mahayana practices, then you are practicing in front of all the buddhas 
and bodhisattvas in the same way as they have practiced. To then dedicate to some 
trivial goal in front of all the bodhisattvas who do all this vast activity would be utterly 
shameful. We should dedicate just like the bodhisattvas do, in  the most extensive way 
for it to become a cause for oneself to become completely enlightened, so that all 
sentient beings can become fully enlightened.  
 
 
 
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 146 – week one to seven   Five of the seven limbs are in the category of accumulating merits.  
 Prostrating  
 Offering  
 Requesting  
 Supplicating  
 Rejoicing  
 
Confession is in the category of purifying obstacles. One aspect of rejoicing, 
rejoicing in one‟s own virtue, actually increases virtue. Through the dedication 
practice, you again accumulate merits. There is even purifica tion through this 
dedication. This means that even if you have practiced with a faulty motivation, at the 
end this fault can be purified by the dedication, if the dedication is done properly. 
Virtue, however small, is strongly increased by the practice of dedication. In addition, 
the type of positive actions which give in one moment a certain result and are 
exhausted, by proper dedication will never be exhausted.  
In short, the seven -limb prayer can be presented as the three aspects: accumulation, 
purificat ion and increasing - making things inexhaustible. This is a short way of 
presenting these practices. Five are to do with accumulation, confession is to do with 
purification, and this one form of rejoicing is to do with increasing.  
 Dedication strongly inc reases the virtue you accumulate; even a small virtue 
from a small practice can become inexhaustible through correct dedication.  
 This practice is very important, elder students will know these, and the younger 
ones should make effort to study them. Bring ing together accumulation and 
purification is very important for us.  If you ask what kind of practice should I do to 
practice Dharma? Just these seven limbs would be such a practice.  
 If we think of practicing Dharma, we shouldn‟t immediately think of re citing 
mantras or something like this. For a beginner, the way to practice is relying on these 
six practices before the session, and within that the seven -limbs to accumulate merits 
and purify obscurations.  
 Before the session, the first of these six pract ices is cleanliness - sweeping you 
room, but also having a clean body, and clean clothes for the practice etc. The Lam -
Rim already expressed that one who teaches has to wash and have clean clothes before 
he teaches the dharma. In Kriya Tantra (Action Tantr a) there is great emphasis on 
cleanliness and hygiene of the body, this would include practices such as Medicine 
Buddha.  
 We also looked at the defining characteristic of a spiritual friend, of the one 
who will rely (the disciple), and how one relies. I‟m repeating this so we don‟t forget 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
147 what was already explained. Then we had the benefits of relying on the spiritual friend, 
and the drawbacks of not relying on the spiritual friend.  
 In this context of how to support this objective, our meditation object, we have 
now arrived at the six dharmas. These six are called the six preparatory dharmas. In 
Tibetan this is jorbä (preparation) chö (Dharma) chug (six), or Jorchö for short. In the 
Jorchö puja, all these six preparatory practices are covered.  
  
2B4A -2A1A -5B2 The need to take the meaning of the words to heart  
If you thus develop an understanding of the meaning of those w ords and gently do as 
w as taught w ithout allow ing your mind to w ander, you w ill hold immeasurable heaps 
of merit.  
 
2B4A -2A1A -5C Categoriza tion of the individual [elements]  
Accordingly the five limbs prostrating, offering, requesting, { 40}  supplicating and 
rejoicing constitute the collection of accumulations. Confessing purifies obscurations, 
one aspect of rejoicing, the cultivation of joy at  one’s own virtue, multiplies the virtue. 
By means of dedicating the virtues of collecting, purifying and multiplying, how ever 
small, are multiplied manifold, and what w ould come to an end after producing a 
temporary effect is made inexhaustible. In brief,  there are the three: (1) accumulation, 
(2) purification, as w ell as (3) multiplication and making inexhaustible.  
 
2B4A -2A1A -6 [Mandala offering and request for blessings]  
Then, w ith a clear visualisation of the objects, offer a mandala and with strong 
aspiration supplicate many times: “I pray that all mistaken attitudes tow ards the 
spiritual teacher such as disrespect may cease and that all the non -mistaken attitudes 
tow ards him may come about w ith ease and I request blessings for a complete 
pacification of all outer and inner obstacles”.  
 
In our text, offering a Mandala is the sixth practice. You first clearly visualise all the 
aspects of the Mandala, and this you do with sincere requests – many times. You 
request blessings so the perverted attitudes like  disrespect to the guru and so on are 
stopped.  
And so on  means any kind of negativity that you did may be stopped. We request 
that whatever attitudes are conducive like being respectful to the guru and so on, are 
easily generated. We might request blessings until one has realised emptiness, or 
request blessings  until the bodhimind has been generated. Whatever mind you want to 
generate. With regard to external or internal obstacles like our afflictions, we request 
that these are completely pacified. Then by making these requests, very sincerely and 
many times ove r and over again. In this way, you offer the Mandala.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 148 – week one to seven   We can also say requests are made in order to receive blessings to prevent all 
perverted attitudes, starting with the disrespect to the guru up to self -cherishing and 
self-grasping minds, as well as a ll the positive states may easily be generated, from 
respect to the guru up to a mind that is cherishing others and a wisdom realising 
suchness.  
 Another type of request is to remove the outer interferences, which means 
interferences to the conducive circu mstances. Also to remove the inner obstacles, 
meaning obstacles to your body and mind. We request that these be utterly and 
immediately pacified.  
 
 
SATURDAY  6 MARCH  2010  - SESSION  1 
 
MEDITATION  
Before generating refuge, we should visualise the refuge field. In front sits our teacher 
Buddha Shakyamuni, surrounded by the lamas of the two main lineages. On his right 
side (our left) is Maitreya and the lineage of Extensive Deeds. On his left side (ou r 
right) is Manjushri and lineage lamas of the Profound View. In addition, all other 
objects worthy of respect surround the Buddha. In front of us, we visualise all those in 
whom we trust.  
 
  
 
Then reflect; those who possess the transcendental wisdoms, w hich is intelligence of 
omniscience, who found the state of dharmakaya – to all the buddhas I go for refuge. 
Those bodhisattvas relying on a wisdom understanding ultimate reality (ultimate 
bodhicitta) and conventional bodhicitta are also our objects of ref uge. Then those who 
have generated a wisdom directly realising emptiness, the hearers, are also our objects 
of refuge. We put our trust in these and go for refuge.  
 
  
 
Then we request blessings to easily pacify all the perverted states from disrespect to 
the guru onwards and any karma or obstacle in our mind up to the level of self -
cherishing and self -grasping. Then we request blessings that all the positive aspects 

unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
149 and qualities starting from correct reliance on the guru, up to the wisdom realising 
emptin ess may easily be generated.  
 
  
 
ADVICE  
To recap; We should repeatedly request that all the negativities coming from disrespect 
to the guru up to the most difficult forms of delusion may be pacified. This includes 
all afflictions and negative karma we ha ve accumulated from these afflictions, up to the 
self-cherishing and self -grasping minds. We request that these may be stopped. Then 
we repeatedly request that all the excellent minds starting from correct reliance on the 
guru, up to all the highest minds such as a wisdom directly realising emptiness, the 
ultimate bodhimind and the conventional bodhimind, may easily be generated.  
 We repeatedly make requests to the objects of refuge to pacify all the inner and 
outer obstacles that hinder engaging and succe ssfully completing Dharma practice.  
 
2B4A -2A1B  What to do during the main part  
1 How to sustain [meditation] in general  
2 How to sustain [meditation] in this context  
The second one has tw o points: how to sustain [meditation] in general and in this 
context.  
 
2B4A -2A1B -1  How to sustain [meditation] in general  
A Identification of and need for meditation  
First: Meditation on the path as it will be explained here serves to make you fit to 
make use of any virtuous object y ou w ish.  
 
This point answers th e question, what is mediation? In Tibetan, it means literally a 
“virtuous focus”. In general, in meditation we talk about the focus and the aspect. 
“Focus” is the virtuous object you are meditating on; the subjective aspect. First, we 
generate a virtuous m ind by using a virtuous motivation. Then one places the mind on 
the virtuous object and meditates on it. That is what enables you to place the mind on the 
virtuous focus, as you wish . This means through meditation and your virtuous motivation, 
you connect to the virtuous object and become more familiar with it, to the extent 
where it is impossible to separate from this object. This is what meditation is about.  
 Our situation is we are under the power of our mind, which has been under the 
power of the affli ctions since beginningless time, but what we want to do is reverse 

                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 150 – week one to seven  this so that we can have power over the mind. Because of this, we have been helplessly 
tossed around by the afflictions, and we have engaged in many negative actions that 
only bring the suf fering we want to avoid. We need to change this and become king of 
our mind. Now that we have become aware of how this process is functioning, we 
want to regain control over our mind, and as a result, achieve control over the 
afflictions.  
 Fit to make use  of means a state where you can use the mind as you wish, this is 
the goal of mediation – you order the mind to accomplish what you wish.  
 
B The faults of bad meditation  
Moreover, if you attend to this and that object, thinking to take merely this number of 
virtuous objects in any order you w ish, nothing w ill come of it and having gone w rong 
from the beginning, your spiritual practice w ill continue to be faulty throughout your 
life.  
 
In meditation, we need to follow the right order of virtuous objects. Yo u start with the 
correct way to rely on the guru. This is done by first training in faith, then practice 
with what you have been instructed to cultivate and so on, and then proceed through 
the whole path. We need to know this and generate a correct attitud e at the very 
beginning. Then all the positive qualities will follow. Without this correct motivation, 
you will not attain anything in this life.  
 We need to begin with establishing our motivation with great care. The small 
scope motivation aims for good results in future lives. The middle scope is aiming at 
liberation, and the motivation of the great scope is aiming at omniscience. One of 
these three motivations needs to be present, because without these, our practice is not 
Dharma. Therefore, we need to be careful with our motivation.  
 If you practice meditation without any of these three motivations, there will be 
results, but it will not be our Dharma practice. If you only practice for things in this 
life, then that‟s what you‟ll get. Maybe you will ge t a more relaxed mind in this life, but 
it does not become Dharma practice. In fact, it may be more effective just to go 
walking, running, or swimming and so on.  
 Engaging into Dharma practice means we are working on being the owner of 
our mind through the  afflictions becoming powerless; that they are ruled by our mind 
rather than the other way around. To go towards Dharma is working with the mind in 
order to decrease the affliction, and bring a change in the mind.  
 
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
151 C How to sustain good meditation  
Therefore, you should first of all develop certainty concerning the number and order of 
objects to be sustained, then you should unleash { 41}  a strong driving force that does 
not allow thoughts to arise other than in accordance w ith what has been ascertain ed, 
and sustain w ith mindfulness and alertness w hat has been ascertained w ithout 
omission nor addition.  
 
There are many virtuous focal objects and they need to be cultivated in the correct 
sequence. We need to develop certainty about knowing these objects and the sequence 
they need to be practiced.  
 We should generate this strong drive and think, “In the way I have understood 
these points about virtuous objects, I will now engage and not waver from 
understanding other objects.” Having understood the correct objects and sequence, 
you then engage in the m without distraction.  
 It is only through good mindfulness that we can place the mind on the virtuous 
object, and occasionally, the mental factor of introspection is needed to see if we have 
left anything out, or added anything in our mediation. These ar e two very important 
factors necessary for the success of our meditation.  
 We would place the mind on the virtuous object and sustain it with mindfulness, 
and if it remains there perfectly, we would continue with mindfulness. If introspection 
finds out the re is some deviation from the object, it can then be corrected. For 
example, if something unnecessary was added, you could cut it off, and if something is 
missing from the virtuous object, you again build the object up and hold it with 
mindfulness.  
 
 
2B4A -2A1B -2  How to sustain [the meditation] in this context  
A By thinking about the benefits of reliance and the faults of non -reliance  
Second: You should first think about the benefits of reliance and about the faults of 
non-reliance.  
 
Reflecting on the bene fits, you think how abiding in the correct way of relying on guru 
liberates you from samsara, whilst an incorrect way will lead to the lower realms. This 
can be explained in much more detail, with many benefits and drawbacks. Correct 
reliance on the guru i s the door out of samsara, but not correctly relying will only 
bring unwished -for results.  
 You reflect on the benefits like this; one thinks of the good qualities of the 
body, speech and mind of the guru, and from this generate faith, and from this faith , 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 152 – week one to seven  one generates a mind that recalls the kindness of the guru. If these two are present, 
faith and recalling the kindness, then a mind of respect to the guru can naturally arise. 
From these minds, one can quickly and easily bring about the two accumulations  and 
purify obscurations in the mind in order to bring about positive results. In this way, 
correct reliance brings all the positive states starting from faith in the guru, 
remembering their kindness, respect, all the way up to the highest attainments of a  
mind that cherishes others, and a mind that realises selflessness.  
 Faith is generated by reflecting on the benefits of correct reliance on the guru, 
and reflecting on the drawbacks of incorrect reliance. If you think about the benefits 
you will receive from correct reliance, you will obviously generate faith. This faith 
enables you to never look at the faults, but only see good qualities. This is the aim. If 
you perceive only qualities with your guru, there will be great benefit for you and you 
can proce ed to all the great states of mind along the path. However, if you only see 
faults with the guru, this is a cause for going to the lower realms.  
 One reflects on the benefits of correct reliance by thinking that the removal of 
even a single flaw in the co ntinuum of the disciple depends on correct reliance on the 
guru. The generation of even a single good quality in the disciple depends on correct 
reliance. By reflecting on these, you generate strong faith towards the guru.  
 If someone is sick, they would rely on the advice of the doctor and the medicine 
he proscribes in order to cure them. You want to take the medicine because you see 
the benefits; you see it is the cure for your sickness. If you don‟t recognise the advice 
and medicine as powerful, you‟ll just think, “Maybe this cures my sickness, maybe it 
doesn‟t. I don‟t know. Maybe it doesn‟t have the power to cure me.” We should think 
that with regard to our continuum, if we take this medicine, then my sickness is 
destroyed. Then one generates some conf idence in the medicine.  
 
B How to train in faith by means of thinking about [his]  positive qualities  
Then you repeatedly resolve  never to allow  your mind to get lost in conceiving faults 
in the guru and think of qualities like discipline and meditative stabilisation that you 
yourself have perceived [in him] , cultivating the faith that is a mind having the aspect 
of faith until it arises.  
 
In the Tibetan there is no mention of the word his in this outline, or in the actual text 
where it says in him . Thi s is a wrong translation of the text.  
Here you look into your mind and whatever qualities you have such as ethical 
discipline, whatever skill of concentration you have, or knowledge of the Dharma – all 
this has come from the kindness of the guru. Specific ally, after listening to the Dharma 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
153 explained to you by the guru. Any kind of realisation or positive quality in your mind 
has come from the kindness of the guru. Then faith is generated.  
 
 
SATURDAY  6 MARCH  2010  - SESSION  2 
 
C Training in the respect that  remembers kindness  
Then you should, in accordance with the above quotations from the sutra collection, 
consider the [instances of] beneficial kindness that you received and are still receiving 
and sincerely cultivate respect until it arises.  
 
Then we reflect on how we have received benefit from the guru in the past, and how 
we will receive benefit in the future. This again leads to generating strong faith. Our 
good qualities have come from what the guru taught us, and in the future we‟ll 
genera te more due to the guru. Reflecting on this kindness of how the guru helped us 
in the past, and will do so in the future, we then generate a stainless faith.  
 By relying on the guru, we have engaged into the six preparatory practices and 
have created grea t masses of virtue. You then dedicate to all the temporary and 
ultimate objects of your aspiration - that all outer and inner obstacles are pacified, and 
that one attains the omniscient state.

1 What should actually be do ne at the end

2B4A -2A1C -1 What should actually be done at the end  
Third: By means of the Prayer of Good Behaviour  and the Seventy about Aspiration

2B4A -2A1C -2 Times and duration of meditation  
A Indication of the times of meditation  
Thus you should meditate in four sessions - at daw n, in the morning, in the afternoon 
and in the evening.  
 
B How to meditate in t he beginning  
Moreover, if [your sessions are] long in the beginning it is easy to fall under the pow er 
of laxity and excitement. As it is difficult to correct this state of mind once you have 
got used to it, you should have many short sessions. If you brea k off the session  w hen 
there is still a desire to meditate, you w ill really w ant to enter meditation the next 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 154 – week one to seven  time, too. They say that otherw ise, you w ill be overcome by nausea at the sight of your 
practice place.  
 
One should have short sessions, because for beginners the danger with long sessions is 
that laxity and excitement will arise. As beginners, we should not force it and not make 
the sessions as long as possible.  Maybe a 20 -minute session and no more, then have a 
break, then maybe another 20 -minut e session and another break. The breaks could be 
about 10 -20 minutes. Then there will be a desire to come back to it. In this way, we 
can pass the day.  
 If sessions are too long, you might have a mind that does not even want to see 
the meditation place, y our cushion and so on – up to the point where you are repelled 
by seeing it. You avoid this by not having long sessions in the beginning.  
 
C How to sustain [the meditation] once it has stabilized a bit and advice on how to avoid 
exhaustion  
When it has sta bilized a bit, you should prolong the sessions, sustaining them all { 42}  
free from the faults of being excessively tense or relaxed. Thereby small obstacles, over -
exhaustion and drow siness w ill be pacified.  
Later, w hen the meditation gets more stable, then  you can increase the session 
duration.  
 
If you do meditation, there is no benefit in being too strict with yourself. Too relaxed 
is also of no benefit, otherwise when you want to meditate you‟ll just go for a walk!  
 If you go to the mountains to meditate and you bring a cell phone, it will mean 
that you make phone calls instead of meditation – this would also be too relaxed.  
 
2B4A -2A2 What to do during the periods between sessions  
A General practices enhancing the object [of meditation]  
B The causes of calm abiding and special insight  
 
Next is a very important instruction to take to heart, also because it comes personally 
from Lama Tsongkhapa. In general, there are many Dharma practices like prostrations, 
circumambulation and so on, but what we should make effort with is these actual 
sessions in cultivating the mind through meditating on the actual instructions.  
In the breaks between sessions, one should stay mindful of the object one is focusing 
on, rather than let the mind be distracted to all kinds of objects. Otherwise, there will 
only be a small outcome from the retreat. In the breaks, you should go back to the 
texts and read and study - in line with what you are meditating on. If you are doing a 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
155 retreat on guru devotion, the four noble truths, impermanence and so on, in the breaks 
look at books on these topics, then you become more and more mindful of the topic.  
 Here we are studying the Lam-Rim, and the way to do this is to only study this. 
You shouldn‟t read many other things on the side, but just repeatedly read this text. 
Some p eople might have read the text 50 times, and by doing this the text becomes 
imprinted on their mind and they come to really know the topic. Then after having 
understood this, they go to another text. After the Lam-Rim one might go to a Lojong 
text and comp are that with what is said there, what the differences are etc. If we go 
back to our rooms and read all kinds of other books, we‟ll come to the end of the 
module and not be able to say what was presented here, so we should be careful from 
the very beginnin g.  
 If we make effort this year, we will come to understand the Lam -Rim. If we 
come to know even one topic really well, and the way things are present in sequence, 
from this we‟ll understand all presentations. If we don‟t understand one presentation, 
we‟ll not understand any presentation. We have a real chance here to understand this 
text well. Back in our room, if we do all kinds of other recitations and so on, we 
should think, “this is what I want to study, and to memorise” and focus on just this 
text. T hen one can get a deep understanding, and from this, come to know all the 
other texts.  
 We all have instructions from our individual gurus, so a thought might come up 
that the guru‟s advice is more important than the Lam-Rim. We should never think like 
this. There is nothing more important than what is presented here in the Lam-Rim. If 
you want to increase your qualities, this is the topic you should practice.  
 We want to bring about the good quality of relying on the guru. To do this we 
need to bring abo ut the conditions, which are accumulating merits and purifying 
obstacles. To bring about some result of this topic, we have now studied this for a 
week, and in the weeks I‟m not here you should meditate on, discuss and explore the 
topic I‟ve presented.  
The basis of all is that one should frequently generate a mind of restraint regarding 
whatever discipline you have taken on. This attitude of ethical discipline is very 
important. Someone might have taken refuge, and with this there are vows. To the 
ordained  here, whatever level of discipline you have accepted, you need a mind to keep 
them as pure as possible through using a mind of restraint. With lay vows, bodhisattva 
vows, tantric vows, whatever level we have accepted, we should generate a mind of 
restrain t.  
 
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 156 – week one to seven   
2B4A -2A2A  General practices enhancing the object [of meditation]  
Generally speaking, there are numerous practices enhancing the object such as 
prostration, circumambulation and recitation, how ever, the main point is that, having 
made an effort at th e essence of the session, if, during the intervals w here you leave the 
session, you let go carelessly w ithout relying on mindfulness and introspection w ith 
respect to the observed object and aspect - w hich is the cause of sustaining [the 
meditation] - the outcome w ill be very little. Therefore, you should also be concerned 
w ith the Dharma [texts] teaching that [object] in betw een sessions, and recall it again 
and again. By various means you should accumulate the collection of conditions 
favourable to the ar ising of positive qualities, and by various means purify the adverse 
conditions, the obscurations. Your determination w ith respect to the discipline you 
have accepted, the basis of everything, should be reaffirmed a lot.  
 
2B4A -2A2B  The causes of calm abidi ng and special insight  
1 Controlling the sense doors  
2 Vigilant behaviour  
3 Knowing the right measure of food  
4 Exerting oneself not sleeping and practicing yoga, and what to do when lying down to 
sleep  
 
Apart from that, train in the collection of four cau ses for an easy arising of the paths 
of calm abiding and special insight, the four accumulations.  
 
 
2B4A -2A2B -1Controlling the sense doors  
In this regard: When the six consciousnesses arise in dependence upon objects and 
sense pow ers, after that attachment  and aversion arise tow ard the six pleasant and the 
six unpleasant objects of mental consciousness. Controlling the sense doors guards 
against their arising.  
 
The process explained here is how relying on the object and sense powers, that the six 
consciousn ess arise. At the end of these, a mental consciousness arises. The six objects 
are divided into the five physical (sights, smells, sounds, tastes and tactile objects), and 
phenomena, the object of the mental sense consciousness. From these six objects and 
the six sense powers comes the six consciousnesses, and at the end of these come this 
mental mind. This mental mind relates to six kinds of attractive objects and six types 
of unattractive objects and from this comes attachment and aversion. Attractive 
objects don‟t generate aversion, only attachment. Unattractive objects don‟t generate 
attachment, only aversion. As this comes in the mental mind from the previous sense 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
157 consciousnesses, one is instructed to guard one‟s senses so that these afflictions of 
attachment and aversion do not arise.  
 
STUDENT:   What are the six objects of mental consciousness?  
TRANSLATOR: In our translation there is a problem here because the words of 
mental consciousness sounds like they are only objects of the mental consciousness,  
but it actually refers to the six objects of form, sound, smell, taste, touch and 
phenomena.  
GEN:   In general, attachment and aversion is generated in the mental consciousness, 
not in the sense consciousness. There is nothing that is not an object of men tal 
consciousness. Both impermanent and permanent phenomena are objects of mental 
consciousness.  
If we look at the Heart Sutra, these negations are listed as no sight, no sound, no 
smell, no taste, no tangible object and no phenomenon - these are the six objects. 
Therefore, in regards to form, first we have a sense consciousness, and then comes this 
mental sense consciousness of that very form.  
You would say, “I saw something” – then you would be able to explain what you saw 
and talk about it, all this is  happening in the mental consciousness.  
 There is a consciousness called an awareness to which the object appears but is not 
ascertained ,   this mind says, “I don‟t know, something happened, so form or some 
colour was there but I don‟t know what it was.” There was no ascertainment of the 
appearance, but something did appear.  
 Another consciousness is called a mental direct perception , even in our continuum, 
an ordinary being. This is very subtle and very short, so in us it is necessarily an 
awareness to w hich the object appears but is not ascertained . This mind comes directly after the 
sense consciousness.  
 Controlling the sense doors refers to the need of guarding against the arising of 
attachment and aversion.  
 
2B4A -2A2B -2  Vigilant behavior  
Vigilant b ehaviour - in Engaging in Bodhisattva Behaviour  it says:  
To examine time and time again,  
the situation of one’s mind and body - 
put concisely that and only that  
is the sign of protective vigilance.  
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 158 – week one to seven  Follow ing this, w henever the body etc. engages in such an d such an action, you come 
to understand w hether or not it should be done and behave accordingly.  
 
Explained next is behaviour connected with introspection ( vigilant behaviour  in our 
text). When you engage with your body, speech or mind - then introspection can check 
what you are engaging in. Whilst you are engaging, it repeatedly checks if it is 
something virtuous or non -virtuous.  
When one is looking inwards, introspection im mediately knows what is going on. If we 
are engaging in a physical activity, then introspection knows immediately if it should 
be avoided or not. If we have a certain state of mind manifest, then introspection can 
immediately tell if it is something conduc ive or to be abandoned. The same applies if 
you are saying something, or are about to say something.  
 
2B4A -2A2B -3  Knowing the right measure of food  
Know ing the right measure of food means to give up eating too much or too little and 
to eat only the amount that does not do damage to one’s virtuous activities. 
Furthermore, having meditated upon the draw backs of craving for food, you should 
also remember  what is said in the scriptures about eating thinking that it should be 
w ithout mental afflictions, that it should be of benefit to the giver, { 43}  that you now  
bring together the micro -organisms of your body by means of material things and that 
in the fut ure you w ill bring them together by means of the Dharma, and that you w ill 
bring about the w elfare of all sentient beings.  
 
If you eat too much, or too little, this will be harmful to your Dharma practice. To 
overeat will be harmful, and if you eat too li ttle you will simply not be able to practice 
Dharma. You have to be wise with your food and take the correct measure.  
 By meditating on the drawbacks of craving for food, one tries to get rid of this 
constant occupation with it. We can think that from this  constant running after food 
we will harm our body. We will end up overeating and harming our bodies - reflecting 
on this drawback will help.  
 We also want to eat in a state free of afflictions; a mind that thinks of the 
kindness of the sponsor. The sponso r is the source of your food, and to them you 
generate a mind that wants to return this kindness.  
 Our body has many organisms living inside it. We imagine giving material things 
to them and think, “in the future, may I be able to gather them to me and gi ve them 
the Dharma.” Now we can give them food, but in the future we can give Dharma.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
159  We can also have the thought to eat in order to achieve the aims of all sentient 
beings. To do this we need to have a long life and stable body, so for that reason it i s 
good to eat to sustain yourself. With this attitude one eats.  
 
In the Letter to a Friend it also says:  
Reasoning that foodstuffs are like medicine  
Depend on them without attachment or hatred  
They are not meant for conceit nor for arrogance   
They are not  meant to fatten you - just keep you going . 
 
To have this outlook on the food as medicine means we are all suffering from aging 
and death, which food is preventing for the time being. With this view, one eats 
without attachment or aversion. It‟s not for yo u to become fat or to become puffed 
up, or to be especially impressive and strong, but just to keep you going.  Just keep you 
going means to be able to practice the Dharma. To bring about changes in the mind we 
need to live long and healthily.  
 This is a verse we should recite before meals.

STUDENT:   What does Seventy about Aspiration refer to? (root text p33)  
GEN:   It‟s a prayer which does still exist. What this point refers to is that one should 
use these kinds of pra yers, like “ May the precious bodhimind... ” or like “TO NY DA WA 
RIN PO CHE” etc., as dedications at the end.  
STUDENT:   I read that the mental direct perceiver that comes at the end of a sense 
consciousness for an ordinary being is a conceptual consciousness for Prasangika, 
because a conceptual consciousness can also be a direct perceiver. Is this true?  
GEN:   It‟s ok to reflect in this way.  
The point is in that in Prasangika, what is normally translated as direct perceiver , the 
second word, perceiver is normally expressing some type of consciousness. But this 
word perceiver is actually not there. It‟s litera lly just direct  or the manifest . In all the 
other systems below Prasangika, this direct ( nyön-sum in Tibetan) refers to a direct 
perceiver, so for them it is necessarily a consciousness, but in Prasangika it refers to 
anything manifest. The table is also m anifest, a direct  (nyön-sum) but it‟s not a direct 
perceiver.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 160 – week one to seven  Objects of knowledge can be divided into manifest and hidden phenomena, so 
anything that is manifest, is this direct according to Prasangika. From this point of 
view, we can say a conceptual mi nd is manifest, or direct. With this is mind, for 
Prasangika, most of our consciousnesses are direct, and most of the wrong 
consciousnesses are also direct. In other systems, this would be a surprising thing to 
say because wrong consciousnesses and direct are complete opposites.  
A valid perception in the Prasangika system would be a manifest ( nyön-sum) because it 
is not a hidden phenomenon.  
 In brief, in Prasangika, direct is of one meaning with manifest phenomenon. In 
this system, we could divide all phe nomena into manifest phenomena and hidden 
phenomena, or divide them into the manifest and the hidden phenomena. This is 
because the manifest and manifest phenomena in the Prasangika system is the same 
meaning. In other systems, the difference is you can di vide all phenomena into 
manifest phenomena and hidden phenomena, but the manifest only refers to direct 
perceivers.  
STUDENT:   [inaudible ]  
GEN:   Up until now I‟ve only answered this questions in regard to nyön-sum which 
normally means direct perceiver, bu t here it refers to the manifest. Most conceptual 
consciousnesses are also part of this manifest. If we specify this as a mental manifest, 
or a mental direct perceiver, then it‟s clear were talking about a consciousness. Then 
this mental direct perceiver w ould not be a conceptual consciousness.  
 For example, if you generate a memory of your parents, is this a mental direct? 
The answer is no, it‟s a conceptual consciousness, a thought. Is it a direct, then yes, it‟s 
a manifest. This memory is a direct, but i t‟s not a mental direct because it‟s a 
conception.  
 To be clear, “direct” in Prasangika means a manifest phenomenon. If it‟s a 
direct consciousness, then it‟s not necessarily a manifest phenomenon, but it is a 
consciousness. For example, a memory consciou sness or an inferential prime cognizer 
is a direct because they are manifest phenomena, but it is not a direct consciousness 
because they are a conception.

2B4A -2A2B -4A What to do when lying down to sleep  
1 [The time to lie down]  
 
Exerting oneself not sleeping and practicing yoga, and w hat to do w hen lying dow n to 
sleep. In the Letter to a Friend it says:  
Sensible one, great being after all the day’s sessions,  
as w ell as the first and la st ones of the night  
also sleep w ith mindfulness in betw een these  
so that during sleep, too, fruit w ill not be absent.  
 
Sensible one , great being is an epithet showing respect. The meaning here is the letter 
was written for a king named Dechö Zangpo. The name means engaging in happy 
conduct , and thus excellent deeds , meaning he is a yogi - someone who is actually 
practicing. After all the days sessions  means one is practicing the yoga all day. As well as 
the first and last ones of the night  – this means that in the last part of the night one 
practices, then one gets up early in the morning to continue practicing. In between 
there is one  phase where one does sleep, but even in this phase, we shouldn‟t sleep in 
such a way that fruit will not be absent .   
Normally for us, when we sleep it is of the non -virtuous type. We go into all kinds of 
torpor or drowsiness and tend to dwell in this sta te. However, for a yogi, even their 
sleep is virtuous through his application of mindfulness while he sleeps.  
Just like Kyabje Zopa Rinpoche; all day he works and then at night he continues to 
work and doesn‟t sleep. He is constantly active, and there is not one empty moment. I 
know he sleeps sometimes because one time when we were in the car to Barcelona, and 
I saw him sleep. In general, even Rinpoche sleeps! Actually, he has no time to sleep, he 
maybe doesn‟t even know the time of the day because he‟s so  busy.   
 Several years ago, I met Kyabje Zopa Rinpoche in Bodhgaya, and asked for an 
audience. They told me to come at 9pm. I waited until 11:30pm because Rinpoche was 
so busy. It was so late that I thought maybe he‟s sleeping by now, so I went back to 
my hotel and slept. Then at about 1am, Venerable Roger came to my hotel in a car and 
said Rinpoche was ready to see me, this was about 2am! The next day was teachings 
with His Holiness the Dalai Lama. That night I didn‟t get chance to sleep, because I 
spent until 6am talking to Rinpoche about going to Nalanda, and asking his advice.
 We then went to the teachings of His Holiness the Dalai Lama at 10am, of 
course you couldn‟t sleep there, but because I was awake all night I nodded off. Other 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 162 – week one to seven  Geshes then said to  me, “did you come here all the way from France to sleep?” 
Rinpoche was of course also there in the teachings. I had at least one hour, but 
Rinpoche probably had no sleep, so he has attained such a state of mind that he is free 
from sleep – this is quite a mazing. Nevertheless, being free from this, he still doesn‟t 
have time to get bored, he still never has enough time. Later Rinpoche came and 
stayed here at Nalanda, during these 5 days I went to bed as usual, and did not see 
Rinpoche in the evenings otherw ise there would be no sleep that week, just like in 
India!  
 Rinpoche might not need sleep, but if we don‟t sleep then the next day is very 
difficult, so we need our sleep.  
 This King, Dechö Zangpo, he probably did yoga in six sessions, so four and 
then tw o, but in -between there is also one phase where he would have slept. Even in 
this one session, the advice is to be careful that even this becomes the accumulation of 
virtue.
