MONDAY 15 MARCH 2010  - SESSION 1 
 
2B4A -2A2B -4A What  to do when lying down to sleep  
1 [The time to lie down]  
Exerting oneself not sleeping and practicing yoga, and w hat to do w hen lying dow n to 
sleep. In the Letter to a Friend it says:  
Sensible one, great being  after all the day’s sessions,  
as w ell as the first and last ones of the night  
also sleep w ith mindfulness in betw een these  
so that during sleep, too, fruit w ill not be absent.  
 
The essence of the sessions of both the entire day and the first and last part of the 
night as w ell as what should be done in betw een is being explained. Therefore, having 
w alked about and sat dow n, you should completely purify your mind of the five 
obscurations and thus make [w hatever you do] meaningful.  
 
This letter was written to the king whose name means something like bli ssful activity 
of excellence.  
 It is expressed here that the mind should be going towards virtue at the time of 
sleep, as well as during the day. Sessions of meditation mention here an earlier and a 
later part. We can talk about a yoga in four sessions or in six sessions for example. 
Next to the actual sessions, you also have the time in between sessions in which you 
should also try to continue the topic of your meditation, along with studying the topic, 
debating it and asking questions about it, etc.  
 This king called blissful activity of excellence generated this kind of 
discrimination of saying that his sleep was an activity in between sessions, just like the 
other phases between sessions, and therefore he also wanted to fill it with virtue. We 
were alre ady reflecting on the example of Lama Zopa Rinpoche, who has pretty much 
no sleep and is continuously practicing virtue. He never just hangs out, but is 
continuously active, and should be our role model. He really practices the yoga of no 
sleep!  
 In our t ext it talks about someone who tries to bring all his time into the 
practice of virtue. As a result, he doesn‟t even want to waste the time of sleep, but use 
it in order to practice virtue.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 164 – week one to seven  The night is presented as having three parts. The first and the l ast part you 
would use as periods dedicated towards virtue. Of course, during the day you also only 
engage into virtue, but even amongst these first and last parts of the night you would 
strongly practice virtue, and during the middle part you would lie do wn for a short 
time, but even then, you try to sleep with virtue. One factor in this is the lion position 
where you lie down on your right side. This is the position that the buddha took on 
when he passed into parinirvana.  
 
2 The behaviour of sleep  
As the behaviour of sleep occurs betw een sessions, it should also not be w asted 
meaninglessly. In this regard, the conduct of the body consists in lying dow n, during 
the middle of three parts of the night, on your right side placing the left leg on top of 
the rig ht one and sleeping like a lion.  
 
I guess that this must be the way in which real lions sleep!   
Apart from this, another explanation of this piece would be understood as the 
lion is the king of all wild animals, and as a result, he can lie down without fe ar of any 
danger, while the other animals have a fear of being killed or attacked in their sleep.  
 So the meaning refers to sleeping without fear. And since the yogi strongly 
practices virtue in the first and last part of the night, he doesn‟t have to be afraid 
about any negative minds arising, because his practice is so strong. So in this way, he 
sleeps without fear, like a lion.  
 Before you would go to sleep you should generate wisdom on the basis of the 
listening, reflecting or meditating that took pla ce during the day. In this way, it will 
have a strong influence in your sleep, and might also come up in your dreams. When 
you wake up, the first thoughts will already be connected with dharma. In another 
context, this is connected to understanding the two  truths. If while awake you 
generated a strong understanding of the difference between the actual way of existence 
of phenomena and their illusory appearance, then if you go into sleep you will naturally 
be able to use all the dream appearances in your ana lysis. You will understand all kinds 
of feelings and experiences as a mere appearance. In this way, even your sleep will 
become a meditation on the two truths, and become very powerful. For the moment, 
this is beyond our capacity, but at least, we should g enerate a strong virtuous mind 
before going to sleep, and like this, this virtuous mind will continue right into your 
sleep.  
 This is expressed in the text [page 35]  where one holds the virtuous object until 
one falls asleep, and because of the strong mind fulness cultivated during the day, the 
object will come up in your sleep and transform it into virtue.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
165  
3 Mindfulness  
As for mindfulness, you should rely on the fact that, until you fall asleep, it pursues 
the predominant virtuous action that you have cult ivated during the day. Therefore, 
you can sustain your spiritual practice such as meditative stabilisation even w hile 
asleep similarly to w hen you are not asleep.  
 
4 Vigilance  
Vigilance: If any mental afflictions arise w hile you cultivate mindfulness, { 44}  you 
become aw are of them and rather than grasping them eagerly abandon them.  
 
We normally call this introspection , with which you can check what is going on in your 
mind, and is applicable to waking and sleeping states . In this way, we can recognize 
when even the smallest of the afflictions arises, and then immediately counteract it. 
For example, if we are about to become angry, we shouldn‟t enter into additional 
negative thoughts, but instead we need to think about patie nce and loving concern. 
This you would do in the wak ing state, and possibly even in the sleeping state . 
 
5 The discrimination of getting up  
The discrimination of getting up consists in projecting the thought of getting up at this 
and that hour.  
 
When lying down at night you already make clear to yourself when you willget up the 
following morning. In this way, you can wake up with a very clear mind that has a 
strong enthusiasm to get up and engage into virtue. You don‟t wake up with a cloudy 
or dist urbed mind. Additionally, before you go to sleep you would reflect about the 
day, the virtuous activities you did,  and make the conclusion that it went very well and 
that you  will do the same the next day. If this i s very clear in your mind , you will wake 
up the following morning with enthusiasm to engage in virtue.  
We can recall instances where we had to do something the next day, like leave for 
the airport  to get our flight, and because  of knowing this ; we woke up at the right 
moment , even without an ala rm clock. In the same way,  before you go to bed you set 
your mind to get up at that time in order to enthusiastically engage into virtue.   
 
2B4A -2A2B -4B  How to apply [all this] to the intervals between all meditations  
That being so, apart from the uncomm on ways of sustaining meditation during the 
actual meditation, [these indications as to] w hat should be done in preparation, 
during the actual meditation, at the end and in betw een sessions should be applied to 
all observed objects and aspects being sustai ned from here up to special insight.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 166 – week one to seven   
Some people would relate to day and night by making six sessions in the day and three 
in the night. Others make three in the day and three in the night , and on the basis of 
this they engage into the different sessions.  But they all end up with sessions and 
periods in between  the sessions . Here it has been explained with regards to the topic 
of the spiritual friend , but it is to be applied to all the topics right up to Special 
Insight . 
 
2B4A -2B The reason why [meditation ] needs to be sustained by means of two modes of 
sustaining  
1 [The two modes of sustaining]  
2 Refutation of the misconception asserting that although there are both analytical and 
placement meditations, one single person does not practice them  
3 Refutation  of the misconception asserting that all conceptions apprehend signs  
4 Refutation of the misconception asserting that if you do a lot of analytical meditation 
prior to the arising of concentration, it becomes an obstacle  
2B4A -2B1 [The two modes of sustaini ng] 
A The reason why both analysis and placement are necessary for meditation  
 
Second: In Ornament of the Sutras it says:  
Here, first of all, proper mental attention arises in 
dependence on study and from proper mental attention, 
again, the exalted w isdom  w ith the meaning of pure reality as 
its object arises.  
 
That is to say that from proper mental attention, through wisdom arisen from 
reflecting the meaning of w hat has been studied, the w isdom arisen from meditation 
directly realizing the meaning of pure reality arises.  
 
In dependence on study : This indeed forms the very basis; you need to come to know all 
the different topics through studying. On this basis, you can enter into reflection on 
them, and then bring forth a meditative wisdom based on your reflections.  
 The proper mental attention  is to be understood as the wisdom arising from 
reflecting. First you listen, and after taking this to mind, i.e. bringing about this proper 
mental attention , meaning you have reflected on it, then the wisdom from reflecting 
arises. And from the proper ment al attention  the exalted wisdom with the meaning of pure 
reality as its object arises , which refers to the wisdom arising from meditation.  
 But this studying is not something you engage into just for a short time. 
You do this for the rest of your life, yo u engage into reflecting for the rest of your life 
and you engage into meditating and cultivating for the rest of your life. And not only 
this, but you engage into these three also in future lives, until you attain the different 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
167 bhumis, right up to the ten th bodhisattva bhumi. And right on this bhumi, what you 
will do is to study, reflect, meditate and cultivate, right up to the state of complete 
enlightenment. This is the path. There is no other path.   
 The exalted wisdom with the meaning of pure reality as its object arises . This is 
a wisdom that understands the ultimate, i.e. realises emptiness directly. This kind of 
exalted wisdom comes from relying on a proper mental attention, which is a wisdom 
arising from reflecting on the meaning of the ultimate. T herefore, having studied the 
meaning of the ultimate and then reflected on it, then finally you can generate this 
exalted wisdom.  
 
The first step of the wisdom arising from study, for which the actual Tibetan word is 
the wisdom arising from listening, is o bviously received through someone else you 
listened to. This person is your guru, who is teaching you the dharma. Through 
listening to his presentation, you come to understand what the dharma is saying, and 
then you go to the next level of reflecting on it  and generating a wisdom that is based 
on reflecting. On the basis of having listened and reflected you come to understand 
the dharma, and you understand it so deeply that you really penetrate, or realise it.  
 You then apply this penetrating understanding of the dharma to your 
practice, and you befriend this understanding or realisation more and more until you 
end up with no gap between your mind and this realisation or topic. You could say that 
you completely befriend this topic, and through this, you come  to the level of 
generating a wisdom arising through cultivation or meditation. This then is the process 
in which through continuous familiarisation you finally end up generating this exalted 
wisdom realising emptiness.  
 Someone might say that he will sto p with listening and reflecting for a 
while, stop their studies and meditate. This is like saying he wants to enter into a 
mouse hole, meaning that it just won‟t work. Bringing about meditation while leaving 
aside listening and reflecting will not work. Yo u won‟t generate good qualities, and on 
top of that you will probably increase your ignorance. If on the other hand you engage 
into studies and come to know many things about the dharma, then you can reflect on 
it and try to understand it. In this way, you  will end up in a state where you will cut all 
your doubts. Having reached this level you can sometimes use the form of placement 
meditation, and bring all of this together.  
 In the context of the five paths, on the first one, the path of 
accumulation, it  is said that listening and reflecting are the most important things to 
do. To get to this path you already need actual bodhicitta and you might even have a 
realisation of emptiness. If even someone on such a path is engaged in listening and 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 168 – week one to seven  reflecting as the most important activities, then this is definitely true for us on our 
level. In short, listening and reflecting is more important than the placement 
meditation.  
 By becoming well trained in the practice of analysing meditation you can 
do it all the ti me and apply it to all kind of situations. Someone who is very capable in 
analysing can fill all his time in this way while being totally absorbed, and going from 
one place to the other. There is a story about someone in the monastery who was very 
absorbed  in this process, and when he put his key in the lock at the end of the day, the 
next morning the other monks found him still standing there, the door still unopened, 
because he was so absorbed in his analysis. So you can engage into such analysis in 
great  depth.  
 There are stories of people like this today, and it doesn‟t only occasionally 
happen that people spend the whole night reflecting about the dharma and don‟t sleep 
at all. But this doesn‟t harm their body because they are constantly in this positi ve state 
of wisdom. In this way, they can analyse without needing sleep. If someone like this 
would be walking alone on the road, he would have arrived at the end of his journey 
unaware of how long it took due to being  so absorbed in his analysis. This pe rson‟s 
intelligence and wisdom will become sharper and sharper, and if he engages into 
placement meditation, he will also have a very fresh, sharp, alert mind at all times 
while reading or listening to teachings.  
 On the other hand, you have people who ar e the complete opposite of 
this; when they read a book or do their prayers, they immediately become sleepy. If 
you have someone who is not trained in analysing, then he is probably someone who 
easily gets sleepy because he lacks the clarity of mind that co mes from analysing and 
discerning. In addition, analytical meditation doesn‟t come easy for such a person. 
People who are very much into films or football also have this certain kind of 
alertness. They might eat something while watching, but not even notic e what they are 
eating because of being so focused. It can happen in the same way for someone 
studying the dharma.  
 Someone analysing the Lam-rim will really come to know what is 
presented here, and will later be able to present it to others without any m istake. This 
means that by analysing he understood the meaning of the topics. He might not know 
individual words or a specific phrase, for example, what it means that the bodhisattva 
is like a snow lion, but he knows the meaning and therefore can explain e verything 
without fault.  
 
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
169 In the Ornament of Direct Realization, too, it says:  
For the four aspects of ascertainment,  
the paths of seeing and meditation,  
repeated reflection, assessment, and certainty  
are the path of  meditation.  
 
That is to say there a re [types of] repeated reflection, assessment, and certain 
understanding (“certainty”) that constitute the paths of meditation of Mahayana 
Aryas.  
 
The first line of For the four aspects of ascertainment  is wrong. There are no four aspects 
mentioned in the Tibetan. In the Lam -Rim Chenmo it says, “As for the branch of 
certain differentiation”.  
 This branch of certain differentiation talks about the path of preparation, 
and in the second line the paths of seeing and meditation are mentioned. The third line 
talks about repeated reflection, then assessment or comprehension and then certainty, 
referring to a certain conceptualisation, a definite thought to meditate in this way. This 
expresses how important it is to do these activities on all three paths but here i t talks 
especially about the path of meditation. This Arya -path of meditation of the Mahayana 
is posited as repeatedly reflecting and assessing in order to come to a definite 
conceptual understanding (which means to repeatedly analyse). This is how the pat h of 
meditation is expressed.   
 
In the Compendium of Trainings it also says:  
Thus you should practice meditation, continuously giving 
away, guarding, purifying and increasing your bodies, 
possessions, and merit in all kinds of w ays.  
 
With regard to the  meditations that should be done according to the statement, for all 
four activities - giving aw ay, guarding, purifying and increasing - w ith respect to each 
of the three - body, possessions and roots of virtue, there are both analytical 
meditations sustai ned through analysis by means of discriminating w isdom and 
placement meditations, w hich are to place [the mind] single -pointedly without 
analysis.  
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 170 – week one to seven  This quote is probably from Arya Asanga , and  is to be understood as all the described 
activities described here are forms of analytical mediation, which is also expressed in 
the commentary above.  
 We need to ap ply two types of mediation. Analytical meditation is 
supported by analysing with the wisdom analysing individu ally, and  the placement 
meditation is done without analysing,  but placement  in a single -pointed way. This 
meditation , which is single -pointedly focused on a virtuous object , is expressed as 
being placement meditation.  
 
 
MONDAY  15 MARCH  2010  - SESSION  2 
Som eone might ask what the two types of meditation are. To this Lama Tsongkhapa 
answers that there are many forms of analytical meditation, and the first ones are 
connected to the virtuous friend. Here you could mediate on cultivating faith, which is 
the root  of the path. You would reflect on his kindness in order to generate respect, 
and you would reflect on how to rely in thought and action and so forth. So, there are 
many topics.  
 Analytical meditation is also applied to the freedoms and endowments, 
when m editating on a precious human rebirth, as well as topics such as impermanence, 
karma and its effects, the drawbacks of samsara and so forth. For example, the 
analytical meditation on the drawbacks of samsara refers specifically to meditating on 
the drawbac ks of true sufferings, and how they are brought about by true origins. Put 
into a nutshell, you meditate on the twelve links of dependent arising. Each of them 
needs to be analysed with analytical meditation. So obviously, there are a lot of topics 
that ne ed this kind of meditation. Another topic mentioned here is the mind of 
enlightenment, including meditations on love and compassion and the actual mind of 
enlightenment. And in all the six perfections, all six apart from the meditation on 
concentration are  mainly analytical meditations. Obviously, the meditation on 
meditative stabilisation has to do with placement meditation. From this we can see that 
the vast majority of meditations are analytical.  
 Meditations on love compassion, bodhicitta the perfection s of ethics, 
generosity, patience and effort are all analytical meditations. The perfection of mental 
stabilization is only placement meditation. And the perfection of wisdom comprises of 
both analytical and placement meditation. We have to engage in this type of analytical 
meditation as long as it takes to change the mind. If we don‟t, then the opposing class 
of virtue (i.e. non -virtue) will prevail because our mind is not powerful enough. If we 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
171 have a virtuous mind of faith towards our guru, the opposite would be non -faith or 
disrespect. .  
 For example, an object of attachment is engaged in by constantly 
exaggerating its good qualities, and thereby generating an awareness of it as being 
completely beautiful. On this basis, you start to relate to it in an u nsuitable way, which 
is out of touch with reality, and as a result, the full form of attachment arises. We 
don‟t get attached to an object by just placing our mind on it, but through cultivating 
an analytical approach in which we constantly come up with ne w benefits and reasons 
for why it is beautiful. It happens in the same way with an unattractive object or 
someone we see as an enemy. We go through analytical types of minds that establish 
why the person is so repelling until finally on the basis of all of  this you end up in 
problems. It is now plain to see that we need to go through many analytical steps in 
order to recognize the unattractiveness of the object of attachment. In the same way, 
we apply a lot of analysis when we get attached or averted to som ething in order to 
eliminate these minds.  
 In this process of attachment, where we relate to the object by constantly 
labelling and superimposing more and more qualities by our over conceptualisations, in 
the end there is an appearance of the object as be ing totally amazing. Because of this 
perception comes this strong attachment. The same process works with the person we 
perceive as an enemy, where we end up in a situation where we definitely need to hit or 
say something nasty to this enemy! This I call a  “mind market” because all the 
negativity you have in your mind towards that person is openly put out on display for 
everyone to see.  
 At this moment, we no longer have any self -respect. So in such a 
situation, everyone would be just terrified of us, and definitely not refer to us as a 
good person. In this moment, you might feel very strong or special but anyone else 
will just think of you as very low and degenerate.  
 This is what happens if you don‟t apply analytical meditation. You will 
just display all  of your bad states of mind, and everyone will see you as degenerate. If 
you do apply the analytical meditation on your own continuum in, for example, 
generating faith in the guru, then it can be so strong that even physical reactions 
appear. Some people m ight even burst into tears of joy when encountering their 
teacher. In the same way as I mentioned earlier, everything is put on display for 
everyone to see, but rather than being afraid, the people will actually be inspired by 
perceiving the good qualities  you have developed. Therefore, we have to engage into 
this analytical meditation until our mind actually changes. This starts from the topic of 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 172 – week one to seven  correct reliance on the teacher, right up to the step of the union of calm abiding and 
special insight.   
 One engages in placement meditation without analysis. Let‟s say you want 
to develop the mind of calm abiding so you only place the mind on your focal object 
and keep it there until you generate the same mind the next week, the next month and 
so on. If you woul d analyse at that point, it would actually become an obstacle to your 
success.   
 
B Identification of analysis and placement  
{ 45}  Now if you ask w hat are the paths of analytical meditation and w hat are the 
paths of placement meditation - for meditations su ch as the cultivation of confidence 
in the spiritual teacher, the preciousness of the freedoms and endow ments and how  
difficult it is to find them, death and impermanence, karma and its effect, the faults 
of cyclic existence and the cultivation of bodhicit ta, analytical meditation is necessary. 
For each of them minds of great strength and the ability to subdue the mind for a long 
time are necessary, because, if they are absent, their counterparts, disrespect and the 
like cannot be prevented and because the generation of these minds depends on 
nothing but the meditation analyzing them by investigating them individually time 
and again. To illustrate, just as intense attachment arises, if you meditate on an 
object of attachment a lot, exaggerating its pleasant features, in the same w ay intense 
aversion arises, if you think of many unpleasant features w ith regard to an enemy. 
Hence, in regard to meditating these paths, it is the same, whether or not the aspect of 
the object appears clearly, but the mind’s mode of  apprehension has to be intense and 
long-lasting, w hich is why you should do analytical meditation. In the context of 
practicing calm abiding, which enables one to place [the mind] on whatever single 
object one w ishes - since someone w hose mind cannot stay  on a single object w ill not be 
fit for mental quiescence if he analyses again and again, it is necessary to practice 
placement meditation for that.  
 
2B4A -2B2 Refutation of the misconception asserting that although there are both analytical and 
placement m editations, one single person does not practice them  
Although some w ho do not know this approach claim that, if you are a scholar, you 
should only do analytical meditation w hile sadhus should only do placement 
meditation this is not so, for it is necessary to do the tw o one by one - because the 
learned, too,  need to achieve calm abiding and sadhus, too, need to achieve strong 
confidence in the spiritual teacher { 46}  and the like. Therefore the understanding that 
repeated analysis by means of discriminating w isdom belongs to the context of listening 
and reflec ting and not to the context of practice is inadmissible.  
 
Here someone thinks that there is one class of person who only practices analytical 
meditation, and one person only practicing placement meditation. This is totally wrong 
as everyone needs to practi ce both.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
173  The commentary says that someone who is a scholar only engages into 
analytical meditation while Kusali (I read this as just a name, not as sadhus) just needs 
to engage into placement meditation. This distinguishes someone with sharp faculties 
who just needs to practice analytical meditation, and someone with dull faculties who 
only engaging into placement meditation. However, if you are sharp or dull, in both 
cases you need to engage into both types of meditation.  
 
2B4A -2B3 Refutation of the misc onception asserting that all conceptions apprehend signs  
The understanding that all conceptions apprehend signs and therefore create obstacles 
to enlightenment has the fault of not discriminating betw een improper mental 
attention, the conception of true ex istence , and proper mental attention, the 
conception of reality.  
 
Here someone says that if it is a conceptual mind , then it necessarily grasps for true 
existence and therefore all conceptual minds need to be overcome because of being an 
obstacle for pene trating to the ultimate state of reality. Such a person can‟t 
discriminate between a faulty conceptual mind, grasping for true existence and a 
correct conceptual mind in accordance with reality.  
 
2B4A -2B4 Refutation of the misconception asserting that if y ou do a lot of analytical meditation prior to 
the arising of concentration, it becomes an obstacle  
The idea “it is necessary to achieve the non -conceptual meditative stabilization 
capable of placing the mind on a single object in w hatever w ay it pleases an d, if you 
do a lot of analytical meditation before, it w ill become an obstacle to the arising of 
[that] meditative stabilization”, is not upheld in the present instructions. To illustrate, 
similarly, w hen a smith burns gold or silver in the fire and w ashes  it in water again 
and again, all the impurities are removed and it becomes very supple and w orkable, 
w hereby it can subsequently be made into any jew ellery like earrings at w ill. Likew ise, 
just as at first the mental afflictions, the secondary afflictions , and faulty conduct 
emerge on the occasion of meditating on black actions and their results and the 
disadvantages of cyclic existence, by meditating on such disadvantages again and 
again w ith the w isdom of individual investigation, the mind w ill become co mpletely 
tormented or disenchanted; therefore it is like burning gold in a fire. Similarly, just as 
the mind turning aw ay from the black side and the defilements being purified emerges 
on the occasion of [meditating on] the positive qualities of the spirit ual friend, the 
great meaning of the freedoms and endow ments, the positive qualities of the Three 
J ew els, w hite actions and their results, and { 47}  the benefits of the mind of 
enlightenment, by meditating on these positive qualities again and again w ith th e 
w isdom of individual investigation, the mental attention of the mind becomes 
moistened or sincere, w hereby it is like w ashing gold w ith w ater. Through the mind 
approaching the w hite side and delighting in it, the mind is moistened by virtuous 
dharmas. Wh en it has been transformed in that w ay, w hatever you w ish to achieve, 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 174 – week one to seven  calm abiding or special insight, if you fix upon it, you w ill achieve it w ithout 
difficulty. That is w hy such an analytical meditation is the best means of achieving 
non-conceptual medit ative stabilisation.  
 
In order to have a successful placement meditation, you need to prepare properly. You 
need to know about the five faults that can arise during the training for calm abiding, 
and the eight discernments abandoning them. And this you com e to know on the basis 
of analytical meditation. You need this as a preparation.  
 The text says that we shouldn‟t end up with the idea that analytical 
meditation as a preparation for placement meditation is wrong. We first need to engage 
into analysis to prepare for all the situations that can occur when doing placement 
meditation, and only then, we can successfully engage into it. The analogy used is the 
one of a goldsmith washing, burning, manipulating and doing various other things in 
order to extract a ll the impurities out of the gold he would like to create into jewellery. 
We have to proceed in the same way with our mind. First, we would have to relate to 
all that  is produced by the root and secondary afflictions, and the faulty behaviour 
they lead to . The black karma and its effects refer to negativities together with the 
drawbacks of samsara; we have to meditate on all of this and purify our continuum 
from it.  
 By using our wisdom that individually analyses, we would repeatedly 
meditate on these draw backs. In this way, the mind becomes utterly 
tormented/concerned, thinking that all these things produce my unhappiness, and if I 
don‟t find a method to eliminate them, I will stay unhappy. In this way, one generates a 
mind that is disenchanted with samsar a. In the same way as a goldsmith purifies his 
gold in order to make workable, analytical meditation brings about these states of 
disenchantment  
  This refers to us being completely under the power of the afflictive minds 
that bring about negative actions,  and us wanting to turn this around and gain control 
over them. This happens on the mental level, and is similar to the goldsmith burning 
away the gross faults of the gold in the fire.  
 Other points to reflect upon and analyse include the good qualities o f the 
teacher, the great meaning of the freedoms and endowments, the good qualities of the 
three jewels, virtuous actions and their effects, and the benefits of bodhicitta. This is 
all done with wisdom that is individually analysing. By repeatedly meditati ng these 
good qualities, the mind becomes moistened, supple and workable. This is similar to 
how the goldsmith, after having burned the gold, now treats it with special liquids to 
purify it from even the smallest of stains or impurities.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
175  If we can penetr ate to this kind of mind, then what happens is that we 
bring our mind completely to the white side, the side of virtue. As a result, we haven‟t 
just changed the mind so that it‟s no longer under the power of non -virtue, but we 
have naturally steered it tow ards virtue. Moreover, we fill the mind with joy in 
practicing virtue and by this virtuous dharma we are moistening the mind. If the mind 
comes to this moistened state by the virtuous dharma, then one is able to work with 
the mind in whatever way one wishe s. For example, if the wish is to attain calm 
abiding or special insight, then this will come about without major problems by just 
focusing the mind on that goal.  
 Furthermore, in the text it says: when you bring about this kind of 
analytical meditation, you end up with a mind that becomes the supreme method for 
attaining calm abiding. Here in the text it says non-conceptual meditative stabilisation , 
which refers to calm abiding. All this long text expresses is that if you are able to 
develop a mind of def inite emergence, then your mind has already gone to the side of 
virtue which makes it very easy to engage into meditations like calm abiding or the 
absorptions. Therefore, the best way to proceed is to focus on a mind of definite 
emergence.  
 If you have p roblems in generating calm abiding, then it is because of all 
kinds of attachments to this and future lives. It is not due to anger, because even that 
arises due to attachment. Therefore, with a mind of definite emergence, there is no 
attachment to this or  future lives and one is only interested in getting liberated. There 
is no attachment of the strong type, and one is joyfully going to the side of virtue. In 
that state, it is very easy to bring about calm abiding or special insight.  
 
 
 
QUESTION:  Does this  refer to a contrived mind of definite emergence?  
GEN:  Yes. In order to bring about an uncontrived mind you would have to rely on 
calm abiding, as well as special insight. This uncontrived mind is the entrance to the 
hearer path, but you would also have th is mind present when entering the 
Pratyekabuddha - or Mahayana path.  
 
That is how  Arya Asanga also put it:  
By w ay of analogy, w hen a smith or his skilful apprentice 
burns gold or silver in a fire and w ashes it w ith w ater a 
couple of times in order to discard all impurities and slag, it 
becomes supple and w orkable for this and that ornament, 
w hereby this is directly perceived. In this regard, through the 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 176 – week one to seven  know-how that accords w ith this of the smith or his skilful 
apprentice, any manner of ornament desir ed is then made, 
w ith their smith’s tools . Likew ise, w hen a yogi does not turn 
tow ard impurities and slag such as covetousness, he thereby 
gets disenchanted and turns aw ay from unhappy afflicted 
states of mind, and approaches manifest joy in the virtuous 
side, thereby becoming manifestly joyful. In that case, that 
yogi definitely trains his mind in either the side of calm 
abiding or the side of special insight, such that it is 
thoroughly applied to this and that, rests in its natural state, 
is immovable an d unw avering, and { 48}  also moves for the 
sake of perfectly achieving whatever purpose he has 
contemplated.  
 
This refers to that the gold is crafted into such a state that any kind of jewellery that 
the smith thinks about can be made from it. It is complet ely up to him what he wants 
to do with it. Covetousness  is a mind of the attachment type that is constantly thinking 
about getting this or that object, or this and that happening. It is the first of the ten 
non-virtuous actions of mind. The yogi turns towa rds the side of virtue, but 
furthermore he also has this strong joy in it ( becoming manifestly joyful ).  
 This quote explains what Lama Tsongkhapa previously explained. The 
very gist of this is that the smith makes the gold completely workable in the same way 
that the yogi has the power over his own mind and can set it on whatever meditative 
goal he wants.  
 
 
Furthermore, the main adverse conditions for the mind staying w ith an object of 
meditation continuously are tw ofold: laxity and excitement. If you have  a strong and 
constant awareness that sees the positive qualities of the Three J ew els etc., it is quite 
easy to overcome laxity, for many valid sources say that its antidote is to uplift the 
mind by looking at positive qualities. If you have a strong and c onstant awareness 
that sees the disadvantages of impermanence, suffering, and so forth, it is quite easy to 
overcome excitement, because, since excitement is a distracted mind belonging to [the 
category of] attachment, many texts praise disillusionment as its antidote.  
 
 
MONDAY  15 MARCH  2010  - SESSION  3 
QUESTION:   Is it right that in lying on the right side of the body in the lion 
posture some negative energy channels are blocked?  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
177 GEN:  There is a presentation about these channels, one central and two side c hannels. 
The two side channels are connected with our afflictions, but the energies pretty much 
never enter the central channel as all our activities are connected with energies flowing 
up and down in these side channels. It is quite possible that there is  such a point as 
yours explained in tantra, but in the context of the Lam-rim it is not explained in that 
way, so this is not the meaning here. Nevertheless, in tantra, especially in highest yoga 
tantra, there might be such a presentation.  
 
The text says that the two main obstacles to calm abiding are laxity, which makes the 
mind unclear and heavy, and excitement, which makes the mind unable to remain on 
the object due to attachment for other objects.  
 By extensively reflecting on the good qualities of th e three jewels and 
meditating on them, a clear faith arises towards them. By reflecting on how they have 
the capacity of a refuge, this generates the second type, the faith of conviction. Finally, 
through making a resolution that you go for refuge until yo u are enlightened, you 
express the third type, being a wishing faith. So through reflecting in this way, all three 
types of faith are generated, and cuts this opposing factor of laxity. This relation 
between laxity and faith is such that by reflecting on t he good qualities or capacities of 
the three jewels or the aim to achieve them, you generate a mind that is uplifted and 
praises them. This state of mind actually cuts the power of laxity.  
 In order to cut the obstacle of excitement one can repeatedly ref lect on 
impermanence, especially in the form of our death and on things being in the nature of 
suffering, either the three types of suffering (suffering of suffering, suffering of change 
and all pervasive suffering) or four types (birth, old age, sickness and death) of 
suffering. In this way, you can examine the faults of samsara and recognize the faults 
of contaminated phenomena until you generate a mind that wants to be separated from 
them. Since excitement is attachment, it is generated on the base of co ntaminated 
phenomena, and therefore if you generate a mind that is exactly the opposite 
excitement can‟t come about.  
 The essential meaning here is that by reflecting on the good qualities of 
the three jewels, and generating the three types of faith, the mind becomes clear, alert 
and uplifted, and the factor of laxity is destroyed as a result. In addition, by reflecting 
on the faults of contaminated phenomena, the fault of excitement is cut and thereby 
the two main obstacles for the attainment of calm abid ing are destroyed. Here it 
becomes very clear how on the basis of analytical meditation the two main obstacles of 
calm abiding are destroyed, and how analytical meditation is a necessary prerequisite 
for placement meditation.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 178 – week one to seven   
Before we move on, I want to address the situation of someone who says he wants to 
meditate, who wants to attain calm abiding. In order to do this he needs to prepare: 
through  studying  the dharma, which  he will require in order to do th e practice. He 
needs to integra te what he has studied to the point that he comes to a wisdom arising 
from listening. Then he has to repeatedly reflect on that basis until a wisdom arises 
from reflecting, and then on that basis he can bring forth very good forms of analytical 
meditation.  With this analytical meditation, he could then bring forth minds like these 
three types of faith or a mind that wants to avoid contaminated phenomena. In other 
words, he would develop a mind of definite emergence, and in this way, he is able to 
fully set the mind on virtue and counteract the main obstacles of laxity and excitement. 
By being prepared in this way, he can indeed go for the placement meditation that aims 
to attain calm abiding. Through this type of preparation, his mind is already on the 
side of virtue, and he will find this kind of meditation quite easy to achieve because the 
mind is workable and easily applied to it.  
 So therefore, first we listen to the dharma for example with regards to 
how to rely on the teacher, and then we reflect on wh at we heard and cultivate it. In 
this case, we are referring to analytical meditation. In this way, through repeatedly 
analyzing  the topic, we generate a strong form of faith and a real change and 
connection between our mind and the topic under analysis. This relation will then 
become stronger and stronger. This connection refers to not wanting the dharma topic 
over there and us here, but we want to have a close connection between the topic and 
ourselves. If we prepare in this way, the mind will become wor kable and conducive.  
 
QUESTION:   At what level do we talk about a wisdom arising from meditation? Does 
this refer to special insight?  
GEN:  This wisdom indeed comes from special insight because before that, there will 
be no wisdom arising from meditation. O f course, we can now meditate, but the 
wisdom arising from meditation will only come about when we have attained special 
insight. The point is that in order to attain a state called „a mind arisen from 
meditation‟ we need calm abiding, which in itself is s uch a mind. On the basis of this, 
you would attain a wisdom arisen from meditation. This means that by relying on this 
mind of calm abiding arisen from meditation, you would then attain to a wisdom 
arising from meditation that is special insight.   
 I feel that there might be a misunderstanding in the area of analytical 
meditation and special insight. Special insight is a state necessarily coming about on 
the basis of calm abiding, and is also necessarily a union with calm abiding. It is a 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
179 wisdom that is an analytical meditation, as well as a placement meditation. At the same 
time, the concentration connected with that is a calm abiding. Therefore, it is a union 
of calm abiding and special insight. The point being that any analytical meditation you 
do before having attained calm abiding is therefore not special insight. In analytical 
meditation before special insight, you would analyse your object, but you would not be 
able to hold it single -pointedly because of this analysis. Later, after having developed a 
concentration that is single -pointed, calm abiding; you would be able to single -
pointedly hold the object in analysis. At that point, you have the union of calm abiding 
and special insight, which is special insight. That is the difference between a 
meditati on that is special insight and an analytical meditation.  
So analytical meditation we can do right now, while special insight we can‟t apply yet.  
 
QUESTION:   Is the object of a wisdom arising from meditation necessarily emptiness 
or can it also be an object like the correct reliance on the teacher?  
GEN:  Even non -buddhists can have forms of a union of calm abiding and special 
insight. For example, someone foll owing an erroneous system in which a creator god is 
propounded, can nevertheless attain a state of calm abiding and special insight. It is 
possible to take any object, such as a flower, as an object of special insight. As a result, 
if you are analysing the  shape and petals of the flower after having attained calm 
abiding with regards to it, you would attain a special insight. However, because of this 
wrong view you wouldn‟t have a special insight with regards to emptiness. The direct 
realisation of emptines s becomes the supreme method for cutting the roots of samsara 
and getting liberated. Someone having this kind of supreme state becomes free from all 
wrong views, and achieves a very high level of attainment. When non -buddhists attain 
calm abiding and speci al insight, it is a temporal measure for them to pacify the 
grossest afflictions. This is because on the basis of these meditations they are for 
example able to penetrate to the form realm, where they can suppress certain 
afflictions.  
 It is advised to tak e a beautiful object as the object to develop calm 
abiding as it is easier to work with. As Buddhists, we are always advised to take a 
statue of the Buddha as the object because we received the teachings through his 
kindness, and since we are naturally dra wn to him, we will receive inspiration and be 
uplifted in the mediation.  
 In a nutshell, in order for something to be special insight it doesn‟t have 
to be referring to emptiness. The wisdom arising from meditation and special insight 
are most likely the same, but it is possible through extended analysis that you find 
some difference. However, just speaking from the heart, it seems to be the same 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 180 – week one to seven  because saying „arisen from meditation‟ refers to arisen from calm abiding. Therefore, 
if you have a wisdom ari sing in dependence on calm abiding, then it is special insight.

2B4B  The stages of how to train the mind once you have relied  
1 The basis of leisure - exhortation to take its essence  
2 How to take the essence  
The second one has tw o points: with regard to the basis of leisure - exhortation to take 
its essence and how  to take the essence.  
 
2B4B -1 The basis of leisure - exhortation to take its essence  
A Identification of leisure and endowments  
B Considering their great value  
C Considering the difficulty in finding them  
The first one has three points: identification of leisure and endowments, considering 
their great value and considering the difficulty in finding them.  
 
2B4B -1A Identification of leisure and endowments  
1 Leisur e 
2 Endowments  
The first one has tw o points: leisure and endowments.  
 
2B4B -1A1 Leisure  
A Identification of leisure  
First: In the Summary  it says:  
Through rules the essence of the many w orlds of animals  
and eight unfree states are renounced – thus constant leisure 
w on. 
Accordingly, leisure consists in the freedom from the eight unfree states.  
 
There are states without leisure, and we need to identify these because in order to 
practice the dharma we need a life that is free of those states that lack  opportunity. 
This is how the precious human rebirth is expressed which has the leisures 
[translator’s note : sometimes translated as “freedoms ”] and endowments.  
 Rules  (tib: thrim ) refer to ethical discipline (tib: Tshül -thrim  being almost the same 
word).  If you guard ethical discipline, you are renouncing the many states of animal 
rebirth. The second two lines express that through ethical discipline you not only 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
181 abandon any possibility of this kind of rebirth, but you also renounce the eight unfree 
states . This is what is meant by Thus constant leisure is won . To do this we need to 
identify these unfree states. Instead of saying unfree states we could also translate it as 
states lacking in opportunity, in that they lack the opportunity to practice the dhar ma.  
 
B The eight unfree states:  
[Living in] a border remote country w here the four types of follow ers [of the Buddha] 
do not roam, stupidity or being dumb, being of incomplete faculties - missing a limb, 
an ear etc., holding w rong view s that regard past a nd future lives, actions and their 
effects and the Three J ew els as non -existent, being w ithout the w ord of a Jina, with no 
buddha having appeared [in the w orld] are the four unfree states of humans. { 49}  The 
three bad migrations and being a long -lived god are the non -human unfree states. As 
for the long -lived gods, the Commentary on the Letter to a Friend discusses tw o types: 
discriminationless ones and formless ones. The former live in one region of the fourth 
concentration, Great Result, as if in a solita ry place far aw ay from a village. The 
latter are ordinary beings born in the formless realms. In Discourse on the Eight 
Unfree States gods of the desire realm constantly distracted by activities of desire are 
explained to be  long -lived gods.  
 
The four typ es of followers : There are different ways to explain this. One is to say that 
the four types are the fully ordained monk and nun (gelong and gelong -ma) and the 
novice monk and nun (getsül and getsül -ma). Another way is to again posit the fully 
ordained mon k and nun as two and then taking the male and female novice as one 
group, and the lay people as the fourth. The second one, stupidity  (tib: länpa ) or being 
dumb (tib: kukpa ): the words used here have a variety of meanings. The first one 
describes someone w ho is deaf, the second someone who is mute. In colloquial 
Tibetan the first word, länpa , means that however much you explain something to a 
person, he won‟t listen. Such a person would be called deaf in the colloquial language. 
Another situation for the sa me word is if there is a life threatening situation and 
nevertheless there is someone who goes into such a situation. Such a person would be 
called deaf or stupid. Another usage would be for someone who becomes a monk for 
example, and after coming to the m onastery he doesn‟t engage into any kinds of 
activities for monks. All this would benefit him but nevertheless he doesn‟t engage. So 
such a person would also be called dumb or stupid. The second one ( kukpa ) definitely 
refers to being mute, having a problem  in the sense faculty and not to being dumb. 
Only in the colloquial this word would mean dumb.  
The second unfree state doesn‟t only refer to being mute or dumb but to being  of 
incomplete faculties , meaning being incomplete in limbs or sense faculties. The  third one 
is holding wrong views . Here it is quite obvious that if someone holds wrong views there 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 182 – week one to seven  is no way that the dharma could benefit him. The fourth one is being without the word of 
a Jina . Meaning that no buddha has come, or not come yet. For examp le in the many 
centuries before Buddha Shakyamuni came, there was no possibility to practice the 
dharma. So our situation is different, since the buddha has come already.  
These are the four unfree states of humans. This means you have been born as a 
human  but you were born in an area which is a barbaric country, with incomplete sense 
faculties, are holding on to wrong views or at a time when the buddha has not 
appeared yet. Although in our time the buddha has come, still you could be born in 
such a barbari c country where the four types of followers are not present. Or you 
might be in a country where there are the four followers, but you are lacking in your 
sense faculties, or are mute. Or you don‟t have any of these impediments, the buddha 
has come and you live in a place where there are the four types of followers, but you 
have wrong views. Also here you won‟t be able to practice. Any time when the buddha 
has not come, even when you are born human and have all three other leisures, still 
you obviously won‟t  be able to practice the dharma. So this talks about unfree states 
although you have been born human.  
There are also four unfree states listed connected to a non -human rebirth. The 
first three are simply a rebirth in the lower realms. In such a rebirth th ere will be no 
possibility to practice the dharma. The same is also true for the long life god. This 
refers to a rebirth in the god realm, which is a certain rebirth in the fourth mental 
absorption of the form realm. In this there is a certain type of god,  called a god 
without discrimination. This is a being that lives for a very long time but has no 
discrimination, and therefore when they are born they know they are born, and when 
they will die they have a discrimination that they will die, but in between there is no 
other discrimination in this long life. They are also unable to practice the dharma and 
therefore lack opportunity.  
Taking these four rebirths together with the other four mentioned previously, 
you get the eight states that lack opportunity. I f you avoided them, then you have the 
leisures.  
 
QUESTION:  If you lack the word of a Jina, because the buddha didn‟t yet appear, does 
this mean there is automatically a lack of the first freedom, the four followers not 
being present?  
GEN:  The speech of the buddha being present or not comes down to the three baskets 
being present or not. I feel that it doesn‟t necessarily follow that you have the speech 
of the buddha because you have the four types of followers. An example would be the 
case if they aren‟t scholars and don‟t have knowledge of the three baskets. In an area 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
183 where Bhikkshus, Getsüls  and so forth are present, but don‟t know about the Tripitaka  
(the three baskets), you would lack this leisure of the speech of the buddha. If one of  
the four followers is present, it is already enough. Because what you need is someone 
who is practicing and studying the dharma.  
 
QUESTION:  If you have the first three leisures complete, but for the time being the 
teaching of the buddha is not present, then surely the buddha would emanate a teacher 
for you since you already have such good karma?  
GEN:  This could be quite possible. If you have strong faith and interest, then this is 
very possible.  
  
2B4B -1A2 Endowments  
 
These refer to ten types of conducive conditions of which some are present in our own 
continuum and some in the continua of others.  
 
A The five personal endowments  
The second one  has tw o points of which the five personal endowments are said to be 
“human nature, birth in a central region, complete faculties, no extremely perverted 
deeds [and] faith in the foundation”. Here “birth in a central region” is to be born in 
a place where the fourfold follow ers roam. “Complete faculties” consist in not being 
stupid or dumb and having complete limbs as w ell as minor body parts such as eyes 
and ears. “No extremely perverted deeds” implies not having committed or caused to 
commit an action of immediate retribution. “Faith in the foundation” is faith in the 
foundation from which all the mundane and supramundane white dharmas are born, 
discipline. Here “discipline” should be applied to all three collections [ of scriptures].  
 As those five are bro ught together in one’s ow n mental continuum and 
constitute circumstances for the accomplishment of [the] Dharma, they are called 
“personal endow ments”.  
 
Faith in the foundation : This refers to the root of all dharma practice, which is 
discipline. This is t o be applied to all three collections of scriptures. [Translator‟s note: 
The word in Tibetan is actually the word for vinaya (dül ba). Maybe it is better translated as 
subduing as it refers to the three collections of scriptures (Tripitaka)].   
 
B The five endowments conditioned by others  
The five endow ments conditioned by others are said to be:  
A buddha arrived and taught the holy Dharma,  
the teachings are abiding and being follow ed,  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 184 – week one to seven  there are hearts w ith loving concern for others.  
 
Here the “A buddha ar rived or emerged” means that, after accumulating the 
collections throughout three countless eons, he has manifestly attained perfect 
Buddahood. “taught the holy Dharma” means that the Buddha or his hearers have 
taught the Dharma. “The Dharma teachings are abiding” means that the Dharma 
that is accomplished by means of actualizing the Dharma of the ultimate { 50}  has not 
degenerated. “The abiding Dharma teachings being follow ed” means that through that 
very realisation of the Dharma, beings see that they have  the pow er to directly perceive 
the holy Dharma and follow  the teachings in accordance w ith that realisation. 
“Hearts of loving concern for others” means that there are alms -givers and benefactors 
w ho grant monastic robes and the like. As those five are pr esent in the mental 
continuum of others and constitute circumstances for the accomplishment of the 
Dharma, they are called “endow ments conditioned by others”.  
 
The teachings are abiding : This means that the ones practicing actually have realisations, 
and t hey haven‟t degenerated. This doesn‟t mean that the teachings are standing 
somewhere on the ground! They have to stand/abide in the mental continua of beings. 
The abiding Dharma teachings being followed : Here you have a teacher who has 
realisations of the dharma, and he perceives whether his disciples have the merit of 
following the teachings. On that basis, he teaches them in accordance with what he 
realised himself. If there are such disciples present, then you have people following the 
dharma (translator s note: maybe it is good to check this point also in the Lam -Rim Chenmo as it is 
a difficult part in Tibetan,  and I think the translator of the text might have had some difficulties 
with it) . Loving concern  talks about people being present who support dhar ma practice. 
We can think about our own situation here in Nalanda. We can ask from where this 
prayer hall, the monastery and everything came from? It is very clear that there were 
people who sponsored all of this, and due to them we can enjoy it.  
 
In this way we have here the five endowments conditioned by others, which together 
with the previous five make ten endowments and eight leisures.  
  
QUESTION:  Taught the holy dharma : does this holy (or sublime) refer to the Prasangika 
view having been taught? Because only this view can liberate you from samsara.  
GEN:  This doesn‟t refer to any specific school. This isn‟t the meaning here, but if this 
comes up in your mind, then that is good! The word holy is here because it is dharma 
that is practiced by holy be ings. The vinaya is such an example of holy dharma because 
it is practiced by holy beings, but it is not necessarily practiced by Prasangika 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
185 followers. The protector Nagarjuna for example practiced the vinaya and held the 
Prasangika view, but there is no c onnection here. We could posit the same example for 
Chandrakirti. The vinaya is “holy” independent of the view that the one practicing it 
holds. You don‟t have to be an Arya being in order to be a holy being. But if you think 
like this, it is positive and you will accumulate virtue!   
 
2B4B -1B Considering their great value  
1 Considering the inappropriateness of someone with this special base acting like an 
animal  
Second: If no practice of the completely pure Dharma for the sake of lasting happiness 
comes ab out at all, you are like an animal despite your birth in a higher realm, since 
animals also have the endeavour merely to attain happiness and eliminate suffering 
until they die.  
 
We have attained such a qualified human rebirth, and therefore have the basis  for 
really bringing about some dharma work. But if instead of using this special state we 
only think about attaining some happiness in this life, then we are no different from an 
animal, and we act as if we wouldn‟t have this precious human rebirth at all . Animals 
only think about this life, and also only try to bring about some happiness and avoid 
problems. In the same way, by only thinking about this life you are really wasting this 
opportunity of a precious human rebirth.  
With this foundation of a prec ious human rebirth, we can achieve all the 
temporal and ultimate goals. Already saying that we want to live this life excellently is 
possible because of this basis, and the mind that focuses on the benefit of future lives 
can also be achieved with it. Not only that, but the one who wants to get liberation 
from samsara can achieve it with this basis, and especially a Mahayana practitioner 
aiming for complete enlightenment has to rely on such a precious human rebirth. We 
can look at others who don‟t have this  basis, such as being born as an animal, a god or 
demi -god (asura) or even a god of the form realm. These states of the gods are in 
general all higher states than the human state, but with regards to practicing dharma 
there is no supreme state to the preci ous human rebirth. Especially for the goal of 
attaining bodhicitta, this precious human rebirth is the supreme basis.  
 
 
TUES DAY 16 MARCH 2010  - SESSION 2 
QUESTION:  In the Lam-Rim Chenmo [Page 121]  Lama Tsongkhapa says that the first 
four opportunities comi ng from others are incomplete for us. Why is that?  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 186 – week one to seven  GEN:  This refers to, for example, where it is said that a buddha has visited (the 
Tibetan uses the present tense here, the buddha has come, you are in his presence, 
which is not the case for us because al ready many centuries passed since he actually 
was present). Nevertheless, through the centuries we have a connection and someone 
who is here instead of him, in his place. So we have something similar to this. When it 
says that the sublime teaching is being  taught, then this again refers to the buddha or 
his disciples imparting the teachings in the present. This is also not the case for us (the 
actual word for disciple is hearer, referring also to pratyeka -buddhas and practitioners 
of the Mahayana as well; a nyone on an arya path). At the time of the buddha the 
dharma was taught by himself or by aryas. Nowadays we might not have that as we 
don‟t know if our guru actually has the arya -path in his continuum (nevertheless, we 
see him as having it). So now we have  a simile, as we have the guru teaching in place of 
the buddha or the arya. But regardless of whether he is on an arya -path or not, he is 
teaching the dharma. So we don‟t have the actual thing but something similar, which is 
sufficient for us.  
 
 
2 How a bo dy of leisure and endowments is best  
For the accomplishment of the Mahayana path a base like the one explained earlier is 
necessary. In the Letter to a Disciple  it says:  
The base of the path of those gone to bliss, the tool for leading 
beings,  
that very st rength of mind, a strength that human beings 
have gained  
that path has not been found by gods or nagas - nor by 
asuras,  
not by garudas, vidyadhara, kinnaras or snakes.  
 
Furthermore, although some gods of the desire realm w ith strong habitual tendencies 
from having previously trained on the path on a human basis, are suitable as a basis 
for seeing the truths for the first time, it is impossible to attain the arya path for the 
first time on an upper realm  basis. How ever, as explained before, the desire re alm 
gods are said to be, for the most part, an unfree state and therefore not the best basis 
for attaining the path for the first time. Again, an inhabitant of the continent of 
Uttarakuru is unsuitable as a basis for vow s, which is w hy the bases of [the ot her] 
three continents is praised - and out of those [especially] the inhabitants of 
J ambudvipa.  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
187 This quote, refers to the path of those gone to bliss [translator‟s note: Gen -la reads it 
differently than in the text] . This refers to the practice of the six  perfections. Then it talks 
about the base of this path. One practices the six perfections on the base of all 
sentient beings. One engages into the deeds of the bodhisattvas in order to lead all 
migrators out of suffering to higher states, and therefore th ese beings form the base 
for the paths of those gone to bliss. As a result, sentient beings form the base for the 
practice of the bodhisattva deeds, in other words for the six perfections, which can 
also be referred to as the path of those gone to bliss.  
So how would someone actually have the strength, confidence and enthusiasm 
to actually engage into such a path? This is done by developing a mind of great 
strength that is so powerful that it takes on the goals and tasks of oneself and all 
others. This pow erful mind is bodhicitta, which is able to engage into the bodhisattva 
deeds in order to lead all sentient beings, and therefore is able to walk the path of the 
ones gone to bliss. The gods and nagas are unable to find this mind of bodhicitta, and 
as a res ult, it is only obtainable by humans. Then there is also a long list of other 
beings who can‟t find this mind. All these god -like beings don‟t have the capacity to 
develop this bodhicitta which enables them to practice the six perfections for the sake 
of all sentient beings. The meaning here is quite profound although the Tibetan is 
quite difficult to understand.  
 
The point is to express how valuable this precious human rebirth is which is qualified 
by the eight freedoms and ten endowments. There is no oth er state which is more 
precious. The commentary says that there are some cases of first having had a human 
rebirth, and through having placed very strong imprints of dharma practice in one‟s 
continuum, then after being reborn as a desire realm god and due to these imprints 
such a person can practice dharma and even generate bodhicitta, understand the two 
truths and attain to an arya path (i.e. attain the path of seeing). However, this is due to 
previously having been a human, as it is impossible to attain t o these states through 
just taking rebirth as a god. Additionally, the gods of the realms above the desire 
realm, the gods of the form and formless realms can‟t generate these minds no matter 
how strong their imprints are, because these imprints can‟t be a ctivated in such a state.  
Nevertheless, the vast majority of desire realm gods won‟t be able to practice 
dharma, and their state is said to lack opportunity as they unable to practice dharma. 
So in general, such gods can‟t generate such realisations, such  minds. The remaining 
three continents of the human realm are better, but even amongst these three 
Jambudvipa  (our continent) is praised as supreme for dharma practice.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 188 – week one to seven  Therefore, we have to understand how special our situation is, and make effort 
to take  the essence from it.  
 
3 How to train in the wish to take the essence, having brought to mind those [two] 
objectives  
‘Consequently, w hy w ould I act in such a w ay that an excellent base like this one I 
have gained { 51}  does not have a result? If I did not  make it meaningful, could there 
be a greater self -betrayal and a bigger stupidity? Having passed through many perilous 
places of unfreedom, such as the bad migrations, again and again, if I w ere to go back 
to those places, forsaking this one -off liberatio n as meaningless, it w ould be like 
turning mindless as though stupefied by a magic spell’. By w ay of thoughts like these 
you should meditate again and again. In  Engaging in Bodhisattva Behaviour it says:  
Having found this kind of leisure,  
not to cultivate  w hat is virtuous - 
no betrayal could be a greater,  
nothing more deluded than that.  
And: 
Having found this place so rare,  
hard to find and beneficial,  
if I, w ell aw are of it,  
w ere taken to that hell again  
as if deluded by magic spells - 
has my mind here w as ted aw ay? - 
through w hat delusion I do not know  either - 
w hat has become of my mind - my feelings?  
 
One-off liberation  refers to becoming aware how this state is one where one is 
temporarily liberated from rebirth in the lower realms. The one-off refers to a one in a 
million opportunity. If having gained this opportunity, the outcome is that one just 
goes back to the lower realms, then there could be no greater deception than that. It 
says here that if someone would act in this way, he would end u p like having no mind, 
meaning he would be like someone who never thinks about anything.  
 Therefore, we should have the outlook that our precious human rebirth is 
extremely valuable and we have nothing more precious than this. This kind of rebirth 
is somet hing that can‟t be bought by money or exchanged. It is exactly the one you 
have right now, this is the precious rebirth, and therefore it would be very sad to waste 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
189 it. We can‟t change the time that has already passed in our life, so when we talk about 
not wasting our time, we talk about the time still ahead of us.  
 We have already wasted a lot of time in our life and lives. For a long time 
we didn‟t engage into dharma practice, but just into pointless activities from which 
there remains no potential fruit ion. We don‟t have any results from the past, and in 
this way, we can reflect on how much meaningless time we have allowed to pass in this 
and past lives. If we continue like this, well aware of our situation and knowing that we 
will definitely die, the ti me will run out. We don‟t know when we will die, but it will 
happen. In this way, if we waste this precious human rebirth by just continuing in 
pointless activities, then there is no greater self deception than that.  
 The same is expressed in a longer quo te here by Shantideva. Having found 
this place so rare  shows how difficult it is to find this precious human rebirth. Well 
aware of it : we know now about this whole situation, how difficult it is to find and 
about the danger of the lower realms. So while k nowing all this, we are taken to th at 
hell again . 
 The last line might be better translated as “what is there inside of me?” 
what is going on inside of me, what delusion is it that makes me waste such a precious 
human rebirth and again leads me to the hell s?  
 
4 The inappropriateness of returning empty -handed, given that not only ultimate but also 
temporary objectives are easily accomplished  
Thus you should also consider the method that allow s you easily to attain the causes 
of a higher realm body, resource s, and a retinue w hich are not only greatly meaningful 
from an ultimate but also from a temporary point of view by means of this basis: 
[practicing] giving, ethical discipline, patience and so on. If you do not apply yourself 
day and night to the causes of  those tw o by means of that greatly meaningful basis, it 
w ould seem like returning empty -handed from an island of precious jew els.  
 
Furthermore, it says that not only ultimate results, like liberation or omniscience but 
even temporal results, like rebirth as a god or human can be attained on this special 
basis. What can be especially attained is to find again such an excellent rebirth as a 
basis to practice in future lives. Therefore,  don‟t waste it. Since you can again attain an 
excellent basis for dharma p ractice, don‟t waste it! You can attain complete liberation 
from samsara, so don‟t waste it. And you can even go to complete enlightenment on 
this basis, so don‟t waste it!  
We should really make effort to use this precious human rebirth and take the 
essen ce from it. If instead we just throw it away, then we would be similar to someone 
who comes to an island full of jewels, but leaves empty handed.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 190 – week one to seven  2B4B -1C Considering the difficulty in finding it  
1 Illustration of the difficulty of finding it  
2 Reasons wh y it is difficult to find
