2B4B -1C1 Illustr ation of the difficulty in finding it  
Third: In the Basis of Scriptural Transmission it says that beings w ho die and migrate 
from the bad and happy realms to the bad realms are like the dust of the great earth 
and that the beings w ho are born from there in to the happy realms are like the dust 
taken up by the tip of a finger nail. { 52}  Therefore, [a precious human rebirth] is 
difficult to find from both the happy and the bad realms.  
 
This expresses the difficulty of obtaining a good rebirth.  
 
2B4B -1C2 Reasons why it is difficult to find  
If you ask just w hy they are it is so difficult to find, it says in the Four Hundred  
[Verses]:  
For the most part people fix  
on the side that is unholy.  
Consequently common beings  
mostly surely go to bad realms.  
 
Accordi ngly, there are many humans and other beings who mostly fix on the side of the 
ten non -virtues and that is also w hy, consequently, they go to the bad realms. 
Furthermore, if, for each moment of anger and the like tow ards a bodhisattva, you 
have to spend on e eon in the [Avici] hell of incessant torment, the negativities 
accumulated over many lifetimes which have not come to fruition nor been overcome 
by antidotes, and w hich are present in your ow n continuum, w ill doubtlessly cause you 
to spend many eons in b ad realms.  
 
Most part (of) people  refers to the vast majority of beings. They engage in non -virtue, 
and as a very definite effect they will take rebirth in an evil migration. Furthermore, it 
is expressed how most people engage into the ten non -virtuous ac tions and thereby 
accumulate causes for the lower realms. As we have these kinds of imprints on our 
continuum, and most people don‟t know how to purify them, all these imprints will 
remain there, and the result will be a rebirth in the lower realms.  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
191 QUEST ION:  Since it is difficult to generate strong regret for something we don‟t 
remember doing, will the little regret we can generate still be strong enough to purify 
sufficiently?  
GEN:  The way to deal with this is to first of all not say that you most likel y did these 
negative actions in the past, but that you definitely did them due to being under the 
power of strong afflictions. As a result, you will do everything in your power to face 
the afflictions, and not give them an opportunity to arise in the futur e. 
 In order to purify one would rely on the Lam-Rim and try to study, reflect and 
cultivate the different teachings given here, and it is right that that is actually the best 
method to purify the mind. You purify the mind by cultivating a positive mind, b y 
changing it so it is steered away from the negative side, and in this way the mind 
becomes pure.  Purification practice doesn‟t necessarily mean that one does 
prostrations or accumulates mantras. Right here this activity of studying and reflecting 
on the  Lam-Rim purifies, but still we need to have this mind of regret towards our 
negative actions in the past. Therefore, one would again go through the process of 
understanding how one has definitely done all kinds of negative actions in the past due 
to the s trength of the afflictions. One can generate this so strongly that the feeling is 
as powerful as recalling things that you have done in this life. This is the first step. On 
this basis, you then strongly engage into the activity of listening, studying and 
reflecting on the Lam-Rim, and by repeatedly doing this, a familiarisation can finally 
come about, and from this a result and purification will arise.  
 
QUESTION:  What is the use then of doing prostrations and Vajrasattva purification 
every day?  
GEN:  When w e study the Lam-Rim we can easily find extensive explanations on what 
to do. And if you act in accordance with that, you will purify. If you sit down and do 
the hundred syllable mantra or do prostrations while reflecting on the meaning, then 
this will puri fy and change your mind. The physical aspect of prostrating won‟t do 
anything. The strongest and most amazing forms of purification are brought about by 
bodhicitta and the wisdom realising emptiness. So we have to understand the meaning 
of what we do and t hen this will bring about purification. Bodhicitta is also the most 
powerful method for accumulating merit. On the merit side this is the most powerful 
mind, while the mind directly realising emptiness is the most powerful mind on the 
wisdom side. With thi s mind, purification is actually happening because the afflictions 
are swept away. Already on the path of seeing, this wisdom dispels certain types of 
afflictions and then in stages it progressively dispels more and more; it dispels 
obscurations which are afflictions and karma, and will also eventually dispel the 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 192 – week one to seven  knowledge obscurations. All of this is done by this mind, and nothing is stronger than 
that. As a result, saying that there is one practice that can bring about purification but 
not another is a wr ong understanding.  
 
 
TUES DAY 16 MARCH 2010  - SESSION 3 
QUESTION:  I thought Vajrasattva practice is the strongest purification practice of all? 
Is that right?  
GEN:  If you do it exactly as it needs to be done, through strongly reflecting on 
emptiness, then i t is the strongest, but there will be no power by only reciting the 
mantra. The strength comes from this mind realising emptiness. There is no stronger 
purification than that. This holds true for any kind of deity practice, because in order 
to do any of th em in a fully -fledged way, all of them need to be done with a mind 
realising emptiness. This mind is the strongest with regards to purification, just as 
bodhicitta is the strongest with regards to accumulating merits.  
 When the different lamas give an emp owerment into a deity, they will also praise 
the great power of the deity. This deity indeed has this power, if you do the practice as 
it needs to be done with a mind realising emptiness. That‟s what makes these deity 
practices so powerful. If you take a s ubsequent permission [tib: jenang ] or 
empowerment [tib: wang] into a deity, then with any of these tantric rituals the lama 
will praise this specific deity and its practice, and emphasize that if you don‟t engage in 
it, you won‟t achieve realisations. And this is true. In this way, teachers will emphasize 
that if you don‟t practice this and that deity, you won‟t have a method to gain 
realisations. This is to be understood in the context of how these need to be practiced 
because if you engage in these practi ces in fully -fledged way with the realisation of 
emptiness and bodhicitta, then there is nothing more powerful than that.  
 In our case, us being beginners, the most powerful practice for us to engage in 
is ethical discipline. This includes all its forms, starting from the one avoiding the ten 
non-virtuous actions. This is the most powerful practice because if you really come t o 
possess ethical discipline, then you possess the foundation on which all good qualities 
can grow.  
Let‟s say we have someone who gained a precious human rebirth. On that basis 
this person generates some level of the mind of definite emergence, and then t akes the 
vows of ordination. Having received these vows he protects them as they should be 
protected, and on top of this he also accumulates and purifies. In this way, he will be 
able to achieve all kinds of attainments.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
193 Another example: In the context of  secret mantra, to become a suitable vessel 
someone would take an initiation together with all kinds of pledges. Through this his 
ethical discipline will be to guard all of his pledges. And in order to ripen his 
continuum he will engage into the generation  stage, and once it has ripened to a certain 
extent, he engages into the practice of the different levels of the completion stage. 
Nevertheless, right from the beginning he is guarding his ethical discipline.  
 Therefore, with regards to accumulation and pu rification the most powerful 
mind is bodhicitta. But in our own practice this would be very difficult to use, as we 
are unable to access this mind at this stage. In order to make it arise, Shantideva says 
in the Bodhisattvacharyavatara  how to first praise bodhicitta and then bring it about 
through purifying and accumulating. Therefore, as a cause for attaining this mind, we 
should engage into these two activities which, can be done for example, by making 
offerings. This is already done here in our assembly hall and our room by making 
offerings, and also by doing work for the monastery. But you have to have the right 
mindset for this. You have to do it with enthusiasm and want to do it very excellently, 
for example when entering the assem bly hall, first you put together your hands and 
make three prostrations and so forth, and in this way one is already accumulating 
merit.  
And accumulating merits alone already purifies negativities. This always comes 
together.  ... If someone says that tod ay I will accumulate merit and tomorrow I will 
purify, then this is a wrong understanding. When you do the one you will also do the 
other, and this kind of practice is very supportive for whatever is your main practice. 
If next to studying the Lam-Rim you are able to do other practices to accumulate virtue 
and purify, like 100.000 migtsemas  or 100.000 refuge prayers, then this is very 
conducive.  
Also if someone says that for the time being he needs to abandon studies in 
order to do his preliminaries, then t his is a wrong understanding of practice as well. 
This is expressed in quotes where it says “don‟t abandon the main thing”. The main 
thing for us is studying the Lam-Rim. So if next to this you do some preliminaries, then 
this will be very helpful.  Howeve r, if you do it the other way around, then this would 
be such a case of giving up the main activity for a less important one.  
 Prostrations are even good for your physical wellbeing. So this is something very 
supportive, and especially if you can do it to gether with the migtsemas  and refuge 
prayers then this will be very beneficial. Another practice where great merits can be 
accumulated is casting tsa -tsas, but once you made them you have to store them in a 
good place.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 194 – week one to seven   So these preliminaries are very sup portive in your practice and they need to be 
done. There is a way of doing preliminaries by doing one hundred thousand of this and 
that. Amongst these different preliminaries, if someone for example would say that the 
Vajrasattva practice is the supreme, t hen this is not really correct. You can‟t really say 
that one is better than the other, and the same holds true for the different deity 
practices as the benefit is completely dependent on your attitude during the practice. 
You can‟t say that any of the ind ividual yidams have more power than the others 
because they are all the same in being completely enlightened buddhas. For example, 
the glorious Ghuyasamaja is said to be the king of tantras. The buddha taught this 
tantra while taking on the form of Ghuyasa maja, and the same is true for Heruka and 
Vajrabhairava. In this way, they are all of the same form and the same capacity.  
One time His Holiness the Dalai Lama was saying that nowadays everyone wants 
to have a Kalachakra initiation, and he doesn‟t underst and how it came about that 
everyone is so much into this. There is really no reason why one would be more 
interested in this than any of the other yidams, since all of them are completely 
enlightened buddhas. If there would be such a difference between the  yidams, then the 
one with smaller capacity wouldn‟t be a buddha. The point is that while all of them 
come from a completely enlightened buddha with no limitations, still all of the 
different aspects are taught and shown by the buddha in accordance with th e disciple 
to be subdued. So for someone who needs to be subdued by strong love and 
compassion there is Chenrezig, if someone needs to be subdued by a strong wisdom 
aspect there is Manjushri, with regards to healing power there is the aspect of the 
medicin e buddhas and so on.  
 We hear about all kinds of lamas and the deities they recommend, and think 
they have all kinds of capacities and special powers, but there is nothing stronger then 
the wisdom realising emptiness and bodhicitta, which act as the found ation for deity 
yoga. If someone is not from the bottom of his heart aspiring for bodhicitta, but at the 
same time wants to have an empowerment into a deity, then there is no way he will 
receive this. The very basis for receiving it is having generated bod hicitta. This is even 
true for Kriya Tantra. As a result, if this is not part of the mental setup of the person 
taking the empowerment, then he can go there but he won‟t receive it. The same is 
true for attending a jenang .  
 
2B4B -1C3  The rarity of those o btaining the causes of leisure and endowments  
Although, if you thus made sure to clear aw ay and purify the causes of bad migrations 
accumulated earlier and closed the door to new  engagement in them, happy migrations 
w ould not be rare, as it is, they are ve ry rare indeed. If you fail to do so, you go to bad 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
195 migrations, and, once you have gone to bad migrations, you do not create virtue, but 
alw ays commit negativites and therefore do not even hear the name of happy realms 
for many eons, as it says in Engaging  in Bodhisattva Behaviour.  
 
2B4B -1C4 The result of sustaining those objectives  
Potow a said: “In the region of Pan there w as a solid fort by the name of Ma’i Chakar 
w hich had been seized by an enemy. For a long time the people had become poor 
servants. One old man used to be in anguish about this fort and when, one day, he 
heard people saying that it had been w on [back], he grabbed his spear and, not being 
able to walk, dragged himself along saying: ‘If only the capture of Ma’i Chakar was 
not dream’. Likew is e, having w on leisure and endow ments, one must also derive such 
joy from it and practice Dharma.” { 53}  In accordance w ith this statement, you should 
meditate in order to gain this kind of attitude.  
 
The region of Pan is the area where Dagri Rinpoche, Lama Yeshe and Lama Künchog 
are from. North of this area is the monastery of the Kadampas, and even further north 
is the area where most of them came from. I actually don‟t understand what‟s written 
here, because it is from a very old time. I also never had the  opportunity to ask but I 
will do it in the future. I have explained the Lam-Rim many times, but whenever I come 
to this passage I just drop it! Anyway, the point here is that we should relate to our 
precious human rebirth in a way that we actually make it  meaningful. One would 
generate four thoughts with regard to the dharma to take the real essence of this basis 
of freedoms and endowments.  
WEDNESDAY  17 MARCH 2010  - SESSION 1 
ADVICE  
When we are listening to dharma we should make effort to abandon the thre e faults of 
the vessel and generate the six discriminations. When you explain the Dharma you 
should have a state which is impartial, not lazy (procrastination), or with a mind which 
is easily fatigued. By generating these discernments, such as seeing the D harma as 
medicine, whilst teaching or listening to the Dharma, there is great benefit 
forthcoming. Then you can retain whatever you are listening to or teaching.  
 The state of having the freedoms and endowments is very precious. We should 
have the motivat ion, “I have come here to study and practice the Dharma. Being here 
and having these freedoms and endowments, I will not waste this precious 
opportunity.”  
 Having found these freedoms and endowments we are asked not waste them and 
to take the essence of th is state. There are three levels of taking the essence, greatest, 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 196 – week one to seven  middling and smallest. The smallest is taking the essence with the attitude of a being of 
the small scope; this is the bare minimum of taking the essence.  
 The person of the small scope would be interested only in creating the causes 
for happiness in future lives. The middle way of taking the essence is practicing with 
the motivation of a being of the middle scope. This being understands that even in 
samsara,  if you are born in a happy migration, it is still full of problems and sufferings. 
They understand that wherever you are born in samsara it is a rebirth of suffering, 
whatever you are using or enjoying is always something in the nature of suffering and 
whomever you are with, they are also in the nature of suffering. This person thinks, 
“to stay in this state is unsuitable; I need to find a way to get liberated from this.” 
They would understand the cause of samsara is karma and afflictions and look for a 
way to remove these. With this attitude of gaining complete liberation they practice the 
Dharma, they take the essence of this life in a middling way. This comes down to 
taking the essence with the motivation of definite emergence.  
Then there is the being o f highest capacity. They see all sentient beings are 
wishing for happiness, but are not meeting happiness. They see sentient beings want to 
be free from suffering, but are tormented by suffering. They see that they want 
happiness, but are unaware of the me thods. All these sentient beings have been his 
mothers in past lives, and have been very close to him. He sees them as closely related 
from past lives and generates an attitude of wanting to free them from suffering, and 
to do this he wants to become fully  enlightened. With this attitude of a being of the 
great scope, you will take the essence of the precious human rebirth in the best way.  
 In order to have this wish to take the essence of these freedoms and 
endowments, the text explains four dharmas that  need to be complete in our mind.  
 
2B4B -1C5 The method of meditation with four special features  
Thus four thoughts regarding the Dharma are necessary for the generation of a fully 
qualified w ish to take the essence of this basis of leisure:  
 1. The need to accomplish [the Dharma] - because all sentient beings only want 
happiness and do not want suffering and attaining happiness and eliminating 
suffering only depends on the holy Dharma.  
 
Here one wants to accomplish the Dharma to set others in Dharma pract ice, so they 
can accumulate the cases for happiness and eliminate the causes for suffering. In order 
to help others one needs to attain the Dharma oneself. This Dharma, which is the only 
path to eliminate suffering, can then be given to others. This is nee ded because all 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
197 others are only looking for happiness and only want to get rid of suffering. This is 
what is meant by the need to accomplish the Dharma.  
 
 2. The ability to accomplish [the Dharma] - because you have the external 
condition, a spiritual teac her, and the internal condition, leisure and endow ments.  
 
We also understand that we have the capacity to accomplish the dharma. This is 
because one has the external conducive condition (having met the spiritual friend) and 
the internal conducive condition  (that one has all the freedoms and endowments). 
Having these, we understand we are perfectly capable of accomplishing the dharma.  
There is a need to accomplish the Dharma and I have the ability. Not only that, 
the third thought is that there is a need to accomplish the Dharma in this very life.  
 3. The need, also, to accomplish [the Dharma] in this life - because if you do 
not accomplish it w ithin this life, it w ill be difficult to find leisure and endow ments in 
many [future]  rebirths.  
 
If I don‟t accompli sh the Dharma in this life, it will be very difficult to find these 
internal and external conducive conditions again. I have to work on the Dharma in this 
life.  
 For example if we think, “In this life I won‟t accomplish dharma, but it‟s ok as I 
will in so me other life,” this is mistaken.  
 Now I have attained the extraordinary state where I have met the spiritual friend 
(the external conducive condition) and I have all the freedoms and endowments (the 
inner conducive condition). This specific and rare stat e has come about in this life, so 
in this very life I‟m going to practice the Dharma.  
 Not only do I have to practice in this life, but right now – today.  
 
 4. The need to accomplish [the Dharma] right now  - because the time of death 
is uncertain.  
 
We mi ght say, “I don‟t have time today, I will do it later.” But we don‟t know when we 
are going to die due to our own impermanence.  
 We think, “As all my mother sentient beings want to experience only happiness 
and be separated from suffering, I need to pract ice the Dharma for them. I am actually 
capable because I have all the conducive conditions so I need to practice in this very 
life. This will be very hard to find in a future life, so I need to practice in this life and 
in this very moment because it‟s not  certain when I‟m going to die.”  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 198 – week one to seven   
The third one of those counteracts the laziness of abandoning it, thinking “I should 
practice the Dharma in later lives” and the fourth one counteracts the laziness of not 
engaging in it thinking “it needs to be accomplis hed in this life, but although it was 
not accomplished in earlier years, months and days, it is enough to accomplish it much 
later on”. It is admissible to make them into three subsuming those tw o under “[the 
need to] accomplish it quickly”. Like that, rem embering death may also be linked in 
here. In any case, as this is a lengthy topic, it should be explained below .  
 
With the third thought a certain type of laziness is counteracted, a mind that puts the 
Dharma aside saying, “I‟ll do this in another life.” The fourth counteracts a laziness 
that says, “I do have to practice in this life, but I can do it tomorrow, next week or 
next year” and so on.  
 This would be an appropriate moment to go into the topic of our 
impermanence, that we are going to die, but Lama Tsongkhapa will explain this in 
detail later in the text.    
 
2B4B -1C6 Differentiated, sustained mode [of practice]  
A How to contemplate from various points of view  
B How to contemplate in particular  
2B4B -1C6A  How to contemplate from various points of view  
As the mind is more significantly subdued, if you contemplate from various points of 
view , you should contemplate as explained above. If you cannot do that much, you 
should subsume them under [threexxviii headings]: w hat the essence of leisure and 
endow men ts is, the manner in w hich they are greatly meaningful from a temporary 
and ultimate point of view , and how difficult they are to find from the point of view 
of cause and effect. You should study each of the points explained above as 
appropriate and medita te. 
 
We need to approach this topic from as many points of view as possible. The topic is 
how we need to take the essence of this life, how to do this, and how this is a very 
special life in which one can take the essence. We need to produce some essence f rom 
our life, starting right now.  
If we look back in our lives, we have engaged into all kinds of activities that 
were not Dharma. We then ask, “What is the outcome from this, the essence? What do 
I have to show for all these years I have spent in this li fe?” Actually, a lot of troubles 
have come from these activities. From all this you finally end up looking for a place 
where there are no troubles. Not having practiced dharma there is no essence. Not 
having produced this in the earlier part of life, we ca n say the same for previous lives. 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
199 There was no meaning achieved. Now I need to take the essence of this life, engaging 
into these four thoughts we think about the need to accomplish the dharma, the 
capacity of this life, right in this very life and right now, not tomorrow.  
 One would reflect on this topic by making clear in one‟s mind the definite 
enumeration of the freedoms and endowments. What is the essence or nature of these? 
Further, we reflect if we have these, we are in a state capable of bringing all temporary 
and ultimate benefit. We think of the difficulty of finding these freedoms and 
endowments. We cultivate the mind of how rare this is from the point of view of the 
cause and from the point of view of the effect.  
 Precious human rebirth does n ot come from one or two causes, but many need 
to come together to attain this rebirth. We can think of all these, the nature of these, 
how it achieves ultimate and temporary attainments, how it is difficult from the point 
of view of the cause and then from  the point of view of the effect.  
 
2B4B -1C6B  How to contemplate in particular  
1 The difficulty in finding [leisure and endowments] from the point of view of their cause  
First: The difficulty in finding [leisure and endow ments] from the point of view of 
their cause.  Generally speaking, { 54}  even for the attainment of a mere happy 
migration, it is necessary to perform one of the pure virtues such as discipline. In 
particular, for the attainment of complete leisure and endow ments, you need a lot of 
roots of v irtue such as getting hold of a base through pure ethical conduct, reinforcing 
it through giving and the like and making connections by means of stainless prayers. 
In that case, it becomes apparent that those who accomplish such causes are very few , 
w hich is why one should think that, proportionately, the result, the general and 
specific base of happy realms is difficult to find.  
 
If we think about our life now, the very fact we are born human shows in previous 
lives we engaged into a life of pure ethical d iscipline, because without this we wouldn‟t 
be born human. We must have engaged into a very good practice of generosity, 
otherwise it would not be possible that we now have such amazing outer conditions 
here for Dharma practice. We definitely engaged into strong and pure wishing prayers 
otherwise we could not have encountered the holy Dharma in this life. We must have 
engaged into a lot of these activities and in a very good way. Then we see that this 
result is very precious and comes from many causes. If b ased on all these causes from 
past lives we waste it, this would be a great loss. Therefore, we should take the essence 
from having gained these freedoms and endowments.  
   
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 200 – week one to seven  2 The difficulty of finding [leisure and endowments] from the point of view of the result  
The difficulty of finding [leisure and endow ments] from the point of view  of the result:  
 You meditate on the fact that, from the point of view  of the beings in bad 
migrations, different in rank from us, even the mere happy realms seem hard to attai n, 
and from the point of view of beings in happy migrations, of the same rank, too, 
distinguished leisurexxix is extremely rare. Geshe Dolpa laid great emphasis on this. He 
said that all other teachings come about brought on by this. As it is like this you 
should strive accordingly.  
 
Here we reflect on other visible migrators, like animals. We think about the quantity of 
beings in this realm compared to the human realm. Even though there are many 
humans on this planet, we can count them. However, the animals a re beyond number, 
no one would be able to count them. Just think about insects or the fish in the ocean, 
no one knows how many there are. Just in this property of Nalanda, all the worms, 
insects and other animals - we probably have more here than there are  humans on the 
entire planet. From this, we have to understand there are many more beings in the 
preta realm, and again more than these are being in the hell realms. From this 
reflection on the result, we see there are many more beings in lower realms than  in the 
human realm.  
 All the beings in these realms only want to be happy – they don‟t want to suffer 
and in this way are just like us. How did this happen, that they are in these realms but 
only wanted to happy like us? The cause is not having guarded e thical discipline. 
Having engaged into strong negativity, they ended up as a hell being. With a moderate 
amount of negativity, they ended in the preta realm, and from just a small amount, 
they ended up in the animal realm. This hasn‟t happened to us becaus e we did abide by 
ethical discipline. This is why we are born in the human realm and not in the lower 
realms.  
 We have all these wonderful outer conditions that are supportive to practice 
Dharma. We have a good financial situation that definitely came fro m having practiced 
generosity in the past. This will come about for any being that previously practiced 
generosity. From not having practiced generosity there is all kinds of poverty. Even 
amongst those humans who have practiced ethical discipline and have  obtained a 
human life, they are so many who are destitute and have very few resources. If this is 
the case for humans, then what need to mention the animals and what they are lacking, 
being in great danger etc.  We have all these wonderful conditions, so i t‟s clear we have 
practiced generosity in our past lives.  
We also made pure aspirations prayers in order for us to have met a religious 
system (Dharma in this case referring to any religious system). If we look at the 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
201 humans in this life, how many are interested in religion? Amongst those, how many are 
practicing  their religion? And amongst those, how many practice a religion which 
accepts cause and effect (karma)? How many of these take them on and practice a 
lifestyle that is in accord with this? This last group, buddhists who are actually 
practicing, is a very,  very small group on this planet. Maybe we are talking some 
thousands and no more. Those who are actually practicing dharma and integrating this 
into their lives, that actions have effects, we are amongst these. This has causes - pure 
prayers that were don e in past lives. This state of a precious human rebirth is 
exceptional. This is how one reflects. How difficult to find and how rare it is from the 
point of view of the result.  
 There is mention of the Kadampa Geshe Dölpa who emphasised these points 
very strongly. This means reflecting on the rarity of this state of having a precious 
human rebirth with freedoms and endowments, how difficult it is to find from the 
point of view of the cause, and from the point of view of the effect. If we then 
generate an u rgency to accomplish the Dharma with the four thoughts, if we reflect 
and understand all this, then we‟ll be able to generate good qualities.

If this precious human rebirth wi th freedoms and endowments is difficult to find, how 
should one take the essence?

B The actual way of taking its essence

presentation of the path and the actual w ay of taking its essence.  
 
2B4B -2A Generating certainty with regard to the general presentation of the path  
1 How all the Buddha’s teachings are contained in the paths of the three [types of] 
beings  
2 Giving reasons for gradual guidance from the point of view of the three [types of] 
beings  
The first one has tw o points: How  all the Buddha’s teachings are contained in the 
paths of the three [types of] beings and giving reasons for gradual guidanc e from the 
point of view  of the three [types of] beings.  
 
 
 
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 202 – week one to seven  2B4B -2A1 How all the Buddha’s teachings are contained in the paths of the three [types of] 
beings  
A How the meaning of all the excellent teachings [of the Buddha] is subsumed under the 
accomplishmen t of two objectives  
First: As everything the Buddha did, in the beginning the generation of the mind [of 
enlightenment], in the middle the accumulation of the collections and at the end the 
manifestation of perfect buddhahood, w as done for the w elfare of s entient beings only, 
all his Dharma teachings only bring about the w elfare of sentient beings, too. That 
being so, the goal to be accomplished, the w elfare of sentient beings is tw ofold: 
temporarily high status and ultimately certain goodness.  
 
This means  how the Buddha in the beginning generated the mind (love and 
compassion and on that basis, generated bodhicitta) and then the vast accumulation of 
merit. At the end, he attained full enlightenment. All these actions were done solely for 
the benefit of bei ngs. Then he taught the Dharma, which again was solely to achieve 
the benefit of beings.  
 Someone might ask, “What is to be achieved here?” For sentient beings, there is 
a temporal benefit and an ultimate benefit. The temporal benefit is that they attain 
high status (happy migrations) and the ultimate goal is certain goodness (attaining 
liberation or complete enlightenment.) All of the activities of the Buddha were for 
these temporal and ultimate goals.  
 
B How lesser beings attain high status  
{ 55}  As for the undertaking to attain the former: everything that has been set forth is 
included in the actual cycle of teachings for lesser beings or in the cycle of teachings 
common to lesser beings. Special lesser beings do not do much for the sake of this life, 
pursuing [instead] the excellence of high status in future lives [and] engaging in 
achieving the causes for that.  
As there are these two types, the Buddha taught many methods and paths in order to 
fulfil even this temporal aim. In other words, he taught man y paths to first get them 
out of the suffering realms. All these methods are included in the practices of a being 
of the small scope.  
 As the Buddha tried to bring all beings to happiness and separate them from 
suffering, the first step he tries is to get them out of the suffering realms and bring 
them to the higher realms. This is achieved through keeping the ethical discipline of 
avoidi ng the ten non -virtuous actions, and will accumulate the causes for a higher 
rebirth. In other words, by avoiding the ten non -virtuous actions, you avoid the 
negative karma that would lead you to the lower realms. This is the first level the 
Buddha teaches  - what the ten non -virtuous actions are and how to avoid them.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
203  A practitioner of the small scope means someone who is looking towards future 
lives and considers them more important than the life right now. However important 
the goals of this life are, t he duration of future lives is far longer. We have a certain 
number of years in this life, but the years in future lives cannot be counted. This 
person‟s motivation is clear - that working for future lives is far more important than 
this life. Wanting to c reate benefit for the future lives, he looks at the presentation of 
karma and their effects, tries to live in accord with this (abandoning the ten non -
virtuous actions) and goes for refuge. He is thinking of his impermanence (he is going 
to die etc.) and f rom this accumulates positive actions and avoids negative actions.  
This person would have to reflect first on the lower realms. To do this he needs 
to be sure they exist and that he could be born there. If this danger exists, then it 
would be unsuitable n ot to have protection from being born there. The interest in 
dharma comes from knowing that when one dies you have to take a rebirth, and not 
wanting it to be in the lower realms. Then he reflects how to avoid this, which actually 
means how to take rebirth  in the higher realms. There is a quote saying, “ Apart from 
ethical discipline, there is no other cause .” So he takes this on, which is the main practice of 
a being of the small scope, avoiding the ten non -virtuous actions. Such an interest only 
comes from  reflecting intensely on the law of karma and its effects. This causes the 
practitioner to have pure ethical discipline, which becomes a cause for a higher rebirth.  
 
 
WEDNESDAY 17 MARCH 2010  - SESSION 2 
QUESTION:  Is ethical discipline something you get a r esult from by keeping it your 
whole life, or is it on a daily basis?  For example, if a Gelong gives his vows back after 
one year, would this be a cause for higher rebirth or would it need to be for his whole 
life? Is this about a single action or the cont inuity?  
GEN: If a Gelong gives back his vows after one year, this shows there were problems 
with the ethical discipline, with the motivation etc. If everything went well, then he 
wouldn‟t give back the vows.  
QUESTION:  What about a certain period of ethical  discipline, is this already causing a 
higher rebirth?  
GEN:  If you kept the ethical discipline for only a year, you would have accumulated 
the positive karma from this ethical discipline, but then the task is to not let it 
degenerate or be destroyed, for example with anger which destroys the roots of virtue. 
Roots of virtue refers to all this merit from prostrations, offerings, keeping ethical 
discipline etc. You would need to protect yourself from these negative minds after 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 204 – week one to seven  having accumulated this merit. When we take vows we say, “I‟m going to keep these 
vows until I die,” not just for a short period of time.  
 Even if we only perform one act of generosity it is the same, we have to protect 
the merit accumulated from being destroyed or degenerating from the se negative 
minds.  
With this topic about giving back the vows, or more correctly „to offer back the 
vows‟, it‟s important to know that it is not easy to achieve. You have to have a very 
good reason – just to say, “I‟ve had enough” is not a good reason. A good reason, 
actually the only reason, would be in a life -threatening situation. The actual act is very 
easy, you just say, “I‟m giving the vows back.” We may come to this later in the Vinaya 
teachings.  
It‟s very difficult to find a situation where offeri ng the vows back is allowed, or 
suitable. This is true for everyone, Rinpoches, Geshes or an ordinary monks. When 
you are in danger of death, this would be such a situation. To accomplish the actual 
offering the vows back, you could just shout the words, “ I‟m giving back the vows” 
and if the person understands this correctly, this is already enough. You can offer them 
back when your life is in danger. The human life is considered more precious than the 
vows. If you have a choice of keeping the vows and loos ing life, or keeping your life 
and loosing the vows, then in this situation you would offer back the vows.  
 For example, there is no mention in any of the lives of the great lamas giving 
back the vows. If someone is doing this, it shows he is looking at s ome lower role -
model and no longer looking at the Masters and the Three Jewels, especially the 
Buddha. There is no story like this in any text, obviously not of the Buddha doing this, 
but also of masters like Nagarjuna, Lama Tsongkhapa himself, Asanga, Ati sha and so 
on. That offering back the vows is very difficult comes directly from the Vinaya. It‟s 
not something one does as a joke, it‟s actually not easy.  
 Some people who are new in the Dharma and don‟t know the Vinaya might say, 
“Oh yes, this monk gave  back the vows, so I can also give mine back.” We can come 
up with many such examples of negative friends. By following this kind of friend many 
negative actions come about. This is like the saying I frequently use, “The negative 
friend does not come along  with horns on his head.”  
QUESTION:  What about in the Lama Chöpa where it says we should protect the vows 
even at the cost of our lives?  
GEN: This is the kind of motivation we should have with regard to the vows, “If there 
is a problem to my life or not, I  will guard my vows in all situations.” This is the 
correct attitude to guard vows. This is also mentioned in Sojong. When it actually 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
205 comes to this kind of situation where your life is threatened, the Vinaya is very clear 
that in this case you should give  back your vows.  
QUESTION:  I don‟t see the benefit of giving back the vows in the situation where you 
life is in danger. We could just give our robes, but not our vows.  
GEN: If you have a way to avoid being killed, then this is better!  
 This situation me ntioned in the Vinaya is not like when you are about to die 
anyway like from old age. For example, if someone wants to kill us but they don‟t care 
if we have vows or not then better to die with the vows. The situation in the Vinaya is 
that by giving back t he vows you would survive, but by keeping the vows you would be 
killed, this is a very specific situation.  
 For example, this has happened frequently since 1959 in Tibet where the 
Chinese have said if you give back your vows you can live, if you keep your  vows you 
will be killed. This has happened many times and is exactly the situation the Vinaya is 
talking about.  
QUESTION:  What about a situation where there is a war and the government says you 
have to go? In this case would it be better to give back the  vows and go to war 
(engaging into a lot of negativity), or to keep the vows and to be killed?  
GEN: This is exactly a situation where you give your vows back, because you are 
forced to go to war. Otherwise by keeping the vows you would be killed.  
QUESTION : I have heard that some Tibetan monks actually gave their vows back 
before they were killed out of compassion for the Chinese, so they wouldn‟t be 
committing the act of killing a monk which would be much heavier. Is this correct?  
GEN: Yes, this happened o ften.  
 
In the Lamp on the Pathxxxi: 
Whoever pursues by any means,  
the happiness of samsara only,  
just for his ow n benefit,  
should be know n as least of beings.  
 
This verse means someone is taking the essence of the precious human rebirth for 
rebirth in the higher realms. To do this he engages into ethics etc. He is actually aiming 
at a rebirth in samsara. Any of the three practitioners are aiming at such a higher 
rebirth, especially such a rebirth with freedoms and endowments. The Buddha said that 
for attaining any of these goals, the precious human rebirth is the supreme basis. All 
the practitioners are aiming at a higher rebirth, but this practitioner is qualifie d by only  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 206 – week one to seven  aiming at a higher rebirth. Someone with this attitude of only aiming for a higher 
rebirth in samsara is a practitioner of the small scope. He‟s not aiming at liberation.   
Someone who is aiming to get out of samsara all together, to get free of samsara 
by practicing ethics, generosity etc., is not a small scope practitioner, but is a 
practitioner of the middle scope.  
Someone having the motivation of complete enlightenment for the sake of all 
sentient beings thinks, “If this enlightenment does no t come about for me, its 
unbearable, it has to arise.” A person with this attitude engages into ethical discipline, 
generosity etc. This is an uncommon practice of the Mahayana.  Here we can see these 
individual practices are coming from the respective mot ivations.  
  
C How intermediate beings achieve the certain goodness of liberation  
There are tw o kinds of certain goodness: (1) the liberation that is merely release from 
samsara and (2) omniscience. In this regard everything that has been set forth starting  
from the vehicles of hearers and solitary realizers is included in the actual cycle of 
teachings for intermediary beings or in the cycle of teachings common to intermediary 
beings. Intermediary beings develop disenchantment with existence as a whole and 
having made the liberation that is release from existence for their ow n w elfare their 
object of attainment, they engage in its method, the three trainings.  
 
Next, we talk about the practitioner of the middle scope. We first mentioned the 
different aims wit h regard to sentient beings, one of which was called definite 
goodness. This has two types, liberation and complete enlightenment. The person 
mentioned here is a person who wants to be liberated from samsara completely, so 
anything the Buddha has taught to  achieve this mere liberation, are teachings for the 
practitioner of the middle scope. He wants to be protected from samsara but not for 
the sake of others, he only wants to get out of samsara to protect himself. If a 
practitioner has this motivation, then  he is a  practitioner of the middle scope.  
 
In the Lamp on the Path  it says:  
Indifferentxxxii to pleasures of existence  
a master desisting from evil deeds -  
w hoever pursues only personal peace  
is called an intermediate being.  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
207 D How great beings attain omniscience  
There are tw o means of attaining omniscience: the great vehicles of secret mantra and 
[of] the perfections. Those tw o are contained in the cycle of teachings of great beings. 
Since great beings are under the pow er of great compassion, they have  made 
enlightenment their object of attainment in order to bring all the sufferings of sentient 
beings to an end and train in the six perfections, the tw o stages etc.  
 
Now we have a description of the great being who‟s aim is omniscience. There are two 
methods taught to achieve this. One is the Secret Mantra Mahayana, the other the 
Perfection Mahayana. All that is presented there is for a being of the great scope, or a 
great being. A great being has generated great compassion for all sentient beings. Due 
to this they are called a great being, great compassion is connected with the great 
being. Great compassion is the entrance to the practices of the great being. A being 
below this would not have great compassion.  
 On this basis of great compassion, they would make enlightenment their aim 
and try to liberate all sentient beings from their suffering, and to attain this they train 
in the six perfections.  
 
In the Lamp on the Path  it says:  
He w hose ow n mind is affected by suffering,  
w ho thus yearns to bring to an end,  
all the suffering of others - completely,  
{ 56}  such a one is a superior being.  
 
As for the means of those beings’ attaining enlightenment - they are set forth under 
tw o [headings]: perfections and mantra.  
 
 This first line of this verse means there is no one who does not want to be free 
from suffering, including the practitioners themselves. Understanding it also applies to 
himself, he is then able to understand the suffering of others.  
TRANSLATOR: This ver se actually reads by the suffering which is aff licting  him, is thus 
yearning  etc.. 
 In brief, this means that being aware of how one only wants to be free from 
suffering, we understand all others are exactly the same. From this one engages into 
separating others from suffering. One wants to completely end the suffering of others, 
one is completely filled with this wish. To bring this about, one aims at complete 
enlightenment. This being is a superior being, or in other words a bodhisattva.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 208 – week one to seven  QUESTION:  I had previously understood an Arhat would also have great compassion. 
I thought the difference was the special intention leading to bodhicitta. Here it says 
that an Arhat would be a great being.  
GEN: We can‟t say this Arhat has great compassion, but he has imme asurable 
compassion. This type is also very strong, “how wonderful it would be if they would 
all be free from suffering.” This is also a strong compassion, but it would not 
overwhelm his self -concern to get liberated for himself. It is therefore not great 
compassion. The great being of the Mahayana also wants all sentient beings to free 
from suffering, but their compassion is so strong that it overrides his own wish to be 
free from suffering. To be free of their own suffering is also present for the great 
being, but the wish for others to be free from suffering is greater. In other words, his 
cherishing others is stronger than his self -cherishing. The Arhat has compassion, this 
immeasurable compassion, but due to his strong self -cherishing, he decides to go for 
his own goal.  
 
WEDNESDAY 17 MARCH 2010  - SESSION 3 
 
E Elucidating the sources of the term “three [kinds of] beings”  
The term “three beings” is mentioned in many texts such as the Compendium of 
Ascertainments  and the Commentary on the Treasuryxxxiii . 
 
Lama Tsongkhapa refers to these three beings often, for example in the Lam -Rim 
Chenmo the topics are arranged around the three beings. Where does this term „three 
beings‟ come from? It is mentioned in many texts such as the Compendium of 
Ascertainments , a text by Arya Asanga, and the Commentary on the Treasury . Treasury  
refers to the Abhidharmakosha  (Treasury of Higher Knowledge  by Vasubhandu) and the 
commentary refers to his auto commentary. In these two texts the term „three beings‟ 
is used.  
 
F Clearing up dou bt 
Although, w ith regard to lesser beings, there are both those w ho devote themselves to 
this life and those who devote themselves to future lives, this is about the latter. One 
should consider them as engaging in the unmistaken means to high status.  
 
Some one has a doubt about the lesser being and says there are two types. One only 
wants the benefit of this life and the other of future lives. Lama Tsongkhapa says in 
our context, a lesser being is only talking about someone who is looking for the 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
209 happiness o f future lives. This comes down to a question about who is a dharma 
practitioner. Someone who is working only for the happiness of this life is therefore 
not a Dharma practitioner. Someone who is working for the happiness of future lives 
is of the first ty pe of Dharma practitioner, the lesser being.  
 
QUESTION:  What can we do to avoid being born as a god?  
GEN: Guard ethical discipline, become a monk and generate the mind of definite 
emergence!  
In Tantra  it describes the following stages: You should have a human rebirth 
with the freedoms and endowments, get ordained, take the vows and guard the vows 
(enter into the Vinaya ) then aim at complete enlightenment and not aim at becoming a 
god. These go together , if you are aiming at enlightenment you are not aiming at 
becoming a god.  
This describes the supreme basis for practicing tantra. The lowest basis is a 
layperson, the middling is a Getsül and the highest is the Gelong. This is said in the 
mantra texts, i t‟s not made up, it is expressed there. These days I‟m concerned with 
memorising these texts so I can say it is definitely there.  
 In that context, there is mention of six ends and four methods. The four 
methods (or modes) are; (1) having to be born as a h uman with freedoms and 
endowments, (2) you have to be ordained, (3) you have to have entered fully into the 
Vinaya and (4) you have to be aiming at full enlightenment.  
 In relation to your question, the specific point here is this aiming for 
enlightenment.  If you have that motivation for all your activities, then whatever action 
you do will become a cause for full enlightenment and not for being born in the god 
realms.  
  
2B4B -2A2 Giving reasons for gradual guidance from the point of view of the three [types  of] 
beings  
 
This section explains why the Lam -Rim presents the path from the point of view of 
the three types of beings. Although there are three types of beings, it is actually about 
someone aiming at full enlightenment. So why are the other levels prese nted here? 
Because this great being also has to engage into abandoning the ten non -virtuous 
actions, which is presented in the small scope. This great being has to train in the four 
noble truths and the 16 aspects which are from the middle scope and so on.  As they 
have to train in the practices of the small and middle scopes, these practices are also 
methods to attain complete enlightenment. It is not correct to say the practices of the 
small scope are only for this person, the middle scope for this person and the great 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 210 – week one to seven  scope only for this person. The practices of the small and middle scopes are contained 
in the Mahayana path, the path of the great being. The great being has to train in all of 
these practices.  
  
A The purpose of guiding someone by way of th e three beings’ path  
B Giving reasons for such gradual guidance  
The second one has tw o points: what is the purpose of guiding someone by w ay of the 
three beings’ path and giving reasons for such gradual guidance.  
 
2B4B -2A2A  The purpose of guiding someone b y means of the three beings’ path  
First: thus, although it is explained in terms of the three beings, the path of great 
beings contains w ithin it the path of the tw o other ones, w hich is w hy Master 
Ashvagosha says that these tw o are branches of the Mahayan a path. Consequently, 
here, they are not guidance on the path of lesser beings making only the happiness of 
conditioned existence their object of attainment, nor on the path of intermediate 
beings making only liberation from samsara for their ow n benefit t heir object of 
attainment. Rather, some paths common to those tw o are considered to be preparatory 
practices leading to the path of great beings and taken to be branches for training in 
the path of great beings.  
 
Lama Tsongkhapa is not presenting the prac tices of the small scope for people only to 
attain the leisure of the god or human realms. He is not presenting the practices of the 
middle scope so that someone can attain only liberation. He presents these practices 
for someone aiming at the great scope,  complete enlightenment, because they will have 
to train in all these.  
 These small and middle scope paths are presented as practices that we share 
with beings of the lower and middle scopes.  
 
TRANSLATOR :  In our translation it says “ common to ” which does n‟t convey the right 
meaning. It‟s not common amongst th ose two (middle and lower) but commo n with 
the great being and those  two. Better to say common with  or shared with . 
 
GEN: The small and middle scope practices are also practices for the great scope. 
Therefore, they are practices the great being and the lower/middle scope beings have 
in common. These shared practices from the point of view of the great being are 
considered preparatory practices. They are part of the great being‟s path and are seen 
as br anches (causes) of the path of the great being.

2B4B -2A2B -1A The entry gate to the Mahayana  
First: The entry gate for entering the Mahayana consists in forming the mind tow ard 
supreme enlightenment. Once it has arisen in the mind - according to Engaging in 
Bodhisattva Behaviour : 
When bodhicitta has arisen, at once , 
the w retched bound in the prison of cyclic existence  
are to be called the children of those gone to bliss.  
 
Mahayana  here refers to practices of the great being. The entrance into the Mahayana 
is mind generation. The generation of a mind which is aiming at full enlightenment, or 
in other words, the bodhimind. This is the sole gate or entry point into the Mahayana. 
If you have this mind, there are effects like those mentioned in this verse from the 
Bodhisattvacharyavatara . 
 This Mahayana mind, or bodhimind , needs to be generated in our continuum. 
The bodhimind that has not been generated, we should make effort for it to arise. Like 
it says in the dedication prayer “ may the precious bodhimind not yet born arise.... ” We make 
effort that it arises. This is done by first thinking of the benefits of the bodhimind, like 
presented in the Bodhisattvacharyavatara , then naturally great enthusiasm will arise. From 
this we‟ll want to train and cultivate the mind and then the b odhimind which has not 
arisen can arise.  
 Someone who wants to enter into the Mahayana must make sure that they 
generate this bodhimind. They look at the benefits, accumulate merits and purify - in 
particular relating to the seven branches mentioned previ ously.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 212 – week one to seven   
{ 57}  For in accordance with this statement, an individual having obtained the name 
of “Bodhisattva” is admitted among the adherents of the Mahayana. If itxxxiv 
deteriorates, he w ill leave the midst of Mahayana practitioners.  
  
2B4B -2A2B -1B  The need to consider its benefits and allow one’s delight to increase before 
generating the mind of enlightenment along with the [relevant] sources  
This being the case, those wishing to enter the Mahayana need to exert themselves in 
many w ays to arouse that mind. H ow ever, before arousing it, they need to meditate on 
the benefits of arousing that mind and allow  delight w ith regard to those benefits to 
increase, do the seven -limbed practice, and go for refuge - that has been set out in the 
Compendium of Trainings  and in Engaging in Bodhisattva Behaviour . 
 
The benefits of this mind are said to be inconceivable  
 
2B4B -2A2B -1C  The benefits of striving for this mind  
If w e summarise the benefits set forth in this w ay, there are tw o: temporary and 
ultimate benefits. The for mer, again, are tw ofold: not to fall to the low er realms and 
to be born in the happy realms. Once this mind has arisen, the causes of miserable 
existences accumulated previously are purified and the continuity of those 
accumulated later on is interrupted. The causes of happy realms accumulated 
previously, due to being embraced by it, increase extensively, and the ones new ly 
created, due to being motivated by it, become inexhaustible. When it comes to 
attaining the ultimate objectives of liberation and omnis cience w ith ease, through 
relying on this mind: if an uncontrived wish to achieve the temporary or ultimate 
benefits is not present, one may say “since those benefits arise from generating that 
mind, I shall endeavour to generate that mind”, but it w ill on ly be w ords. This is very 
clear if w e examine it in our ow n mind.  
 
In brief, these many benefits are two -fold, temporary and ultimate. Temporary are for 
example the small benefits of not falling to the lower realms and being born in the 
upper realms. In a ddition, the causes you have previously accumulated for the lower 
realms are purified and you don‟t engage into the same kind of actions again because 
the continuity is interrupted by the bodhimind. Also, whatever causes for happy realms 
you have already a ccumulated are intensely increased, and new ones become 
inexhaustible.  
 This means that by engaging into virtue whilst under the power of the 
bodhimind, what you accumulate will not be exhausted by enjoying the fruition of that 
virtue, but the result will  be inexhaustible.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
213  If we have not been able to accumulate causes for these temporary and ultimate 
outcomes, then due to the generation of the bodhimind, you‟ll be able to bring about 
all these results. We should therefore definitely think about the benefi ts of this mind.  
 Then there is a question about if ones engagement of the bodhimind is mere 
words. We need to look in our own minds, then we will know if its mere words or not.  
 
2B4B -2A2B -1D  How the lesser and intermediate [branches of the path] are ne cessary for laying 
the basis of those benefits  
In order to develop the w ish to attain those tw o benefits, high status and certain 
goodness, it is necessary to develop the thoughts common to lesser and intermediate 
beings. { 58}  
 
When you are reflecting on t his bodhimind you ask what are the causes for this mind? 
The causes are love and compassion. Then you come to understand how these two 
minds are extremely important for you.  
 If you don‟t have a mind that wants to be liberated, in a strong way, a mind of 
definite emergence, then we won‟t be able to strongly generate the mind which wants 
others to be liberated - in others words the minds of love and compassion.  
 
2B4B -2A2B -1E  How the lesser and intermediate [branches of the path] are necessary when 
trainin g in the actual mind  
Having thus developed the w ish to achieve the tw o benefits, w hen you cultivate the 
beneficial mind it is necessary to develop the root of that mind, love and compassion. 
In this regard, if your body hair does not shiver and quiver as y ou think about the w ay 
you yourself w ander in cyclic existence, deprived of happiness and oppressed by 
suffering, there is no w ay the inability to bear how  other sentient beings are deprived 
of happiness and oppressed by suffering can come about. In Engagi ng in Bodhisattva 
Behaviour  it says:  
In beings to w hom previously,  
such a thought, even for their ow n sake,  
did not even appear in their dreams,  
how  could it arise for others?  
 
This verse talks about those who don‟t have this mind even for their own sake. If this 
mind wanting to get out of samsara hasn‟t arisen even for oneself, not even in a small 
way like in a dream – how could you then generate a similar mind for others?  
 
For that reason, having thought about how , in the context of lesser beings, the har m of 
the sufferings of bad migrations falls upon you and how , in the context of intermediate 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 214 – week one to seven  beings, even in [rebirths of] high status there is suffering and no peace nor happiness, 
meditating - in accord w ith your ow n experience - on sentient beings w ho h ave become 
dear to you, becomes the cause for love and compassion to arise. As the mind of 
enlightenment is born from them, training in the thoughts shared w ith lesser and 
intermediate [beings] is the means of generating the uncontrived mind of 
enlightenme nt. 
 
Firstly, one has to train the attitude of the first two scopes. We generate a mind that is 
aware of the difficulties of the three lower realms and being aware of this one 
generates an attitude that these must be avoided. If we were to end up in such a  realm 
there would be no method to get out. This leads to one practicing to prevent it which 
is the mind of the small scope being.  
Further, even with regard to the higher states, one sees that they are still 
samsara and in the nature of suffering, that th ere is no happiness but suffering is 
overflowing. By reflecting on this intensely, day and night one works to get out of 
samsara. This motivation is of the middle scope being and also needs to be generated.  
We need a mind that intensely wants to escape fr om all this suffering of samsara 
– only with this mind can we extend the mind to others. To see how they only want 
happiness, don‟t want suffering and how they are only suffering. When you have the 
strong intention to liberate all beings from their sufferi ngs, then love and compassion 
arises. This is a cause for entering into the Mahayana.  
 What is presented here is how one needs to rely on the practices that we share 
with the lower and middle scopes which are a cause leading to the important sates of 
love and compassion.  
 If one then engages into the practices of the small or medium scopes and love 
and compassion are not generated, other beings will still have great benefit from us. 
Our family and friends will benefit from us abandoning aggressive and har mful 
behaviour for example. There is so much activity to benefit others already in the small 
and middle scopes.  
 
THURSDAY  18 MARCH 2010  - SESSION 1 
ADVICE  
We should reflect on the benefits of listening to the Dharma, the benefits of correct 
reliance on the  spiritual friend and the drawbacks of not doing so. Then reflect on the 
actual way to rely on the spiritual friend, these points are very important.  
 By relying on the spiritual friend one brings about the collections of merit that 
purifies ones continuum , this is also very important.   
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
215  In the Lam-Rim it was explained how to use the merit field and how to practice 
accumulation and purification by relying on the merit field. You would first rely on the 
six preparatory practices and within this are the seve n limbs.  
 Also explained was how one generates placement and analytical meditation, in 
other words how one is aiming towards calm abiding and special insight. There was an 
explanation of the four dharmas to be applied in this context. Controlling the sense  
doors, acting with introspection, knowing the correct measure of food and what to do 
at the time of sleep.  
 Sleep was discussed in detail. One method was to divide the day into six parts 
and the night into three parts. Another way was three parts during the day and three 
parts in the night. In all these parts one would apply the Dharma. Having been 
encouraged to practice in the waking state, it was shown that even the time of sleep 
could be a practice of virtue.  
 Two types of meditation were explained, a nalysis and placement. It was 
explained what these types are and how to engage into them.  
 Initially one needs to listen to the Dharma, then one brings this to mind with a 
fully qualified mental attention. With this mental attention, one can actually see the 
Dharma. There was a quote expressing this.  
 With this analytical meditation you would ask yourself, “What objects are there 
and how does one engage into analytical meditation?”  
 We have to understand how the topics of analytical meditation start with correct 
reliance on the spiritual friend through to the highest topics. Those Dharma topics 
that need to  be cultivated by the being of the small capacity are all included in the 
topics for analytical meditation. The middle scope practices can be presented as the 
three higher trainings. Within these higher trainings, all that is included in the higher 
training of ethical discipline are topics for analytical meditation. Then on this basis of 
training in ethical discipline you can bring forth the training of conc entration – all that 
is included here is a type of placement mediation. From this, you come to special 
insight or the higher training of wisdom, the third training. This has two types, those 
that are analytical meditations and those that are placements med itations.  
 These topics starting with the correct way to rely on the spiritual friend, how the 
freedoms and endowments are rare and difficult to find, how we can lose this because 
of impermanence (death), meditating on the law of karma and its effects, re fuge, then 
onto the middle scope topics – the drawbacks of samsara up to the higher practices of 
love, compassion and the bodhimind and so on, all these are analytical meditations. 
None are placement meditations.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 216 – week one to seven   By engaging into these meditation topics again and again over a long period, 
actual transformations can happen in our mind.  
 One would then meditate to destroy attachment by taking an object of 
attachment. An object that has good qualities and with this destroy attachment. The 
same for an object  that is ugly or has negative qualities, on this basis one would 
destroy anger and these types of afflictions.  
QUESTION:   It is suspiring that you would use an  object of attachment to 
destroy attachment.  
GEN: With what other type of object would you try to destroy attachment? Likewise, 
with an object of anger, you use that object to destroy your aversion to that object.  
QUESTION:   What is the difference between reflecting and the analytical 
meditation that we  are able to do?  
GEN: These are the same. Whatever we have listened to, by reflecting again and again a 
stronger understanding develops. This is coming from the analysis.
