217 We also touched on a quote from the Letter to a Friend , where it says the precious 
human rebirth is the best basis for practicing the Mahayana. We also talked about 
taking the essence of this precious human rebirth, how do you do this? There were 
four points extremely important points mentioned; (1) It is nec essary to attain the 
Dharma, (2) we are capable of attaining the Dharma, (3) Dharma needs to be attained 
in this life and (4) Dharma needs to be attained from today onwards.  
 You should make use of these many questions for your discussions and study.  
 
 We are going through many important points here. It is difficult that these will 
stick by going over it just once. By going over these points repeatedly, we will 
understand what stages are presented and how they are to be practiced.  
 If you come to understa nd all these stages, if you know the outlines for example, 
then you come to have a good grip on the whole text. For example, in the beginning 
we talked about the life of the master Atisha. In a brief, first was mentioned how he 
was born in an excellent fam ily, second how based on this lineage he searched for 
good qualities. The third point was how on the basis of these good qualities he 
benefited beings in both India and Tibet. So in this way we have the complete 
biography of Lama Atisha.  
 How to correctly  rely on the spiritual friend came next. The spiritual friend had 
certain qualities, what are they? There were several presentations. This is the first 
point. Second, what kind of good qualities does the student have to have? The third 
point was the actual  way to rely on the spiritual friend. This had two points, how to 
rely in thought and how to rely in actions. Relying in thought had two, how to train in 
the root (faith) and secondly, how by recalling the kindness of the guru you develop 
respect. Fourth, if such a student relies on such a teacher, what benefits are there? The 
fifth point is what are the drawbacks if he does not do so?  This is the complete topic 
of how to rely on the spiritual friend in the correct way.  
 The benefits of correct reliance al so includes all the points of the benefits of 
listening to the Dharma.  
Working in this way and answering these questions is very important. From this 
you can develop an understanding of the outlines, and from this you‟ll be able to keep 
it in mind. It wil l be very beneficial for you.  
 For example, if you sit and read your book, and you read all these outlines then 
close the book and try to recall them and nothing comes to mind, this is a sign you 
haven‟t done enough study.  
 This is our job, to study and t o hold all these texts in our mind. All the other 
jobs are to be let go, are only task is this, to constantly engage into studies.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 218 – week one to seven   If we look in all of Europe, apart from what they do at Institute Lama 
Tsongkhapa, this is the only place where full Dharma  studies are done. This is where 
we are. Because the place has a reputation like this, we should engage into studies. By 
thinking like this, we can generate some strength of mind.  
 Someone might train their mind by thinking of the existence of the three l ower 
realms. For example, the suffering experienced in hell realms and then thinks how he 
could end up in this situation. In those realms one would only engage into negativity 
and could not find a way out. From this a strong mind arises thinking, “this wou ld be a 
disaster for me. I‟ll do everything to avoid this and create the causes for a higher 
rebirth.” By thinking about this over and over, one can generate a motivation that is 
similar to a being of the small scope.  
Following this would be to think that  wherever we are born in samsara is a state 
of suffering. Whomever we are with is a state of suffering, whatever we are enjoying is 
a state of suffering - they are all in the nature of suffering. If we perceive this, and we 
wish to escape as strongly as we  would wish to escape from being on fire, then a fully -
fledged wish of definite emergence has been generated. If you don‟t have this intensity 
in your own mind, to then say you are going to liberate all sentient beings from their 
suffering is pretty much i mpossible. That you would have compassion for other beings 
stuck in samsara whilst not wanting to be free yourself is not possible. We talk about 
love and compassion on the basis of understanding the suffering involved. We‟re not 
just talking about if some one has an accident or some pain and then you generate some 
compassion, we‟re talking about the compassion that sees this person is stuck in 
samsara. This other type is not our topic, this compassion even the animals have. We 
are talking about the compassi on that wants sentient beings to be free from all the 
suffering of samsara.  
 To train in the attitudes of the small and middle being is the path to attaining 
the state of love, compassion and the bodhimind - the mind of the great being.  
  
2B4B -2A2B -1F  How the lesser and intermediate [branches of the path] are also necessary for the 
ritual accumulation and purification of this mental attitude  
Efforts to purify and accumulate by many means, through the thoughts of going for 
refuge, actions and results, an d so forth in the context of the tw o paths, also constitute 
methods for training the mental continuum - preliminary practices for the generation 
of the mind [of enlightenment]. As they become [part of] the seven -limbed practice 
along w ith going for refuge,  these thoughts common to lesser and intermediate beings 
should also be understood as methods for the arising of the mind of enlightenment.  
 
All these different practices in the context of the two (small and middle capacity), like 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
219 going for refuge, reflect ing on actions and their results, accumulation and purifying 
and so on, all these are to be seen as preliminary for the bodhimind. By training in 
these practices, they can become a cause for the bodhimind, so we should definitely 
engage into them.  
    
2B4B -2A2B -1G  Advice to cherish the way that the teaching cycles of lesser and intermediate 
beings become branches of that of great beings  
Here the spiritual master indicates how the teaching cycles of lesser and intermediate 
beings become branches for the ari sing of the mind of enlightenment { 59}  and the 
disciple also gains certainty in this regard. Through recalling those [points] w ith each 
practice, you should thoroughly cherish this training. If this is not done, the actual 
path of great beings and the indi vidual paths become unconnected. As you do not gain 
certainty w ith regard to the mind of enlightenment until you reach the actual path of 
great beings, this w ould be an obstacle to the arisal of that mind or, meanw hile, the 
degeneration of that great objec tive. Therefore you should exert yourself at it.  
 
In this point, the guru explains and the disciple needs to listen well and put it into 
practice according to the instructions. He needs to practice the two lower scopes by 
clearly identifying these as cause s for the bodhimind. If not, the paths of the two lower 
scopes become completely unrelated to the higher scope.  
 If you think like this, it would act as major obstacle for your generation of the 
bodhimind. This view is to be utterly avoided. This view wou ld also make you lose or 
degenerate your great aim. We should take on these practices of the two lower scopes 
and not abandon them.  
 
2B4B -2A2B -1H  [The order on the path]  
1 The order of the two minds etc. and how to train [[in] them]  
Having trained in this manner, you should make the uncontrived mind of 
enlightenment arise in your mental continuum as much [as possible], then, in order to 
stabilize it, first go for uncommon refuge and then perform the ritual of the aspiring 
[mind of enlightenment]. Havin g taken the aspiring [mind of enlightenment] through 
the ritual, practice its trainings. Then, time and again develop the w ish to engage in 
the trainings - the six perfections, the four w ays of gathering etc. Once the w ish to 
engage in them has come forth,  from the bottom of the heart take the pure vow of the 
engaging [mind of enlightenment].  
 
The sequence of how to generate the bodhimind is this. First, generate such a 
bodhimind and to make it firm you take the uncommon refuge. Following this, you 
perform the ritual of aspiration – taking the aspiring bodhimind through a ritual. You 
then take on the trainings of the aspiring bodhimind. Then you generate a strong wish 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 220 – week one to seven  to train in the six perfections and the four ways of collecting disciples9. Once this wish 
has arisen, you then take the vows of the engaging bodhimind. Just this is not enough; 
you then need to guard them. If you take the vows and don‟t guard them, this will be a 
big drawback.  
 
THURSDAY 18 MARCH 2010  - SESSION 2 
 
2 The order of explanations  
Then even risk your life in order not to be tainted by a root dow nfall. Strive not to be 
tainted by small and medium contaminations or infractions either. If you still get 
tainted, purify them w ell by means of w hat has been expounded with regard to 
restoring dow nfalls. Then, train in the six perfections in general and especially in 
meditative concentration, the essence of calm abiding, in order to make the mind 
serviceable w ith respect to w hatever virtuous object you w ish. The statement in Lamp 
on the Path  that you train in calm abiding in order to develop superknow ledges is only 
an example. Atisha himself has stated on other occasions that it also serves to develop 
special insight. Therefore you should develop calm abiding for that purpose, too. Then, 
{ 60}  in order to cut the bonds of grasping at the tw o selves, decide on the meaning of 
emptiness through the view , sustain this mode of meditation flaw lessly and accomplish 
the essence of w isdom, special insight.  
 
When you have taken these vows, you need to guard them, even at the cost of your 
life, which we discussed yesterday.  
 To guard the bodhisattva vows means you guard them so you don‟t transgress 
the root vows. Whilst this is clear, you should guard them so that even small or medium 
contaminations  do not oc cur. These are the different levels of transgressions or 
different levels of afflictions that come about. If you are guarding your mind well, you 
actions of body and speech will be pure - guarding the vows by guarding the mind. If 
there is some pollution i n your mind regarding the vows, you don‟t leave it at that, but 
you engage into purification practices right away.  
Then you engage into the practices of the six perfections in general, meaning all 
six, which are generosity, ethics, patience, joyous perseverance, meditative 
concentration and wisdom. One especially aims at being capable of focusing the mind 
on a virtuous  object in the way you wish and for as long as you wish. You achieve this 
by training in calm abiding which is the fifth perfection, meditative concentration . 
 In the Lamp on the Path,  Atisha says you should train in calm abiding to produce 
the superknowle dges. Lama Tsongkhapa says this is only an example. Actually, we should 
                                            
9 Giving material aid, speaking eloquently, giving right counsel and setting a good example.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
221 train in calm abiding to develop special insight. Atisha in other places expresses this. 
Why would one focus on this benefit of calm abiding? Because based on special insight 
you are a ble to directly destroy the afflictions, this is why one trains in calm abiding.  
 After having attained calm abiding, one develops the view about the meaning of 
emptiness, to destroy the fetters of the grasping for the two types of self, the self of 
perso ns and self of phenomena. We are bound because of these two types of grasping 
and we want to destroy them. To cut these fetters, which are the grasping for these 
two types of self, we need the view that is realising emptiness. This means special 
insight. W e need to first have a strong view of the meaning of emptiness, and then 
supporting this with a strong and unmistaken way of meditating, we need to attain 
special insight that is the entity of wisdom.  
 
2B4B -2A2B -1I  All paths are contained in the three tr ainings  
Accordingly, except for the accomplishment of calm abiding and special insight, 
[everything] up to and including the trainings of the engaging [mind of enlightenment] 
constitutes training in ethical discipline. Calm abiding is training of the mind and 
special insight is training in wisdom. So it says in the Commentary on the Lamp on 
the Path . 
 
 One is not only working on calm abiding and special insight, but also on the 
other trainings connected with the vows of the engaging bodhimind. Everything be low 
calm abiding and special insight is included in the higher training of ethical discipline. 
Calm abiding is included in the training of concentration (or mind  as it says in our 
text). Special insight is the training in wisdom. This is an explanation com ing from the 
Commentary on the Lamp on  the Path . Lama Atisha is showing here how all the practices 
are contained in the three higher trainings.  
 
QUESTION:  What does it mean that the three trainings are higher? Are the three 
trainings the same as the three  higher trainings?  
GEN: We cannot say the three trainings are the same as the three higher trainings 
because the three trainings are also found with non -buddhists. They also have trainings 
in ethics, concentration and wisdom, but these are not higher train ings because they 
are not buddhist trainings.  
 The three trainings are higher because they are connected with going refuge to 
the Buddha, Dharma and Sangha. Therefore, we can say the ethical discipline practiced 
by a being of the small scope is the higher  training of ethics because it is connected 
with refuge. We can also say each of the three scopes have their own three -fold set of 
the three higher trainings. The small scope being has a higher training in ethics, a 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 222 – week one to seven  higher training in concentration and a h igher training in wisdom. Therefore, the higher 
training of wisdom is not necessarily referring to the wisdom realising emptiness, or 
necessarily to do with emptiness at all. In our context here, it does refer to the wisdom 
realising emptiness. A small sco pe practitioner is not interested in emptiness, it is not 
one of his practices, yet he does have a higher training of wisdom. We could also say in 
general about ourselves, that we have all these three higher trainings. We took refuge, 
we train in ethical d iscipline, we have occasionally have some type of concentration 
and we have some type of wisdom, maybe it‟s only a small type, but there is some 
wisdom there. This is all a certain level of the three higher trainings.  
QUESTION:  Is it possible to have an un contrived aspiring mind of bodhicitta without 
calm abiding? The text seems to present that calm abiding comes after this uncontrived 
mind.  
GEN:  We cannot say someone does not have to train in calm abiding in order to attain 
the uncontrived aspiring bodhi mind. Someone first generates the aspiring mind, then 
after some steps generates the engaging mind, this is done by taking the vows of the 
engaging mind and trains in the vows. Training in the vows means to train in the six 
perfections. Within the six perf ections you have meditative concentration, the nature 
of which is calm abiding. If you have taken the bodhisattva vows you train in the six 
perfections, therefore you train in meditative concentration, therefore you train in 
calm abiding. So you have alrea dy attained it previously, but now you have to train in 
it, make it better and better.  
QUESTION:   I previously understood that when you meditate on a topic you analyse, 
then when you get a feeling you hold it. Now I‟ve heard with analytical meditation you  
just do this analysis over and over again but have no part where you hold the feeling. 
Which is correct?  
GEN: The best thing is if y ou just analyse repeatedly  all the points we‟ve mentioned by 
using the various reasons  etc. Within that, you might have a moment where you place 
your mind. If you generate compassion  for example , what would be your object? 
Suffering sentient beings. You analy se their sufferings. In general, there are many 
presentations of suffering in the Lam -Rim, three types, six types, eigh t types and even 
46 types. This comes from the eight types of suffering each having five aspects, plus 
the six types making 46. If you then add the three types, you end up with 49 types of 
suffering. All this is in the Lam -Rim Chenmo, in our text there wil l be a shorter 
presentation. You go through all these reasons, going though them again and again, 
then with a compassion meditation you think about the suffering sentient beings, not 
just that they are sick, but on the great variety of their sufferings. Th ere might be a 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
223 moment where you place the mind – but this would be part of the analytical 
meditation, not a placement mediation.  
QUESTION:   Does the uncontrived bodhimind have to be based on calm abiding 
or not?  
GEN: Your understanding here is a little mi staken. No one has ever said you can 
generate the uncontrived bodhimind without calm abiding.  
 In order to generate the aspiring bodhimind, you need two bases, a physical and 
mental. The physical basis is described in the Ornament of Clear Realisations  as being a 
body of any of the six types of migrators. The mental basis, not just for the bodhimind 
but for any realisation, needs to be either the attainment of an actual meditative 
absorption, or the preparatory step leading up to the meditative absorption.   
 With the mental basis that is the actual meditative absorption, it‟s clear here you 
need to have calm abiding beforehand because it is a cause for such a state. There is 
one level in the preparatory stages leading up to the actual meditative absorption called 
not being unsuitable . This is the one we would need in order to generate such a 
bodhimind. This also has calm abiding as its cause. So for both these two mental basis, 
you would need calm abiding.  
 The bodhimind is a main mind, and as such it has th e five object ascertaining 
mental factors associated with it. Intelligence, concentration and wishing are three of 
these. The wishing object ascertaining factor is the wish for enlightenment. 
Concentration has to be calm abiding. The third, intelligence (w isdom) has to be 
special insight. The meaning of all this is it‟s not easy to get the bodhimind.  
 Special insight in this case is not the special insight that is focusing on 
emptiness, it is a different type. Calm abiding in this case is also of a differen t type 
from the calm abiding which is focusing on emptiness.  
 Take this popcorn for example, it comes from a previous substance – a corn 
kernel. You put it into the microwave and, pop, it becomes popcorn in a second. 
Similarly, you have this preparatory s tate leading up to the meditative absorption and 
in the next moment, it transforms into the bodhimind, like a seed opening up into a 
flower. The popcorn has many parts to it, similarly the bodhimind has many parts – 
concentration (calm abiding), intelligen ce (special insight) and these two form a union. 
There is a quote which says, “ the bodhimind is a union of calm abiding and special insight. ”  
 All the paths need to be such a union of calm abiding and special insight. 
Whatever path it is, Hinayana or Maha yana, they all come about from a union of calm 
abiding and special insight. Because you need calm abiding for this, the instruction of 
Lama Tsongkhapa is very important when he says you should make effort to generate a 
mind of definite emergence. If we hav e this, then the mind of calm abiding will be easy 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 224 – week one to seven  to generate. If we have the mind of definite emergence, the mind has already focused 
completely on the side of virtue, so then it‟s very easy to focus on one single object. 
We tend to think, “Oh I should d o meditation, I need to attain something,” but 
actually what we should focus on is the mind of definite emergence, this is the basis 
for calm abiding. Once we have definite emergence, calm abiding will easily come 
about. Once we have calm abiding, then act ual realisations can be generated.  
 One needs a union of calm abiding and special insight to bring forth such a 
path, either a Hinayana or a Mahayana path. If one then asks, “Are all the paths in the 
continuum of such a practitioner a union of calm abidin g and special insight?” We 
would have to say no. Minds like calm abiding are connected with the absorptions, and 
the absorptions are connected with the form realm. They are not the form realm, but 
they are of the form realm type. Practices like guarding th e Gelong vows or Getsül 
vows or abandoning the ten non -virtuous actions are trainings for a Hearer, a 
Pratyekabuddha  or a Bodhisattva practitioner. These are all paths connected with a 
mind of the desire realm, so we cannot say they are paths of a union of  calm abiding 
and special insight.  
 In the continuum of these kinds of practitioners are things like ethical discipline 
or minds understanding reality, for example, an inferential cognition of emptiness. It‟s 
possible that an inferential cognition arises on the basis of reflection, not on th e basis 
of meditation. First listening, then reflection and from this they come to a realisation 
of emptiness. The same can be said for a mind reflecting on impermanence and so on. 
These are not minds on the path of accumulation, not minds arisen from medi tation, 
but are in the continuum of someone with minds such as the union of calm abiding 
and special insight. These minds in our examples here, are minds that have not arisen 
from meditation, and are not a union of calm abiding and special insight. On the other 
hand, minds like love, compassion and the bodhimind are only based on meditation. 
They are, in the continuum of such a practitioner, a union of calm abiding and special 
insight. However, this inferential realisation of emptiness that has not arisen f rom 
meditation but from reflection, is in the continuum of someone who is on the path. 
This inferential realisation of emptiness is a path, but not a path that is a union of calm 
abiding and special insight. This person then gains calm abiding in regards t o 
emptiness at the end of the path of accumulation, and then when entering into the 
path of preparation there is a special insight with regard to emptiness. Before this, 
there are paths (or minds) which are realising emptiness inferentially, which have not  
arisen from meditation and are not a union of calm abiding and special insight.  
  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
225 THURSDAY 18 MARCH 2010  - SESSION 3 
2B4B -2A2B -1J  For the sake of skill in classification: all paths are contained in the two 
collections  
Furthermore, everything up to and i ncluding calm abiding is method, the collection of 
merit, the paths based on conventional truths are stages of the vast path while the 
generation of the three special insights constitutes special insight, the collection of 
w isdom, that w hich is based on ul timate truth and stages of the profound path. 
Therefore, you should generate great certainty w ith respect to their order and set 
number as w ell as the fact that enlightenment cannot be attained by means of method 
and w isdom that are separate from each one another.  
 
All these practices  including calm abiding and below (ethical discipline, generosity, 
patience etc. ) are all the method side and the accumulation of merit. They are  the path 
based on the concealer truth and stages of the vast path. The three spec ial wisdoms are 
a wisdom realising the concealer truth, a wisdom realising the ultimate truth, (wisdoms 
realising the two truths) and thirdly, the wisdom able to bring about the aims of 
sentient beings. The production of these  are all of the wisdom type an d are the 
accumulation of transcendental wisdom. That is what is relying on the ultimate truth, 
and is the stage of the profound path.  
 
TRANSLATOR : In our text it says generation of the three special insights constitutes special 
insight, the collection of  wisdom , but this should read „ generation of the three special 
wisdoms  constitutes  special wisdom , the collection of transcendental wisdom ,...”
  
2B4B -2A2B -1K  The need to enter the Mantrayana  
Having thus trained one’s mental continuum by means of the commo n paths, it is 
certainly necessary to enter the Mantrayana, for, once you have entered it, you quickly 
complete the tw o collections.  
 
Four qualities are needed here:  
1) Having trained the common path  
2) Having the wisdom of correct mental attention – a wis dom of how it should be  
3) Faith without doubt  
4) Uninterrupted joyous perseverance.  
 
First one needs to train well in the common path. Common paths are everything 
starting from training in correct reliance on the spiritual friend up to the correct view. 
This includes the three principle aspects of the path. Then you need to generate a 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 226 – week one to seven  mental attention, which is the correct wisdom. Then one generates a faith free from 
doubt, which is faith in the Mantra path. Lastly, one needs an uninterrupted joyous 
perse verance. Someone who has these four good signs is qualified to enter the path of 
Mantra. If he does so, then the two accumulations are quickly completed.  
Someone might ask that as we have trained in the common path, would we not 
already have the wisdom of  correct attention and joyous perseverance? No, because 
they are of a different type and are in addition to those already generated on the 
common path.  
 If you do not have these four prerequisites, one cannot actually enter into the 
path of Tantra; therefo re, one should make effort to train in generating these four.  
 
2B4B -2A2B -1L  How to pursue the perfection path alone  
If you cannot manage more than [the perfection path] or due to the small pow er of 
your lineage you do not w ant to, you should just expand on these very stages of the 
path more and more.  
 
2B4B -2A2B -1M  The stages of engagement in mantra  
As for engagement in mantra, proper reliance on a spiritual teacher is generally lent 
great emphasis in all the vehicles and particularly in mantra, w hich is w hy you should 
make it [even] more pre -eminent [in this context] than before. Then, having ripened  
your mental continuum by means of an empow erment that comes from a pure tantra 
class, you should risk your life for, that is guard, the commitments and vow s that you 
obtained at the time. { 61} . Especially, if a root dow nfall occurs, even though you may 
take them again, this w ill spoil your mind and it w ill be difficult for positive qualities 
to develop. Therefore, do not be tainted by them. Do not get tainted even by a branch 
dow nfall and, if you do, you should not be unconcerned about it, but purify them 
through confession and restraint. Then you should be guided as appropriate in the 
yoga w ith signs in accordance w ith the low er tantra classes or in the yoga of the 
generation stage in accordance w ith the higher tantra classes. On that basis you 
should trai n as appropriate in yoga w ithout signs in accordance w ith the low er tantra 
classes or the yoga of the completion stage according to the higher tantra class[ es].  
 
There is only one higher tantra class, so the text at the end here should read higher 
tantra c lass and not classes . 
 For someone who can enter into the practice of Mantra, of great importance is 
relying on the spiritual friend. This was already emphasised in the general paths, but 
now it is especially emphasised. Here it is also qualified by seeing the guru as the deity . 
On this basis, you request an empowerment that ripens your continuum. In this 
context, you will also have obtained vows and samayas. You then need to guard these 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
227 at the risk of your life. Especially making sure you don‟t transgress the root vows. It is 
true that if you have transgressed a tantric root vow you can take them again. 
Nevertheless, you will have degenerated you continuum. Whilst it is possible to purify, 
you should in the beginning aim to not transgress any of the vows. If you have been 
sullie d by a transgression you shouldn‟t just leave it there without doing anything 
about it, you should engage into purifications practices.  
 Then you would engage into the practice with signs, something coming from the 
lower tantras (Kriya, Carya and Yoga Tan tras), or you engage into the generation stage 
practice of Highest Yoga Tantra. If you have done this well you can enter into the yoga 
without signs (second level of the lower tantras) or the yoga of the completion stage in 
Highest Yoga Tantra.  
 Higher ta ntra class is only referring to Highest Yoga Tantra, and not Yoga tantra. 
Lower tantra  classes refers to the other three tantras.  
 
2B4B -2A2B -1N  Explanation that the intention of Lamp on the Path etc. is the engagement in both 
sutra and mantra [vehicles]  
Such is the structure of the presentation of the path set forth in Lamp on the Path , 
and the Lamrim also guides [ students] in the same w ay.  
 
What Lama Tsongkhapa says, for example in the Lam -Rim Chenmo and the Middle 
Length Lam -Rim, is in accord with Atis ha‟s Lamp on the Path . 
 
2B4B -2A2B -2  The purpose  
A The actual purpose  
B The sources  
 
2B4B -2A2B -2A  The actual purpose  
1 Question  
Second: If the dharma -cycles of lesser and intermediate beings constitute the 
preparatory practices of great beings, it shoul d indeed be all right to consider them the 
stages of the path of great beings. What is the use of saying “the stages of the path in 
common w ith lesser and intermediate beings”?  
 
As all of this is part of the path of the great being, why are we talking abou t the small 
and middle beings? Why not just say it is the path of the great being?  
 
2 Answer  
There are tw o major purposes for classifying and guiding three kinds of beings. In this 
w ay, (1) the conceit of claiming oneself to be a great being, w hile the mental attitudes 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 228 – week one to seven  that are common to lesser or intermediate beings have not even arisen is overcome and 
(2) all three - the highest, intermediate and least mental attitudes - have great benefits. 
The w ay in w hich they are great is that since the tw o more ad vanced beings also need 
to strive for high status and liberation, there is no fault in teaching great or 
intermediate individuals [w ho are] to be guided how to train in thoughts of those tw o, 
and positive qualities are developed. How ever, if a lesser perso n trains starting off 
from the more advanced ones, the more advanced thoughts w ill not arise and the lesser 
ones w ill be cast away, { 62}  so nothing will develop. Moreover, through being taught 
the common path and through training, those w ith the good fortu ne of superior 
capacity w ill soon develop positive qualities that had or had not arisen before w hereby 
they can be guided to higher and higher [levels], once the low er ones have arisen. Their 
path  w ill not be prolonged.  
 
There are two great benefits of us ing this presentation of the three types of beings. 
Firstly, someone who has not generated the minds shared with the small or middle 
being, but says he is a practitioner of the great scope, has a kind of pride in this. 
Talking about the three scope will cr ush the pride.  
 It explains here how all three minds (great, middle and lesser) have benefit. This 
is because the two higher beings (great and middle) also need a higher rebirth and 
liberation. Due to this, it‟s good for them to be led along with practice s which train 
these two thoughts. If one is teaching the small scope to practitioners of the two upper 
scopes, there is no fault because they will generate good qualities. The benefit of a 
practitioner of the middle or great scope engaging into the practic es of the small or 
middle scope, are that their qualities greatly increase.  
 The point here is a being of the middle scope needs to train in the paths of the 
small scope, and a being of the great scope needs to train in the path of the middle 
scope and sm all scope.  
 If for example, a being of the small scope goes straight to the practices of the 
great scope, they will not attain anything of that scope. As he also abandoned the 
practices of the small scope, he won‟t achieve these either, and so achieves not hing. If 
someone who is of great merit trains in the practices of the small and middle scope, all 
his previous qualities will be supported, and those he has not generated yet will 
increase. If the lower realisations arise then one can proceed to the higher  ones. 
Proceeding in this way is conducive to your path; it does not make your path any 
longer.  
 
 
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
229 2B4B -2A2B -2B  The sources  
The need to develop mental states gradually is proclaimed in [the Sutra] Requested by 
the King Lord of Dharanis  through the analogy of a skilled jew eller cleaning precious 
stones gradually w hich is applied to the meaning. The protector Nagarjuna also 
taught to gradually lead in the path of high status and certain goodness:  
First the Dharma of high status,  
then that of certain goodness  comes forth.  
Therefore, once you achieve high status,  
certain goodness w ill come by stages.  
 
Applied to the meaning  means applied to practicing the path.  
First the Dharma of high status  refers to someone who is practicing the small 
scope, the second line  is someone of the middle scope, someone looking for liberation. 
Then it says how definite goodness is depending on these previous stages.  
 
Arya Asanga also said:  
Also, in order that the side of virtue be gradually 
accomplished properly, the bodhisattva i nitially teaches those 
w ho have the w isdom of childish sentient beings easy 
Dharma, and causes them to follow the easy directions and 
instructions. Realizing that they have developed middling 
w isdom, he causes them to follow intermediate Dharma 
teachings a nd intermediate directions and instructions. 
Realizing that they have developed vast wisdom, he teaches 
them profound Dharma teachings and causes them to follow  
subtle directions and instructions. His beneficial activities 
descend on those sentient beings progressively.  
 
Here Asanga expresses how bodhisattvas who have relied on method and wisdom are 
benefiting sentient beings. They do this in accordance with the level of intelligence of 
the respective sentient being. Those who have the wisdom of childish se ntient beings  means 
someone who is of child like intelligence, like someone who is new to the Dharma. To 
this person they will teach easy Dharma and easy advice to follow. When they then 
develop into a being of middling intelligence, they then teach a midd ling Dharma and 
advice of the middling type. Then this persons continuum ripens further into the 
greatest of intelligence, and now the bodhisattva will give them very subtle Dharma 
and subtle types of advice. In this way, the guidance is given to sentient beings in 
stages.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 230 – week one to seven    
Aryadeva, in [his] Lamp of Summarised Behaviour , establishes that first the training 
in contemplations of the perfection vehicle and after that the mode of engagement in 
mantra { 63}  needs to be gradual. Summarising the meaning [of this]  he says:  
In order for beings w ho act like beginners  
to get involved in ultimate truth  
the means the perfect Buddha expounded  
resemble the steps of a flight of stairs.  
 
In the Lamp of Summarised Behaviour , Aryadeva says one first trains in the thoughts of 
the perfection vehicle, which comes down to the three principle aspects of the path. 
Then one enters into Mantrayana. He is showing the need to practice in stages. Steps  
can also be translated as gradual.  
QUESTION:   As y ou have to practice the perfections in both the Perfection 
Vehicle and the Mantra Vehicle, why is one specified as the Perfection Vehicle?  
GEN: Yes, you do practice the perfections in both, but it doesn‟t follow that if you are 
practicing th e perfections, you are therefore practicing the Perfection Vehicle. For 
example, to practice the perfections in the Mantra Vehicle you might practice 
generosity by making water bowls. By mixing this with Secret Mantra methods you 
would generate yourself as  a deity, send out all kind of offering deities and make 
limitless offerings. This is a practice of the perfection of generosity, but in a tantric 
way.  
QUESTION:  But still, we have the practice of the perfections in both vehicles, so why 
is one specified as the Perfection Vehicle?   
GEN: Actually it‟s more the other way round, the other one is qualified as the Tantric 
Vehicle. In the Tantric Vehicle you practice the six perfections connected with Secret 
Mantra, so it is called the Secret Mantra Vehicle. The other way of practicing the six 
perfections is not connected with Secret Mantra, so it is not called the Secret Mantra 
vehicle.

In Aryadeva‟s Four Hundred V erses there is a verse that says:  
 
 “In the beginning you restrain yourself from non -meritoriou s actions ”  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
231 This refers to the practice of a being of the small scope, abandoning the ten non -
virtuous actions, which is the central practice of the small scope.   
 
 “in the middle you restrain from grasping for a self ” 
 
The second line talks about the pe rson of middle scope working at destroying the 
grasping at a self of person and grasping at a self of phenomena. These are the two 
types of self -grasping, there are no other. From these one restrains the mind.   
  
“and then you destroy all the imprints of grasping for a self.  
The one who understands this is a skilled one (or scholar). ”  
 
Finally, in the third line, the person of the great scope is described as destroying even 
the imprints of these two types of grasping and by this destroying the obscuratio ns to 
omniscience and gaining omniscience. If someone understands this presentation of the 
three types of beings, then this person is skilled (this refers to the last line).  
 This quote comes from the Lam-Rim Chenmo . In our text there is a quote 
of Aryade va which is from a text teaching secret mantra called Lamp of Summarised 
Behaviour . 
Aryadeva, in [his] Lamp of Summarised Behaviour, establishes that first the training 
in contemplations of the perfection vehicle and after that the mode of engagement in 
mantra { 63}  needs to be gradual. Summarising the meaning [of this] he says:  
In order for beings w ho act like beginners  
to get involved in ultimate truth  
the means the perfect Buddha expounded  
resemble the steps of a flight of stairs.  
 
This is to be underst ood in the context of tantra. Someone having trained in the 
perfection vehicle, in order to become a suitable vessel for mantra, would first of all 
request an empowerment to ripen his continuum. Having received this, he also would 
have accepted pledges and  tantric vows which need to be guarded perfectly. With this 
he enters into the practice of the generation stage. This is what the first line refers to. 
When someone practices in this way at some point he masters the generation stage and 
becomes ready to en ter the completion stage practice which is expressed in the second 
line. In Tibetan there is no word for truth; it says “ to get involved in ultimate meaning ” in 
another context this can mean ultimate truth, but hear it refers to meaning, which 
refers to th e highest practice of the completion stage.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 232 – week one to seven   The last two lines: even in tantra the buddha presents a structure to be 
practiced in stages. So in general we have the extensive presentation of going through 
the practices of a being of the small scope up to a  being of the great scope in the 
perfection vehicle. On the basis of this you receive an empowerment and keep the 
pledges and vows, then enter into the generation stage and once you have mastered 
this, you go to the second great step of tantra, the complet ion stage. As it is made clear 
here that even with the tantric teachings you have to follow a gradual sequence, then 
we can apply the same to the perfection vehicle. Also in the common vehicle we have 
to follow the steps as presented by the buddha. Just li ke someone can‟t jump ahead 
right to the completion stage in tantric practice, before he did the generation stage, in 
the same way someone can‟t jump to the great scope without having practiced the 
lower two scopes.  
  
No we come to another main heading of  the whole text.  
This still comes in the context of taking the essence of our freedoms and endowments.

3 Training the mind in the stages of the path of great beings  
 
All these three are a way to take the essence of this precious human rebirth.  
 
The second one has three points: Training the mind in the stages of the pat h common 
to lesser beings, training the mind in the stages of the path common to intermediate 
being, and training the mind in the stages of the path of great beings.

233 CHAPTER  II 
SMALL  SCOPE  
 
EXPLANATION OF THE ST AGES OF THE PATH COM MON TO LESSER BEINGS  
 
2B4B -2B1 The stages of the path common to lesser beings  
A The actual training in the contemplations of lesser beings  
B The gauge of those contemplations having arisen  
C Clearing up misconceptions about them  
 
Gauge  could also be translated as measurement.   
 
{ 63}  The first one has three points: the actual training in the contemplations of lesser 
beings, the gauge of those contemplations having arisen, clearing up misconceptions 
about them.  
 
2B4B -2B1A  The actual training in the contemplations of lesser beings   
1 Developing a mental attitude of interest in future worlds  
2 Relying on the methods for the happiness of future worlds  
 
Here the methods of how to attain the happiness of future worlds are presented.  
 
The first of those has tw o points: developing a mental  attitude of interest in future 
w orlds and relying on the methods for the happiness of future w orlds.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -1 Developing a mental attitude of interest in future worlds  
 
A Being mindful of death: the thought that one is not going to abide in this world  for 
long  
 
This is a recollection of impermanence in the form of the certainty of your own death. 
If you reflect on this, an interest for future lives will come about. If you don‟t do this 
no such interest will arise.  
 
B What things will be like in future worlds: thinking of the happiness and suffering of 
the two kinds of migrating beings  
 
You think about future lives and what they might be like, what could happen to you, 
happy or suffering. Thinking about these two types of migration, you then reflect on 
how these depend on the positive and negative deeds you did in this life, which all 
come about through your own actions.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 234 – week one to seven  The first one has tw o points: being mindful of death - the thought that one is not going 
to stay in this w orld for long and w hat things w ill be like in future w orlds - thinking of 
the happiness and suffering of the tw o kinds of migrating beings.  
 
EXPLANATION OF HOW TO  BE MINDFUL OF DEATH -  
THAT ONE WILL NOT ST AY IN THIS WORLD FOR  LONG  
 
2B4B -2B1A -1A Being mindful of death - the thought that o ne is not going to stay in this world 
for long  
1 The disadvantages of not cultivating mindfulness of death  
2 Advantages of cultivating it  
3 What kind of mind that is mindful of death is being generated  
4 How to cultivate mindfulness of death  
The first of t hose has four points: the disadvantages of not cultivating mindfulness of 
death, advantages of cultivating it, w hat kind of mind that is mindful of death is 
being generated, and how  to cultivate mindfulness of death.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -1A1 The disadvantages of not  cultivating mindfulness of death  
A Demonstration that the mind’s holding on to the idea that one is not going to die is the 
gateway of harm  
First: Although in all of us there is the thought that at the end of our days death w ill 
come, every day w e think “ I w ill not die today”, “today I w ill not die either“. That 
w ay, even right until w e are about to die, the mind holds on to the idea that w e are 
not going to die.   
 
While we all agree that everyone has to die, we think it won‟t happen to us today, this 
mon th or this year. This kind of mind even remains until right before our death and 
might even get stronger. Elderly people for example can have quite a strong form of 
grasping at permanence. They might start to be very careful about their possessions. In 
general, they will say that they will die, maybe even this year, but still they hold to the 
idea that it won‟t happen today.  
 This kind of grasping for permanence is going on right up to the moment you 
die, and really blocks the entrance gate to taking the es sence of your freedoms and 
endowments. Therefore, it is one of the greatest obstacles to dharma practice.  
  
B How this prevents us from even taking an interest in future lives  
{ 64}  If you do not take to heart an antidote to this and you are obscured by su ch an 
idea, and then develop the idea that you will remain in this life, you w ill keep 
thinking about w ays of achieving happiness and eliminating suffering in this life only 
thinking: “I need this and I need that… ” As a mind investigating meaningful goals 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
235 such as future lives, liberation, and omniscience does not arise, no allow ances are 
made for engaging in Dharma.  
 
Constantly thinking about the one‟s needs right now is something you can also see 
with elderly people who, due to their strong grasping for permanence, develop even 
greater needs as they grow older. With this mind, you are only concerned with the 
happiness  and suffering of this life, and there is no opportunity to develop an interest 
for future lives, liberation or omniscience. As such, an interest doesn‟t come about, 
obviously there is no entrance into dharma practice, and your freedoms and 
endowments will  be wasted.   
 
C How this results in meaningful endeavours also having little power  
Even if you engage in study for a hundred [years], contemplation and meditation, 
w hatever virtue you do w ill have little pow er, as it is done for the sake of this life 
alone. As you w ill engage in them linked with faulty behaviour, negativities, and 
dow nfalls, it w ill be rare for them not to be mixed w ith causes of bad migrations.  
 
You might for example listen to the dharma and reflect, or even meditate on it, but if 
you do  it only for the sake of this life, any kind of virtue you practice will only have a 
very small force. You might for example listen to dharma in order to explain it to 
others later, and become a famous teacher. With this kind of attitude of listening to 
the dharma you will most likely end up doing all kinds of negativities. You might enter 
into teaching out of competitiveness, through jealousy, or become completely arrogant. 
All this can happen due to practicing dharma without an understanding of your own 
death, and this can actually become a cause for going to the suffering realms.  
 Such a person accepts future lives, but being subject to the grasping for 
permanence never thinks about them.  
 
D How this results in long -term goals, too, being postponed  
Even if you aim at future w orlds and allow  for their attainment, you w ill not be able 
to cease the laziness of postponement that thinks: “I shall do it later”. A major effort 
at accomplishing it properly w ill not come about, as you w hile aw ay your time w ith 
sleepiness, pointless chatter, and distractions of food, drink and the like.  
 
Such a person thinks about and accepts future lives, and feels something should be 
done for the benefit of others, but due to the strong grasping for permanence puts 
dharma practi ce off for later. He says that he can practice in future lives, and in his 
prayers he might even pray to be able to practice and realise the dharma in future lives. 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 236 – week one to seven  So although already having the wonderful opportunity to practice in this life, he prays 
to be able to practice in a future life.  
We will not be able to block out such a kind of laziness if we are under this 
grasping for permanence. Furthermore, your mind will become very cloudy or sleepy in 
dharma teachings, and outside of the teachings you mig ht engage into all kinds of 
useless things, like always going to museums and chatting constantly! Like this, you 
will never be able to generate a great joyous effort for dharma practice, and due to 
being constantly distracted you will never work at attaini ng anything in the correct 
manner.  
  
E How holding to permanence induces attachment, aversion, and so forth and how they 
induce faulty behaviour  
Not only that, through putting great effort into the excellences of this life the mental 
afflictions and faulty  behaviour induced by them increase and then you turn your back 
on the nectar of Dharma. Since that leads to bad migrations, w hat could be more 
inappropriate than that?  From the Four Hundred Verses :  
If someone sleeps as if at ease,  
As if the ruler of thr ee w orlds,  
Lord of his death did not exist - 
What else could be more horrible?  [1] 
 
And in Engaging in [Bodhisattva] Behaviour  it says:  
Everything has to be abandoned,  
Unaw are that this is so,  
I did negativities of all sorts,  
For the sake of friend and foe. 
 
We exist under the rule of the Lord of Death, but act as if he didn‟t exist at all. Being 
under his power means that he can show up anytime and lead us anywhere he wishes 
to, just as a person being carried away by the police is powerless to prevent i t, and can 
be treated in any way the police decide.  
 If in such a situation we are having a nice nap, we don‟t even have a clue about 
the danger of the situation, what could be more horrible? The reference to the three 
worlds (desire -, form - and formless r ealm) shows that wherever you go in samsara, 
there is no protection, and you will be at the mercy of the Lord of Death. You will die, 
wherever you go.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
237  For example, if you did something very illegal, and the police arrested you, by 
putting up the bail or v arious other methods, you might manage to escape. But you 
won‟t be able to find any method to escape  the Lord of Death.  
Even though we don‟t stay for long in this life anymore, we spend what remains 
of it engaging into all kinds of negativities. This is e xpressed in this second verse from 
the Bodhisattvacharyavatara . But we will not be accompanied by any of the objects we are 
so attached to, for which we engage into all these negativities. Our parents, relatives 
and even their children won‟t come with us, even if we ask them to. Instead, we will go 
completely alone, and what will come with us are the negativities we accumulated for 
their sake, which they also won‟t share with us. By not realising this, due to our strong 
grasping for permanence, we engage in  all these actions.  
     [2] 
2B4B -2B1A -1A2 The advantages of cultivating it  
It refers to the mindfulness of death  
 
A  The great value  
Second: { 65}  Once a genuine awareness mindful of death has developed, for instance 
if you are convinced you w ill die tom orrow , and you have just a little understanding of 
the Dharma, you w ill see that henceforth you w ill be without the company of relatives 
and friends and you w ill avert craving for them. In the majority of people the w ish to 
take the essence of this life by  means of giving things aw ay and the like will naturally 
arise. In that w ay, seeing that all effort for the sake of w orldly goals such as fame and 
gain are futile, you w ill turn aw ay from faulty behaviour, accumulate the good karma 
of going for refuge, eth ical discipline and the like. What could be more meaningful 
than ascending to sublime states oneself by those means and guiding migrating beings 
there, too? That is w hy it is also praised w ith many analogies. In the Parinirvana 
[Sutra]  it says:  
Among all p loughing of fields, that in autumn is best. Among 
all footprints, that of the elephant is best. Among all 
discriminations, those of impermanence and death are best: 
they eliminate all the desire,  pride and ignorance of the three 
realms.    [3] 
 
Similarly, it is praised, among other things, as a hammer destroying all mental 
afflictions and all faulty behaviours instantly as w ell as a gate to the simultaneous 
accomplishment of all that is virtuous and excellent.  
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 238 – week one to seven  In summer some weeds and undergrowth grow on t he field, and when it is ploughed in 
autumn the undergrowth that grew will be turned under the soil, thereby making a 
good fertilizer.  
   
One advantage of being mindful of one‟s death is not to be fearful or surprised by it. 
Additionally, one will come to  a certain understanding of dharma, one will not engage 
into strong negativities for the sake of one‟s companions, and a certain type of 
generosity and the wish to take the essence of the freedoms and endowments will arise.  
 By having this kind of mind yo u will be able to understand how meaningless all 
your efforts are towards worldly things, like gain and fame , then as a result, you will 
engage into excellent refuge, ethical discipline and so on. By seeing this you are able to 
turn away from negativities .  
 
 
FRIDAY 19 MARCH 2010  - SESSION 2 
If you generate a mindfulness of death, you will be able to see the marvels of this life 
as without essence, and be able to turn away from all negative behaviours. In other 
words, you will be able to accumulate positive karma and be able to ascend, like on  a 
staircase, to all the holy states.  
From one point of view, our aggregates are suffering, but from another point of 
view, if you take the essence of the freedoms and endowments, and use them to end 
suffering, they transform into a jewel form from which you achieve all the benefits 
starting from the benefits connected with future lives, and onwards up to liberation 
and omniscience.  This precious human rebirth is the physical basis for all dharma 
achievements. What could be more meaningful than that? You will be able to guide all 
sentient beings to these higher states, so what could be more meaningful than that? 
For all these reasons, through many examples, there are so many benefits to 
mindfulness of death, and why it is so highly praised.  
 
If you generat e this awareness of death you won‟t be attached to the phenomena of 
this life, and therefore won‟t be full of fear at the time of death. In other words, 
through this mindfulness of death, you regain the power of your situation which at the 
moment is still in the hand of the Lord of Death, and then this power will be with you.  
 
Many different analogies are used to express the impermanence of the body; the 
unstableness of our life and all phenomena.  One is that our life and body is like 
something made of clay , which brakes very easily, and all phenomena are like a dream, 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
239 meaning they are not stable. Other analogies are that phenomena are like a water 
bubble, bursting in the next moment, or like a reflection or an illusion. If you have this 
outlook of impermane nce, then you will not relate to these phenomena in the wrong 
way, and at the time of death you will not have a problem to let them go.  
Therefore, this mindfulness is really very special and powerful, and can benefit 
us in many ways, like setting us on at taining the benefits of future lives and other 
dharma topics, helping us to relate correctly to the phenomena of this life and 
eliminating fear at the time of death. We might be interested in all kinds of mantras, 
but there is no knowledge mantra that has such a capacity as this mindfulness of death.  
 
B It is the gate to all excellence  
In brief, the time for achieving the goals of beings is only this time where w e have 
gained the special basis. We mostly remain in bad migrations and even if w e make it 
to happy migrations just once they are predominantly places of unfreedom, so that w e 
do not find opportunity to accomplish the teachings. If w e gain a basis suited to 
accomplishing the teachings and still do not accomplish them properly, it is due to this 
thought of “I’m not going to die yet”. Therefore, this mind that holds to the position of 
not dying { 66}  is the door to all manner of degeneration and mindfulness of death is 
the door to all excellence.  
 
The special basis  refers to the precious human rebirth. Right when having attained it, it 
is time to work for the aims of sentient beings. Places of unfreedom  refer to lacking the 
freedoms and endowments, and therefore lacking a basis for dharma practice. The door 
to all manner of degeneration  means that this m ind that holds to the position of not 
dying is the entrance door to all kinds of negativities.  
 
C How it is necessary to give up misunderstandings and meditate from the bottom of one’s 
heart  
Therefore, you should not think: “This is a practice for those w ho do not have 
profound Dharma, other objects of meditation to meditate on “ and “You should 
meditate on this, but only a bit in the beginning, it is unsuitable to practice it 
continually”. You should not take it like that, but rather you should meditate upon it, 
until you gain certainty from the bottom of your heart that it is necessary in the 
beginning, in the middle,  and at the end.  
 
The first misunderstanding here is that someone thinks the mindfulness of death is a 
minor practice for people who don‟ t engage into deep practices like Tumo  (developing 
inner heat) or Dzogchen .  
 The second one is that someone thinks it is necessary to mediate on this but just 
in a brief or superficial way. This is not the case, and it is especially clear with regards 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 240 – week one to seven  to how Buddha Shakyamuni taught the dharma. The first teachings he gave were the 
four noble truths, of which the first one is suffering, of which the first of its four 
aspects is impermanence. In this way, the very first teaching he gave was on 
impermanence, and just as he was passing into parinirvana, it was the last teaching he 
gave. In additions to these, he taught the subject of impermanence many times 
throughout his life. This makes it clear how, in the context of the deeds of the Buddha, 
that this teachi ng and the encouragement to cultivate an understanding of 
impermanence was important in the beginning, the middle and the end.  
  
In general, tantric teachings are very precious, but when the buddha was teaching on 
this planet, he didn‟t teach tantra in pub lic. This is clear from the fact that many 
people said that the tantric teachings were completely made up by Nagarjuna, because 
at the time of the buddha no one heard them. This is because he taught them only to 
specific people in the respective form of Ka lachakra, Heruka etc.  We don‟t have the 
capacity to directly encounter Kalachakra, Heruka and so forth, and therefore we are 
of the type of the vast majority to whom the buddha taught especially about the four 
noble truths and impermanence. As a result, a s we are of this type, and even though in 
general the tantric teachings are very important, what is most important to us are the 
four noble truths. Therefore, we should be very interested in them.  
  
As a preliminary for tantric practice, you need to obtai n a tantric empowerment to 
become a suitable vessel, and in order to receive this empowerment you first need to 
have trained in the common path (you need bodhicitta and emptiness), and in this way, 
you will be mature enough to receive the empowerment. But if you haven‟t trained 
completely in this, but still go to receive the empowerment or initiation you at least 
need to develop an extra strong faith in the lama giving the empowerment. For 
example, if he touches your crown with the vase, you have to generat e the strong 
visualisation that he is giving you the transcendental wisdom of great bliss. You have 
to visualise how this completely fills you up, and if you can do this out of great faith, 
then you received the minimum type of empowerment. If you just sit  there and think 
about how they give you some food and drink, then you will receive nothing at all!  
 
QUESTION:  Is the last line above also to be understood in the context of one 
meditation session. Meaning that one meditates about impermanence in the begin ning, 
middle and end of one session?  
GEN:  It is very suitable to do this, but in general this is important for the whole path. 
Reflecting on impermanence is important in the beginning, because it is your entrance 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
241 gate into dharma. While you are practicing,  this you should repeatedly reflect on it, and 
when you come to the end of your practice, where you really aim for complete 
enlightenment, it is important to support this through repeatedly reflecting on it. In 
this way, it refers  to the whole path.  
 But even in the context of one life, it is important because in the beginning it 
will bring you to dharma practice, then it is important to think about impermanence 
during all the different dharma activities you do all your life, and even in old age it is 
important to reflect about this again and again.  
 Also for one day it is important to think about death when you get up in the 
morning, repeatedly remind yourself during the day, and before you lie down you 
reflect on it again. All of this is about constantly reminding yourself how you should 
not waste ti me, but get active and do something.  
So then also, within one meditation session you can do this by starting it with 
this reflection. During the session you can remind yourself of the urgency of 
producing a good dharma practice, and at the end of the sess ion you can think about it 
again. It is constantly important and beneficial, and there are many ways to see it as 
important in the beginning, the middle and the end.  
 
QUESTION:  Is what is described about reliance on the teacher, and about the precious 
huma n rebirth, also practiced by the small scope practitioner? Because both these 
topics are presented before the actual presentation of the small scope begins.  
GEN:  Yes, it is a common practice. Do you also think they are not the path of the 
middle and great  scope, because they are also not presented there? You could say 
either that they are a practice shared with the small scope, or you could say they are 
not, because of being introduced before. Both views are good.  
 Everything that belongs to the common pat h, starting right from the correct 
way of reliance, up to special insight, are preparations for receiving an empowerment 
to become a suitable vessel for tantra. The complete shared or common path is from 
that point of view a preparation for taking an empow erment. At the same time we also 
have to say that all these practices are actual practices that need to be realised. So from 
that point of view they are an actual practice, but from another they are a preparation 
for something else. In general, when talkin g about preliminaries like 100.000 
prostrations, then this is an actual practice, but at the same time, it is a preparation for 
other things. So it is kind of both. So if you say it is an actual practice, you could ask 
for who is it an actual practice? The  answer would be for the person who is doing it! 
Talking about the six preparatory practices (the Jorchö ), for the one engaging them it is 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 242 – week one to seven  his actual practice, and the same can be said concerning the seven limbs and other 
practices.  
 If we ask ourselves w hy these two practices are in the introduction, even before 
the actual presentation of the small scope [translator‟s note: this word is actually not there in 
the Tibetan but was added by the people editing this translation ], then we have to be aware that 
in these presentations there was already talk about all kinds of dharma topics like calm 
abiding and special insight, even if only in an abbreviated manner.  
 
 
FRIDAY 19 MARCH 2010  - SESSION 3 
QUESTION:  Can a layperson attain liberation (in this life)?  
GEN:  This depends on the layperson and the practice. By perfect practice one can 
attain liberation. Someone might be a monk for many lifetimes and still not attain 
liberation while a layperson who engages into excellent practice can attain it. But when 
a pract itioner comes to a higher state of practice, referring to the path of seeing, 
meaning one attains ultimate bodhicitta and the direct realisation of emptiness, then 
through the power of these minds the person transforms automatically into a gelong. 
So it se ems like eventually everyone will become a monk!  
 If someone is a monk but his thinking remains like that of householders, then 
we have to say that he is a monk, but his thinking remains that of a householder. A 
layperson could appear to follow the househo lder‟s way, but by guarding his mind 
excellently, he comes to a state similar to a monk who guards his vows properly. There 
is a big difference between a householder and an ordained person, who generates the 
mind of, “I have gone forth, I have ordained” an d by this cuts off the concerns of 
laypeople and leads a simple life. A layperson, not being ordained, engages into all 
kinds of activities of family, children a house, etc. that bring lots of worries. Right this 
is cut off for an ordained person. Such a p erson thinks that their objective is to engage 
into listening, reflecting and meditating.  
 
QUESTION:  But even though we are ordained we have a family, isn‟t it?  
GEN:  Yes, but you are not a couple and don‟t build your own family, and so you have 
pacified t hese problems. Someone might be ordained but still be overly concerned with 
his family, and in this way, he again has all the worries of a householder. Even though 
on a physical basis he is ordained, his mind is like the one of a householder.  
 
QUESTION:  What does it mean that a layperson will transform into a gelong or 
gelong -ma at this high level of practice? Does this mean that at this level they are able 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
243 to guard their mind like a gelong who guards his vows, or do they actually generate the 
vows, which a re a kind of subtle form?  
GEN:  I am not sure if we can say that the person becomes an actual gelong, if the 
vows are actually generated. Normally they are generated once you take them and here 
there is not such taking of vows. But for sure such a person is  similar due to having 
generated the wisdom realising emptiness directly, the afflictions are at bay and the 
person will behave like a gelong. Especially desire and attachment will be controlled at 
this level.  
 
QUESTION:  Could Gen -la give some advice about  memorizing texts and verses?  
GEN:  First of all, in the morning you would only recite one four -line verse for one 
mala [108 times]. Then you leave it there and after lunch you recite it another ten 
times, and before you go to sleep another ten times. Then the next morning when you 
get up, even if you didn‟t have it the day before, the next morning you will definitely 
be able to recall it.  
 Then you go to the next verse and do the same, but as this is already the second 
verse, you sometimes disperse the mala  with reciting the previous verse, and in this 
way you also make a connection between the two. If you are of great mental sharpness, 
then on one day you will be able to memorize a whole page. If this is not the case, then 
maybe just one verse. Such sharp m inded people don‟t have to do one mala, but maybe 
after ten or fifteen times they have already memorised it.  
 
QUESTION:  Could Gen -la please explain what intuition is. Is it a mental factor? An 
example could be that someone already knows or feels that they will receive a phone 
call from a certain person. And is it possible to get some knowledge from such 
intuition?  
GEN:  [translators note: In Tibetan, there is no word for “intuition ” so it is quite difficult to 
translate]  What happens in this example of the p hone call is that there is the ripening of 
a karma that produces this knowledge. A conceptual mind has arisen from this karma.  
Doesn‟t this talk about situations where one for example thinks, “Ah, tomorrow 
the weather will be nice” and then indeed it is n ice, or maybe one even has seen this in 
the weather forecast! But with regards to the dharma, there is no such intuitive 
understanding. The dharma needs to be studied in order to understand it. For example, 
to understand emptiness you have five different r easonings, the first being the 
reasoning of one and many with which you understand that there is no single or many 
things which are not a dependent arising. The second one is the king of reasonings, 
dependent arising. So following these reasonings, discuss ing and analysing them, you 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 244 – week one to seven  will finally come to realise emptiness. These are the methods to understand these 
topics like emptiness. To put them aside and say that there are alternative methods to 
these will not bear any fruit.  
 
QUESTION:  In such situations where a phone call is previously apprehended, does 
this happen because everything is interrelated?  
GEN:  Of course, we can say that everyone is interrelated because each sentient being 
has been father and mother to each other numberless times. There are also different 
types of relationship. For example, some people might talk to each other or not! 
Amongst us for example there is a very special relationship. We have all come together 
due to this specific relationship of having the same aim .  
 Knowing another person‟s thought comes on the basis of having a strong 
relationship with the other person. If you know a person well, then at some point you 
will understand what the other person is thinking. If you say for example that one 
person alway s gets angry, then you will relate to this person in a very careful way 
because you have understood that this person is easily angry. This kind of knowing 
comes from your relation to that person. With regards to another person you 
understand that whatever you say that person will not get angry. All these different 
characters you understand by having a long lasting relationship with that person.  
 
QUESTION:  Could Gen -la explain what uncommon refuge refers to.  
GEN:  This refers to the Mahayana refuge, where you  take refuge with the Mahayana 
motivation.
