245 WEEK 4: 12  16 APRIL 2010  
 
MONDAY 12 APRIL  2010  - SESSION 1 
QUESTIONS  
 
QUESTION:  What is the task of conscientiousness in meditation? Does it bring the 
mind back to the affliction‟s antidote or just to the object of meditation?   
ANSWER:  Conscientiousness brings mindfulness back to the object of meditation 
once the power of distraction  has made us lose it, for example the image of the 
buddha. By renewing the mindfulness of your object of meditation, you are employing 
the antidote to the distraction. Moreover, if attachment is there in addition to 
distraction, you don‟t have to rely on t he direct antidotes but rather you just go back 
to the virtuous object in order to abandon the affliction.  
 In general, conscientiousness can be understood as practicing ethical 
discipline, which is mainly referring to guarding the mind against the ten non -virtuous 
actions.  
 Introspection functions to check if the mind is holding the virtuous or 
non-virtuous side, while conscientiousness is described as being intent on practicing 
virtue, and mindfulness functions to hold the object itself.  
 
Introspection i s a mental factor that functions to check if a fault has appeared in the 
mind. It is like a police officer who diligently watching for any negative activity or 
fault. Its function is only to observe this, while it is conscientiousness that leads the 
mind a way from the negativity, and mindfulness that holds the virtuous object in order 
to return to it.  
 
QUESTION:  Could you also do this without conscientiousness by just employing 
mindfulness?  
ANSWER:  Introspection just becomes aware of negative aspects in our  mind, but also 
of the virtuous aspects. The mental factor of conscientiousness is the mental factor 
that wants to guard the mind, and it is mindfulness that is supporting it in this work by 
checking that things don‟t get lost. Another word for mindfulness  is recollection. We 
could use the analogy of someone learning how to drive. When he does his driving 
test, it is introspection that checks the speed in order to stay within the speed limit, 
and what is happening on both sides of the road. Mindfulness woul d be the factor that 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 246 – week one to seven  knows what is too fast or too slow, and to use the mirrors. Conscientiousness is the 
factor that is driving very carefully, and by bringing all of this together, the driver can 
pass his exam. On the other hand, there are many people wh o fail this test.  
In order to practice ethical discipline we really need to rely on the recollection of 
consciousnesses that are aware and mindful. The actual task of ethical discipline comes 
about due to conscientiousness that refrains from negativities and goes towards virtue 
by taking oneself or others as a reason. This shows the close relationship to the two 
mental factors of self -respect and embarrassment. These two are a cause for 
conscientiousness.  
 
QUESTION:  How do the three causes for the precious human rebirth come about? 
Could they come about in one life, or only through several lives?  
ANSWER:  These three causes don‟t have to come about over many lives. Even in one 
life, you can accumulate the causes for m any precious human rebirths. For example, if 
day by day you practice the ethical discipline that abandons the ten non -virtues, you 
can accumulate many causes for the precious human rebirth, just as continuously 
practicing generosity can do the same. It is ethical discipline that is the basis, which is 
accompanied by generosity and stainless wishing prayers. Practicing ethical discipline 
doesn‟t necessarily talk about having done all this in one life, or it needs to be 
practiced continuously or completely, b ut you practice it as much as you can, and this 
then can become the cause for the precious human rebirth. It is the strength of these 
three causes that dictates if one acheives the result of precious human rebirth, and as a 
result, you might be able to acc umulate them within one week or even within one day.  
 The throwing karma that leads to the precious human rebirth refers to 
taking a human rebirth achieved from the ethics of abandoning the ten non -virtuous 
actions. Let‟s say you did this, maybe with grea t effort within one day, and in that way 
you accumulate the throwing karma for a human rebirth. However, it would also 
extremely beneficial to be endowed with several other factors such as a beautiful body, 
plentiful resources and a wide circle of friends.  The cause of a beautiful body is the 
practice of patience, the cause of plentiful resources is the practice of generosity and 
the cause for a wide circle of friends is the non -engagement in jealousy.  Additionally, 
if you are protecting life, for example by saving the life of small insects, you would be 
creating the cause for a long lifespan as a human. As a result, if you are interested in 
these additional features, you will have to practice in this way. All this will come in the 
Lam-Rim in the section on  the person of middling capacity. I think the most extensive 
presentation of karma will also be in this section.  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
247 QUESTION:  The throwing karma for a mere human rebirth is created by practicing 
the ethical discipline of abandoning the ten non -virtuous acti ons. For example, if 
someone is about to kill a horse, but in his mind the thought about the suffering of the 
horse occurs and he refrains from it. Would that moment already qualify as a cause for 
a human rebirth?  
ANSWER:  It would not be enough just to hav e this ethical mind for an instant. It has 
to be of a certain strength. If a person is about to engage in the actions of killing, but 
reflects on that the other being has a mind, and the suffering this being would have to 
endure,  on that basis some forms of compassion would be generated and one would 
abstain from killing. This action of refraining from killing would remain, and in this 
way one would abandon this negativity over a over such a prolonged period of time, 
and its intensity might accumulate the causes for such a rebirth.  
On the other hand, if the gun is already on the horse, and one just thinks “well, it 
might be painful for it” and then one just walks off, then this will be far too weak.  
 In general, these very weak minds of, “Well, there‟s some thing negative in 
it so I‟ll just leave it aside”, are not enough. They have to be minds in which 
compassion is generated and decisions according to the dharma are made. The Tibetan 
word for ethics is Tshül -trim. The first syllable, „Tshül‟, refers to the way or the fashion, 
and the second syllable, „trim‟, refers to law or dharma. Therefore, you rule yourself in 
the fashion of dharma. If someone is obliged to go to war, but refrains from killing 
another human, then this will not be strong enough even to be  classified as ethical 
discipline. Ethical discipline comes about through having thought in accordance with 
the dharma. When we talk about giving up killing in the context of ethical discipline, 
then one would see the other being as a living being just as oneself, and that he is no 
different in his wish to avoid suffering. As a result, one would decide to refrain from 
such an action as it would be totally incorrect to inflict suffering on this living sentient 
being. If the action is accompanied by the refle ction on such dharma thoughts, then it 
qualifies as ethical discipline. In order for something to be ethical discipline, you 
definitely need to generate a mind that has no doubt that it would be completely 
inappropriate to engage in a negative action.   
 
QUESTION:  So would refraining from killing be sufficient to qualify as ethical 
discipline, and refraining from the other nine non -virtues is more of a secondary 
importance?  
ANSWER:  In itself, abandoning to take life is a form of ethical discipline. We could  
specify it as the ethical discipline of abandoning the taking of life. If a monk takes a 
life, it is called the defeat of taking life because it  breaks one of the four root vows of 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 248 – week one to seven  an ordained person, and it is the most important of all negativities to a bandon. 
Moreover, if you guard your mind against this defeat, then this in itself is ethical 
discipline. Just by abandoning the negativity of taking life, one will create the cause for 
a mere human rebirth. You can understand that in this action you are re garding the life 
of someone else as precious and therefore don‟t want to bring violence or harm.
 The second of the ten non -virtuous actions, stealing, refers to other 
people having some material support for themselves and under the influence of great 
greed  you take it from them. The result of this might be that since you abandoned 
taking life you will create the cause for being born human, but through the action of 
stealing you create the cause to be born into a poor area where you lack the conditions 
to su stain your life.  
 
QUESTION:  If a horse would suffer a severe disease and you kill it out of compassion 
to relieve it from its pain, what would be the effect of that?  
ANSWER:  This would be a case where although there is some compassion, this 
compassion woul d be distorted. If you see that the horse is very sick and you think 
that killing it would relieve it from its suffering, then the question remains of where it 
will take rebirth. It could be possible that it would take rebirth in a much more severe 
form of  suffering, and in this way, such an action would be a kind of perverted or 
mistaken form of compassion. If this horse dies then in the very next moment, it will 
take the body of the intermediate state, of the bardo being. If it doesn‟t have the 
appropriat e positive karma, then right that state as a bardo being will already be 
characterized by fright and terror, and as a result, you might bring this being from a 
state of being sick into a state of sheer terror.  
 A related yet different example: Someone is i ntent on harming society by 
placing a bomb somewhere. If on the basis of compassion you kill this person, then 
this is the right action to do. You have the clear understanding that this person is an 
object of compassion because he has the intention to kill  many people through which 
he will accumulate the cause for rebirth as a hell being. In order to protect him from 
accumulating this karma, and because of having compassion for all the potential 
victims, you kill him. Nevertheless, it is a negative act, but  due to you being filled with 
these strong forms of compassion, the negativity coming from the killing will be very 
small, and the virtue from this compassion will be overwhelming, and bring huge 
amounts of virtue.  
 
QUESTION:  Is it possible to accumulate at the same time virtue and negativity?  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
249 ANSWER:  Yes. For example, you might do prostrations and while doing this you are 
angry with another person. Or while doing the prostrations you insult another person. 
As a result, you get some virtue from the prostra tion and some negativity from the 
insult.  
 
QUESTION:  Does the stainless wishing prayer necessarily refer to wishing for full 
enlightenment or can it also be for liberation or something else?  
ANSWER:  It doesn‟t have to be a prayer for complete enlightenment . Dedicating for 
liberation is also a stainless wishing prayer and even a wishing prayer for a happy 
future life qualifies as such. An example of a stained wishing prayer would be to 
dedicate for things within this life. You are dedicating for resources wi thin this life, 
meaning you do it with a certain attachment.  
 
QUESTION:  If the person killing the terrorist planting the bomb was a gelong, would 
he commit a defeat of his gelong vows?  
ANSWER:  Yes. 
 
QUESTION:  Could a Christian or a Hindu be a practitioner  of small capacity even 
though he doesn‟t take refuge?  
ANSWER:  We can‟t say that they are such a practitioner in the sense of the buddha -
dharma, but they are practicing in a certain way that would result in accumulating the 
causes for a higher rebirth as a  god or human. When we talk about a practitioner of the 
small capacity, then this is someone aiming at a happy migration, and he would do this 
by reflecting on the law of cause and effect. This is something that a Hindu might 
have, but a Christian probably  doesn‟t think in these terms. When we look at the 
practices of the small capable being, then this is someone who knows that he will die 
and lose his precious human rebirth. In this way, he understands that there is rebirth 
and reflects on karma because of  this, and as a result, a certain fear is generated, along 
with a wish to take refuge.  
 
MONDAY  12 APRIL  2010  - SESSION  2 
 
QUESTION:  I read that it is not possible to kill without a moment of anger. So in the 
case of killing the terrorist out of compassion, would the initiating motivation be 
compassion and the motivation that completes the act be anger?  
ANSWER:  I am doubtful whether yo u need a moment of anger to kill someone else. 
For example, someone says “if I don‟t kill this person I won‟t get his reaches, so I‟m 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 250 – week one to seven  really sorry but I need this”. Such a person is not motivated by anger! Another 
example could be that some doctors practic e euthanasia and in this activity they don‟t 
have any anger. In addition, there is no anger used when young women abort an 
unborn child or in soldiers who try to assassinate a particular person, but also kill 
innocent bystanders without using anger. A sold ier might also shoot a friend out of 
compassion because he is a lot of pain. In all these examples there is no anger used.  
QUESTION:  In the example of killing someone in order to prevent him from harming 
others, how would we purify this, as there probably  would be no regret present?  
ANSWER:  In general, it is not necessary for all types of purification to rely on the 
four forces. You can only apply them with regards to things you have a memory of. In 
this process, your approach would be to purify individual  negative actions. However, 
you have accumulated already all types of innumerable negativities since beginning less 
time and most of them you can‟t recall, and therefore can‟t purify through applying the 
four forces in meditation. Therefore, in general in the practice of purification one 
should have the attitude to purify whatever negativity is there. Another issue is that the 
strongest forms of purification come from bodhicitta and the wisdom realising 
emptiness. They purify everything anyway.  
 
QUESTION:  Would an individual have a precious human rebirth if he is originally 
born in an area where there is no dharma and he is without faith, but later on 
encounters the dharma and generates faith?  
ANSWER:  Such a person would have a precious human rebirth from th e first moment 
on. We could look at the situation of Tibetans who are normally referred to as 
buddhists. In these circumstances, a newborn baby doesn‟t know anything about 
dharma and doesn‟t have faith, but as he grows up he will encounter the dharma and a  
lama and it will generate some faith. In this case, even though the child is born in a 
place where there is dharma, the faith is only developed later on. One would already be 
born with a precious human rebirth, but some of the ten endowments, in particula r 
those that are self -based, would need to be brought about in the course of our life; for 
example the faith in the dharma. But still, one would say that one is born with this 
precious human rebirth. There are also some cases where a person only gains thei r 
complete sense faculties, like their eyesight, later on in life. Of course, I am not 
referring to all of the five self -based endowments, but in this case, I‟m especially 
referring to faith.  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
251 QUESTION:  It was said that having abandoned killing is suffici ent as a cause for the 
human rebirth. What about any of the remaining nine non -virtuous actions; is 
abandoning any one of them enough to acquire a human rebirth?  
ANSWER:  For example, just giving up stealing would not be enough to gain a human 
rebirth. Here  in the West you can see that some people take great care of pets. These 
animals live very well, but through the actions of taking life, they were reborn as an 
animal, despite giving up the action of stealing and therefore creating the result of 
being care d for in such a good way. They even have human assistant working for them 
and taking care of them! Traditionally, the example used in the texts of a very rich 
animal would be a naga.  
 
QUESTION:  We say that virtue is the cause for a human life but at the s ame time the 
body is a kind of suffering, isn‟t it? So how can virtue produce suffering?  
ANSWER:  Now you already have been a Buddhist for such a long time and studied so 
much, but it seems you haven‟t reflected well on the twelve links. The first link is 
ignorance and the human body is the effect of the ripening ignorance. Is ignorance 
virtue? Of cou rse, seen from one side we have entered into a virtuous activity, but the 
ignorance also comes along with this virtuous mind, and as a result of that, our actions 
keep us caught within samsara. We are here due to this ignorance. We might live in 
samsara wi th a sense of being very free and independent, but actually, we have no 
freedom and are under the power of a very evil master.  
 We have to reflect that we want happiness, but yet we experience 
suffering, and this comes about due to this evil overlord who p revents us from 
achieving  our happiness. We are under the power of this mind, which is under the 
power of the afflictions. Therefore, we are completely other powered. In order to 
reverse this, we need to overpower the afflictions. This is what all the dhar ma paths 
are about; changing the mind so that we achieve control over the mind through 
overpowering the afflictions. We can only talk about the mind being free once we 
succeed in this. And this can only be achieved through the practice of ethical 
disciplin e. It is about changing the mind and freeing it from the power of the 
afflictions. From the two aspects of the path, method is the one where we want to 
change the mind into state free from the afflictions by increasing our capacities. We do 
this by relying  on method on the one hand, and wisdom on the other. In this way, we 
can actually gain freedom.  
 
QUESTION:  So is virtue always a cause of happiness, and non -virtue always a cause of 
suffering?  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 252 – week one to seven  ANSWER:  Yes, we can say that. All the different sufferings you undergo come from 
previous non -virtuous activity. The word „contamination‟: the contaminated aggregates 
are contaminated because they came about in an unfree state, other -powered through 
karma and afflictions. A headache for example comes from having a bod y that you 
took on under the power of karma and afflictions. Whatever is non -virtue leads to 
suffering, and whatever is virtue leads to happiness.  
 
QUESTION:  So can we then say that contaminated virtue is not virtue because it leads 
to these contaminated a ggregates?  
ANSWER:  Contaminated virtue leads to contaminated happiness, and even though it 
leads to this contaminated form aggregate, you experience quite some happiness on 
this basis. So even contaminated virtue is to be seen as virtue that leads to happ iness.  
 
QUESTION:  Are throwing and completing karma activated at the same time at the 
time of death, or do they activate one after the other? Because is it correct to say that 
these karmas can be different, one being positive and the other negative?  
ANSWE R: There are different types of completing karma. In one situation the 
completing karmas ripen together with the throwing karma, and then there are other 
completing karmas which ripen later. But in general, we can say that they ripen 
together. An example f or the concomitant ripening would be one person having a 
throwing karma for being human and together with this comes the completing karma 
of having incomplete faculties. Here they ripen together. Another example would be a 
human rebirth due to virtuous thr owing karma, but due to non -virtuous completing 
karma the person ends up in a destitute place. Here again they ripen together.  
 
 
MONDAY  12 APRIL  2010  - SESSION  3 
QUESTION:  Are there any persons who remain small scope beings for their entire 
life? 
ANSWER:  The majority of people are like this! If you don‟t have a wish to be 
liberated from samsara coming from the depth of your heart, then you are a being of 
the small scope, given that you are someone who practices dharma. But I am not 
saying that the book yo u have in front of you (the Lam -Rim) is a book of the person 
of the small scope. This talks about that we are practitioners of the small scope. Don‟t 
lose your spirits, as we will make all effort to enter into the category of the great being!  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
253 QUESTION:  But isn‟t it that one is really only a practitioner of the small scope if one 
has given up all (or most) of the attachment to this life?  
ANSWER:  There is a presentation of the smallest, middling and utter small scope 
practitioner. This is what the dharma sa ys; I don‟t make up this division! In our own 
case, we are also generating thoughts in which we are trying to do things not only for 
ourselves but for all sentient beings; at least we recite the words for that. We should 
keep doing this, and having this ai m to become a great being. By generating these 
thoughts, we are doing the practices of a great being, and therefore we have to say that 
someone who is doing the practices of a great being doesn‟t have to be a great being. 
If at one point we attain to the l evel where we can indeed be called a great being, then 
we would have attained very high states.  
 When we come to such a state of a great being then we get out of this 
unfree state that‟s under the power of our afflicted mind. For someone of the great 
scop e this comes about very easily because he will effortlessly and directly counteract 
the afflictions. Not only that, but he will be able to directly face self -cherishing. A 
being of such a capacity is truly amazing. If you attain to the state of a being of the 
middle scope, then you would have this clear and resolute mind that definitely wants to 
get liberated from all the afflictions, even though it might take some time.  
 The supreme small scope practitioner is completely able to forget about 
the happiness  of this life and single -pointedly focus on the happiness of future lives. 
The middling small scope practitioner could be where we are because we are probably 
mainly working for future lives but also for the benefit of this life. So even though the 
emphasi s is mainly on the happiness of this life, such a person will also think about the 
happiness of future lives. The least of small scope beings is only thinking about the 
happiness of this life. You will find this division in some texts.  
 
QUESTION:  About the  activation of throwing and completing karma, where one is 
positive and one negative: I understood a positive karma has to be activated by a 
positive state of mind, so if the accompanying completing karma is negative, how does 
this get activated, since the  state of mind is not negative?  
ANSWER:  Another word for completing karma is achieving karma. Karma is actually 
referring to action, so it is an activity, not an imprint. When we talk about a throwing 
karma, we mean a certain activity, which then leaves an  imprint for taking a certain 
rebirth. The completing karma is the completing action, but it is a consciousness rather 
than an imprint. It refers to the state of mind at the time of death. In past lives, or this 
life, you accumulated certain imprints throu gh actions, which are throwing actions, and 
these imprints have still to ripen. Let‟s say it is a good imprint, and at the time of 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 254 – week one to seven  death you have the completing karma which is not an imprint but a mind like anger or 
miserliness. We see people dying with th ese minds. This is the completing action that 
is a mind that activates an imprint, rather than being an imprint itself. This is similar to 
me inviting you to drink some of my tasty apple juice, but someone pours some milk 
or yoghurt into it, and therefore spoils the whole thing. In the same way, you have the 
positive throwing karma that is soiled by the activation of a negative state of mind. In 
short, throwing and completing karma don‟t refer both to imprints but one is a mind 
and one an imprint.  
 
QUESTIO N: So a negative state of mind can activate a positive karmic imprint?  
ANSWER:  Yes, this is possible. The negative state of mind would be the completing 
karma. Even if you have a negative state of mind at death, it could activate a positive 
imprint and als o vice versa. For example, a negative throwing karma that leaves a 
negative imprint can be activated at the time of death by a virtuous completing action. 
We had an example earlier, where a being takes rebirth as an animal but lives in riches. 
This comes a bout due to a virtuous completing karma.  
 An example; someone is accumulating riches during his lifetime through 
illegal activities, but dedicates all his riches to society as well as his children due to 
suffering intense regret at the end of his life. Or iginally, there was a very negative 
motivation but later on there was the positive mind of regret and a mind of generosity. 
As a result, it is sure that merit will also be accumulated.  
 Alternatively, someone is strongly affected by the state of poor peop le, 
and decides to find money to help them, but in order to get this money he steals from 
the rich. In this case, the initial motivation is good, but then some negative action 
comes in. All of this is said as an analogy for the situation where there is a v irtuous 
throwing karma, but a non -virtuous completing karma.  
 
QUESTION:  If the positive state of mind at the time of death is one of refuge, could a 
negative state of mind still be activated?  
ANSWER:  It is possible for negative karma to ripen. Imagine some one not going for 
refuge all his life, and doing all kinds of negativities, but out of fear at the time of 
death he develops a sense of refuge. This will not achieve a real sense of refuge or 
protect him from the ripening of the negative karmas he accumula ted. There is no 
habit of seeking refuge. In this way, we can understand that we can‟t wait until the 
time of death to practice dharma; we need to practice right now. Of course, there is 
still benefit for such a person to develop this mind of refuge, but w e can‟t say that this 
will prevent him from going to the lower realms. Therefore, right now we should make 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
255 effort with going for refuge and in the trainings connected with refuge; and here 
especially the practice of non -violence meaning that we don‟t give space to the harmful 
minds of anger, jealousy or pride; the minds which are of the type of wrath; the 
grossest of the gross. These we have to counteract right now, this is our main and 
most urgent practice.  
 Therefore, all our different practices, like re citing mantras and so forth, 
should mainly be done in order to pacify these very rough minds. In the beginning, the 
main emphasis should be on these gross minds and we should make a lot of effort to 
apply the antidotes. Once this has been accomplished we c an turn to the comparatively 
more subtle afflictions. If we succeed in this, there will be no problems in our mind, 
and actions that would necessarily accumulate negativities would be blocked out. As a 
result, our mind would be more serviceable and our rel ationships with others will 
become excellent.  
 
TUES DAY 13 APRIL  2010  - SESSION 1 
QUESTIONS  
Yesterday we were talking about the beings of the small scope and there was a division 
within this of small, middling and great capacity. Here we are talking about someone 
going for refuge to buddha, dharma and sangha and as a result, is a Buddhist, but 
within these there are a great many people who only think about this life. This is the 
small scope being of small capacity. The middling capacity of a small being is someone 
who is still strongly interested in this life, but also occasionally thinks about future 
lives to influence them. There are also quite some beings of this type of capacity. The 
great small being is someone who completely abandons the happiness of t his life, and 
only works for the happiness of the next life. There are only few practitioners of this 
type.  
 Those beings only thinking about the happiness of this life also have to deal 
with great number of afflictions in this state, and do whatever they can to achieve the 
marvels of samsara. As a result, they end up in negative behaviour through which they 
accumulate negative karma. With this behaviour they have turned their backs on the 
very essence of dharma and are led to the lower realms on the basis of the karma 
accumulated through their negative conduct. Despite having attained this perfect 
opportunity of a precious human rebirth, this opportunity becomes wasted and 
meaningless because of their reckless behaviour.  
 In our case, we really need to mak e effort to avoid ending up with such a 
meaningless life, and make effort to bring about the methods for leading a meaningful 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 256 – week one to seven  life. All the good conditions which we try to bring together, however many may come 
about for us, there is no doubt that within th e next hundred years we need to leave; we 
will die and not even an atom of these good outer conditions can come with us. We 
have to leave them behind. Therefore, we need to understand how these good outer 
conditions lack any kind of essence, and this is wh y we will have to leave them behind 
at the time of death. Nevertheless, because of being so caught up in them we have 
most probably accumulated all kinds of negativities. Even our very body, which has 
arisen at the same time as we have in this life, is som ething that will be left behind. 
Additionally, any circle of family or friends, however close they might have been, also 
have to be left behind. We need to abandon these things at the time of death, and we 
might have entered into all kinds of negative cond uct and have accumulated a lot of 
negative karma. It is this negative karma that will certainly follow us into the next life.  
  
So what would be of actual benefit to us? If you engage in listening, reflecting and 
meditating on the dharma and through this accumulate virtue, then from this you will 
only receive benefit. Our life has taken shape in such a way that we have become 
buddhists, meaning we took refuge, and promised to be a disciple of the buddha, and 
we have also developed the thought to listen to,  understand and train in his teachings. 
In previous lives we practiced pure ethical discipline, which acted as a cause for our 
precious human rebirth, and we also engaged into all kinds of generosity, which acted 
as a kind of companion for this cause. Addi tionally, we did all kinds of wishing prayers 
connected to the dharma, called stainless wishing prayers, for example to encounter 
the spiritual friends. This is the kind of neighbouring factor to these other two 
conditions, and through all three together; we achieved this precious human rebirth.  
 In this way, we can understand that we already acted very excellently in past 
lives, and therefore we should take care to do the same again in this life to increase all 
the good actions we did in the past. To actua lly attain the bodies and the mind of a 
buddha, i.e. to gain enlightenment, in this life is rather difficult. But by reflecting on 
how things come from certain causes, and that the enlightened state of a buddha has 
come about from certain causes, then you can understand that there are many stages 
and steps leading up to buddhahood. Through this, you can develop a mind that again 
and again engages into accumulating these causes, and thereby come closer and closer 
to buddhahood. Therefore, the cultivation of the correct ways of thinking is something 
we can accomplish on a daily basis. We need to strengthen these thoughts and attitudes 
daily, and through repeatedly counteracting the different faults we have in the mind, 
we will finally be able to abandon the ne gative minds. If you repeatedly study on a 
daily basis your knowledge and understanding of the dharma will also increase, and as 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
257 a result you will come t oknow the dharma and constantly improve the state of your 
mind.  By relying on these factors, the enem ies of anger, attachment and so forth can 
be counteracted and  slowly but surely, all the different good qualities can be 
developed.  
 
One should generate an outlook that all the conditions for the arisal of afflictions, in 
other words the objects of focus of the afflictions are without any essence, and can be 
only enjoyed in this life. As a result, they are without benefit for us. Through reflecting 
in this way, you can emancipate yourself from these objects and the afflictions, and 
finally you will be able  to develop a genuine mind of impermanence. If one then 
reflects on karmic cause and effect, referring to that at the time of death, one will have 
to take on another set of aggregates under the power of karma. If you really come to 
an understanding of this , especially that it is not under your power, then you will be 
able to generate a pure form of going for refuge; of entrusting yourself to the three 
jewels. In this way, the reflection of impermanence helps to develop a pure form of 
refuge, and through kno wing that you have to repeatedly take on aggregates, you will 
be able to engage into a pure form of ethics by abandoning the ten non -virtuous 
actions. In this way, you will be able to transform your actions of body speech and 
mind.  
 Acting in this way can  be called “taking the essence of the freedoms and 
endowments.” Therefore, if you act in accordance with this presentation you will be 
able to take the smallest form of essence of the precious human rebirth. By thinking 
about impermanence and death, you wi ll be able to engage into the meaningful 
activities of dharma practice and abstain from negative conduct. By constantly 
reflecting on impermanence throughout your life, you will have no worries and be well 
prepared at the time of death. These are the benef its of reflecting on impermanence. 
This is quite the opposite from evading the fact of impermanence, but rather you 
actually dive into the reflection about it and thereby you dive into a pure dharma 
practice. If on the other hand you try to avoid it, which  is impossible anyway, you will 
not be able to bring about such a practice.  
 On the basis of this precious human rebirth, which is qualified by the freedoms 
and endowments, you can achieve all your wishes. It is right this rebirth we attained 
right now. Ev erything presented in the dharma can be attained on the basis of such a 
rebirth. Whether you then actually attain it or not is dependent on you. The path is 
clearly presented here, but if you engage in it or not is up to you. On the basis of this 
precious human rebirth you can get enlightened in one or three lives, or even in three 
years and three months, but in any case you have the basis to attain all of this. In other 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 258 – week one to seven  words, this refers to a need to engage into a dharma practice that abandons the four 
perverted aspects. We already talked about this a lot, and also this is in one of our 
weekly questions for discussion. You should check this out.  
 
The most excellent basis for attaining dharma is right this precious human rebirth. 
This is exactly what we hav e attained. If someone would try to find a better basis 
somewhere else, he wouldn‟t find anything. We also have the most outstanding place 
to do our studies. In fact, we have the best place I nthe world with good weather, a 
harmonious community and good fo od! Maybe in other worlds you could find better 
places, but in this world it is the best place!  
 This is the situation we have. We have gained this perfect place for dharma 
practice and we have every ideal condition. Even the assembly hall we have here, d ue 
to the kindness of Jean François, is very amazing. Due to him having done all this 
work, due to his kindness, we are now able to study here. This is something we should 
remember. This is what is referred to as recalling the kindness. We have this ideal 
situation for study and practice in this ideal place; therefore, we should use it exactly 
for this purpose. We should take this opportunity and engage into it. Having such 
wonderful conditions, and then doing other things or being here with an angry or 
jealous mind, using harsh words or maybe even making decisions like not talking to 
this or that person anymore, are all types of mind that have given in to the afflictions. 
And this comes down to wasting your opportunity here instead of practicing the 
dharma.  Therefore, while having this precious dharma place here, if you are  under the 
power of the afflictions, you will see all kinds of faults with it and finally wish to leave. 
If you end up with this kind of perception, then it comes from this faulty afflict ed 
mind. The faults seen here are not produced by the place or by the dharma, but by a 
mind with a wrong outlook.  
 The first door to enter into the dharma is this presentation about death and 
impermanence. This topic needs to be studied with a personal ou tlook with the 
thought of “I will die; this talks about me”. Impermanence has to do with the excellent 
things of this life and how they don‟t have any essence (this is the gross form of 
impermanence), but in this context we are talking about the impermanen ce which is 
our own death and impermanence. This counteracts our normal outlook of our 
existence as being permanent, which is a form of wrong consciousness. This outlook 
holds the impermanent as permanent.  
  
You can‟t escape the impermanence that is death.  It is right on your path, so you 
should accept it. You can‟t hide even by flying in to space or hiding in the earth, and 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
259 there is also no escape in between the two. This is expressed as that the three realms 
have no region whatsoever where one is free fro m death. Therefore, you should accept 
this fact, through familiarising yourself with it, and meditating on it.  
  
2B4B -2B1A -1A3 What kind of mind that is mindful of death is generated  
Third: The fear [born] from w orries about separation from relatives etc. is the kind of 
fear of death of those w ho are not trained in the path. Therefore, here this is not w hat 
is to be generated. Well then, w hat [kind of fear is to be generated]? All bodies taken 
under the pow er of karma and mental afflictions do not pass beyo nd death, w hich is 
w hy – even though this may give rise to fear – it cannot be prevented for the time 
being. How ever, w ithout having blocked the causes for bad migrations and 
accomplished the causes for high status and certain goodness, you w ill fear death . 
With regard to that, if you think of the resulting fear this can be accomplished, 
w hereby you w ill not be afraid on the verge of dying. If you do not achieve this 
objective, for fear of not being liberated from the cycle of existence in general and of 
falling to bad migrations in particular, you w ill be tormented by remorse at the time of 
death.  
 
In general, there is the statement that we should generate fear of death. This we could 
understand as a fear in the sense of being separated from all one‟s frie nds and 
relatives, all important things, and people and places. However, this is not the type of 
fear we are talking about. We are talking about a fear which is used in the dharma 
context, and this is very different from the one just described.  
The kind o f fear that should be generated in this dharma context: first of all one 
needs to be aware of one‟s age and think, “If I look at my life, I have wasted all these 
years by being distracted, and it is sure that I engaged into all kinds of negative 
behaviour to gain these distractions. On the one hand, I engaged into many 
negativities, and on the other hand I didn‟t engage in pure dharma practice. As a result, 
there are all kinds of causes that can lead me to the lower realms. Therefore, from now 
on I need to change my mind, knowing that death is certain, its time is uncertain and 
that at the time of death the only thing of benefit will be my dharma practice.”  
 
All bodies taken under the pow er of karma and mental afflictions do not pass beyond 
death, which is w hy – even though this may give rise to fear – it cannot be prevented 
for the time being.  
 
This means that you are someone who has taken on a body under the factor of karma 
and afflictions, and therefore you will have to die. Having fear of this will also not help 
you. Rather than the fear being the fear of death as such, which is unavoidable, this is 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 260 – week one to seven  referring to a fear of not being able to block out the causes for the lower realms 
through a pure practice of ethical discipline, and not having been able to accomplish 
the causes for a higher rebirth or definite goodness. In this way, you would generate a 
fear of what would happen if you die right now, and through this fear you make the 
immediate wish to block out the causes for a bad migration and bring about  the cause 
for higher rebirth through listening, reflecting and meditating on the dharma.  
 In short, if you don‟t bring about a good feeling for your own impermanence, 
then a pure dharma practice will not come about. Therefore, we need to counteract the 
outlook of our aggregates being something permanent and lasting. Even very old 
people have the outlook that clings to permanence that thinks they will live on. Right 
this outlook is what stops us from dharma practice, and this is what we need to 
counteract.  So we need to develop a certain deep fear of death right now and through 
by this engage into dharma practice. In this way, the reflection on death should have 
the effect of impelling us to practice dharma right now rather than a fear arising at the 
time o f death when there is no time left for dharma practice.  If someone doesn‟t 
reflect on his death, then there will not be this compelling force which sets him on 
dharma, and there might be a certain awareness of all the negative things that were 
done and the  lack of any positive imprints, which could bring a strong fear and regret. 
Unfortunately, it is too late to do anything about it as time has run out, just as when a 
predator puts his teeth into his prey and this victim is full of fear knowing there is 
nothing that can help him.  
So where will such a person find protection at that moment of great fear? 
Through having entered into dharma practice during life due to the correct type of 
fear; then at the time of death there will be no such fear. One will die, knowing that 
one turned away from negative behaviour, and even though there are lots of 
negativities from past lives, they have been purified. And through listening, reflecting 
and meditating according to one‟s capacity even a lot of positive imprints have  been 
stored in the mind. As a result, the whole situation is very positive, there is no fear of 
death and one will be protected. So what is protecting this person? All the dharma 
practice he did. Since this is the situation, it is hundred percent sure tha t a positive 
rebirth will come about.

reasons and the three resolutions. In this regard, the three [root thoughts] are: (1) it is 
certain that w e w ill die, (2) it is uncertain w hen w e will die, and (3) at the time of 
death nothing except for the Dharma w ill be of benefit.

The first one has three points: (1) the Lord of Dea th w ill certainly come and there are 
no conditions for w arding him off, (2) one’s life span cannot be increased and 
diminishes incessantly, and (3) you die w ithout having had time to practice Dharma

A The Lord of Death will certainly co me and there are no conditions for warding him off  
First: In the Udanavarga it says that whatever body you have taken, w hatever country 
you reside in, and w hatever the time – you are defeated by death. It w ill come to pass 
that w ay and cannot be turned bac k by fleeing sw iftly aw ay or by force, w ealth, 
material substances, mantras or medicine as it says in Advice to a King [Sutra]. 
Kamaw a said: “Now  some fear of death is necessary. At the moment of death you 
must not be apprehensive. We do it the other w ay r ound: no w e are not afraid [but] at 
the moment of death w e dig our fingernails into our chests.”  
 
Whatever body you have taken : here we should be aware that the buddha actually walked 
the planet at some point in a body like ours. Then he showed the aspect of parinirvana, 
meaning that the teacher is no longer with us in this body. So even here, this aspect of 
impermanence can be seen. In the same way, after the buddha there were many arhats 
who left their bodies and are no longer with us, and all the bodhisa ttvas of the past 
have passed away. And four hundred years after the buddha Nagarjuna, Aryadeva, 
Chandrakirti and others came but also all of them showed this aspect of death. This 
talks about India, but also in Tibet, six hundred years ago, Lama Tsongkhap a was 
present together with his spiritual sons but he is not here now. We still have his text, 
but we don‟t even have a photo of him. So even these outstanding holy beings passed 
away even though they had a very specific, special body. As this is true for these 
outstanding practitioners, then it is definitely true for us.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 262 – week one to seven   The quote from Kamawa: he is saying that we need to generate this fear for 
death in the present moment, and the effect of this is that at the time of death we will 
be without fear becaus e we will be without regrets. We do it the other way around; at 
the moment, we have no fear and then at the time of death we will have a lot of regret 
about all the negative things we did and held on to, but at that time it will be too late.  
Whatever coun try you reside in : this means that there is no place in the ocean where you 
can escape death or no space where to fly to or no tunnel you can drill into the 
mountain. There is no place to escape; death is everywhere, in every place.  
Where it says at the e nd you are defeated by death : if we go a hundred years into 
the past, all the people from a hundred years back are dead. All that is left is history. 
Also talking about Nalanda, which has existed for over twenty years, we can already 
look into its history.  When I came here there were four monks living here and now 
there are thirty ordained people with the hope of even more. So in the future, they will 
talk about how the Basic Program was the first step, and all this will talk about history. 
A hundred years from now someone will talk about the students who were in Nalanda. 
All of them will be dead and all that will be left are stories. In the same way, we talk 
about the people who were a hundred years before us. Just as it has happened to all the 
people of th e past, it will also happen to us. We might not even live to a hundred 
years.  
The context of all of this is that we try to cultivate a mind that is certain about 
our death. Death will come and there are no conditions repelling it. Therefore, we start 
in th is way, seeing that there are no conditions which can protect us. In your 
meditation you think about reasons for that, and that whatever type of body I have, 
even the body of a buddha, I have to die. It will definitely happen to me, there is no 
region wher e I could escape it, and it destroys life in any given time period. As my 
death is definite, I need to turn away from any activity which accumulates the causes 
for the lower realms. Instead, from now on I want to practice the dharma and practice 
ethical be haviour that leads me away from the lower realms.  
 
 
 
 
2B4B -2B1A -1A4A -1B One’s life span cannot be increased and diminishes incessantly  
Second: As it says in [the Sutra of] Entering the Womb, reaching a life span of a 
hundred years seems to be the limit of w hat is possible. How ever, even if you reach 
that, the years until then w aste aw ay w ith the passage of month after month, day 
after day , and day and night. As they too w aste aw ay w ith the passage of morning, 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
263 and so on, it seems that the general lifespan has [already] been w asted a lot previously 
and the remainder also diminishes incessantly w ithout anything being added.  
 
If you take grea t care and gather all the conducive conditions for good health, then 
you might live a hundred years, but this is the maximum. That someone might live 
longer is just a mere possibility. Even if we do manage to live for a hundred years, if I 
manage for examp le, then the first half of my life is already over. However, of course 
most of us will not be able to live all of one hundred years. Let‟s say we have another 
fifty years left; if one minute passes you no longer have fifty years. And so it goes on 
day afte r day, minute by minute. There is no refill! There is no way to get any life back. 
Shantideva expresses the same thought.  
 
In Engaging in [Bodhisattva] Behaviour it says:  
This life decreases constantly,  
Day and night, w ithout respite,  
If from now here there  is increase,  
Either, how  w ould I not die?     [4] 
Furthermore, you should consider this by way of numerous analogies such as a w eaver 
w eaving cloth, animals to be slaughtered approaching the place of slaughter w ith each 
step, cattle being lead w ithout any control to their place by a herder, and so forth.  
 
There is a constant decrease in our life span. If a day is over, this day has to be 
subtracted; the same with the night and the next morning. If your lifespan is constantly 
decreasing and there is no way to replenish it, then there is no doubt at some point it 
will be over and you will die.  
 The same thought is elucidated by some analogies here: A weaver weaves only 
one loom at a time but at some point he will have completed his work. Every step the 
animal  takes, takes it closer to the slaughter house. It isno different for us. The cattle 
being led by the shepherd is without any freedom, just as we have no freedom at all.  
 
 
 
This is also taught in [the Sutra of] Extensive Display by means of many analogies : 
Impermanent – autumn cloud -like: the three existences  
Birth and death of beings resemble a show

  Page | 264 – week one to seven  The passage of life of a being – a flash in the sky  
It passes fast like a w aterfall from a steep mountain.  [5] 
 
The waterfall from a steep mountain is an image expressing great force. In the same 
way, death and impermanence are a great force and we rush towards our death. To gain 
certainty about how our body and life are very impermanent, you have to employ many 
analogies. If you are skilled in this, then all impermanent phenomena around you 
become like teachers showing you impermanence. If someone does this, then on the 
basis of understanding this fact about his life, there will definitely come a strong mind 
towards  the dharma, a strong faith and a strong reliance. Such a person will enter into 
dharma without any doubt. What we are talking about here are facts, this is what is 
actually going on. Just to say, “Oh this time for dharma practice has not come about 
for me , I can‟t really do it so I just listen”; to say this is not of much benefit. We need 
to put all the advice we receive here into practice.  
 
For someone w ith the resolve of inner contemplation { 68}  there are no external objects 
that do not point out imperm anence to him. Since this is taught, certainty arises, if 
you consider it often and from many angles. There is no benefit in thinking one has 
[considered] something several times, but still no certainty arose. As Kamaba said: 
“You say nothing has come abou t through contemplation – w ell, w hen did you 
contemplate? During the day you indulge in distractions, at night you sleep – don’t 
lie! ”  
 
We can apply what Kamaba says to someone who says, “I am already a monk for so 
many years but no change has come about in my mind.” Here Kamaba answers, 
“During the day you were distracted, going for walks and so forth, at night you were 
asleep. So don‟t lie”  
 
QUESTION:  Isn‟t it that we can extend our life by doing sport?  
ANSWER:  However much sport you do, even if you do n othing but sport, you will 
not live more than a hundred years. If someone drinks a lot of coffee, eats a lot of 
chocolate, smokes and doesn‟t do any sport, even though such a person might have 
fifty years to go, due to his behaviour he might only live anot her twenty five years.  
QUESTION:  Are we already born with a certain lifespan?  
ANSWER:  In general, we could say that it is like this, but you might be able to extend 
it a few years through sport and such. For such a person we say that the lifespan has 
run out, but the merit has not run out. For another person the lifespan might not have 
run out, but his merits have. This means he will prematurely die due to certain adverse 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
265 conditions. When we say that it is not possible to increase the lifespan, then this 
expresses that we can‟t do much.  
 Of course, there are some tantric methods like relying on long life deities; 
For example relying on the practice of White Tara through an initiation. Through this 
practice, you might be able to change a lifespan that initi ally would only have only 
been twenty -five years, but you can‟t go much beyond that. Then in Kriya (Action) 
Tantra you have certain practices called “achievement of long life” where a long life is 
achieved . Here the idea is that, through a storng motivatio n to attain enlightenment, 
one creates the cause for a timeframe to do it as it will take a long time. However, 
these are only special cases. For us if we rely on White Tara, but however much we 
manage to increase our lifetime, in the end we will have to d ie. Through having such 
methods to dispel obstacles for living out our complete lifespan we can be happy. 
However, if you take such an initiation on the basis of a strong fear of death, and you 
think to deal with this fear by taking the initiation, then th is is a flawed motivation and 
it will probably not have much effect. But, if you take such an initiation into a long life 
deity with the understanding that you want to engage into pure dharma practice and 
gain a great mass of virtue, and for this you need time, then definitely it will have the 
effect of increasing your lifespan.   
 
TUES DAY 13 APRIL 2010  - SESSION 3 
QUESTION:  In Kriya Tantra, one could prolong the lifespan up to the point of 
enlightenment?  
ANSWER:  In general, there is talk about these methods of increasing one‟s life span 
with Kriya Tantra. But if you are asking if through just relying on these practices you 
will not die until you get enlightened, then this will not be the case. A newcomer to the 
dharma definitely wouldn‟t be able to do this, but there are indeed some beings who 
can practice in a way that they don‟t die until they attain enlightenment. There are 
stories about people who practiced these methods and lived an exceptionally long life. 
Nevertheless, they must still die because they are an impermanent phenomenon.  
 
QUESTION:  What is the difference between timely and untimely death? If you have a 
lifespan of seventy years but you die before that point, then surely you don‟t have this 
lifespa n? 
ANSWER:  Timely death means that you have a certain lifespan and you complete it. 
Untimely means that it is interrupted by outer adverse conditions like an earthquake, a 
fire, etc. So if someone doesn‟t encounter adverse conditions, then he will live his  
complete lifespan.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 266 – week one to seven   Lets say a person was born from a throwing karma which had the power of a 
lifespan of seventy years, but he encounters adverse conditions and dies at age twenty. 
Here we have to say that from the original force of the throwing karma f ifty years are 
left, and therefore his next life would have a lifespan of fifty years.  
 
QUESTION:  So we could remove the causes  for untimely death by some practices?  
ANSWER:  Yes, this is possible. In general, inner or outer obstacles can be addressed 
by ce rtain practices. However, if you engage into a meditation on your own death, and 
together with this generate an understanding of the law of karma, and come to regard 
this presentation as very precious, then because of this you will be able to engage into 
a pure practice and avoid negativities. This is actually the best method.  
 Shantideva said that bodhicitta is the king of knowledge mantras. This he said to 
show how powerful this mind of bodhicitta is. If you generate it, it will affect your 
whole being, and also your body. Therefore, now the time has come for us to engage 
into dharma practice. Meaning we should not engage into a single negativity, but only 
bring about perfect virtue. This means to engage as much as possible into any kind of 
virtue, and re frain from any type of negativity. This talks especially about refraining 
from negativity in our mind. There it starts.  
 There are many monks and even Gelongs  amongst us. So here there is even a 
physical change (note: due to the vows), but it is very impo rtant to have an actual 
change in the mind. This will protect you. Just to shave the head and wear some red 
clothing will not help you. The vows taken on through ordination need to be lived up 
to, and they need to be kept as much as one is able to. There i s always the possibility 
of some faults appearing in the ethical discipline of keeping all the vows because we 
are samsaric beings. Nevertheless, one would protect them as much as possible, and if 
there are occasions where a fault appears, then one would c lean it away through 
engaging into purification. This is how we should look at it.  
 Still, in any case a very fortunate situation has arisen because of the thought that 
one wants to become a monk. On this alone, it is most fortunate just for this wish to 
arise, but then taking the vows and trying to keep them according to one‟s capacity is 
even better.  
 
It is not just at the end of our lives w hen death overcomes us that w e proceed tow ards 
another w orld, for up until then there is not a moment where the lifespan does not 
diminish. Therefore, since w e entered the w omb w e have been going straight tow ards a 
future life w ithout remaining still even for a mere instant. In the meantime life also 
w astes away w ithout a moment of respite, as w e go tow ards death lead by its 
messengers, disease and aging. Therefore, do not rejoice in the view  that you abide 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
267 during you r life w ithout approaching future w orlds. It is like the analogy that when 
falling off a high mountain peak the time of falling through the sky before crashing to 
the ground is not w orthy of joy.  
 
Our lifespan is constantly getting shorter, whether we are  chatting with someone or 
joking around. So if one sits down for a coffee and feels that one enjoys oneself then 
what is really happening is that life is running out. This doesn‟t mean that having a 
coffee is harmful for you, but that while you are doing t his life is running out. You 
might say what a wonderful day you had hiking in the mountains or going to the ocean, 
but the fact is that this time you spent there is just gone. There is constant change on a 
momentary basis leading us closer to old age and d eath. You were a young person, but 
bit by bit you transformed into an old person, and it doesn‟t stop there, but goes right 
on to the moment of death. In the same way as someone falling out of a plane is 
rushing down to the ground, in the same way we are f ree-falling towards death from 
the moment we are born.  
 
Four Hundred Verses there is the quotation:  
Hero of men, in that night in the very beginning,  
Where someone comes to dw ell in a w omb in the w orld - 
Starting from that point he moves w ith each day  
Without the pause of a step tow ards the Lord of Death.  [6] 
 
The quote can be understood in a way that even a hero with great strength has been 
born from a womb and also is rushing towards death.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -1A4A -1C You die without having had time to practice Dharma while alive.  
Third: Even if you can live for so long, it is inappropriate to think you have time. As 
it is stated in [the Sutra on] Entering the Womb, initially, during the first ten years of 
childhood it does not occur to you to practice the Dharm a and at the end, during the 
20 years of old age you do not have the pow er to practice the Dharma. Of the time in 
betw een sleep snatches aw ay one half, illness and the like also w aste much time and 
no more than a few  opportunities remain to accomplish the Dharma.  
If someone is ninety years old, then in his seventies or eighties he won‟t have the 
capacity to practice the dharma.  
I will stop here for the day since I have been requested to give an oral 
transmission of Atisha‟s Lamp on the P ath.

  Page | 268 – week one to seven  WEDNESDAY  14 APRIL  2010  - SESSION 1 
ADVICE  
Yesterday we spoke about the first point of death that is impermanence - death is 
definite. This was in the context of the three roots:  
1) Death is certain.  
2) The time of death is not certain.  
3) At the time of death, nothi ng except the dharma is of benefit.  
 
The first root has three reasons:  
1) The Lord of Death is definitely coming and no conditions can keep him away.  
2) There is no way to add to our lifespan, but it is constantly decreasing.  
3) Even while we are alive, we have little opportunity to practice the Dharma.  
 
In regards to this first root, what we have left to talk about is the decision.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -1A4A -2 The decision to practice Dharma  
{ 69}  Since it is like that, at the time of d eath all the excellence of this life will only be 
an object of recollection – like the recollection of a happy experience in a dream after 
you have w oken. Why w ould you delight in being beguiled by this life, if the enemy 
w ho is death w ill certainly come? With this thought in mind you should take the 
decision that you definitely need to practice the Dharma and reiterate this innermost 
feeling many times. From the J ataka Stories:  
Alas, afflicted w orldly beings,  
Your stay is not stable, there is no joy,  
Even this w ater lily’s splendor  
Will soon be but a memory.     [7] 
 
There is an analogy here to do with whatever excellent things we own. We are asleep 
and having a wonderful dream, and in this dream we experience many wonderful outer 
conditions and generate mu ch joy in them. At the moment we wake up, all these good 
conditions are a mere memory. In the same way, all the good conditions and 
possessions we have now in the waking state will become a mere memory at the time 
of death.  
 This enemy, which is death, is  definitely onto us. Just as at the time of waking 
up, we cannot take any of the things in the dream with us, likewise at death, we cannot 
take any of life‟s good conditions with us. Nevertheless, to gain these good conditions 
in this life we engaged into  many negativities which will necessarily be carried with us 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
269 after death. Not one atom of our good circumstances or possessions can come with us, 
so why are you so attached and concerned with these outer conditions?  
 Think, “I will not fool myself with thi s deceptive mind which is only looking for 
outer conditions. Instead I‟m going to engage into the Dharma.” We should repeatedly  
establish this promise.  
 This is expressed in the Jataka Tales . Afflicted worldly beings  are those full of their 
afflictions an d full of suffering. Your stay is not stable means you have a lot of suffering, 
but you fool yourself by holding onto things that you enjoy. By being overly concerned 
with them, you create causes for suffering. You hold them as permanent and stable, 
but th ey are in the nature of suffering, cause suffering and are not stable. They cannot 
be taken with you.  
 Water lily  here is the Kumada flower, which is supposed to be extremely 
beautiful. It only flowers for one day, and in the evening dies. We can imagine there is 
a whole garden full of these flowers of splendid beauty. You experience this, but in the 
evening, they wither and die. The next day you recall the garden full of these beautiful 
flowers, but apart from this memory, there is nothing there – they ar e all gone. This is 
similar to the wonderful things of this life.  
 Just as this flower becomes a memory, so it is with all our outer conditions and 
objects that we use and surround ourselves with. If someone has great riches and is 
quite occupied with all  these, to them we say, “All these are very unstable, doesn‟t this 
worry you? Only a memory of these will remain. Isn‟t it that you are greatly fooling 
yourself with all these objects?”   
 This means that in the end we have a path that leads to more samsara . In the 
end, we have to encounter death and follow this one path into samsara.  
 We have no say in this process. Even the second moment of birth is aging, then 
comes sickness, and at the end of all this there is death.  
 By thinking in this way, one concl udes, “If this is the situation, then the only 
thing which is of benefit is to practice the Dharma. The rest I can put aside, so this is 
what I‟m going to do. I‟m going to put aside these deceptions.” This is the strong 
pledge we place in our minds.  
  
 2B4B-2B1A -1A4B  The time of one’s death is uncertain  
1 The need for generating a mental state mindful of death every day  
[2 The actual reasons  
3 The decision that from now on you need to practice the Dharma]  
 
2B4B -2B1A -1A4B -1 The need for generating a mental state mindful of death every day  
Second: It is certain that death w ill come betw een now and 100 years [from now ] and 
it is not certain on w hich day in betw een it w ill. Therefore, it is not definite on any 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 270 – week one to seven  day like today whether you w ill die or not. Nonetheless, the mind should side w ith the 
idea of death and you need to generate the awareness that thinks: “I w ill die today”, 
for if the mind sides w ith the idea of not dying, thinking: “I will not die today”, you 
constantly prepare for staying in this life and w ill not prepare for future lives. Due to 
this, if you get seized by the Lord of Death in the meantime, you w ill have to die in 
anguish. How ever, if you prepare to die every day, you w ill accomplish a lot that is 
meaningful for the future, so that , even if you do not die, it is good you have done it 
and if you die it w as all the more necessary.  
 
Here we remember that death will happen to us, and we should recall this every day.  
 If we think of a time period starting today and going 100 years into  the future, it 
is 100% sure you will be dead. Look at everyone in this room – without exception they 
will all be gone. Everyone will be something to talk about, but they will no longer be 
present. We don‟t have to ask anyone if this is the case, you could  put your signature 
to this. Within those 100 years, we cannot say which year death will come, which 
month, or week or day. The moment is unsure. Whilst it is sure we are going to die, it 
is not sure when we are going to die.  
 “This week I‟m not going to die.” Who can really say that? This is something 
that no one can say about themselves. No one has this freedom to make such a 
statement.  
 Therefore, one should think that it is possible, we can die this week, today even, 
or maybe at the weekend. It is pos sible I will die like this. Establishing this fact in your 
mind, one should really engage into Dharma practice right now. On the other hand, if  
we have the outlook, “I‟m not going to die today, this week, this year or in the next 20 
years etc,.” we will n ot engage into Dharma practice.  
If in this remaining lifespan, we come under distractions of laziness and so on, 
we‟ll end up in great distress.  
 On the other hand, if you have the outlook that you will die today, this will 
enable you to practice the dha rma and use your time with urgency. You will engage, 
listen, reflect and cultivate the Dharma. You would change your conduct of body and 
speech, work with your mind etc. Even if you don‟t die today, but live for many years 
with this attitude, you‟ll still gain great benefit from this process. If you live for many 
more years, you can just imagine what a great amount of virtue you‟ll be able to 
accumulate in this time. Death will come of course, but at that time, you‟ll have no 
regrets and die with a comforta ble mind.
