271 2B4B -2B1A -1A4B -2 The actual reasons  
A There is no certainty with regard to a life -span in Jambudvipa which is why the time 
of death is uncertain  
B There are many conditions that lead to death and few that promote life  
C The body is extremely v ulnerable and the time of death therefore uncertain  
This has three points: there is no certainty with regard to a life span in J ambudvipa, 
w hich is w hy the time of death is uncertain, there are many conditions that lead to 
death and few that promote life and the body is extremely vulnerable and the time of 
death therefore uncertain.  
 
Jambudvipa , we can say means our planet. The lifespan of humans that live here in 
this region is especially indefinite. There are other planets (regions) where there is a 
definite lifespan, but for us, it is indefinite.  
 For us there are many causes for death, and only a few causes supportive of life.  
Our body is not something solid, but quite the opposite. It is weak and feeble, and so 
when we are going to die is uncertain. This was the three sub -headings for this root.  
 
A There is no certainty with regard to a life -span in Jambudvipa which is why the time 
of death is uncertain  
First, in general a lifespan of the beings of Uttarakuru is set, and for most beings of 
the other [continents] there is more certainty, even though it may not be sure that the 
full extent of the lifespan of each individual be reached. For the beings of J ambudvipa 
a life-span is extremely uncertain. In the beginning they reached even immeasurable 
years, { 70}  later ten years w ill be the maximum duration to be reckoned w ith and even 
now adays it does not seem certain w hether they die in their old age, in their youth or 
in betw een. In the Treasury  it says:  
Here it’s uncertain: in the end:  
ten years – initially: infinite.    [8] 
 
You should take to heart the fact that people like your gurus or friends have died 
through sudden internal or external conditions for death before reaching the end of 
their lives, and you should think over and over again: “I too have  similar qualities.”  
 
Here there is reference to another region (sometimes called continent) Uttarakuru, for 
the beings there the lifespan is set.  
 This refers to stories about thousands and thousands of years ago when humans 
had a lifespan of immeasurabl e length. This is no longer the case.  
 We know of cases where humans have died at the age of 10, and even cases of 
children dying before their grandparents. There are such cases where young family 
members die before the elder members.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 272 – week one to seven   This is supported by a quote from the Treasury  (Abhidharmakosha ). In the past, 
there was such an  immeasurable life span, but that has been constantly decreasing. 
Now it is 100 years, and in the future, it is said to be only 10 years.  
 In my own experience, I was once in Co lombia and a student of Lama Yeshe 
had set up an association for those with cancer. Every day I went and gave Dharma 
advice. I went again a year later to this association, and everyone there was new, all the 
previous people had died. This is an example of how uncertain our lifespan is.  
 Amongst us, we have three types of people. We have some who are very young, 
some who are middle aged, and some older. Nonetheless, we cannot say who is going 
to go first, this very fact is definite! Who will be the first to get a visa to the other side, 
we don‟t know yet, but we will all do the same journey.  
 One thinks how ones lama has already died, ones friends have already died, that 
people younger than you have already died. So when is it my turn?  
 When you think in thi s way, there is no point in being terrified. Quite the 
opposite, we should take on death, and integrate it. By seeing this as a reality, we can 
practice Dharma and use life well. This is the benefit of engaging into this topic. There 
is no benefit in tryin g to forget about this topic, trying to avoid remembering death. 
We should voluntarily take it on and reflect on it.  
   
B There are many conditions that lead to death and few that promote life  
 
This reason refers to that even small things can bring about o ur death.  
Second: There are various harmful influences – animate as w ell as inanimate – on 
this life, so you should thoroughly consider how it is threatened by human beings and 
non-human malevolent influences, how  types of animals endanger one’s body and life 
through manifold harm, and likew ise how  both internal illnesses and external elements 
cause harm. Furthermore, one’s body is necessarily formed from the four elements and 
they also harm each other if their domains expand or diminish out of balance, th ereby 
causing illnesses and ravishing one’s life. Since they exist simultaneous w ith oneself, 
even though body and life appear to be stable they cannot be relied on.  
 
Harm can come from sentient beings and from things which are not alive, but still they 
harm our life.  
 There are many ways one can be attacked by humans and non -humans. Animals 
can also bring about harm to our bodies and life. These are the different animate 
sources of harm.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
273  Apart from the living sources of harm, inside us we have so many t ypes and 
classifications of sickness. Harm is also possible from the outside elements, such as 
earthquakes (earth element), tsunami and floods etc. (water element).  
 This body arises from the four elements. Due to their changing, increasing or 
decreasing, an imbalance is created, and in this way life obstacles are created.  
 While these four elements have arisen at the same moment as our life and body, 
they are actually capable of destroying our life and body.  
  
In the Parinirvana  [Sutra] it says:  
The discrimination of death is that this life, constantly 
surrounded by hostile enemies, w eakens in each moment and 
that there is nothing that increases it.    [9] 
 
Here one generates the outlook that one is surrounded by all the life threatening 
causes, and in  all this there are no friendly causes which increase life.  
 
And in the Precious Garland : 
We live amidst the conditions for Yama,  
Like a butter lamp in a gale.     [10] 
 
This means we are surrounded and under attack by many factors which can kill us. 
There is such an onslaught of these that our situation is like the flame of a butter lamp 
in a strong storm. In the middle of strong violent winds, we have this feeble flame. 
Will it stay lit? Similarly, we are dwelling in amongst these many causes for death.  
 Factors which normally support life, they are also causes for death. For example, 
these great supermarkets are full of many foods and drinks. These seem to be 
wonderful and are there to nourish us. However, if we check out what is in them, they 
all contain some substances that are harmful. If we examine every item in the store, we 
wouldn‟t find a single item which is not harmful. For example, if we find something 
has a lo t of sugar, we know that by consuming a lot one can get diabetes.  
 When you really look for a food that does not harm you, then normally it‟s not 
delicious. For example, someone bought some sort square cheese for me, but it has no 
taste. This is supposed t o be good for my health, but I have to put some honey on 
order to add some taste!  
 I travel on many trains and planes, and because these journeys take time, I 
usually drink something, usually Coca -Cola Lite. For a long time I‟ve been drinking 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 274 – week one to seven  this. Then s omeone pointed out to me what harmful substances there are in it, so now 
I drink nothing!  
 All these things in the supermarket that are supposed to sustain you, are actually 
quite harmful. This is why the analogy of a flame in a violent storm is used, bec ause of 
the great flood of things that can harm you, even if they are supposed to sustain you.  
 Another example, in Barcelona people brought me to three different doctors 
due to cholesterol problems. All three doctors said that I must be heavily into drin king 
alcohol. I have not had any in my whole life! When I was very young I drank some 
chang  [beer] but I‟ve had nothing in the last 30 years. Nevertheless, I have the problems 
of someone who has drunk a lot of alcohol. In the end, the conclusion was it com es 
from all the dried fruit I eat. Therefore, even dried fruit is harmful as it somehow 
produces alcohol in the body. This is normally described as healthy food!  
 Our life is like a flame in the wind, because from all sides there are things that 
can harm l ife. One doctor explained to me that if you are above 40 you really have to 
check what you eat. So the young amongst us, you have some benefit!  
   
Besides life itself permeates the reality of death so that many conditions for life are 
unreliable: From the  Precious Garland : 
There are many conditions of death  
Those for living are only few  
Even they may w ell be deadly  
Therefore,  alw ays practice dharma.    [11] 
 
Even things which present themselves as life supporting cannot be trusted.  
 Different medicines ar e also said to be good for the body, and normally we 
would trust this. However, if it is used wrongly, it can be of great harm to the body.  
 This precious human rebirth with freedoms and endowments is something very 
precious. One decides to treat it with great care, and not harm it with bad food and 
drinks. In addition, one engages into things like exercise that are good for the body.  
This is expressed in the quote from Nagarjuna, that there are many conditions 
that bring about death, and even those thing s that are supposed to sustain life can kill 
us. There are very few things that sustain life, and understanding this we make the 
strong decision to only practice Dharma.

275 WEDNESDAY 14 APRIL 2010  - SESSION 2 
  
C The body is extremely vulnerable and the tim e of death therefore uncertain  
{ 71}  The body is as vulnerable as a w ater bubble -  therefore this and that such as a 
thorn piercing it may be to blame and occasion the destruction of life w ithout the need 
for major harm. Thus it is very easily overcome by conditions of death. In the Friendly 
Letter  it says:  
If physical things – the ground, Mount Meru, and oceans - 
Are burnt up by the seven blazing suns  
And even ashes are not left behindxxxv  
What need to mention exceedingly feeble humans?    [12]  
 
The body is by no means stable. Maybe it‟s only something quite small that will kill us. 
From something insignificant we can die.  
 We can relate to this by thinking, “Yesterday this person was well, but today 
they are dead.” From being in a completely hea lthy state, death can come about 
through the smallest of factors.  
 For our life to be destroyed, the smallest cause is sufficient. It doesn‟t need a 
big catastrophe like an earthquake where even mountains are destroyed or there is a 
fissure in the earth w here land falls into it. We don‟t need something big like this, it 
can be something really small.  
 This quote describes the end of the world, and utter destruction on a cosmic 
level. Even the oceans and mountains which have been there for eons are complete ly 
destroyed, where not even ashes are left. Compared to this, the human body is far 
more fragile, short -lived, and much easier to destroy.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -1A4B -3 The decision that it is necessary to practice Dharma from now on  
At the end of thoughts like thes e you should reiterate many times the innermost 
decision to practice Dharma from this very moment, for it is uncertain w hen the body 
and life w ill be overcome by the Lord of Death and it should not be assumed that 
there is still time. As Shri J agatamitra s aid: 
Earth lord as long as this borrow ed body  
Abides in pleasure – not sick not decayed,  
At that time do take its essence  
Not fearing illness, death, decay.  
In times of illness, old age, and decay  
You may be concerned, but to w hat avail?   [13] 
 
This is the  second decision. One is aware that the time of death is uncertain; it can 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 276 – week one to seven  come at any moment. There is no time where we can just hang out, and we make the 
decision that we need to engage into Dharma practice from now on.  Then there is a 
quote from Shri Ja gatamitra. Earth lord  means how our body arises from the four 
elements. There is a certain ritual you do before you build a house, especially in regard 
to the earth that you build on. You address those who are of a non -human type who 
might live there, thei r leader in particular, and ask him to move so there are no 
interferences from non -human beings. This is taken as an analogy in that our body has 
also arisen from the four elements and is just borrowed from these elements.  
At a time where one is not sick , not decayed  and such, is the time when we should 
take the essence from the freedoms and endowments. In this time, we can practice 
without fearing sickness and decay. Later, when you might be sick or reach old age, you 
may be concerned  means that in this s tate, you remember Dharma practice, but to what 
avail , you will not be able to practice. When you are not in these states is the time to 
practice, because when sickness and old age come you will be unable to practice.  
 
Among the three root [thought]s the one about the uncertainty of the time of death is 
most important, w hich is w hy you should make an effort.  
 
Of the three roots, this second one is the most important. One meditates on it by 
cultivating the three reasons.  
1) On our continent the life span is uncertain. This is to be understood in the sense 
that there is no definite lifespan. Reflect on humans dying in the womb, at birth, 
within a month of birth, or a year. Reflect on all these different early deaths as well as 
those who die old. It is entir ely uncertain when death comes in our region.  
2) Even the things that are supposed to sustain you can become a cause for death or 
lead us to a quicker  death. We used many analogies here, as well as using me as an 
analogy.  3) How our body is very feeble, no t stable. Even a small factor can easily kill 
us.  
 Reflecting on these three reasons, we decided to only engage into Dharma 
practice, and engage from right now onwards. This was further elucidated by the 
moving and important statement from Shri Jagatamitr a, who likened us to the Earth 
Lord – the ritual where the earth lord arises from the earth - likewise our body also 
arises from the four elements. Currently it is without sickness, decay or old age and is 
still very capable. This is the moment when we sho uld engage into Dharma practice. 
When we reach our old age or sickness, remembering Dharma practice at that time is 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
277 of no benefit. In this way, we need to take the essence of the freedoms and 
endowments.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -1A4C  At the time of death nothing is of benefit except for the Dharma  
1 The actual reasons  
2 Decision  
 
In this context there are three reasons.  
   
2B4B -2B1A -1A4C -1 The actual reasons  
A [You cannot take along friends]  
Third: Thus w hen you see you have to go to another w orld, leaving this w orld, even if 
at that time you are surrounded by loving and grieving friends or relatives, you cannot 
take even one of them along.  
 
One has already understood that one has to necessarily go to the next world via death. 
Whilst this is the case, in this journey to  the next world, none of your friends or 
relatives can be of help or come with you. You might die with these close ones 
surrounding you, and they might be tormented by your condition, but none will go 
with you.  
 
B [You cannot take along possessions]  
Whate ver heaps of attractive riches you have obtained – you cannot take along even a 
small particle of it.  
 
Whatever wealth or beautiful objects you possess, you cannot take even an atom of 
these with you.   
 
C [You cannot take along your body]  
If you have to give up even the flesh and bones you w ere born w ith, w hat need to talk 
of anything else?  
 
You will even need to leave your flesh and bones behind. If this is the case, then there 
is no need to mention the other things we will have to leave behind.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -1A4C -2 The decision to practice nothing but Dharma  
 
The point here is that whatever group you belong to, as well as your friends or 
relatives will all need to be left behind, and none can come with you and be of any 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 278 – week one to seven  support on this path. All the poss essions you own cannot help you at the time of 
death. Even if you have a very handsome body, it is of no support. Then the question 
arises, is there anything then that is of benefit at the time of death? The only thing that 
can support you is the Dharma pr actice you have done in your life. At this time, there 
is no friend other than the Dharma you have engaged in during your life.  
 
 Someone might have been able to bring together the three marvels:  
1) Marvellous entourage (circle of friends)  
2) Marvellous o bjects of utilisation  
3) Marvellous body  
This is mentioned in the traditional Tibetan greeting Trashi Delek Pün Sum Tsog . 
Trashi Delek means auspiciousness, everything excellent and happy. Sum is three, Pün 
means marvels and Tsog means made to come togethe r. Pün Sum Tsog  then means the 
assembly of the three marvels , the same marvels mentioned above. This is exactly what we 
need to leave behind at the time of death, none of them can help at the time of death. 
Even if we have these, at the time of death there  is nothing to say to them except, “be 
comfortable and goodbye”. They also cannot say anything to you except, “be 
comfortable and goodbye”. The text expresses this by saying that the three marvels 
will abandon you, and you become completely separated from them as you continue on 
your path.  
  
 A The actual [decision]  
Then you think: “At that time, it will certainly come to pass that all the excellences of 
this life leave me, just as I leave them, and I w ill proceed to another w orld. It will 
happen today.” { 72}  You should make the decision many times to practice Dharma 
w ithout submitting to the conditions like the body, friends and possessions.   
 
Reflect on this separation. No one can say it is not going to happen this afternoon or 
tonight. It can happen exactly this afternoon or tonight, this is the point in saying the  
time of death is uncertain.  
 By generating this mind, that it can happen today, what benefits you at the 
moment of death is the Dharma practice you have done. When the three marvels are 
abandoning you and you are abandoning them, Dharma practice becomes your 
bodyguard and protects you.  
 Lama Tsongkhapa encourages us to not come under the power of friends, body 
or the objects of utilisation (things we run after), but to decide to focus on Dharma 
practice.  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
279 B The need to try hard since it is the foundation  of the path  
Although this arises w ith difficulty, you should try hard, as it is the foundation of the 
path. In the w ords of Potow a:  
These are my meditations on impermanence, appearances 
and [their] elimination. Eliminating all the phenomena of 
this life  – close relationships, possessions and the like – and 
aware that I w ill go here and there to another life alone 
w ithout anything, I think: “there is nothing to do other than 
to practice the Dharma”, and non -attachment to this life first 
arises. As long as  this [attitude] has not arisen in my mind, I 
w ill sit w ith the paths to all the Dharma blocked.  
 
This quote is stunning. The first line refers to all the inner problems that are cleared 
away by meditating on this impermanence. These inner problems are of three types:  
1) problems connected with ones friends and companions  
2) problems connected with objects of utilisation  
3) problems connected with one‟s body  
All these three are cleared away by the meditation on impermanence. He 
describes it as clearing away  the appearance of these inner problems. An understanding 
then arises that one (himself in this case) has to go to the next life alone without 
having another person with him. It is a lonely journey to another life. He comes to 
think, “I will not do anythin g but Dharma practice.” This mind, if it arises, is 
something very powerful. Initially, the attachment to this life is destroyed. Indicating 
these stages, he then points out that as long as this mind has not arisen in one‟s own 
continuum, all the paths of the different dharma practices are blocked .  
 
And Dolpa said:  
Incidentally, if you accumulate the collections and purify 
obscurations, make request to the deities and [your] guru and 
contemplate insistently w ith perseverance, even that w hich 
you think w il l not come about w ithin 100 years w ill come 
about effortlessly, for compounded phenomena do not remain 
static as they are.  
 
In addition to this other meditation, you engage into accumulating virtue and purifying 
obscurations. You make requests to your medi tational deity and your guru, by 
employing a mind that is enthusiastically insisting on the result. Then you engage into 
a sincere reflection on this topic. If this comes about, then things that appear difficult, 
even things that we think in a hundred year s we could not generate, will come about 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 280 – week one to seven  effortlessly if we practice in this way. This is because all compounded phenomena are 
unstable and can change.  
 
When someone asked Kamaw a about moving on to another object [of meditation] he 
said: “repeat the previ ous one!” Asked about the remaining ones, he said: “There is 
nothing beyond it.”  
 
Someone asks Kamawa about changing the meditation object. He says you should stay 
on the meditation topic you were on previously, and to repeatedly go over the same 
topic. Th en someone asks how much of a break they should have? He said you should 
not break, but you should meditate continuously. Then someone says but when should 
we stop? The answer is you meditate until there is a mind that is not in any way 
attached to the hap piness of this life.  
What would this mind be like? There is an analogy of someone who is taken to 
their own execution in front of a large crowd. As he‟s being led there, many ornaments 
like golden chains, armlets, bracelets and so on are placed on him. Ho wever, since he 
is going to his execution, he‟s completely uninterested in these things. The mind in this 
situation has complete disinterest with things that are normally considered marvels.  
Similarly, this is the mind one would have upon having realised this topic.  
 
C How to carry out the practice of all the excellent scriptures [of the Buddha]  
Thus, if you know  how to practice reliance on a spiritual teacher, leisure and 
endow ments as w ell as impermanence in this and that context of the excellent 
scriptures and their commentaries, and sustain them, you will easily find the intention 
of the J ina. In this w ay, you w ill also become aw are of them in other contexts.  
 
This refers to all the points that have been explained up to now. The way of relying on  
the spiritual friend, the freedoms and endowments and here - about impermanence. 
They have all been explained by using the teachings of the Buddha and the 
commentaries, and these need to be thought about again and again.

281 EXPLANATION OF WHAT W ILL BECOME O F YOU IN FUTURE WORL DS: 
HOW TO THINK ABOUT TH E HAPPINESS AND SUFF ERING OF THE TWO [TYPES OF ] 
MIGRATING BEINGS  
 
So far, we have talked about that there will be death, now we talk about what happens 
after death.  
As death is certain, there is no way to stay in this life. It is sure we are going to 
die. Whilst this is the case, at death you don‟t go into non -existence, but you have to 
take on another life. There are only two paths you can follow, either a path to a happy 
migration or a path to a unhappy migrat ion. This happens entirely under the power of 
karma, in other words, by another factor over which you have no control.  
 You will arise in this other life in accordance with how the white or black karma 
projects you, meaning the throwing karma. Here we hav e to think about the 12 links of 
dependent arising. The first link is ignorance and on this basis the second link of 
karma is produced. The karma that you create here will become the propelling karma, 
and if it is activated, it propels you into the respect ive life. In this way, where you are 
born is entirely dependent on this propelling karma.  
 These two migrations refer to the good and the bad migrations, and here we 
reflect on the suffering in connection with the bad migrations.  
 We said that because on e is afraid of death, one decides to practice Dharma. 
This decision comes about due to this danger of suffering after death.  
 When we think of the state of taking a rebirth in hell, as a preta or an animal, 
their suffering is clear. Even if we think about the human realm, there is suffering. As 
we do not want any of this, we make the decision to practice the Dharma. We can say 
this is the very aim of Dharma practice.  
 In general, this is what Dharma is about. We do not want to experience 
suffering - no one  wants this. What is unwished for is suffering, and what is wished for 
is happiness, and we practice the dharma in order to achieve this.  
 
 
WEDNESDAY 14 APRIL 2010  - SESSION 3 
First, one thinks, “If I‟m reborn in the lower realms, what would I experience there?” 
Then one reflects on the sufferings of the lower realms. There is benefit in doing this: 
1) you generate compassion for those who are in the lower realms and  
2) you generate a mind of definite emergence.

2B4B -2B1A -1B1 Thinking about what will become of you in future worlds  
Second: That being so, since it is certain that you w ill die soon, { 73}  you have no time 
to spend in this life and, after death, it is not that you become non -existent; you must 
take rebirth. Since apart from the tw o there is no other place of birth, you w ill be born 
in either the happy migrations or the bad migrations. In addition, since you are not 
under your ow n control but under the control of other - karma, you w ill be reborn 
according to the w holesome and unw holesome karma that impels you.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -1B2 From among the happiness and suffering of the two migrations, considerations about 
the suffering  
A The need to think about suffering  
[B How thinking about suffering is a vital point  
C How to think about suffering]  
 
2B4B -2B1A -1B2A  The need to think about suffering  
You should think about the suffering of the bad migrations considering: What w ill 
become of me, if I am reborn in the bad migrations? Nagarjuna said:  
Every day remember  the hells  
That are fiercely hot and cold.  
Also remember the hungry ghosts,  
Emaciated by hunger and thirst.  
Look at animals and remember  
[How  they’re]  beset w ith the sufferings of dullness.  
Give up their causes, use causes for happiness.  
Once you have gai ned w hat is hard to gain,  
A human body in J ambudvipa,  
Endeavour to cut the cause of bad realms.   [14] 
 
The last part of Nagarjuna‟s quote refers to the special situation we have due to this 
human body, which gives us the ability to practice. By thinking of  the suffering of the 
lower realms, we can use this special situation to abandon the causes of going to a 
lower rebirth.  
 In order to generate this urgency to practice the Dharma, one reflects on the 
general sufferings of samsara, in particular the three l ower realms. This strongly 
encourages one to engage into Dharma practice.  
 If you reflect on this suffering, you generate a mind that wants to abandon it 
and its causes. In addition, arrogance is eliminated.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
283  By understanding how suffering is the result of non -virtue, one becomes very 
capable of avoiding all transgressions and downfalls.  
 Our situation is that we are solely interested in wanting happiness and not 
wanting suffering, and as happiness comes from virtue, joy arises that wants to engage 
into virtue.  
 Having assessed your own experience, one is able to generate compassion for 
others who have similar experiences. By coming to see only faults in samsara, 
becoming disillusioned with it, one generates a mind that aims at liberation. We see the 
faults of samsara, and the benefits of liberation, and so one turns away from samsara 
and turns toward liberation.  
 A certain fear is generated through understanding this whole situation. This fear 
of suffering leads to a pure form of going for refuge that is  firm, strong and sincere.  
Reflecting on suffering then brings about many essential points of practice.  
   
2B4B -2B1A -1B2B  How thinking about suffering is a vital point  
Meditation about the sufferings of cyclic existence in general and in the three bad 
migrations in particular is a very vital point, for if you thus consider how you yourself 
fall into the ocean of suffering, despair will repel pride and arrogance. Having seen 
that suffering is a result of non -virtue, you w ill be very careful to avoid negati vities 
and dow nfalls. As you do not want suffering but happiness, once you realize that it is 
the result of virtue, you w ill also enjoy accomplishing virtue. In proportion to your ow n 
experience compassion for others arises and out of aversion to cyclic ex istence you strive 
for liberation. For fear of suffering, you take refuge forcefully. These positive qualities 
of suffering comprising many important points of practice { 74}  are stated in Engaging 
in [Bodhisattva] Behaviour  in terms of suffering [already] arisen in one’s ow n 
continuum, how ever, they are equal[ly applicable] to suffering one is yet to experience.  
 
We should be clear that reflecting on suffering has benefits, it‟s not that suffering has 
benefits, but the refl ection has benefits. This is also expressed in the 
Bodhisattvacharyavatara  where it mentions reflecting on suffering that has already 
happened, but we also get benefit from reflecting on our potential suffering of the 
future.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 284 – week one to seven  2B4B -2B1A -1B2C  How to think a bout suffering  
1 The actual [considerations concerning the suffering in bad migrations]  
2 How it is necessary to meditate on this, until the experience is being produced  
2B4B -2B1A -1B2C -1 The actual [considerations concerning the suffering in bad migrations]  
A Thinking about the sufferings of hell beings  
B Thinking about the sufferings of animals  
C Thinking about the sufferings of hungry ghosts  
 
Thus, the considerations about the suffering of the bad migrations have three points: 
thinking about the  sufferings of hell beings, animals, and hungry ghosts.  
 
The point is that we have to reflect on suffering, and in particular, the suffering of the 
hell realm, preta realm and animal realm. When we talk about the hell realm, this is 
presented by talking ab out the great hells, neighbouring hells, cold hells and finally the 
occasional hells.  
 In dependence on the strength of a sentient being‟s negative karma, these 
respective sufferings come about.  
 When we talk about hells, it‟s not like you can go there a nd take a photo. We 
can easily see humans undergoing hellish sufferings through famine and disease. In the 
same way, the Hungry Ghosts endure sufferings of hunger and thirst. There is strong 
suffering from hunger and thirst in African and even in India. In  Nepal there is 
something like this and in China – there are many places where this type of suffering is 
experienced.  
 In South America, there are many people living in poor conditions without food 
and drink, which are similar to the sufferings of Hungry Ghosts. It‟s not to say they 
are Hungry Ghosts, but that they undergo suffering similar to Hungry Ghosts. All 
these experiences come from the ripening of karma. A creator God has not made it for 
example. Why would a creator God create this suffering? If th at is the case, one would 
regard Him as an evil creator God and He would be suitable to be abandoned. But this 
is not the case, it depends on the ripening of one‟s own respective karma.  
 The hell beings and pretas are also described as having an incredibl y long life.  
 When we think of the suffering in the animal realm, it is clear there are many 
forms. For example, a stronger animal will kill another by eating it alive. Animals also 
get sick sometimes, but this is nothing compared to the suffering of bein g eaten alive. 
In the oceans, bigger animals eat the smaller ones. In the mountains, predators eat 
other animals alive. In the realm of birds, there are bigger birds that eat the smaller 
ones. Some insects eat other insects, so the whole realm is packed wi th animals eating 
each other alive.  
 By reflecting on this suffering, we need to understand there is no other way to 
explain this suffering apart from the result of actions, karma. These sufferings are as a 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
285 result of negative actions, and as we don‟t want  to experience these sufferings,  we 
need a path that does not create karma that produces this suffering. Once we check, 
we can clearly see that we have collected karma for all these experiences. This being 
the case, we definitely need to engage into Dharm a practice to eliminate the causes for 
these sufferings.  
 One needs to continuous engage in methods that decreases all this negative 
karma. To get rid of it completely is very difficult, but to decrease some is possible 
with purification practices. One al so engages into practice to make sure that negative 
minds like anger, jealously and arrogance do not arise. These minds are very 
destructive, so one especially needs to guard the mind from them in order to prevent 
oneself accumulating new negative karma. T his is also very difficult, but by engaging 
into a practice that wants to work with these, we can weaken them on a daily basis. In 
this way, it is possible to guard the mind and prevent engagement into negative 
conduct.   
 One would also make wishing praye rs that in the future one is completely 
liberated from all this negative karma (actions) and the minds that create this karma. 
One is aiming at complete liberation from these causes of suffering.  
Not only that, one takes one‟s own experiences in this as a  basis to extend it to 
all other sentient beings. One makes prayers that all others may be free from suffering. 
Whatever virtue you have, you make prayers that they may be of benefit to other 
sentient beings, and that they may also abide in virtue.  
 One i s trying to practice in such a way that gives meaning to the freedoms and 
endowments, not just on a daily basis, but also in every hour.  Therefore, one 
repeatedly reflects on the sufferings of samsara in general, and specifically the 
suffering of the lower  realms.  
Something that is quite striking for me personally is thinking of the many people 
in a peaceful and happy country like France, who then commit suicide. They must have 
thought their life was unbearable, without help or support from anyone they cou ld 
turn to and in this way came to the decision that there is no other path than stopping 
life altogether. There must be an overwhelming form of suffering in these people. 
Their suffering must be even stronger than the animals.  
 By reflecting on all this s uffering, one then engages into Dharma practice.  
 If we engage into this kind of reflection on suffering, a strong wish will arise 
that wishes for some kind of protection. Then we‟ll engage into the practices of refuge, 
exploring the qualities of the thre e jewels for example. By understanding all the 
qualities of the three jewels, we generate a mind that is a fully qualified form of going 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 286 – week one to seven  for refuge. This mind has strong trust (faith) that is based on reasoning. It‟s not a 
blind faith. This reasoned or sen sible faith can come from this reflection on suffering.  
 Someone might say, “I have no wish to reflect on death or suffering. I‟m not 
going to go there; I want to avoid the subject.”  To think you should avoid this topic is 
a wrong attitude, and will only  turn out badly for you in the end. In fact, through not 
integrating this, you could even go crazy. There is no escape from this topic or hiding 
from these facts. You should voluntarily take on these topics and explore them. On 
this basis of reflecting on death and suffering of the lower realms, one can generate a 
strong understanding of karma and its effects and going for refuge – this is what can 
be gained. By this refuge, you will not end up being crazy, or feeling you are alone and 
cut-off. You will be protected from all these problems by reflecting on death and the 
suffering of the lower realms.

of all w ell-being : generating the confidence of firm conviction in actions and [their]

on them, the objects you go to for refuge , the way in w hich to go for refuge as much as 
one can, and the stages of training after having gone for refuge  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2A1 The causes that going for refuge depends on

over w here w e are going to be reborn controlled by [our] karma. Also, karma, 
according to Engaging in [Bodhisattva] Behaviour  is: 
As a flash at night amidst the cl ouds 
Illuminates the darkness for a moment,  
Likew ise on the road through the pow er of buddhas

In general there are many causes, but here talking about going for refuge based on

                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 288 – week one to seven  We are definitely going to die. In this life, we undergo many sufferings such of 
birth, sickness, aging and at th e end the suffering of death. Death is certain. After 
death, there is not non -existence, but we have to take rebirth, and there is danger of 
taking rebirth in the lower realms of utter suffering. We reflect on these sufferings by 
thinking, “Would I be able  to bear that suffering?” Then a certain fear arises at this 
possibility of ending up in the lower realms. This is one of the causes of going for 
refuge.  
  We can also analyse if we would be able to bear this suffering in the hells. For 
example, to test i f you would be able to bear the suffering of the hot hells you could 
boil some water, put your hand in, and check if you could bear it. This would be a 
mere part of the suffering that is going on in the hot hells, but you could use this to 
get a small idea  of how it would be. In the winter, you could go out into the snow 
naked and see how you would bear it. Alternatively, you could give up food and drink 
for several days and see how it feels, see if you could bear the suffering of the preta 
realm. You could  do something to your body like cut yourself or put your finger on the 
desk and hit it strongly with a hammer and see how it feels. Then compare this with 
the suffering of animals that are sliced up by humans or eaten alive, and see if it is a 
suffering yo u could endure.  
 
THURSDAY  15 APRIL  2010  - SESSION 1 
ADVICE  
QUESTION : When we talked yesterday about generating fear, we said it‟s not the fear of 
separating from friends etc, but the fear of not having acted purely in terms of 
Dharma. Couldn‟t this first type of fear be helpful, because you see they are not a 
proper refuge,  and so you take refuge in the Three Jewels?  
GEN: In general, such a fear may be helpful, but this fear brings thoughts like, “I will 
be separated from my house, my objects, my riches” – only thinking about “I”, “I”, 
“I”. This is not the thinking of a Dha rma practitioner. This is a fear with regard to a 
certain suffering right now.  
 When we die, we will not end in non -existence, but we will have to take a 
rebirth. We have no say in this; rather it is under the power of our karma and 
delusions, specifically  our karma. The only two ways to be reborn is in a happy or bad 
migration. If we take rebirth in a lower realm, would we be able to bear this suffering 
or not? Even if we take rebirth in an upper realm, there are still so many sufferings to 
endure. The ver y fact of taking on a life in samsara means you take on the five 
contaminated aggregates. As a human, you are in a happy migration, but you still have 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
289 the suffering of birth, aging, sickness and death – again because of the five 
contaminated aggregates. Th en a wish arises to be free from any type of contaminated 
aggregates. Reflecting on these general sufferings of samsara, and in particular the 
sufferings of the lower realms, generates a fear. From this, one engages into Dharma 
practice and aims for libera tion by taking refuge - to get rid of the five contaminate 
aggregates. These are the thoughts a Dharma practitioner produces to bring about fear.  
 One produces a fear regarding all the sufferings, this is the basic way of thinking 
of a Dharma practitioner . The first way you mentioned in your question was only with 
the suffering of being separated from your possessions, friends and constantly thinking 
about what is happening to you. A non -Dharma practitioner would be afraid in this 
way. On the other hand, a  Dharma practitioner generates fear on the basis of all 
sufferings, especially having to repeatedly take the five contaminated aggregates due to 
karma without any freedom. He wants to be free of this process.  
 Another way of producing fear and taking refug e is taking into account the 
sufferings of all sentient beings. Seeing how this is unbearable, one is terrified by the 
huge ocean of sufferings, and is only interested in protecting beings from this 
suffering. One then understands that the sole refuge for all sentient beings is the Three 
Jewels, and so one takes refuge in them to get rid of sufferings of all types. Of course, 
included here is one‟s own family and friends.  
 Fear generated from our own suffering becomes the root cause for going for 
refuge. F ear based on the sufferings of all beings becomes a root of compassion. 
 This is how one generates fear regarding the suffering that exists. For example, 
there has recently been a strong earthquake in Tibet. On the news they say in China, 
but this in the K ham region, there were many thousands affected and many hundreds 
dead. This is quite amazing because there is so much empty space and empty 
mountains in this area, but the quake occurred at the only big city in the entire region. 
This is an example of how things happen under the power of karma. This is how one 
can generate fear by reflecting on karma. It‟s really startling. There is so much empty 
space in Kham, but this is the only place where there is a large population of people, 
maybe a few tiny villages  here and there, but mostly empty space. Now the whole city 
is completely flat, not a thing left standing.  
It‟s also a basis for generating compassion because of all the suffering. Children 
were killed as schools collapsed; hospitals also collapsed killin g doctors and nurses. In 
the news, it said 600 dead but from what I hear on the phone it‟s more like 3000 dead.  
I‟m not sure how many people live in this city but it must be over 100,000.  
   
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 290 – week one to seven  The four points on refuge are:  
1 The causes that going for refu ge depends on  – these causes we ourselves have to 
bring together.  
2 Depending on them, the objects you go to for refuge  – These objects are already 
there, they already exist.  
3 The way in which to go for refuge as much as one can  – again this is somethin g that 
depends on oneself.  
4 The stages of training after having gone for refuge  – this refers to refuge vows and 
trainings, and again depends on oneself.  
This is how Dharma practice actually functions. The objects are already there, then it 
depends on one self if one brings together the causes or not, in this case the causes for 
refuge. If you have not brought together these causes in a good way, then going for 
refuge will not happen in a good way. Apart from saying, “I go for refuge” - there is no 
heartfelt  going for refuge, and so no transformation. Without this, then with regard to 
the trainings of going for refuge we‟ll say, “Oh yes, there are these trainings with 
regard to going for refuge in the Buddha, Dharma and Sangha, I know what they are,” 
but you won‟t be able to engage in them because there is no heartfelt refuge.  
  
2B4B -2B1A -2A1 The causes that going for refuge depends on  
First: Although, in general, there are many causes, here it is the fact that, as explained 
above, w e do not stay in this life, w e die soon and after death w e do not have control 
over w here w e are going to be reborn controlled by [our] karma. Also, karma, 
according to Engaging in [Bodhisattva] Behaviour  is: 
As a flash at night amidst the clouds  
Illuminates the darkness for a moment,  
Likew ise on the road through the pow er of buddhas  
A rare understanding of merit comes forth in the w orldly.  
Therefore, that w hi ch is virtuous is alw ays w eak  
While evil is extremely strong and unbearable.  [21] 
 
In general, there are many ways to go for refuge, but here we talk about what was 
previously explained, that you have a precious human rebirth, remembering 
impermanence and d eath and the possibility of being born in the lower realms. Then 
one takes to mind that one is not able to stay in this life, one is soon going to die.  
 One generates fear because after death we will have to take a rebirth under the 
control of this karma,  without having any choice.   
 This is shown in the quote from the Master Shantideva. It describes a moment 
in the night, which is already dark, and whatever light exists is obscured by thick 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
291 clouds. There is also a strong rain and so there is complete darkness. If we were going 
to Lavaur in this we wouldn‟t be able to see even the road. In this moment, for a 
second, there is a flash of lightning which eliminates everything, and you can see the 
way ahead.  
We are in a state of strong spiritual darkness, e nveloped by the afflictions that 
are the cause of our darkness. Within the afflictions, it is ignorance that completely 
envelopes any form of our wisdom. For a mere moment, through the power of the 
Buddhas there comes an understanding of merit. Now we have  an intelligence that is 
capable of practicing virtue. This means that right now we have attained this possibility 
– the attainment of the precious human rebirth with the freedoms and endowments – 
and in this life we are capable of practicing the dharma an d accumulating virtue. This 
moment of being capable is extremely short, like lightning. These few years of human 
life compared to the millions and millions of years we‟ve already gone through is like a 
flash. In this moment, we are able to see the path to virtue, due to the power of the 
Buddha, but this capable life is very short. Normally we don‟t have this opportunity, 
it‟s very rare that we can engage in virtue and so our virtue is always weak . On the 
other hand, the times when we engage in negativities are very frequent, and so our 
engagement in negativities is very strong because of their great number. It is unbearable  
because it brings all this suffering. When we say we should generate fear with regard to 
suffering, it is due to this very reason, that our virtue is very feeble but our negativity 
is very strong.   
 In the last line, evil is very strong , in Tibetan it says of great strength . This refers to 
that we have already engaged into many negativities from many lives. Great strength  
refers to that w e have accumulated numerous negativities, for a long time, and the 
individual acts were of great strength. On the other hand, we have not accumulated 
virtue in the same way, in that they have been very infrequent in comparison, very 
weak and over a very sh ort period of time. This is why virtue is described here as 
feeble, while the negativity is very strong and complete. The ripening of these cannot 
be anything other than suffering, and this strong negativity will bring a strong suffering 
result and therefo re will be unbearable . From this, one generates fear or terror. One is 
able to generate this fear because one wishes for happiness, and does not wish for 
suffering for oneself or others.   
 To generate this state of fear, you need to be able to reflect on k arma and its 
effects. You can understand that from very strong negativities, a strong suffering will 
ripen, and from feeble virtue not much positive result can arise. If you understand this, 
you generate a mind of being genuinely afraid, and a wish to find  a refuge will arise. 
Who will be able to protect you from the suffering that is about to happen to you? 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 292 – week one to seven  Apart from the Three Jewels – the Buddha, Dharma and Sangha - there is nothing to 
protect you. Then you see the Three Jewels as the only object of trus t, and your refuge 
is then very strong.  
 In the context of a small scope being, fear is generated by understanding you 
could be born in the lower realms and would undergo its unbearable suffering. Then 
this person turns to the Three Jewels as the only pro tection from these intense 
sufferings. With this fear of suffering in the lower realms and trust in the Three Jewels 
that can protect him, a small capacity being goes for refuge.  
 If this fear is generated from the depths of your heart, and you have trust  that 
the Three Jewels have the capacity to help you, then a proper refuge is generated, but 
if this fear and trust is not strong enough, the refuge will be fake. If we then count 
many refuge prayers on a mala, apart from wearing down your fingernails and the mala, 
there is not much effect. Whether refuge is strong and actually comes about is entirely 
depending on the mind.  
 
QUESTION : While faith is a virtuous metal factor, fear isn‟t. Because this fear is not a 
wrong consciousness, would it be a type of w isdom?  
GEN:  There are many types of fear; fear whilst knowing the situation, fear where there 
is doubt, or fear where one is completely ignorant. In our case, it is a fear that 
understands the actual situation. It is a wisdom in this context because it un derstands 
the actual facts. This is why it is not something to reject, but rather to generate.  
 One would generate forms of introspection and go over topics like; What is the 
nature of this fear? What is the cause of this fear? What is the effect? With su ffering, 
we do the same analysis, what is the nature, the cause and the effect? From this 
analysis, fear would be generated.  
 When this kind of fear is successfully produced, it will not bring you down, but 
it will actually generate courage and make one ac t. The same is true if you dive into the 
topic of understanding death, which is impermanence. If you reflect in the correct way 
and get a feeling for it, it will not lead to you becoming depressed, but quite the 
opposite, you‟ll get active and feel courage ous. When thinking about suffering, if you 
think about the nature, the cause and so on, you will not become depressed, but you‟ll 
become active and want to act on the problem.  
{ 84}  Thus, as you think about how you fall to bad migrations because the pow er of 
w holesome karma is small and that of unw holesome karma is excessively extremely 
strong, the tw o arise:  fear of bad migrations and the conviction that that w hich is 
rare and preciousxxxviii [the J ew els], can protect you from them. Consequently, if these 
tw o remain but [/mere] w ords, going for refuge w ill be similar. How ever, if they are 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
293 present intense and firm, going for refuge w ill also come to tame the mind. Therefore, 
you should strive at those tw o causes.

2B4B -2B1A -2A2A  Identifying the objects [o f refuge]  
First: In the Praise in One Hundred and Fifty Verses it says:  
To one in w hom perpetually  
All faults are definitely absent,  
To one in w hom in every manner,  
All good qualities abide,  
In case someone like that exists,  
It is fit to go for refuge,  
To praise him, to attend on him,  
To abide in w hat he teaches.     [22]

which read:  
If a mind exists, i f you are sensible,  
Go for refuge to just that one , 
To praise him, to attend  on him ,  
To abide in what he  teaches. 
There is no talk about if someone exists. It means if you are able to think, you go to 
refuge to the one who is perpetually absent of all faults, you attend him, praise him 
and so on.  
 
Thus, if someone has the intell igence to differentiate10 betw een w hat is a refuge and 
w hat is not, it is appropriate for him to go for refuge to the undeceiving protector, the 
Buddha Bhagavan. This also characterizes the Dharma J ew el and the Sangha J ew el.

Buddha, Dharma, Sangha are

                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 294 – week one to seven  GEN: Those of you who are new here, you might ask what it is that makes you a 
Buddhist? It is this going for refuge. By taking refuge in the Buddha, Dharma and 
Sangha, you become a buddhist.  
 Some of you have now become buddhist, but what is the measure of thi s? It is 
by having gone for refuge, you have gone for refuge in the Buddha, Dharma and 
Sangha. This is exactly described here in the Lam -Rim and there is no other way to go 
for refuge than how it is explained here.  
 Some of you may have encountered the Bu ddhadharma at Nalanda and think 
that the monks and laypeople here are completely relaxed and easy -going etc., and on 
this basis you decided to become a buddhist or a monk so you can be relaxed and easy -
going. Now you really have to check if this is what re ally makes you buddhist. By now, 
you can see that to be a buddhist means you go for refuge in the Buddha, Dharma and 
Sangha, and how it comes from certain causes presented in this text. It is up to oneself 
alone if all these causes are in the mind, and ref uge has been generated. You need to 
know all the different points explained here.   
 Someone might say, “I‟m a buddhist because I have taken on the precepts of 
refuge after having done the refuge ceremony.” To receive the precepts of refuge, you 
have to ha ve gone for refuge. To have gone for refuge you need to have the causes of 
going for refuge. The first one is that you have fear with regard to suffering, of 
samsara in general (contaminated aggregates) and particularly here of the lower realms. 
This fear only comes from seeing that you are full of very strong negativities and your 
virtue is very feeble, and that the result of the strong negativity is necessarily ripening 
into unbearable suffering. Due to this, a fear arises. In addition, we understand that  
most of our life in the past was engaging into negative paths and undergoing suffering 
states of mind. Then one looks for a refuge or protection from this suffering.  
One might have the outlook that if I‟m suffering from thirst, then I drink some 
water an d I‟m fine. If I‟m hungry I eat something, then I‟m fine. However, this doesn‟t 
eradicate the suffering from the root because the problem is with the contaminated 
aggregates. We are looking for protection from having to take on these five 
contaminated aggr egates. On this basis of fear of the contaminated aggregates, and the 
suffering of the lower realms in particular, you are turning to the Three Jewels as they 
are the only ones who can protect you from the unbearable suffering, and on this basis 
you take r efuge.  
 The first lines of this quote [ Praise in One Hundred and Fifty Verses , pg 65] in 
Tibetan says, with regard to whatever fault exists, someone who does not possess any 
of these fault s. Someone who with regard to all objects and all times is an omnis cient 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
295 knower, and has within this omniscient knowledge all good qualities; this is someone 
to go for refuge in.  
 If a mind exists, If you are sensible  means if you can identify good qualities as good 
qualities, faults as faults, what is to be adopted and w hat is to be abandoned and so on. 
If you have just a little of this intelligence, or if you have great intelligence even better, 
either way, you should then go for refuge in such a person.  
 The last few lines indicate the valid procedure. Valid because th ere are a lot of 
reasons why one should do this.  
 The very last line in Tibetan reads it is correct to abide in the teachings of that one . 
This is someone who has cleared away all mistakes and possesses all good qualities. 
Based on this they are able to c lear away all the faults of others and teach all the good 
qualities to others. Whatever such a person is teaching, it is most logical of you to 
listen, reflect and cultivate it.  
 Lama Tsongkhapa comments on this by saying if you have an intelligence that 
can discriminate between that which can and cannot protect, you should go for refuge 
in the undeceiving refuge of the Buddha, the Bhagavan. As this is most suitable, it is 
also suitable to go for refuge in his Dharma and in the supreme community, in other 
words the Refuge Jewels of the Dharma and Sangha.   
 Then there is a quote from the Seventy Verses on Going for Refuge, a work by 
Nagarjuna. It explains how all the holy beings of the past and skilled practitioners have 
gone for refuge in the Buddha, Dharm a and Sangha - and how it is the refuge for all 
those who want liberation.

2B4B -2B1A -2A2B  The reasons why they are worthy of refuge  
1 The actual reasons  
Second: Someone who is free from all fear, skilled in means of  liberating others from 
fear, steeped in great compassion tow ard everyone w ithout closeness and distance and 
w ho w orks for the benefit for the w elfare of all regardless of w hether they help him or 
not – such a one is w orthy of refuge. As only Buddha has th ose [qualifications], w hile 
Ishvara and the like do not, he is a refuge. For that reason the Dharma taught by him 
as w ell as the Sangha of hearers are also { 85}  w orthy to be refuges.  
 
Four factors are listed which qualify the Buddha as a worthy object of r efuge.  
Firstly, he is completely liberated from all his own fears. He himself is free from all 
fears. What does fear refer to? Is fear a sickness or being unhappy? What does this 
mean?

Back to the text, the other points are:

  Page | 298 – week one to seven  This compassion of the Bu ddha is without partiality [TRANSLATOR : close and far 
in Tibetan]  with regard to anyone. He is working for both those who benefit him and 
those who are not benefiting him as he regards them all as a mother regards her two 
children, with equal love and comp assion. Say one then acts kindly towards their 
mother, but the other tries to harm her. Nevertheless, the mother‟s love for them both 
is the same and impartial.  
 When we say the Buddha is completely liberated from all fears, and skilled in the 
ways to liberate all beings from this fear, what is this fear here? Hasn‟t it become easier 
now to see what this fear is talking about?  
STUDENT  1: It is a heavy, suffocating substance that prevents action.

GEN: Well said. This is it exac tly. It is an unhappy feeling of the mental type (from the 
mental factor of feeling), of which there are two forms; 1) an unpleasant mental feeling 
coming about through not wanting to be separated from what you are longing for and 
2) and unpleasant mental feeling that comes from encountering what you don‟t wish 
for. This is to be explored by everyone to see if it is actually like that or not.  
 When it says here that the Buddha is qualified in being an expert in the methods 
of liberating others from their f ear, this means liberating them from unpleasant mental 
feelings coming from either of the two types mentioned above. The Buddha is said to 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
299 be an expert in liberating others from suffering, but here is it specified as being an 
expert in liberating you from mental suffering.  
 In these two, all the other forms of fear such as the fears of Samsara and 
Nirvana, fear connected with cherishing one‟s self and grasping for a self, all these 
fears are included in these two.  
 Only a Buddha has these qualities. Even worldly gods like Ishvara, Indra, 
Brahma and so on don‟t have these qualities.  
 For that same reason, the Dharma that has been shown by the Buddha, and the 
sangha of the hearers, are worthy of giving you protection, and so are worthy objects 
of refuge.  
  
2 Advice to be  persistent about conviction, the principal cause  
That being so, once you have derived certainty w ith regard to these [objects of refuge] 
set forth in the Compendium , w hen you are able to rely on them w ith a single -pointed 
mind, it is impossible for you not to be protected. Therefore you should generate 
certainty from the bottom of your heart.  Of the tw o causes for being protected, there is 
no incompleteness in the e xternal one – our teacher [, Buddha,] has achieved it – 
how ever, since the internal cause, taking hold of that refuge trustingly has not come 
about, w e suffer.  
 
In accordance with what is expressed in Arya Asanga‟s Compendium , one makes certain 
the qualit ies of the Three Jewels and entrusts oneself to them. If you can do this with 
single -pointed trust, it is impossible not to be protected. Therefore, we should make 
great effort to generate such trust from the bottom of our heart.  
 The objects of going for  refuge are already present. Nevertheless, we are still in 
this state of suffering because we have never taken hold of the objects of refuge with 
deep trust.

The second one has four points: Going for refuge (by) understanding their positive 
qualities, understanding their distinctions, as sertion and going for refuge by not 
advocating other ones.

With lotuses how  w ould it be lovelier?   [24] 
 
The Buddha's body is presented as having 32 major marks and 80 minor signs. Even 
on a physical level, the Buddha had a very outstanding body, and anyone who 
encountere d him would become very subdued. There were people who tried to kill the 
Buddha at that time, but when they came into his presence, they became subdued and 
didn‟t pursue the act. This was only due to the marks and signs, as they obviously can‟t 
see the min d of the Buddha. This shows the good qualities of the Buddha's body.  
 We won‟t be able to go into all these marks and signs now, but we will in the 
Masters Program when we study the Abhisamayalamkara .  
You should remember them  in accordance w ith this sta tement.

them w ith w i sdom that possesses a single  instance of mind and answ ers all the 
questions w ith one utterance that they understand in their respective languages In the 
Chapter of the Truthful One  it says:  
 
If a great many beings ask many questions to the Buddha at the same time, his exalted 
knowledge would be able to take in all these different questions and respond. By giving 
just one response, all these questions would be answered.  
In this way, one reflects on the good qualities of the Buddha speech.

  Page | 302 – week one to seven  THURSDAY 15 APRIL 2010  - SESSION 3 
 
 Here we can also reflect that in the whole world, there was no one but the 
Buddha who taught that the causes of the true sufferings are the true origins. No other 
teacher has ever propounded the causes of true cessations are the tru e paths. It was 
only the Buddha who taught that which actually destroys the suffering of sentient 
beings.  
 The Buddha was able to answer many questions at the same time in the 
respective language of the person, by a single word. There are many people who ask 
questions in many languages, at the same time. The Buddha answers with a single word 
or phrase and each person gets their respective answer in their respective language.  
  
If thus all beings, in one single moment,  
ask a question in  a lot of terms  
his mind comprehends them in a single moment  
and answ ers them all  w ith one melodious [statement].
