You reflect along the lines of this statement.     
 
These last two lines of the quote express that only the Buddha has taught the way to 
completely overcome suffering.  
     
2B4B -2B1A -2A3A -1A3 The positive qualitie s of [the Buddha’s] mind  
A The positive qualities of [exalted] knowledge  
B The positive qualities of mercy [caring attitude or compassion]  
The third one has tw o points: the positive qualities of know ledge and the positive 
qualities of mercy.  
 
A The positi ve qualities of knowledge  
First: As all know able objects – modes and varieties - are engaged by the Muni’s 
unobstructed know ledge like a dhatri berryxxxix placed on the palm of his hand, the 
diverse know able objects are pervaded by the Muni’s know ledge. How eve r, they are 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
303 not pervaded by other’s [know ledge], as know able objects are vast and their 
consciousnesses are narrow .  
 
The Tibetan says here “all the knowable objects as they are, and many as there are”. As 
they are ( modes  in our text) refers to ultimate tr uths, the emptiness of phenomena. As 
many as there are ( varieties  in out text) refers to concealer truths. These are the two 
types of phenomena. All knowable objects are an object for the exalted knowledge of 
the Buddha. Exalted knowledge refers to the wis dom of the Buddha that perceives all 
knowable objects, ultimate truth and concealer truths, in a very clear way. In other 
words, they are all visible to him as if you would hold something in the palm of your 
hand. The text mentions a dhatri berry which is something that exists today, maybe 
similar to a grape. If it‟s in your hand, you would clearly see it. In the same way, the 
Buddha sees all objects as they are, and many as there are, clearly with his exalted 
knowledge.  
 For new people, this subject of „as  they are, and „as many as there are‟ is 
probably rather difficult. „As they are‟ refers to objects that are the actual situation, 
ultimate truth. „As many as there are‟ refers to concealer truths, objects which appear 
in one way and exist in another. The way they appear and the way they exist do not 
correspond. This is why there is something fake about them. „As they are‟ refers to  
ultimate truths – they do appear in the way they exist, are not fake and have something 
ultimate about them, so it is called an ultimate truth.  
 This table here appears to us as being something very firm, not constantly 
changing - whilst actually it is impermanent. It looks permanent and looks exactly like 
the same thing it was yesterday. Actually, every second there is a change in the 
particles et c., but it does not appear like this. Just on this level of change, how we 
perceive it, and how it actually exists are not in harmony. The appearance is 
permanent, but it exists as impermanent. This constant change is already proven by 
science – that the a toms are constantly moving and so on. That the table is 
impermanent is already established, but still we have the appearance of permanence. 
However, both this table and the impermanence of the table are referred to as 
concealer truths. The ultimate truth o f the table refers to a very fine level of the mode 
of abiding. As we already have a wrong appearance of the gross level of the table such 
as its impermanence, we can forget about seeing it in accordance with the final mode 
of abiding. It exists one way, b ut appears in another, so we say the mode of abiding 
and the appearance are not in harmony.  
 The perfect view, or the wisdom realising the ultimate, is a wisdom realising the 
ultimate mode of abiding of phenomena. The Buddha expressed this when he said 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 304 – week one to seven  phenomena are a dependent arising. If we understand this level of dependent arising, 
then it is easy to understand the emptiness of phenomena. It also works the other way 
around, if you understand emptiness, then you also understand things are a dependent 
arising. This is the way ultimate truth is presented in Buddhism; that things are a 
dependent arising. A wisdom that is perceiving this level of dependent arising is called 
a perfect view or an ultimate view.  
 Then there is this other phenomena called „the self‟ or the „I‟. It is talked about 
a lot in the different philosophical systems. Buddhists have a presentation of what the 
„I‟ is, its nature etc. In other systems like Christianity also talk about a self (Soul) and 
have their own presentations. This is exactly where the biggest difference lies between 
the different philosophical systems, the self and what is the nature of the self.  
 In Christianity for example, they would say this self is the soul and it had a 
beginning. They say God made it, but there i s no end, it will either be lead to God‟s 
own realm, or to hell – but there is no end. The nature of this self or soul would be 
permanent. This is held by Christianity, most likely by Islam and also Judaism. Maybe 
within Hinduism, but because there are man y types this needs to be explored. These 
non-buddhist systems say exactly the opposite to what Buddhism is saying. Buddhism 
has a different explanation with regard to the beginning, end and nature of the self. 
The nature of the self is impermanent, constan tly changing and so not permanent. It 
has no beginning, rather it is a dependent arising - it has constantly arisen since 
beginningless time. In the future, it will continue as an impermanent uninterrupted 
continuity. This is quite different from other rel igions  
 The presentation of the self or the „I‟ is the biggest difference between 
Buddhism and other systems. The self is actually impermanent, constantly changing, it 
has arisen in dependence on many parts, from causes and conditions, and therefore it 
is a dependent arising. Other systems say exactly the opposite, that this self is 
permanent and has an independent existence.  
 Buddhism then says that if it is the case, that the self is permanent and has an 
independent existence, then we should be able to f ind it somehow. By investigation, 
we should be able to see in what phenomena it can be found etc. If it can be found, 
then Buddhism would accept this and say, “Ok, you‟re right. Then there is such a 
permanent and independent self.” However, as it can‟t be found when we look for it, 
this unfindability is how it exists. This is the ultimate truth according to Buddhism.  
 This is what is referred to when we say „as they are‟ and „as many as there are‟. 
The self is impermanent, a dependent arising and under ana lysis it cannot be found. 
Therefore, the ultimate mode of this self is its unfindability – this is how it exists – „as 
they are.‟ The same is also true about the five aggregates. They are also not permanent 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
305 or independent but are a dependent arising. Then you look for the aggregates as being 
permanent and independent, or we could say look for the aggregates that are able to 
establish themselves – independent. As we‟re looking for the self in this case, we could 
look for it outside of the five aggregates or within the five aggregates. As we don‟t find 
this independent self, or a self which is able to establish itself, either outside or within 
the five aggregates, then we have to say I looked for it, but I could not find it. As it 
exists („as they are‟) or we can say ultimately, means that it cannot be found.  
 
FRIDAY 16 APRIL  2010  - SESSION 1 
 
A The positive qualities of knowledge  
First: As all know able objects – modes and varieties - are engaged by the Muni’s 
unobstructed know ledge like a dhatri berry placed o n the palm of his hand, the diverse 
know able objects are pervaded by the Muni’s know ledge. How ever, they are not 
pervaded by other’s [know ledge], as know able objects are vast and their consciousnesses 
are narrow . In this regard it says in Praise of the Pra isew orthy:  
 
The first part means that there is no object not pervaded by the Buddha's exalted 
knowledge; whether phenomena as they are or how many they are. The second part 
refers to that there are vast objects of knowledge, and the buddha perceives all o f 
them, but compared to this, the consciousnesses of other beings are quite narrow and 
since objects of knowledge are vast and limitless theses narrow consciousnesses are 
not able to perceive all those objects of knowledge. This is also stated by the follo wing 
quote:  
Only your exalted w isdom,  
{ 87}  Encompasses all the know able objects  
For all the others apart from you  
Some know able objects remain to be know n.   [26] 
 
The word some  here is not so clear; it means that the problem for other 
consciousnesses is on  the side of objects of knowledge. The others have 
consciousness, but there are objects of knowledge left for them to understand.  
 
And: 
Bhagavan, dharmas of all times,  
All aspects, all places of origin,  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 306 – week one to seven  Are spheres of activity of your mind  
Like dhatri berries in your palm.  
 
As regards dharmas – static or mobile,  
Single  or various and distinct,  
There’s nothing to w hich your mind is obstructed - 
Like w ind moving to and fro in space.  
 
That the exalted knowledge of the buddha can know everything is describ ed here in 
many ways. One way describes that he knows single phenomena and their variety. Also 
static phenomena, like this house for example, and mobile phenomena like a human 
[TRANSLATOR: actually it says moving and non -moving] . Therefore, since all pheno mena 
are clear to a buddha, we could say that he perceives the world which is like a 
container and all the beings within, being the contained. All of this he knows as clear 
as a dhatri berry in the palm of his hand. So there is no obstacle, no obstruction to his 
perception, and therefore his mind is like the wind moving to and fro in space; the 
wind can also go wherever it wishes without any obstruction.  
 
You should reflect in accordance w ith these statements.  
 
B The positive quality of mercy  
Second: J ust as sentient beings are bound by the afflictions w ithout any control, the 
Muni is bound by great compassion w ithout control and therefore great compassion 
continuously arises in him w hen he perceives suffering migrating beings.  
 
Just as there are strong fo rms of afflictions binding sentient beings making them 
other -powered, so there is strong love, compassion and bodhicitta binding the buddha, 
he is kind of under the power of these minds. Therefore, whenever he sees the 
suffering of all sentient beings he g enerates an uninterrupted great compassion.  
 
In the One Hundred and Fifty Verses it says:  
All of these migrating beings  
Are equally bound by the afflictions.  
In order to untie their afflictions  
You have long been bound by compassion.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
307  
Should I first prost rate to you  
Or to that w hich causes y ou  
To stay in samsara that long, aware  
Of its faults – to great compassion?    
 [27] 
 
Sentient beings are imprisoned by their afflictions. This is their state. In comparison to 
that, the buddhas are only intent on liberating them from these fetters of the 
afflictions through their compassion. As a result, they are also other -powered through  
compassion, and therefore are bound to constantly work to liberate sentient beings 
from their afflictions.  
 Should I first prostrate to you... : Becoming aware of this strong love and 
compassion of the buddha one venerates him for this. The buddha constan tly acts for 
sentient beings until all existing suffering of samsara has been completely extinguished. 
This activity comes about due to the presence of his great compassion.  
 To stay in samsara aware of its faults : all theses sentient beings are in this 
wretched state of samsara. How does this come about? This comes from the 
afflictions, that keep beings bound in samsara. The buddha completely understood 
these faults of samsara, and in this way great compassion arose in him. Since he is 
aware of all these f aults and acts upon this knowledge, he is the being that we prostrate 
to.  
 
In the Chapter of the Truthful One it also says:  
 
When he sees the many beings alw ays  
Obscured by ignorance and mental darkness  
Imprisoned in the prison of samsara  
Great compass ion is born in the holy sage.   
 [28] 
 
You should become aw are of it in accordance w ith these statements.  
 
We should reflect on these statements and become aware of the good qualities of the 
buddhas mind, especially his great compassion. Beings are continua lly in the darkness 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 308 – week one to seven  of ignorance that obscures their mind; meaning that all our wisdom and intelligence is 
obscured by this. In this way, all sentient beings are imprisoned in samsara. This 
situation is clear to the buddha, and due to this, great compassio n is born in him and 
impels him to work for the liberation of all sentient beings. This we should reflect on 
to understand the good qualities of the buddha.  
 The loving concern of the buddha described here should be reflected on and 
internalised. If you b ecome aware that he has this all pervasive knowledge and great 
compassion for all sentient beings, then you will also understand that he indeed is a 
being worthy of going to for refuge, and in whom we can place hope and trust.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2A3A -1A4 The posit ive qualities of [the Buddha’s] exalted activities  
Fourth: By means of both spontaneous and uninterrupted exalted activities of [his] 
body, speech and mind he benefits all sentient beings. In fact insofar as a trainee is fit 
for guidance, it is impossible for the Muni not to give that trainee w hat is excellent 
and pull him out of his degeneration. That is w hy it is stated that they certainly 
perform all the appropriate actions. { 88}   
 
The point here is that everything depends on us. There is constant benefi t for sentient 
beings due to his exalted activities. However, everything else depends on us; whether 
we engage into dharma practice or not. If we do four sessions or six sessions a day and 
so on; all the paths are completely presented by the buddha, and it  is up to us whether 
we engage in them or not; this is what is meant by insofar as a trainee is fit for guidance . 
Such a person is someone who is practicing dharma, who really wants to engage into it. 
Nothing has been held back by the buddha, he gave us all the practices and we can 
engage in them if we really want to practice the dharma. Moreover, if we do engage 
into this practice, then excellent states will definitely arise in our continuum, and 
degeneration will come to an end.  
 The buddha made clear what suffering means, but before that we never 
understood what the suffering of change means. Of course, it was rather clear to us 
what the suffering of suffering referred to, but the suffering of change is a different 
matter. It refers to the different experiences we hold as being happiness, which 
normally experience and cling to, despite this very happiness of ours be ing in the 
nature of suffering. In this way, he revealed to us that what we consider to be 
happiness is actually the suffering of change, and even going beyond that, he showed 
how our five aggregates are the all pervasive compounded suffering. The latter b eing 
something we would never find out ourselves. Furthermore, he even explained that the 
causes of this suffering are karma and afflictions, which is something else we would 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
309 not have understood by ourselves. In addition, he showed us the antidotes to us. For 
the cause of suffering, karma for example, to oppose the negative karma we 
accumulate, he showed us the four opponent powers. As a result of all this, we gain a 
lot of information and guidance that we couldn‟t have come upon ourselves. So in the 
realm of dharma there is nothing that the buddha didn‟t show. He taught everything 
which we need to know, and it is now up to us to put it into practice.  
 By reflecting on all the topics presented up to now, if you investigate whether 
the three jewels, especiall y the Buddha, are a worthy refuge, and you find out that this 
is the case, then this is so because there are these four aspects which make the buddha 
a worthwhile refuge. He is free from all his own fears, is an expert in the methods of 
liberating others f rom their fears, he is not partial in his compassion, and he is 
constantly working for everybody‟s aims, no matter if he himself is benefited or not. If 
you really are able to reflect well on these qualities of body speech and mind, if you 
manage to enter into this topic from the depth of your heart, then you will be able to 
develop a strong form of faith.  
  The faith we talk about here is not blind, but a faith that came about on the 
basis of knowing all that is explained here and having engaged into studies, and then 
exploring it through questions and answers. By studying in this way, you will come to 
know all of these points, and on this basis generate this informed kind of faith which 
will be stable. This means that whatever situation you undergo, this faith will never 
decrease, but rather increase your faith, making it even more stable.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2A3A -1B Supplementary points  
1 Gaining certainty regarding the conviction: the cause of going for refuge purely   
Potow a said this: “If you keep contemplating again and again, the firmer your 
conviction and the purer your mental continuum becomes, the more ble ssings will 
arise. As you gain certain know ledge in this regard, you w ill go for refuge from the 
bottom of your heart and then, if you merely train in the precepts, everything you do 
w ill become an instance of practicing the Buddha’s teachings.  
 
You will be able to generate a strong faith, purify your continuum and receive blessing 
of body, speech and mind, if you repeatedly investigate and reflect on all these aspects 
of how the buddha is a worthy refuge due to these four aspects. In particular, reflect 
on all the good qualities of the buddha‟s body speech and mind, especially his exalted 
knowledge and compassionate attitude. There is no other way to receive the blessings; 
they won‟t just fall from the sky.  
 About the second part; As you gain certain knowl edge... : First of all you are 
gaining what is called “certainty in knowledge” about all these good aspects, and 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 310 – week one to seven  through this you develop this faith from the bottom of your heart and put your hopes 
and trust in the three jewels. In this way, you go for refu ge, and as a result you enter 
into the trainings or precepts of refuge, and by doing this a pure form of dharma 
practice will come about.  
 
2 Not gaining the conviction  
We do not even value the Buddha’s exalted know ledge as much as [w e value] a keen 
w oman f ortune teller. If a keen fortune teller tells you “I know there w ill be no bad 
luck for you this year”, you w alk away happy and comfortable. If she says: “This year 
[there w ill be] great misfortune – do this, don’t do that! ”, you w ill try hard to 
accomplis h it and if you do not manage , you w ill think: “ I did not accomplish what 
she told me [to do]” and your mind w ill be anxious. If the Buddha says: “Do this and 
that. Accomplish this and that”, do you take it on? If you do not accomplish it, does 
your mind  become anxious? To say: “from the [point of view of] Dharma those w ords 
[may be] explained as follow s: due to place and time that is inconvenient now . I must 
do this” is to ignore the Buddha’s statements and proceed on the basis of your ow n 
understanding.  If you turn the mind w ithin and investigate this in detail, rather than 
being unquestioningly happy and confused w ith respect to the mere w ords, it w ill strike 
you as very  true.   
 
Geshe Potowa continues in the above quote by saying that we don‟t even reg ard the 
buddha as highly as someone who is a very good fortune teller. Westerners are very 
much into this, they like to ask for divinations, for Mos, and also they like this feng 
shui a lot. Tibetans like astrology a lot. This is what is meant by the first  line, this talks 
about us. A fortune teller tells us that this year there is no obstacle to this or that, and 
we are relaxed. When she tells us we have to do this or that we try to put a lot of effort 
into it, and if we don‟t do it we become worried. The buddha also gives all kinds of 
advice, but if we don‟t follow it, we aren‟t uncomfortable at all. So do we have the 
same regard for the buddha's words as we do for the words of a fortune teller?  
 Further on, we are asked the question: “If you don‟t do what  the buddha said, 
do you get worried? Do you have such a mind at all?” due to place and time... : one says, 
“Yes, the dharma says that and that, but nowadays we don‟t have to do this anymore.” 
Such an attitude neglects the dharma, and one has come under a s trong power of „I‟ 
where you decide what is applicable and what is not. He concludes by saying that we 
have to turn within ourselves, and ask whether we are actually putting into practice 
what the buddha is saying or not.  
 
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
311 3 Insistent advice to achieve cer tainty  
Therefore, think  about the Buddha’s positive qualities again and again, and strive to 
achieve as much certainty as possible. If it develops, you have reached the main point 
of going for refuge, because a similar certainty will come concerning the D harma that 
arose from him and the Sangha of those w ho have accomplished that Dharma. 
Without it there is no w ay going for refuge, a tamed mind, let alone other paths, w ill 
come either.  
 
By reflecting again and again on these four points that make the budd ha a worthy 
refuge  some change can really happen in our mind with faith and a strong mind of 
refuge arising. If in this way we can generate a strong faith in the buddha, then from 
this a strong faith with regards to his dharma and to the sangha will also arise. If on 
the other hand, we don‟t enter into this way of cultivating refuge, but try out some 
other training, then for the moment we can forget about any other path being 
generated in our mind, and even the path of refuge will not be properly be genera ted.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2A3A -2 Positive qualities of the Dharma  
Second: On account of [your] respect for the Buddha, you should be mindful  thinking: 
the fact that the Buddha has { 89}  limitless qualities arose from manifesting and 
meditating the Dharma of scriptu res and realization, the truths  of cessation and the 
path, the epitome of the abandonment of faults and the achievement of positive 
qualities. That is the meaning of the Compendium of the Dharma [Sutra].  
 
Taking the respect generated as a reason or start ing point, we can then reflect in the 
following way: all the good qualities the buddha possesses now have come from the 
scriptural and realisational dharma. This refers to the true cessations and true paths, 
and therefore has the nature of that which aband ons all faults and attains all good 
qualities. These arise by manifesting and cultivating all this dharma. Recollecting this 
process, we are advised to do the same here. This is expressed in the Compendium of the  
Dharma S utra. 
 For the people new here I will explain a bit more. The buddha has completely 
abandoned all possible faults and has gained all the good qualities to an extent 
unperceivable by our minds. How did this happen? What did he train in? The answer is 
that he achieved this by training in al l the practices and paths of the small, middle and 
great scope. Through having trained in all the practices of the small scope, the buddha 
was able to block out all negative karma and it‟s ripening through reflecting on all the 
suffering coming from negati ve deeds created due to the afflictions. First of all, the 
afflictions refer to ignorance, and from that point come all the others like anger, 
attachment and so forth. Moreover, due to their presence negative actions are 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 312 – week one to seven  accumulated and suffering come abou t as a result. The buddha then understood that by 
getting liberated from these causes, from this ignorance and the other afflictions the 
effects of suffering could no longer be produced. Through this, he was training in the 
practices of a middle scope bein g. 
 Through relating to all the imprints left by the afflictions on his continuum and 
to the mind of self -cherishing as having faults, he was also practicing to purify these, 
and in this way he was practicing the trainings of the great being. as a result o f 
following this path all faults were eventually destroyed and the buddha became a 
Sangye (Tibetan for buddha), meaning the “vast awakened one”. He had woken up 
because he destroyed all possible faults; he had awoken from all these faults, and full 
of qual ities. In this way, he is described as vast.  This is meant by where it says: the fact 
that the Buddha has  limitless qualities .  
 
arose from manifesting and meditating the Dharma ...:   The dharma of scripture and 
realisation refers to both being free from  faults, this is what true cessations here refers 
to, and the other aspects are the true paths, which bring about the cessation of these 
faults. These two aspects are the dharma that the buddha manifested and cultivated, 
through which he abandoned all faul ts and gained all limitless qualities.

2B4B -2B1A -2A3A -3 Positive qualities of the sangha  
Third: This refers mainly to persons w ho are aryas. Recalling the positive qualities of 
the Dharma you are mindful of those w h o accomplish it correctly. That is the meaning 
of Compendium of the Dharma.  
 
The Arya -sangha refers to practitioners who are on the ten bhumis. Starting from 
someone who is on the path of seeing, meaning he gained a wisdom directly realising 
emptiness. The  ultimate bodhicitta is such a wisdom realising emptiness directly but 
comes together with a mind qualified by love, compassion and bodhicitta. This is also 
a true path that is able to destroy the afflictions and the karmas. It can destroy all 
faults, the obscurations to liberation and omniscience. Therefore, any type of fault can, 
and will be, purified by this true path, and all qualities achieved through it. As a result, 
a person having developed this path can bring about true cessations, and is an arya 
being. These are the main references when we talk about the sangha jewel. That is the 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
313 meaning of Compendium of the Dharma : This is what this text is expressing with regards 
to the sangha jewel.   
 
2B4B -2B1A -2A3B  Understanding their distinctions  
Second: As i s apparent  from the Compendium, you go for refuge aw are of the mutual 
distinctions betw een the three jew els.  
 
By being aware of the different qualities of buddha, dharma and sangha, then one will 
be aware of their special qualities and one will be able t o distinguish them well.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2A3C   Assertion  
Third: As is apparent  from the Extensive Commentary on the Vinaya, you go for 
refuge in terms of  considering the Buddha to be the one w ho teaches  the refuge; the 
Dharma to be nirvana, which is the actu al refuge; and the Sangha, to be the 
companions accomplishing refuge.  
 
The buddha is here referred to as the teacher of the refuge, one could say the one 
demonstrating or explaining the refuge. He is like a doctor explaining our sickness and 
giving advice  about the treatment. For the treatment, you rely on helpers, like nurses, 
someone friendly who is accompanying you. These are the sangha jewel, therefore 
called here the companions in refuge , and the actual refuge is the dharma which would be 
the actual m edicine. Nevertheless, while these are the functions of the refuge jewels, 
you yourself need to take all this on and engage into these modes of dharma, and then 
you will attain the refuge, which is the dharma of nirvana within yourself, i.e. the 
dharma tha t is liberation. This of course is the actual refuge; if you are able to attain to 
this state you are safe, you have found the actual refuge. In order to gain this, you need 
to practice the paths shown by the Buddha, and you are helped in this by applying the 
sangha jewel.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2A3D  Not advocating other ones  
Fourth: Having understood the superiority and inferiority of Buddhist and non -
Buddhist teachers, teachings and students, respectively, you consider the Three J ew els 
alone to be a refuge, and do not consider teachers and so forth w ho are incompatible 
w ith t hem to be a refuge. With respect to the difference betw een the tw o [sets], the 
difference betw een the teachers is that the Buddha is w ithout faults and has perfected 
positive qualities w hile other teachers are the opposite to that.  
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 314 – week one to seven  First of all, we need to understand that there is a difference between the teacher in the 
buddhist context and in the context of other religious systems, like for example with 
Christ in the Christian system. If we look at the teachings we will see a great 
difference, the teache rs themselves are very different and also those training 
themselves in these teachings are very different. There are great differences for all 
three, showing the superiority of the one over the other, and these we should come to 
know. So here, it says we s hould make effort to understand what is an unmistaken and 
what is a mistaken refuge.  
 The buddha cleared away all faults, he awoke from them and he has a complete 
accumulation of all qualities. For other teachers this is not the case. Similarly, we have 
to understand this for the mode of dharma as expressed here. It is complete with 
regards to each method leading us to a transformation of the mind and to higher 
states. Regarding the sangha jewel, meaning the sangha of bodhisattvas, or more 
specifically the  sangha of the arya -bodhisattvas or the arya beings; they are 
continuously and exclusively engaging in dharma activities. They are not concerned 
with anything that is not dharma or non -virtuous. This is the type of community we 
are talking about here, and in addition to this, this community is qualified by love, 
compassion, bodhicitta, etc.  
 
In the Praise of the Particularly Exalted  [One]   it says:  
Having abandoned the other teachers  
Bhagavan, I take refuge in you.  
If you ask “For w hat purpose?” – Because 
You have no faults but have qualities .    [28] 
 
The one praising here says he abandoned other teachers and only goes for refuge to 
the buddha. The reason for this is that the buddha only has an abundance of qualities 
and eliminated all faults.  
 
and: 
The more I’ve thought about the scriptures  
Of others, tirthikas , the more  
My confidence in you has grow n, Lord.  
Those w hose minds are w asted through the  
Faulty tenets of non -omniscience,  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
315 Whose thinking is w asted do not see,  
{ 90}  Faultless teachers – not eve n you.  
 
[Translators note: in Tibetan it says: you the faultless teacher]  
This talks about how one looks at the different modes of behaviour and scriptures of 
other philosophical tenet holders. By exploring and analysing them one is more and 
more dissatisfied with them and gains confidence in what the Buddha taught.    
By really  analysing others systems, and especially their philosophical view, one 
sees that there are only faults, and sees that the teachers who composed this are 
definitely not omniscient. Therefore, someone following this system ends up with a 
flawed, or ruined m ind; here it says, “whose minds are wasted” . Someone ending up with 
this faulty view will not even be able to see the faultless teacher.

B Eliminating inaccuraci es [with respect to the precepts of going for refuge]

and common precepts [for all three objects of refuge].  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2A4A -1 Specific precepts [for the individual objects of refuge]

The first one has tw o points: precepts of w hat is to be stopped and precepts of w hat is 
to be accomplished.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2A4A -1A Precepts of what is to be stopped  
1 Not to go for refuge to other gods  
2 To give up causing harm or difficulty  to sentient beings  
3 Not to associate with non -Buddhists [/Conni: proponents of non -Buddhist views]  
 
First: From the [Sutra of Great Final] Nirvana:  
Going for refuge to the three  
One is a true upasaka.  
One w ill never ever go  
To some other gods for refuge.  
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 316 – week one to seven  One going for refuge to the  Saddharma  
Is free from the mind to harm or kill.  
One going for refuge to the Sangha,  
Does not mingle w ith Tirthikas.     [30] 
 
By taking refuge, you become an upasaka and therefore a basis for taking the upasaka 
vows. Moreover, after having taken refuge o ne doesn‟t turn to any other refuge (like 
gods). The Tibetan word for Tirthikas, literally means “those supporting extremes” or 
“extremists”.   
 
In accordance w ith this statement there are three  [inhibitory precepts]:: not to go for 
refuge to other gods, to give up causing harm or difficulty to sentient beings and not to 
associate w ith Tirthikas .  
These three trainings presented here are the main trainings of refuge.  
 
1 Not to go for refuge to other gods  
The first of these: If one does not even regard po w erful w orldly gods, Rudra, Vishnu 
and the like as long -term refuge, no need to speak of nagas and local spirits belonging 
to the hungry ghosts. In this regard, w hile it is not appropriate to rely on them from a 
lack of conviction in the three objects of r efuge, it is not inappropriate to seek their 
assistance for some action of temporary character. By w ay of example, it is like seeking 
assistance from a donor for one’s livelihood or like relying on a doctor in hope for a 
cure.   
 
As one doesn‟t take the wo rldly gods as one‟s innermost or deepest felt refuge, one 
doesn‟t need to mention beings like nagas and so forth. However, if you rely on these 
gods as you would rely on a sponsor or on a doctor for example, then in this temporal 
way you can rely on such s pirits or gods. If you have the discernment that the need is 
superficial, like being healed from a sickness, then finding someone to support you in 
this is ok. What this is saying is that if you are sick, go to the hospital! It is for example 
very good to make offerings to the medicine buddha, but also you should go to the 
doctor. Both of them support each other and by consulting both there is a good 
chance of being healed.  
 
2 To give up causing harm or difficulty to sentient beings  
The second one consists  in giving up causing harm or difficulty  to humans or animals, 
and so forth through thought or actions like beating, binding, ensnaring them, piercing 
their noses and loading them unbearably .  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
317  
Binding and ensnaring them  refers to putting them into prison. Loading them unbearably  
refers to loading them until they are able to stand anymore. In the past, yaks and 
horses would have been heavily loaded with all types of goods as there were no other 
means to do it, and on top of this the leader of the caravan rode on them. Such a thing 
needs to be abandoned. This might be similar when we go travelling and our bag is fit 
for ten kilos but we stuff another five into it and it breaks! It‟s then that we say it is of 
bad quality and  get angry! But we don‟t see any fault with what we did.  
  
3 Not to associate with non -Buddhists  
The third one consists in not conforming to those who are unconvinced by the 
protection of the three jew els, and denigrate them.  
 
Here we talk about people who have no faith in the three jewels as an object of refuge. 
Quite the opposite, they are belittling them. We don‟t want to act like these people.

These are also very important for us to practi ce!

3 How to train with respect to the representations of the sangha

It is important to follow up these instructions on how to be respectful towards images 
of the buddha. The point of the quote is that we have to be very careful in relating to 
representations of the buddha, regardless of if they are made from wood, clay or s tone. 
They all should be treated as if they were the actual buddha. This is quite a topic for

QUESTIONS  
QUESTION:  The first bodhisattva vow is about not praising oneself and not putting 
down others. If I need to correct someone else‟s behaviour, could this interfere with 
this first vow?  
GEN:  Here we are not talking about someone else‟s faulty behaviour, but rather we  are 
talking about an individual developing strong pride on the basis of strong self -
cherishing. Through this self -cherishing one naturally looks down on others as inferior 
and praises oneself. Addressing the faults of others is not the same as despising t he 
person.   
 
QUESTION:  It seems that „The Tibetan Book of the Dead‟ [in tib.lit: liberation through 
listening in the Bardo]  is more connected to the Nyingma tradition, is this also used in 
the Geluk tradition?  
GEN:  I myself don‟t have any experience with this. I haven‟t even seen this text, 
therefore I also don‟t know whether it‟s Nyingma or not. In the Geluk tradition for the 
person who is about to die there is the prayer of eight aspects. These are eight prayers 
within which there are ‟The King of Prayer s‟, a prayer to be reborn in Amitabha pure 
land, The Prostrations to the Thirty -five Buddhas, a prayer of the 
Bodhisattvacharyavatara , a prayer by Lama Tsongkhapa and so forth. Another thing that 
is done is the Lama Chöpa ; there is no stronger practice than  doing this.  
 
QUESTION:  What is the difference between the refuge field and the merit field?  
GEN:  The refuge field refers to all the objects of refuge with which you take refuge. 
The merit field: If I give something to Tharchin, then he becomes my merit fi eld. 
Anyone who you can do something to bring about merit is a merit field. The field 
connected to the practice of Lama Chöpa is the refuge field. However, as it is the 
refuge field it is also the merit field, in that you can accumulate a lot of merits fro m 
this field. However, in general there are limitless merit fields, i.e. fields for merit.  
 
QUESTION:  Could Gen -la say something about Phowa (mind transference) practice.  
GEN:  Meditate on definite emergence, bodhicitta and the correct view. There is no 
superior practice than that; the buddha didn‟t give anything superior to that. If he 
couldn‟t give anything superior, who else could?  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 320 – week one to seven   
In the  Lam-Rim prayer of the Lama Chöpa  it says,  
“Please bless me to reach a pure land should the points of the path not be  
completed at death either the forceful means of enlightenment, the Guru‟s 
mind transference or by the advice on applying the five forces.  
 
In short, please bless me my protector to be cared for by you from birth 
unremittingly throughout all my lives unti l I become your chief disciple, 
holding every secret of your body speech and mind.”  
 
This indicates that by having engaged intensely into listening, reflecting and 
meditating, and then encountering a fully qualified lama, then the lama could act as a 
condition for this mind transference. However, without listening, reflecting and 
meditating, and cultivating the three principle aspects of the path, there is no way that 
this mind transference will happen.  
 When Lama Zopa Rinpoche for example tells someon e to do many hundreds of 
thousands of Vajrasattva mantras or prostrations, then what he is actually is saying is 
“purify and accumulate”. He gives us these numbers in order for us to engage in the 
practice, because without the numbers we would not engage! What it comes down to is 
that he tells us to accumulate and purify. We receive these big numbers for a specific 
practice, and we are able to accept it because we have the outlook that we received this 
from Lama Zopa Rinpoche out of his great caring attitud e, with great affection, and 
therefore one is able to engage into these practices and accumulate and purify. Because 
of all this, one might be able to experience some sort of situation where Phowa, i.e. 
mind transference could happen. But without purifying, accumulating and dispelling 
obstacles there would be no benefit from this Phowa practice or reading „The Tibetan 
Book of the Dead‟. If this would be possible just like that, then the buddha woul d 
already have done it long ago.  
 If someone incredibly rich makes the most exquisite offerings to all the buddhas 
and bodhisattvas, he would then accumulate great merit. In comparison, if someone 
just meditates on love, the merit he creates would be far g reater. The relation between 
the two is such that there is no comparison, and no way to express how much bigger 
the second kind of practice is. It is limitless. We have to understand that there is great 
meritorious power from practices such as generating a  mind of definite emergence, 
compassion, bodhicitta, engaging into the six causes one effect practice. We should not 
see them as the only practices generating merit, but right from these practices of 
generating love and compassion, you generate limitless a mounts of merit.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
321  
QUESTION:  Out of the four aspects making the buddha a valid object of refuge; what 
is the difference between the last two? Is it that the third one refers more to a 
compassionate attitude and the fourth refers to the buddha actually actin g upon this?  
GEN:  This is quite correct. The third one is a quality of mind of the buddha, and the 
fourth one has to do with his activity. The first quality mentioned, is that he himself is 
free of all fears, then he is an expert, or scholar in the methods  of liberating all other 
beings from their fear. The third one is a non -partial affection for all beings, whereas 
the last one is to be understood in the way that there are some beings who benefit the 
buddha, and some who do not, but he is achieving the ai ms of all of them while 
completely free from any aversion or anger towards those that do not benefit him. In 
this way, he achieves the aims of others without any discrimination.  
 The main way he benefits beings is by teaching them the dharma; and within 
that by showing the different forms of ethical discipline. The gift that the buddha gave 
to us is the complete Kangyür . The buddha himself said very clearly that he is teaching 
the dharma, but he can‟t wash away negativities with water, he can‟t take away a ny of 
our suffering with his hands or bestow any good qualities. What he can do is to teach 
the dharma through the Kangyür . And that we have access to this is also thanks to the 
great Lotsawas, the great translators of the past.  
  
We can recall that the b uddha walked on this planet about 2600 years ago. That we 
have any knowledge of this is due to us having the Kangyür . Due to the kindness of the 
Lotsawas we can read these texts, and therefore we can develop faith and liking in 
what the buddha said. Otherw ise, there would be no way to get into contact with 
something that happened so long ago. Maybe a hundred years later it would still be 
possible, but not for much more.  
 When we look at the advice of the buddha, we can see that none of it is out of 
date, b ut rather it is also very applicable to modern times. Normally, the older one‟s 
possessions then the more likely they are to be thrown out, but this is not the case 
with the words of the buddha as they are extremely important and powerful. If we 
compare wh at he said with what science says today, then still the buddha has a lot to 
say. As a result, there is something very amazing and useful in the words of the 
buddha.  
Saying, “as long as there is time” meaning as long as there are beings who want 
to be happ y and not to suffer; this is true for all sentient beings and therefore if there 
is any such being in the future, the teachings of the buddha will be very precious for 
such a being. They offer all the different methods for attaining happiness and the 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 322 – week one to seven  freed om from suffering. If someone has a headache, he takes medicine, but he 
understands that a headache can come back at any time and is not necessarily 
eliminated. In the same way, someone may want to temporarily escape their sufferings 
by taking drugs even t hough the drugs will do nothing to completely eliminate the 
sufferings. The methods to really uproot all suffering are presented in the dharma, and 
as long as there is a moment that someone wants to be happy and doesn‟t want to 
suffer – as long as samsara exists – these teachings will be of benefit. Of course, if in 
the future there are sentient beings who don‟t wish for happiness and wish for 
suffering, then the teachings of the buddha become useless!  
The advice of the buddha is replete with methods for a ccumulating virtue and 
purifying negativities. Among this great variety of methods, the primary one is 
listening, reflecting and meditating. In comparison, any other methods (like Mandala 
offerings, prostrations, etc.) become side, or branch activities. Th is doesn‟t mean that 
they are useless; they are very beneficial and we should engage into them as these 
secondary activities act as support for the main activity of listening, reflecting and 
meditating through accumulation and purification.  
  
The same hol ds true for the practice of secret mantra. It is very powerful and positive; 
but even more important than that is to engage into practices as shown in the Lam-Rim 
and in the Bodhisattvacharyavatara . From these texts, we get all the practices leading to 
the mind of definite emergence, bodhicitta and the perfect view. Therefore, if you are 
able to bring forth these states of mind, then they can act as a stable foundation on 
which you can put the practice of secret mantra. Without this foundation of the three 
principle aspects of the path, the practice of secret mantra will be like reaching out to 
get something from the empty sky.  
 When we talk about practicing dharma, we talk about changing our mind 
through firstly subduing and disciplining the rough minds. Ho w does this come about? 
First of all, one has to listen to the explanation on what kinds of minds these are, and 
cultivate an ability to identify them. Furthermore, one needs to listen and reflect in 
order to understand the method to transform problems, in  order to counteract these 
gross minds. What are the antidotes? How would we know about them without 
listening and reflecting? These are not things to be found by just hanging out. You 
have to get them from the mouth of the guru and reflect on them. On tha t basis, you 
can actually engage into a practice that pacifies and disciplines your mind.  
 It is possible that someone comes to Nalanda, concludes that this is not a 
conducive place for him, and that there are better places to transform the mind. For 
exam ple, such a person might think he needs to move to Bodhgaya, and it would 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
323 provide him with the real possibility for him to change his mind because he has a good 
feeling or special energy about the place. Or someone says, “with this lama there is a 
special energy. I need to go to him to transform my mind.” To rely on this kind of 
feeling or energy with a lama will not achieve the result. Saying that one  needs to go to 
a special place, while having texts like the Lam-Rim or the Bodhisattvacaryavatara  in front  
of oneself is strange; there is no better blessing or energy to be found than from these 
texts. From them one understands the problems and the antidotes, and can bring about 
real change. The trouble with relying on a feeling from a “special place” is that  it can 
be experienced for a couple of days, but then it‟s lost. Rather, we have to really engage 
in what is described in the texts, as it is only these that instruct us in the methods to 
transform the mind. These texts come from the great masters of India  and Tibet. We 
should really sit down and reflect about this. We are here engaging into dharma, 
studying the dharma, and therefore it is really necessary for us to reflect on this.   
  
2B4B -2B1A -2A4A -1B Precepts of what is to be accomplished    
1 How to tr ain with respect to the representations of the Buddha  
2 How to train with respect to the representations of the Dharma  
3 How to train with respect to the representations of the Sangha  
2B4B -2B1A -2A4A -1B1 How to train with respect to the representations of t he Buddha  
A The actual way to train with respect to the representations of the Buddha  
{ 91}  Second: How ever good or flaw ed painted pictures of the Buddha’s body may be, 
do not disparage them, do not put them on the ground w hen inconvenienced. Having 
given up the irreverance of pawning them etc. and defaming them, regard them as 
fields  of respect like the teacher [himself]. In the Letter to a Friend it says:  
The w ay the learned honour any form  
of the Sugata – even made from w ood.    [31] 
 
It is necessary to treat statues, tsa -tsas, thangkas, photos etc. as if they were the actual 
buddha regardless how well they are made or the quality of the material used. Since we 
perceive it as the body of the buddha, and it is without flaw, we would neve r say “this 
kind of buddha is ugly”. On the other hand, we may say that the person who made the 
image may not be such a good artist, but not the statue itself. In the same way, since 
we have a lot of dharma books, all of them are to be treated with utmost respect as 
they are the speech of the Tathagatha . So stepping over them, putting them on the 
ground or throwing them into the bin are all actions to be avoided.  
 Additionally, you should not travel with dharma books and such things as shoes 
in the same bag , but they must be kept separate. When travelling you carry images of 
the buddha in your bag then they should not be put together with your shoes or boots; 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 324 – week one to seven  they should be separated. You might have a lot of holy images or books on your 
computer, but if the electricity is turned off, then I think this is not a problem.  
 In short, we should be very respectful with any representation, especially 
statues, texts and images. For example, nowadays you can see in all kinds of 
restaurants and shops a buddha statue s tanding there in order to beautify the 
restaurant, irrespective of if they are buddhists or not. In this way, the Buddha is like 
some sort of doorman, and definitely fulfilling the purpose of generating faith. In fact, 
it may even be the cause for creating  negativities.

QUESTION:  In the context of not carrying scriptures in an inappropriate way in our

GEN:  I feel that still it would be better to keep them separate, as we experience them

outlook towards something like this being a teaching, then it might be better to keep 
them separate.  
 
QUESTION:  Lama Zopa Rinpoche explaine d that at a certain level, a bodhisattva sees 
a statue of the buddha as the  nirmanakaya,  and an arya bodhisattva sees it as the 
samboghakaya. What does that mean?  
GEN:  I wonder if Rinpoche really said it in this way, that the bodhisattva really sees 
the bu ddha, or the emanation - or enjoyment body there. Maybe it was in the context of 
that a bodhisattva on the great path of accumulation is attaining to a state called the 
concentration of dharma continuity . In this state, he continuously receives teachings, e ven 
from statues and other images. In this case, he would turn to a statue or thangka and 
receive teachings from it. This is not to say that he hears it from the actual image, but 
that when he turns towards the image, a buddha arrives and gives teachings. He 
doesn‟t receive the teaching from the statue. Also to say that he gets teachings from 
the actual thangka would be incorrect; you can‟t say that the thangka is a buddha.  
 On the basis of one‟s individual accumulation of merit, and on the basis of 
having  gained any of these previously mentioned concentrations, it might be possible 
that a buddha arrives and gives teachings through an individual focusing on an image 
such as a thangka.

that all the buddhas are invited there to abide in the statue?  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
325 GEN:  This is something that one has to imagine, or visualize. It is difficult to say that 
they are actually there or not there. That they would permanently abide there is 
unlikely, and they certainly don‟t teach dharma from abiding in the statue. 
Nevertheless, there is still a blessing coming from the statue.  
 
QUESTION:  In our desk we have some books on top and also in the compartment 
below where there might be some dirty handkerchi efs, etc. Is it suitable to place books 
there?  
GEN:  It is probably not correct to have all kinds of things in the compartment below. 
It would be best to put the texts on top of the desk. There is the habit of storing them 
below, but actually they should on ly be placed on top of the desk, but by putting it in 
the compartment below the desk is at least better than putting them on the ground.  
 In addition, we can see that when the great lamas are coming here they always 
spread a cloth on the table to put the t exts on. They would never just put them on the 
bare table. This is something we should observe. The meaning of this is that when we 
speak about body speech and mind, we have to see the speech as the most important 
aspect.  
 
The quote by Nagarjuna in this se ction refers to exactly this point of showing respect 
to representations of the buddha. This is also the way in which the skilled ones are 
relating to buddha images.    
 
B Examples of the fully ripened effects of irreverence that have occurred in the past  
In the Scripture on Discernment [/Conni: Unterteilung der [Vinaya -]Schriften] it is 
stated that Manavakapila abused members of the sangha who w ere learners and non -
learners in 18 different w ays saying things like: “You elephant heads! How  do you 
know w hat  is Dharma and w hat isn’t?”. Because of that he w as born as a sea 
monster, a species of fish w ith 18 different heads, and remained an animal from the 
time of the teacher Kasyapa to that of the Shakya king [Shakyamuni].  
 
The Scripture on Discernment is the Vinaya -vibangha . The two sangha are the ones in 
training and the ones without training (or the no more learners). The ones with 
training are the ones who did not yet attain the arhat state. The ones without training 
are the ones who are already arhats.  
 
QUESTION:  Why is this example not part of the section about (dis -)respecting the 
sangha jewel?  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 326 – week one to seven  GEN:  This is because the buddha is included in the sangha of no more learners. There 
are many kinds of sangha in this category who don‟t need to learn (or train) anymore; 
for example the bodhisattva on the 8th bhumi and above; because he destroyed all 
afflictions. In addition, you have the Hearer - and Pratyekabuddha arhats in there as 
they have also destroyed their afflictions. Therefore, whoever has destroyed the 
afflictions is included in the sangha of no mor e training.  
A Hearer or Pratyekabuddha on the path of seeing or meditation is a sangha 
who is still training.  He is the sangha jewel because he is an arya being, but within this 
he is of the still training type. When this Hearer or Pratyekabuddha becomes an arhat 
then he becomes the sangha jewel of the type of no more training. Arya bodhisattvas 
from the first to the seventh bhumi are sangha jewel who still train, but from the 8th 
bhumi onwards they belong to the sangha jewel of no more training. Neverthel ess, 
they are not the sangha jewel of no more training of the Mahayana type, because this 
kind of Mahayana sangha jewel without training only refers to the buddha. The point is 
that when we say that someone is a sangha jewel of no more training it refers t o that 
this person who no longer has to make effort to abandon the afflictions, because they 
are abandoned. Therefore, the bodhisattva before the eighth bhumi still needs to train 
to abandon the afflictions, and is therefore still a sangha refuge jewel in training.  
  
Are study and practice the same? In Tibetan, we use the word studying for training the 
body and the speech, as well as for mental training. In our context, as long as you 
haven‟t abandoned the afflictions you still have to learn, study or trai n in order to 
eliminate the afflictions. When these have been abandoned you become a foe destroyer 
and you don‟t have to study, learn or train anymore to abandon the afflictions, even 
though you are still left with the obscurations to knowledge. Concerning  these, you 
then have to continue to train yourself, and these until you have attained the Mahayana 
state of no more learning (which, again, refers to the Mahayana sangha jewel without 
training, which only refers to the buddha). So maybe there is a problem  here with 
different words in Tibetan and English, but the meaning is like this.  
 If we use these terms without this understanding, then someone might think it 
as strange when a term like sangha that do not need any more training is used. We 
need to refer  it to the context of not needing to train anymore in abandoning the 
afflictive or the knowledge obscurations [translators note: one might think it refers to good or 
bad monks, who are doing their studies or not! Which is not the case].  
 Way back Geshe Ten gye went to Paris to give a teaching. The only translator 
they could find was some Tibetan who didn‟t know any dharma. Geshe Tengye said 
that way back in time the Baghavan came to India and taught the Four Noble Truths, 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
327 but since this translator didn‟t hav e a clue about dharma he said there was this man 
called Baghavan and he told the truth four times in this life!  
 But anyway, both of these sangha, the ones of no more training and the ones 
still training are objects of refuge; we fold our hands to them.  
 In Minor Precepts [of the Vinaya] it says that after the teacher Krakucchanda 
had passed into Nirvana, w hen King Carumat had a big stupa built, a hired 
w orkman tw ice scoffed at it saying: “w ho know s when such a huge stupa w ill ever get 
finished”. Later on  when the stupa had been finished nicely he felt sorry, had a golden 
bell made from his w age and tied it to the stupa. Due to that he w as born as “The 
Well-Sounding One” w ith an ugly complexion and a small body but a very pleasant 
voice. Therefore, do not talk about representations saying “this is like that”. It is 
inappropriate to disparage the establishment of a great representation from good 
materials and to dissuade [others]  from it through disruptive remarks.   
 
The minor precepts of the vinaya are the  Vinaya -kshudraka -vastu .  
The buddha Krakucchanda is also a past buddha, I think the one before buddha 
Kasyapa. This story makes me think how some famous sports personalities are great 
performers, but very ugly! The point from these kinds of stories from t he past is that 
we should not discriminate representations of the buddha according to their substance 
or according to being beautiful or not. Therefore, we should not just value  the 
beautiful statues and put them in a high place, and disregard the ones we don‟t find 
beautiful and put them in a low place. We should value them equally.  
 The Great Yogi [J angchub Rinchen] gave Atisha a statue of Manjushri to look 
at and asked: “What is the quality like? If it is good I’ll take it and give the four gold 
coins R ongwa Garge has given me”. { 92}  Atisha said: “There is nothing that is not 
good about the Venerable One’s body. As for the sculptor – average.” Having said 
this, he placed the statue on the crow n of his head.  
 
This talks about a statue that was already fi nished. Therefore, any completed statues 
lama Atisha would treat in this way. This is emphasised in the text because while the 
statue is still being crafted by the artist it is possible and necessary to let the sculptor 
know that he is making a mistake, as  he is even accumulating negativities through his 
mistakes. If we don‟t do this, we ourselves also might accumulate some negativity.

representations of the Dha rma]  
A The actual [precepts]  
You abandon irreverence to as little as four w ords of Dharma. You abandon paw ning 
volumes of scripture, trading them, putting them on the bare ground or in 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 328 – week one to seven  inconvenient places, carrying them around together w ith shoes, steppin g over them and 
so forth. Instead you treat them w ith respect like the Dharma jew el.  
 
It does not refer to four words but actually to four lines (of which a classical verse is 
composed), which are to be seen as dharma and to be treated respectfully.   
 
QUESTION:  In the box for burning dharma there are sometimes envelopes where the 
name of Nalanda or the name of a monk is written. Is it really necessary to burn this 
with the dharma items? And what about CDs and cassette tapes, what should be done 
with the m? 
GEN:  These envelopes can be put into the normal garbage as this is not really dharma. 
They don‟t contain of four lines of dharma text.   
It is very unhealthy to burn CDs or tapes; the smoke from these harms all the 
beings in the whole area. It would be much better to put them in the plastic recycle.  
 
B How the masters of former times acted  
It is said that Geshe Chengawa  used to put his palms together and rise up  w hen he 
saw scriptures [of the Buddha’s teachings] being brought. Later on, w hen he could not 
get up any more, he used to [simply] put his palms together.  
 It is said w hen Atisha came to Ngari, one tantric practitioner did not listen to 
Dharma [teachings] from him. How ever, as one scribe smeared residue from his teeth 
on a Dharma text, Atisha s aw  it and, unable to bear it, said: “alas!  That’s no good, 
that’s no good! ”, the tantrika gained  faith and listened to the teachings .  
 Sharaw a said: “We play around w ith the Dharma in all sorts of ways – 
irreverance for the Dharma and expounders of Dhar ma is a cause for distorted 
w isdom. We are already confused enough now . What are w e going to do if w e get 
more confused?”  
 
This Kadam Geshes says that first of all you should check whether you are showing 
respect towards the dharma or not. Because if you are not, it becomes a cause for your 
wisdom to be distorted.
