2B4B -2B1A -2A4A -1B3 Precepts with regard to the Sangha  
A The actual [precepts]  
You do not abuse or despise the Sangha or even anyone merely bearing the marks of 
ordination. Nor do you look at them as opponents in any way having made [arbitrary] 
divisions in terms of “your group and my group”, treating them w ith respect as if they 
w ere the Sangha J ew el.  
 
Your group and my group : I read this as an insult. For exampl e, I say that this translator 
is a good translator, but someone else says, “Ah, I know this one” (in the sense of him 
having no good qualities).  
In Exhortation to the Special Attitude it says:  
Those w ho w ish for qualities, dw elling in forests  
Should not lo ok for faults in other [ people],  
They should not produce the attitude:  
“I’m especially noble, I’m the best”.  
 
{ 93}  All carelessness grow s  out of this complacence.  
Don’t let contempt for lesser monks arise – 
You w on’t reach liberation for one eon - 
This is  the succession   of this teaching.    [32] 
 
The whole point in this context is that we should respect anyone who even merely has 
the signs of ordination. Such a person wants to proceed on the path of dharma, and as 
a result, we should generate respect towa rds that person.  
Should not look for faults in other people : We should take care that we don‟t have this 
outlook towards other monks here, and reflect that it is not our job here to find the 
faults in some other monk. If we look for faults, we will find t hem and then generate 
pride, a harsh attitude and so forth.  
They should not produce the attitude.. .: This exactly talks about what I explained here. 
You see yourself as high and look down on the faults of others. This state is a mind 
you should not produce .  
All carelessness grows out of this complacence : the word translated here as complacence 
also means being fat or being puffed up. So this disrespect grows out of being full of 
yourself.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 330 – week one to seven  Don’t let contempt for lesser monks arise : here we talk about a gel ong who might have 
good qualities and another gelong of an inferior standing in this regard, meaning that 
he doesn‟t study, has lots of faults, is not very intelligent, etc. Here definitely, 
assuming you are the supreme one, you should completely avoid loo king down on the 
lesser monk because he lacks good qualities.  
You won’t reach liberation for one aeon : This is referring to this supreme gelong, a 
scholar; let‟s say he is so advanced that in a short period of time he would attain 
liberation. If he comes under the sway of this mind of looking down on another, then 
he will not attain liberation for a whole aeon.  
This is the succession of this teaching : This line might be better translated as “He is 
following the teaching in steps”. The point is that this a dvanced gelong could quickly 
attain liberation and the other, lesser skilled one might not be so fast. However, the 
lesser one is by no means an object to be looked down on, because by gradually 
training, he will also attain such a high state and become li berated.  
 
 
TUES DAY 27 APRIL  2010  - SESSION 1 
QUESTIONS  
The Three Jewels are presented as an outstanding object of refuge. This is because 
they are free from all fear and expert in all the methods to free all other sentient beings 
from their fears. With th eir loving and compassionate attitude, they have no 
discrimination between beings, but are impartial. In this way, they free sentient beings 
from their fears in many different ways. Like this, the Three Jewels are fully capable of 
providing refuge, but fro m our side, we need to establish contact with them, in that we 
need to establish trust and a faith of conviction towards them. Without this, no refuge 
will come about. It is not that they are unwilling to help us if we don‟t have this faith, 
but the point is that we aren‟t able to make any contact with them. Therefore, even 
though there are these three fully qualified objects, because we didn‟t make any contact 
we end up with no refuge at all.  
 This is similar to the situation where we are ailing from a str ong sickness. Let‟s 
say there is a skilled doctor who can give a therapy and medicine to rely on, and there 
are also nurses who will help to apply the treatment. However, from our side, we don‟t 
make any contact with any of the three; and therefore we will  not receive any benefit 
from what is available. If from our side we don‟t make the connection with the Three 
Jewels, if we don‟t work to generate this faith of conviction, then no protection will 
come about. So to reflect on the qualities of the Three Jew els, to generate this faith of 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
331 conviction and then to train in the refuge trainings becomes very essential. This is not 
just a side thing. The methods to train in this faith of conviction, and then to abide in 
the trainings of refuge are very important.  
 
By reflecting in this way, we can understand why these objects of refuge are an 
undeceiving refuge. We can think that we have met this excellent path for pacifying 
our mind and disciplining it, and that this is all possible due to the kindness of the 
Three  Jewels. If we engage in this path, through listening, reflecting and meditating, 
then there is no doubt that it can liberate us from all our fears and problems. It is in 
our hands. We are in this fortunate situation of this path being set out for us in te xts 
like the Lam-Rim or the Bodhisattvacharyavatara . All the masters like Lama Tsongkhapa 
composed texts like these, but they didn‟t do this to become famous, but out of the 
sole motivation of a strong feeling of love and concern for us.  
  
B How the masters of former times acted and the benefits [of that]  
Geshe [Drom] Tonpa and the great Yogi, instead of stepping over any piece of yellow  
material on the ground, w ould shake it and carry it to a clean place. You should train 
in accordance w ith their pra ctice, for others  w ill respect you as much as you respect 
the [three] jew els. In the King of Concentration  it says:  
 
What kind of actions you have done,  
that kind of effects you w ill obtain.    [33] 
 
The great Yogi mentioned here is probably Gön Bawa . Bec ause the Buddha‟s robes 
were yellow, these two regarded every piece of yellow cloth as being like the robes of 
the Buddha. They took merely the colour as a reason to show this kind of amazing 
respect, and because of this strong respectful practice, we can be sure that they had 
strong faith and trust in the Three Jewels. By this strong kind of faith of conviction, 
there is no doubt that there will be protection from the side of the Three Jewels.  
   
This brings us to the second point, the common precepts (the  first one having been 
the uncommon ones).  
 
 
 
 
 
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 332 – week one to seven  2B4B -2B1A -2A4A -2  Common precepts [for all three objects of refuge]  
A Remembering the differences  and positive qualities of the three jewels and, due to 
that, going for refuge again and again  
B Remembering their great kindness and, due to that, exerting oneself at all times to 
make offerings to  them and offer them even the first portion of one’s food and drink  
C Remembering their great compassion and, due to that, establishing other migrating 
beings in such  a mode [of behaviour]  
D Whatever you do and whatever you need, worshiping the three jewels, then 
supplicating them and abandoning all other, worldly methods  
E Aware of the benefits, going for refuge three times a day and three times at night  
F Not givi ng up the three jewels for one’s life nor for a laugh and guarding against this  
The second one has six points: Remembering the differences  and positive qualities of 
the three jew els and, due to that, going for refuge again and again, remembering their 
great kindness and, due to that, exerting oneself at all times to make offerings to  them 
and offer them even the first portion of one’s food and drink, remembering their great 
compassion and, due to that, establishing other migrating beings in such a mode [of 
behaviour], w hatever you do and whatever you need, w orshipping the three jew els, 
then supplicating them and abandoning all other, w orldly methods, aw are of the 
benefits, going for refuge three times a day and three times at night, not giving up the 
three jew els for one’s life nor for a laugh and guarding against this.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2A4A -2A Remembering the differences  and positive qualities of the three jewels 
and, due to that, going for refuge again and again  
First: As explained above, you think about the differences betw een things non -
Buddhist and Buddhist as w ell as among the three jew els and [consider] the positive 
qualities of those.  
 
This refers to for example the good qualities of the body, speech and the mind. Within 
the category of the mind we talk ed about the good qualities of exalted knowledge and 
the compassionate affection of the Buddha. All these different points were mentioned, 
and they need to be frequently reflected upon, and on this basis one repeatedly goes 
for refuge.  
 First, we should co me to know about the special features that make up the 
difference between the Three Jewels of the Buddhists, and external teachers and 
teachings. In other words, you need to know what your refuge jewels are. They have 
their respective good qualities, and a lso this is something you should investigate and 
come to know.   
 
2B4B -2B1A -2A4A -2B Remembering their great kindness and, due to that, exerting oneself at all 
times to make offerings to them and offer them even the first portion of one’s food and drink  
 
First, one would be aware here of the Buddha being under the power of this strong 
compassion for all sentient beings, and his wish to clear away all their problems 
without exception. Out of this compassion, he brought forth all the teachings, 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
333 methods and path s to clear away this suffering. All of this came out of this great 
compassion and kindness.  
 Recalling this, we relate to this kindness by wanting to repay it. You might offer 
the first part of whatever you are eating or drinking, and by doing this you ac cumulate 
great waves of merit. It is not that the Three Jewels like us more if we make offerings, 
or they don‟t like us if we don‟t, it is a practice only for the sake of us accumulating 
merit. You remember the kindness of the Three Jewels, and then offer the best part of 
what you eat or drink to them, and in this way you accumulate great masses of merit.  
 
QUESTION : How does it work to offer the first part of every food or drink?  
GEN: Once we have offered the food, then we eat it. We don‟t have to wait for  any 
time after having offered it, but you offer the best part of your food and request 
blessings and then you eat it.  
 
QUESTION : Couldn‟t this be seen as a kind of stealing?   
GEN: No, this is not stealing. The Three Jewels do not have any mind of grasping . 
 
QUESTION : When having something that I want to eat myself, and then I offer it 
thinking that anyway I will eat it myself a second later, isn‟t that selfish?  
GEN:  This is indeed a strong form of self -cherishing but the offering is very excellent!  
 
QUES TION : What is the karmic result of offering a pure land to our guru?  
GEN: It is not necessary to offer a pure land. Offer that which you have, what you 
own. It is better to offer something that exists, rather than something that doesn‟t 
exist, and even tho ugh the pure land exists, it is the Buddha‟s pure land; he owns it.  
 
QUESTION : There is a line in the Mandala offering prayer that talks about offering a 
buddha field, what does that mean?  
GEN: I understand this differently. For me this means to offer it to the buddha field, 
not as a buddha field. Anyway, you are focusing on the buddhas, offer it to them and 
by the merit of this may all sentient beings enjoy a pure land. I find the notion of 
offering a buddha field rather funny! I‟ve now heard this for the  first time.  
 
QUESTION : How does the faith of conviction make this connection to the Three Jewels 
and why is it such a powerful virtue? How does this by itself create virtue and how 
does it protect us?  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 334 – week one to seven  GEN: If there is a very good hospital in Toulouse and  someone who is sick sits here 
and has complete faith that this hospital exists, but he doesn‟t go there, then how will 
he be protected?  
So how could we answer this question?  
 
STUDENT : Faith is the basis for joyous perseverance that will be a cause for ou r 
protection.  
GEN: Yes, this is kind of the direction we have to go in order to answer this question. 
Here we can refer to the presentation of the teachings for calm abiding. The first fault 
is having no interest in attaining calm abiding. This is countera cted by becoming 
familiar with the great benefits of calm abiding, which will lead to a faith of conviction 
towards it; then an aspiration to attain it will come about, which will lead to joyous 
perseverance, from which one will actually attain calm abidin g.  
 Here, in the same way, first one becomes aware of the good qualities of the 
Buddha, as well as understanding the good qualities of the Dharma taught by him and 
of the Sangha following him. One develops this to a point where one generates a faith 
of co nviction towards the Three Jewels, which then leads to the mental factor of 
aspiration to attain to these states. This then leads to joyous perseverance, which then 
leads you to engage into Dharma practice that actually protects you.  
 In the case of Ven. Lekshe for example, since you have a certain conviction in 
the Three Jewels, you became ordained. This already blocks out many possibilities to 
accumulate causes for going to the lower realms. Now with this faith of conviction, 
you will apply yourself in a ny way to the Dharma, and by your practice becoming 
better you will block out any basis for being born in the lower realms, and you can 
even abandon any basis for suffering. In this way you proceed. By you having engaged 
into this Dharma practice, in this way it has protected you from the suffering of the 
lower realms.  
 The manner with which to be protected is through the practice of the dharma. 
This is the only form of protection. If we think about cats for example; if we think 
they need to be protected f rom the lower realms, then this cannot be brought about. If 
it is cold or hungry we can protect them, but with regards to protection from the lower 
realms, no one can bring this about for a cat.  
 None of all the high lamas of the past, such as the master Nagarjuna, Lama 
Tsongkhapa and nowadays His Holiness the Dalai Lama or the Buddha himself were 
able to just take away the suffering or the problems of beings. The only thing they 
were able to do is to teach the path, and encourage us to engage into practic e. This is 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
335 the way to be protected. There is no other path, no lama that will come and do Phowa  
with you. This didn‟t happen in the past and it won‟t happen in the future.  
 Let‟s imagine there is a lama who has the power to just pull you out of samsara. 
Since we have heard that they are impartial in their compassion (one of the four 
characteristics of being a qualified refuge), then why would he have pulled some 
person out of samsara but not everyone else? Reflecting in this way we can see the 
great import ance of the faith of conviction. If you possess it, then this connection is 
made and you will enter into this Dharma practice. Having done this you take on 
certain trainings and by protecting them, guarding what needs to be guarded and 
engaging in what nee ds to be engaged, the causes for the lower realms will be averted.  
 By generating this faith for the Three Jewels, you will be able to engage into 
Dharma practice in stages. You will also generate faith in the presentation of the 
selfless, and then throug h practicing on this basis you will be able to destroy all the 
causes for samsara. Ven. Lekshe for example has family in Argentina. His family 
probably doesn‟t have faith in the Three Jewels, for which reason they didn‟t go for 
refuge, haven‟t become Buddh ists and don‟t engage into Dharma practice. This is 
because they don‟t have this faith of conviction in the Three Jewels. Furthermore, 
because you have this faith, and engage into Dharma practice, it could happen that 
they even create obstacles for you. No t only are they not engaging into Dharma 
practice due to this lack of conviction, but they even create obstacles for people who 
want to engage into Dharma practice. In this case, you would have to take it upon 
yourself to deal with these obstacles, and you  would be able to because you have this 
faith of conviction in the Three Jewels.  
  
Regarding especially all the ordained people here: First someone takes ordination on 
the basis of this great faith, and later as the mind changes, one might think that one is 
incapable of remaining ordained and give back the vows. Regardless of the type of 
ordination, irrespective of the person being a lama or Rinpoche , this happens on the 
basis of the faith of conviction becoming very small, and as a result, no self -respect  or 
embarrassment is present. These two act as a basis for conscientiousness or vigilance, 
which causes you to abide in ethical discipline and therefore one will be able to stay 
ordained. Probably such people who give back their vows don‟t lose all their f aith; they 
will remain Buddhists, but from their original kilos of faith a lot will have been lost. 
Through this, we can understand how this faith is greatly important, with regards to 
the Three Jewels to the presentation of karma and so forth.  
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 336 – week one to seven  QUESTION :  At what point can we say that this faith of conviction has become a stable 
mind, so that it would no longer degenerate?  
GEN: For such a stable mind of faith of conviction, you need to generate the mind of 
definite emergence. Then you wouldn‟t lose it anym ore. In this way, this mind of 
definite emergence becomes very important.  
 An uncontrived mind of definite emergence means that it is without effort, and 
this is what places you on a path. Previously, this mind would have required effort in 
order to gener ate it, but still it would have been clear and definitely decided, seeing 
only drawbacks with samsara and only benefits in liberation. It is still a very powerful 
mind because, for example, you would see only benefits and attractiveness in liberation 
from samsara, and there would no reason for a monk to give back their vows.  
 
QUESTION : What would be the measure of having realised death and impermanence?  
GEN: Everyone realises this because you can see it plainly. If you go to a hospital you 
can see it with your eyes, what better realisation could there be?  
 However, talking about that, one realises that there is a next life that one needs 
to take on and there is only a suffering or happy rebirth as a possibility. If from this 
there is a certain experience co ming forth in your mind, then this would mean that you 
are no longer interested in the happiness or suffering of this life, and you become only 
concerned for the happiness of future lives. This attitude comes from this experience 
arising from the cultivati on of the thought that after death you will not become non -
existent, but will have to take either a happy or suffering rebirth.  
 
QUESTION : How would such an experience be kept, so it is not lost?  
GEN: If you bring forth this kind of experience, then you will not lose it anymore. You 
for example never lose your glasses, because you are constantly wearing them! This is a 
small example.  
 
1 Constant offerings  
 
Second: You should come to understand all the w ell-being you experience as the 
kindness of the three jew els and w orship them by acknow ledging that .  
 
Any wellbeing or happiness you experience you should think of as having come about 
on the basis of the kindness of the Three Jewels. The cause of your pleasant 
experience or good situation is the Three Jewels. Remembering this, the wish arises to 
repay this kindness, and in order to do so you make offerings to the Three Jewels.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
337 This is not referring to firstly offering something to the Three Jewels that  you 
have received through an understanding that it is only through their kindness that you 
have received it. Rather, it refers to that any good situation, like for example being 
here and having encountered such an excellent path, enabling you to deal with  all your 
states of mind, eliminate negativities, accumulate virtues, and abandon what should be 
abandoned. It is through the kindness of the Three Jewels that this situation in which 
you can practice in order to improve your situation and mental continuum  has come 
about. Through being aware of this, a mind wishing to repay this kindness can come 
about, through for example, making offerings.  
  
2 The peculiarity that the collections are completed with little trouble  
Since you constantly need to eat and dri nk, { 94}  you w ill complete a great amount of 
collections with little trouble if you keep offeringxl the first portion of them each time.  
Therefore, you should offer the first portion of w hatever you enjoy, including w ater, 
from the bottom of your heart.  
 
If someone would ask how he could easily accumulate a great deal of merit, then the 
method is presented here. It doesn‟t take a lot of time or effort. By simply offering 
whatever you eat or drink to the Three Jewels you accumulate a huge amount of merit. 
This can be from simple water up to any of the most precious substances. This 
expresses that water is the bare minimum of offering, and of course we can imagine 
the more sophisticated ones. If you offer it from the bottom of your heart, there will 
be a great  accumulation of merit.  
 
3 Impure offerings  
Moreover Sharaw a said: “You don’t need to make [offerings] from the blue [mould] on 
coagulated milk and yellow leaves but from something good. [Worship] comes about 
each time through offering the first portion o f tea, but not through something like 
cleaning out the dust.”  
 
Imagine that you have a flower garden in which you have a row of nice flowers. Rather 
than choose one of the perfectly opened flowers to cut and offer, you choose one of 
the wilted or less perfect ones. This is not a good offering. Other examples would be if 
you have some mouldy cheese that no one wants any more, or rather than offering a 
very good grade of tea you offer one of a lesser quality. This is not correct, and rather 
than accumulating merit from these offerings, you will accumulate negativities. W hat 
we should understand here is that the things we should offer should be of only of the 
most exquisite quality.  
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 338 – week one to seven  4 The manner of respect when offering  
In a sutra it says: For instance, if at the time of planting seeds in a fertile field 
something  remai ns unplanted, it cannot just be left. Likew ise, may the field of merit 
be tilled with the plough of confidence, that sublime field from w hich all future w ell -
being springs and that just needs to be  planted w ith the seed of w ell -being throughout 
all four s easons. If that w hich needs to be done does not occur, it will be an extremely 
heavy loss.  
 
This talks about a field that is not only fertile but also well prepared; therefore, it is the 
best type of field, and whatever you plant in it will grow well. In a  similar way, we 
should plant into the field of faith. If we have strong faith, then all the positive actions 
we did in the past with such a faith in the Buddha and his teachings, will act like a field 
that we have previously planted, and then from this al l happiness and excellent 
situations will grow.  
 Another way to read this example is that someone has this very fertile and well -
prepared field, but he doesn‟t plant anything in it. This would be regarded as a great 
loss. Similarly, we gained the supreme field into which we can sow our virtuous 
activity; we have the best basis for engaging into Dharma practice - in other words, we 
have this field of the precious human rebirth. We should plant the seeds of our 
virtuous activities, and really use this precio us human rebirth. If we don‟t do this, then 
a great opportunity will be lost.  
  
5 The need to exert oneself in making offerings  
Not to have even as much regard for the best of fields as for an ordinary field is not a 
good attitude of ours. Therefore, w e sh ould strive, at all times, to make [reverential] 
offerings to the three jew els.  
 
This means that while being in the presence of an excellent field such as the Three 
Jewels we suffer a great loss as we don‟t make use of it. This is expressed here when it 
says that the field of the object of refuge is excellent, but we treat it in an ordinary way 
and don‟t relate correctly. Maybe we have a thangka with Chenrezig on it, but we don‟t 
relate to it like it really is a representation of Chenrezig. We might just t reat it like 
something nice to hang on the wall. We should really treat this field of the Three 
Jewels as very special, show respect and make offerings to it.  
 
6 High status, paths, etc. are attained through making offerings in that way  
If you do that, th rough the pow er of the roots of virtue grow ing in the most excellent 
field, the pow er of your intelligence concerning the stages of the path increases. When 
your intelligence is very feeble such that you do not retain the w ords w hile listening, do 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
339 not unde rstand the meaning w hile contemplating, and nothing arises in the mind 
during meditation, the quintessential instruction is to rely on the pow er of the field.  
 
When it says here in the headline High status, paths, etc. , then what is actually meant by 
othe r paths is the path of definite goodness.  
 Once again, the three objects are expressed here as the holy field for sowing the 
roots of virtue. We bring forth these roots, and then due to the power of this we will 
be able to bring forth a strength of mind fo r the different stages of the path. Here it 
talks about situations where we listen to the Dharma or sit down to reflect, but we 
can‟t keep in mind what is taught or come to an understanding even though we try. In 
these kinds of states where the mind lacks the capacity or is in a degenerate state, then 
one is advised to turn to this special field of the Three Jewels and generate a special 
strength of mind by making offerings and showing veneration. We use the field of 
refuge to accumulate merit and purify ob scurations. Once this has happened we can 
then also really understand the text.  
 
7 How the size of offerings and so forth is a distinction to do with the mind  
Also the offering is not confined by the thing [ offered], it is confined by your 
confidence. As it is stated [in the scriptures], if you have faith, it is enough to offer 
mandalas, water, and offerings that are not considered by anyone [to be their 
property], therefore you should act accordingly even if you do not have any material 
things [to offer].   { 95}  Not to be able to give, even though you have something, saying: 
“I am a w retched pauper w ithout any merits, I do not have any other possessions to 
offer”xli  – in the w ords of Potow a, that is like a blind person putting some cheap 
aromatic plant into a smelly conch -shell container and fooling someone w ith eye -sight 
by calling it sandalw ood and camphor perfume.  
 
Here someone might say, “I don‟t have anything to offer, and also don‟t have the 
means to acquire it, so I can‟t practice generosity.” Neverth eless, here it is made clear 
that the power of the offering is not so much about the size or price but about the 
faith with which you offer. Therefore, if someone makes many offerings of the best 
substance but without faith, then not much will come from th is offering. However, if 
someone just offers something imagined, some water or a Mandala, but does so with 
strong faith, then strong positive results will come about.  
 For example, if you offer the Mandala without anything in your hand, but you 
do this wi th strong faith in the Three Jewels, then great merit will come about. In 
general, this means that anybody can engage into the practice of generosity, even 
people who don‟t own substances as they can make imaginary offerings or offerings of 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 340 – week one to seven  un-owned object s. Shantideva explains this in the second chapter of his 
Bodhisattvacharyavatara . 
 Someone might also say that because he was miserly in pervious lives he now 
has nothing to offer, so what should he do? So here, the advice could be to offer water 
of some q uality, mineral water for example. If you only drink mineral water from the 
bottle, but then offer water from the tap, then this is strange. However, if you offer 
the water first, which then you yourself will drink, then this is a good form of offering. 
Therefore, even someone who has no resources need not worry as they too can make 
offerings in this way.  
The meaning of the quote is that in the beginning one might offer a single stick 
of incense because one can‟t afford more. Later, one might be able to of fer two sticks, 
or even a whole bundle at some point. The point is that you make effort in whatever 
way you can offer.  
 
TUESDAY 27 APRIL 2010  - SESSION 2 
 
8 How to train in upright[ness with regard to] offering substances, following the masters 
of former times  
Puchungw a said: “At first I used to offer a pungent aromatic plant. Then I had the 
means to offer sw eet incense prepared from four [different] substanc es. Now  I offer the 
most exquisitely fragrant aloe w ood and durushka etc.” In accordance with this 
statement, if you do not give anything out of contempt for small offerings, it w ill go on 
like that throughout your entire life. How ever, if you develop pers everance from a 
lesser stage, it w ill get better and better. Therefore you should train follow ing his 
practice. It is said that he once prepared incense [w orth] 22 gold coins.  
 
The point made here is that one should not despise small things thinking, “Why  should 
I offer some tea or a piece of fruit?” Thinking in this way one wastes a lot of 
opportunities for offerings; you waste your life. You can start with however small an 
offering may be.  
 Again, the point is that there is a difference in willingly eng aging into the 
practice of offering, seizing every opportunity for it, or lacking this mind. If you are so 
disposed, then even if you have great riches, because of not thinking about offerings, 
then it is almost like you don‟t have any riches. You should b ring forth a mind that 
very voluntarily engages into making offerings on the basis of great faith. By 
cultivating such a mind, everything and every situation can become an opportunity for 
accumulating merit.  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
341 WEDNES DAY 28 APRIL  2010  - SESSION 1 
 
9 Refutat ion of the manners of speaking [to the effect] that the way Bodhisattvas make 
offerings, having gained control over material  things, is impure  
If great bodhisattvas, having gained control over material things, emanate many 
hundreds of thousands of bodies and for each body, again, emanate hundreds of 
thousands of hands etc. and [if they] appear in all the fields making offerings  to the 
J inas for many eons, the statement by those w ho know  only  the superficial appearance 
of positive qualities that “As for m e, I do not hope [to reach] enlightenment that w ay,” 
– is the idle talk of [people] w ho have very little familiarity w ith the Dharma.   
 
Someone who is satisfied with only a little virtue or a similarity to it says, “I can‟t hope 
to get enlightenment like this”. This person doesn‟t have any idea about the Dharma, 
they are not capable of understanding the practice of the bodhisattvas, and their small 
minds can‟t even perceive the vast affection of the bodhisattvas. That they make such 
a statement shows they have no familiarity with Dharma.  
 In this context, we can also think of the prayer we recite during dedications:  
Just as the brave Manjushri and Samantabhadra too,  
Realized things as they are,  
Also I dedicate all these merits in the best way,  
That I ma y follow their perfect example.  
 
This refers to the eight close bodhisattvas ( Mahabodhisattvas ), who were particularly 
close to Buddha Shakyamuni. The first of these is Manjushri, and the last is 
Samantabhadra. All eight are thus included in this first lin e. The practice of these great 
bodhisattvas is to dedicate in a vast way due to their minds of great insight and 
compassion. We cannot perceive the way of making offerings of those on the high 
grounds, so we just make reference to the practice. We cannot g rasp it, so we refer to 
it so we can make a similar dedication of merits.  
 In the offerings chapter of The B odhisattvacharyavatara  there is reference to 
oneself making offerings in the same way as the bodhisattvas Manjushri and so on 
made offerings. Again , this is beyond what we can grasp, but still we can refer to this 
kind of offering by saying, “May I also be able to do this kind of offering” – this is 
called unsurpassed offerings. In our text, we are talking about unsurpassed dedication; 
dedicating lik e the bodhisattvas on the high grounds.  
 
Having control over material things  is showing they are on the high grounds. They do this 
by emanating bodies with many hands and going to many lands etc. Someone then has 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 342 – week one to seven  a doubt because they can‟t even grasp this vast activity, and from not understanding 
this says, “I‟m not going to practice like that.” So that point is refuted here.  
 
QUESTION : It sounds like some of their bodies stay there all the time and do nothing 
but make offerings. Is this is what is meant here?  
GEN: No, it doesn‟t mean they stay there all the time. This means the bodhisattvas 
would emanate many bodies and engage into all kinds of activity to bring about the 
accumulations, merit especially. Here it describes the aspect of how you make 
offeri ngs, but in general, these bodies are used for all kinds of activity to accumulate 
merit.  
 One needs to do these practices with an attitude that is as vast as possible, as 
the power of the practice depends on the power of the mind. Practicing Dharma is no t 
about something just physical, but depends on one‟s attitude. By these states, the body 
and speech are put to use through the mind ordering them to act in a certain way. 
Making offerings is not so much having a lot of possessions or riches, but mainly 
about if one can do these practices with an excellent mind that wants to benefit others, 
with compassion and affection. If this mind is present, you can accumulate merits even 
if you don‟t have much material things to offer. In comparison to the merit coming  
from making offerings to all the buddhas in the different fields in a vast way, just a 
single moment of love or compassion has far more effect. This shows how Dharma 
practice depends on the mind.   
 
10 How to make offerings with other vast  things  
In the  J ew el Cloud [Sutra]  it says: “From the sutra collections you should study w hat 
are very vast offerings and honours and from the bottom of your heart dedicate them 
w ith the most excellent special  attitude  to the buddhas and bodhisattvas.” You 
should do i t like that.   
 
Sutra collections  means all the sutras in the Kangyur, so all the sutras. If you are into 
making vast offerings of a trans -worldly type, you should first study what the sutras 
say, and by understanding this, you can engage into making such v ast offerings.   
 From the bottom of your heart  means that one offers in a sincere way. You should 
not do this kind of offering to accumulate merit for a long life for oneself or to gain 
good outer conditions in this life. Rather, we should make the offeri ng for all sentient 
beings - a mind that wants to place them all in the bliss of non -abiding nirvana. The 
most excellent special attitude  is exactly that. Even the offering of just a single flower can 
be very meaningful. If you offer this for your own sake , only a little merit will come 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
343 about. If you offer for the sake of all limitless sentient beings, it‟s like all the limitless 
sentient beings are making the offering, then a vast amount of merit can come about.  
      
2B4B -2B1A -2A4A -2C Remembering their g reat compassion and, due to that, establishing other 
beings in such a mode [of behaviour]  
Third: Out of loving concern you establish other sentient beings in going for refuge as 
much as you can.  
 
As we say that we are Dharma practitioners, and especially  Mahayana Dharma 
practitioners, then we should cultivate the very root of the Mahayana of loving 
affection and great compassion. To recollect or be mindful of great compassion means 
to generate it. You sit down and try to cultivate this compassion, bringin g this forth as 
a strong motivation in your continuum. In this way, you also try to establish other 
migrators in this mode of going for refuge.  
In the previous training, it was said that we recollect the great kindness of the 
Three Jewels, and then make o fferings on this basis through which we accumulate 
great merits. Here, by having generated this great compassion for all sentient beings, 
we try to establish all of them in a similar practice so they can also accumulate these 
merits.  
 Here there is a refer ence to “establish by great affection.” This means that first 
you generate a mind such as great compassion (of the bodhimind type) which is 
including all sentient beings without exception. It‟s like your mind of compassion is a 
house and you pack all senti ent beings in and none are left outside. Motivated by this 
great compassion, you go for refuge for the sake of all sentient beings. With this mind, 
you are aiming at establishing them all in the state of bliss beyond peace, and you 
engage into virtuous act ivities with this non -exclusive and limitless affection.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2A4A -2D Whatever you do and whatever you need, worshiping the three jewels and 
supplicating them, abandoning all other, worldly methods  
{ 96}  Whatever you do and w hatever requirements you see, rely on the three jew els and 
do what is in agreement w ith them such as making offerings to them. How ever, it is 
inappropriate to rely in every w ay on that w hich is not in agreement w ith them in 
every aspect such as Bon. Consequently, you should trust the three jew els at all times.  
 
This refers to first going for refuge, making requests and offerings before beginning in 
major tasks or projects you want to start, in order to gain protection or support from 
the Three Jewels rather than going for refuge to  some worldly method. This comes 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 344 – week one to seven  down to looking for support and protection from the Three Jewels and not from some 
worldly god etc.  
 Here we are talking about tasks and projects that are of importance, rather than 
all the small actions you do. You would make supplications to the Three Jewels by 
making offerings or venerating them and by relying on that, you benefit your project, 
and you would rely on the Three Jewels in accordance with the Three Jewels, meaning 
not relying on those who are not the Three J ewels. In our text, it gives the example of 
not relying on the Bön religion.  
   
2B4B -2B1A -2A4A -2E Aware of the benefits, going for refuge three times a day and three times 
at night  
1 It is measure of being a Buddhist  
2 It is the basis of all vows  
3 The karmic obscurations accumulated previously are reduced and exhausted  
4 One accumulates vast merit  
5 One will not fall into the bad migrations  
6 One will not be harmed by human and non -human obstacles  
7 One will attain everything one wishes for  
8 One will s wiftly attain buddhahood  
 
The fifth one has eight benefits: It is the measure  of being a Buddhist, it is the basis 
of all vow s, the karmic obscurations accumulated previously are reduced and 
exhausted, one accumulates vast merit, one w ill not fall into  b ad migrations, one w ill 
not be harmed by human and non -human obstacles, one w ill attain everything one 
w ishes for, and one w ill sw iftly attain the state of buddhahood.  
 
Here we are encouraged to go for refuge six times in six sessions. This of course 
bring s us to the practice of the six -session guru yoga where you indeed have this 
practice of going for refuge, this is the commitment mentioned here.  
 This is a presentation of the benefits of going for refuge, which we should be 
aware of. There are eight men tioned here:  
1) If you have generated well a faith of conviction in the Three Jewels, then you have 
become a Buddhist.  
2) Then you can receive vows such as the pratimoksha vows, bodhisattva vows etc., 
because you have generated such a mind of faith of co nviction. This means you have 
become a suitable basis for vows.  
3) Whatever negative karma you have already accumulated, that would normally lead to 
unwished for results, becomes purified. This doesn‟t mean that in the moment you 
take refuge all the negati vity is destroyed, but it means that on the one hand it gets less 
and less from the daily mind of refuge, and  
4) on the other hand you accumulate merit.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
345 5) Leading on from this, you will not take rebirth in the lower realms due to the causes 
no longer be ing there, and the causes for higher rebirth being present.  
6) There will be no harm inflicted from human or non -humans beings. Many people in 
the world refer to things like people doing black magic on them or feeling as if their 
life force has been snatc hed away by some other being or some force. This has a 
strong association with one‟s over -conceptualisation. Nevertheless, when you have this 
strong faith of conviction in the Three Jewels, a strong protection comes about, like a 
bodyguard protecting you f rom the fear of these types of interferences.  
7) When you have this faith of conviction, then whatever you are thinking of or aiming 
for, you‟ll be able to achieve it. This is not talking about having success with negative 
behaviour, but talking about suc cess with positive activities that are in accord with the 
Dharma.  
8) On this basis, one will quickly become enlightened.  
 
1 It is the measure  of being a Buddhist  
First: Although, generally speaking, there appear to be many w ays of positing [w hat 
constitut es] non -Buddhists and a Buddhists, it is w idely know n that J ow o [Atisha] and 
Shantipa distinguished them by [the fact of] going for refuge. Therefore, Buddhists 
should be understood as those having achieved going for refuge and not having given 
up on it. H ence, as for the measure of being a Buddhist, it is necessary to consider the 
three jew els as teacher etc. from the bottom of one’s heart. Without that, w hatever 
virtuous deeds y ou do, y ou are not included among Buddhists.  
 
There are different ways of pre senting someone as being a Buddhist, being an inner 
being. One mode of positing a Buddhist could be based on their view, or another way 
by the behaviour they try to bring forth. In this case, we are looking at how Jowo 
Atisha and the Pandita Shantipa make this distinction, which is made by a person 
having gone for refuge or not.  
 The difference between a Buddhist and a non -Buddhist is often addressed by 
His Holiness the Dalai Lama, especially with regard to their view. View means the view 
they hold of the self. Firstly, there is a difference with regard to the very nature of the 
self, then from how they relate to the initial moment of the self, and then from the 
other extreme, the final moment of the self.  
 For example, regarding the nature of the self (or  entity of the self), Buddhists 
would hold it is completely impermanent. It is also described as being merely imputed 
on the basis of imputation, which is the respective five aggregates. Meaning that if you 
look for the self, you will not be able to find i t, therefore it is not inherently existent. 
On the other hand, the non -Buddhist systems of any type would all hold to the view 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 346 – week one to seven  that the self is permanent, unitary and independent. This is then a clear difference 
between Buddhists and non -Buddhists with reg ard to the nature of the self.  
 In regard to view of the initial moment (extreme moment in the past), Buddhists 
would say it can‟t be found. There is no first moment. This is true for all kinds of 
things, like ourselves - we have a body and a mind. Both o f these have their respective 
substantial causes. The body comes from the two substantial causes of the father and 
mother, whereas the mind comes from the previous moment of mind. The body and 
mind are therefore caused by these substantial causes as well a s having their own 
substantial causes. The father and mother have their own substantial causes, which is 
their respective father and mother, and so on back through the generations. In this line 
of causes bringing about certain effects, there is no first mo ment. So even for just the 
material line of causation, there is no beginning to be found. With regard to the mind, 
this comes into one‟s body at the beginning of this life, but as it is also a causative 
phenomenon, it comes from the previous moment of mind . This cause is therefore in a 
previous life, and this came from the life before that and so on, so one can‟t find the 
initial cause or first moment for the mind. In general, we have to understand that the 
mind is impermanent, comes about through causes, a nd is therefore constantly 
changing through a moment being caused by its previous moment, and then the 
present moment is a cause for the next moment. This is true for all caused 
phenomenon. For example, when one compares any caused phenomena with their 
previous moments, there is always a certain change. At one moment, this table was 
completely new, then by changing in every moment it becomes an old object. 
Therefore, we can say that this aging is happening all the time, rather than suddenly 
coming about. Th e same is true for ourselves, as we can see that after living so many 
years, we start to get old, whereas when we were born we had this new, fresh body. In 
addition, when we were born we didn‟t know anything, but now we have a certain 
amount of knowledge. This is a sign that the mind has been trained over time. We 
started by knowing nothing, but by studying over time, we gradually acquire this 
knowledge. These are signs that all these phenomena are constantly changing.  
 Our mind that is now under the power  of the afflictions, but it can be trained 
and the afflictions can be counteracted. They can weakened and progressively 
eliminated until more and more the mind can become excellent. This is also because it 
can change. In the same way, if we let the mind go  randomly under the power of the 
afflictions, then the mind will get progressively worse and degenerate, which comes 
about because the mind is changeable. Therefore, the mind is in no way permanent. If 
it was, then the mind would stay exactly as it is now,  it couldn‟t move, and it couldn‟t 
change. So now that we have heard many proofs to show the mind is not permanent, 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
347 and it can change, we can therefore deal with the afflictions and become completely 
liberated from them.  
 For example, if we repeatedly address a person who is so full of anger at even 
the smallest thing with the disadvantages of anger, the advantages of patience, and the 
good qualities of minds like love and compassion, even this person can change. This is 
because the mind is not permanent. By the mind meeting the right causes and 
conditions, there is an influence and there is a gradual change. At one point, this 
person can even be completely free of anger. Negative behaviour can be changed, if we 
meet the cau ses and conditions that pacify this behaviour.  
We came to this place to change our mind. If we keep remembering this 
motivation, then we engage into all these practices of the Buddha in order for the 
mind can change. No matter how rough the mind is or how  many afflictions it has 
right now, it can be changed by repeatedly meeting the causes and conditions. If 
someone says, “I‟m going to do something different, I‟m going to bring about the 
accumulations,” and does all kind of other practices, then just by th ese practise alone a 
change will not come about. Rather, the change comes about by bringing forth the 
causes and conditions that counteract our negative minds.  
 If we are not counteracting these negative minds and just let the mind go, like 
constantly loo king for faults in others and so on, then all kinds of over -
conceptualisation will happen, negative minds will come about and we‟ll be very 
unhappy. Even if we‟re alone, for example in the mountains, if we haven‟t 
counteracted these negative minds, we‟ll s till be unhappy. We should constantly hold 
onto the idea that we have come here to pacify these negative minds, no to see what 
faults other‟s have. We came here learn the Dharma and to pacify the mind. Keeping 
this initial motivation awake, we can work wit h our minds on a daily basis. This is not 
easy. We came here in order to pacify the mind. We didn‟t come here with a mind that‟s 
already disciplined, so it‟s possible that problems will arise as we work because just as 
we come here to work, so have others come here with an undisciplined mind and the 
wish to change it. If this is the case, then we have to understand that we all have these 
undisciplined minds, generate some patience, and then we can all live together 
harmoniously.  
 When we talk about the dis tinction between a Buddhist and a non -Buddhist, the 
basic practice and attitude of all the religions systems is the same. In the majority of 
religions, the practice is to abandon harm to others. In the majority of religions, there 
is the practice of benefi ting others. Then the question arises, “What are the means for 
abandoning harm and benefiting others?” We then see many methods and differences 
in these systems.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 348 – week one to seven   These many methods show that they all agree violence has to be abandoned and 
a beneficial mi nd is to be adopted. Whilst this is the case, it refers to this self, the “I” - 
“I” need to give up violence; “I” need to generate a mind of benefit. Buddhists would 
say I want to bring this “I” to a state beyond sorrow, in other words to nirvana. We 
want join it to happiness and separate it from suffering and any harmful activity. We 
perceive this self as needing love and benefit from others, and having the capacity to 
give love and benefit to others.  
 In the context of refuge, there is a difference betwe en Buddhists and non -
Buddhists in terms of the view - about the nature of the self. The Buddhist view is that 
the self has arisen in relation to cause and effect, and is therefore a dependent arising. 
This “I” being a dependent arising is only held by Budd hists and is not found in other 
systems.  
 
2 It is the basis of all vows  
Second: In the Commentary on the Treasury  it says:  
Those w ho go for refuge have gained access to taking all the 
vow s correctly.      [34] 
 
and in Seventy Verses on Going for Refuge  it also say s:  
Upasakas go to the three for refuge - 
That’s the root of the eight vow s.    [35] 
 
the intention being that through going for refuge the thought of passing beyond 
sufferingxlii is stabilized and that the vow s spring from that.  
 
If you have this faith of conviction in the Three Jewels, meaning you go for refuge in 
the Three Jewels, then you become a basis for receiving vows. From this, you can gain 
good qualities and evolve to states such as nirvana. Alternatively, we could say that by 
holding the  view that the self is a dependent arising means we can change. This is then 
a good basis for taking vows that can transform our state of mind.

 There is a quote here from Vasubhandu‟s  Commentary on the Abhidharmakosh a, and 
from the Seventy Verses on  Going for Refuge  by Nagarjuna. Upasakas  go for refuge in the 
Three Jewels, which becomes the root for the eight vows. We need here a mind that is 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
349 aiming for a state beyond sorrow, which means a mind that is aiming to be fr ee from 
the afflictions that accumulate negative karmic imprints, from which suffering 
experiences arise. Someone intent on going beyond sorrow wants to be free of all this. 
Such a mind is brought about by going for refuge, and if this has become stable, a ll the 
vows can be established. In the first quote from the Treasury , it says that going for 
refuge becomes the excellent door for taking all the different vows perfectly.  
 
  
3 The obscurations accumulated previously are reduced and exhausted  
Third: In the Compendium of Trainings  it says:  
Here, in the context of show ing that negativities are purified 
by going for refuge, the story of the pig should be presented as 
illustration.    [36] 
 
Shantideva is quoted here. When it says that negatives are  purified  it means purification 
as mentioned in the headline, by reducing and exhausting the negativities. Shantideva 
says this point is to be illustrated by telling this story. The story is actually quite long, 
but here it is abbreviated. There was a god called Devaputra (son of a god) and when 
he came to die, he saw he would be born in the animal realm as a pig. With this fear, 
he went to refuge and by the power of this, he didn‟t go to the lower realm.  
 
For instance, a son of a god w ho was going to be re born as a pig, { 97}  through going 
for refuge w as not born as one. It is stated that:  
Those gone for refuge to the Buddha,  
Will not go to the bad migrations.  
They give up their human body  
and obtain that of a god.     
 
This quote also express that by going for refuge you avoid lower rebirths, just like 
Devaputra, but not only that, you gain rebirth in a god realm. For a human being he 
would give up the human realm and go to the god realm.  
 
It has been stated similarly w ith respect to the Dharma and Sangha.  
 
4 One accumulates vast merit  
Fourth: In the Summary of the Perfections  it says:  
If the merit of refuge took shape  
These three realms w ould even be small as a container.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 350 – week one to seven  Great oceans, foremost of w ater bodies  
Could not hold or measure it.     
 
That is how  [vast] it is.  
 
The point here is that the merit from going for refuge is a limitless, huge amount.  
 
QUESTION : Is this story saying that just by this one instance of going for refuge you 
are saved from the lower realms, without having guarded it for a long  time, but just in 
the last moment?  
GEN: To answer this I‟ll have the tell the story in more detail. This was a god who 
lived in the Heaven of the Thirty -Three11. They have a certain type of clairvoyance and 
can see when and where they are going to die. Fo r example, if they are going to die in 
10 years they would know this and where they will go. This person being utterly 
terrified from seeing his rebirth as a pig explored many methods to avert it. Love, 
maybe compassion he thought, then another god gave hi m advice. This other god said 
that the best method was to go for refuge. He then practiced refuge, guarded the 
trainings and so on for these 10 years, the effect of which was that he was reborn in 
Tushita heaven and not in the animal realm. He died from on e god realm, and was 
born in another from having gone for refuge and practiced for a period of time. Ten 
years is only my example. A god‟s lifespan is incomparably long compared to a human 
life span.  
 Here we can think of gods who enter a medium and speak  through them, like 
the Nechung Oracle. The god, Nechung, is entering the monk and speaking through 
him. He was around at least 500 years ago and is still around today. We don‟t know for 
how long he was around before this, so we can see he has a very long lifespan.  
 Arya Asanga encountered the Buddha Maitreya directly in the god realms where 
he received and brought back five texts. When he returned, fifty human years had 
passed, but only one morning had passed in the god realm.  
 
STUDENT : Did Asanga experi ence it for 50 years or just for that morning?  
GEN: Who knows? He would have experienced it as quite long because he was 
studying and memorising all these texts. If I have to memorise even a few pages it 
takes a long time! Even though Asanga was aware it was a long time for a human, it 
was only a morning in  the god realm. So please make some effort that you will be born 
in Tushita in the future, and then you can experience it for yourself!

karma, especially the negative karma to be born as a pig in the animal realm. Then his 
friend, due to his powers, saw Devaputra had been born in Tushita and was very 
surprised, so he also took refuge.

not a god. The Nechung is a god and not a Buddha. The monk is also not a Buddha, so

joy at having caught a fish . They think they have done something amazing like having 
won a lot of money. Through being aware that animals also have feelings, and only 
want happiness and don‟t want suffering, we can relate to them as living sentient 
beings and include them into our p ractice. It is very important to know about the 
existence of the different life forms in the six realms.

7 One will attain everything one wishes for
