                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 354 – week one to seven  Before you begin a Dharma activity, you should make offerings to the Three Jewels 
and then go for refuge, make requests and so on. In this way, you will easily attain 
what you are a iming for. This refers to preparatory practices before you do a session or 
some other Dharma practice, such as cleaning the room, nicely presenting 
representations of the Three Jewels, and offerings. Then you sit down and the first 
thing you do is go for r efuge. By following this sequence, everything you aim for will 
be achieved.  
  
8 One will swiftly attain buddhahood  
In [the Sutra] Requested by Senge  it says:  
Through faith unfreedom is abandoned.   [37] 
 
This unfreedom  is abandoned , or lack of leisure bei ng abandoned, refers to a state of 
having the freedoms and endowments. By having refuge, the mind of faith of 
confidence, you will attain the state of freedoms and endowments that is a precious 
human rebirth. By having this, you‟ll encounter the Dharma tea chings and all the 
conditions for practicing the Dharma. Then you can actually engage into the Dharma 
practice, and it will not take long until you become enlightened.  
 
In accordance with that you obtain distinguished leisure and, having encountered 
refuge, train in the special paths, whereby you w ill become a buddha w ithout long 
delay. Mindful of these kinds of benefits you should go for refuge three times a day and 
three times at night.  
 
By thinking of these benefits, you should go for refuge three times in the morning and 
three times in the evening; again, this refers to the six sessions.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2A4A -2F Not giving up the three jewels for one’s life nor for a laugh and guarding 
against this  
There is no doubt that w e will be separated from this l ife, body, and possessions. If w e 
give up the three jew els for their sake, there w ill be continuous suffering in all 
successive lives [to come], therefore w e should vow not to abandon [our] refuge, come 
w hat may, and not even utter w ords about  giving up [ our]  refuge for a laugh.  
 
Even at the risk of our life we should not let go of the Three Jewels, but guard them 
and the refuge trainings. You should not even jokingly let go of them, but protect 
them.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
355  These aspects of the body, life and resources – there  is no doubt we‟ll have to 
separate from all of them. So we should never give up the refuge Jewels for the sake of 
the body and so on because this situation will definitely change, whereas we constantly 
need the protection of the Three Jewels. If we do giv e up refuge in the Three Jewels 
for the body, resources etc., there will be continuous suffering , not only in this life but 
also in future lives.  
 We should then make a strong decision, “Whatever happens, I will never give up 
the refuge.” This state of mi nd thinks, “In happy times I will not give up my refuge, in 
the unhappy times I will not give up the refuge, not in any situation, not even as a 
joke.”  
 
STUDENT : These six sessions, do we really have to do three different times in the day 
and then three a t night? Can we do all six at once, or three in the morning and three in 
the afternoon?  
GEN:  It‟s ok to do all six in one session, or three in the morning or three at night. The 
best is to do it in six sessions, so after every four hours. In the night, th is might be 
difficult, so you do it as you can.  
  
2B4B -2B1A -2A4B  Eliminating inaccuracies [with respect to the precepts of going for refuge]  
1 Refutation of that which is not a precept  
Former masters stated a precept to the effect  that you should train in going for refuge 
to the Tathatgata of w hatever direction you go in, but I have not seen a source [for 
that].  
 
This does not mean that there are buddhas in the four cardinal directions and you 
prostrate to the respective one depending on which direction  you go. It means that 
whatever direction you go, like going to see the stupa, you make reverence to the 
Buddha at the stupa. When you go to the gompa, this is your direction, so you make 
prostrations to the Buddha in the gompa. Here Lama Tsongkhapa says t here is an oral 
tradition that you prostrate to the Buddha in the direction in which you are going, but 
he has not seen a source for that. This very instruction is in the commentary of the 
Vinaya . The instruction is there, but there is no special explanati on why one should do 
this. Not seen a source  means Lama Tsongkhapa has not seen a reason for this, but this 
is a precept to be trained in.  
 
QUESTION : Is it that we should prostrate to the stupa or is the training that we should 
go for refuge to the stupa?  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 356 – week one to seven  GEN: It is good to do both. Prostration is the physical way to go for refuge, reciting 
the refuge formula is going for refuge on a verbal basis, and then mentally you 
generate the mind that you want to go for refuge for the sake of all sentient beings, in 
order  to be free from your own fear and to liberate others. You say, “Until I‟m 
completely enlightened, you are my sole object of trust, where I place my mind.” In 
this way, you practice three ways of going for refuge.  
 
2 The sources for the specific precepts  
Thus the six common precepts [for all the objects of refuge] are just like they appear in 
the Commentary on the Lamp on the Path . { 98}  The first three specific precepts are 
explained in the sutra collection, w hereas the latter three appear in the Six Branc hes 
of Going for Refuge . 
 
This mentions the different precepts. We have discussed many here in the last few 
days. Six are common and six are specific. Some trainings are in regard to the Buddha, 
some in regard to the Dharma and some in regard to the sangha – these are the 
specific trainings. Common means precepts that are in connection with all Three 
Jewels.  
 
3 The difference between degenerating and relinquishing [the precepts]  
As for the w ay transgression against these precepts becomes a cause of degenerating 
and relinquishing th em, transgression of the rule not to give up your refuge even for the 
sake of your life actually constitutes relinquishing it. Similarly, even if you do not give 
up the three jew els, but dualistically uphold the three – teachers and so forth – that 
are inc ompatible w ith them, you trangress the precept of not advocating another 
refuge. By not having confidence in the refuge you also relinquish it. I think that, if 
these do not occur, it is a mere transgression of the precepts but not a cause for 
relinquishin g them.  
 
Someone says that when you go against these advices, there is degeneration that 
becomes a cause for giving up refuge. How does this actually work? Earlier we were 
talking about the pledge of, “Even for the sake of my life I will not give up the 
refuge.” If we do opposite to this, give up refuge to save your life, then the refuge has 
been lost.  
 Then there is a case where someone is not really letting go of the Three Jewels, 
but he is taking on a second refuge at the same time. He holds onto a diff erent teacher, 
teachings and a community that is not harmonious with the Three Jewels. This goes 
against the precept of not propounding others. This mind of not entrusting oneself 
would mean you have also lost the refuge. For example, if someone says, “I‟m  a 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
357 Buddhist and go for refuge in the Buddha, and also I‟m a Christian and go for refuge 
in Christ.” This is a sign that you do not trust the Buddha, Dharma and Sangha from 
the bottom of your heart. There not being a deep felt conviction means there is no 
refuge. Holding these two at the same time is a sign that you have lost the refuge.  
If none of these two come about, then by a mere action which is opposite to the 
other instructions, this does not become a cause for losing refuge. For example, the 
instruc tion connected with the Dharma Jewel, to give up harm towards others. Let‟s 
say I have this training and at one moment I have anger towards Ven. Tharchin. I 
would not lose the refuge because I still have deep faith in the Three Jewels. There is 
some kind o f degeneration, but I would not have lost the refuge.  
 
STUDENT : Do you go first go for refuge, then request, or the other way around?  
GEN: What kind of request?  
STUDENT : On page 77of the text, it says the word supplication, like prayers for your 
practice,  to be beneficial to others, things like this.  
GEN: There is a small difference between requesting and making aspirational prayers. 
Requesting would come together with refuge by thinking, “Please liberate me from all 
fears. Please support me.” These are r equests, asking for something. The aspirational 
prayers are thoughts like, “May this happen, and may this happen” and so on. We can 
also talk about dedication - with your roots of virtue, you dedicate them to a certain 
attainment or project.  
 This request  comes down to taking refuge at the same time. The request is, 
“please liberate me from all my fears.” Or more completely saying, “Teacher, please 
protect me and all sentient beings from all our fears.” This phrase would be going for 
refuge and requesting at the same time. The aspirational prayer would be, “May all 
sentient beings be free from their sufferings.” Dedication would be to say, “By my 
roots of virtue, may all sentient beings be completely free from all their fear and 
suffering.”  
 You can reques t a lama by saying, “Please give teachings” or “Please have a 
stable long life.” These are requests and not going for refuge. However, saying, “Please 
teach these points to elucidate the Dharma so the ignorance which causes our 
problems is dispelled. Pleas e protect me in this way.” This would be a request in the 
form of refuge.  
 
4 The advice not to transgress the precepts because of how great the benefits are: being 
the entrance gate to the teachings etc.  
Thus going for refuge is the great entry gate to th e Buddha’s teachings. If your going 
for refuge does not consist in mere w ords, y ou w ill be relying on a superior  pow er.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 358 – week one to seven   
Make sure your refuge is genuine, and not just mere words.  
 
Therefore you w ill not be thwarted by external or internal obstacles, an d the various 
qualities w ill come about easily and hardly deteriorate, w hereby they w ill increase 
more and more.  
 
You will easily attain the good qualities of Dharma, and they will constantly increase.  
So it is vital that you uphold [your] refuge by means  of being mindful of the fear and 
the positive qualities explained above, and that you put effort into not transgressing its 
precepts.  
  
These good qualities will not degenerate, but quite the opposite, they will increase 
more and more. One should rely on this mindfulness of fear as explained above, on 
the good qualities as explained above and by these two factors uphold the refuge. Not 
only that, but we should uphold this refuge by not transgressing the refuge trainings. 
In other words, guarding the precepts.  
 
 
THURSDA Y 29 APRIL  2010  - SESSION 1 
No one wants the experience of suffering, and everyone wants to be free from it. If 
you want to  be separated from suffering, you need to be separated from the causes of 
suffering. Suffering experiences are caused by negative karma. To get rid of the causes 
you have to restrain yourself from negative actions, and so by not accumulating the 
causes you  don‟t experience the suffering results. Within suffering there is the 
especially strong suffering of the lower realms, and to avoid this you restrain from 
engaging in the ten non -virtuous actions. Thus, we block the path to the lower realms 
through blocki ng its causes.  
 In comparison to the suffering of the human realm, the suffering of the lower 
realms is far greater, and the beings who suffer there do so completely exposed 
without any help or support from others. This is definitely something we don‟t wa nt to 
encounter. As humans, we have the sufferings of birth, old age sickness and death, but 
even with these there are always people or situations that can help us deal with this 
suffering, but in the lower realms, there is no one who is going to help you with the 
suffering. We need to engage into activities that are not creating the causes for any 
kind of suffering, we need to understand how to act in an unmistaken way.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
359  
 It is very essential to generate a faith of conviction with regard to the law of 
cause and effect because it will help us to understand the causes of suffering. Because 
of this, we will abstain from the negative actions that are the causes of suffering. We 
will stop creating the causes and be protected from suffering.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B Gener ating the faith of conviction in karma and its results  
A Considering karma and its results in general  
B Considering karma and its results in particular  
C Having considered them, how to engage [in virtuous actions] and counteract  [the 
non-virtuous]  
The sec ond one – generating the faith of conviction in karma and its results – has 
three points: considering karma and its results in general, considering karma and its 
results in particular, and having considered them, how to engage [in virtuous actions] 
and cou nteract  [the non -virtuous].  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1 Considering karmaxliii  and its results in general  
A The actual way to consider karma and its results in general  
B Considering the specific divisions  
The first one has tw o points: the actual w ay to consider karma an d its results in 
general and considering the specific divisions.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1A  The actual way to consider karma and its results in general  
1 How karma is certain  
2 The great increase of karma  
3 You do not meet with karma you have not created  
4 Karma you have created does not dissipate  
The first one has four points: how  karma is certain, the great increase of karma, you 
do not meet w ith karma you have not created, and karma you have created does not 
dissipate.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1A -1  How karma is certain  
1 The actual way how karma is certain  
First: All happiness having the aspect of pleasant feelings, w hether in ordinary beings 
or aryas – even as little as that which is generated in a sentient being born in hell in 
dependence on a cool w ind springing up - arises from virtuous karma accumulated 
previously. { 99}  There is no w ay for happiness to arise from non -virtuous karma. All 
suffering having the aspect of painful feelings – even as little as that w hich is 
generated  in the mind of an arhat – arises from non -virtuous karma accumulated 
previously. There is no w ay for suffering to arise from virtuous karma.  
 
All happy feelings without exception come from previously created virtue. Pleasurable 
feels can be from a being in the hot hells experiencing a cool breeze to  the happiness 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 360 – week one to seven  of ordinary and Arya beings. This happiness is said to have arisen from a virtuous 
action because a) it has to have a cause and b) this cause cannot be a non -virtuous 
action. Only suffering can arise from a non -virtuous action, never happine ss. From 
this, we conclude it must have come from a virtuous action.  
 It is the same with any kind of unpleasant feeling. From the small suffering that 
can arise in the continuum of an Arhat and below, all these come from having 
previously accumulated non -virtue and from this arises the unpleasant feeling. This is 
because the cause for such an unpleasant feeling cannot be a virtuous action, as 
virtuous actions only bring happiness and never suffering. This is expressed in the 
quote from the Precious Garlan d:  
In the Precious Garland  it says:  
From non -virtuous deeds: all suffering;  
Likew ise all the bad migrations.  
From virtuous deeds: all happy realms – 
Happiness in all rebirths.     [38] 
  
QUESTION : When it says the Arhat has suffering, does this mean physi cal suffering as 
they don‟t have mental suffering?  
GEN: Why wouldn‟t they have mental suffering?  
STUDENT : Because an Arhat has attained true cessations and so cannot suffer, but 
because he has the remainder of the contaminated physical aggregates some phy sical 
suffering can happen.  
GEN: Wouldn‟t this remainder also apply to mental suffering?  
STUDENT : No. 
GEN: What about Arhats that immediately want to proceed into the Mahayana? To do 
this they need to generate strong forms of compassion, but for the time being they are 
not able to and this makes them distraught. Arhats do finally proceed to the Mahayana. 
They come to have this strong wish to enter, but they can‟t because they don‟t have 
the strong minds needed to enter the Mahayana.  
STUDENT : At least t his wouldn‟t be a mental suffering that arises from negative karma.  
GEN: Why?  
STUDENT : Because the Arhat is free from the causes of suffering which is karma and 
afflictions. Whatever residue is there, especially karma, would not be there.  
GEN: Doesn‟t he ha ve some remainder of suffering aggregates?  
STUDENT :  Yes, that‟s why he can experience physical suffering.  
GEN: Isn‟t that a suffering aggregate? But according to you he has abandoned the 
causes.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
361 TRANSLATOR :  But not the effect.  
GEN: It is the same for the mental suffering, the causes have been abandoned but 
there is still the effect. Both would be the same from the point of view that the cause 
has become non -existent. So what is the difference? This Arhat has the suffering form 
aggregate (remainder). Do esn‟t he have the wish to be separated from this?  
STUDENT : Yes. 
GEN: Doesn‟t he experience it as a difficulty that he cannot separate from?  
STUDENT : He knows that the separation will come soon, so it‟s no problem.  
GEN: If it‟s like that, then the fact that he has a little bit of mental suffering is also no 
problem.  
STUDENT : Where would this suffering come from? Negative karma?  
GEN: It would be the same question with the suffering reminder of the form aggregate. 
Where di d it come from, the cause is already non -existent?  
STUDENT : So the Arhat is not free from suffering.  
GEN: You say he is not free from suffering , but it is you who says he still has the 
remainder of the suffering form aggregate. It is you who says he stil l has suffering.  
STUDENT : Me and Lama Tsongkhapa ! 
GEN: So you and Lama Tsongkhapa say the same thing.  
STUDENT 2: For every feeling we need a karma in every instant. One moment you‟re 
happy so a positive karma is ripening, the next your unhappy so a negat ive karma is 
ripening.  
GEN: There is this story of an Arhat who is beaten up by a gang of people, I think at 
this time he wasn‟t very happy! What do you think?  
STUDENT 3: I think he was ok with it. He probably had compassion for the people who 
beat him.  
GEN: Here we are talking about Maudgalyayana . He was regarded as the best at 
performing  miraculous feats. In this moment, he forgot about all his powers and was 
not able to use them – he didn‟t even think about it, so we can forget about him 
having compass ion at that time! Later on Shariputra asked him, “As you were the one 
with all this power, why didn‟t you use it?” He replied, “At that time I couldn‟t 
remember anything.” It‟s not due to the pain that he forgot, it‟s about when karma 
ripens your methods f all away, and there was no way for him to access the methods to 
avoid it.  
 In this situation, the Arhat would have experienced some suffering, but it would 
not have been a samsaric suffering. This means it is not a suffering that is included 
within the Tw elve Links.  
QUESTION : So is there a non -samsaric suffering?  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 362 – week one to seven  GEN: Yes, all the suffering feelings you have during the path. For example, there is a 
suffering feeling coming together with compassion. It is still a suffering feeling, but 
not samsaric suffer ing. In addition, regarding this remaining suffering form aggregate, 
this is no longer a samsaric body, but it still a suffering aggregate. This type of 
suffering is nothing like what we experience. An Arhat would not be unhappy or 
weighed down by it, but the suffering would be very light even though it remains as 
suffering.  
QUESTION :  But we said this suffering came from the ripening of a karma. Karma 
normally is true origin, which he has abandoned.  
GEN: Here you have two origins, samsaric true origins a nd non -samsaric true origins. 
True origins refers to karma and afflictions, and these two are strongly connected. By 
this strong connection they produce samsara. Then you have other true origins that are 
producing suffering but not samsara. This is the tru e origins such an Arhat has, and on 
this basis he was experiencing this suffering.   
 If something is suffering, it is not necessarily true suffering. For example, 
suffering which is of the nature of the true paths. This is very subtle description. We 
shou ld ask a high lama or high Geshe these kind of questions; Is all suffering true 
suffering? What about the suffering that is of the nature of a path? For example, the 
suffering in the continuum of someone on the path of meditation? Because it‟s a true 
path,  is this suffering? Or what about the suffering which comes about from 
compassion, is this true suffering? At that time we can say to them, “Please explain 
because our Geshe didn‟t explain this to us!” If we ask him, “Is there a true path which 
has the ent ity of suffering?” and he says, “No, something like this does not exist.” 
Then you can say, “Oh, thank you!”  
 Also maybe ask someone, “Does an Arhat still have the true origin of karma?” 
The point here is an Arhat does not have any kind of true origin of samsara, neither 
karma nor afflictions that produce samsara – those that are included in the  Twelve 
Links. Still he has true origins that produce suffering, like in our example here and he 
even has true origins, which are karma. Here you have to be specif ic with the 
terminology. The Arhat doesn‟t have true origins for samsara, afflicted true origins or 
karmic true origins, but he does have true origins which are karma. The term karmic  
true origins  is reserved for karma which is the true origin of samsara. An Arhat therefore 
does not have karmic true origins  but does have true origins for his non -samsaric 
suffering which are karma.   
 We are only talking here about an Arhat, but there are many other examples. 
Bodhisattvas on the ten grounds (bhumis) - do they  experience sufferings or not? Yes. 
Do they have samsara? No. Then here again we have beings that have suffering but not 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
363 samsara. Bodhisattvas on the ten grounds would not have samsaric suffering because 
the first link of the Twelve Links (ignorance) is no  longer there. Therefore, they don‟t 
have the true origins, and they don‟t have the true sufferings. They do have suffering, 
small types like suffering feelings. So how is that?  
 Each of the ten grounds is associated with ten perfections. The first ground  is 
the time for the perfection of generosity. [TRANSLATOR : Generosity that has gone to the 
other side is the literal meaning.] , and ethical discipline is perfected on the second ground. 
What factor is it that prevents the bodhisattva on the first ground from perfecting 
ethical discipline?  
 The meaning is that if the perfection of ethical discipline only comes about on 
the second ground, the bodhisattva on the first ground cannot keep all the subtle and 
very fine types of ethical discipline. Patience seems  to be even more difficult because it 
comes on the third ground. He can‟t perfect patience on the first two grounds as this is 
done only on the third ground. There is some problem why he can‟t perfect ethics on 
the first ground or perfect patience on the f irst and second grounds.  
QUESTION :   Gen-la once said that this Arhat being beaten up was the result of a 
negative karma ripening due to being overlooked and not purified, wasn‟t this a 
negative karma?  
GEN:  Yes, indeed this suffering arose from negative karma. It says this exact same 
thing in our text, All suffering having the aspect of painful feelings – even as little as that 
which is generated in the mind of an Arhat – arises from non -virtuous karma accumulated 
previously.  I don‟t have to explain anyth ing - it is right here in the text!   
QUESTION : Would this be a true origin that is not samsara?  
GEN: Yes, it would be a true origin, but it would not be samsara.  
There is the following statement in debate, “Take the subject the continuum of 
an Arhat, does  he have the afflicted true origins and karmic true origins?” Someone 
then replies, “Yes, because he still has true origins.” Then the reply is, “It doesn‟t 
follow. Just because you have the true origins, it doesn‟t mean you have either karmic 
true origins  or afflicted true origins. For example, an Arhat.” This is how things are, 
but this is already a subtle level of presentation.  
 This quote from the Precious Garland  says that from non -virtue all suffering 
arises. Virtue cannot bring suffering, only non -virtue can bring suffering. Specifically, 
suffering rebirths in the lower realms arise from non -virtue. All the happy migrations 
come from virtue. In addition, in all these different rebirths the happiness within these 
lives also comes from virtue. In the he ll realm for example, whatever pleasant 
experience you have there comes about through virtue.

  Page | 364 – week one to seven  2 Explanations [of this] as the basis of all white dharmas  
Therefore, happiness and suffering do not arise w ithout cause or from an incongruous 
cause like the primal matter, Ishvara, and the like. In general, happiness and suffering 
arise from virtuous and non -virtuous karma, and the manifold specifics of happiness 
and suffering also arise individually from the manifold specifics of the tw o [types of] of 
karma w ithout any error. To gain certainty w ith regard to actions and their results 
being certain and infallible is said to be the correct view  of all Buddhists and praised 
as the foundation of all w hite dharmas.  
 
Happiness and suffering do not arise without a ca use, or from a discordant cause like 
the general principal or a creator god. They arise from individual general forms of 
virtue and non -virtue. Why can‟t we say that happiness and suffering have arisen 
without a cause? What is the problem with that?  
STUDEN T: We would then be powerless. We couldn‟t create causes for happiness or 
abandon causes for suffering.  
GEN: Most people say things like, “It just happens, without a cause.”  
STUDENT : The problem is it  would be inherently existent.  
GEN: What‟s the problem  with being inherently existent? If it arises like this, then it‟s 
wonderful.  
STUDENT : Then if you were suffering you would stay suffering. This is not so nice!

in my feet!

second point is about the discordant cause. This is what you are answering.  
STUDENT  3: Cause and effect has many levels, so why does it have to be this level,

365 hunger. Otherwise, what else would it mean by karma? What do we mean when we say

 In Tibetan, it is clear because lä is action and work is läka. “I‟m going to do 
some work” – “läka jepa.” Therefore, it‟s obvious that karma is connected with doing 
something.  
 Someone said that we wouldn‟t have freedom, but actually we do not have 
freedom because we are in samsara. What about if we say happiness and suffering arise 
without a cause because we don‟t have freedom? What would be the problem with 
that?  
 If I say, “We don‟t have freedom, we are completely determined by other 
factors. Suffering and happiness have no cause and w e don‟t have any power over our 
lives.” This is actually the proof that happiness and suffering have no cause because we 
have no freedom.  
STUDENT : There is freedom in a certain way, because when you take on the idea of

GEN: At the same time, isn‟t it that we get sick without wanting it, we get cold without 
wanting to be cold, we get hot from the sun when we don‟t want it. Therefore, all this 
suffering is arising without causes, and we don‟t have any freedom.  
 In conclusion then, my answer would be this. If things could arise without a 
cause, then with regard to all the people here, some have a tall body, some small, thin 
etc., - many differences, then where would these differences come from? If there were 
no causes for these  bodies, and they don‟t have to rely on specific causes, then they 
should all arise in the same size and shape. Another example would be that sometimes 
we are happy and sometimes we are not. How could this come about without a cause? 
If it was without a ca use, either it would never arise or it would constantly arise, but 
our experience is we sometimes have happy feelings and sometimes we have unhappy 
feelings.  
Because they depend on their individual causes and conditions, sometimes they 
arise in one way an d sometimes in another. If there wasn‟t this dependence on 
individual causes and conditions, there wouldn‟t be the possibility that sometimes they 
arise and sometimes they don‟t.  
 For example, all the trees outside are now green. A few weeks ago it wasn‟t  like 
this, so this situation changed. Why did it change? Because there are causes and 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 366 – week one to seven  conditions for them not having leaves, and there are causes and conditions for them 
having leaves. This shows clearly that things arise from causes and conditions.  
 
THURSDAY 29 APRIL 2010  – SESSION 2 
QUESTION : Would the Buddha also have suffering when he sees sentient beings hurting 
each other?  
GEN: There is no suffering in the continuum of a Buddha. A Buddha cannot be 
unhappy. There are quotes where it says if we beh ave in a certain way the buddhas 
become unhappy. This is not actually the case, but it is a useful mind for us to 
cultivate to stop us behaving in a certain way.  
QUESTION : Gen-la said compassion is related to a suffering feeling, but then a Buddha 
having the greatest form of compassion would also have suffering feelings?  
GEN: When compassion is generated in someone‟s mind for the first time, it is 
connected with an unbearable suffering feeling. This means he would cry from this 
feeling, he would shake and  not be able to speak.  
Next, he generates a strong courage to do something about the suffering. This is 
connected with bliss that is of such intensity that it out outshines that of the Arhat. So 
we could say that this unbearable suffering comes together w ith compassion when it is 
first generated, then from this comes a great courage leading to great bliss. We can say 
that this suffering, that comes together with this compassion, is something good 
because it leads to courage and bliss.   
 
QUESTION : When we talked about the god who was born from the Heaven of the 
Thirty -Three to Tushita, is Tushita (Ganden) a god realm or a pure land? What is the 
difference between the two? It is said that the beings in the pure realms are 
temporarily not in samsara, but the god realms are still in samsara.  
GEN: Tushita is a god realm. The god realms in the three realms are all included within 
samsara. Therefore, gods being born there are also in samsara. Tushita is from one 
point of view such a god realm and from another poi nt of view a pure land. The pure 
land is not part of samsara and nor are the beings born there. However, there is a 
connection between the god area and the pure land. For example, we would say the 
Protector Maitreya is a god living in a god region, and thi s god region is a pure land, 
and this pure land is Tushita.  
 Tushita is where this god from the Heaven of the Thirty -Three was born due to 
his karma. Then in Tushita, there is also this pure land where the Protector Maitreya 
abides. It‟s like we are here at Nalanda and Lavaur is over there, on the same plane but 
in different areas.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
367 QUESTION : Could someone just walk from one to the other?  
GEN: None of the gods who were just born in the god realm could enter the pure land, 
but all of this is still called Tu shita.  
When we say the Protector Maitreya is a god and his realm is a pure land, we 
have to think about how the Buddha was born in the human realm free of samsara. In 
contrast, we are born in the human realm and are not free of samsara. In the same way, 
we say that Maitreya is a god, but to go to this pure land depends on great merit and 
aspirational prayers, and therefore has different causes than just being born in the 
heaven of Tushita.  
QUESTION : So the beings in Tushita pure land would not go over to the Tushita god 
realm?  
GEN: The beings in the pure land have a great freedom because they are not under the 
power of the afflictions. They could leave the pure land and visit other realms 
according to their wishes, in order to benefit beings there for exam ple. By just visiting 
these realms, they would not become samsaric beings. However, the gods in this region 
do not have freedom like this as they are completely under the power of the afflictions 
and so on.  
QUESTION : Is the Protector Maitreya in this pure  land already enlightened? If yes, is he 
manifesting as a tenth level bodhisattva or a supreme emanation body?  
GEN: The Protector Maitreya is already enlightened; this is made clear in the texts. The 
aspect he takes now in this pure land is an emanation bo dy, but not the supreme 
emanation body. When in the future he manifests on earth in the human realm to teach 
the dharma he will manifest the supreme emanation body.  
QUESTION : Is he alr eady showing he‟s a Buddha with this emanation body?  
G: Yes, he‟s show ing the body which shows he is already enlightened. All those 
dwelling in Tushita pure land know he is a completely enlightened being.  
QUESTION : Wouldn‟t the buddhas always show the enjoyment body in a pure land?  
GEN: In some pure lands the Buddha shows t he enjoyment body, and in some the 
emanation body. If they show the enjoyment body, then they can only surrounded by 
Arya bodhisattvas. In Tushita pure land there can be Arya bodhisattvas and ordinary 
bodhisattvas because it is an emanation body.  
If suffe ring is posited as coming from a discordant cause such as the General

GEN: Yes, let‟s say that. It is permanent, it  produced everything and is the elements.

GEN: I hold that Ishvara is an element, but none of the four elements and he i s

STUDENT : Does Gen -la hold that things come into existence from the desire of 
Ishvara to create?

STUDENT : If the causes and conditions are there, then the phenomenon comes into

GEN: There is no other cause - there is only Ishvara.  
STUDENT : I‟m out  of ammunition!

GEN: With regard to each and every phenomena, there is a different Ishvara, like a 
water Ishvara, sun Ishvara and so on.  
STUDENT : How could Ishvara be the one cause for something so different such as 
happiness and suffering?

unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
369 STUDENT 4: If Ishvar a is permanent, then he would permanently create happiness so 
therefore we‟d have to experience happiness constantly which is not our experience.   
GEN: If this is true for you, then it‟s your problem. We have this continuous 
happiness.  You‟re creator is probably a little bit negative!  
STUDENT 5: Didn‟t we say earlier that permanence implies inherent existence and 
inherent existence cannot produce anything?  
GEN: Firstly,  in general, that something is permanent shows that it is not inherently 
existent. If you say something is inherently existent, then it becomes clear that it is not 
a permanent phenomenon. Secondly, you said from an inherently existent cause 
something impermane nt cannot be produced. If we modify this a little and say from an 
inherently existent cause, an effect which is not inherently existent cannot be 
produced, then this is a very excellent answer.   
 If we say that an impermanent phenomenon has arisen from a permanent 
phenomenon like Ishvara, then an effect would have been produced from a discordant 
cause. Isn‟t it that an effect is necessarily produced from a concordant cause? 
Whatever we can point at around us we would say it comes from a cause that is simil ar 
to itself. If it is impermanent, it has to come from something impermanent. If you 
hope to get tomatoes from an onion it will not work – what you get from an onion is 
an onion. The cause and the effect are in accordance. With Ishvara, this would not be 
the case as it would be a permanent phenomena producing an impermanent 
phenomenon.  
 Depending on the seed, we can understand what is of the concordant class or 
not. For example, if a child is born and the father is black, then the child will have that 
colour. Even if the mother is white, the child will still be like the father. This is an 
effect being concordant with the cause.  
 There has to be the connection with the effect and its respective cause. With an 
impermanent phenomenon and a permanent phenomen on, there is no concordance, 
not even in the smallest way.  
 Things cannot be produced from a discordant class, like the principal or Ishvara. 
Happiness and suffering arise from the concordant class. This means the general 
happiness and suffering come from  virtue and non -virtue, and specific varieties come 
from their specific causes. This is the certainty of karma in that from a virtuous action 
only happiness is produced and non -virtuous actions will only bring suffering. In this 
way, karma is definite.  
 This presentation, that specific and various sufferings and happiness come from 
specific and various actions (virtuous and non -virtuous), is definite and is totally 
reliable. One should develop certainty with this process, as it is universally agreed 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 370 – week one to seven  upon throughout all Buddhist traditions,  and as such it is praised as being the basis of 
all white dharmas (actions).. From virtuous actions come happiness and from non -
virtuous actions come suffering.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1A -2  The great increase of karma  
 
From one s mall virtue, there can be a great ripening. Similarly, from a small non -virtue 
a great suffering can come about through this increase of karma.  
 Further, it says this presentation of cause and effect of actions refers to an inner 
process of cause and effe ct increasing. This increase cannot be seen with regard to 
external forms of cause and effect like those we see in nature.  
 
Second: There is nothing among external causes and effects that w ould resemble the 
increase of internal causes and effects, for eve n from small wholesome karma the result 
of enormous happiness arises while even from small unw holesome karma the result of 
enormous suffering arises. In the Udanavarga  it says:  
Even committing a small negativity,  
Will effectuate great fear and  
Great disast er in future w orlds.  
Like poison seeping through to the core.  
 
Even committing a little merit,  
In future w orlds w ill bring great happiness,  
Causing greatly meaningful things,  
Like grains that ripen to excellence.    [39] 
 
The first verse above says that thr ough engaging in some small negative action, a great 
fear can arise in future lives. Great suffering can also arise from such a small cause. 
The last line of this first verse is like having ingested some poison, and there is a 
danger it could spread. When you become aware of this, you‟ll do everything possible 
to get rid of it. In the same way, negativities produce this kind of suffering, so you 
should get rid of them with the same urgency.  
 From even a small merit, the great joys of the other worlds are i nduced and one 
can bring forth great meaningful tasks. The last line in the second verse is like the 
following analogy; say you use 100 kilos of maize and sow it into a field. When it 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
371 ripens into a harvest, you‟ll get many thousands of kilos of maize as a result. This 
shows how grains ripen into many.  
QUESTION : Doesn‟t this show that there is increase of cause and effect in the external 
world?  
GEN:  While there is increase from one seed in the external world, the increase in the 
internal world from one mer it is much stronger than that.  
 
THURSDAY 29 APRIL 2010  – SESSION 3 
 
QUESTION : Why does karma increase? Shouldn‟t it be that a small cause would bring a 
small effect?  
GEN: A certain action becomes very vast or increases strongly. For example, sweeping 
the gompa. We sweep and this is already an action. You can sweep with the thought, 
“I‟m clearing out all my mistaken views and obscurations.” Or, by thinking, “This is 
the place of the Three Jewels, my refuge, so I‟m keeping it clean.” The action is the 
same whether you have this attitude or not. If you do this action with these thoughts, 
you‟ll be able to accumulate great waves of merit and so the ordinary action has 
increased enormously. If you do this every day, then even the bodhimind can come 
about withou t much meditation or much sitting in formal sessions etc. If you‟ve done 
this regularly, you can at one point stop and become aware of how many days you‟ve 
been cultivating this attitude and how much merit you‟ve created. Then you can rejoice 
and this also  increases the action. You can also dedicate for the sake of all sentient 
beings that all their suffering and obscurations will be removed, that you can become 
enlightened and help all sentient beings. Then this simple action becomes very 
powerful. The act ion of sweeping itself is just that, but due to all these other powerful 
minds like rejoicing and dedicating, the action is increased.  
 You can imagine you have a quarrel with someone and you say something 
negative to them using harsh speech. In your room  later you reflect on this, you think, 
“I didn‟t say enough, I need to say more, this guy needs to hear more. Tomorrow I‟m 
going to say something stronger.” You just said a small hurting remark, but by dwelling 
in this kind of mind it is increased.  
 As you think you didn‟t say enough and there needs to be a stronger comment 
made you would then regret having said so little. You‟ll have a mind which doesn‟t 
want to purify the small comment but actually you want to harm him more and you 
have no intention to  abstain. You think, “Even if I‟m born in hell for this, I‟m going 
to say something stronger to him tomorrow.” Maybe you don‟t want to go so far, but 
maybe you think, “If I say something stronger there won‟t be much karmic outcome.” 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 372 – week one to seven  You think that it is no t a strong negative action, and maybe think there will be no 
effect. You might even go so far as to say there is no outcome at all from this action, 
and as a result, you will have no faith of conviction in the law of cause and effect. Due 
to all this, the one small negative karma of harsh speech has rapidly increased.  
 Another case might be that you do accept that it was a negative thing to do, but 
you don‟t engage into purification. Then on a daily basis, this negative karma is 
increasing.  
QUESTION : How?  
GEN: If you have a leak in the roof and a drop of water constantly drips, first one, 
then another, after some time everything will be completely wet. It‟s not that 
everything becomes wet from only one drop. Things become wet because there are 
constantly th ese drops leaking in. Similar to that there is this increase.  
QUESTION : Then the increase is coming from us engaging in other similar actions?  
GEN: This is not what it means. Just by having done one action, then not having 
generated a mind of regret and pu rification, just this will make it increase. Similar to 
the drops constantly falling - and you don‟t do anything to stop them.  
QUESTION : Is it increasing from moment to moment?  
GEN:  Yes, that is what it means.  
QUESTION :  Is it increasing from its own side  because we are not adding anything?  
GEN: It‟s not just increasing by itself, it meets many conditions for this. If we throw a 
piece of garbage on the floor, it would not increase by itself. Rather there would be 
another piece of garbage collecting there, and so on until the whole room would be a 
mess.  
 In our example of saying something offensive, this is the first piece. Then the 
negativity is increased in that you regret that you didn‟t say more, and so on while you 
think of the harm you want to do to t his person.  
 In the case of an ordained person, there are small transgressions that are called 
faults. He might think, “Oh, it‟s only small, no problem.” Nevertheless, by thinking in 
this way for a long time he repeatedly engages into the small action, an d increases the 
negativity as a result.  
QUESTION : What are the individual drops referring to in this analogy? Because it seems 
there is an individual collection of karmas, whereas before we said that it was only one 
action that was increasing.  
GEN: An exa mple for an individual action is this person saying just one word. This is 
the first drop. Later he regretted not saying something stronger, this was the second 
drop. Later he is clear that he won‟t purify it, this is another drop. However, the first 
actio n was only one drop.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
373 QUESTION : I understood previously that even if you say something abusive and you 
forget about it, you don‟t purify or don‟t even think about it etc., nevertheless this 
would increase by its nature.  
GEN: In this case, the first drop w ould be the saying something abusive. The second 
drop would be that you leave it at that, you don‟t purify it because you see it as 
something small. There would be some increase, but it wouldn‟t be a great increase. 
Going under this mind of forgetfulness i s also planting drops of dullness or ignorance.  
 The analogy that if you don‟t stop the leak the whole place is filled with water is 
used in many texts. Therefore, you have to repair the leak right at the source. For 
example, the texts says that someone w ho takes Vajrasattva initiation and recites the 
mantra 21 times, ill stop negative karma from growing but not that it would remove 
them.  
QUESTION : What text best explains this aspect of karma increasing?  
GEN: The Lam -Rim. I myself have not seen anything b etter than what is presented 
here on cause and effect. In Sera, there is no study of the Lam -Rim, you only hear of it 
a few times. Coming to Nalanda I had the opportunity to go through the Lam -Rim, 
and then I become aware that there is no better text expla ining karma.  I have nearly 
explained the entire Lam -Rim Chenmo at Nalanda over a three -year period with only a 
few pages remaining unfinished.  
QUESTION : Does this increase of karma explain collective karma? Or is it only about 
one‟s own karma?  
GEN: From one‟s individual karma, then the collective karma comes about. For 
example, I say something nasty to one person and they say something nasty to another 
and so on. These are all individual actions, but from this there is a general pattern of 
behaviour.

Here we talk a bout how happiness and suffering arise on the basis of the cause 
accumulated. From virtue, happiness comes about. If no virtue was accumulated, then 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 374 – week one to seven  there can be no happiness. Similarly, suffering cannot arise if the cause, negative 
actions, were not accum ulated.  
 The second line talks about how the Buddha ( Teacher ) first collected the two 
accumulations during three countless eons by practicing the six perfections etc. Finally, 
he brought about the result, and became completely enlightened. Having gained 
complete buddhahood, by the power of his compassion he engaged into the 
affectionate deed of teaching the Dharma – showing the paths. We then receive these 
instructions about how to follow the paths and this is something we can utilise. This is 
what those w ho enjoy the effects of the Teacher’s accumulation  means. The Buddha has, 
from his enlightenment, shown the path which we can enjoy. We could say the Buddha 
has given us some of his merit by showing us the path. At the same time, we also need 
to accumulate  further merit.  
An analogy here would be the pure land of Amitabha, Sukhavati (Dewachen). 
This is his pure land, he brought it about. Whilst it is his, if we accumulate a special 
amount of merits, purify obscurations and make special prayers, on this basi s of effort 
from our side, we can be born in and enjoy this pure land. From the side of Amitabha 
there is the pure land, and from our side we have to create that causes to be born 
there.  
 Another example would be if we were sick. The actual power to cure us is in the 
medicine. From our side we also have to do other things like adopt certain behaviour 
or change our diet. We have to put something into this process and from these two 
factors there is healing.  
 In the context of going for refuge, we refer to t he fears of others and ourselves 
and turn to the Three Jewels to protect us by being aware of their good qualities and 
capacity to protect us. This is similar in that on the one hand, we have the Refuge 
Jewels with their good qualities that can protect us from fear, and on the other hand, 
we need to engage into activities like accumulating merits and purification.  
  
2B4B -2B1A -2B1A -4  Karma you have created does not dissipate  
 
All these different pleasant or unpleasant results come from having previously en gaged 
into virtue or non -virtue respectively; they could not have come about any other way.  
 We all wish for happiness (pleasurable experiences) but we don‟t have these all 
the time. We have different amounts etc. Why is it that we wish for them but we don ‟t 
always get them? If there is a pleasurable experience or not depends on the individuals‟ 
previous activity.   
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
375 Fourth: The virtuous and non -virtuous actions that have been done issue forth 
pleasant and unpleasant results. In the Praise to the Particularly  Noble  it says:  
Brahmins say they can transfer  
Virtues and negativities  
As though giving and taking them.  
You said, w hat is done w on’t vanish  
What is not w ill not be met.     [40] 
 
I read this first line as, “The Brahmins say there is a giving and taking o f virtues and  
negativities.” This refers to assertions of Hindu systems where you receive happiness 
through asking the creator god for happiness. He is pouring it out and you collect it in 
your cup. This outlook shows an utter lack of power in your life, a view that does not 
accept the law of cause and effect, but rather this god just pours out happiness and 
you receive it. This belief is a pitiful state because it means this person is in ignorance 
of cause and effect, and this ignorance has power over the ir life.  
 In the last two lines, you refers to the Buddha and he says actions done will not 
go to waste. If we have not created the causes, then we cannot encounter the result. 
This is quite amazing. We can relate to this with a more mundane example, like  the 
suffering of being sick. If you ask, “Where did this sickness come from?” your doctor 
will tell you it came from either eating wrong foods or other unhealthy habits. There is 
no creator god in any of this. Due to the conditions there is sickness, if t hese 
conditions are absent, then there is no sickness. A doctor can explain this in some 
detail, and what he is explaining is the reality, isn‟t it? There is no creator god here. If 
all the conditions are present, then what you have done will necessarily a nd without 
fail bring a result. If causes have not been accumulated, they will not bring a result.  
 
And from the King of Concentration [Sutra]: 
Plus you cannot but touch your deeds,  
What others did you w ill not feel.     [41] 
 
This again expresses that t hose things you have done, you yourself will come into 
contact with. This is what you the Buddha said. What someone else did will not be able 
to generate a feeling in me, you the Buddha also said this. The meaning here is only our 
own actions will bring a result for us, and not the actions of others. For example, if I 
eat something bad for the stomach, you won‟t get the stomach problems, but I will. 
Likewise, if you eat something bad for the stomach, I will not get stomach problems 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 376 – week one to seven  but you will. You‟re own deeds will ripen upon you, and you will have to experience 
them. This is the meaning of karma does not dissipate.  
 This presentation of actions and their effects is something amazing and comes 
down to the term of dependent arising. The effects in the mind  are a dependent 
arising, meaning they depend on their respective causes.   
 
And from the Foundation of [Vinaya] Scriptures : 
Karmas do not go to w aste  
Even in a hundred eons  
When they find the collection [of conditions] and time,  
They w ill become effects for beingsxlv.  [42] 
 
 
Actions we do don‟t wear off, but they stay with us and when they meet their 
respective conditions they ripen into an experience, even if they were created hundreds 
of lifetimes ago. These actions will not dissipate, but they will wait there right up to 
the moment they meet the right conditions in order for them to ripen into an 
experience. We need to apply this to our purification practice. We should recall and 
include all the negativity we have done since beginningless time. Whatever we have 
created under  the power of ignorance, the condition, we want to purify. With this 
attitude, whatever was collected since beginningless time can be purified.  
  
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B  Considering the specific divisions  
1 Indication that there are mainly ten paths of action  
2 Establishing actions and effects  
 
The second one has tw o points: indication that there are mainly ten paths of action 
and establishing actions and effects.  
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -1 Indication that there are mainly ten paths of action  
A Explanation that there are te n in the sutras and treatises  
 
 
This means the sutras and treatises explain these negative actions as being ten. First is 
a recap of the four general aspects of karma:  
1) Regarding happiness and suffering, their individual causes and effects are certain. 
This means that from virtue the only ripening result is happiness, and from negativities 
the only ripening result is suffering. This relationship is definite.  
2) Karma naturally increases.  
3) If you didn‟t perform an action, you can‟t encounter the effect .  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
377 4) Actions will not go to waste.  
Someone being aware of these four then thinks, “If this is the case, then what do we 
need to adopt, and what do we need to give up?”  
The answer is that with regard to virtue and non -virtue there are a great many 
action s. We can abbreviate them into three created through actions of the body, four 
created through actions of speech, and three created through actions of the mind. 
Turning away from these ten non -virtueous actions constitutes the ten virtuous 
actions.  
 As th e emphasis is on this definite number of ten, is it then that all virtue is 
included in the ten virtuous actions and all non -virtue is included in the ten non -
virtuous actions? No. So what is so special about these ten? The answer is these ten 
non-virtuous  actions are the strongest or coarsest actions. Likewise, the ten virtuous 
actions are very strong.  
  
First: Thus you have first generated certainty concerning the ways of actions and 
results through realizing that happiness and suffering results are cert ain to have their 
individual causes, that karma increases greatly, that you do not meet w ith anything 
you have not created and that karma does not dissipate. Now , if you ask about what 
actions to adopt and w hich ones to reject [it is as follow s]. In genera l, the gates for 
engaging in good conduct and faulty conduct are determined to be three. Although not 
all virtues and non -virtues of the three doors are included in the ten paths of action, 
the Bhagavan, summing up the essential points, has stated the more  obvious or coarser 
virtues and non -virtues, the great foundations of misdeeds, as the ten black paths of 
action. Having seen that, w hen they are given up, the most significant points are also 
included in ten,{ 101}  he taught them to be the white paths of a ction.  In the Treasury  
it says:  
Subsuming the most obvious among them  
The various virtues and non -virtues  
Were stated as ten paths of action.    [43] 
This quote from the Treasury  is expressing how this number of ten is shown in the 
different sutras, and it only subsumes the strongest ones.  
 
Also, in the Scripture on Discernment  it says:  
Guarding one’s speech and completely restraining one’s mind  
Refraining from unw holesome deeds w ith the body,  
Once you have purified these three paths of action,  
You w ill attain the path that w as taught by the seer[ s]. [44]  
 
This quote first says how you should guard your speech, and that you do this by 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 378 – week one to seven  guarding your mind. Then not only speech will be guarded, but all the non -virtuous 
actions of the body will be stoppe d. Three paths  of action  refer to actions of three 
doors. Through guarding and restraining the mind through mindfulness and 
introspection, you will be able to guard the three doors of body, speech and mind, 
purify these three paths, and then attain the pat hs of the sages.  
  
B Explanation of cherishing the adoption and abandonment of the ten [paths of action]  
Therefore, the meaning of the praise to ethical discipline of abandoning the ten non -
virtuous [paths of action] from the Sutra on the Ten Grounds , is summarily stated in 
Entry to the Middle Way :  
For high status and for certain goodness  
Of ordinary beings, those born from the [Buddha’s]  speechxlvi 
Of those with the nature of self -enlightenmentxlvii, 
The Jinas’ childrenxlviii - the cause is not other than discipli ne.
      [45] 
 
In the Sutra on the Ten Grounds  there is great praise of the ethical discipline that 
abandons the ten non -virtuous actions. When we talk of wanting to give up harming 
others, restraining from the ten non -virtuous actions is the best way.  
Chandrakirti in his Entry to the Middle Way  elucidates the meaning of this praise. 
Ordinary beings  and those born from the speech  refer to the hearers. Nature of self -
enlightenment  refers to the pratyekabuddhas (self - buddhas) and the jinas’ children  refer s 
to the bodhisattvas. Any of these solely rely on the practice of ethical discipline  as a 
cause for high status and for certain goodness . As those high beings rely on this cause, 
then definitely those born in human realms or god realms have also relied on  ethical 
discipline. This then is the cause for definite goodness and high status. This quote 
praises how important the practice of ethical discipline is.  
 The fact that we have taken a human body shows that previously we 
accumulated the cause of practici ng ethical discipline. That we are not always sick and 
live for a long time shows we practiced perfect and pure  ethical discipline. If we are 
handsome and good looking, then we practiced patience by abandoning anger and so 
on. That we don‟t have a lot of p roblems with our health and lifespan or that people 
don‟t attack us, and we have a happy easy going mind, all this comes from having a 
mind that abandoned violence towards others. We had a mind that regarded the life of 
others as very precious. That we hav e many resources in this life and things to enjoy 
and don‟t live in hunger shows we practiced generosity. All these virtuous activities we 
did in the past have led to a happy result.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
379  To put into practice means guarding ethical discipline. Some might reci te a lot, 
do many prostrations or practice a lot of calm abiding meditation, but if together with 
this they doesn‟t master ethical discipline, then this is not practice. Practice is 
practicing ethical discipline, the pratimoksha discipline, the bodhisattva  discipline or 
the tantric discipline. We need to guard all these disciplines.  
 To practice the respective ethical disciplines we should really make effort to 
know what the vows are and know what discipline is.  
 When we talk about the three higher traini ngs, the higher training of ethical 
discipline, mental stabilisation and wisdom, the first training is the higher training of 
ethical discipline. If this has not come about, then the higher training of mental 
stabilisation will not come about nor will the higher training of wisdom.  
 In Tibetan, the word tsültim  means ethical discipline. Trim  means law or 
regulations and should be used as a verb, to restrain yourself or to rule yourself. Tsül 
means the mode of something or the manner of something. The manne r here is the 
manner the Buddha has taught – as the Buddha has taught. So together they mean: 
with what the Buddha has taught ( tsül) you have to rule yourself ( trim). To discipline 
yourself in accordance with what the Buddha has taught. Maybe we could say 
„discipline in accordance.‟  
 Ethical discipline in the context of a bodhisattva refers to guarding against harm 
towards others, abandoning any self -cherishing and guarding the mind that cherishes 
others.  
 Then there are all the individual points of ethic al discipline presented in the 
Vinaya. There is a great mass of topics there, so too much to explore here.  
   
C Giving up hypocrisy  
That being so, it w ould be quite disgraceful if someone w ho does not rely on the mind 
of restraint again and again, and gua rd even a bit of ethical discipline, w ere to say: 
“As for me, I am a Mahayanist.” In the Kshitigarbha Sutra  it says:  
By means of these ten virtuous paths [of actions] you become 
a buddha. On the other hand, if one w ho does not guard 
even a single virtuous  path of action throughout his life and 
yet says: “I am a Mahayanist. I seek unsurpassed, perfectly 
complete enlightenment”, that person is a thorough hypocrite 
and great liar, deceiving the w orld in front  of all the 
bhagavans and propounding nihilism. He  w ill die deluded 
and go to the w rongly fallen.   [46] 
 
“Wrongly fallen” should be understood as a synonym for the utterly bad migrations.  
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 380 – week one to seven  Each vow has to be protected and all faults associated with each vow are to be 
abandoned. Unfortunately some people don‟t think like this and say, “I‟m a Mahayana 
practitioner” or “I‟m a Mahayana Tantric practitioner. I‟m practicing Mahayana and 
Tantra but  not Vinaya – this is not my business.” This is a completely wrong idea. 
People can have this idea, and it seems in previous times people did have this attitude. 
Such a person will probably end up in a hell realm.  
The quote from the Kshitigarbha Sutra  first says by means of these ten virtuous paths 
[of actions] you become a Buddha . This shows how the practice of abandoning the ten 
non-virtuous actions is so strong that it is a cause for becoming enlightened. Someone 
not seeing this at all says he‟s not eng aging into that because he‟s a Mahayanist or a 
Mahayana Tantrica. He‟s rejecting this practice of abandoning the ten non -virtuous 
actions, even though it is such a strong cause for achieving enlightenment.  
 They say, “I am a Mahayanist. I seek unsurpassed, perfectly complete enlightenment”  
and use this as a reason to not practice the ethical discipline of abandoning the ten 
non-virtuous actions. To not practice ethical discipline, and to say they are looking for 
complete enlightenment is a great  hypocrisy and they are a great liar. They have not 
understood even a little bit of the Buddhas Dharma. Maybe they even teach this to 
others, and then they‟ll utterly confuse them and cheat others. This person is far from 
making any steps towards enlighten ment.  
 He will di e deluded and go to the wrongly fallen  means this type of person  is left in 
confusion and darkness and then at death he‟ll have no other path to follow except to 
go to the lower realms. Wrongly fallen  are the words the Buddha used and Lam a 
Tsongkhapa says this means a bad migration , they are just different terms for the same 
thing. Therefore, we have to be very careful.  
 As this is a very important point, we have to take care with what we are doing 
and saying to others. For example, if on e has encountered the Dharma and heard 
Dharma for some time, then maybe one has become a little calmer and less distracted, 
and might think, “Oh, now I‟ve some realisations” or “Now I‟ve realised emptiness”, 
or “I‟ve generated the bodhimind.” Then tell thi s to others, “I‟m an amazing 
practitioner!” With this self -proclaimed fame, done advertises it to others. The 
ordained here have to very careful with this. For example, if an ordained person 
becomes an Arhat and he tells others he‟s an Arhat, he incurs a d ownfall. Despite this 
being the truth, still it incurs a downfall! To make completely sure there is no opening 
for pride or self -praising there is this vow that one is not allowed to tell others about 
your attainments, even if you‟re an Arhat.

All the ten actions have these four divisions.

                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 382 – week one to seven   
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1A -1B Attitude  
 
Attitude has three divisions, the first of which is the discrimination. There can be four 
types of discrimination, and here it is specific to the action of killing.  
  
1 Discrimination  
The attitude has three points, the first of w hich is discrimination, which [again] has 
four points:  
- w ith regard to the basis, such a sentient being, the discrimination of it as such,  
Discriminating a sentient being as a sentient being.  
 - the discrimin ation that it is not a sentient being,  
Discriminating a sentient being as not being a sentient being.  
- the discrimination of w hat is not a sentient being as not a sentient being, and  
Discriminating what is not a sentient being as not being a sentient be ing.  
 - the discrimination of it as a sentient being.  
Discriminating what is not a sentient being as being a sentient being.  
 
The basis in each of these is a sentient being. The first and third are non -mistaken, the 
second and fourth are mistaken.  
  
 The first and third discrimination are unmistaken, the second and fourth ones 
are mistaken. That is, if you have the motivation, for instance, thinking to kill only 
Devadatta and then, when it comes to the performance, you mistakenly kill 
Yajnadatta there is  no negativity to do with the actual basis. For that an unmistaken 
discrimination w ould be necessary. How ever, if, at the time of the performance, you 
have the general motivation thinking to kill w hoever is at hand, an unmistaken 
discrimination is not nece ssary. You should be aw are of this rule w ith regard to the 
remaining nine, too, as appropriate.    
 
Normally one would need an unmistaken discrimination. If we had a mistaken 
discrimination, then the complete negativity does not come about. The example in the 
text is if one has a specific motivation and talks about Devadatta  and Yajnadatta . 
Making it more modern, let‟s say I want to kill Ven. Tharchin. I have this specific 
motivation, but in the actual action, I have a mistaken discrimination and it was 
actually Ven. Gendun. I actually kill Ven. Gendun but I thought it was Ven. Tharchin. 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
383 In this case, the full negativity does not come about because of the mistaken 
discrimination.  
Now if I think, “I‟m going to kill Ven. Tharchin” and arrive at a situation wh ere 
there is someone in front of me who happens to be Ven. Gendun. I then think, “I‟m 
going to kill this person whoever it is” then I kill this person. The discrimination had 
changed. Now I‟m discriminating “whoever”, and this person is included in that. T hen 
by killing Ven. Gendun I accumulate the full transgression.  
In the first example, where I only want to kill Ven. Tharchin because he is such 
a negative person, and does many negative things, if with no change in the 
discrimination I kill by accident t he wrong person due to a mistaken discrimination, 
then the actual negativity does not come about. Actually, by killing the wrong person 
you would generate a lot of regret. You only wanted to kill Ven. Tharchin and are 
utterly shocked you killed someone els e.  
 Let‟s say whilst driving we kill someone. We did not drive with a motivation of 
wanting to kill someone with the car, so as a result, the actual deed of killing has not 
come about. If this was a monk for example, whatever the government or law says, 
from the point of view of the monks vow about killing12 he does not lose his vows.   
 Another example is two people go to the airport together. There are several bags 
in the trunk of the car, one person is going to Germany and the other to India. The 
one goi ng to India takes out two bags saying, “I‟m think I had two bags,” but he 
actually only had one. Then he flies off with this bag all the way to India. When he‟s in 
India he realises, “Oh, this is not my bag!” If there is no possibility of making contact 
with the owner or to send them anything back, did he steal? We would have to say no, 
because at no moment in all this did he have the motivation to take someone else‟s 
possessions.  
 
QUESTION : But now you have this bag that you didn‟t steal, so do you use it or not, 
and if we use it isn‟t this stealing?  
GEN: It wasn‟t stealing up to now, and if you just throw it all away then this is 
unsuitable, so if you use it from now on this is not stealing .  
 This happened to me once. I was going to India with about four others and we 
were driven to the airport in Paris. There were several people in the car, a woman 
driver, some others and me. With so many people we had a lot of luggage. We took all 
the lug gage out of the car, including a bag of this woman and we checked it in at the 
airport. We took the luggage all the way to Bangalore then to the monastery where we 
distributed all the bags. But nobody knew who‟s this one bag belonged to. We opened 
                                            
12 One of the four  defeats for a monk is to kill a human. Defeat in the sense that he would no longer be a 
monk.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 384 – week one to seven  it and i t was full of women‟s clothes because this woman was about to go on holiday! 
There was no way of sending it back, so in the end we gave it to a monk who had a 
woman sponsor they could give it to.  
QUESTION : If a hunter wants to kill a rabbit but accidently  he kills his dog, isn‟t this 
still an act of killing because he wanted to kill?  
GEN: This would also be an example of mistaken discrimination. The hunter is not 
going out with the discrimination to kill any sentient being, but a specific sentient 
being l ike the rabbit.  
 For example, let‟s say you sit in a cafe and just eat, drink, chat and so on, and 
then when you leave you take someone else‟s bag by mistake. If there is no way you 
can encounter this person again, maybe they were a stranger and you‟ll ne ver see them 
again, then you can keep it. If there is a way to return it, then you should give it back.  
QUESTION : Then you get some benefit  from this.  
GEN: That remains to be seen.  
 The topic of discrimination being mistaken or unmistaken is to be applied to all 
the others actions such as lying. For example, some distance in front of you there is an 
object like a round rock and you perceive it this way. You throw a stone at it and it 
turns out to be a cat. You didn‟t have the intention to harm a cat or anyone, so there is 
no negativity here because it was a mistaken discrimination.  
This was the first aspect of the attitude. The second aspect is the affliction, 
which can be attachment,  anger or ignorance. The motivation is the wish to kill.  
 
2 Mental affliction  
The [decisive]  mental affliction is any of the three poisons.  
3 Motivation  
The motivation consists in the w ish to kill.  
 
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1A -1C Performance   
1 The performer  
It is the same w hether you yourself do the performance or w hether you cause someone 
else to do it.  
 
It doesn‟t make a difference if you do it yourself or get someone else to kill – they 
both qualify as the performance.  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
385 2 The essence of the performance  
The essence of the performance is to do it by means of a w eapon, poison, mantra, or 
w hatever it is.  
 
Anything that is able to kill the other person is the entity (essence) of the performance. 
For example, weapons, poison or even knowledge mantras are mentio ned here.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1A -1D Completion  
The completion consists in the death [of the other being] due to condition of the 
performance, at that time or at another time before you yourself die.  
 
The completion is when the other person dies. It is spec ified here as they have to die 
before you. For example, let‟s say you do an action to kill someone like throwing 
something, and it‟s clear from their injuries that they will die. However, before this you 
die in a car accident, like we see in the movies! In  this case, the complete action would 
not be there, but the performance would be.  
QUESTION : Why is this difference made between dying now or later?  
GEN: This difference is especially important for a monk. If its certain they will die 
because you wounded them and you die first, you die without losing your vows. You 
don‟t have the vows after death, you‟re not a monk in the bardo. Due to the other 
person dyin g after you, you died as a monk having the vows.    
QUESTION : But still why do we have to wait until they have died for the completion? 
We already did the action from our side.  
GEN: If you are reborn in the bardo, that bardo being has not done any of these 
actions. After that if you are reborn as a child, they also did not do any of these 
actions. Let‟s say Ven. Tharchin does something to Ven. Gendun and this means Ven. 
Gendun will die in ten years. But Ven. Tharchin dies before this and is reborn as a  
bardo being. The bardo being then takes rebirth in another life as a child and is no 
longer Ven. Tharchin. Now Ven. Gendun dies whilst this child is living in the next life. 
Did this child kill? This child didn‟t do anything.  
STUDENT : It‟s the same mental  continuum.  
GEN: Yes, but did the child have this motivation? The way of thinking of this child is 
different and there is no motivation etc.

  Page | 386 – week one to seven  2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1A -2 Stealing  (talking that what is not given)
