B Attitude  
C Performance  
D Completion  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1A -2A Basis  
The basis of stealing is anything that someone else has obtained.  
 
The object has to be someone else‟s.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1A -2B Attitude  
As for the attitude, among the three, discrimination and mental affliction, are as 
abovel. { 103}  The motivation consists in the w ish to take it away from the other 
[person] w ithout being given it.  
 
This is similar to the previous point of killing. The motivation means you have the 
wish to take away from the other person something that they ha ve not given you.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1A -2C Performance  
1 The performer  
As regards the performance, the performer is as [explained] above.  
 
It is the same result if you steal yourself or get someone else to steal for you.  
 
2 The essence of the performance  
As for the essence of the performance, it is the same whether you take something by 
force or w hether you steal something sneakingly. In addition, being deceitful w ith 
respect to a debt or a trust or and using other fraudulent methods also constitutes 
stealing. And w hether it is done for one’s ow n or someone else’s purpose or to harm 
someone else etc., it is stealing  
 
There are many types here. One is to steal by force; one is to steal by stealth , for 
example if you hide and wait until you can snatch it wit hout any force. There is no 
difference; both are the performance of this deed. In addition, things like being 
deceitful regarding taking a loan or receiving a trust or using other fraudulent methods 
and so on. All this comes down taking what has not been g iven.  
 For example, let‟s say I approach Ven. Tharchin and ask, “Can you lend me 
€100?” with the hope Ven. Tharchin will forget. At one point Ven. Tharchin does 
indeed forget and then I think, “Now it‟s mine.” Then this is stealing. When it says to 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
387 harm s omeone else , this means stealing something in order to harm the other. They have 
benefit from something, you don‟t need it at all, but in order to harm them you steal it 
from them so they can longer benefit from it. This is stealing in order to harm. These  
are all different types of stealing.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1A -2D Completion  
The completion consists in the arising of the thought that one has obtained it. Or, if 
you have caused someone else to rob or steal, it is enough [for that thought] to arise in 
him. This is like w hen someone kills someone else due to one’s order and, even w ithout 
his know ledge, w hen the latter dies, the negativity of the actual [path of action] befalls 
the one having given the order.  
 
This is the mind of thinking, “Now it‟s mine, I ha ve attained it.”  Another example: I 
borrow €100 from Ven. Tharchin with a mind that does want to give it back, but as 
time goes by I forget about it. Ven. Tharchin however, has not forgotten and thinks, 
“When will he give it back? Will he ever give it back ?” Later on Ven. Tharchin thinks, 
“He doesn‟t want to give it back and I‟m too embarrassed to ask for it, so he can have 
it.” In same moment that Ven. Tharchin has let go, the €100 has become mine. If in 
the future Ven. Tharchin comes and by various method s tries to take the money back 
by force, this would be stealing from me! This is important as it can happen to us, so 
we have to be careful.  
 The point is that if you have a mind that gives something away or accepts that 
the object belongs to the other pe rson, it is no longer yours. If you then try to retrieve 
it, and the owner has a mind of, “I can‟t remember, but if he‟s asking me then it‟s 
probably true” then they pay it to you, this is stealing because you have no right to it. 
This can easily happen, s o we should be careful.  
QUESTION : But the other person doesn‟t know that it belongs to them.  
GEN: The other person doesn‟t need to know that it belongs to them in order for it to 
belong to them. A king will not know how much money he has in his treasury, but if 
you steal from it, you are still stealing from the king. As there are these differences, we 
have to be extremely careful.  
QUESTION : What about if Gen -la borrows something, but then forgets he borrowed it , 
and Ven. Tharchin  also forget s he lent it ? It loo ks like there is no completion from 
Gen-la‟s side.  
GEN: There is no completion, no motivation, and no attitude, so none of this is 
stealing. If I pretend that I have forgotten, then this would qualify as the action of 
stealing.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 388 – week one to seven   Further, it says in the text that if you send someone else to kill a person, but you 
are unaware if the murderer has succeeded or not, by the fact that the person is dead 
qualifies as a completion. On the other hand, with the action of stealing there is a 
difference in that y ou have to think, “Now it belongs to me.” You yourself have to 
generate this mind.  
 Say there is €100 in the gompa. It happens to be mine, but I‟m not aware of that 
- I think it‟s someone else‟s €100, and so I decide to take it. In this case, the wish to 
take it is there, I generated the mind, “Now I have it,” but because the basis is wrong, 
the actual negativity does not come about. However, because these other minds were 
there, what comes about is called a fault of the performance. There are many cases l ike 
this.

2B4B -2B1A -2B1B-2A1A -4C1 The actual performance  
The performance consists in indicating something by saying it, by making sure not to 
say anything, or through body language.  
 
Lying is distorting the truth by:  
► saying you have not seen something you have seen  
► saying y ou have not heard something you have heard  
► saying you have not encountered something you have encountered  
And so on. Actually, it is quite easy to tell what lying is. Within that, there are 
differences of the strength of a lie. For example, there is som ething called the great lie, 
which is someone purporting to have realisations when they do not, with an aim to 
confuse someone. . So, there are grades from great lies to small lies.  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
391 2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1A -4C2 Clearing up doubts  
A Clearing up the doubt that it depends on the purpose  
[B Three approaches concerning the distinction between the path of action being complete 
or not if you enjoin someone else to lie]  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1A -4C2A  Clearing up the doubt that it depends on the purpose  
Moreover, [in the s criptures] it says that it is the same whether you say it for your ow n 
or someone else’s purposes.  
 
If it‟s for your own sake, or for the sake of someone else, there is no difference in that 
they are both lying. If we think, “There is no need to tell lies for my own sake, but for 
this person it‟s ok to distort the truth a little bit.” With this action, you have 
accumulated the act of lying.  
 
QUESTION : There  is this story about a monk walking  in the woods and a deer runs by . 
Then a hunter comes by and asks where the deer went  and the monk says , “The deer 
went over there”, pointing in the opposite direction to where the deer went.  The monk 
was fully aware it wasn‟t true, but said this in order to save the deer‟s life. Was this a 
lie? 
GEN: Yes, this was a lie.   
QUESTION : Was this a negativity?  
GEN: Yes, this was a negativity – he accumulated a negativity because he lied.  
QUESTION : Did this monk accumulate merit?  
GEN: Yes, this monk accumulated merit. It‟s that same as the story we all know about 
when the Buddha was a bodhisattva, and on a boat he killed this one person to save 
many. The killing itself is a negative act. There is nothing that can change a negative 
act into not being a negative act. The Buddha on this boat, or the monk in the forest - 
they both accumulated the respective negativity, but due to a positive motivation they 
also accumulated oceans of merits.  
Here we are not talking about what is a big lie or  not, but just what a lie actually 
is. The monk knew full well that the deer had gone in the other direction. There are 
other versions of this story that the monk pretended not to hear properly and said 
something like, “What?” or, “I can‟t hear you, come c loser”, and in the meantime the 
deer had escaped. In this case, we are not talking about the strength of the lie, but just 
identifying what a lie is.  
QUESTION : Which of the three poisons would have been present for this monk?  
GEN: For something to be a l ie, the three poisons don‟t have to be present. Here we 
are talking about a lie that is one of the ten non -virtuous actions. For this, you have to 
have all these together. Killing, in terms of the ten non -virtuous actions is killing a 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 392 – week one to seven  human. Stealing in th is context is stealing something of worth. Lying in this context 
means all these aspects have to come together. In general, the ten non -virtuous actions 
are very strong forms of all these actions. For something to be any of these it must 
come with a strong  wish to harm the other person.  
With regard to lying, it doesn‟t matter if it is for your own sake or for the sake 
others , in both cases it is still a lie.  Moreover, [in the scriptures] it says that it is the same 
whether you say it for your own or someon e else’s purposes.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1A -4C2B  Three approaches concerning the distinction between the actual path of 
action being incomplete if you enjoin someone else to do it  
1 The approach of the Treatise on the Grounds  
Here it is stated that w ith respec t to lying, divisive speech, and harsh w ords, even 
through enjoining [someone else], these three w ill come about.  
2 Vasubandhu’s approach  
In the Treasury , the root text and its Commentary , it is explained that all four 
associated w ith speech become paths of action through enjoining [someone else to 
perform them].  
3 The approach of the vinaya is to join them to the downfalls  
In the Vinaya  it is stated that in order to produce a complete dow nfall, you yourself 
must speak.  
 
For the non -virtuous actions of s peech, it would seem like you have to say them 
yourself, but this is not so. For example, let‟s say I tell Ven. Tharchin to go and say 
some harsh words to Ven. Gendun. If Ven. Tharchin then goes to Ven. Gendun and 
says these harsh words, although I have no t actually said them myself, I still 
accumulate the negativity of engaging in the action of harsh speech. The same applies 
to the other non -virtuous actions of speech; lying, divisive speech and idle gossip. This 
also applies to emails and SMS text message s, and maybe this is even worse than saying 
things directly. The results of this will come later.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1A -4D Completion  
1 The actual completion  
The completion consists in the other person understanding [w hat has been said].  
2 Clearing up d oubts  
The Commentary on the Treasury  explains that, if it is not understood, it becomes merely idle 
talk. This is the same with divisive speech and harsh words . 
 
For example, I don‟t know enough English to express harsh words, so I have to say 
them in Tibetan. As you don‟t understand Tibetan words, you won‟t be offended 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
393 because no matter what I say you won‟t understand it. As a result, it is not the 
complete action  of harsh speech. According to the Commentary on the Treasury  it 
becomes the action of idle talk, like singing songs or something like that.  
 For example, if I say to one of you, “kheyrang la tsa tshang yo ma re ” you won‟t 
understand it, so there is no ha rm done. This actually means, “You don‟t have all your 
channels!” which in Tibetan is quite an insult. You can also say, “You have one 
channel to many!” or “You‟re short of a channel!” By now, Ven. Tharchin knows what 
this means so he can be insulted, but when I say these sounds to you, you won‟t even 
have the recognition of this being harsh speech so you won‟t be offended. This is why 
the text states that the person must understand the harsh words otherwise it can‟t 
qualify as a complete action of harsh sp eech.  
If you are alone in your room and you use all possible abusive words about 
someone, then it also doesn‟t qualify as harsh speech because no one is there to 
understand the words. This then qualifies as idle gossip.  
 In the context of killing someone,  the other person has to die in order for the 
completion to take place. In the context of stealing, the completion is thinking, “Now 
it is mine.”  
 
QUESTION : Is suicide killing?  
GEN: No, because as the basis has to be someone else, it is not complete. Howev er, it 
is something close called the preparation or the application.   
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1A -5 Divisive speech  
A Basis  
The basis of divisive speech are sentient beings who are in agreement or in 
disagreement w ith each other.  
 
The basis here is sentient bei ngs that are harmonious. The action then is to destroy 
this harmony by your words.  
A second type of basis is sentient beings who are not in harmony , and you use 
speech to separate them further. This is a particularly evil type of divisive speech.  
  
B Attitude  
Among the three [aspects] belonging to the attitude, the discrimination and affliction 
are as above.  
 { 105}  The motivation consists in the w ish to divide sentient beings in agreement 
and the w ish that those in disagreement not to be reconciled.

The performance consists in expressing either pleasant or unpleasant subject matter 
w ith either true or untrue w ords and to say them for either one’s ow n or others’

You could perform this by friendly, smooth talking speech. The point is that even if 
you use this, the motivation is to separate harmonious people, or to further separate 
those who are already disharmonious. Irrespective of how sweet sounding your words 
may be, it still constitutes divisive speech because the motivation is a negative one.

2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1A -6 Harsh words

This means that we have this hostile attitude, and they are basis on which this attitude 
has arisen.

With respect to the attitude, discrimination and affliction are as above. The

C Performance  
The performance consists in speaking unpleasantly, either truthfully or falsely, about 
[the person’s] faults of extraction, body, ethical dis cipline or behaviour.  
 
You might put the other person down regarding their physical features, like race, or 
religion etc. Ethical discipline  here refers to someone possibly having faulty ethical 
discipline, and you point this out with an attitude to harm the other person. It was said 
that discrimination and affliction are as above  so one of these afflictions has to be 
present. If you want to help the other person, with a gentle mind, then of course you 
can address faulty ethical discipline or transgression s. However, here we have someone 
attacking a person who has flaws in his ethical discipline with a mind wanting to bring 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
395 him down or harm him. This is not pointing out their faulty behaviour in order to help 
them overcome their faults, but rather to bring them down and harm them.  
  
D Completion  
The completion consists in the one to w hom it w as said understanding the meaning.  
 
This we already mentioned, the other person has to understand the meaning of your 
speech.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1A -7 Idle talk  
A Basis  
The basis of idle talk is a meaningless topic.  
 
Here you talk of things that are of no benefit to others and waste their time. It‟s not 
that you have to have the intention to waste their time or harm them. For example, 
Ven. Tharchin might come to my room and, as a result, he interrupts me and stops me 
doing my practice. Or Ven. Yonten goes to Ven. Tharchins‟ room and chats. He‟s not 
trying to harm him, but the result is that it stops him from doing whatever virtuous 
activity he would have done otherwise. A ctually, Ven. Tharchin likes this and goes 
along with it. The effect is that time is wasted, despite there not being any intention to 
harm.  
  
B Attitude  
Although with regard to discrimination, [one] of the three [aspects] belonging to the 
attitude, nothin g has been stated other than “[it] is the discrimination of that as 
that”, here it is to talk having discriminated the very topic one wishes to express as 
that, because for this  there is no need for someone’s understanding. The mental 
affliction is any of  the poisons. The motivation is the w ish to talk disconnectedly and 
randomly.  
 
C Performance  
The performance consists in setting about uttering idle talk.  
 
D Completion  
The completion is to finish uttering the idle talk.  
 
TRANSLATOR : I don‟t think this is talking about an example of finishing after an hour

                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 396 – week one to seven  GEN: In order for something to qualify as idle talk, it does not have to be understood 
by someone else. For example, you cou ld be alone in your room, and you just talk to 
yourself. However, to be the idle talk of the ten non -virtuous actions, it does have to 
be understood and interfere with someone.   
 
That was the four non -virtuous actions of speech, and now the text refers to  the four 
of mind, but as our time is up, I‟ll just mention these in brief:  
► Covetousness is a mind that whatever it encounters says, “Oh, I would like to have 
this.” Or, “What if this would happen to me!” You see some clothes or a nice car and 
think, “H ow wonderful it would be for me to have these clothes or that car”  
► Ill will (malice) is a mind that has the wish to bring some harm to someone else. 
Whatever you are planning, either physically or verbally, having the wish to do so is ill 
will.  
► Wrong  views are mainly referring to despising or denying that there is cause and 
effect of actions. Alternatively, we can say it is seeing an existent as a non -existent.  
Turning away from these ten non -virtuous constitutes the ten virtuous actions. 
The ten vir tuous actions are abandoning killing, stealing, sexual misconduct, and 
abandoning those of speech that are lies, divisive speech, harsh speech and idle gossip 
through generating a mind of, “I‟m not going to do these”. As well as abandoning 
covetousness, th rough knowing contentment and not following desire, abandoning ill 
will, through giving up any kind of wish to harm others or wrong view through 
cultivating a mind of, “I‟m going to hold onto the undeceiving view of the cause and 
effect of actions.”  
 
  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
397 WEEK 6: 1  4 JUNE  2010  
 
TUESDAY  1 JUNE  2010  - SESSION 1 
 
Do not commit any non -virtuous actions,  
Perform only perfect virtuous actions,  
Subdue your mind thoroughly - 
This is the teaching of the buddha.  
 
In a nutshell, the first line means to abandon any harm to others, and instead to 
benefit them. We all came here because we want to take on what was shown by the 
buddha. We want to put the essence, which is not to commit any non -virtuous actions 
of body, speech and mind, into practice. You have to check th at none of these actions 
are negative, or in short, we have to make sure we do not harm others.  
Perform only perfect virtuous actions : We want to go to the side of virtue with our 
actions of body, speech and mind, or in short, generate a mind that wants to  benefit 
others as much as possible. This is the teaching of the buddha. We have come here to 
listen, practice and take on the teachings of the buddha, and therefore I myself will 
abandon harm to others, and bring them as much benefit as possible. This is the 
motivation with which we came here, Now let us cultivate this awhile.  
 
 
 
Now let‟s meditate like this: For any perverted view, like covetousness or wanting to 
harm others or wrong views, I will rely as much as possible on the antidotes to these 
mistak en minds, and in this way I will completely abandon all the seven non -virtuous 
actions of body and speech. Having dealt with these, I will then go directly against 
these views of covetousness, wrong view and ill will, and through relying on the 
antidotes g o so far as to destroy even the most subtle wrong views and thereby attain 
liberation. This is what we want to do; through relying on the antidotes, we find the 
final liberation from all problems.  
 
 

                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 398 – week one to seven   
In addition, we reflect like this: Samsara is in the n ature of suffering; here we talk 
about the six realms of migration within samsara. All of them are suffering. Even if 
you take rebirth in a pleasant migration, in the end it will turn into a cause of suffering. 
Through the practice of virtue, you might hav e good conditions and things to enjoy, 
but in the end, because they are samsara, they will only become a cause for suffering. 
In addition, whatever good companions or entourage you have, they will also become a 
cause for suffering. Wherever you are born, o r live within samsara, whatever you 
enjoy, it is in the nature of suffering because all of it is born from karma and 
afflictions. Therefore, whatever you experience in samsara is in the nature of suffering. 
On the basis of this thought, generate a strong d etermination to free yourself from this 
suffering state; to liberate yourself from karma and afflictions. This you do by 
cultivating the direct antidote of the perfect and correct view.  
We generate a determination to free ourselves by relying on the corre ct view; by 
thinking that whatever amount of time it might take to cultivate this perfect view, to 
counteract these afflictions, I will apply myself to cultivate it, apply it to my mind and 
stay on this path to liberate my mind from samsara.  
 
 
 
Now refle ct like this: I only want happiness and do not want any suffering.  I only 
want to be happy and I only want to escape from my suffering. This is true for all 
living beings; in this respect, they are just like me. Since this is the case, however 
important I  may seem, I am only one being, while sentient beings are limitless, beyond 
number. From this, it is obvious that the happiness of these limitless beings is by far 
more important than my own happiness or my own liberation from suffering. 
Therefore, we gene rate the awareness that it is a faulty thought to give up the 
happiness of all sentient beings for our own sake, while the perfect way to proceed is 
by giving up the happiness of one being in order to achieve the benefit of all other 
sentient beings. This being the case, I will do everything in my power to develop 
bodhicitta or, in other words, from now on I will work for the sake of all sentient 
beings. May I become completely enlightened for their sake.  
In addition, we become aware of our very special st ate of having attained a 
precious human rebirth. We met the teachings of the buddha, and here especially the 
Mahayana teachings; we met a Mahayana virtuous friend and all the conducive 
conditions to practice the Mahayana, and generate bodhicitta have come about. With 

unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
399 this in mind, we generate the thought to attain enlightenment for the sake of all 
sentient beings.  
 
 
 
Now we reach out to all the buddhas and their sons of the ten directions, request their 
attention, and say the following: “Just as you first generated bodhicitta in the past, and 
on the basis of this generated the vast activities of bodhisattvas to bring about 
enlightenment, now I want to generate the mind of bodhicitta that is longing for 
buddhahood, and after having generated this I want to i mmediately engage in all those 
vast activities. In this way, I want to become completely enlightened. So please, 
buddhas and bodhisattvas, listen to this!”  
 
 
 
We strongly generated bodhicitta, just like the previous buddhas and bodhisattvas have 
done, and  pledged that we ourselves will engage into the same vast activities as they 
did. Through this, we have been born into the clan, or family of the Buddhas, and 
committed to only engage in activities that are in accordance with this family. This 
means we wil l shun all types of motivations of self -cherishing or any mind grasping for 
inherent existence, or wrong views. Instead, we will apply antidotes to these minds.  
 
 
 
Due to the faults of the afflictions, we are in this state of being under the power of 
self-grasping and self -cherishing minds. Due to these two factors being present in our 
mind, due to them having the power over us, the happiness in this life is not comi ng 
about. All the unhappiness we experience in this and all future lives is produced by 
these two minds. If we fight to weaken them, happiness can come about and we can 
achieve what we long for; to be free from suffering. However, if we engage into other 
activities instead of applying the antidotes, then suffering will not end just by itself. As 
a result, we should make all possible effort to counteract these two minds.  
  
The buddha taught the dharma by using the two models of the great and the small 
vehic le. Looking at the Mahayana, the main practice is to counteract self -cherishing, 
whereas the main practice of the Hinayana is to rely on antidotes for the ignorance 

                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 400 – week one to seven  that is grasping at an inherent self. Here again we can see how the buddha taught 
about the se two problem minds and their direct antidotes, and in this way, he taught 
the two vehicles and the respective antidotes to the two faults. If we look at the 
fruition of the practice, then with the correct view you can destroy self -grasping and 
achieve li beration from samsara. By eliminating self -cherishing, you have a path that is 
leading to the fruition of the omniscient mind. However much we listen, reflect and 
meditate on the dharma, what it really comes down to is to counteract these two minds 
as much  as possible.  
 These two vehicles could also be called the great and the small support; in the 
Mahayana your practice is to take on a great load and thereby become a carriage for all 
sentient beings. Therefore, it is a great vehicle because you abandon th e attitude to 
think only about your own wellbeing, and instead think about the happiness of all 
other beings. In the small vehicle, this thought is not cultivated; but it is focused on 
oneself only and therefore it is a small vehicle, or a small carriage. With this smaller 
attitude the question is how would you benefit yourself? By getting rid of any 
suffering, i.e. liberating yourself from samsara completely, which you do by 
counteracting the ignorance, which is grasping for a self. This comes about throug h 
the view that understands selflessness. This is the main practice of the Hinayana.  
 
TUESDAY 1 JUNE 2010  - SESSION 2 
QUESTIONS  
 
QUESTION:  Regarding the ten non -virtuous actions, what is the difference between 
the four (basis, attitude, performance, compl etion) being complete or incomplete? Is 
the only difference the strength? Or is there also a difference as to which of the three 
results of a karma can come about?  
GEN:  There can be great differences between them being complete or not. E.g. you 
have a stro ng wish to steal and you see one hundred Euros lying around. The strong 
wish comes up; you engage, take it and bring it to another place. But actually you were 
mistaken; it was your own money. So even though there is the aspect of the attitude, 
the perform ance and the completion (in rejoicing; feeling that you got it), no one was 
harmed. So here, the effect of your action is by far smaller than if you would have 
taken something that actually belongs to someone else.  
 In the same way, let‟s say you mistake a distant tree trunk for a human, 
and a strong wish arises to kill this human. You shoot your gun and feel that you 
achieved the kill. However, here a completion is impossible, as the object can‟t be 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
401 killed. Again, there is no harm done, and as a result, t he effect is very different. 
Another example would be carefully driving a car, but a drunken person falls in front 
of the car and you kill him by hitting him. There is no intention at all in this case. This 
is greatly different from having the wish to kill ; it is almost nothing. However, there is 
a possibility that there is a very tiny negativity created in such a situation, but nothing 
compared to killing with intention. In the case of stealing your own money, there is 
also a small negativity accumulated, as well as in the case of wishing to kill, but no one 
being there.  
 When we refer to the completely ripened effect, it is concerning taking on 
a rebirth. This is a powerful thing, and would not occur in the previous examples as  a 
small negativity cannot c ause a rebirth to come about. It is most likely that in the 
majority of the cases you will have to have the four factors complete in order to bring 
about the effect of a new rebirth.  
 
QUESTION:  If someone takes a Chenrezig Jenang or takes the Mahayana prec epts, but 
has not gone to a refuge ceremony; would that person receive refuge and the jenang in 
that ceremony?  
GEN:  This mainly depends on the persons mind, intention and understanding. It 
doesn‟t depend so much on the ritual itself. If he is someone who has strong faith in 
the three jewels and wants to rely on them; then, since he sees himself as a follower of 
the buddha and goes for these vows, then this is sufficient. But if he has doubt 
whether the buddha or the dharma exist for example, then there wou ld be no mind of 
refuge and he could not receive the vows due to not having the refuge, the basis for 
the vows. Nevertheless, if he thinks he received the vows and tries to keep them, then 
still he will accumulate virtue. In addition, he would not receive the actual jenang, but 
get a strong imprint.  
 It is ok if he later takes refuge in a formal way, even though, through his 
faith he might have already taken refuge in the jenang. Actually, this is even better. 
Another point in this context is that there is  something called the one -day vows. This 
is different from the eight Mahayana precepts, and it is unsuitable for a monk because 
ordained people have a higher form of vows. However, to guard one‟s refuge vows and 
continuously say that one goes for refuge un til one dies is of course perfectly fine as a 
monastic. These one -day vows have to guard eight precepts, and you can also regard 
them as a purification and restoration ceremony. However, they are done without the 
motivation of bodhicitta. This is the diffe rence to the Mahayana sojong as it doesn‟t 
have the Mahayana precepts. On some occasions, these eight one -day vows are being 
given, and then ordained people are not allowed to participate.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 402 – week one to seven   
QUESTION:  Setting someone else on performing a negative action; I n sexual 
misconduct it is not clear whether you can encourage someone to do this and if so, if 
one also incurs this karma?  
GEN:  No, this is not possible with regards to sexual misconduct. This has to be 
performed with one‟s own body. The same is true for the three non -virtues of mind. 
They need to come up in your own mind; you can‟t encourage someone else to do 
them.  
 The point i s that if you set someone else on killing, then your goal is to 
kill the other person. In sexual misconduct, you are looking for an experience. So 
someone else can‟t make this experience for you. The goal of sexual misconduct is the 
experience of the bliss  of desire. This is not achieved by sending someone else to do it.  
In the case of a General sending his soldiers out to rape women; this General would be 
in a state of ill will, but he would incur no result of sexual misconduct in this case. If 
he would se t his soldiers on wounding others, then this also would be a clear -cut case 
of ill will. Ill will is the second most heavy of the ten non -virtuous actions. [translators 
note: The actual word in Tibetan is “a mind of harm”]  Within this, you have strong form s 
like anger or jealousy; all these strong negative minds are included within this.  
Wrong view is the strongest of the ten non -virtues, and especially refers to a denial of 
cause and effect of actions. Such a person will only think about this life and eng age 
into much negativity as a result. Therefore, this is the strongest negative mind, since it 
prevents you from eliminating the negative states of mind and developing positive 
ones.  
However, the mind that wants to harm can produce a result even if you ac cept the law 
of cause and effect. If you have this type of mind that only wishes to harm, you can 
forget about any mind wanting to benefit others. Killing, stealing and sexual 
misconduct have a very strong relationship with this mind. Lying, divisive speec h and 
harsh words definitely also have a strong connection with this mind wanting to harm, 
but idle talk is a bit of a special case, as there are all kinds of it;  I would see these first 
three non -virtues of speech as a form idle talk as well. In general, we can see how all 
these negative actions have a strong connection with the mind that wishes to harm. 
Therefore, if the mind wanting to harm is not present, then you are not committing 
the respective action.  
 If for example, I say a lot of harsh words to s omeone, then I can say as many as I 
want, but if I don‟t have the intention to harm, then there will be no negativity 
accumulated. The other person will also be aware of there being no harmful intent, and 
therefore it will be a basis for laughter and jokin g. However, if he has this wish to 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
403 harm, then even in just saying a small word, this would qualify as this non -virtuous 
action. It just depends on the intention of the person‟s mind.  
    
QUESTION:  It is said that refuge is a mind that is of the type that is faith of 
conviction. Why is that so?  
GEN:  When someone goes for refuge, why does he do that? Because of all the fears of 
samsara; the lower realms, etc. If you then ask this person if he has faith in receiving 
protection from the three jewels, then of c ourse he will say yes. Therefore, this is why 
it is a faith of conviction. With the other forms of faith, like clear faith, you would 
have to clearly see the qualities of the three jewels, which comes later on and is a 
higher form of faith.  
 
QUESTION:  I assumed that first one generates clear faith and then the faith of 
conviction and then aspiring faith. This seems to be the sequence in the text.  
GEN:  In the text it comes in this sequence, but think about these different types of 
faith; it is called cl arifying faith because if it is generated, the mind becomes clear; the 
aspiring (or manifestly wishing) faith refers to the results which you really wish for; 
this is also a specific type. However, here we just talk about a conviction in the 
capacity of th e three jewels to protect you. Clear faith especially refers to a state where 
the mind has inspiration or faith without being helped, in some ways it comes naturally 
through perceiving the qualities of the three jewels; it is almost out of your control.  
 
QUESTION:  If I see someone else‟s possession, I want to possess it, and then I see 
the same thing in a shop and want to buy it. Are these both cases of covetousness?  
GEN:  If you go and buy this object, it is ok. But don‟t think about the other person‟s 
possession. One of the five types of covetousness is if you own something, and then 
you clearly decide not to share this. Another type is if you constantly want to have this 
or that, which shows a complete lack of contentment. Then there is another form 
cove tousness of tricking or using deceit towards someone to give you something they 
own, because you have the wish to have it.  
QUESTION:  One case of sexual misconduct is to use the wrong orifice. So if a couple 
does this, then there is no wish to harm. So thi s would be a case of sexual misconduct 
that is not ill will, right?  
GEN:  There are many forms of sexual misconduct, which might not be connected with 
ill will, but if it is sexual misconduct that is included within the ten non -virtuous 
actions, then it is connected with ill will.  
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 404 – week one to seven  QUESTION:  Isn‟t it that sexual misconduct can be done with any of the three poisons 
as a motivation, and therefore doesn‟t have to be ill will (i.e. a mind wishing to harm)?  
GEN:  Again, what we have here in our text is a general p resentation of what sexual 
misconduct is. In order to be sexual misconduct within the ten non -virtuous actions, it 
needs to be a mind that wishes to harm. This does not become clear from the text 
itself, which broadly lists what could fall under sexual mis conduct, but to be a sexual 
misconduct, which is included in the ten non -virtuous actions, it has to be connected 
with ill will. This explanation is transmitted like this by holy lamas like His Holiness 
the Dalai Lama.  
 
QUESTION:  So then, in the context o f accumulating the causes for a precious human 
rebirth; would it therefore be enough to abandon sexual misconduct as included in the 
ten non -virtuous actions (i.e. connected with the wish to harm)?  
GEN:  Yes, it is like this. On the other hand, if you are a ble to guard yourself from any 
type of sexual misconduct, then this is the best, and this approach will also increase 
the quality of your precious human rebirth.  
 In general, we have the explanation that there is benefit in practicing 
patience as this is n ecessary for a beautiful body or to be reborn in a beautiful area.  
 
QUESTION:  From a small negativity, like accidently running over a person while 
you‟re driving, at some point can a ripening result occur, even though this was not 
possible from the initia l negativity?  
GEN:  Such a strong result of ripening into another rebirth will not come from such a 
small negativity, even through strong increase. While karma is strongly increasing, such 
a small karma will never increase to such a strength that it could q ualify as a cause for 
a rebirth. In comparison to the quality of karma accumulated by having the wish to 
kill, such a result will never come about by this small negativity.  
 For example, we are able to see what might come about from the story of 
the arhat Mahamaudgallyana being beaten up. This was the result of such a small un -
purified karma that remained on the mind stream for countless aeons.  
 
QUESTION:  How is it that an action, like killing an animal, can result in being run 
over by a car in a later lif e (as such a car accident is something coming about in the 
outside world, and is not an experience of a happy or sad feeling in the mind)?  
GEN:  We can see this in the context of most problems we experience. We might 
experience cold or heat, have all kinds  of pains, etc. all of this comes through certain 
outer physical conditions. Like being hungry depends on whether there is food or not. 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
405 If we encounter these conditions or not comes about on the basis of our karma. For 
example, we can see how all our sickn esses come from certain conditions. Normally a 
doctor can say that such and such a pain or illness comes about from a certain 
behaviour, diet, etc. These are all outer factors we encountered, like bad relationships 
with our relatives. So we don‟t have to t ake such a big example as being run over by a 
car to look at this.  
 That we are encountering these negative conditions, is due to us having 
accumulated certain negative karmas. Without having accumulated these karmas, we 
couldn‟t encounter these condition s. This talks about adverse conditions that can‟t be 
stopped or avoided in any way, just as one can‟t avoid the car when one is run over. 
However, in situations like being under the sun and being hot, this is something you 
can easily avoid by going inside.  What we are talking about are adverse conditions that 
you can‟t shield yourself against. These come from previously accumulated negative 
karma.  
 
In the case of the car accident, you would already have accumulated the causes for this 
in past lives. In ord er for this to ripen, you then need the condition, which is being 
driven over by the car, which ripens the cause you previously accumulated. This is also 
true for sicknesses for example. However, it is not that our karma produces the 
condition because our karma does not produce the car. It is because we encounter the 
condition that our karma is ripening.  
 We already have this karma accumulated, so when you stand in a certain 
spot and the car is behind you, then the karma will give you a small push and you meet 
the condition. It is like the karma is pushing you in front of the car. Here we are 
talking about a big situation, but actually all forms of experiencing karma come about 
like that. For example at the time of death, the power of karma is right there o n the 
spot, pushing you into the next life; there is no gap between this life and the next. The 
moment the karma for this life is used up, you are pushed into the next by another 
karma.  
 The tenth link of the twelve links of dependent arising is becoming (or 
existence), this is actually addressed as being karma  because it is the one that produces 
the eleventh link, birth, in the next moment. This comes right at the moment of death; 
at the last moment, there is existence, and then the next moment birth.  
 
QUESTION:  Would the action of accidently running someone over with a car be a 
karma that is created but not accumulated?  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 406 – week one to seven  GEN:  Yes, it is like this. This is similar to dream experiences, where you might go out 
to kill someone, but the result is not achieve d. 
 
QUESTION:  To purify negative karma, is it enough to regret in general actions of 
body, speech and mind, or is it necessary to remember the specific negativity that one 
has done?  
GEN:  It is best to remember the specific action. But still, if you have th e wish in 
general to purify the negative actions of body, speech and mind, then this will also 
work.  
 If you purify, then the karmas become non -existent. If you make a small 
purification, a small part gets purified; if you engage in extensive purification a big part 
gets purified. If you purify, then no result will come about.  
 
QUESTION:  I understand that if a monk hands back his vows because he feels he 
does not have the right motivation, it would not work. On the other hand, if a monk 
loses his vows by ki lling, this would work. Why is it like this?  
GEN:  Who said that your vows would become non -existent if you kill someone? Do 
you become non -existent if you kill someone else? You might get arrested by the 
police, but you will not become non -existent. In the  same way, if you kill someone else 
you have then committed the strongest fault, but you did not lose your vows. You will 
no longer be allowed to wear your robes, but you will not lose your vows.  
 It is the same situation if you still steal something. You  don‟t lose your 
vows, but you do severely damaged them. The point is that you did something contrary 
to your root vows. Losing vows is meant in the sense of degenerating. However, it 
does not mean that they become non -existent. In the same way, someone mi ght lose 
his strength, but this does not mean that he becomes non -existent. As a result, losing 
the vows refers to vows that were previously good or perfect, but then they degenerate 
into something that is not good at all.  
 Some causes for losing vows are  dying, giving them back, and developing 
a strong wrong view in which one is not accepting cause and effect. As faith in the 
view of cause and effect is the basis for virtue, there is no way to generate virtue 
without it,  and since the vows are virtue, th e root is kind of lost. There is a verse from 
the Gelong Sojong that refers to losing the vows, and this should be checked. Another 
cause of losing the vows is that two signs appear on three occasions (i.e. the two sexes; 
meaning one becomes a hermaphrodit e three times). Here the point is that if this 
happens, then the mind is in a very unstable state, and as a result the mind can no 
longer act as a basis for holding the vows.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
407  If a monk were to have sexual contact with a woman, a root vow would 
be transgr essed. This is a defeat. Such a person is no longer a monk, but still he has 
the vows. In this case, he definitely has to offer the vows back, because otherwise he 
stays with this degenerated vow and constantly accumulates negativities. The vow he 
has tran sgressed is kind of finished. But this is only one vow. He has not transgressed 
the root of stealing, expressing a great lie or killing. He has only transgressed the 
celibacy vow, and he can offer back this vow. He is no longer a monk, and this one 
vow is so utterly degenerated, so if he doesn‟t offer it back, he will continuously 
accumulate negativities. However, he could decide to keep the others from which he 
will accumulate virtue. He could offer all the vows back, but he could also just offer 
the one h e has destroyed (i.e. utterly degenerated). The essential point here is to forget 
about offering back your vows, as it has to be a very strong situation for this to be 
possible. We have the attitude that we will guard our vows even at the cost of our 
lives . 
 
WEDNESDAY  2 JUNE  2010  - SESSION 1 
He w ho caring w ith compassion,  
Taught the holy dharma, so that  
All w rong view s w ould be abandoned:  
Gautama, homage to Him.  
 
Ignorance is a phenomenon that is grasping at, or apprehending, something as having a 
self. Fir stly, we have to understand that ignorance is focusing on the self. Secondly, we 
have to understand that it is apprehending this self as something that is standing out 
from amongst the fives aggregates, as something that is not dependent on them. In this 
way, it grasps at this self. It is focusing on the dependently arisen self but then 
apprehends it as independent. Like this, we have the root wrong view, and on the basis 
of this come all the other wrong views. Like that, there are no previous or future liv es 
for this self, or that the actions done by this self don‟t have any effect, or that the four 
noble truths don‟t exist, i.e. that there is no liberation for this self and especially that 
suffering and its causes can‟t be averted. This is the point where we are in the text, 
where the tenth of the ten non -virtuous actions is this wrong views.  
 We can see how all the negative wrong views arise from the basic wrong 
view of ignorance that is focusing on the self, but then misapprehends it as 
independent. These  are the wrong views that the prayers speak about, and which the 
buddha states we need to abandon as he perceives with great compassion that all 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 408 – week one to seven  beings have numerous forms of these wrong views, and the suffering that comes about 
as a result of them.  
 
One process goes like this: due to this ignorance, we grasp for a self, which is 
independent, sticking out amongst the aggregates, and therefore is findable separate 
from them. This self is perceived as permanent, and there is grasping for permanence. 
From thi s comes the view that denies cause and effect of actions, i.e. one says that 
there is no such person which produces virtuous or non -virtuous actions. Since this 
self is conceived as permanent, it can‟t be affected by anything or affect anything else, 
and t herefore it can‟t accumulate virtue and negativity. In addition, it is not coming 
from anywhere or going anywhere, i.e. future lives are negated. So apart from saying 
that it is created by a god, there is no possibility to assert such a permanent self as 
something that‟s come from causes and conditions. As a result, this permanent self is 
leading to wrong views, which deny things that exist.  
 
Within this, as there is no accumulation of virtue and non -virtue, there are also no 
effects for this person. By gra sping for this permanent self, you end up with all these 
wrong views, and unable to accept all the presentations and practices of what to adopt 
and what to abandon. In this way, the whole topic of practice becomes impossible, and 
from this strong effects w ill come about. The buddha was aware of this, and with his 
great compassion, he taught the dharma to destroy these wrong views.  
 Therefore, we have to rely on what is called a perfect view. How would 
one gain this? One needs to have an interest in this se lf, to ask what its nature, its 
entity is. Where does it abide? Moreover, we have to especially ask where we would 
find it if we would look for it. This would include looking in the continuity‟s previous 
and later moments as well. Through this, we can conc lude that wherever you would 
look for it, you would be unable to find it, which is quite the opposite from being 
independent from the aggregates. So on the basis of this experience, when you ask 
yourself what this un -finable self is, you then understand th at it is merely labelled on 
the five aggregates. This you can then extend to the other aggregates, which also exist 
as a dependent arising, as merely labelled. Therefore, with regard to the presentation of 
the selflessness of person and phenomena there is a sequence. First, the selflessness of 
person is presented, and having understood that, one can then extend this 
understanding to the selflessness of phenomena. The buddha taught this to benefit all 
sentient beings.  
 
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
409 2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1A -8 Covetousness  
We talked a bit about this yesterday. This mind of covetousness is of the desire type, 
and has to do especially with attachment to resources. In general, we have to be clear 
why covetousness is included in the ten non -virtuous actions. This is because it i s a 
very heavy form of attachment and doesn‟t just talk about not owning things; seeing 
yourself as the owner of something. The fivefold presentation we talked about 
yesterday is presented in the Lam -Rim Chenmo [page 224] . 
 The first one is a mind exceedin gly attached to your own resources, and the 
second is a mind attached to accumulating resources. This refers to someone who 
already has a lot of things, but wants more and more (you could also include 
miserliness here). It also talks about one‟s own resour ces. The third one talks about the 
possessions of someone else: Having a mind of longing due to comprehending or 
experiencing the wealth and so forth of others. Here there is this strong craving for 
someone else‟s possessions. The fourth one in English is to have an envious mind, 
thinking that something that is owned by another should be mine. I think envy 
includes a negative mind towards the other person, that the other person should not 
have what they possess. This is not necessarily the case, although th is envy is very 
close to covetousness. But you can see someone else possessing something and then 
you have a strong longing to have it for yourself, without necessarily feeling that the 
other person should not have it. Let‟s say I want a computer from Thar chin, and then 
I present myself as being his friend in order to obtain it. Of course, as soon as I have 
the computer  the friendship is over. This mind of covetousness is so strong that it 
would even deceive someone else. This actually happens quite frequent ly. If you think 
about it, you might be able to come up with some examples.  
 The fifth one has a connection with envy or jealousy. One has a strong longing 
for others possessions, but there is no way to obtain them; then if the other person has 
problems w ith his possessions, one is happy about this. This is especially connected 
with shamelessness. It reads in the Lam -Rim, “Having a mind that is overcome, due to 
covetousness by shamelessness and obliviousness about the determination to be free 
of the faults  of covetousness.”  
 One example could be that there are two students who feel as if they are in 
competition with each other at school. One fails an exam but the other passes, and 
when the successful one fails, the other is happy. This is not only jealousy,  but also 
covetousness. It might be that these two are actually friends, but in a competition this 
mind might come up. Another example: let‟s say there are two translators; one is 
capable of translating, but has no opportunity because the other is always d oing the 
translator‟s job. It might be the case that the waiting translator has his opportunity 
because the translator becomes sick and he becomes very happy about  that. These two 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 410 – week one to seven  examples don‟t exactly talk about resources (rather any enjoyments or good things) but 
still can be included.  
 Another example is of two people looking for a job, but only one person gets it, 
or gets a better one, and the other person is unhappy about this. Therefore, even 
though the two might be friends in general, jealousy and  covetousness could arise 
because of the strong longing for the job. Another example might be if two people, 
who are friends, buy a car but one person is jealous because the other person has the 
better car, and because of his mind of covetousness wants to possess it.  
In most cases, covetousness and jealousy arise towards people with whom you 
are close. You see that they have something new for example, and you feel you should 
also have it. This situation does not arise so much with people you don't know.  
 
A Basis  
The basis of covetousness is a possession or provision of someone else.  
It is an attachment to resources or objects, but this does not necessarily have to be the 
resources of others. In the text, it is stated like this, but I would hold the opinion  that 
a mind clinging to one‟s own wealth is miserly and being incapable of giving it away is 
also a form of covetousness.  
 
B Attitude  
Among the three [aspects] belonging to the attitude, the discrimination consists in the 
discrimination of that basis as t hat. The affliction is any of the three poisons. The 
motivation is the w ish to make it one’s ow n.  
C Performance  
The performance consists in making and effort for the object of that thought.  
D Completion  
The completion is the thought: “May this possession  etc. be mine! ”  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1A -9 Harmful intent  
A / B   Basis and attitude  
The basis, discrimination and affliction of harmful intent are the same as w ith harsh 
w ords. The motivation is the wish to hit etc. and { 106}  the thought “how nice it 
w ould be if [this person] w as killed or bound or if [his] resources declined by themselves 
or through someone else” and the like.  
The motivation is the wish to harm someone else. We can find many examples and 
levels of this. Small examples can even be found in a c lassroom where two pupils don‟t 
like each other, so if the pen of one falls to the ground, then the other steps on it and 
breaks it. Or if the two walk along with each other, one gives the other a push. A 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
411 stronger example would be if there is a society or group, in which there is someone 
you dislike, and you make all kind of effort to harm him in order for him to leave. This 
is a strongly negative form of this harmful intent. In such a situation, one regularly 
engages in small harmful actions towards this p erson, so that he becomes more and 
more unhappy and finally decides to leave. And even though it is his own decision to 
leave, the reasons are all your harmful actions. It is not easy to purify actions like this 
through mantas. Rather, the way to do it is through generating the fully -fledged four 
opponent powers and within that, you generate the wisdom realising emptiness or 
bodhicitta Other than that it is very difficult to get rid of a negativity like this, and 
therefore you will have to experience its re sult.  
 
QUESTION:  Could a hell rebirth result from such an action, since this seems out of 
proportion, even for such a negative act? Or does that only come about if the karma 
stays on the mind stream and grows for a long time.  
GEN:  Here we can remind ourselves of what the master Shantideva says: “Whatever 
good conduct was amassed over thousand eons is destroyed in one flash of anger”. So 
a mere instance of anger is enough to destroy eons of positive karma. In addition, one 
would say  that due to the amount of moments that the anger lasted, you take that 
many lives in hell. This refers to strong forms of anger. The same is true for harmful 
intent; if it is very strong, it can have a very vast or long lasting result. We have spoken 
abou t examples of harmful intent where you are constantly harming someone until he 
can‟t put up with it anymore, and might even lose his job or he might even die. All of 
these are very strong forms of harmful intent, and therefore would have a very strong 
effect. This is also expressed in the text: “how  nice it would be if [this person] was killed or 
bound or if [his] resources declined by themselves or through someone else”     
 
C Performance  
The performance consists in applying oneself to this thought.  
This g oes beyond the mental level. An example of its meaning could be that you had 
the wish to hit someone, and you now apply yourself to this and really hit him or 
verbally insult the other person to harm him. In this way, the performance is actually a 
physical  or verbal action because if you just have the wish that harm may come to this 
person, but he has no knowledge of it, then there is no harm done. In the same way, if 
I‟m not happy with Tharchin having this computer for example, then I would check 
where it is and destroy it.  
 As I mentioned, the performance could be a physical act or an act of speech. 
The majority of harsh speech for example comes from harmful intent. The three 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 412 – week one to seven  mental non -virtuous actions are encouraging the physical act or the act of speec h; so in 
this way you could say there is an action for which the impulse is one of the three non -
virtues of mind. These three are actually not called karma, or action because an action 
would be physical or verbal. Rather, they are seen as the impulses, whi ch are 
motivating the actual action of body and speech. Therefore, within the ten non -virtues 
the three physical and four verbal would be actions, but the three of mind are just 
motivating an action. There are also passages in the text where it talks about  the seven 
non-virtuous actions. When the three factors that are behind these seven are added on, 
we have ten as a result.

unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
413  
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1A -10C2  Divisions  
In this regard there are four [divisions]:  
A Denying causes  
Denying causes consists in [the view ]: “Excellent conduct and faulty conduct do not 
exist.”  
In other words, virtue and non -virtue are denied. Altern atively, we could say that the 
cause, which is true origins, is denied.  
 
B Denying effects  
Denying effects consists in [the view ]: “Fully ripened effects of those tw o do not exist”.  
This is also especially to be addressed as denying the effects of the tru e sufferings that 
is true origins.  
 
C Denying activities  
Denying activities has three points:  
 1. Denying the activity of plantings seeds consists in [the view ]: “There is no 
planting  of a seed by the father and no retaining of a seed by  the mother “.  
 2. Denying the activity of coming and going consists in [the view ]: “There is no 
coming  from a former existence to this one and no going from this one to a 
future one”.  
 3. Denying the activity of being born consists in [the view ]: “There is no 
intermediate  state of miraculous birth of beings“.  
Miraculous birth refers to not being born from father and mother. An example is to be 
born into the bardo.   
 
D Denying existing things  
Denying existing  things consists in [the view ]: “Arhats and the like do not exist.”  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1A -10D Completion  
The completion is certainty w ith respect to one’s denial.  
 
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A1B Distinction between actions and paths of actio n  
Among them, the intentions in the mind are actions, but no paths of action. The 
seven actions of body and speech are actions, yet they are also paths of action, since 
they are also the basis for the intentions proceeding. The three, covetousness and so on 
are paths of actions, but they are no actions.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 414 – week one to seven  If something is a path of action, then it is a cause. Therefore, the three of mind are 
causing physical actions, and therefore they are paths of actions, but not actions 
themselves. Intention is a mental a ction. Therefore, there are mental actions, but the 
three non -virtues of mind are not mental actions. For example, intention refers to a 
thought of guarding the mind, like thinking that acting in this way is not suitable for 
me; I will not do this. This is  a negation and therefore doesn‟t lead to something else. 
Therefore, it is an action but not a path of action. While the three of mind are 
themselves not mental actions, they are the impulse for an action following from them. 
As a result, they are a path o f action.  
 
QUESTION:  What about the case of someone getting angry, and out of harmful intent 
wishing to say something harmful to another person, but before committing the verbal 
act he stops himself. Would then just having this anger for a while, but not f ollowing 
up with the verbal action, be a complete karma?  
GEN:  In general, there was the advice in the Bodhisattvacharyavatara  that when we are 
disturbed by anger we should become like a piece of wood. This is what you need to 
do; but anyway, you will have a very dark face and from your composure and physical 
actions, your anger will be very clear to the other person. Therefore, you should 
contain yourself for some seconds or minutes, and then deal with it with an analytical 
mind. When you are angry, in fron t of a computer for example, then you will deal with 
it in a rough way, like hitting on the keyboard!  
 
QUESTION:  I understood that it is a path of action when it is a cause, but isn‟t 
intention also a cause, and therefore a path of action?  
GEN:  So now, we are talking about a special cause! This anger, for example, is the 
cause for some negative verbal or physical action. However, from someone restraining 
from acting out his anger we can see that not all forms of harmful intent are 
necessarily the cause of a n action, and are therefore not necessarily the path of an 
action; but in general, harmful intent becomes a path of action.  
 This is similar to the case where we say that from anger you are born in 
the hell realm. However, this does not mean that all anger  necessarily leads to a hell 
rebirth because if it were, we wouldn‟t have any hope! Again, we can‟t say that all types 
of intention are necessarily not a cause for action. There are forms of intention that 
become a cause for action, but in general, they ar e not. An example would be you 
engaging in an action, and by your action an insect is endangered, but you don‟t care, 
you continue your action, and the insect is harmed. Here you are doing, continuing an 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
415 action, and in this process you had this intention w hich becomes the cause or path of 
an action. But in general this is not the case.  
 As an example of how harmful intent is a path of action, let‟s say you 
want the good situation of someone to be destroyed, and you motivate someone else 
to do this for you.  Through your harmful intent, you set in motion this chain of 
actions, and this acted as a cause for the action, the karma of the other person.  
 An example for such an exceptional case where harmful intent is an 
action/karma: First, you have the wish to ha rm the other person, and then set 
someone on destroying their things; this is a path of action, and upon hearing that this 
happened you rejoice. It is this rejoicing that is a mental action of harmful intent. So 
this rejoicing is a case of harmful intent t hat is an action.  
 
QUESTION:  So when is harmful intent complete? Is it just in the thought or does one 
have to engage into a physical or verbal action?  
GEN:  I think the very generation of the harmful intent is the completion.  
 
QUESTION:  What is Gen -la‟s definition of karma?  
GEN:  There is no general definition, but in general we can approach it like this: the 
buddhas come in this world and teach the dharma. None of this is an action/karma. 
Having said this, we can say that a karma is an a ction which has a result of either 
happiness, suffering or a neutral state for the person who acted. The buddha‟s 
happiness is not increased or altered by his activities. His happiness is not dependent 
on his actions.  
 
QUESTION:  Wouldn‟t the definition of  karma be the mental factor of intent?  
GEN:  No, this is a mental factor, right? For example, when using harsh words this is 
also karma of speech. All the seven actions of body and speech are not a mental factor 
because of being form.  
 
QUESTION:  I underst ood that the intention before the physical or verbal action is the 
karma and the actual activity of body or speech is the path of action.  
GEN:  It is similar to this, but not like this. Actually it is opposite from what you are 
saying, but the way you said  it was nice!  
 
The three, covetousness and so on are paths of actions, but they are no actions.  
Therefore, these three are paths of actions, which then bring forth the physical or 
verbal activities, and these activities are actions. The impulses or motiva tions are paths 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 416 – week one to seven  of actions while the actual physical or verbal activity is a karma. Nevertheless, you said 
it nicely; this is a good way to think!  
 
QUESTION:  I heard some western dharma teachers say that karma is defined as any 
movement of the mind. Is tha t wrong?  
GEN:  So what about concentration, this would be excluded here as there is no 
movement; or what about the state of sleep?  
 
QUESTION:  But wouldn‟t no karma be produced in concentration because of the 
mind being so still?  
GEN:  If you are saying that  apart from concentration any other mind is karma, then 
this is not correct. It is not expressed in the Abhidharma  like that. As an example, we 
can look at covetousness. This is a strongly distracted mind, running forcefully after 
objects, and this is clea rly expressed here as not being a karma.  
 
QUESTION:  I understood that since intention is one of the five omnipresent mental 
factors, each moment of mind is producing karma.  
GEN:  We can say that every mind is performing some activity; has some intention. 
Even a mind of concentration has that. We could also say that every mind has an 
action, but this is not the action we are talking about here in this context of the ten 
non-virtuous actions. Therefore, there seems to be a difference between karma in 
general,  and that every mind is performing an action or activity.  In addition, since 
every mind engages an object, there is activity with every mind. However, this is not 
what we are talking about when we talk about karma. [Translators note: Gen -la makes a 
differ ence between the word cha -wa, which I translate as activity , and le which is karma/action]  So 
every mind has an activity, at least that of engaging the object.  
 
QUESTION:  Gen-la said that the performance in the case of harmful intent is that one 
applies on eself to a physical or verbal action; this goes into the realm of activity, and 
therefore should not only be a path of action but action itself. Is that correct?  
GEN:  We have the two stages: first, the wish to say something harmful; this is the 
actual harm ful intent, and then you engage into the harmful speech. The performance 
is the verbal action of harmful speech because it was motivated by harmful intent. If it 
was not, it would be something you laugh about. But still, the actual performance (i.e. 
saying  the rough words) is not a form of harmful intent, but the verbal action of harsh 
speech.  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
417 QUESTION:  What is the difference between virtuous karma and merit?  
GEN:  Cultivating compassion and bodhicitta for example, are not karma but are merit; 
the highest f orm of merit. Bodhicitta is not karma because in order to be karma it has 
to be a mental factor, and bodhicitta is a main mind. There is clearly no physical or 
verbal action, and even no mental action since in order for it to be a mental action it 
has to b e a mental factor.  Further on, we have the wisdom realizing emptiness which 
is a mental factor but is not karma. However, together with this there is karma. Then 
you have the different mental factors of feeling, which are also not karma, but mental 
factor s. In addition, compassion is not karma, as it is of the class of feeling.

2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A2 Differences in weight  
A The weight of the ten paths of action  
B Incidentally: Summary indication of the gates of strong karma  
The second one – differences in w eight – has tw o points: the w eight of the ten paths of 
action and incidentally: { 107}  summary indication of the gates of strong karma.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A2A  The weight of the ten paths of action  
On the one hand, actions are heavy because of the cause, i.e. the motivation of the 
three poisons. Another reason is due to the actual performance. It is stronger to kill 
than to steal, and there is also a difference with regards to the object the negative 
action is directed towar ds. It is for example stronger to kill a human being, than to kill 
an animal. If the object is special, like your guru or your parents for example, then this 
also makes the action heavier; so these are fields towards which it is possible to 
commit a heinou s crime, an immeasurable negativity.   
Therefore, all the ten non -virtuous actions can be classified into great, middling 
or small negativities. Let‟s look at how an action can be especially heavy. To kill an 
animal because you want its skin or flesh is ru led by a motivation of a strong 
attachment. Alternatively, killing it because you dislike it is ruled by a motivation of 
anger, or you do it through being completely obscured to the fact that there are effects 
coming from actions. Just by catching the anim al, you generate great joy, and now you 
want to engage in killing it, and think nothing of doing it in a very barbaric way. In 
fact, you rejoice in this too. This would be a case of an action being very heavy.  
For example, in Spain there are bullfights in  which the bull is killed in a very 
gruesome way, and as a result, the person killing the bull probably accumulates very 
heavy negative karma. Firstly, this person engages in this because of attachment to 
fame, and then the killing might be done on the bas is of strong anger, because at one 
point in the performance the bull wounded him. Moreover, the actual killing is done in 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 418 – week one to seven  a slow way, playing around with the bull until the audience rejoices in its death, and 
the actions of the Torero as if he was the king  of Spain. All of this is very heavy. If the 
bull should kill the Torero, there can also be heavier or lighter karma, but the bull just 
has no choice. Just as we have discussed here,  it can also be applied to the other non -
virtuous actions.  
First: In Actu al Basis of the Levels six w ays of being w eighty are stated.  
1 Strong compounding  
Strong compounding is the motivation of actions by the strong presence or absence of 
the three poisons.  
 
2 Habituation  
Habituation is have resorted to virtuous and non -virtuous actions for a long time and 
to have familiarised w ith them many times.  
 
3 Essence [of the action]  
As for their essence, w ith the seven actions of body and speech each preceding one is 
w eightier than the subsequent one, w hereas w ith the three actions  of mind, a 
subsequent one is w eightier than the preceding one.  
 
4 Basis  
The basis is benefiting or harming the Buddha, the Dharma, the Sangha, gurus etc.  
 
5 One -sided  fixation on  the discordant  side  
A one -sided  fixation on  the discordant  side consists in spending one’s w hole life 
adopting non -virtuous actions w ith one -sided determination and not even doing one 
single virtue.  
 
6 Elimination of the discordant  side  
To eliminate the discordant  side is to give up the side of non -virtue, to become free of 
attachment, and train in virtuous actions. In the Letter to a Friend it also says:  
Persistence, strong adherence, no counter -agent,  
A basis w ith foremost qualities – actions that spring from  
[Them] a re the five great types of non -virtue and virtue.  
From them endeavour to practice virtuous behaviour!  [47] 
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
419 Moreover there are five because of the division [of the basis] into tw o: (1) a basis 
endow ed w ith good qualities, such as the three jew els, and a ba sis of help, such as 
one’s parents.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A2B  Incidentally : Summary indication of the gates of strong karma  
1 Great strength by way of the field  
2 Great strength by way of the support  
3 Great strength by way of the thing  
4 Great strength by way  of the attitude  
The second one has four points: great strength by w ay of the field, great strength by 
w ay of the support, great strength by w ay of the thing, and great strength by way of the
