2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A2B -1 Great strength by way of the fie ld 
If you engage in an action of generosity, or even a small virtuous action, towards your 
parents or your guru, then the result is very strong, just as it is when you perform a 
non-virtuous action towards them. In the case, someone steals from a stupa or the 
buddha, but after a number of days he generates regret and it is returned. By offering 
back it becomes completely purified. However, if you steal from the shangha, 
especially things that are needed or difficult to obtain, this would be a negativity so 
strong that it would lead to a rebirth in the lower realms.  
 
A The field of the three jewels  
First: the three jew els, the guru, someone like the guru, parents, and so forth : [are 
gates of strong karma] because w ith these, even if the thoughts for them a re not 
intense and the benefit or harm done [to them] are small, the merit { 108}  and misdeed 
are great. In this regard it says in Establishment of Mindfulness that the karma of 
stealing even a little thing from the three jew els is purified - in the case of  the Buddha 
and the Dharma - if it is returned.  
B The Field of the Sangha  
How ever, in the case of the Sangha it does not get purified until the feeling [of the 
effect] is experienced – in the case of something belonging to the Sangha. Furthermore 
it is sta ted that, if it was a means of providing for the mouths of the Sangha, one is 
born in the great hell and otherw ise one is born in the great darkness surrounding 
Without Respite.  
C The field of bodhisattvas  
Bodhisattvas are an extremely pow erful field of virtue and non -virtue. In the Sutra of 
the Seal of Engagement in the Generation of the Pow er of Confidence it says that 
compared to someone w ho, in anger, puts all the sentient beings of the ten directions 
into a dark prison, if someone [else], in anger , turns his back on a bodhisattva, saying 
“I shall not look upon this horrible person [again]”, he generates incalculably greater 
negativity. Also compared to someone who has destroyed and burnt dow n stupas as 
numerous as sand [grains] of the river Ganges, if someone [else] generates anger and 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 420 – week one to seven  harmful intent tow ards a bodhisattva and says unpleasant things [the relationship] is 
like before. In the Sutra of the Seal of Entering Certainty and Uncertainty  it says 
that compared to someone w ho with a mind of love makes eyes develop again in the 
sentient beings of the ten directions whose eyes have been plucked out,  and w ho after 
releasing the previous sentient beings from prison, establishes them in the happiness of  
a universal monarch  or Brahma, if someone [else] w ith clear faith looks at and with 
clear faith w ishes to look at a bodhisattva w ith devotion to the Mahayana and 
proclaims  his praise, he generates incalculably more merit. Also, in the Sutra of the 
Magic  of the Complete Certainty of Total Peace it says that compared to killing all 
the sentient beings of J ambudvipa or seizing all their possessions, obstructing even as 
little as the virtue of a bodhisattva giving a handful of food to an animal, generates 
incalculably greater negativity. { 109}  so you should be very careful w ith respect to this 
point.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A2B -2 Great strength by way of the support  
A The difference between the skilful and the unskillful  
Second: Even a small lump of iron sinks to the bottom, but a container made from it 
floats on the surface. It has been set out that, similarly, the negativities committed by 
the skilful and unskillful are heavy or light. In fact, it is said that w ith for the skilful 
w ho regret previous negativities , restrain future ones, do not keep them secret, and 
apply their virtuous antidotes, they are light whereas for those w ho arrogantly assume 
they are skilful and do not do this, but out of contempt deliberately engage in them, 
they are heavy.  
B The actual strength of the support  
In the J ew el Heap Sutra it also says: if all the sentient beings of the billion universes 
having entered the Mahayana, w ere to possess the kingdom of universal monarchs and 
each of them offered butter lamps in vessels the size of an  ocean and w ith w icks the 
size of Mount Meru to a stupa of the Buddha, this w ould not match even the 
hundredth part of the merit a bodhisattva w ho has left his home and w ho holds up a 
little bit of w ick smeared w ith butter in front of a stupa.  This makes clear the 
strength of the support even if there is no difference in the attitude and the field and 
even if the object [offered] is very different.  This reasoning also makes clear that 
depending on whether or not the support has vow s and, if so, w hether it  has one, tw o 
or three, the cultivation of the path will be the more effective the more it has.  There is 
a great difference in the strength of the roots of virtue whether, for instance, a 
householder, at the time of handing out donations etc., acts on the  basis of keeping 
vow s like for instance one -day vow s, or w hether he acts w ithout any vow s.  
Here we talk about the strength of the support. This could be seen like this: First thing 
in the morning after waking up, we should go for refuge and generate the mind of 
bodhicitta. If we have taken a tantric empowerment, then we could engage into the six 
session guru yoga where we would again generate bodhicitta and take the bodhisattva - 
and tantric vows. In this way, we become a basis possessing or strengthening these 
vows, and in this special state, all the activities of the day will have a different quality. 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
421 They become much more meritorious as much more strength can be gained from 
them. Let‟s say we are making water bowl offerings, and as a result of the streng th of 
support, this becomes very powerful. If someone doesn‟t have this strength due to not 
having vows or not having generated bodhicitta, there will be a great difference. The 
activity is the same, but the support is greatly different.  
 
C The faults of discipline being adulterated   
 
This is something very frightful. We should understand from this that we should be 
very careful.  
In the Sutra of Annihilating Adulterations of Discipline it says that { 110}  compared 
to a human being possessing the ten non -virtues w ho, for a hundred years, 
continuously and uninterruptedly accumulates negativity, a fully -ordained monk w ho 
has [completely] broken his [vow s of] discipline and who, for one day, dressed in the 
victory banner of sages, enjoys that w hich has been of fered to him with faith 
[accumulates] far more negativity. Again, by w ay of the support negativity has greater 
strength. In the Scripture on Discernment it also says:  
It is better to eat lumps  
Of iron blazing w ith tongues of fire  
Than unrestrained, undisci plined  
To eat the alms -food of the area.     [48] 
 
This has been stated w ith regard to both adulterated discipline and slackness in 
training. The Teacher’s statements that compared to negativities associated w ith the 
Dharma, the negativities of the ten non -virtues are small, appear to be true.  
Adulterated discipline  refers to someone who has lost his monkhood due to his 
transgressions, or someone who is slack in training, who has not yet lost his 
monkhood, but he has accumulated a great many of transgressio ns.  
 Negativities associated with the dharma : here someone takes on a lot of 
vows and constantly goes against them. There is a great difference to whether a 
householder or an ordained person engages into negative actions. The same holds true 
for accumulat ing merit; so therefore ordained people should be very careful.  
  
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A2B -3 Great strength by way of the thing  
Third: With regard to giving gifts to sentient beings, giving Dharma [is far more 
excellent] and w ith regard to making offerings to the buddhas, offering one’s 
realizations is far more excellent than gifts and offerings of material things. From that 
exampl e you should also understand the [differing strength of] other [thing]s.  
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 422 – week one to seven  2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A2B -4 Great strength by way of the attitude  
 
This reminds us of the purpose of practicing dharma, of why we are here in Nalanda.  
 
A Greatness by way of the intensit y of the attitude, its duration etc.  
Fourth: In the Heap of J ew els Sutra it says that compared to each one of the sentient 
beings of the billion universes creating a buddha stupa as massive as Mount Meru 
and all those w ho are objects of respect paying res pect to them for ten million eons, a 
bodhisattva undivorced from the mind of omniscience w ho throw s one single flow er 
[generates] more merit. That being so  you should understand this in terms of the 
differences in attitude – w hether w hat you w ant to attai n  is superior or inferior, 
w hether you observe your ow n benefit or that of others and so forth - as w ell as the 
effect of intensity and duration . When it comes to faulty conduct a strong mentally 
afflicted attitude { 111}  and long duration also have more strength.  
Ten million aeons : it is already hard to imagine how long one aeon is! A bodhisattva 
undivorced from the mind of omniscience : I.e. who has bodhicitta .  
 
Let‟s say someone has a lot of wealth and wants to build a large stupa, but changes his 
mind  and chooses to offer his wealth to build housing for monks. This second idea is 
much more meritorious because here people will engage into practice, and study about 
bodhicitta and the perfect view etc. so this is much stronger and more meritorious. 
This i s like building a real stupa in the mental continuum; this is where the real stupa 
should be built!  
 Big results come about due to the power of the actual motivation. A practitioner 
with a strong bodhicitta motivation while making an offering of one flower  has the real 
stupa, which is bodhicitta, in his continuum.  In this way, we can understand that this 
bodhicitta is so powerful because it is the essence, or the root of the Mahayana dharma 
because through it we can enter into buddhahood. There is nothing more meritorious 
or better than cultivating this mind.  
 Then we have to ask ourselves how this strong difference comes about. It comes 
from a mind of bodhicitta, which says that I will stay in this world until every sentient 
being has been liberated. Such  a strong mind has the power to produce such huge 
merit.

423 THURSDAY  3 JUNE  2010  - SESSION 1 
 
QUESTION : In yesterday‟s example of being driven over by a car, and the karma 
pushing you into this, does this mean the karma ripens into the intention to cross th e 
street? In what mental or physical aspect does it ripen?  
GEN: Yes, it‟s possible that this imprint from a past action ripens in this moment into 
the intention to step into the street.  
 Let‟s say we step into the street and the car hits us and breaks our  leg. In a 
previous life, we collected the karma from maybe breaking the leg of another or maybe 
the legs of animals – from this, an imprint was stored. Then the conditions arrived for 
this imprint to ripen.  
QUESTION :  What does it mean to ripen? Does it become the intention?  
GEN:  No, it just becomes the wish to step in to the road, so there is a difference 
between wish and intention. Due to this imprint, the strong wish to step in to the road 
arises, and then the car comes by and hits you.  
QUESTION :  Would this be a specific wish, like you cross the street to get to your work 
place? It seems like we don‟t need karma here, we already wanted to cross the street 
and then we are hit by the car.  
GEN:  For example, you got to a bar, drink a lot and become dru nk. Because of this, 
you fall asleep and when you wake up you are still sleepy and stumble around. Then 
you run into the street and are hit by a car. The imprint ripened and woke you up, and 
it lead you into the street in this unclear state. You mentioned just the wish to go to 
work every day, this would not be an example of the imprint ripening. If you are run 
over by the car on one of these days, then it would be karma.  
QUESTION :  What about if someone comes to Nalanda every day for teachings, isn‟t 
this a wish?  
GEN: This wouldn‟t be karma because nothing happened.  
   
Another example is if someone is dying. In this process, just before their death 
many appearances arise brought about by imprints ripening - the mind becoming 
unclear etc.  
You can die from many conditions. Before this condition happens, a person has 
all kinds of feelings and appearances in his mind. They feel odd, they don‟t want to eat 
and all kinds of other strange states occur. If a murderer is coming to kill him but he 
doesn‟t know it, he feels death is coming due to all these feelings arising. This is all 
coming from karma ripening.   
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 424 – week one to seven   For an imprint to ripen, there are many conditions needed. If one condition is 
missing, the fruition will not come about. In general, if there are inauspicious signs, 
this means ones merit is running out.  
 This person might feel uncomfortable in their place and want to move. They 
might go out, and then meet this death condition. It might be a need to go outside that 
won‟t be understood by others, they might say, “Why do you need to go?” Being 
driven to go out, he then meets the condition and dies. In the 12 links, this imprint is 
made to ripen based on afflictions that are craving and grasping. This would bring 
forth these inauspicious sign s and feelings that make the person get up and leave. This 
wish to leave is not the craving or grasping, nor the ripening of the karma. The 
sequence is this. The craving and grasping brings forth the inauspicious signs, and due 
to this there would be a wis h to go outside, the condition is then met which is the next 
link of becoming, and then death.  
 For example, someone might decide to leave a very enjoyable party despite his 
friends telling him not to leave. Nevertheless, he leaves and as he walks into the  road, 
he is hit by a car and dies. This person might not normally drink alcohol, but on this 
occasion he does, and from this has a strong impulse to go outside. As a result, he is 
killed by the car. This often happens, where someone engages in an action o n an 
impulse. This is the ripening of the karmic imprint.  
There are many examples of normally careful people not being careful on one 
occasion and as a result, they are killed. For example, someone accidently leaves the 
gas on, and they are killed in the explosion. They would normally be very careful about 
this sort of thing, but this lapse produces this outcome, which is in fact a ripening of a 
karmic imprint.  
 Another true example; there was a woman who came to the Kalachakra centre in 
Paris. Her daught er normally didn‟t smoke or drink, but on one occasion she went to a 
party and got drunk. When she returned home, she smoked a cigarette, and fell asleep 
with it lit. The fire was so big that she couldn‟t escape, her clothes caught fire and she 
died. Her m other almost became crazy from this.  These negative conditions are not 
external, but they ripen within our mind, and result in problems arising. This all about 
karmic conditions.  
 There are many examples of people having accidents from a strong desire to go 
somewhere, like going to see a game. By going, something falls on them and they break 
their arm or their leg. This comes from their strong impulse to do something. They say 
it was because they did this and that, but why would they do this and that when it 
brings harm?  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
425  We have to be aware that these factors of craving and grasping are mind, and we 
can‟t see them. These factors are invisible.  
 
QUESTION : There has been a lot of earthquakes this year, what does this mean?  
GEN: This is clearly  to do with ka rma. In Tibet, there was a large earthquake and I was 
quite baffled about where this occurred as it is surrounded by empty space. This region 
has 200 -400 square kilometres of empty space, but the earthquake occurred in exactly 
the one place where people we re living. We cannot say this was one person‟s karma, 
but the karma of many people. If we look into the history of that area, an earthquake 
had never occurred before.  
 We live in a beautiful area, but if we have such a collective karma, then we are 
finish ed. If you have done the respective action, and the causes come together, the 
effect will necessarily ripen.  
 At the same time, we have to be aware that there are many methods explained in 
the teachings to change and deal with this negative karma.  For exa mple, when Mao Tse 
Tung was in power there were thousands of people put into prison in China and Tibet. 
In fact, no one knows how many people died or went to prison because he arrested 
anyone who didn‟t agree with him. When he died in 1975 a new president called Ho 
Koo Fung came into power. The Tibetans referred to him as a Buddha because he 
freed everyone and the prisons were emptied. Some people had been in prison for ten 
or twenty years.  
 In 1975 things were better, and in 1979 there was some religious freedom. 
Nevertheless, the teachers who were released said they got the best opportunity to 
practice in prison. They said that if they‟d stayed in the monastery, they wouldn‟t have 
had such a retreat, so they thanked Mao Tse Tung . These older men were not put to 
work in the prisons, but were just left and given food, so as a result, they used all their 
time as practice, and cultivated their minds‟ to the point of being yogis or scholars. 
They didn‟t have any books, but you don‟t need these to study, nor did  they need 
teachers, as it was the very teachers themselves that were in prison.  
 Some people had a very difficult experience in prison, and some had a happy 
and blissful experience. This is all dependant on their karma, on how their mind 
experiences thin gs. Some had such hardship that they actually died there – some 
thought they had such an opportunity that they did retreat and came out as scholars. 
This is due to the individual‟s karma.  
 For example, in our case, we are in a very happy situation. Whilst  this is the 
case, we don‟t have a clue what karma will ripen for us in the future. The point here is 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 426 – week one to seven  if we now accumulate the positive causes, there is no doubt that they will benefit our 
future.  
 
QUESTION : Pabongka Rinpoche says that if you kill an elep hant there is more 
negativity than if you kill and ant. Why is that?  
GEN: Large animals experience death differently from small animals. Small animals die 
quite easily, but for larger animals, the suffering is bigger as the process is longer. 
However, betw een an elephant and an ox dying for example, there is not much 
difference.  
QUESTION : Is this a difference in experience due to the complexity of mind, or due to 
the size of the animal? For example, the difference between a whale dying and a human 
dying.  
GEN: The difference is in the basis you take. A precious human rebirth is referred to as 
having a human basis, the human body. It‟s not just about the size, but about the basis 
you have, being a human or an animal.  
 We can say that killing a human is the s trongest negativity, but within that, we 
can be more specific and talk about the heinous crimes - referring to killing beings that 
are kind and precious to you, like your father and mother, an Arhat, or making the 
blood flow from the body of the Buddha etc . As they are very kind, within the human 
basis, they are an even greater special basis. Here again the difference is due to the 
basis.  
QUESTION : When the sense consciousness meets an object and we like or dislike it, 
does this come from a karmic imprint ripening which propels us to like or dislike?  
GEN: The causes that bring about a certain perception are three conditions of the 
observed object condition, the empowering condition and the immediately preceding 
condition. We can also add a fourth of the condition of a continuity.  The observed 
object condition is the sense object, and the empowering condition is the sense power. 
The continuity condition means there has to be a continuity of mind, as only then can 
there be a perception. The immediately prec eding conditions is an imprint of a karma 
which ripens as an experience in the next moment. Does this ripen only into the 
perception of the colour or also in the likening or disliking? The sense consciousness 
doesn‟t like or dislike, this is only done in t he mental consciousness, it‟s the same for 
seeing something as attractive or unattractive.  
 In general, we can say that a sense consciousness has a part which is of the 
desire type, or that they have an aspect of the anger or dislike type. The sequence 
would be that first there is a perception of the object, this brings about a mental 
consciousness which either likes or dislikes it. From this, desire or hate in the mental 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
427 consciousness spreads out into the sense consciousness. This makes the sense 
consciou sness see the object differently, for example due to the influence of the 
attachment.  
 
THURSDAY 3 JUNE 2010  - SESSION 2 
QUESTION : Is it karma that determines the overall appearance of things? Like when we 
hear about how the preta perceives a glass of water  as puss, or how through our karma 
water appears as water.  
GEN: In general, all our appearances have a strong connection with our karma. For 
example, let‟s take this water here in front of me, it would be experienced by a certain 
type of preta as puss and blood due to his karma - although it is water. If a god comes 
here, they would experience it as nectar due to their karma, but it is water. The human 
experiences it as water, which it is. The human experiences it as it is, whereas pretas 
and gods do not.  
 Due to our different karmas, and the different aspirational prayers we made, 
there are many different experiences. For example, some people come to Nalanda, and 
think it is a very peaceful and beautiful place. Which is the actual situation, they 
experienc e it as it is. Another person comes here and thinks it is very busy and dislikes 
the place. These different perceptions come from different karmas ripening. Maybe 
someone comes into the gompa and is completely inspired, but another comes and 
thinks it is c rowded or cramped, or they see the translator and don‟t like him. All this 
comes from karma ripening.  
 These are all examples of the different appearances just among humans. If we 
think of beings in other realms, then there are huge differences, such as t he completely 
different way a human or a dog would experience a room. A human thinks it is very 
nice, but for the dog there is no liking or disliking, as you can put them in one corner, 
but there is no feeling, you put them in a car, there is no feeling. T his shows they have 
a different mode of what is appearing.  
 We experience our own rooms at Nalanda and very good, and Nalanda as a 
place we like. If a very wealthy person comes here, they may find many faults in the 
rooms etc. and will not see them as ver y comfortable at all. They would have a 
different experience of the same object.  
 We can see how there is this huge difference in how human beings experience 
the world around them. A preta for example is described as not having the merit to 
enjoy objects what we experience. While on the other hand, a god is described as 
having a vast store of merit that results in everything they use being of an amazing 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 428 – week one to seven  quality and cleanliness. They live in areas where there is no dirt or dust, and their 
dwellings are huge  palaces (immeasurable mansions).  
 
QUESTION : What about the Buddha whilst he was here; wouldn‟t he have had a 
discrimination of certain sights and sounds as pleasant and unpleasant?  
GEN: The Buddha needs this kind of discernment.  
QUESTION : Would this com e from karmic imprints?  
GEN: No, because he has no imprints. If the Buddha comes here, he would definitely 
see that I am big and that Ven. Gendun is slim, for example.  
 Good and bad exists, and we can say there is faulty and correct behaviour. As 
these ar e existents, the Buddha has to perceive them. If he would not perceive it like 
this, he would have a fault, and it would be a wrong consciousness.  
 
QUESTION : If the Buddha sees something as pleasant, then this would be the truth, but 
the preta would not se e it like this. This would mean that from the objects side it 
would exist as pleasant because the Buddha sees it this way.  
GEN: Yes, the food is delicious. It is not inherently there, but to say it is delicious 
would be correct.  
 If the Buddha was next t o me, he would see Ven. Gendun as slim and me as big. 
If it is there, the Buddha would perceive it like this. If the food is delicious, then he 
will see it like this. If he saw everything as the same, and didn‟t see these differences, 
then he would have a wrong consciousness.  
QUESTION : Would the Buddha prefer some foods over others?  
GEN:  This is possible. We have to be aware that if non -delicious food is offered to the 
Buddha he has the power to transform it, and purify and increase it. This is what is 
meant by reciting OM AH HUM.  
 It is said that with HUM, the faults of colour, smell and capacity are purified. If 
this food has some fault with regard to colour this is changed, the bad smell can be 
changed, and if there is no nutrition in the food, the nat ure can be changed so it 
become nutritious. With AH the substance is transformed into the entity of nectar. 
With OM the nectar is made vast and limitless. In this way, it is fit to be consumed.  
 All this transformation comes through the power of concentra tion. We can see 
that in the first question about the three types of migrators. The preta was a low being, 
the exalted being was a god and middling was a human. They then perceive according 
to their karma in their respective realms. If we are speaking of t he water in the human 
realm, then this is actually water, but a god would perceive this same water as nectar 
due to his karma, and in the same way, a  preta would only see this human realm water 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
429 as puss or blood. We are talking here about a preta or a god coming to the human 
realm, perceiving things of the human realm, and then these things happen. Normally 
they are not in this realm, so this in only an example.  
 
B How anger is particularly strong  
Among [mental afflictions], again, anger is particularly st rong. In Engaging in  
[Bodhisattva ] Behaviour  it says:  
Whatever good conduct w as amassed  
In thousand eons by giving and w orship  
To the sugatas and the like  
All is destroyed by  one [flash of] anger.   [49] 
 
Amongst all the afflictions, anger is especially powerful and the most negative of all. 
As it is the most dangerous, we have to make a concerted effort not to allow it to arise. 
This is true for lay people as well as ordained people. Anger refers to a mind  of 
disliking someone. We‟re not necessarily talking about hitting someone, but it is a state 
of mind of dislike.  
 Good conduct  is whatever ethical discipline you are guarding. Any activities that 
were accumulating merits, are all destroyed by a mere mome nt of anger. We engaged 
into all kinds of effort to amass this merit, and then we end up in anger and it is all 
destroyed. Then again, we accumulate merit, and again we become angry and all is lost, 
so all our activities becomes pointless. From this anger,  we can understand we are 
suffering great losses.  
 No one will hold an angry person as being excellent, actually they will see them 
as very negative. We can see this outcome within this very life.  
 If we are angry, we also act as a condition for others t o generate a mind of 
dislike towards us when they see our behaviour. In this case, in this one moment, a 
great many people generate negativities in this way.  
 We have to be careful with this. If we generate this anger towards those whose 
conduct accords w ith purity  or towards the spiritual offspring of the conquerors, the 
anger is then so much heavier. This is expressed in the next quote.  
 
In this regard anger tow ards those w hose conduct accords w ith purity w eighs very 
heavily, [and] anger tow ards the jina s’ children more heavily still. In the King of 
Concentration [Sutra]  it says:  
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 430 – week one to seven  If one has harboured harmful intent for another,  
Discipline and study cannot save one,  
Nor can concentration or a retreat place,  
Nor can gifts, nor w orship of the Buddha.   [50] 
 
The Buddha expresses this heaviness in his own words. Retreat place  here means 
isolation.  
 For another  is referring to a bodhisattva or dharma practitioner who is the object 
of our anger. The result of this becomes very strong. If we have generated ange r 
towards such a person, then what is the ripening effect? It is extremely negative, and 
something you want to avoid. Even all the positive things you do, like keeping ethical 
discipline purely and have positive imprints from that, or having listened well to 
Dharma teachings and have positive imprints from that, or this concentration etc., all 
these imprints will not be able to protect you from the result of generating anger 
towards a Dharma practitioner.  
 Concentration  means that if we have entered into a  practice of mental 
stabilisation, or calm abiding meditation, any outcome from this kind of activity will 
also be unable to protect you from the ripening effect of anger.  
 A retreat place , abiding in solitude, is also a name for a monastery. If you come 
to a place like Nalanda and study and practice here, or you at least say you are going to 
such a place, it will help to stop the ripening effect of anger. Making vast offerings 
won‟t be able to protect you, not even offerings and veneration to the Buddhas will 
protect you.  
 I have mentioned this verse before from the Bodhisattvacharyavatara , where the 
Master Shantideva refers to the number of eons a practitioner will spend in the hell 
realms for each moment of anger generated to such a person. For each mom ent of 
anger towards such a person, one will spend an eon in a hell rebirth.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2A3 Indication of their effects  
A The fully ripened effect  
B The effect in conformity with the cause [causally concordant effect]  
C T he conditioning or empowering  effect   
The third one – the effects of these [actions] – has three points: the fully ripened effect, 
the effect in conformity w ith the cause and the conditioning  or empow ering effect.  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
431 A The fully ripened effect  
First: A s each of the ten unw holesome paths of action depends on the basis, the three 
poisons, and w hether it is great, intermediate, or small 13, the fully ripened effect is 
also threefold. In Actual Basis of the Levels  it says that through each of the ten, a 
great [act of] killing and so on, you are reborn as a hell being, through each of the ten 
intermediate ones you are reborn as a hungry ghost and through each of the ten small 
ones you are reborn as an animal. In the Sutra on the Ten Levels  the effects of small  
and intermediate ones are said to be the other w ay around.  
 
QUESTION : At the end of this paragraph, it says  the other w ay around . How can there be 
a difference like this in the sutras?  
GEN: In general, the lower rebirth is that of the pretas, and the hig her of the bad 
migrations is that of the animals. The exception saying the other way around  is because 
an animal is considered a lower rebirth than a human rebirth, from the point of view 
of suffering. However, some people are born human, but everything in  their lives is 
complete misery, they are missing limbs, there sense faculties are not complete, they 
have a short life span etc. In comparison to that, a being born in the animal realm 
might have very good conditions and their suffering is less. In this c ase, the animal has 
a better rebirth than the human. In the same way, we might find an animal who is 
suffering more than a preta who has good conditions.  
  
B The effect in conformity with the cause  
Second: The effects in conformity  w ith the cause are tha t even having left the bad 
migrations and taken rebirth as a human being, respectively, your life w ill be short, 
you w ill be lacking in possessions, you w ill not keep your partner, there w ill be a lot of 
slander, you w ill be separated from loved ones, you w ill hear unpleasant sounds, others 
w ill not listen to your w ords, and the three [poisons], attachment, hatred, and delusion 
w ill predominate.  
 
This is referring to the effects that you experience after you have come out of the 
lower realms, and are born as a human. Lama Tsongkhapa presents this in stages in 
accordance with the ten non -virtuous actions. For example, the first one is a short 
lifespan, then lacking resources from stealing, followed by losing your partner or being 
unable to keep your family t ogether. Further, it says people will deprecate you a lot and 
looking down on you.  
You will be separated from loved ones  refers to not having any friends or people we 
can trust. We have to listen to many unpleasant sounds  means people are saying things 
we feel are unpleasant and we don‟t want to listen or we have to constantly listen to 
                                            
13 Nalanda Translator: The Tibetan says and they can be great, intermediate or small . 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 432 – week one to seven  harsh speak and so forth. Then in general, our anger, attachment and ignorance 
dramatically increase. This is the presentation of the causally concordant effects in 
associa tion with each of the ten non -virtuous actions, meaning that each of these 
actions has one of these effects.  
 
C The conditioning effect  
Third: The conditioning effect:  
 - of killing is that in future w orlds , food and drink, medicine, harvests and the 
like have little pow er; that of stealing great droughts and rains and { 112}  poor 
harvests, etc.;  
 - that of sexual misconduct sw amps, much filth, unpleasant experiences, etc.;  
 - that of lying, that field w ork and w ork to do w ith boats w ill not succeed etc. ;  
 - that of divisive speech that the location [w here you live] w ill be bumpy and 
uneven, and it w ill be difficult to travel, and so on;  
 - that of harsh w ords that the location [w here you live] w ill be full of logs, 
brambles, rocks, pebbles, potsherds, e tc.; 
 - that of idle talk that fruit trees do not bear fruit or only out of season, etc.;  
 - that of covetousness that everything excellent deteriorates year by year, month 
by month, and day by day, etc.;  
 - that of harmful intent a lot of epidemics, injuries, infections, disputes, wars 
w ith enemy troops, etc. and  
 - that of w rong view s that in the w orld [you live in] the best resources w ill 
decline, etc.  
 
This is again presented in association with the te n non -virtuous actions. Killing is first, 
and the outcome of this action is that in the external world, things that would usually 
nourish you lose their capacity to do so. Examples could include situations such as if 
becoming sick, but there was no medicat ion available to you or that it has no affect. 
Alternatively, your food and drink would be indisgesable, and you become weak.  
 
 QUESTION : What does the conditioning or empowering effect mean?  
GEN: It is similar to talking about the empowering condition in other contexts. This 
can also be seen like a condition, in that it acts like a condition for the effect similar to 
the cause, the second effect listed here (causally concordant effect). For example, with 
stealing, the causally concordant effect is that you  lack resources. For this to come 
about you have this condition, this fruition which is an empowering condition, so that 
whatever activity you do in order to produce wealth, it doesn‟t work out, so you lack 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
433 resources. The empowering (or conditioning) effec t acts as a condition that brings 
about the ripening of the second type, the causally concordant effect.  
 With sexual misconduct, we saw that the casually concordant effect was that 
partnerships will not last and you lose your partner. The empowering effe ct is that the 
place where you live is foul smelling and dirty.  
 Lies lead us into the verbal negatives. The effect here is that you are not able to 
complete your work. The effect of divisive speech is that the place where you have to 
live is rough or bar ren. From harsh speech, its possible that you are reborn in an area 
where there is a lot of forest that is unpleasant or very dense, or maybe the roads are 
very difficult to travel on. For idle talk, the example given here is that you plant many 
trees in o rder to produce a harvest, but there will be no fruit, it produces fruit at the 
wrong time or even the trees produce flowers that give you hope, but nothing comes 
about. These are all the results of confusing others and filling their heads with idle 
talk. The conditioning effect of covetousness is that you have some high quality 
substance, and in a very short time it breaks or loses its qualities. The effect of harmful 
intent is that you live in a place where on the one hand, there are many sicknesses and 
infectious diseases, and on the other hand there is lot of quarrelling. Just quarrelling 
between two or three people or finding faults of each other can make very beautiful 
places unwelcoming to others. They don‟t necessarily have to be physically fighting.  If 
a few people here are like this, then it will affect the whole community, not just those 
involved. For us living here in a Dharma situation, it is very important to be 
harmonious with others because so many relationships can be harmed through 
quarrelli ng within the community. These are all such effects of the mind that wants to 
harm.  An actual war, or an army arriving can also be such an effect of harmful intent.  
 If we listen to what is offered here, like the Lam -Rim, but we are constantly 
looking for  faults in others and destroying the harmony between people, then the 
whole community suffers.  
 Then comes the conditioning effect of wrong views, which we have to 
remember is specific to the wrong view of denying the existence of cause and effects. 
It sa ys here that in this world the good sources are setting or waning. Sources  refers to 
something like a certain species of animal dying out. There are many such examples 
today of species having died out and examples where only a few of a certain species are 
left. It can also refer to types of humans like families or lineages dying out or that have 
already died out.  
Another example would be an area where there was a lot of gold or iron ore, but 
the supply has now been exhausted.

2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2B White actions and their effects  
1 White actions  
2 Their effects  
The second one has tw o points: the actions and the effects.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2B1 White actions  
A Brief indication by means of scripture  
First: They are the bodily actio ns of correctly abstaining from killing, stealing, and 
sexual misconduct due to possessing a w holesome mind having thought about their 
disadvantages, and of carrying this abstention to its conclusion. Similarly, this is to be 
applied to the four verbal one s and the three mental ones, the difference being that 
they are called “actions of speech” and “actions of mind”. This is how it is stated in 
Actual Basis of the Levels .  
 
Here, with regard to the individual physical negative deeds, you reflect on the 
drawbacks by thinking of their effects. For example, you reflect on the ripening effect 
of killing, the causally concordant effect, and empowering effect. You do the same 
with stealing and sexual misconduct. Not wanting these effects, you generate a strong 
wish to avoid the causes, and as a result one refrains from the activity. This is the 
presentation of the white physical actions.   
 After reflecting on the drawbacks, one has  the clear recognition of the unwished 
for, and guards the mind against such activity which will  bring such an effect. One 
then constantly refrains from engaging into such activity, which is the completion.  
 
QUESTION : As this is about refraining, what is  the performance, because it seems to be 
a mere mental activity, so how can it be physical action?  
GEN: There is a physical action here. If you wash your hands and there are some ants 
nearby, you take care that you don‟t harm them, especially that you don‟ t kill them. 
This is not a verbal or mental action, but physical. In stealing, you have the attitude 
not to take anything, and in this way you make clear distinction between what is your 
money and what is not your money, this being the physical action.  
 From this, we can also understand how it works with the white verbal and 
mental actions.  
 This was a general presentation, now comes a more detailed explanation.  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
435 B Extensive explanation  
This should therefore be related to the basis, attitude, performance  and completion as 
appropriate. When applied to the path of action of abandoning killing, the basis is 
another sentient being. The attitude is the w ish to give it up having seen its 
disadvantages. The performance is to put effort into completely abstaining  from 
killing. { 113}  The completion is the physical action of correct abstention having been 
completed. The other [path]s [ of action] should also be understood in this manner.  
 
For example, the white action of killing is abstaining from killing and the ba sis is as 
before, a living being. The attitude is a wish to abandon killing, which comes from 
having seen the drawbacks of killing through reflecting on the three different types of 
effects of killing. From this comes a strong wish to abandon killing, and this is the 
attitude. On one hand we can say that the performance is an action that is perfectly 
refraining from killing, and is therefore a non -action. On the other hand, we can say 
that this performance is an action that is making sure there is no killin g. The 
completion is that you are engaging in a physical action and there is a certain danger 
that someone might die, but then you take measures to make sure that no one dies. 
Taking these measures is the performance. When you have achieved keeping the 
sentient being alive, this is the completion.  
 From this, we can understand the process, and apply it to the other white 
actions.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2B2  The effects  
Second: From among the three [effects of virtuous paths of action] the fully ripened 
effect is  to be born as a human, a desire realm god, and a god of the tw o upper realms 
though a small, intermediate and great virtuous action.  As for the effect in conformity 
w ith the cause and the conditioning effect the opposites of the [respective effects from]  
non-virtuous actions apply.  
 
Again, there is a threefold presentation. From small, middle or great virtuous actions, 
you are born as a human, a desire realm god or a god of the upper realms respectively.  
QUESTION : It sounds like its saying that by any k ind of strong virtue, you could be 
born in the upper realms, but I thought you had to rely on the meditative absorptions 
to be born there?  
GEN: Yes, to be born as a god in these realms, so not in the desire realm, you have to 
rely on the mental absorption s. In other words, attain mental absorptions. This person, 
in order to generate such a meditative absorption, has to abandon all negative actions. 
because if he were to generate a single negative act, he would fall from the meditative 
absorption. This pers on has eliminated all these negativities, but only temporarily. This 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 436 – week one to seven  is the most powerful form of eliminating the ten non -virtuous actions and by this, 
together with these meditative absorptions, creates the causes for going to the 
meditative states, which  are these upper realms. However, this person has a faulty 
motivation. Their motivation is only to be born as a god in these upper realms.  
QUESTION : Do people in these upper realms have the capacity to achieve any Dharma 
or are they beings without any discrimination?  
GEN: They definitely have discrimination. There‟s no such thing as not having 
discrimination.  
QUESTION : Someone in meditative absorption is not creating any non -virtue, but are 
they creating any virtue? Is the mere fact they are attaining these states already virtue?  
GEN: Yes, they are accumulating virtue from being in this meditative absorption. We 
shouldn‟t think that someone who is born into these states is just sitting in meditation. 
There are a great many objects of enjoyment there, ma ny are just going for these. They 
have no anger in their minds or jealousy, but they do have other strong afflictions, 
usch as a very strong form of desire. This is all in the form and formless realms, and 
they are ordinary beings within samsara.  
QUESTION : Can they create negativity in these realms, otherwise how could they fall to 
the lower realms?  
GEN: Are you saying they can create negativity?  
STUDENT : I don‟t see how they can.  
GEN: It‟s true, they can fall to bad migrations at the end of their life. For example, 
let‟s say this year you haven‟t worked and couldn‟t save any money, but you are still 
able to eat now because of the money you had previously saved. What you are eating 
with now is from the money you accumulated a year ago. Similarly, let‟s say this person 
is born into these concentration realms from a lower realm. They have accumulated 
many imprints, positive and negative. These negative ones haven‟t ripened yet, but 
somehow the positive karma has ripened first, the positive ones from having attained 
an actual meditative absorption, so he goes to a rebirth in the upper realms. During 
this time, maybe he is not accumulating negativity, but the karma to be in this realm 
becomes exhausted. Then one of these other imprints ripens, not necessarily an 
imprint he created whilst being in this upper realm, but something he had previously 
accumulated, and from this he falls to the lower realms.  
QUESTION : In the form and formless realms,  wouldn‟t he create a lot of positive 
karma, as he would frequently be in meditative absorption?  
GEN: Yes, they accumulate a lot of virtue.  
QUESTION : So they can also take a good rebirth from this realm, and not necessarily 
fall to a lower realm?  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
437 GEN: Yes, they don‟t necessarily  have to go to the lower realms from here. It‟s also not 
necessary  for you to go to the lower realms, you should definitely aim at being human 
again!  
QUESTION : Why are they not always born in this realm once they are there, because  
this meditative absorption karma can only ripen as rebirth in this realm?  
GEN: It‟s similar to what you said. Let‟s say someone is born in the first meditative 
absorption, then it‟s possible they work on attaining a higher absorption. Then when 
they get t o the second absorption they work on getting the third, and then to the 
fourth and then to the formless realm. They are accumulating positive karma in all this. 
However, the negative imprints for a lower rebirth are all still there and they are not 
purifie d by the power of this concentration. This person is only interested in the god 
realms and will not be engaging into a perfect pure practice so the imprints remain as 
they have not been purified. At one point, the moment will come where such an 
imprint is stronger than any of the virtues, and then it ripens as a rebirth in a lower 
realm.  
 
FRIDAY  4 JUNE  2010  - SESSION 1 
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2C Indication of other classifications of actions   
1 The classification of throwing and completing ones   
 
This is actually a topic connected with a being of middle capacity, and will be 
discussed in detail there. Here is just a brief presentation.  
 
2 The classification of what will definitely be experienced and what will not  
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2C1 The classification of throwing and completing ones  
1 [Presentation of the first classification]  lv 
The third one has tw o points the first of w hich is the classification of throw ing and 
completing [actions].  
 Throw ing actions for [rebirth in] the happy migrations are virtuous [actions]  
and throw ing actions for [rebirth in] the bad migrations are non -virtuous [actions].  
 The completing ones are uncertain, how ever, even in the happy migrations, 
incomplete limbs, minor body parts or sense pow ers, hideous looks, a short life -span, 
many ill nesses, poverty, and so forth are caused by non -virtue. Also for animals and 
hungry ghosts, perfect prosperity is caused by virtue.  
 
Completing karmas are not as certain as throwing karmas. Even in the happy 
migrations, completing karma can ripen as not h aving the major and secondary limbs, 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 438 – week one to seven  not having complete senses, having an ugly  complexion, a short lifespan, frequently 
being sick or living in poverty and so. These all come from non -virtuous actions. On 
the other hand, we can see that even in a lower r ebirth, such as a preta or animal, 
beings can possess wealth and many great things as a result of virtuous actions. This 
shows that completing karma does not have the certainty that is seen with propelling 
karma.  
 This topic is normally considered in the context of the 12 links of dependent 
arising. Of these 12 links, three are propelling causes and four are propelled effects. 
Three are attained causes, and two are attained results.  Of the three propelling 
causes, only one is throwing karma, the second lin k of action. Ignorance (1st link) is a 
propelling cause of a motivational type ( completely taken by  literally in Tibetan), because 
ignorance completely has you. Actions (2nd link) that we engage in under the power of 
ignorance are only very short, but a ce rtain capacity remains and is stored on 
consciousness (3rd link). These three propelling causes are the first three links, 
ignorance, action (motivated by this ignorance) and consciousness (where the capacity 
of the action is stored). Here we are only conc erned with the throwing karma, the 
second link.  
 To imagine the process we can think of a human being whose mind has a great 
liking for not killing. They are clear about the disadvantages of killing, they regard the 
life of others as very precious, and fr om this, they generate a strong mind of 
abandoning killing. From this strong mind, the action of guarding life creates very 
positive karma. Let‟s say this results as throwing karma to be born again in the human 
realm. Whilst he has this strong liking for p rotecting life, with wealth he has a strong 
mind of miserliness, so as the latter is a negative mind, he is also accumulating 
negative karma. In our example, this acts as completing karma so that in his human 
rebirth (that comes from the positive throwing karma of refraining from killing) he is 
born in poverty.  
 In another case, someone might have both virtuous completing karma and 
throwing karma. They have a strong mind of guarding life, as well as a mind of 
generosity that is willing to give. Abstaining from killing results in being born human 
(throwing karma ri pening), and from his generous mind the result is he has wealth 
(completing karma ripening).  
 Let‟s say another has a strong mind which likes to kill, doesn‟t consider the 
wishes of sentient beings and kills a lot. From this strong liking of killing, he 
accumulates strong propelling karma that might result in rebirth as an animal or a 
preta. On the other hand, they might be very generous and like to give, so additionally 
they accumulate virtuous karma. This would then ripen as being propelled into the 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
439 anim al realm from the negative propelling karma, but the positive completing karma 
from generosity would result in him having good circumstances. Like the cats here at 
Nalanda, they have all the good food they want, human servants that prepare food for 
them, t ake them for walks, take them to the doctors – they have more servants than we 
have. No one would do this for us! Whilst they are born in the lower realm, they have 
all this food, medicine, human servants and so forth, and they can enjoy all this.  
If howe ver, we think of cattle or pigs, they also have servants that bring them 
food and so on, but their motivation is very negative, as the aim is to kill them for 
meat. This is quite bizarre in that on the one hand they have servants, but on the 
other, it is o nly in order to kill them.  
  
This is the whole situation of throwing karma and completing karma.  
  
2 The four possible combinations of virtuous and non -virtuous throwing and completing 
actions  
There are thus four alternatives:  
 - projected by a virtuous  throw ing [action] and completed by a virtuous or a non -
virtuous completing [action],  
 - projected by a non -virtuous throw ing [action] and completed by non -virtuous or 
a virtuous completing [action].  
 
This I explained above.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B1B -2C2 The class ification of what will definitely be experienced and what will not  
3 [Presentation of the second classification]  
With respect to what w ill definitely be experienced and w hat w ill not, what w ill 
definitely be experienced is w hat has been done intentionally  and accumulated. What 
w ill not definitely be experienced is w hat has [not] been done intentionally but not 
accumulated.  
 
The word not [in brackets above] isn‟t in the Tibetan, and is incorrect.  
 Here we are looking at the different combinations of having  done something, 
and having accumulated something in order to explain the differences.  
 Firstly, we look at having done and having accumulated. In the context of 
killing, let‟s take the example of a pig that is fed by a farmer so it can be killed and 
sold for meat. He is constantly feeding the pig, but the pig is in a very pitiful state and 
it believes everything is fine because it is getting food even though the farmer‟s 
motivation is to kill it and to sell the meat. When the pig reaches a certain size, t he 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 440 – week one to seven  farmer kills the pig and rejoices at the amount of meat and all the money he will make. 
This is an example of having done the action and having accumulated.  
 
QUESTION : Is the farmer accumulating negativity every day that he feeds the pig with 
the motivation to eventually kill it?  
GEN: Definitely. The farmer would sometimes check how fat the pig has become, 
maybe by inserting a pin into the neck to measure the fat. The p ig then cries in pain 
but doesn‟t relate this pain to being measured up to see if it is fat enough to be killed.  
 Secondly, an example of having done but not accumulated. a person goes out to 
shoot a rabbit, finds one, aims and shots, hits them but it doe sn‟t die immediately. The 
hunter is then struck by deep regret as he sees the animal in pain, and he generates a 
strong regret. Maybe he goes as far as purification, or taking refuge, or dedicating that 
the animal will not be born in a lower realm. He does  all this before the completion 
has happened. In this case, the action is done, but will not be accumulated.  
 In the Lam-Rim Chenmo , there are many examples, but in our text there is just 
the difference between doing and accumulating. It says that with re gard to the intent, 
or by having the intent engaging fully with body and speech, these kinds of actions 
would be done. On other hand, if in a dream you do any of the ten non -virtuous 
actions, these are neither actions done nor actions accumulated.  
 Let‟s take the action of stealing as another example of karma done but not 
accumulated. Someone might engage into stealing but the object can‟t be stolen. 
Although he had the wish, and engages into the activity, because he‟s mistaken he‟s 
unable to perform the a ction. In this case, he engaged in the action of stealing, but he 
didn‟t accumulate the action of stealing.  
 Another example would be if I see Ven. Tharchin in the distance and I shout 
some  harsh words at him, but it‟s someone else and I‟m mistaken. I only wanted to 
express this harsh speech to Ven. Tharchin, and even though I engaged in the action 
of saying hars h words to this person, the action is not accumulated because of being 
mistaken.  
 Another example in the context of sexual misconduct; let‟s say someone wants 
to commit adultery, but instead ends up lying down with his own wife – he mistakes 
the person. T his is possible! Maybe there are two couples who are very friendly and the 
man from one couple wants the woman from the other couple. Maybe in the dark he 
goes to lie down with her, and because they are friends his wife is also there visiting 
and he actual ly lies with his own wife. Although he intended to engage in sexual 
misconduct with the other woman, and did the action of going to this place, he was 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
441 mistaken with regard to the person, and as a result, this is sexual misconduct that was 
done but not accu mulated. We can apply this to all the ten non -virtuous actions.  
 There is a story about a man who suspected that his wife was sleeping with a 
friend of theirs. When she left to go and meet with this friend, the husband had 
arranged for the friend to be ou t, and he replaced him in order to wait for his wife to 
arrive at the meeting place. In this way, she lay down with her husband thinking it was 
the friend, and as a result, she had only engaged in the action of sexual misconduct, 
but had not accumulated it , because she was mistaken. With this, we have to say it is a 
karma not definite to be experienced.  
 
QUESTION : In the normal case of sexual misconduct, this being a non -virtuous action 
there should be some harm done to someone. Is the harm in adultery com ing from the 
mental suffering of the other person of the couple?  
GEN: Yes, that‟s it. The harm is done to her husband in this case, she is creating all 
kind of mental suffering for him, and she is aware that this is harming her husband.  
QUESTION : What abo ut if there is consent between the couple, maybe if the husband is 
physically handicapped and cannot perform the actual act?  
GEN: This would not be sexual misconduct.  
QUESTION : What about the harm done to herself in the sense that the relationship isn‟t 
valued? Isn‟t this another aspect of the harm done?  
GEN: In this context of the ten non -virtuous actions, we‟re talking about a very direct 
form of harm. Not harm where we have to construct a little bit, and also here we are 
talking about harm done to some one else. When we talk about the seven actions of 
body and speech, these all have to be actions done to someone else.  
QUESTION : If you perform adultery, but your partner is unaware, how is any harm 
done?  
GEN: The main type of sexual misconduct we are talk ing about here is rape, where the 
other person doesn‟t want to engage into sexual activity, and you force it on them.   
 
QUESTION : If someone has very strong anger towards another and wants to kill them, 
they go out to kill them, but kill the wrong person and they think they have killed the 
right person, is it done but not accumulated because it doesn‟t have the four complete 
factors?  
GEN: In this case, the strong anger or hate itself shows a complete mental action of ill 
will. This is done and accumulated,  as well as being a complete action because of the 
motivation to destroy, which would also accumulate negativities. At the mental level, 
all these actions are accumulated. In the error of not killing his enemy, but his 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 442 – week one to seven  rejoicing thinking he did, because th ere is a strong form of the action, the mistake is 
not having the effect of not being accumulated, but it is accumulated. All our other 
examples were quite light, and as there was this mistaken element in all of them the 
action was not accumulated. However , in this case of killing, because this action is so 
strong, although there is a mistake, it is still done and accumulated in a lesser way. The 
action is still very heavy due to this strong anger, the strong willingness to go out and 
destroy life and rejoi cing in this, and they did kill someone! Due to all this, the action 
is done and accumulated, but there would be a difference if it had been the actual 
person.  
 Another case would be that someone sees something that looks like a human in 
the distance and wants to kill them, but it‟s actually a tree or something else not living. 
He shoots to kill it, it falls over and this person rejoices. This is an example of having 
accumulated but not having done. It was not done because there was no human there, 
but bec ause he had the strong wish to kill someone, something is accumulated. In a 
previous example where you are driving a car, and you hit someone without having the 
intention, this is an example of lacking the intention but carrying out the action. 
However, in  your example here, they had the motivation to kill someone, but didn‟t 
carry out the action, and so this is an action accumulated but not done.  
To be more specific, one has not done the action of killing and not accumulated 
the action of killing, but one  has accumulated the action of harmful intent. This is also 
a case where something has been accumulated but not done, and so as a result, it is an 
action that is not definite to be experienced.  
 
QUESTION : In the case of farmers who raise animals to kill t hem later, it‟s possible they 
don‟t have the view of them as being sentient beings that have feelings, and so they 
would also be mistaken. Their intention is to make a living, so what kind of karma is 
accumulated from this?  
GEN: If the animal is sick, woul dn‟t they think, “Oh, this animal is sick.” This is how 
they think, isn‟t it? Therefore, they are aware that they have feelings and get sick, so 
they are sentient beings.  
QUESTION : In the catholic context, animals are said to be like objects and have no 
feelings and I‟ve heard farmers themselves say this, that they are objects and don‟t 
have feelings.  
GEN: But with their cats and dogs, they would send them to the doctors when they get 
sick.  
QUESTION : It‟s possible that the farmer sees the sick animal as n ot working or 
correctly performing just as he would consider a machine or process. In this way the 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
443 animal needs to go to the doctor. In this way, he would not consider them as having 
feeling and as being sentient.  
GEN: Here we would have people under a th ick type of ignorance, who are unable to 
see or understand the situation at all. In this case it would be the action of killing 
through ignorance, and still, I would hold that at the time of their death, the actual 
moment of killing, it would become obviou s that this object does not want to die and 
that they are doing something against its wishes, even with this ignorance. We could 
say that if this lack of understanding is so thick, it would then be killing out of 
ignorance, and would be a lesser form than the action of killing whilst understanding 
the actual situation. It would still be killing however.  
 For example, at the time of Hitler when they wanted to eradicate all the Jews, 
they tried to bring about a discernment of them not being human. It was sai d, “The 
Jews are not human beings.” They brought them to camps and when they were actually 
being killed, it was obvious these people didn‟t want to die. Even though the people 
who were carrying out the killing had built up this strong ignorance, at that mo ment 
things would have been quite apparent. This is again killing with a fault in the 
discernment, but still there were clear signs of what was going on, some kind of 
emotional response.  
 With Mao Tse Tung, the outlook was that those who were in powerful positions, 
with intellectual status or had wealth and so on all had very bad and destructive views. 
It was thought that these views couldn‟t be removed by convincing them, and so the 
only way to deal with them was to kill them. This kind of killing happene d in China 
and Tibet, but it mainly happened in China.   
This method of dealing with people‟s views by killing them started in China 
from 1946 until around 1959. Here we are talk about millions! In Tibet, it started from 
1959 until about 1976 where about a  million were killed.  
If you look at the most severe examples of harsh people like Hitler and Mao Tse 
Tung, then Mao Tse Tung killed more than Hitler did, but the point here is that Mao 
Tse Tung did all his killing in the context that people‟s views were to be removed by 
killing them, so he had this kind of ignorant motivation. On the other hand, Hitler‟s 
view was different, he killed the Jews by seeing them as animals, for example there 
were cases where their skin was used etc. Different outlooks, but bot h are examples of 
mass killing from these strange views.  
 If we were to take rebirth in the hell realms, then we‟d definitely see people like 
Hitler, Mao Tse Tung, Stalin and Sadam Hussein there!

QUESTION : Did Gen -la say that actions done in a dream don‟t have to be purified?  
GEN: You don‟t have to purify these because you don‟t accumulate any karma. 
Whatever kind of wish to kill you have in a dream, it is not the same as the wish to kill 
a living being in the waking state . You don‟t actually kill in a dream, and there is no 
object which you kill, so in no way is it the action of killing.  
QUESTION : Isn‟t it that high practitioners can practice in their sleep? It would follow 
they can‟t accumulate virtue in their sleep if y ou can‟t accumulate the karma of killing 
in your sleep.  
GEN: These actions have no outcome. Why do they have to practice in their sleep 
when these yogis can get up and practice? If they practice like Kyabje Zopa Rinpoche, 
this is ok isn‟t it? If you are g oing into a dream state, then it is ok to sleep some. I have 
to memorise great portions of text this year, but if I didn‟t have to do this in the wake 
state, but could do this whilst sleeping in the dream state, this would be very nice!  
 Seriously, those w ho are advanced and have the realisation of emptiness, before 
sleep they would meditate on emptiness. Then they would use their dreams by seeing 
them as fake, as not truly established and so on. They would remind themselves 
thinking, “This is now the dream  appearance, the example that is always used in the 
texts,” and use this to further their understanding. This is quite an advanced level. For 
us, we should generate as much as we can a mind of emptiness and the bodhimind 
before we go to sleep. By generatin g this kind of mind, it is possible something similar 
will appear in dreams. Then the dream experience isn‟t going to the side of ignorance, 
but there is hope the dream state also becomes virtuous.  
QUESTION : Does an action done under the influence of wrong  views become heavier 
or lighter? For example, someone killing insects due to not seeing them as animals, 
those having no faith in karma killing masses of insects due to the possibility of 
preventing disease, and also because wrong views are said to be the  heaviest action.  
GEN: The statement that wrong views is the heaviest is due to if someone engages in 
an action without wrong views, but with the strong wish to do harm, he accumulates 
negative karma from this, but because he doesn‟t have wrong views he i s aware of the 
consequences of this. Alternatively, he could engage in purification practices such as 
the four opponent powers, and therefore change the karma he has accumulated. 
Whereas if he doesn‟t accept cause and effect, whatever negativity he does, h e will 
never engage into any antidotes because he doesn‟t accept that he has accumulated 
anything. Over time, this will continue to grow and become progressively heavier. 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
445 From the point of view of one person engaging into purification and the other not, 
this is the heaviest aspect of having wrong views.  
 For example, killing is strongly forbidden for monk. It is one of the pillars of a 
monk‟s vows to refrain from taking the life of others and oneself. Within that, there is 
a difference made with the basis.  The Buddha himself stated that killing a human is 
much heavier than killing any other being because this human basis is considered more 
important than others.  A human life is more important than other lives, for example 
an animal life. If someone then kil ls all the life forms in one area, and another kills just 
one human, the killing of the many life forms would be heavier because there are so 
many. This isn‟t coming from the text, but there are examples like this we can think of.  
QUESTION : Does the wrong  view of not seeing a sentient being as sentient make the 
action lighter?  
GEN: If we look at a situation where one person kills a whole swarm of insects due to 
disease, or to protect his crops, and doesn‟t see them as sentient, and a second person 
does see  them as sentient but kills them for the same reason of protecting his crops – 
in both cases they have wrong view. However, in only one case do you have ill will. 
They both have wrong views because they don‟t consider future lives and don‟t 
consider the ou tcome of this action. They both act as if there is no outcome in a future 
life. This is what we mean with wrong view, whether or not you accept cause and 
effect of actions and past and future lives. It‟s not about if you accept animals have a 
mind or not, this is not the wrong view we are talking about in this context. The 
difference in these two examples is that one has ill will. He accepts that they have a 
mind, but also thinks if I harm them, it doesn‟t matter. In addition to the wrong view, 
he also has the wish to harm because he knows he will inflict suffering. Due to this 
additional ill will being present, his action is much heavier but they both have wrong 
view.  
 Is the action of this first person who doesn‟t see them as having a mind, is this 
heavy?  Yes, because he is under the power of wrong view in general. Whilst it is heavy, 
it is an action done but not accumulated because he doesn‟t understand the object has 
a mind. He sees the object as just like dirt to be washed out of clothes, if he doesn‟t 
get rid of them the crops will go to waste. He has an error in the action so it is done, 
but it is not accumulated. Nevertheless, this wrong view becomes a cause for the 
wrong view getting progressively stronger, and may end up in very strong forms of this  
view in the future, and create very strong actions. Having this wrong view he‟ll never 
see this action as negative and won‟t engage into purification. As a result, this action 
will grow and become stronger.
