  Page | 446 – week one to seven  QUESTION : What is the right way to kill? For ex ample, there are a lot of poisonous 
snakes in Brazil or if there are a lot of mosquitoes spreading disease. In some 
situations, it seems necessary to kill. How would the person who has to do this job 
deal with this?  
GEN:  This person would have to do it in  the way he normally does it. If you yourself 
are killing a poisonous mosquito, then you will be reborn as a poisonous mosquito. If 
you kill a poisonous snake, you will be reborn as a poisonous snake.  
QUESTION : Poisonous means that they can kill humans, s o sometimes it is necessary to 
get rid of these animals. How would a Dharma practitioner do this and purify it?  
GEN: Is this a snake -killing dharma practitioner?  
QUESTION : For example, if I‟m at the farm of my father, I would tell the caretaker 
there is su ch a poisonous snake, and then they would go and kill it, so I‟m part of that 
killing.  
GEN: Then you have engaged someone into killing. This is the same as killing it 
yourself, so you have performed the act of killing. This is something to be purified and  
the best way is to cultivate love and affection.  
 This is also expressed in a verse by the bodhisattva Shantideva,  
“the cultivation of love , compassion and the bodhi mind is the supreme amongst all the 
knowledge mantras .”  
This means that some people eng age into purification by all kinds of knowledge 
mantras, but Shantideva says that cultivating love, compassion and the bodhimind is 
the supreme knowledge mantra, meaning that there is nothing better for purification 
than that.  
QUESTION : What about self defence, if you are being attacked by the snake and you kill 
it before it kills you?  
GEN: This is such a case where the human life is more important than the animal life. 
In this case, you would kill the animal to protect the human life. You would do this 
out of compassion to stop the animal creating the negative karma of killing a human. 
This would be killing out of the attitude of compassion.  
QUESTION : Is there still negative karma on your side?  
GEN: By killing out of compassion you would accumulate a sma ll negative karma, but 
also a great mass of merit. On the one hand, we are killing, but we also have the 
attitude to stop the snake creating karma that would lead them back to the hell realms 
and all kinds of difficult states.  
QUESTION : How is it possible  that one action can both generate virtue and non -virtue 
at the same time?  GEN: The compassion is not killing, this is just the motivation. From 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
447 this, you get the great waves of merit. The killing is a different act, and from this, you 
get the small negati vity. You have two acts here.  
QUESTION : Would an arya bodhisattva let the animal kill him in order not to harm the 
animal?  
GEN: A poisonous snake would not be able to bite an arya bodhisattva. They are 
beings who have attained the path of seeing, and by th is time the body has completely 
changed and cannot be bitten by such a snake.  
  
4 Classification of what has been done and [what has been] accumulated  
The difference betw een what has been done and w hat has been accumulated [is as 
follow s]. What has been d one is w hat has been intended or w hat has manifested 
through the body or speech after having been intended. What has been accumulated 
are actions apart from the ten [exceptions] like those done in a dream. What has not 
been accumulated are the ten [excepti ons] such as those done in a dreamlvi.  
 
5 [Further classification of that which will definitely be experienced]  
{ 114}  What w ill definitely be experienced has three [categories] in terms of the time 
w hen the result is experienced:  
 1. That w hich w ill be exp erienced as a visible phenomenon is an action  w hose 
effect w ill be experienced in this life.  
 2. That w hich w ill be experienced after taking rebirth is [an action] w hose effect 
w ill be experienced in the follow inglvii life.  
 3. That w hich w ill be experienced at another time is what w ill be experienced 
from the next but one life onw ards.  
 
Visible phenomenon  means you accumulate and experience a certain karma  in that very 
life it was accumulated. The second type is experienced in the next life, and the third 
type is experienced in a life after the next life.  
 
QUESTION : What does it mean not definite to be experienced? Does it mean you won‟t 
experience it at all, or that it‟s not definite what you will experience?  
GEN: This means you will not have to  take on a life from this, meaning the completely 
ripening effect. It can be from having done the act, but having purified it completely 
so there is no result whatsoever, and so not definite to be experienced. Or, you engage 
in purification and it is utter ly changed. For example, you kill and this action is strong 
enough to send you to the hell realms. Then you engage in purification and whilst you 
can‟t get rid of it completely, it is changed so instead of leading you to a hell realm you 
experience a heada che in the human realm. This is showing that you can change the 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 448 – week one to seven  strong power of a fully ripened result leading to a rebirth into some small effect in 
another realm. Therefore, it‟s not definite in many ways.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B2 Specific considerations on karma   
A The way in which it is necessary to achieve a fully qualified basis  
[B Divisions]  
2B4B -2B1A -2B2A  The way in which it is necessary to achieve a fully qualified basis  
Second: Through abandoning the ten non -virtues you do indeed obtain a good basis. 
How ever, if you achieve a fully qualified basis for accomplishing omniscience, it w ill 
be quite unlike others [w ith respect to] covering ground in meditations on the path. 
Therefore, you should attain that basis.  
 
If you practice ethical disciple that abandons the ten non -virtuous actions, then you 
attain the special basis of a precious human rebirth. This is definite, and I will put my 
name to this. If you train like this, w hen you get old and die, you will then get another 
human rebirth.  
 All the ten non -virtuous actions are about bringing harm and violence to others. 
If we are refraining from these, the result will be that we are not harmed by other 
factors. We will take a  life that is free from harm or violence, other beings will not 
harm our life span, and all unpleasant experiences are blocked by practicing the ten 
white Dharmas. These ten virtuous actions also have completely ripening results, 
causally concordant result s, empowering results – all these will come about by 
restraining from the ten non -virtuous actions, or in other words practicing the ten 
virtuous actions.  
 For example, we have attained the excellent basis of being reborn in the human 
realm, but still we have unwished for experiences. We need to practice a pure form of 
restraining from the ten non -virtuous actions and practice the ten virtuous actions, so 
we will again have a human rebirth and will not have all these unwished for situations.  
 We have atta ined such a human birth and within that we have taken ordination, 
and this is indeed a perfect basis for attaining the omniscient state. This necessarily has 
to be attained in the future so we should work to bring together the conditions to 
attain this per fect basis for attaining the omniscient state in future lives.

cause of the fully ripened [ effects].

QUESTION : As we now have a precious human rebirth that is an excellent basis for 
Dharma practice, is this a condition for a lot of negativ e karma to ripen in order for us 
to work with it using the Dharma?  
GEN: This rebirth is the excellent basis for Dharma practice, so it includes being a 
basis for purification.  We should think, “I will now do purification so the negative 
actions in the pas t do not have to ripen, to purify them before they ripen.” This is why 
we do purification - so they don‟t ripen.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B2B -1  The fully ripened positive qualities and their function  
A Excellent life span  
B Excellent complexion  
C Excellent extraction  
D Excellent power  
E Respectable words  
F Renown as being very powerful  
G Being male  
H Possessing Strength  
The first one has eight points: excellent life span, excellent complexion, excellent 
extractionlviii, excellent pow er, respectable w ords, renow n as being very pow erful, being 
male, and having strength.  
 
Next, we look at the good qualities of the fully ripening effect and their function. What 
kind of life do we want to achieve? We want an excellent basis , meaning the human 
basis, and within that, we want to achieve a long life. We engage into strong positive 
actions to attain these states. Further, from engaging into beneficial actions for others, 
the effect would be that we have a long life and secondly,  we do not meet many 
obstacles. The third aspect would be that we bring forth great benefit for others, 
meaning we are able to achieve great deeds for the sake of others and have a long life 
to be able to do many projects for others.   
 
A Excellent life sp an 
The first one means that due to an earlier throw ing [action], a long life has been 
throw n and one lives accordingly. Due to this, you undertake actions for the purpose 
of yourself and others accumulating a lot of virtue over a long period of time.  
 
B Excellent complexion  
The second one refers to good complexion and shape, complete sense pow ers and 
balanced proportions. Due to this, disciples having been delighted at one’s very sight, 
are gathered and listen to one’s w ords.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 450 – week one to seven  The next aspect of trying to achieve an excellent basis for Dharma practice is being of 
good complexion, colour or form. It would be qualified as having complete sense 
faculties, having a perfectly proportioned body and so on. This comes from having 
practiced virtue in the past. You w ould aim at attaining a body that people are naturally 
attracted to, and has many aspects that show others you have practiced a lot of virtue.  
 Another effect of this kind of body in a Dharma rebirth is that you would try to 
help others by giving them Dha rma advice, and they would be very inclined to listen to 
you and follow your advice.  
 
C Excellent extraction  
The third one is to be born into an exalted and w ell -know n family respected by the 
w orld.  Due to this, one’s commands w ill be accomplished and no t transgressed.  
 
The third point that fully qualifies a Dharma rebirth is to be born in a family where 
people have the outlook of this being an excellent person.  
 
D Excellent power  
The fourth one consists in massive resources, friends and follow ers. Due  to this, one 
gathers a lot of sentient beings and ripens them.  
 
The fourth point is about having great resources, many friends and extensive retinue, 
which would enable you to gather people and ripen them, to help their consciousness 
evolve.  
 
E Respectab le words  
Fifth: As you do not deceive others through body or speech, sentient beings uphold your 
w ords. Due to this, you gather sentient beings w ith the four [w ays of] gathering 
[disciple]s, and ripen them.  
 
By not having deceived others through your body  and speech, the effect is that all 
others will accept your words. The point is that they perceive you as being a truthful 
person and they might, for example, call on you to mediate problems as you are non -
partial and trustworthy etc. Through this, one is able to gather disciples in the four 
ways and ripen them.  
 
F Renown as being very powerful  
Sixth: Due to possessing positive qualities such as diligence in generosity and so forth, 
you become an object of veneration of great beings. { 115}  Due to this, the y help you 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
451 w ith all activities, whereby they acknow ledge your deeds and quickly heed your 
commands.  
 
Due to having the good qualities of giving and so forth (making effort in ethical 
discipline, meditative absorption and so on) one becomes an object of veneration by 
the holy beings, in other words they will praise you by saying, “They are very excellent 
and have all these qualities.”  
 By this, all your activities become well known to others. The news of your 
qualities reaches others, and by them knowing your activities they generate a form of 
gratitude towards you and an awareness of all the good things you do. Then they want 
to repay this kindness, and are inclined to listen to your advice. They say, “Oh, this 
person is a very kind person, I will definit ely listen to any advice they have to give.”  
 
G Being male  
The seventh one is to possess a male organ. Thereby you become a vessellix  for all 
qualities and, through aspiration and effort, a vessel for expanding w isdom. You are 
not afraid among people, and n obody will prevent you from going w ith all sentient 
beings or living in seclusion.  
 
The women here should not be angry with this point! This is expressed here as having 
a male organ. The point here is if you are born as a man, wherever you go you will 
have  fewer situations in which you can be harmed. If you want to go to the mountains 
as a Dharma practitioner, as a man there are fewer occasions where someone will harm 
you. As a woman, there are many more situations where, if you are in isolated places, 
peop le will make problems for you. This is one such example of aiming to be a man.  
 Another example mentioned here is that being born male will create fewer 
problems when you have a crowd around you. You will also have fewer obstacles if 
you are alone, for ex ample doing a retreat, than if your physical basis is that of a 
woman. This is reflecting that there are fewer problems for Dharma practice if you are 
a man.  
 Of course, situations can change. In our area, I feel that by now the women are 
stronger than th e men, and maybe the men are overpowered by the women! Maybe we 
should aim for a future rebirth as a woman.  
 
H Possessing strength  
Eighth: By the pow er of previous actions, there naturally being only few harms and no 
diseases, one will have great enthusias m that arises from the conditions of this life.  
Due to that,  you w ill not get discouraged by w hatever goals you or others may have, 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 452 – week one to seven  and in dependence on intense enthusiasm you attain the pow er of individual 
investigation  w hereupon supernormal cognitive pow ers  w ill be quick [to develop].  
 
Through the power of previous actions (previous karma, previous aspiring prayers, 
previous merit, and so on) you naturally end up in situations where there is little harm. 
You are also without sickness etc., meaning ob stacles are fewer. Also due to these 
good conditions, there arises an enthusiasm and feeling of being capable, which comes 
from having performed good actions in the past, and having these good conditions.  
 Through achieving both the aims of oneself and ot hers, naturally a great 
enthusiasm arises.  
 Further, you attain the wisdom realising emptiness and the power that comes 
from this. Then you can quickly attain to states of concentration, expressed here as 
supernormal cognitive powers , refereeing to miracu lous power s.  
This is then the presentation of the eight good qualities of a future life.

B Threefold division  
 
Then come the causes for such a qualified rebirt h, first listed as eight.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B2B -2A  Division of the causes in eight  
 
The second one has eight points the first of w hich is not to cause harm to any being 
and to cultivate an attitude of non -harm. Furthermore it says:  
 
Through freeing those caught in places of slaughter  
And likew ise helping through [giving] life,  
Through reversing harming creatures  
You w ill gain longevity.  
From a doctor’s respect for the ill,  
From the administration of medicine  
From not harming creatures w ith sticks  
Or lumps of earth, you w ill be w ithout illness.   
 
Firstly, not hurting or harming others and relying on an attitude of non -violence, in 
other words generating a mind of non -harm. This is backed up by the quote. The point 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
453 here is that by freeing beings from situatio ns where they would be put to death and 
helping them by giving life, you yourself receive a long life. By a doctor having respect 
for the ill, serving them, giving them medicine, there will be no illness or harm from 
other animals. This is how I read these  last lines, there is no word for sticks here.  
  
The second one consists in offering light, such as butter lamps and new clothing. Apart 
from that:  
From the absence of anger as basis  
And offering jew elry – a good physique.  
From lack of envy [you w ill gain ] 
Good fortune as fruit, it is explained    
  
This consists of offering. The word light here is more like illumination or appearance. I 
read this as giving something to others that illuminates them or makes their mind clear, 
giving them good conditions so t heir mind is illuminated. Then with regard to their 
body, where there is any kind of situation where  sentient beings are destitute or poor, 
you give them the conditions to help them out of their sad situation and make them 
more joyful. This is two forms o f giving.  
 These last two lines raise the question, “How does good fortune arise from a 
lack of envy?” With any excellent state that others have obtained and enjoy, you keep 
yourself free from any kind of jealousy. Not only that, but you rejoice in them h aving 
these wonderful enjoyments. In this way, you abstain from any negative behaviour and 
through this achieve an excellent physique. This is explained here as jewellery . We could 
say that someone who already has an excellent physique is made even more be autiful 
by all kinds of ornaments. You can achieve this excellent physique from refraining 
from jealousy, anger etc.  
Not only that, but the text says good fortune as fruit . This is a play on words as 
the word for “fortune” can also be the word for eon. Th is means there is good fortune 
of a type that includes all good conditions and all marvellous things - and these are 
innumerable.

2B4B -2B1A -2B2B -2  The causes of the fully ripened [effects]  
A Division of the causes in eight  
B Threefold division  
 
The subtle explanation of this topic  of cause and effect , which makes up the Four 
Noble Truths,  will actually come  in the section on the medium scope. It will be 
presented from the point of view of  how the true origins are causes for their result, the 
true sufferings. And from the true causes , the true paths , and the different levels of 
true cessations come as a result. Here in the context of the being of small capacity , we 
talked about the aspects  of ka rma and its effects. The specific point s presented here 
are the causes that achieve positive effects; i.e. how a positive result or life is attained . 
This is presented in two ways, as a presentation of eight and three causes. The 
first of these eight is an  absence of harm towards sentient beings and comes together 
with a non -harming attitude. So on the one hand one doesn‟t actually cause harm , and 
on the oth er hand , one also cultivates the corresponding  attitude.  
   
2B4B -2B1A -2B2B -2A  Division of the cause s in eight  
The second one has eight points the first of w hich is not to cause harm to any being 
and to cultivate an attitude of non -harm. Furthermore it says:  
Through freeing those caught in places  of slaughter  
And likew ise helping through [giving] life,  
Through reversing harming creatures  
You w ill gain longevity.  
From a doctor’s respect for the ill,  
From the administration of medicine  
From not harming creatures w ith sticks  
Or lumps of earth, you w ill be w ithout illness.    
 
This quote especially expresses the way to attain the first cause  of not acting in a 
harmful way and relying on the mind of non -harm. Here one tries to save beings who 
are about to be killed  or slaughtered  from their fate . For example  to buy fish and 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
455 release them into a body of water  is something we can see Lama Zopa Rinpoche doing 
often. Also in the monastery , this is something that the monks are very interested in. 
In one instance, the Tibetan community were holding a lot of chicken s to kill them , 
but His Holiness bought all of them  and liberated them, ending this custom in the 
Tibetan society.  
 An example of the second line  would be saving someone from being 
carried away by water or endangered by fire.  
 Third line: some harm is done to a living being and you act to stop this. 
Let‟s  say Tharchin verbally insults Gendün , but then I interfere and correct this, but in 
a very friendly and skilful way.  The fourth line: Having engaged into all this activity, 
the effect will be that one‟s own life doesn‟t have any obstacles. This means that  there 
will be no sudden death , and you will be able to live out your complete lifespan. You 
won‟t for example encounter people or situations that are very harmful to your life.  
 The fifth line: this talks about serving the ill. You are benefiting people 
who are sick , or you might help people with all kinds of problems; the elderly for 
example or the people who are alone. In this way,  you can help people in many 
different ways.  
 Sixth line:  a doctor giving medicine to a patient to free him from pain. So  
the meaning here is to support  someone who has  problems.  
 Seventh line: this talks about protecting sentient beings from all kinds of 
harm. All this is expressed as being virtuous activity.  
 
The second one consists in offering light, such as butter lamps a nd new clothing.  Apart 
from that:  
From the absence of anger as basis  
And offering jew elry  – a good physique.  
From lack of envy [you w ill gain]  
Good fortune as fruit, it is explained    
  
Examples of this could include fixing someone‟s electric  supply or g iving clothes  to 
someone who has none.  It is especially emphasised not to generate anger towards 
others , or to be jealous towards what they have. Quite the opposite, we should 
generate a mind that is happy about the good situation of other people.  
 From the absence of anger : this encourages us to cultivating patience.  
If you are able to engage into the methods to abstain from anger, then the effect from 
this will be a beautiful body , and through relating to the wealth of others  with a mind 
of rejoicing ra ther than one of jealousy will also create similar resources.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 456 – week one to seven  The third one is to overcome one’s pride and respect gurus etc. and others like a 
servant.  
 
What needs to be destroyed is the pride of self -cherishing. One practices respect 
towards the teacher who shows you the actual dharma , as well as showing respect to 
others. We should see them as being of great importance . Alternatively,  we could also 
say that we want to behave as good persons towards others, so they experience us as a 
person bringing joy t o them;  they should be happy to meet us.  
 
 The fourth one is giving food and clothing, and so forth to those w ho ask and 
also benefit [others] w ithout being asked, giving aw ay [freely] to the suffering and to 
those w ho are fields of good qualities but lack  necessities.  
Therefore,  if someone is very destitute  of food and drink,  we would try to support that 
person with food and drink. This would be the case even if this person had good 
qualities, good education , but still lacked food and drink.  
  
 { 116}  The fifth one is the habit of abandoning the four non -virtues of speech.  
In general , we can understand  that we should abandon anything that is of harm to 
others. Especially the second non-virtue of speech, divisive speech;  where two 
harmonious peopl e are brought apart through our speech . There is probably nothing 
more horrible than this, this is really heavy. With harsh speech you are directly 
harming one person , but with divisive speech you are harming at least two , so is 
probably the heaviest  of th e four non-virtues of speech. It seems like telling lies is the 
worst because it is the first to mentioned , but if we reflect on it, then divisive speech is 
probably the worst thing you can do with your speech. One might use harsh speech or 
tell lies to di vide people , so these might be even included in divisive speech.  
 It is the same if you actually utter words , or if you write an email for example. 
So here , we have to be very careful as we are in a place of dharma, in a place where 
people are practicing virtue; so here we should be careful not to constantly engage into 
non-virtue.  
 The sixth one is to make aspirational prayers to accomplish all kinds of positive 
qualities  in the future and make offerings  to the three jew els, one’s parents, 
shravakas, pratyekabuddhas, abbots, masters, and gurus.  
This describes  how to attain all good qualities through aspirational prayers. This needs 
to be reflected on as well. Let‟s imagine that someone on the one hand constantly tells 
subtle lies or is deceiving people , but then pretends to have sincere refuge. As there is 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
457 no way of deceiving the buddhas and bodhisattvas who are directly aware of what we 
are doing , we really  should be very careful with our action s, knowing that these holy 
beings are perceiving us.  
 The seventh one is to rejoice in the good qualities of men and, w ith regard to 
w omen, to look at their disadvantages w ithout rejoicing, to oppose the inclinations of 
those w ho w ish for a female body, and to liberate those whose male organs are going to 
be cut . 
 
I read this to mean that there are people with all kinds of different wishes and 
longings. There are men for example who would like to have a female body , and men 
who have the wish to have a male body. So being occupied with this kind of thought is 
somet hing that should be abandoned. Whatever you are born as you stick with that , 
rather than being  constantly being preoccupied about changing your body. Such states 
of desire as being unhappy about our certain type of body, and wanting a different one 
are all  very strange. If this existed in the past is not clear, but that this mental state of 
desire existed in the past is very clear.  
 
 The eighth one is to carry out yourself what others cannot do at all, to help 
them w ith w hat they can do together w ith you an d to give food and drink to others.  
This refers to someone being sick for example , and as he is unable to go and get the 
medicine he needs himself, you do it for him . There are many examples, like helping 
the elderly to walk around or carry something for them. In Tibetan,  the second part 
literally means „befriending or accompanying to that with regards to what one is able to 
binding oneself ‟. I read this rather as tak ing on some responsibility. For example , 
someone is able to accomplish his needs , but as you anyway have the same thing to do, 
you do it for them. For example , if you go shopping and the other person also needs 
to go, then you do their task as well. In general, one should be helpful towards o thers 
and give food and drink.

  Page | 458 – week one to seven  2B4B -2B1A -2B2B -2B1 The first cause: a perfectly pure mind  
A Aspects relating to oneself  
B Aspects relating to others  
As for the three causes, [the first one,] a perfectly pure mi nd has tw o aspects relating 
to oneself.  
 
A Aspects relating to oneself  
They are:  
 1. to dedicate the virtues of accomplishing those causes to unsurpassed 
enlightenment w ithout hoping for a fully ripened effect, and  
 2. to accomplish the causes intensely from the bottom of one’s heart.  
You dedicate for all sentient beings, rather than thinking about wanting a good rebirth 
or a good life just for yourself. The second refers to really going into it with your 
entire  mind  instead of  just do ing things casually without much thought. In this way , it 
becomes a strong way of achieving these causes.  
 
B Aspects relating to others  
The tw o aspects relating to others are:  
1. to give up envy, competitiveness and scorn at the sight of t he highest, 
intermediate and least [ practitioners]  in agreements w ith the Dharma and  
2. to rejoice, even if you do not manage [completely]  to examine many times a 
day w hat to do.  
You practice in order not to generate jealousy to the one who is supreme to you, not 
to generate competitiveness to the one who is equal to you , and not to generate 
disregard for the least practitioner. Instead , you try to rejoice. If you are unable  to do 
this, then you should at least, on a daily basis tr y to do it as much as pos sible 
[translators note: the way it is translated is a little bit incorrect: the rejoicing is actually part of 
number one; so the second one tells what to do if o ne is not capable of doing that] . 
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B2B -2B2 The second cause: pure performance  
A Relating to oneself  
Pure performance w ith regard to oneself implies long duration, continuity and 
intensity.  
B Relating to others  
With regard to others it consists in causing someone w ho has not correctly  taken [the 
rules of ethical discipline] to do so, prai sing those w ho have, so that they generate 
delight, to make them do so continuously, and to make them not abandon them.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
459 This refers to any kind of engagement in virtue , and so we try to steer someone to do 
this when they are not,  as well as trying to gene rate joy in those who have engaged in 
virtue  through praising  them , and encouraging them to continue.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B2B -2B2C  The third cause: a pure field  
Since the very tw o - attitude and performance - produce plenty of  good fruit, they 
resemble a field.  
The point made here is that fr om this pure kind of behaviour  there is not only a 
positive  outcome, but a great many positive outcomes of an excellent quality. By  
reflecting on this causes one engages  into the se first two. Therefore,  we could say that 
the t hird cause is to reflect on how the first two are a good field , which is acting as a 
strong encouragement for the practitioner  to engage into them .  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B3 After these considerations how to engage [in virtuous actions] and oppose [the 
non-virtuous]  
The point is that one has already reflected on cause and effect , and therefore one 
thinks about how to engage in positive  actions and turn away from negative ones.    
 
A General presentation  
B Specifically how to purify by means of the four powers  
{ 117}  The third one - after these considerations how to engage [in virtuous actions] 
and oppose [the non -virtuous] – has tw o points: general presentation and specifically 
how  to purify by means of the four pow ers.  
 
If we reflect on these things as much as we can , we will be able to make our life 
excellent . Actually, if you are becoming a good person and lead ing a good life, then 
this is exactly what it means to engage into virtue. Therefore,  achieving virtue  doesn‟t 
mean to get up and go somewhere special;  it mea ns that you should  continuously 
cultivate these virtuous states of mind; to lead an excellent life. You don‟t necessarily 
have to sit down upright in meditation posture , and meditate on virtue; it simply 
means that you need to develop positive states of mi nd and positive activities; that 
already produces virtue and avoids negativities.  
We are people who accept past and future lives  that can be of  a happy or of 
suffering type . If we live a good life, then we will definitely have a good rebirth. We 
need a way of acting which is in accordance with others. If we have this , then naturally 
we will have a positive life, be in harmony with others and have a good rebirth. On the 
other hand, if we  don‟t try to think of and be in accord with others, then this is a sign 
that we haven‟t generated the right form of behaviour , and from this many instances of 
negative behaviour and misfortune can come about  in this and future lives.

  Page | 460 – week one to seven  MONDAY 7 JUNE  2010  - SESSION 2 
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B3A  General presentation  
1 How to bring forth a mind cultivating this perpetually, every day and night  
First: In Engaging in [Bodhisattva] Behaviour it says:  
Suffering springs from w hat is non -virtuous.  
‘How  to reach certain freedom from it?’  
It is appropriate , day and night,  
Alw ays to think about nothing but this.    [51] 
Here the great son of the conquerors, Shantideva  explains how  negative s tates of mind 
are tormenting us;  how they not only bring suffering to othe rs but also to ourselves. 
This not only causes  s suffering in this life, but also in future lives too  through leaving 
a negative imprint from which suffering will be generated in the future . Therefore,  
since these non -virtuous states only bring suffering in this , and in future lives we 
should definite ly try to avoid them.  
 Having  understood this , the question is which method we can apply to free 
ourselves from the se negative states . So here , it is clearly said that the way to do this is 
to reflect day and night on the very truth  of how these negative actions bring  suffering . 
Therefore,  in order to develop  a mind that completely avoids engaging into 
negativities , we have to repeatedly reflect on cause and effect , and in this way, we  will 
be capable  of turning  away from these negative states . Always to think about nothing but  
this: since we are dharma practitioners, one of our main activities should be to reflect 
on this and cultivate this thought.   
And: 
The root of everything that’s virtuous  
Is aspiration, the Muni said.  
Again, the root of that is always  
To meditate on fully ripened effects.  
 
The cause for achieving  states of joy and excellence  is to engage in virtue , and by  
understanding this there will come a great longing to do this . The text mentions here 
the aspiration  for virtue; the process i s as usual: after having studied how from positive 
actions happiness ripens, faith is  generated in this process , and if this faith is strong 
enough, it leads to aspiration . You are inspired to engage into virtue as you have 
understood that it will lead to happiness , which  then leads on to joy ous effort in order 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
461 to actually bring about these causes of happiness. So again, the cause to bring about 
this aspiration is to always meditate on fully ripened effects .  
 Therefore,  if we reflect as is mentioned in the se two verses we are able to live 
our lives in a way that avoids negative acts , and leads us to aspiration and joyous effort 
to en gage into positive acts , which are the  cause for our happiness in this and future 
lives.   
 
QUESTION:  What is meant by roots o f virtue; what does it mean when it is said  that 
our roots of virtue will be destroyed?  
GEN:  The root of all virtue is actually faith,  or we could say a conviction with regard 
to the presentation of karmic cause and effect. This means w e have generated a 
conviction in  the fact that from a virtuous  action , only ha ppiness ripens , and from a 
negativity  only suffering comes. So first,  we generate faith , and using this as a basis 
aspiration can be generated. Such a mind wants to engage into virtue and abandon 
negative actions , and  by having this strong wish to engage into virtue you then 
generate joyous effort with which to practice. Therefore,  through this joyous effort 
you are actually attaining virtue, the roots or causes of happiness. All of this comes 
from a conviction in the pres entation  of cause and effect , and therefore this conviction 
is regarded as being the root of virtue.  
 There is also the other quote from the Bodhisattvacharyavatara :  
“Whatever good conduct was amassed in thousand aeons by giving an d worship to the 
sugatas and the like; all is destroyed by one flash of anger” .  
 
Anger  would destroy a previously accumulated virtuous imprint. Something that 
you are about to accumulate right now is also destroyed , and for the future it will act 
as an ob stacle to accumulate new virtue.  This shows how destructive anger is towards 
virtue. In the English,  it says „destroyed‟ , but this does not mean that these virtuous 
minds become  non-existent  or that you can‟t  generate a virtuous mind anymore. It 
means more  that it diminishes, that this mind will be harmed and weak ened in 
producing  new positive  karma. In addition,  if you had a strong virtuous mind and were 
about to gain a realisation , the n because of the arising of  anger this attainment would 
be postponed  for eons, to the point where you think it its not longer possible to attain 
the realisation.  
 We could use the following analogy: out of a moment of anger, you could say 
some very harsh and harmful words to someone you‟ve always had a good relationship 
to. As a result, this person is very offended to the point that your relationship could 
become very distant or even break. In this way, what was previously very strong and 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 462 – week one to seven  close , has been greatly damaged by this anger. It would take a  long time to repair th is 
through continuous  effort from your side , but  even then , the relationship would  not be 
like before. The other person might have had a strong affection towards you , and the 
hope that this relationship w ill be of great help , but this was crushed with a ha mmer 
blow of your harsh words . In this quote, Shantideva talks within  a dharma context , and 
so is not really  interested in the effect on  this life , but on the effects  anger has  on the 
future.  As a result, he describes how anger has the power to destroy our  virtuous 
imprints , which would have the cause of happiness  in future lives . As this is destroyed, 
what you are left with to take to the next life are negative imprints ready to ripen into 
suffering.   
 
QUESTION:  Previously Lama  Tsongkhapa  described that t he effect corresponding  to 
the cause of killing is a short lifespan. However,  here the positive result of saving 
someone‟s life is to have a long life oneself, but this is presented here as a ripening 
result. Why are these two results  explained as being  different?   
GEN:  Any karma that we a re accumulating is contaminated to some extent, and leads 
to a life in samsara. So all of these are kind of ripening karmas. So , from saving a life 
for example you will always get a karma that ripens as a good rebirth , like a human . 
Then depending if the action was small , middling or great , it has the power to keep 
you in th is rebirth for a small, middle  or long time. This already comes with the 
ripening result. Let‟s say you have a rebirth out of a very strong karma , and the karma 
that brings you there has the power to keep you there for a long time. However,  there 
comes along  a negative completing karma, which brings obstacles to that other karma , 
and shortens your lifespan by acting as an interruption. This completing karma would 
be a result similar to the cause; i.e. you had previously interfered in the lifespan of 
someone else.  The same thing can happen with a positi ve ripening karma that is weak;  
i.e. can only hold you in that life for a short time. If you have a str ong store of positive 
karma , then it can support this and therefore make it into a long life.   
 Tharchin for example took on this Tharchin body coming from positive 
ripening karma. From this , he encounters all kinds  of nice situations, which come  from 
the additional completing karma.  This would be called karma that is in accordance 
with the cause (causally concordant karma). However,  even though  he took on this 
body as a fully ripened  result  of virtuous karma, he also has all kinds of unwished for 
situati ons, like his allergies, which come from the non -virtue he has previously created 
and is now ripening as  causally concordant.  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
463 QUESTION:  What about the completing karma of Yönten‟s cats ? They have been 
born into the animal realm from a negative ripening karma , but even then, they were 
dumped in a garbage bin and abandoned, which seems to be a negative completing 
karma . However, they were then saved , which seems to be a new completing karma , 
and then when they are given away to new homes , there seems to be  even another 
karma ripening. So does one completing karma get exhausted at some point , and then 
another ripens?  
GEN:  All of these different situations are the ripening of different completing karmas. 
Being abandoned in a garbage bin was one, then when this ran out there was a new 
situation for which a new karma ripened, and so on. In this way , the mind of the 
experience  is like an ocean where there are constantly waves coming and going , rising 
and falling ; karmas are constantly  ripening and going again.  We aim at changing the  
disturbed ocean of our mind in to the peaceful and undisturbed water of a swimming 
pool. We want to end those endless waves coming out of the mind , and dissolving 
again by taking care of our body and mind; abandoning the negative an d engaging in 
positive actions.   
 If we say  that in order to gain this state we have to take care of our body and 
mind, then this is mainly talking about the mind. Since this is in our hands, we can 
choose to have complete power over it , if we want. The b ody might end up under the 
power of someone else, but once we have complete control over our own mind, no 
one else has the power to do anything to our mind.  
 
MONDAY 7 JUNE  2010  - SESSION 3 
QUESTION:  In the context of the three caus es making an action excellent, the first 
one is relating to one self . With this, on one hand we should dedicate without hoping 
for the effect , but on the other hand we should constantly reflect on what ripening 
effects come from the various actions. How do es that go together?    
GEN:  In general , we have to say that the motivation for any dharma practice should be 
the aim of complete enlightenment for all sentient beings. We should dedicate our 
merits  to this goal . Someone else might dedicate these very same merits to a ripening 
result like wanting to have a good body, friends, riches and so on , but for  us this is 
unsuitable, because it is not what we are aiming for. We really need to try and avoid 
this.  
However,  later on it says you should accumulate merits and shun negativities to 
gain such an excellent body, riches, etc. Here, we have to unders tand this in the 
context of  the being of small capacity, who indeed practices with the motivation to 
achieve  these things. Nevertheless,  we at least want to get liberated, so we don‟t aim at 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 464 – week one to seven  a rebirth in samsara , even though  to achieve liberation  we need a continuous rebirth in 
the upper realms, which enables us to practice the dharma and get liberated from 
samsara. Therefore,  we do need t he same things , but our aim is different  in that we are 
aiming at a rebirth outside of samsara .  
 
QUESTION:  It is said in the Lam-Rim Chenmo  that whatever karma is heaviest will 
ripen into the next life. This means that if someone kills, then this should e ither ripen 
into a rebirth , or could even ripen within this life  (for example in the case of soldiers) . 
But this doesn‟t happen  often , why is that? Also there is a sequence as to which karma 
ripens first, but the text also  says that it depends on the condi tions at the time of 
death, this seems to be different.  
GEN:   About the first part: if you kill someone , the police will eventually get you and 
put you into prison for many years. This is what happens here, but in China,  they will 
even beat you up and kill  you. These effects  are visible phenomena , and if you are not 
caught in this life, it will ripen in a future rebirth caused  by the heaviest karma 
ripening . Soldiers for example are mainly ordered  to kill, it is not their own 
decision. They might kill a lot , and possibly  have the four conditions of a complete 
karma , but mostly they are not doing this out of their own incentive . While they are 
fighting I nthis war, they might generate the wish to kill, but originally they were 
ordered to go to war. There are actions  that are so strong that  they need  to ripen as a 
visible phenomenon, meaning as a deed of which the fruition is experienced in this 
life. However,  not all heavy karmas are like that , and the required conditions could just 
ripen into a rebirth.  
Let‟s say you killed one person today and one yesterday. Both actions are  equally 
heavy, so which one ripens earlier? There is a sequence of the heaviest ripening first, 
and if two are equally heavy, the one that was created earlier will ripen, and so on.  
 If the police catch a criminal who committed negativities  all his life;  killing, 
stealing, etc. then they will sit do wn with him and interrogate him. They will  ask him , 
“So and so many years back you killed this and this person”;  they would not talk abou t 
the things he stole , as this is small in comparison,  but first they talk about the killing  
he did.  Moreover,  they would not talk about what he done most recently, but would 
start at what he had done at the earliest point in his criminal career and then w ork 
towards what he had done most recently.  Another guy , is not such a career criminal, 
but more of a petty thief who was arrested for a car that he stole today. When  the 
police now talk to him they don‟t talk about the food he stole from the supermarket, 
but about the car he stole, because it is heavier. In the same way , it is our heaviest  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
465 karma  that will ripen first, and in both cases, the person caught by the police is the 
analogy for having met the conditions for the karma to ripen.  
 
QUESTION:  Aren‟t th e conditions themselves also a ripening of karma?  
GEN:  Yes. But I‟m not saying that the karma is creating the police  themselves, but 
rather I‟m saying that the karma is creating to get caught by the police. The police 
themselves come about through their ow n karma, their own parents!   
 
QUESTION:  But how does the karma of the police, which is someone else‟s karma , 
connect with mine?  
GEN:   To understand this you need to study the sixth chapter of the 
Bodhisattvacharyavatara .  
In such a  situation , you are encountering an adverse situation . If you wouldn‟t 
have the karma for it, you couldn‟t encounter this situation . Additionally, consider it 
from the point of view that your capture by the police is actually the ripening of a 
positive karma, because  if you had not been caught you might have continued to act 
with negative behaviour. Therefore, there could be some danger connected with 
positive karma!  
 You will encounter situations that will bring suffering to your body and mind 
because you have previo usly caused suffering to the body and mind of other people. 
This is what is meant by the term causally concordant effect. Questions like what exact 
action did I do and for how long and with who m, are what is called a very hidden 
phenomenon, which you can o nly understand if you have the capacity to do so . The 
actual relationship between actions acting as causes to bring about certain effects is 
not a hidden phenomenon , but it can be clearly established by logic an d reason.  We 
have heard these reasons through out the teachings. For example , how from anger you 
yourself become stirred up and engage into negative actions towards others , and 
destroy the good relationships you have with  them.  In his teachings, the Buddha gave 
so many direct examples and explained th ings as they are. He also did this with the 
mental states, and how in the internal and external states go together. This is what 
Western science is so interested in nowadays. Let‟s take the example of how imprints 
are stored on the mind; we can use the ana logy of how an incredible amount of data is 
stored on a microchip or a memory stick. However, if you look for the information on 
it, you are unable to find it. In the same way, there are an incredible  number of 
imprints left on our continuum. We should rep eatedly go over these  analogies and 
reasonings, and in this way, we can see how totally amazing the buddha‟s teachings are. 
Through this , a certainty and  strong faith of conviction will arise due to having 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 466 – week one to seven  investigated the buddha‟s with reason. In this way , one will understand the qualities of 
the buddha and his teachings, and adopt them completely.  
Later on there is a quote in the text where  it is stated that  both the migrators in the 
world  and the world itself are  constantly changing, but  compared to thi s space itself is 
not changing . Therefore,  if somehow the mere possibility of space changing would 
come about, then still there would be no possibility for the teachings of the buddha to 
be at fault. In this way , the quote makes very clear that what the bu ddha is teaching is 
very reliable. Therefore,  such understanding or trust in the buddhas teaching , and an 
amazement about the fact that someone could teach something like this , will come 
about on the basis of having thoroughly analyzed cause and effect and gained strong 
certainty about it.  
 
QUESTION:  I am born as a human being and my lifespan is not short; also I didn‟t kill 
any human being in this life.  Could  I conclude from this that I have not accumulated 
the karma of having  killed anyone in the past?  
GEN:   Yes, this is correct. Therefore be relaxed, be easy; if from now on  you continue  
to completely refrain from harming others , then you will definitely have a long life.  So 
be relaxed!  [see “2B4B -2B2B The actual [training]” i n Week 7 Day 3 transcript for clarification 
on this point]  
 
QUESTION:  What kind of reasoning establishes that f rom virtue comes happiness and 
from non -virtue comes suffering?  
GEN:   As an example:  How does it come about that all of us are born human, yet 
some of us have incomplete sense faculties? This comes  from a cause , and not just 
unexpectedly . So what could be the  causes  for this ? Maybe it is the sperm and egg of 
the father and mother? This is not a possibility. Maybe the mother didn‟t take care 
while the baby was in the womb? This is also not the case. So the cause  must be 
something else, it must be seen as an action comi ng from a past life , and  this action 
must have been a negative action which now ripens into this kind of condition.  
 Similarly , some people have a very beautiful appearance , and this also has a 
cause. What is the cause here? Again, did it come from the parents? No. Does it come 
from the time in the womb? No. So again, here the cause has to be seen in the mental 
realm, in a previou s life. Would it originate from a positive or negative cause ?  T he 
cause has to be in accordance, in harmony with the result , and therefore we see that 
the beautiful body must come from a positive action. We will come back to this later.

467 TUESDAY 8 JUNE  2010  - SESSION 1 
 
2 The vital importance of gaining conviction concerning the Buddha’s statements about 
how actions [bring about] effects  
 
The whole presentation of cause and effect is not easy, and so we have to engage into 
studying it. We need to study th e cause and effect of the world or environment, 
described as the container of sentient beings, what leads to what and so on. We also 
need to study the cause and effect relationship of sentient beings, which could also be 
called the contained. It is not eas y to understand the different negative behaviours, 
where this comes from, and kind of effects it all brings. Then there is how all the 
different suffering and happiness comes about. Even just between two human beings, 
there are many differences, how this c omes from different causes and how it works 
and so on, all this is not easy to understand. Therefore, we need to analyse and reflect 
a lot. This kind of understanding will not happen unless we develop a trust with regard 
to the words of the Buddha. This is  essential and this is what this section is about.  
In this regard the King of Concentration [Sutra]  says:  
Even if the moon and stars fell dow n,  
The earth collapsed w ith mountains and w ith tow ns,  
And if the heaven’s aspect w as transformed  - 
Still no w ord  you say w ould be untrue.   [52] 
 
You should thus develop conviction in the w ords of the Tathagata and sustain it. 
Unless you have gained uncontrived certainty about them, you w ill not gain the 
certainty w ith regard to any Dharma that gladdens the J ina.  
 
[Translator: gladdens the Jina  refers to the certainty, not the Dharma. So it should read , “will not 
gain certainty the gladdens the Jina with regard to the Dharma. ”] 
 
What is expressed here is that all these normally stable things might change. Moon and 
stars fell down  refers to their normal way of abiding is changing. The earth collapsed with 
mountains and with towns  again means that what is very stable is also changing. And if 
the heaven’s aspect was transformed - Still no word you say would be untrue.  is referring to 
what the Buddha is presenting is true, and this truth will not change, even though 
these other seemingly stable things will change. Therefore, there is this basis for our 
trust with what the Buddha is presenting, especially with regard to wha t the causes are 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 468 – week one to seven  of happiness and suffering. This is a good basis for trust or faith that is developed by 
analysing these points rather than just having faith because the Buddha said it. 
Through analysing and knowing what the Buddha said this faith can com e about, and 
we can understand dependent arising and the cause and effect of actions. We have 
many ways of cultivating our mind through meditating, and within all these 
meditations, the meditation on cause and effect is the most important one.  
 The method  with which to reflect on cause and effect of actions was presented 
by Lama Tsongkhapa. He presented how our actions have different effects. When we 
talk about the ripening effect, there are two types; 1) a contaminated virtue that is 
bringing a ripening e ffect or 2) a non -virtue that is bringing a ripening effect. These 
both lead to an effect, which is a birth within samsara. This is the ripening effect. 
Next, is called the effect which is in accordance with the cause and most of these are 
the completing k armas. We also looked at the empowering result. With regard to any 
of these three, the effect would be similar or in accordance with the cause. We also 
saw that there were different results, some were experienced in the same life 
(experience that is a visi ble phenomena), some were to be experienced in the next life, 
and then a third type, which was experienced in a future life other than the most 
immediate future rebirth. We should reflect and meditate of this presentation of karma 
and effects.   
 
3 Meditat ion on emptiness becoming helpful for gaining certainty with respect to actions 
and their results  
Some profess to have gained certainty about emptiness and do not have certainty w ith 
respect to actions and their results nor care about it. They have arrive d at a mistaken 
understanding of emptiness for if you understand emptiness, having seen it to be the 
meaning of dependent relation, it becomes helpful for gaining certainty w ith respect to 
actions and their results.  
 
There is a strong connection between me ditating on emptiness and cause and effect of 
actions. We know that something being empty from one point of view means it is a 
dependent arising; things arise depending on a basis. With impermanent phenomena, 
this means they arise depending on a cause. For  example, there are all kinds of plants 
and trees growing outside, which all arise from their respective cause. From these 
causes, arise the present phenomenon such as a tree etc. These are clear cause and 
effect relationships, the present phenomena arisin g from their causes, as a dependent 
arising. Living beings also arise from many causes and through these coming together, 
the result is they come alive and have a body. Within that, living beings experience 
many types of suffering and happiness and this al so has causes, their previous actions. 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
469 We not only have a cause and effect relationship, but also a cause and effect of actions. 
These experiences of happiness and suffering are also a dependent arising, arising in 
dependence on their basis that is their r espective cause, in this case an action. It‟s not 
just a dependent arising, but within this there is a relationship between the cause and 
the effect in that the effect is in accordance with the cause. From a concordant cause, 
a concordant effect is produce d. Whilst this is always the case, if we analyse and try to 
find this concordant cause, there is nothing to be found. From this concordant cause 
which cannot be found, a concordant effect is produced. In this way, dependent arising 
connects with emptiness.   
 This needs to be reflected on again and again. Especially in our present precious 
situation of attaining this state of a human. This very state is outstanding.  
 Older people might have directly witnessed the Second World War, but a long 
time has passed since then, and there are many who have experienced war at first hand. 
However, none of us has had to experience this kind of destruction right in front of 
our eyes . When I was born, the Chinese were still in Tibet, but since the killing and 
fighting was over, I only heard of this from my parents. I saw other things with the 
occupation and suppression, but not the war itself. We are very fortune not to have 
experienc ed this.  
 If in this situation, we think about cause and effect of actions, the fact that we 
live in a time without any war, proves that we have accumulated virtue in the past. This 
is visible, the manifest proof that we have accumulated very positive vir tue.  
 Right now in our world, there is a lot of war going on, a lot of fighting where 
people have to experience violence and destruction, and there are many areas where 
people in poverty don‟t have enough to eat and drink. People experience these strong 
problems right now. We‟ve never had this kind of suffering, this kind of war, this kind 
of poverty and suffering of hunger and thirst. For example, when we went to India last 
winter, there we some people with us that had never seen that sort of poverty befo re. 
It had quite an impact on them. nt seen the poverty. This was incredible, that they had 
never before seen this kind of poverty and craziness. We are living in such a state that 
we‟ve never had to experience this before, and have to go somewhere else to  
experience it. From this, we can see there is an immense difference in the experiences 
of human beings. We must have collected a whole mass of virtue in order to 
experience our present situation.  
 We need to be aware that we are constantly experiencing t hese causally 
concordant effects ripening on us from past virtue. In other words, we have these 
excellent experiences, and they must have a similar cause to each experience.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 470 – week one to seven   The presentation that from positive acts a similar experience of happiness 
comes  about, and that from negative acts a similar experience of suffering comes about 
is quite clear. The Buddha presents this dependent arising of cause and effect; arising 
by depending on something. He‟s not trying to deceive us or giving us extra problems, 
he‟s just describing how things function as they are.  
 In our case, dependent arising refers to what we wish for (happiness) arising 
from its concordant cause of a virtuous action. The suffering we don‟t want is also 
dependant on a case of a similar type of negative past action. Apart from these two 
dependent arising relationships, there is no other possibility. If we understand this 
dependency, we can say, “As this negative action is a dependent arising, and will result 
in suffering, I will not create thi s kind of negative cause. This suffering is depending 
on the negative cause, so I will completely avoid it. As I want happiness and this 
depends on a positive action, there being this relationship of dependent arising, I will 
plant this cause for happiness  and opt for the virtuous activity.” In a nutshell, this is 
what it is all about – avoiding negative actions from which suffering arises, and 
engaging in virtue from which happiness arises. However much we study and read, it 
comes down to this. Avoiding ne gative actions and engaging in positive actions due to 
the dependent arising of the respective effects of suffering and happiness.   
 We talk frequently of the six perfections, and within that, we practice ethics. 
[Translator: Ruling oneself with what is a ppropriate (Tsültrim) is the literal translation.]  We are 
not practicing this perfection to achieve a precious human rebirth, but we practice it to 
become completely enlightened for the sake of all sentient beings. If you practice in 
this way, because you created the virtuous causes of that type, that result naturally 
comes about. From giving up harm towards others and practicing ethical discipline, the 
similar effect is a precious human rebirth including a long life span and being free of 
sickness. Similar ly, by practicing the perfection of generosity, what you‟re aiming at is 
complete enlightenment for the sake of all sentient beings, and so because you‟re 
practicing generosity in this way, the effect is you gain all kinds of resources to enjoy. 
This is na turally happening because you planted the causes, and the concordant effect 
is that you have all these resources, even though you didn‟t aim for them. With 
practicing the perfection of patience, you accumulated virtuous karma, and this results 
in a excelle nt body, abode and retinue. Through patience, you get excellent friends 
rather than non -virtuous friends, an excellent abode rather than a rough abode, and a 
beautiful body rather than an ugly body. All these effects came about because they are 
similar to the cause when you practiced patience, naturally as a dependent arising, even 
though you were not aiming for them, but you were rather aiming at full enlightenment 
for the benefit of all sentient beings. It is the same with the other perfections.  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
471  Some sa y, “This reflection on cause and effect is not very special, you should 
meditate on something deeper like emptiness.” This is an utterly wrong attitude. If 
someone really understands emptiness, then they necessarily come to an understanding 
of dependent ar ising and within that, dependent arising of actions and their effects. To 
the extent that you understand emptiness, your understanding of dependent arising will 
grow. You‟ll come to a mind which has certainty with regard to cause and effect, and 
especially  here the cause and effect of actions as this comes together with the mind 
understanding emptiness. With this strong certainty, you even gain certainty with the 
very hidden aspects of cause and effect.  
 
From the same sutra:  
Like an illusion, a bubble, a m irage or lightning,  
All phenomena are like the moon in w ater.  
Although human beings w ho after death  
Pass to other w orlds do not perceive this,  
{ 118}  Actions once they are done do not disappear,  
White or black they cause like effects to ripen.  
The logical approach is good, but the subtle,  
And hard to see is the J ina’s object of thought.  [53] 
 
Bubble  is like a water bubble bursting on a lake. All phenomena are like the moon in water  
refers to that they appear to be truly existent, but they are not. There is the appearance 
of „truly‟ being there, but under analysis, it cannot be found. This discrepancy with 
what appears and what is found shows that all phenomena are like illusions, an d are 
not as they appear.  
 The first two lines refer to phenomena. The next two lines I read as although 
human beings  who after death pass to other worlds, are not perceived. These two lines posit 
that on the one hand, humans take on a  life, live and die , and they don‟t becoming  
non-existent , but continue  as there is a continuity . On the other  hand , none of this can 
be found. The person who c ontinues cannot be found, so these two lines are  about the 
selfless of person. They appear to have a self but they a re selfless. The first two lines 
are about the selflessness of phenomena. These two levels of selflessness , persons and 
phenomena,  summarise  the presentation of emptiness. Then comes the next line , 
actions once they are done do not disappear . By understand ing emptiness, you come to a 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 472 – week one to seven  stronger understanding of dependent arising  of actions. Dependent arising is expressed 
in these lines, and selflessness was expressed in the first four lines.  
 The person , although it goes  through this life and so on , is no t inherent ly 
existen t. Whilst it is not inherent ly existen t, it has  to take on another life due to the 
power cause and effect of actions. These actions will not go to waste, but quite the 
opposite, white and black actions will bring about their respective effects. Although 
this person is not inherently existent, nevertheless it is in this process of having to take 
rebirth without any freedom, under the power of karma.  
 The logical approach is good  means we should really come to know this 
presentation. Whilst this is the case, with regard to the subtle and hard to see aspects 
of karma, known as very hidden phenomena, we won‟t be able to penetrate this by 
logical analysis. Only the Buddha himself directly perceives this, the Jina’s object of 
thought . There are l evels of cause and effect we can penetrate with our reasoning and 
logic, and it is very good to do so.  
  
4 The uselessness of mere knowledge, if you do not think about the karmic process  
Therefore you should generate certainty about the tw o [types of] karma, the causes 
along w ith their effects, and constantly, day and night, cut [yourself off from] rebirths 
in bad migrations by means of examining the three gates. Not to be proficient at fi rst 
in the distinctions of cause and effect and to know  a bit but still to act carefree w ith 
regard to the three gates is nothing but opening the gate to bad migrations.  
 
Therefore, regarding  these two actions (virtuous and non -virtuous ) we should try to 
produce certainty  in their relationship with cause and effect. Cause referring to virtue 
and non -virtue,  and effect  referring to  happiness  and suffering. On this basis  of having 
produced certainty , one engages  into practice that constantly analyses  the three doors  
of body, speech and mind. One analyses w hat one is doing. For example, if one has 
engaged into negativity,  then one turns away from this  and protects oneself . Once we 
can turn away from negative actions , the causes for lower realms cannot be  creat ed, 
and so being reborn in the lower realms will become impossible . It is really like this. If 
you haven‟t created the causes , then it can be written down and signed, “This person is 
not going to the lower realms.” This is sure.  
 If someone is not skilled with the presentation and division s etc. of cause and 
effect  and doesn‟t know about them, or just knows a little and is prepared to leave it 
like that, it will lead to his three doors coming under a mind that is carefree and does 
not guard against negative  actions. This attitude of ignoring the presentation of cause 
and effect, or becoming complacent with knowing a little is what is described as 
opening wide the gates to the lower realms so they can be easily entered .  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
473  
In the Sutra Requested by [the Naga k ing] Sagara  it says:   
Lord of Nagas, one Dharma of bodhisattvas cuts right through falling back dow n and 
being born in bad migrations. What is that one Dharma? It is this: the individual 
investigation regarding virtuous phenomena thinking: ‘How am I [doin g]? Am I 
straying away [from them] or abiding [in them] night and day?’   
 [54] 
 
This quote says that with all unwished for experiences, like going to bad migrations or 
other perverted paths, or any kind of suffering result, all these processes are cut by o ne 
Dharma. What is this one Dharma? This is talking about bodhisattvas who think, 
“What am I doing?” They analyse if their actions are Dharma in that moment or if 
there is any non -virtue in them. By doing this analysis continually, day and night, one 
is always aware. If one is in virtue one stays there. If not, one counteracts this by not 
allowing the mind to stay there and keeping it on the side of virtue. You then end up 
with the three doors continually on the virtuous side, and as a result, it is impossi ble to 
accumulate the causes for taking rebirth in the lower realms.  
 
TUESDAY 8 JUNE 2010  - SESSION 2 
 
5 How to turn away from faulty conduct having thought about it in this manner  
As for how to turn aw ay from faulty conduct having thought about it in th is manner, 
it says in the Chapter of the Truthful One :  
O king, do not commit the act of killing,  
For every being life is a great loss  
Therefore, w e14 all w ish to keep it long.  
Even deep dow n you should not consider killing. [55]  
 
Here the Buddha is giving advice to definitely abstain from any kind of killing to a king 
who was one of his followers. This is in the context that at that time kings were in 
charge of their subjects and would have been able to kill them. For every being life is a 
great loss  means  if a living being is in danger of being killed, this is a situation where 
one should have empathy and compassion because it is utterly distressing for them if 
they think they are going to be killed. This third line is describing t he effect of 
protecting beings from killing. I read this line as; therefore, you will keep your life long . The 
                                            
14 Nal: Not in the Tibetan.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 474 – week one to seven  effect from having compassion for those about to be killed is you yourself have a long 
life. Even deep down you should not consider killing.  As ther is this relationship between 
not killing and having a long life, one should not have the wish to kill anywhere in 
one‟s mind, even in a dream.  
  
Like this, you should get used to relying on the mind of restraint a lot without your 
motivation even movi ng tow ards misdeeds such as the ten non -virtues.  
 
The quote only mentioned the act of killing, but here Lama Tsongkhapa says you 
should relate it to the other actions of body and speech, through developing a mind of 
restraint. In addition, you should see h ow the non -virtues of mind – covetousness, ill -
will and wrong view – are non -virtuous, and that they have their respective suffering 
results. From the bottom of your heart, without your motivation even moving towards the 
misdeeds , you should restrain yours elf from any of these ten, even in a dream.   
 If you engage into all kinds of negative actions, as described in the ten non -
virtuous actions, even in a brief way, you yourself will most definitely have to 
experience the unwished for effects of suffering a nd dissatisfaction.  
 
6 The principle of action in accordance with statements of the former Kadampas  
Said Khamlungpa to Puchungwa: “Geshe [Dromdon]pa taught only actions and their 
results are of vital importance, how ever, now adays explaining this, listening to it or 
meditating on it are made out to be  w orthless. I w onder, is this just because it is 
difficult to practice?” and Puchungw a said: { 119}  “J ust so.”  
 
Now there are quotes from the Kadampa masters. This reflecting on how our actions 
bring ab out certain results is of utmost importance, and needs to be reflected upon 
repeatedly.  
  
 Dromtonpa said: “Follow ers of J ow o, audacity is totally inappropriate – 
dependent origination is subtle.” Puchungw a said: “In my old age I am [at last] 
getting on w ith the Sutra of the Wise and the Foolish  lx .”  
 And Sharaw a said: “The Buddha taught that whatever errors and faults come 
about, they should not be blamed on a bad direction or a bricklayer’s w ork; it is simply 
that ‘this action having been done this is p roduced’.”  
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
475 The last quote has the essential point. The Buddha taught that if you have performed a 
certain action, this acts as a cause for the arising of that kind of effect. This is exactly 
the cause and effect relationship that shows how different proble ms arise.  
 Someone might then discover they have already engaged into all kinds of 
negative behaviour. Even in this case, it is said, “Don‟t worry – because worries will 
not help you.” For these situations, the Buddha also taught methods to completely 
purify negative behaviour. We need to generate some enthusiasm to purify our mind 
from these negative actions, and not to worry about having accumulated negative 
karma.  
  
QUESTION : When we reflect on the lower realms, and the suffering we might 
experience the re, this is to generate fear about taking a rebirth there. I would say that 
fear and worry are the same kind of thing.  
GEN: Fear and worry here are not the same. Fear coming from meditating on the 
suffering we might experience in the lower realms is a stat e that is accepting it, and 
voluntarily engaging in accordance with what we have become aware of. By generating 
the fear of this possibility, we become energised to engage into guarding ethical 
discipline, and so we don‟t accumulate the causes to go to the  lower realms. This fear 
encourages us to become active. On the other hand, to worry is not activating you, and 
doesn‟t set you on a path of ethical discipline. If you just sit there and worry, it‟s better 
to just forget about the negativity. Here we are t alking about generating an alarming 
fear, which then makes you engage into ethical discipline.  
 For example, if someone is driving a car they think, “I have to be careful whilst 
I‟m driving, I have to take into account the other things around me and not be come 
distracted all fall asleep.” He is very careful and active with this mind. However, 
someone else might drive thinking, “I‟m so afraid, I‟m so afraid...” This person will 
most likely have an accident. This is not the state we should be aiming at, but r ather 
we are talking about someone that, whilst being aware of the danger, is alarmed or 
alert.  
 Another example is a student who knows that he has to take an exam at the end 
of the year. Knowing this, he makes plans and says, “I‟m going to engage into st udies 
right now.” Dealing with the future problem like this, he prepares and then does well 
in the exam. Another student thinks, “I‟m so afraid...” and worries. This will probably 
mean he won‟t take anything in, he won‟t be able to study and will have prob lems 
when it comes to sit the exam. We are talking about the first situation. We see there is 
a problem coming, and so we take it on and prepare for it. This generates a lot of 
confidence and enthusiasm to deal with the problem with various methods.  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 476 – week one to seven   One person reflects on suffering and accepts that suffering is possible. Another 
reflects on the suffering that can happen in the future, and so becomes constantly 
worried and stirred up. There is a great difference between these two. We are looking 
for this m ind of confidence of the first type, a fear [Translator:  maybe better to say 
alarmed] , and from this getting the strength to actually deal with the problem.  
 Such a clear or empowering fear actually enables you to develop minds like 
definite emergence, o r minds of love and compassion for others by seeing their 
suffering. Based on these two minds of definite emergence and compassion, we can 
then generate the bodhimind that aims for full enlightenment for the sake of all 
sentient beings. These minds can all  come about from the minds that actively and 
willingly engage in the topic of suffering in an empowering way. If someone relates to 
suffering by being defensive, afraid and utterly blocked, they could end up in a very 
narrow state and could possibly think they are helpless, alone or overwhelmed, or that 
that they problems to communicate with others and so on – all these kinds of strange 
minds can come about. Then this type of mind can fall into suffering experiences right 
now.  
What do you think, are fear a nd worry the same? If someone sits there being utterly 
afraid of suffering, it is impossible they will generate a mind of definite emergence.  
 Maybe someone is utterly afraid of the topic of impermanence and death; they 
think they can‟t even touch the top ic. They will generate a fear of it now, and will not 
be able to deal with it when death does happen. Another person thinks, “This difficult 
state is coming and I won‟t to know all about it, I will take it on.” They generate a 
capable mind, and they create  causes that help them prepare for death. Then when the 
problem happens, they will be capable of dealing with it.
