2B4B -2B1A -2B3B  Specifically how to purify [non -virtues] by means of the four powers  
1 How negativities and downfalls need to be purified by various means in accordance with 
statemen ts of the Teacher Buddha  
[2 How to redress downfalls  
3 How to redress negative actions  
4 How the four powers are complete with all the antidotes  
5 The manner of purification  
6 How it is necessary to strive not to be tainted by faulty conduct from the sta rt] 
 
Someone who reflects in a correct way on the presentation we have been given on the 
right and wrong ways of behaviour and the presentation of the respective effects will 
come to a state where they can abandon negative behaviour and cultivate conducive  
behaviour. Although it is possible to gain this state, and you do everything possible to 
stay on the positive side, nevertheless there is a possibility that you sully yourself with 
polluted actions. We should not leave it at that, but we should engage int o all the 
different methods of purification that the Buddha has taught.   
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B3B -1 How negativities and downfalls need to be purified by various means in 
accordance with statements of the Teacher Buddha  
Here the text talks about the transgressions  of vows, or in other words, having broken 
the promise to restrain from certain behaviour. With the three sets of vows of the 
pratimoksha, bodhisattva and tantric vows, there are different methods taught by the 
Buddha to purify each of these. You would rel y on the specific methods just as we do 
with Sojong for the Sangha. However, there are negativities that do not have their 
respective methods of purification, and so with these, one relies on the purification 
practice that contains the four opponent powers . 
 
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
479 QUESTION : How do you purify transgressions of lay vows or the vows of refuge?  
GEN: These kinds of transgressions also need to be purified, and this is a case where 
you would use the four opponent powers.  
You can always choose to go to the person with whom you made the 
transgression. You see them, ask for forgiveness, and admit that you made a mistake. 
You could say to them with regret, “I now understand this came from my side, I‟m 
sorry about that. Knowing this is the wrong way to deal with this proble m, I‟m now 
going to abstain from that.” To actually to say this to them is the best, and easiest way 
to deal with this problem. You can do this with friends, family, monks and so on. In 
this way, you can use it as the purification process, and it‟s importa nt that you do 
something like this when you become aware of your mistaken behaviour. Don‟t just 
stay there thinking, “I did this wrong, this went wrong...” and worry about it. Get 
active about it, and engage into some kind of method to counteract it.  In a situation 
like this, were a problem has arisen between people in some kind of relationship, the 
best way to deal with it is to say something to them directly. In situations where this is 
not possible, then you would rely on the four opponent powers.  
QUEST ION: Is it possible to give back the lay vows once you have taken them, or to 
give just one of them back?  
GEN:  It is possible to offer a vow back. The Buddha has always given a way to deal 
with these different situations. The best way to do this is to giv e it back to the person 
who gave it.  
Second: If despite your effort not to be tainted by such fault conduct, some fault occurs 
under the influence of non -conscientiousness or a multitude of mental afflictions etc., 
it is inappropriate to let the matter re st, w ithout giving it any consideration. It is, 
therefore, necessary to make an effort at the means for redressing it taught by the 
compassionate teacher.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B3B -2 How to redress downfalls  
More specifically, as regards redressing dow nfalls, this  should be done as explained 
according to the three individual [types of] vow s.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B3B -3 How to redress negativities  
A The power of remorse  
B The power of thorough application of antidotes  
C The power of preventing faults in future  
D The power o f the support  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B3B -3A The power of remorse  
Redressing negativities should be done by means of the four pow ers. The first of those 
is the pow er of remorse, which is much regret about the non -virtuous deeds one has 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 480 – week one to seven  done since beginningless times. For it to arise it is necessary to meditate on how  the 
three [types of] effects such as the fully ripened ones, are produced. When applying this 
[pow er], you should do it through the confession in the Sacred Golden Light Sutra  and 
the confession by means o f the 35 buddhas.  
 
This power of remorse means you become clear that you have done certain negative 
things or transgressions, and don‟t want to experience the suffering that will come as a 
result. You understand the relationship between the negative action  and the karmic 
ripening of that by understanding the three types of ripening results of the fully 
ripened result, the causally concordant result and the empowering result. From this, 
you generate a mind of regret that generates a mind of regret, and this immediately 
leads to a mind that wants to abstain from this in the future. This is with things we do 
remember. With things we don‟t remember we can think, “Due to my mental set -up, I 
most likely did other negative actions like that.” Once again, being awar e of the cause 
and effect relationship, you generate regret. Then you definitely decide to restrain from 
these kinds of actions in the future.

2B4B -2B1A -2B3B -3B The power of thorough application of antidotes  
1 Based on  the profound sets of sutras  
2 Aspiration for  emptiness  
3 Based on recitation  
4 Based on images  
5 Based on making offerings  
6 Based on names  
The second one, the pow er of thorough application of antidotes, has six points: based 
on the profound sets of sutr as, aspiration for emptiness, based on recitation, based on 
images, based on making offerings, and based on names.  
  
The second power is called the power of applying all antidotes. This is about applying 
an actual antidote to the negativity we previously accumulated, using a virtuous action 
to purify the negativity. There are many types of antidotes, some of the wisdom s ide 
and some of the method side. The wisdom realising emptiness is the strongest of the 
wisdom side and the minds of love and compassion, cherishing others and the 
bodhimind are the strongest of the method side. They are the strongest in the sense 
that the y completely  remove the negativity.  
 If we‟re not able to generate these powerful minds, there is then a six -fold 
presentation of groupings of things that are the power of completely applying all 
antidotes.

This refers to memorising or reciting sutras.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B3B -3B2 Aspiration for emptiness  
The second one consist s in to engage in the reality that is selflessness and clear light 
{ 120}  and to be convinced w ith respect to primordial purity.  
 
This is an aspiring mind to reality, emptiness.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B3B -3B3 Based on recitation

As the flames of forest fires i n spring,

  Page | 482 – week one to seven   
B The signs  
In the Dharani of Exhortation  the signs are said  to be dreaming of vomiting bad food, 
of drinking and vomiting things like yoghurt or milk, of seeing the sun and moon, 
travelling in the sky , fire burning, withstanding buffaloes and black people , seeing a 
sangha of fully -ordained monks and nuns, climbi ng a tree from which milk emerges, an 
elephant or a chief bull, a mountain, a lion throne or a fine house, and of hearing the 
Dharma in your dreams.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B3B -3B4 Based on images  
The fourth one is to create images of the Buddha, having gained confid ence in him.  
 
Literally, this reads as relying on bodies , meaning statues of the Buddha. Here in order to 
purify, people would build representations of the Buddha, stupas and so on, or they 
could sponsor someone to paint a thangka.  
  
2B4B -2B1A -2B3B -3B5 Based on making offerings  
The fifth one is make various types of offerings to the Buddha and his stupas.  
 
For example, offering an elaborate tsog offering, or any other kind of generosity is 
included here.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B3B -3B6 Based on names  
The sixth one is to listen to and retain the names of buddhas and sons of the jinas.  
 
The last one is called relying on the signs, or names. This is happening in some sutras 
where many names of buddhas are mentioned. These would be read out, recited or 
memorised and so on . For example, the mantra OM MUNI MUNI MAHA MUNIYE 
SOHA  is referred to as the name mantra of Buddha Shakyamuni, so reciting mantras 
like this are included in this category.  
 
These are the ones { 121}  that are explicitly mentioned in the Compendium of 
Instructions . There are many others.

483 2B4B -2B1A -2B3B -3C The power of turning away from faults in the future  
1 The actual [power] and its benefits  
The third one - the pow er of turning away from faults in the future – is to completely 
restrain yourself from the ten non -virtues. In the Sun Essence [Sutra]  it says that 
thereby actions of the three doors [committed] by means of killing and so on, as w ell  as 
the afflictive obscurations and obscurations to the Dharmalxi, whatever you have done, 
caused others to do, or rejoiced in before, are destroyed.  
 
Simply, this is a strong decision to no longer engage into the negativity in question. 
One makes the strong  decision, “From now on, with my body, speech and mind I will 
no longer engage into these kinds of negative acts.”  
 
2 The importance of sincere restraint  
The Extensive Commentary [on Vinaya ] explains that the intention of the question 
“[Is there] restrain t henceforth?” being asked in the [basic] text is that a confession 
w ithout sincere restraint is nothing but w ords. Therefore the mind of restraint of not 
doing it henceforth is important. Its development also depends on the first pow er.  
 
It is also made c lear here that such a decision to purify and turn away from the action 
in the future has to come from the bottom of one‟s heart. To just say this will not have 
any benefit or have any effect.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B3B -3D The power of the support  
The fourth one – the pow er of the support – is to go for refuge and cultivate the mind 
of enlightenment.  
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B3B -4 How all the antidotes to negativities are complete within the four powers  
The J ina said that in general there is a diversity of ways for beginners to p urify 
negativities, how ever, the altogether perfect antidote is the four pow ers in their 
entirety.  
 
There are many methods to purify, but in order to purify them completely, the four 
powers have to be complete in that method.  
  
2B4B -2B1A -2B3B -5 The manner  of purification  
A How the karma is not absolutely certain [depending on] the antidotes being complete or 
incomplete etc. in the one doing the purification etc.   
The manner in w hich negativities are purified is as follow s. Whether causes for the 
arising of great suffering in the bad realms become causes for the arising of minor 
suffering, or w hether despite one’s rebirth in the bad realms their sufferings are not 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 484 – week one to seven  experienced or purified in one’s present form as a mere headache, and likew ise w hether 
something that w ould need to be experienced for a long time becomes something short 
or does not have to be experienced at all, is not absolutely certain, because it is al so 
depends on the pow er of the one doing the purification being great or small, as w ell as 
the antidote - the four pow ers - being complete or incomplete, intense or not [intense], 
the duration being long or short and so on.  
 
The power of the purification depends on one‟s own intelligence and motivation. If 
you have these in strong states of mind, there is nothing that cannot be purified. Even 
if you have done something very strong, you can completely transform it so the 
experienced result in very minor. It ‟s possible that something would have ripened as a 
hell rebirth, but with these strong minds you transform it into a headache within this 
life. It is also possible that these strong positive minds have the ability to purify it 
completely, and so there woul d be no result to be experienced. The point is that all 
this depends on the strength of one‟s intelligence and motivation.  
  
B How even karma that is certain [to be experienced] is purified from the root   
As for the statement in sutra and vinaya “Actions  are not lost even in a hundred 
eons”, { 122}  it implies w ithout cultivating the antidote of the four pow ers. How ever, 
in the commentary on the [ Sutra of the Perfection of Wisdom in ] Eight Thousand  
[Verses ] it says that through the antidote of the four pow e rs, if purified in accordance 
w ith the explanations, even karma that is certain to be experienced is purified.  
 
Someone then raises a question, “You say that with these practices you are not only 
able to diminish the result of a negative action but you ca n completely purify it. How 
is this possible?”  
This question refers to statements in the Sutra and Vinaya like “Actions are not 
lost even in a hundred eons.”  If the karma is accumulated, then it doesn‟t dissolve, but 
waits for the time when it is activate d, and so someone questions whether karmic seeds 
can actually be completely purified. The answer is this is talking about karmas you have 
not dealt with by purification, such as not applying the four opponent powers. We 
talked earlier about karma that is n ot certain to be experienced – so this is clear. 
However, even with regard to karma that is definite to be experienced, this can also be 
purified completely if all the four opponent powers are present and fully qualified. As 
a result, it is possible to eli minate this karma.  
  
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
485 C Even if the one doing the purification does not completely purify karma of little strength, 
its effect will not come forth due to the full ripening having been weakened  
In Blaze of Reasoning  it says that once you have thus w eakened the potential to bring 
forth fully ripened effects by means of confession, [the resolve to exercise] restraint and 
the like, the fully ripened effects w ill not come about even if other conditions are met. 
Likew ise, the destruction of roots of virtue throug h w rong view s and anger is also 
similar.   
 
If you have been able to completely purify a karma, or purify it to the point that you 
only receive a minor result, even if it then meets with the conditions that would 
normally bring a ripening effect like takin g a rebirth, it would no longer be capable of 
doing so. It is the same with positive karma. If a moment of anger or wrong views 
could normally destroy the roots of virtue, as it has been transformed, it may not 
completely destroy the positive result. For e xample, you could be about to realize a 
certain level on the path, but through this negativity it would take so much longer to 
attain it, rather than it being impossible to attain it.   
 
2B4B -2B1A -2B3B -6 How it is necessary to make an effort from the start not to be tainted by faults  
Through confession and [the resolve to exercise] restraint you may be able to  purify 
[karma] so that nothing at all remains, how ever, there is [still] a big difference betw een 
the purity of not being tainted by faults from the beginning and the purity after 
confession. For example in the Bodhisattva Levels  it says that in case a root dow nfall 
has occurred, it can be restored by taking the bodhisattva vow  [again], how ever, it w ill 
be impossible to reach the first ground in this l ife. In the Sutra Gathering All the 
Threads , too, it says that once the negativity of abandoning the Dharma, w hich is 
explained in that sutra, has occurred, it w ill be purified, if one confesses it three times 
every day over seven years, but still it will take ten eons even at the quickest to attain 
[the level of] tolerance [on the path of preparation]. Therefore purification w ithout 
remainder means that the production of unpleasant effects is purified w ithout 
remainder. How ever, since the arising of realiz ations of the path and so forth w ill be 
much delayed, you should endeavor not to be tainted from the beginning. Again, for 
that reason it says that aryas do not know ingly engage even in subtle negativities or 
dow nfalls not even for the sake of their lives,  because [otherw ise], if there w as no 
difference betw een purifying them through confession and their not arising from the 
beginning  there w ould be no need to act like that. In the w orld, { 123}  too, if a 
w ounded arm, leg etc. heals again, it still w ill not  be as if it had never been w ounded 
from the start.  
 
Of course, it is by far better to completely refrain from engaging in negativities, than it 
is to purify them after the event. For example, we could apologise and try to repair a 
relationship after we h ave been angry with someone or caused a problem, but better 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 486 – week one to seven  still would be not to break the relationship in the first place. This is what the text says, 
and it‟s quite true isn‟t it?  
 An example of the result of transgressing vows would be that your attain ments, 
which were at hand, are now far away. Although you can purify, there is still some 
effect on your progression along the path, and this is expressed in the Bodhisattva 
Levels. Even if a bodhisattva has committed a root downfall, they can purify and 
restore it by taking the vows again. Nevertheless, by having transgressed the vow in 
the first place, he will be unable to get to the first bodhisattva ground in this life, and 
will have to practice for a much longer period of time to get to this first grou nd.  
 This comes in the context of the sutra path. Then we have the path of Secret 
Mantra where it is not like this. There we have the methods to purify everything 
completely and receive attainments. If we are person who can practice the preparation 
stage and completion stage, you would still be able to attain buddhahood in that very 
life. In Secret Mantra, you can attain enlightenment within one life, even within three 
years etc.  Another sutra quote here is showing there are many discussions in the 
sutras about what you can purify and what you cannot. It says that if you have 
accumulated the negativity of abandoning the Dharma, it is possible to purify it 
through strong purification. However, it also says that a bodhisattva who has received 
such a downfall will take ten eons even at the quickest to attain [the level of] tolerance [on 
the path of preparation] . This is the third of the four levels on the path of preparation, 
the four being heat, peak, forbearance and highest dharma. This is again to be seen in  
the context of the sutra presentation, whereas in Secret Mantra it is different.  
 These all show that it is best to engage into a path that doesn‟t transgress the 
bounds of ethical discipline in the first place, rather than transgressing and then 
engagin g into purification. We could say that rather than getting angry with someone, 
repairing this, then getting angry with them again, then repairing this again – better to 
not get angry in the first place by cultivating the mind of patience. In other words, t o 
forbear the situation which would have otherwise made you angry. By cultivating this 
mind of patience, you will not enter into the negative mind of anger in the first place. 
To not create negativity, and not have to purify it is much better.  
 There are only drawbacks from anger. I say this because there are some who say 
there are benefits from anger. There are no benefits from any point of view. As it is 
only harmful to others and ourselves, we should definitely avoid it. There is no benefit 
for us here in a Dharma community, and we should take this to heart and not engage 
into any harsh words with others, or avoiding others and so on. These activities all 
have drawbacks and there is no benefit for us. As it is not good from any point of 
view, we can thin k of how others perceive someone who is angry or someone who uses 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
487 harsh speech, or avoids others and so on. No one sees them as a good person, but 
rather they see them as being a harmful person. This shows there is no positive aspect 
to anger.  
 An analogy  here might be a woman with a child that is very dear to her. She has 
a lot of affection for the child, and someone then comes along and wants to develop a 
good relationship with the mother. Whilst this person is very friendly with the mother, 
he is consta ntly irritated by the child, getting angry at it and treats the child badly. Will 
he be able to have a good relationship with the mother? Probably not because he‟s 
harming the very object of the mother‟s affection. No way could he be angry and 
harmful to t he child, and at the same time try to establish a good relationship with the 
mother. In the same way, on the one hand, we get angry at living beings, and on the 
other hand we try to establish a good relationship with the Buddhas, the bodhisattvas, 
our guru , and we make prayers to the Three Jewels and so on. Their object of 
compassion is all sentient beings – this is where their compassion and affection is 
directed, and this is whom we are harming. Then afterwards we make all kinds of 
prayers to establish a good relationship with the Three Jewels. This is quite bizarre, 
and it‟s not going to work out.  
 Let‟s say you get up in the morning, take refuge, make all kinds of prayers, make 
prostrations, remember your guru etc, then immediately afterwards you go out  and do 
something negative towards sentient beings, like getting jealous, getting angry and so. 
You just recited all this in front of all the buddhas and the guru in your room, then 
you step out and harm sentient beings, the objects of compassion of the bu ddhas and 
the guru. Needless to say, you will not be able to establish refuge with this kind of 
behaviour.  This is also true in the context of purification. Let‟s say you have 
transgressed something, then you generate regret, go for refuge, generate the 
bodhimind as the antidote, and also make the strong promise to turn away from this in 
the future. Then you do exactly the same negative action again. Then you purify again. 
You again generate regret, apply antidotes, and make the strong promise not to do it 
again, but then you do the action again. You already made this promise that you are 
not going to do it again, but then you do it again. This comes down to deception. This 
is something we have to be careful with, and should turn away from.  
To summarise, if  you engage in a fully qualified form of purification,  then it is 
possible to attain complete purification without any remainder. No suffering result will 
come about .  
 However, whilst this is the case, it is much better not to engage into negative 
actions  from the very beginning , because one aspect of complete purification  is that it 
takes a long time  to get rid of the stains that are left by the negativities .  
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 488 – week one to seven  The Arya Sangha are not even engaging in  even  small negative actions. The re is 
a strong reason f or this. The two situations of having engaged into purification and 
purified the negativity on the one hand, and that you haven‟t accumulated any 
negativity to begin with on the other, are very different. There is a great difference 
between these two.  
 The analogy here is if we have an accident and break a leg. We can go to a 
hospital and get medical treatment and it will heal , but still, it‟s  much better to not 
have broken the leg  in the first place. We shouldn‟t think it‟s ok to break our leg 
because we can get it mended . Actually,  it takes a long time to heal , and even then , it 
will not be exactly the same again . 
 
 
WEDNESDAY 9 JUNE 2010  - SESSION 1 
   
2B4B -2B1B  The measure of the attitude [of lesser beings] having developed  
Second: the measure of those thoughts [of lesser beings] having developed . Previously you had an 
uncontrived interest in this w orld only, and your interest in future existences w as just 
a [vague] understanding fascinated w ith the w ords. When those tw o have been 
exchanged, future w orlds have become the main interest, and this w orld nothing but a 
by-product, [the attitude of lesser beings] has developed.  As it nevertheless needs to be 
made stable, you should stri ve, that is train, in it even after it have arisen.  
 
What is the measure of the mind of a small scope practitioner h aving arisen ? We 
normally have a mind that is only  running after the good things we can achieve in this 
life. This is something we natural ly have without making a lot of effort . This is called 
an uncontrived interest  in our text. Uncontrived is sometimes called non -artificial, which 
means that it takes no effort or training for  this mind to arise. However , to think about 
achieving good situations  in future lives is almost like mere words for us, the actual 
interest or a mind that wants to engage in this is very difficult for us. We have to put 
in a lot of effort  as it‟s  normally a contrived interest for us. This is our situation now , 
and we need to reverse it . If we  can bring about an uncontrived  (without effort) 
interest in future life , whilst interest in  things of this life are just on the side and not 
always arising in  our mind, then this is when we have the mind of a practitioner of the 
lower scope.  
 
 
 
unchecked  transcript                      Gen Losang Jamphel  
 
489 2B4B -2B1C   Eliminating misunderstandings about [the attitude of lesser beings]  
1 Setting out the assertion  
[2 Its refutation]  
 
2B4B -2B1C -1 Setting out the assertion  
In the scriptures it says that it is necessary to turn the mind aw ay from all the 
excellence of cyclic existence, w hich has been a cause of confusion: Some [people] think 
it is inappropriate to develop interest in the high status of an excellent body, 
possessions, and so fo rth because they are [part of]  cyclic existence.   
 
The situation for the assertion is set out here. Turn away  means to  go in the opposite 
direction, meaning that  we need to reverse any attachment to the marvels of samsara. 
It is possible for someone to re ad this and misunderstand it as saying something like, 
“All these higher states of having an excellent body, enjoyments and so on, are all  
samsara , so to generate a longing to attain these excellences is unsuitable because it is 
samsara , and I would be aim ing at samsara.” They think if you are looking for a good 
body etc. then you‟re looking for samsaric objects . This is a misunderstanding.  
 
2B4B -2B1C -2 Its refutation  
A How a continuous succession of excellent bodies is necessary  
With respect to that w hich is to be striven for, there are both temporary and ultimate 
goals to strive for. Even those who strive for liberation from the excellences of a body 
and so forth of cyclic existence need to temporarily strive for them since defin ite 
goodness is to be gained in the end from a continuous succession of such supports.  
 
In order to gain higher states like liberation (nirvana ), or even complete enlightenment, 
you need to practice dharma. To do this you need a Dharma rebirth , that is a body that 
can act as an excellent basis, and  excellent resources to rely on in order to practice 
dharma . We need both of these, the body (the inner condition) and resources (the 
outer condition), in order to be able to practice Dharma, and able to come to these 
states of liberation and complete enlightenment.  
  
B Refutation looking at ultimately high status  
Not all that is the high status of an excellent body, possessions, and retinue are 
included in cyclic existence, for the culmination of an excellent bo dy etc. is the form 
body [of a buddha], the w ealth of its field and its retinue. [It is] w ith this intention 
that the Ornament of the Sutras  says that the first four perfections accomplish the 
high status of an excellent body, possessions, retinue, and und ertakings, { 124}  because 
many [ other] scriptures state that they accomplish the form body.  
 
                 Middle Length Lam -Rim                                 lightly edited transcript  
 
 
  Page | 490 – week one to seven  Not all forms of excellent body, resources and retinue are forms  of samsara. This is 
because the form body of a Buddha is an excellent body. In addition, the wealth  of 
that field (the Buddha field) is an excellent resource that is not samsara. In this field, 
there is the retinue of the buddhas , which is also an excellent retinue , but is not 
samsara.  All these aspects, which in this case are aspects of the enlightened  state, are 
excellent body, resources and retinue, but they are not samsara.  
 This quote from the Ornament of the Sutras  I read as , “the first four perfections 
accomplish an excellent body, possessions and retinue, and by that you are able to attain to th e higher 
states.” Higher states refers to  liberation and complete enlightenment.   
Many other  scriptures state something similar  for the accomplishment of the 
form body.   
 
That concludes the explanation of mental trainings on the stages of the path common  
to lesser beings.  
 
The practices of the lesser beings have now been taught.

The oral teaching was translated live by Ven. Sonam Tharchin and simultaneously transcribed and, later lightly 
edited, by Ven. Lobsang Jinpa and Tom Cerny.  
Whilst the perfect Buddha’s teachings have been presented faultlessly by both Lama Tsongkhapa and Gen 
Jamphel -la, the translator and transcribers are particularly ordinary beings. As the teaching was translated and 
transcribed live, errors are sure to have  been made and for this the translator and transcribers apologise 
unreservedly and request the reader’s patience with their limitations!

xliv Geshe Thubten Ngawang (Tib. Centre Hamburg) offered the following explanation fo r this passage: 
Buddha Shakyamuni, apart from accumulating the collections of merit necesssary for attaining Buddhahood, 
also spent some time accumulating further merit which he dedicated to the goal that his followers may meet 
with the necessary living co nditions. However, according to the laws of karma, they can only experience the 
corresponding results, if they themselves accumulate part of the causes.
